topics.title: Fundamentals of Petroleum Geology and Exploration: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Petroleum Geology and Exploration: Introduction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Petroleum? **Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock + *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, liquid hydrocarbon mixture found beneath the Earth's surface. It encompasses **crude oil**, **natural gas**, and **natural gas liquids (NGLs)**. It is the single most important energy resource of the modern industrial era and the primary feedstock for the petrochemical industry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Petroleum is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons (predominantly alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics) with minor amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and trace metals (Ni, V, Fe), formed over geological time scales from the thermal maturation of buried organic matter. ## 1.2 What is Petroleum Geology? **Petroleum Geology** is the branch of Earth Sciences that applies geological principles to the study of: - The **origin** (source rocks and maturation), - **Migration** (primary and secondary), - **Accumulation** (traps and reservoirs), and - **Distribution** (basin analysis) of hydrocarbons. It integrates stratigraphy, sedimentology, structural geology, geochemistry, and geophysics to predict the location of hydrocarbon accumulations. ## 1.3 What is Petroleum Exploration? **Petroleum Exploration** refers to the engineered, multi-stage process of searching for and evaluating subsurface hydrocarbon accumulations. It progresses from regional reconnaissance (basin-wide studies) to prospect-specific delineation (drilling). ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (The Intuitive View) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Detective and the Hidden Vault"** > Imagine a detective (the petroleum geologist) hunting for a hidden bank vault full of treasure (petroleum) buried somewhere beneath a city (the Earth's crust). > - **Clues on the surface** (surface geology, seeps, satellite imagery) suggest the vault *might* exist. > - **Geophysical surveys** (seismic, gravity, magnetics) act like the detective's ground-penetrating radar to map the underground "rooms and tunnels" (subsurface structures). > - **The vault itself** is the **reservoir rock** (porous, permeable rock that holds oil/gas). > - **The lock on the door** is the **cap rock / seal** (impermeable rock preventing escape). > - **The "tape" that shows the vault has been visited** is the **source rock** (organic-rich, mature). > - **The "delivery truck"** is the **migration pathway** (carrier beds and faults). > - **The "blueprint"** of the entire system is the **Petroleum System**. Just as a detective cannot guarantee a find without drilling, an exploration team cannot confirm a discovery without a **wildcat well**. ## 1.5 Importance in Engineering & Industry Petroleum engineering underpins the **upstream sector** of the oil and gas value chain, including: - Exploration and appraisal drilling, - Reservoir characterization and modeling, - Well design, drilling, and completion, - Production operations and enhanced oil recovery (EOR). The industry is commonly divided into three sectors: | Sector | Scope | Key Activities | |---|---|---| | **Upstream** | E&P (Exploration & Production) | Exploration, drilling, extraction | | **Midstream** | Transportation & Storage | Pipelines, tankers, terminals | | **Downstream** | Refining & Marketing | Refineries, petrochemicals, retail | > [!TIP] > **Standard Metric to Remember:** Global primary energy consumption from oil and natural gas together accounts for approximately **55–60 %** of the world total (as of recent IEA reports). The global proved oil reserve life is roughly **50 years** at current production rates. ## 1.6 Historical Milestones at a Glance | Year | Milestone | Significance | |---|---|---| | 1859 | Drake Well, Pennsylvania, USA | First modern commercial oil well (Col. Edwin Drake) | | 1924 | Seismic refraction survey, Texas | First geophysical exploration success | | 1930s | Reflection seismology | Revolutionized subsurface imaging | | 1947 | Well logging (Schlumberger) | In-situ formation evaluation began | | 1970s | 3-D Seismic | Step-change in trap definition | | 1990s | 4-D Seismic, horizontal drilling | Reservoir monitoring and reach | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Petroleum System — Conceptual Framework The **Petroleum System** is the unifying model used worldwide to describe how an oil/gas deposit forms. It consists of **eight essential elements** (Magoon & Dow, 1994) working in concert over geological time: 1. **Source Rock** — Fine-grained, organic-rich shale/carbonate (TOC > 0.5–1.0 wt %) that generates hydrocarbons upon thermal maturation. 2. **Reservoir Rock** — Porous (φ > 5 %), permeable (k > 0.1 mD) rock that stores hydrocarbons (e.g., sandstone, carbonate). 3. **Seal / Cap Rock** — Impermeable rock (e.g., shale, evaporite) that prevents vertical/ lateral leakage. 4. **Trap** — Geometric configuration (structural, stratigraphic, or combination) that concentrates hydrocarbons. 5. **Migration Pathway** — Carrier beds, faults, and unconformities that allow hydrocarbon movement from source to trap. 6. **Hydrocarbon Charge** — The volume and composition of generated/expelled oil/gas. 7. **Timing** — Trap formation must predate or coincide with hydrocarbon migration. 8. **Preservation** — Post-accumulation integrity (no breaching, biodegradation, or thermal destruction). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** Any **4 of 8** elements failing makes the system non-prospective. "Critical moment" is the geological time at which all elements become synchronously favorable. ## 2.2 Stages of Petroleum Exploration (A Logical Chain) The exploration workflow is **sequential, costly, and irreversible** — each step narrows uncertainty and consumes progressively larger capital: ### Stage 1 — Basin Reconnaissance (Regional) - **Objective:** Identify prospective sedimentary basins. - **Tools:** Remote sensing, geological maps, gravity & magnetic surveys. - **Output:** Basin-scale play fairway maps. ### Stage 2 — Play Analysis (Sub-Regional) - **Objective:** Identify "plays" (combination of source, reservoir, seal, trap type). - **Tools:** 2-D seismic, well correlation, geochemical screening. - **Output:** Risk-weighted play inventory. ### Stage 3 — Prospect Evaluation (Local) - **Objective:** Delineate individual drillable prospects. - **Tools:** Detailed 2-D / 3-D seismic, AVO analysis, reservoir modeling. - **Output:** Volumetric estimates and chance-of-success (CoS) calculations. ### Stage 4 — Exploration Drilling (Wildcat Wells) - **Objective:** Test the prospect directly. - **Tools:** Rotary drilling, mud logging, wireline logging, formation testing. - **Output:** Discovery (commercial) or dry hole. ### Stage 5 — Appraisal & Delineation - **Objective:** Define field boundaries, fluid contacts, productivity. - **Output:** Reserves classification: **Proved (1P)**, **Probable**, **Possible (3P)**. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Approximate dry-hole ratios range from **1 in 5** (in mature basins) to **1 in 10** (in frontier basins). The cost ratio between successive stages is roughly **1 : 10 : 100** (Reconnaissance : Seismic : Drilling). ## 2.3 Classification of Crude Oil by API Gravity The **API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity** is the industry-standard measure of oil density: $$ ^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\text{Specific Gravity at 60 }^\circ \text{F}} - 131.5 $$ | API Gravity (°) | Classification | Commercial Type | |---|---|---| | **< 10** | Extra Heavy | Bitumen / Oil sands | | **10 – 22.3** | Heavy Oil | Requires thermal EOR | | **22.3 – 31.1** | Medium Oil | Conventional | | **> 31.1** | Light Oil | Conventional, premium | | **> 40** | Condensate | Gas-condensate reservoir | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** "Lighter than water floats, heavier sinks" — Water has API = 10. Anything above 10 °API floats on water. ## 2.4 Composition of Natural Gas Natural gas is dominantly methane, with secondary components grouped as: | Component | Typical Mole % | Significance | |---|---|---| | Methane (CH₄) | 85 – 95 | Main combustible | | Ethane (C₂H₆) | 1 – 5 | NGL feedstock | | Propane (C₃H₈) + Butane (C₄H₁₀) | 1 – 3 | LPG | | CO₂, H₂S, N₂ | Trace – 5 | Acid gases, contaminants | **Calorific Value of Natural Gas:** typically **38 – 42 MJ/Nm³** (HHV basis). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $^\circ \text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\gamma_{60}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | Oil density classification | | 2 | $\gamma_{60} = \dfrac{141.5}{^\circ \text{API} + 131.5}$ | dimensionless | Reverse API conversion | | 3 | Specific gas gravity: $G = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}}$ | dimensionless | Gas identification | | 4 | Air-standard molecular weight: $M_{air} = 28.97$ | g/mol | Reference constant | | 5 | Hydrocarbon pore volume: $\text{HCPV} = \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \cdot V_{bulk}$ | m³ or bbl | Reserve estimation | | 6 | STOIIP (volumetric): $N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w)}{B_{oi}}$ | STB (stock-tank barrels) | Oil in place | | 7 | GIIP: $G = \dfrac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w)}{B_{gi}}$ | scf | Gas in place | | 8 | $B_o$ (formation volume factor) | RB/STB | Reservoir fluid shrinkage | | 9 | Recovery factor: $RF = \dfrac{N_p}{N} \times 100\%$ | % | Reserves vs. OOIP | | 10 | Seismic two-way time: $TWT = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{v_{int}}$ | s | Depth-to-time conversion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for KTU 2024:** Conversion constants — **1 acre = 43,560 ft²**, **1 bbl = 5.615 ft³**, **7758** is the acre-ft to barrel conversion factor. The conversion to SI is **1 bbl = 0.158987 m³**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — API Gravity Conversion **Problem:** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of **0.85** at 60 °F. Determine its API gravity and classify it. **Step 1 — Identify the working formula.** $$ ^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_{60}} - 131.5 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given value** $\gamma_{60} = 0.85$. $$ ^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the division** $\dfrac{141.5}{0.85}$. $$ \frac{141.5}{0.85} = 166.47 $$ **Step 4 — Subtract 131.5.** $$ ^\circ \text{API} = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 $$ **Step 5 — Round and classify.** $$ ^\circ \text{API} \approx 35.0 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Light Crude Oil} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Since 31.1 < 35.0 < API limit, this is a **light conventional crude**, suitable for most refineries without prior upgrading. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Specific Gravity from API **Problem:** A crude oil is reported as **40 °API**. Compute its specific gravity and density in **kg/m³**. **Step 1 — Rearrange the API formula** to solve for specific gravity. $$ \gamma_{60} = \frac{141.5}{^\circ \text{API} + 131.5} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $^\circ \text{API} = 40$. $$ \gamma_{60} = \frac{141.5}{40 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{171.5} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the ratio.** $$ \gamma_{60} = 0.8251 $$ **Step 4 — Convert specific gravity to density in kg/m³** using water density at 60 °F, which is **$\rho_w = 999 \;\text{kg/m}^3$** (standard reference). $$ \rho_{oil} = \gamma_{60} \cdot \rho_w = 0.8251 \times 999 $$ $$ \rho_{oil} = 824.3 \;\text{kg/m}^3 $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the reference temperature (60 °F / 15.6 °C) when quoting specific gravity. Marks are frequently lost for omitting this. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — STOIIP Volumetric Calculation **Problem:** A sandstone reservoir has the following average properties: - Area $A = 2000$ acres, - Net pay thickness $h = 30$ ft, - Porosity $\phi = 0.20$, - Water saturation $S_w = 0.25$, - Formation volume factor $B_{oi} = 1.30$ RB/STB. Estimate the **Stock Tank Oil Initially In Place (STOIIP)**. **Step 1 — Write the standard STOIIP volumetric equation.** $$ N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ N = \frac{7758 \times 2000 \times 30 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1.30} $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator step-by-step.** $$ 7758 \times 2000 = 15{,}516{,}000 $$ $$ 15{,}516{,}000 \times 30 = 465{,}480{,}000 $$ $$ 465{,}480{,}000 \times 0.20 = 93{,}096{,}000 $$ $$ 93{,}096{,}000 \times 0.75 = 69{,}822{,}000 $$ **Step 4 — Divide by $B_{oi}$.** $$ N = \frac{69{,}822{,}000}{1.30} $$ $$ N = 53{,}709{,}231 \;\text{STB} $$ **Step 5 — Express in convenient units (million barrels, MMSTB).** $$ N \approx 53.71 \;\text{MMSTB} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** This volumetric estimate is the **starting point** for reserves booking. Real recoverable reserves $= N \times RF$ where typical primary $RF$ is **20–35 %** for light oil, giving here roughly **11–19 MMSTB** recoverable. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Exploration Method Summary | Method | Physical Principle | Depth of Investigation | Typical Cost (rel.) | Data Output | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Surface Geology** | Outcrop mapping, seep surveys | Surface only | Low | Maps, sections | | **Gravity Survey** | Density contrast ($\Delta\rho$) | Basin to prospect | Low – Medium | Bouguer anomaly map | | **Magnetic Survey** | Magnetic susceptibility | Crustal | Low | Magnetic anomaly map | | **2-D Seismic** | Acoustic impedance contrast | 1–10 km | Medium | Time sections | | **3-D Seismic** | 3-D acoustic imaging | 1–8 km | High | 3-D volume, attributes | | **4-D Seismic** | Time-lapse 3-D | Reservoir scale | Very High | Production monitoring | | **Exploratory Drilling** | Direct penetration | Up to 10 km | Extremely High | Cores, logs, tests | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Petroleum System — Block Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **eight elements of the petroleum system** are functionally interconnected, with the **critical moment** as the central synchronizing event. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SOURCE ROCK
Organic-rich Shale or Carbonate] -->|Thermal Maturation| B[HYDROCARBON GENERATION] B -->|Primary Migration| C[EXPULSION FROM SOURCE] C -->|Secondary Migration| D[CARRIER BEDS / FAULTS] D -->|Buoyancy-Driven Flow| E[TRAP CONFIGURATION] E -->|Accumulation| F[RESERVOIR ROCK
Porous and Permeable] F -->|Sealed by| G[CAP ROCK / SEAL
Impermeable] G -->|Preservation Over Geologic Time| H[HYDROCARBON ACCUMULATION] H -->|Discovery| I[EXPLORATION and DRILLING] I -->|Appraisal and Development| J[PRODUCING FIELD] K[CRITICAL MOMENT
Synchrony of Elements] -.-> A K -.-> E K -.-> G K -.-> H L[Regional Geology Context] --> A L --> E L --> G M[TIMING and TECTONIC HISTORY] --> K ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Solid arrows show the directional process flow (source → trap → field). Dotted arrows show the role of the **critical moment** in synchronizing all elements. The diagram is deliberately abstract to highlight functional relationships rather than physical geometry. ## 4.2 Exploration Workflow — Sequential Stage Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[STAGE 1
Basin Reconnaissance] -->|Narrows Area| S2[STAGE 2
Play Analysis] S2 -->|Identifies Prospects| S3[STAGE 3
Prospect Evaluation] S3 -->|Risk and Volumetrics| S4[STAGE 4
Wildcat Drilling] S4 -->|Tested by| D1{Discovery?} D1 -->|Yes| S5[STAGE 5
Appraisal and Delineation] D1 -->|No| S6[Plug and Abandon / Re-evaluate] S5 -->|Reserves Booking| S7[STAGE 6
Development and Production] S7 -->|Surface Facilities| S8[STAGE 7
Surface Operations] S1 -.->|Cost: Low| COST[Cost Rises ~ 10x per Stage] S2 -.-> COST S3 -.-> COST S4 -.->|Cost: Highest| COST S5 -.-> COST S6 -.->|Re-enter Loop| S2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the workflow:** Notice the **feedback loop** from a dry hole back to Play Analysis. This is essential — even unsuccessful wells provide geological information that refines the next attempt. Exploration is therefore **iterative and information-driven**, not purely linear. ## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationships between Key Terms ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PETROLEUM] --> O[CRUDE OIL] P --> G[NATURAL GAS] P --> NGL[NATURAL GAS LIQUIDS] O --> H[HYDROCARBONS] G --> H NGL --> H H --> GEO[PETROLEUM GEOLOGY] GEO --> SRC[Source Rock Studies] GEO --> RES[Reservoir Characterization] GEO --> TRP[Trap Analysis] GEO --> MIG[Migration Pathways] GEO --> EXP[PETROLEUM EXPLORATION] EXP --> GEOCH[Geochemistry] EXP --> GEOP[Geophysics] EXP --> DRG[Drilling] EXP --> LOG[Well Logging] EXP --> DIS[DISCOVERY] DIS --> DEV[DEVELOPMENT] DEV --> PROD[PRODUCTION] PROD --> REF[REFINING / DOWNSTREAM] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Define the term "Petroleum System" and list any four of its essential elements.** **Model Answer:** The **Petroleum System** is a unifying geological model that encompasses all the essential elements and processes required for an oil or gas accumulation to form. It describes the genetically related, time-dependent hydrocarbon fluid system from source to trap, including all geological events and processes. **Any four essential elements:** 1. **Source Rock** — fine-grained, organic-rich sedimentary rock capable of generating hydrocarbons on thermal maturation. 2. **Reservoir Rock** — porous and permeable rock that stores hydrocarbons. 3. **Seal / Cap Rock** — impermeable rock that prevents the upward or lateral escape of hydrocarbons. 4. **Trap** — a geometric arrangement of sealing and reservoir rocks that concentrates hydrocarbons. 5. **Migration Pathway** — the route (carrier beds, faults, fractures) along which hydrocarbons move from source to trap. 6. **Critical Moment** — the geological instant at which all elements are synchronously favorable. *(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **What is API gravity? An oil has a specific gravity of 0.92. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity is an inverse measure of the density of crude oil relative to water, expressed in degrees. Higher API corresponds to lighter oil. **Formula (0.5 marks):** $$ ^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_{60}} - 131.5 $$ **Calculation (1 mark):** $$ ^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 = 153.80 - 131.5 = 22.3 \;^\circ \text{API} $$ **Classification (0.5 marks):** Since 22.3 °API falls in the range **10 – 22.3**, this is a borderline **heavy / medium crude** requiring conventional recovery techniques. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > - **Failing to state reference temperature (60 °F)** when computing specific gravity → **−1 mark**. > - **Confusing STOIIP units (STB) with RB (reservoir barrels)** → **−1 to −2 marks**. > - **In Part B answers, omitting a labelled diagram for trap / petroleum system** → **−2 marks** as per KTU valuation key. > - **Skipping unit consistency** (mixing acre-ft with metric) → **−1 mark**. > - **Writing definitions without examples** in 7-mark sub-parts → examiner deducts for "incomplete elaboration". --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **eight essential elements of the petroleum system**. Briefly explain the concept of the **"critical moment"** with a neat sketch description. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **various stages of petroleum exploration** from basin reconnaissance to field development. Highlight the relative cost escalation and the role of geophysical methods at each stage. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The petroleum system (Magoon & Dow) comprises the following eight elements: 1. **Source Rock:** Organic-rich (TOC > 0.5 wt %), fine-grained shale or carbonate deposited under reducing conditions. Generates hydrocarbons on thermal maturation. The **kerogen type** (I, II, III, IV) determines whether oil or gas is generated. 2. **Reservoir Rock:** A porous (φ) and permeable (k) rock — most commonly sandstone, limestone, or dolomite — capable of holding and yielding hydrocarbons. 3. **Seal Rock:** An impermeable rock (e.g., shale, halite, anhydrite) that traps hydrocarbons by capillary entry pressure. A **cap rock** overlies the reservoir. 4. **Trap:** A geometric arrangement that allows hydrocarbons to accumulate. Classified as **structural** (fault, fold), **stratigraphic** (reef, lens, unconformity), or **combination**. 5. **Migration Pathway:** The route by which hydrocarbons move — primary migration (expulsion from source) and secondary migration (movement through carrier beds to the trap, driven by buoyancy). 6. **Hydrocarbon Generation / Charge:** The volume of hydrocarbons generated, expelled, and available to fill traps. Quantified using source-rock pyrolysis (e.g., Rock-Eval, Pyrograms). 7. **Timing:** The relative timing of trap formation, source maturation, and migration. A trap formed **after** migration cannot accumulate hydrocarbons. 8. **Preservation:** Post-accumulation conditions preventing breaching, biodegradation, wash-out, or thermal destruction (e.g., regional uplift, fault reactivation). **Critical Moment:** The geological instant when the petroleum system is complete — i.e., when the trap exists, the source has matured, and migration pathways are open — and hydrocarbons are actively accumulating. It is determined using burial-history modelling, source-rock maturation plots, and trap-formation dating. #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Stage | Objective | Key Methods | Relative Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Basin Reconnaissance** | Identify prospective basins | Surface geology, satellite imagery, gravity & magnetic surveys | 1 × (baseline) | | **2. Play Analysis** | Identify play fairways | 2-D seismic, geochemical surveys, well correlation | 10 × | | **3. Prospect Evaluation** | Delineate drillable prospects | High-resolution 3-D seismic, AVO analysis, reservoir modelling | 50–100 × | | **4. Exploration Drilling (Wildcat)** | Test the prospect directly | Rotary drilling, mud logging, wireline logs, DST | 1000 × | | **5. Appraisal** | Define field extent | Appraisal wells, extended well tests, 3-D / 4-D seismic | 500–2000 × | | **6. Development** | Plan and execute production | Production wells, surface facilities, injection schemes | Very High | | **7. Production & Surface Ops** | Recover hydrocarbons | Artificial lift, EOR, gathering, processing | Continuous | **Discussion of Geophysical Methods:** - **Gravity and magnetic** surveys rapidly cover large areas, mapping basement structure and basin thickness. - **2-D Seismic** is the workhorse of regional mapping — identifying structures, leads, and prospects. - **3-D Seismic** provides high-resolution volumetric imaging — essential for fault definition and reservoir heterogeneity. - **4-D Seismic** (time-lapse 3-D) is used during production to monitor fluid fronts and bypassed oil. > **Key insight:** Cost escalation between stages is roughly an order of magnitude per step. Geophysical methods progressively de-risk the prospect, lowering the geological chance of success — and the decision to drill remains an **economic**, not just a geological, judgement. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the **origin and migration of petroleum**, including the role of source rock, kerogen, maturation, and the difference between **primary and secondary migration**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sandstone reservoir covers **1500 acres**, with a net pay of **25 ft**, porosity **0.18**, water saturation **0.30**, and oil formation volume factor **1.25 RB/STB**. Compute the **STOIIP** in MMSTB. State two assumptions of the volumetric method. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Origin of Petroleum:** Petroleum originates from **organic matter** (plankton, algae, and higher plant remains) deposited in anoxic sedimentary environments — typically marine basins, swamps, or deep lakes. Upon burial, the organic matter is converted to **kerogen**, a solid, insoluble macromolecule dispersed in the source rock. As the source rock is buried deeper (2 – 5 km typical), temperature rises (60 – 150 °C) and **catagenesis** (oil window) followed by **metagenesis** (gas window) converts kerogen into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons. **Kerogen Types:** - **Type I** (algal, lacustrine) → predominantly oil. - **Type II** (marine, mixed) → oil + gas. - **Type III** (woody, terrestrial) → mainly gas. - **Type IV** (inert, oxidized) → negligible generation. **Primary Migration:** Expulsion of generated hydrocarbons from the fine-grained source rock into a more permeable carrier bed. Driven by internal pressure, micro-fracturing, and diffusion through the kerogen network. Occurs in the oil/gas window (Ro ≈ 0.6 – 1.4 %). **Secondary Migration:** Movement of hydrocarbons through **carrier beds** (high-permeability strata) and along **faults** under buoyancy (oil/gas is lighter than formation water). Secondary migration may cover several kilometres vertically and tens of kilometres laterally. The hydrocarbons continue to migrate until they encounter a **trap** (sealed geometry) where they accumulate, displacing water. > **Key idea:** Migration stops only when a **low-permeability seal** is reached. The seal must have entry pressure greater than the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Identify the STOIIP equation.** $$ N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $A = 1500$ acres, $h = 25$ ft, $\phi = 0.18$, $S_w = 0.30$, $B_{oi} = 1.25$ RB/STB. $$ N = \frac{7758 \times 1500 \times 25 \times 0.18 \times 0.70}{1.25} $$ **Step 3 — Numerator step-by-step.** $$ 7758 \times 1500 = 11{,}637{,}000 $$ $$ 11{,}637{,}000 \times 25 = 290{,}925{,}000 $$ $$ 290{,}925{,}000 \times 0.18 = 52{,}366{,}500 $$ $$ 52{,}366{,}500 \times 0.70 = 36{,}656{,}550 $$ **Step 4 — Divide by $B_{oi} = 1.25$.** $$ N = \frac{36{,}656{,}550}{1.25} = 29{,}325{,}240 \;\text{STB} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to MMSTB.** $$ N \approx 29.33 \;\text{MMSTB} $$ **Assumptions of the Volumetric Method (any two):** 1. Reservoir properties (A, h, φ, S_w) are uniform and average values are representative. 2. The fluids are in static equilibrium (no flow / pressure gradient effects). 3. The OOIP (or STOIIP) remains constant; the method is independent of production rate and recovery mechanism. 4. The hydrocarbon pore volume is connected and continuous. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 1 Introduction** - **Petroleum** = crude oil + natural gas + NGLs; **API gravity** inversely measures oil density; water = 10 °API. - **Petroleum Geology** studies origin, migration, accumulation, distribution; **Exploration** is the engineered search process. - **Petroleum System** has **8 elements** + **2 processes** (generation, migration) + 1 **critical moment**. - The **exploration sequence** is **linear, costly, and irreversible**: Basin → Play → Prospect → Wildcat → Appraisal → Development → Production. - Cost rises roughly **10× per stage**; wildcat drilling is the costliest single step. - **API conversion** uses $^\circ \text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\gamma_{60}} - 131.5$; always specify 60 °F. - **STOIIP** (in STB) = $\dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$; **GIIP** (in scf) is the gas analog. - **Geophysical methods** in order of cost: gravity/magnetic < 2-D seismic < 3-D seismic < 4-D seismic. - **Source rock** must be organic-rich (TOC > 0.5 %), fine-grained, and mature (Ro > 0.5–0.6 %). - **Kerogen types** I & II → oil-prone; III → gas-prone; IV → inert. - **Primary migration** = source-to-carrier; **Secondary migration** = carrier-to-trap (buoyancy driven). - **Trap types**: structural, stratigraphic, combination. **Seal** must have sufficient capillary entry pressure. - **Dry-hole ratio** in mature basins ~ 1:5, frontier basins ~ 1:10. - **API < 10** = bitumen; **10–22.3** = heavy; **22.3–31.1** = medium; **> 31.1** = light; **> 40** = condensate. - The **drilling decision** is the highest-risk, highest-cost irreversible commitment in the exploration chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Wisdom:** When answering 14-mark questions, always **(i)** start with a one-line definition, **(ii)** use labelled bullet points or small tables, **(iii)** include a **diagram** (even a simple block sketch) wherever the question says "describe" or "explain with a sketch", and **(iv)** close with one application / industrial significance line. This guarantees full marks on presentation as well as content.

topics.title: Origin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Origin # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock + *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, liquid hydrocarbon mixture found beneath the Earth's surface, composed primarily of paraffinic, naphthenic, and aromatic hydrocarbons, along with small amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen compounds. The term broadly encompasses both **crude oil** and **natural gas**. **Origin of Petroleum** refers to the comprehensive set of geological, chemical, and thermodynamic processes by which organic matter (deposited in sedimentary basins over millions of years) is progressively transformed into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons under the influence of heat, pressure, microbial activity, and geological time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme, Module 1):** > The "Origin" topic establishes the foundational premise for the entire petroleum engineering discipline. You must master the distinction between *Inorganic* and *Organic* theories and the multi-stage transformation of *Kerogen → Bitumen → Crude Oil → Natural Gas*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Slow Kitchen" Model) Imagine cooking a giant, buried stew that takes **100 million years** to "finish": 1. **Ingredients (Organic Matter):** Dead plankton, algae, and plant debris settle at the bottom of a calm lake or sea — like vegetables falling to the bottom of a slow-cooker. 2. **Sealed Pot (Source Rock):** Layers of mud and silt bury the organic matter, sealing it away from oxygen — just like a pressure cooker lid. 3. **Low Heat Cooking (Catagenesis):** As the pot sinks deeper into the Earth (1–4 km), gentle geothermal heat (~60 °C–150 °C) "cooks" the organic matter, breaking complex molecules into oil. This is the **"oil window."** 4. **High Heat Cooking (Metagenesis):** At greater depths and higher temperatures (>150 °C), oil is "cracked" further into lighter natural gas. This is the **"gas window."** 5. **Migration (The Food Leaving the Pot):** Oil and gas, being lighter and more mobile than the surrounding rock, squeeze out of the source rock and rise upward through porous rocks until they hit an impermeable barrier — like a sealed lid trapping steam. The "stew" (petroleum) is now trapped in a **reservoir rock** beneath an impermeable **cap rock**, awaiting discovery and extraction. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Kerogen** | Insoluble organic matter in source rocks; the precursor to petroleum. | | **Bitumen** | Soluble, heavy organic fraction; intermediate product during kerogen breakdown. | | **Source Rock** | Fine-grained sedimentary rock (shale/marl) rich in organic carbon (TOC > 0.5%). | | **TOC** | Total Organic Carbon — mass fraction of carbon in a rock sample. | | **Catagenesis** | Thermally-driven hydrocarbon generation phase (~60 °C–150 °C). | | **Metagenesis** | High-temperature cracking phase producing dry gas (>150 °C). | | **Oil Window** | Depth/temperature range where liquid hydrocarbons are generated. | > [!NOTE] > The Earth's crust is the **"reactor"**, geothermal gradient is the **"burner"**, and geological time is the **"catalyst"** — together they convert dead biomass into the world's most traded energy commodity. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Two Competing Theories of Origin ### 2.1.1 Inorganic (Abiogenic) Theory Originally proposed by **Mendeleev (1877)** and later championed by **Thomas Gold (1992)**. **Core Hypothesis:** Hydrocarbons were formed deep within the Earth's mantle from inorganic reactions between metal carbides and water/steam at very high temperatures and pressures. $$ \text{Metal Carbide} + \text{Water} \rightarrow \text{Hydrocarbon} + \text{Metal Oxide} $$ **Key Reaction Example:** $$ 2\text{FeC} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 $$ **Supporting Arguments:** - Hydrocarbons detected in meteorites and interstellar space. - Methane found in deep mantle-derived rocks. - Mendeleev observed oil seeps near volcanic regions. **Weaknesses:** - Cannot explain the optical activity of crude oil (chiral biomarkers). - Cannot explain the consistent association of oil with sedimentary basins. - Carbon isotope signatures ($^{13}$C/$^{12}$C ratios) of oil match biological sources. > [!WARNING] > Although inorganic theory is academically interesting, **modern petroleum geochemistry overwhelmingly supports the organic theory**. KTU questions almost exclusively accept the organic theory as the working model. ### 2.1.2 Organic (Biogenic) Theory — The Accepted Paradigm Originally proposed by **Engler (1888)** and **Hofer**, then strongly supported by modern biomarker studies. **Core Hypothesis:** Crude oil and natural gas originate from the buried remains of once-living organisms (primarily marine plankton, algae, and bacteria) that accumulated in anoxic (oxygen-poor) sedimentary environments. **The Four Sequential Stages:** 1. **Deposition:** Organic-rich sediments accumulate in quiet, low-oxygen basins (e.g., deep marine shelves, anoxic lakes). 2. **Burial & Diagenesis:** Sediments are buried under younger layers; mild compaction and microbial action convert soft tissue into **kerogen**. 3. **Catagenesis (Oil Window):** At depths of ~1,000–4,000 m and temperatures of **60 °C–150 °C**, thermal cracking breaks kerogen into liquid hydrocarbons. 4. **Metagenesis (Gas Window):** At depths >4,000 m and temperatures **>150 °C**, remaining kerogen and oil crack into dry natural gas (mostly methane). ### 2.2 Kerogen Classification (Van Krevelen Diagram Logic) Kerogen is classified into **four types** based on its H/C and O/C atomic ratios: | Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Yield | |---|---|---|---| | **Type I (Algal)** | Freshwater algae, lacustrine | High (>1.5) | Excellent — high oil potential | | **Type II (Planktonic)** | Marine plankton, liptinite | Moderate (~1.2–1.5) | Good — oil and gas prone | | **Type III (Humic)** | Higher plants, terrestrial | Low (<1.0) | Mainly gas (with some oil) | | **Type IV (Inert)** | Oxidized/recycled organic matter | Very low | None — dead carbon | ### 2.3 The Maturation Window (Oil & Gas Generation) | Stage | Depth (approx.) | Temperature | Product | |---|---|---|---| | **Diagenesis** | <1 km | <60 °C | Kerogen formation | | **Early Catagenesis** | 1–2 km | 60–90 °C | Wet gas, condensates | | **Peak Oil Generation** | 2–4 km | 90–150 °C | **Crude Oil** | | **Late Catagenesis** | 3.5–5 km | 150–200 °C | Wet gas | | **Metagenesis** | >5 km | >200 °C | Dry gas (methane) | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Geothermal Gradient | $G = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta z}$ | °C/km | Estimate temperature at depth | | Formation Temperature | $T(z) = T_s + G \cdot z$ | °C | Temperature at depth $z$ | | TOC (Total Organic Carbon) | $\text{TOC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{carbon}}}{m_{\text{rock}}} \times 100$ | wt% | Source rock richness | | Transformation Ratio | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{1}+S_{2}}$ | dimensionless | Maturity indicator | | Hydrogen Index | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{S_{2} \times 100}{\text{TOC}}$ | mg HC / g TOC | Kerogen type & potential | | Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) | $R_o$ | % | Thermal maturity scale | | Arrhenius (kerogen cracking) | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | s$^{-1}$ | Rate of hydrocarbon generation | | Lopatin's TTI | $\text{TTI} = \sum \Delta t_n \cdot r^{n}$ | dimensionless | Time-Temperature Index | | Pressure Gradient | $P = \rho g z$ | Pa | Lithostatic / hydrostatic | | Petroleum Density API | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho} - 131.5$ | degrees | Crude oil classification | > [!NOTE] > The **API gravity** formula is universal in petroleum engineering: °API < 10 means extra-heavy oil (like bitumen), °API > 31 means light oil, and °API > 40 means condensate. ### 2.5 Engineering Significance Understanding origin is **not merely academic** — it directly guides: - **Exploration Strategy:** Geochemists analyze source rocks to predict hydrocarbon type and migration pathways before drilling a wildcat well. - **Reserve Estimation:** Maturity maps (using $R_o$, TTI, and biomarker ratios) help estimate the volume of expelled hydrocarbons. - **Reservoir Quality Prediction:** Source-rock characteristics influence whether generated fluids will be trapped or leaked. - **Production Planning:** API gravity (a downstream consequence of origin) dictates refining process selection and well completion design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Temperature at Depth (Geothermal Gradient) The temperature inside the Earth increases with depth due to radioactive decay and residual primordial heat. Assuming a **linear** geothermal gradient (valid for most sedimentary basins over short depth intervals): **Step 1 — Define the geothermal gradient:** $$ G = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta z} $$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:** At the surface ($z = 0$), temperature equals the mean surface temperature $T_s$. At depth $z$, the temperature is $T(z)$. Therefore: $$ \Delta T = T(z) - T_s \quad ; \quad \Delta z = z - 0 = z $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the gradient equation:** $$ G = \frac{T(z) - T_s}{z} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T(z)$:** $$ T(z) = T_s + G \cdot z $$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A basin has mean surface temperature $T_s = 25$ °C and geothermal gradient $G = 30$ °C/km. Find the temperature at the top of the oil window ($z = 2.5$ km): $$ T(2.5) = 25 + (30)(2.5) = 25 + 75 = 100\ \text{°C} $$ This lies inside the oil window (60–150 °C) ✓. ## 3.2 Derivation: Lopatin's Time-Temperature Index (TTI) Lopatin's model assumes that **each 10 °C increase in temperature doubles the rate of organic maturation**, and that maturation is **cumulative** over geological time. **Step 1 — Discretize the burial history into temperature intervals:** Divide the burial column into layers of thickness $\Delta z_n$, each with mean temperature $T_n$. **Step 2 — Define the temperature factor:** $$ r^{n} = 2^{n} $$ where $n$ is the integer index of the temperature interval relative to a reference (typically 100 °C). **Step 3 — Compute the time-temperature index by summation:** $$ \text{TTI} = \sum_{n} \Delta t_n \cdot r^{n} = \sum_{n} \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{n} $$ where $\Delta t_n$ is the time (in **millions of years**, Ma) the layer spends in temperature interval $n$. **Step 4 — Interpret the TTI value:** | TTI Range | Maturation Stage | |---|---| | $< 15$ | Immature | | $15–75$ | Oil generation begins | | $75–160$ | Peak oil generation | | $160–1{,}500$ | Wet gas / condensate | | $> 1{,}500$ | Dry gas only / overmature | ## 3.3 Derivation: API Gravity Classification API gravity expresses crude oil density on a scale where **lighter oils have higher °API**. **Step 1 — Define specific gravity:** $$ \gamma_o = \frac{\rho_o}{\rho_w} $$ where $\rho_w = 1{,}000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at standard conditions. **Step 2 — Apply the API definition:** $$ \text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5 $$ **Step 3 — Example Calculation:** An oil has specific gravity $\gamma_o = 0.85$: $$ \text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 \approx 35\ \text{°API} $$ Since °API > 31, this is a **light crude oil** — characteristic of Type II kerogen maturation. ## 3.4 Derivation: Arrhenius Rate for Kerogen Cracking The thermal cracking of kerogen follows first-order kinetics: $$ \frac{dC_k}{dt} = -k \cdot C_k $$ **Step 1 — Apply Arrhenius rate law:** $$ k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Integrate (from initial kerogen $C_{k,0}$ to remaining $C_k$ at time $t$):** $$ \int_{C_{k,0}}^{C_k} \frac{dC_k}{C_k} = -\int_0^t k\ dt $$ $$ \ln\left(\frac{C_k}{C_{k,0}}\right) = -k t $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the fraction remaining:** $$ \frac{C_k}{C_{k,0}} = \exp(-k t) $$ **Step 4 — Fraction converted to hydrocarbons:** $$ X = 1 - \exp(-k t) = 1 - \exp\left[ -A t \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \right] $$ This equation confirms why **time + heat** are both essential — without millions of years, even high temperatures cannot generate commercial volumes of oil. > [!IMPORTANT] > Typical values for kerogen cracking: $E_a \approx 50\text{–}80\ \text{kcal/mol}$, $A \approx 10^{13}\text{–}10^{16}\ \text{s}^{-1}$. These are empirical ranges; exact values depend on kerogen type and laboratory pyrolysis (Rock-Eval) data. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Source Rock → Reservoir Rock | Stage | Input | Process | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Production | Marine plankton | Photosynthesis + deposition | Organic-rich ooze | | 2. Preservation | Anoxic bottom water | Bacterial sulfate reduction | Sapropel | | 3. Diagenesis | Burial to ~500 m | Microbial + mild compaction | Kerogen (immature) | | 4. Catagenesis | 1–4 km burial | Thermal cracking | Crude oil + wet gas | | 5. Primary Migration | Overpressure | Expulsion from source rock | Oil into carrier beds | | 6. Secondary Migration | Buoyancy | Upward migration | Accumulation in trap | | 7. Trap Formation | Tectonic / stratigraphic | Structural deformation | Reservoir + seal | | 8. Preservation | Cap rock integrity | Sealing over geological time | **Commercial accumulation** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Petroleum Generation Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Marine Organic Matter
Plankton Algae Bacteria] --> B[Deposition in Anoxic Basin] B --> C[Burial and Diagenesis
Depth less than 1 km] C --> D[Kerogen Formation
Immature Organic Matter] D --> E{Catagenesis
60 to 150 degree C} E --> F[Peak Oil Generation
Type I and Type II Kerogen] E --> G[Type III Kerogen Path
Gas Prone] F --> H[Primary Migration
Expulsion from Source Rock] G --> H H --> I[Secondary Migration
Upward via Carrier Beds] I --> J{Trap Encountered} J -->|Yes| K[Reservoir Rock Accumulation
Beneath Cap Rock] J -->|No| L[Leakage to Surface
Seep or Tar Sand] K --> M[Commercial Oil and Gas Field] D --> N{Maturation Stage Check} N -->|Ro less than 0.6| O[Immature] N -->|Ro 0.6 to 1.3| P[Oil Window] N -->|Ro greater than 1.3| Q[Gas Window] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Source-Reservoir-Trap System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SurfaceLayer["Surface Zone"] SURF[Ground Surface and Mean Temperature Ts] end subgraph SourceRockLayer["Source Rock Layer Shale or Marl"] KER[Kerogen Maturation] BIT[Bitumen Intermediate] OIL[Generated Hydrocarbons] end subgraph MigrationLayer["Migration Pathway"] M1[Primary Migration through Microfractures] M2[Secondary Migration through Porous Carrier Beds] end subgraph ReservoirTrapLayer["Reservoir and Trap System"] RES[Reservoir Rock Sandstone or Carbonate] CAP[Cap Rock Impermeable Shale or Salt] TRAP[Hydrocarbon Accumulation] end SURF --> KER KER --> BIT BIT --> OIL OIL --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> RES RES --> TRAP CAP -.Seals.-> TRAP ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Kerogen Type vs. Hydrocarbon Yield ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph KerogenTypes["Kerogen Classification"] T1["Type I
H to C ratio above 1.5
Algal Lacustrine"] T2["Type II
H to C ratio 1.2 to 1.5
Marine Planktonic"] T3["Type III
H to C ratio below 1.0
Higher Plant Humic"] T4["Type IV
H to C ratio very low
Inert Oxidized"] end subgraph YieldMap["Hydrocarbon Yield"] Y1["High Oil Yield"] Y2["Moderate Oil and Gas"] Y3["Gas Dominant"] Y4["None Dead Carbon"] end T1 --> Y1 T2 --> Y2 T3 --> Y3 T4 --> Y4 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above intentionally use simple block-style flowcharts (per the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules). All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`A`, `B`, `KER`, etc.) and contain no markdown formatting inside labels. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **"Briefly explain the organic theory of origin of petroleum. Mention any two key evidences supporting it."** **Model Answer:** The organic theory states that petroleum originates from the buried remains of once-living organisms — primarily **marine plankton, algae, and bacteria** — that accumulated in anoxic sedimentary basins. **Formation Pathway:** 1. Organic matter settles in oxygen-deficient environments (e.g., deep marine shelves). 2. Burial under sediments prevents complete oxidation. 3. Over geological time, microbial and thermal action converts the biomass into **kerogen**. 4. With continued burial (1–4 km, 60 °C–150 °C), kerogen thermally cracks into **crude oil and natural gas**. **Two Supporting Evidences:** - **Optical Activity:** Crude oil rotates plane-polarized light, indicating the presence of chiral biological molecules (a feature absent in inorganic hydrocarbons). - **Biomarker Molecules:** Compounds like **pristane, phytane, and porphyrins** found in crude oil can only originate from chlorophyll — definitive proof of biological origin. - (Optional third evidence) **Carbon Isotope Signature:** $^{13}$C/$^{12}$C ratios in petroleum match those of biological carbon, not mantle-derived carbon. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently omit the **evidence** part and only narrate the formation steps. Marks for the second half of the question will be lost. Always tie theory to **observational proof**. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **"Distinguish between Kerogen and Bitumen. Why is Type I kerogen considered the best oil-prone kerogen?"** **Model Answer:** | Property | Kerogen | Bitumen | |---|---|---| | Solubility | Insoluble in organic solvents | Soluble in organic solvents (e.g., CS$_2$) | | State | Solid, dispersed in source rock | Semi-solid/liquid, mobile | | Origin | Direct product of diagenesis | Product of kerogen thermal breakdown | | Role | Precursor to oil & gas | Intermediate / heavy oil fraction | | H/C Ratio | Higher | Lower | **Why Type I is the Best Oil-Prone Kerogen:** Type I kerogen originates from **freshwater algae and lacustrine deposits** and has: - The **highest H/C atomic ratio** (>1.5), indicating abundant hydrogen for forming liquid hydrocarbons. - The **lowest O/C ratio**, meaning minimal oxygen-bearing groups that would otherwise form CO$_2$ instead of oil. - Algal lipids provide long aliphatic chains that crack efficiently into paraffinic crude oil. As a result, Type I kerogen yields the **largest volume of liquid hydrocarbons per unit mass** during catagenesis. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **organic theory of origin of petroleum**, with a neat sketch of the four stages of hydrocarbon generation. Discuss the role of **temperature, pressure, and time** in the maturation process. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Derive an expression for the **geothermal temperature at depth** and calculate the temperature at a depth of **3.2 km**, given a mean surface temperature of **22 °C** and a geothermal gradient of **28 °C/km**. Comment on whether this depth lies within the **oil window**. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Answer to Q.A (a):** The organic theory, proposed by **Engler and Hofer (1888)**, holds that crude oil and natural gas are derived from the **buried remains of once-living organisms**, predominantly marine plankton, algae, and bacteria. The transformation occurs over millions of years in four sequential stages: **Stage 1 — Deposition:** Organic-rich sediments accumulate in quiet, oxygen-poor aquatic environments such as deep marine shelves, stratified lakes, or restricted basins. The anoxic conditions prevent complete decay, preserving organic carbon (sapropel formation). **Stage 2 — Diagenesis:** As new sediments bury the organic layer to depths of a few hundred meters, mild compaction and bacterial action transform soft tissues into **kerogen** — an insoluble, high-molecular-weight organic polymer. Temperatures remain low (<60 °C). **Stage 3 — Catagenesis (Oil Window):** At depths of **1,000–4,000 m** and temperatures of **60–150 °C**, thermal cracking of kerogen (driven by C–C bond scission) generates **liquid hydrocarbons (crude oil)** and associated wet gas. This is the peak oil-generation phase. **Stage 4 — Metagenesis (Gas Window):** At greater depths (**>4,000 m**) and temperatures **>150 °C**, the remaining kerogen and previously formed oil undergo secondary cracking to produce **dry natural gas** (predominantly methane). **Role of Temperature, Pressure, and Time:** - **Temperature** is the dominant control — kerogen cracking is endothermic and follows Arrhenius kinetics. A 10 °C rise roughly doubles the reaction rate. - **Pressure** primarily affects physical compaction and primary migration (expulsion of hydrocarbons from source rock). - **Time** compensates for low temperatures — old basins at shallow depths can still be mature if given enough geological time (Lopatin's TTI principle). The three factors together are summarized in the "**Tetrahedron of Petroleum Generation**": organic matter + heat + time + pressure → petroleum. --- ### **Model Answer to Q.A (b):** **Derivation of Temperature at Depth:** The geothermal gradient is defined as the rate of temperature increase with depth: $$ G = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta z} = \frac{T(z) - T_s}{z - 0} $$ Rearranging to solve for $T(z)$: $$ T(z) = T_s + G \cdot z $$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given: - Mean surface temperature $T_s = 22$ °C - Geothermal gradient $G = 28$ °C/km - Depth $z = 3.2$ km $$ T(3.2) = 22 + (28)(3.2) $$ $$ T(3.2) = 22 + 89.6 $$ $$ T(3.2) = 111.6\ \text{°C} $$ **Interpretation:** Since the oil window lies between **60 °C and 150 °C**, the calculated temperature of **111.6 °C** falls comfortably within the oil-generation window. Therefore, at a depth of 3.2 km under the given geothermal regime, source rocks are expected to be in the **peak oil-generation phase**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert units (°C/km requires km, not meters) or write $T_s + G \cdot z$ without showing the substitution step. Always **substitute numeric values explicitly** to secure full marks. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative Question [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Classify **kerogen into four types** based on the Van Krevelen diagram. Discuss the hydrocarbon-generation potential of each type and identify which one is most suitable for **oil generation**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A crude oil has a specific gravity of **0.92**. Calculate its **API gravity** and classify it as light, medium, or heavy crude. Also explain how API gravity is influenced by the **origin and maturation** of crude oil. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Answer to Q.B (a):** The **Van Krevelen diagram** classifies kerogen based on the atomic ratios **H/C** (hydrogen-to-carbon) and **O/C** (oxygen-to-carbon). Four types are recognized: **Type I — Algal (Sapropelic) Kerogen:** - Highest H/C ratio (>1.5), lowest O/C ratio. - Origin: Freshwater algae, lacustrine deposits, botryococcus. - Hydrocarbon potential: **Excellent — predominantly oil**. - Example basins: Green River Formation (USA), Bohai Bay (China). **Type II — Planktonic Kerogen:** - H/C ratio ≈ 1.2–1.5, low O/C ratio. - Origin: Marine plankton, liptinite-rich material. - Hydrocarbon potential: **Good — both oil and gas**. - Example: Jurassic-age North Sea source rocks. **Type III — Humic (Woody) Kerogen:** - Low H/C ratio (<1.0), higher O/C ratio. - Origin: Higher terrestrial plants, vitrinite-rich coals. - Hydrocarbon potential: **Gas-prone** (with minor oil/condensate). - Example: Tertiary coal-bearing formations. **Type IV — Inert (Residual) Kerogen:** - Very low H/C ratio, high O/C. - Origin: Oxidized, recycled, or combustion-derived carbon. - Hydrocarbon potential: **None — dead carbon**; does not generate hydrocarbons. **Most Suitable for Oil Generation:** **Type I kerogen** is the best oil-prone kerogen due to its high hydrogen content, which provides the necessary atomic building blocks (H atoms) to saturate long-chain hydrocarbons during thermal cracking. --- ### **Model Answer to Q.B (b):** **Calculation of API Gravity:** The API gravity is given by: $$ \text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5 $$ Given specific gravity $\gamma_o = 0.92$: $$ \text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 $$ $$ \text{°API} = 153.80 - 131.5 $$ $$ \text{°API} = 22.30 \approx 22.3\ \text{°API} $$ **Classification:** | API Range | Classification | |---|---| | < 22 | Extra-heavy / heavy oil | | 22 – 31 | Medium crude | | 31 – 40 | Light crude | | > 40 | Condensate | Since °API ≈ 22.3, the oil falls at the **borderline of heavy-to-medium crude** (slightly above heavy). **Influence of Origin and Maturation on API:** - **Origin:** Crude oils derived from **Type I algal kerogen** at peak maturity are generally **lighter** (higher API) because they contain more paraffinic (alkane) hydrocarbons. Oils from Type III kerogen tend to be heavier and more aromatic. - **Maturation:** As source rocks mature further into the metagenesis stage, **thermal cracking breaks heavy long-chain molecules into lighter short-chain ones**, increasing API gravity. Biodegradation (microbial alteration in reservoirs) has the opposite effect — it **decreases** API gravity by preferentially consuming light fractions. Thus, API gravity is a **downstream fingerprint** of both the source-rock type and the thermal history experienced by the oil. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is computing $\frac{141.5 - 131.5}{\gamma_o}$ instead of $\frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5$. These are algebraically different operations. Memorize the correct form and verify with a sanity check (water = 10 °API, light oil should yield > 31). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Origin of petroleum is governed by the organic theory** — crude oil and natural gas form from buried marine/lacustrine biomass over millions of years. - **The two inorganic theory proponents (Mendeleev, Gold)** are historical references; their model is **not** the accepted paradigm. - **Kerogen is the precursor** to petroleum; it is classified into **four types** (I, II, III, IV) based on H/C and O/C atomic ratios. - **Type I kerogen = best oil source; Type III = best gas source; Type IV = inert.** - **Oil window = 60 °C to 150 °C** (depth ~1–4 km in typical basins); **Gas window = >150 °C**. - **Geothermal gradient formula:** $T(z) = T_s + G \cdot z$ — must use consistent units (km vs m). - **API gravity formula:** $\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5$ — light oil > 31 °API. - **Lopatin's TTI** quantifies cumulative thermal exposure: TTI 15–75 = oil generation; > 1600 = dry gas. - **Arrhenius kinetics** ($k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$) explain why **heat + time** are both essential — geology cannot be rushed. - **Biomarkers (pristane, phytane, porphyrins) and optical activity** are the strongest evidence for organic origin. - **TOC (Total Organic Carbon)** is the primary screening parameter for identifying source rocks; TOC > 0.5% is the minimum threshold for a "good" source rock. - **Migration** occurs in two stages: **primary (source → carrier bed)** and **secondary (carrier → reservoir trap)**. - Always cite **geological time, temperature, and pressure** when describing petroleum origin — these are the three pillars examiners look for.

topics.title: Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Petroleum Geology, the **"Formation" of Oil and Natural Gas** refers to the complete geochemical and geological sequence of processes by which dispersed organic matter (kerogen) buried in fine-grained sedimentary rocks is transformed into accumulations of liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons within porous reservoir rocks. The formation process is conceptually divided into three primary stages: **Generation**, **Migration**, and **Accumulation** (often summarized as the GMA model).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Formation" in Module 1 explicitly refers to the *origin and genesis* of hydrocarbons — i.e., how dead organic matter (plankton, algae, spores, woody tissues) becomes crude oil and natural gas deep within the Earth over millions of years.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **slow-pressure cooker buried deep underground for 100 million years**:
- You start with raw vegetables (organic matter — algae, plankton) sealed inside a thick steel pot (fine mud/clay on the seafloor).
- As more dirt piles on top (sediment burial), the pressure rises and the temperature slowly increases.
- After millions of years of slow "cooking" at low heat, the vegetables transform into a different chemical substance — not the same food, but a black, energy-rich liquid (crude oil) and gas (natural gas).
- That liquid then seeps out of the pot (migration) and gets trapped inside a sponge-like rock layer (reservoir rock) sealed under an impermeable lid (cap rock).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Hydrocarbon formation is *not* a chemical reaction that happens in a refinery — it is a **slow, natural, geological process** requiring (i) source rock, (ii) heat, (iii) pressure, and (iv) time. Without any one of these four ingredients, no petroleum will form.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Oil Window Temperature | **60 °C – 120 °C** | Depth where kerogen cracks into oil |
| Gas Window Temperature | **120 °C – 225 °C** | Depth where oil/kerogen cracks into gas |
| Source Rock Depth | **1,000 m – 5,000 m** | Typical burial depth for oil generation |
| Geothermal Gradient | **~25 °C/km – 30 °C/km** (avg) | Temperature rise per km depth |
| Time Scale for Formation | **10 – 350 million years** | Geological time required |
| Organic Carbon (TOC) in Source Rock | **> 0.5 wt%** | Minimum for viable source rock |

> [!NOTE]
> The **H/C and O/C atomic ratios** of kerogen are the most important diagnostic parameters used by petroleum geochemists to classify organic matter. These ratios are plotted on a **van Krevelen diagram** — a mandatory concept for KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of Hydrocarbon Formation

The formation of oil and natural gas is a continuous geological pipeline. The five stages are:

**Stage 1 — Accumulation of Organic Matter (Deposition)**
- Dead organisms (mainly **marine plankton, algae, and bacteria**) settle on the seafloor or lakebed.
- Preservation requires **anoxic (oxygen-free) conditions** to prevent microbial decomposition.
- Burial begins under layers of fine mud and clay.

**Stage 2 — Diagenesis (Early Transformation at Low Temperature)**
- Burial depth: **< 1,000 m**, temperature: **< 60 °C**.
- Microbial activity (methanogenesis) produces **biogenic methane**.
- Organic matter is converted into **kerogen** — a complex, insoluble solid polymer.
- The kerogen is classified based on its **H/C and O/C atomic ratios** into **Type I, Type II, Type III, and Type IV**.

**Stage 3 — Catagenesis (Thermal Cracking — The "Oil & Gas Window")**
- Burial depth: **1,000 m – 4,000 m**, temperature: **60 °C – 200 °C**.
- Kerogen undergoes **thermal cracking (pyrolysis)**, breaking C–C bonds in long-chain hydrocarbons.
- Liquid hydrocarbons (crude oil) form first (**60 °C – 120 °C**), then wet gas and condensate (**120 °C – 200 °C**).
- This is the most economically important stage.

**Stage 4 — Metagenesis (Late Stage — Dry Gas Generation)**
- Temperature: **> 200 °C**.
- Remaining kerogen and previously formed oil are cracked into **dry methane gas** and graphitic carbon residue.
- Above **~225 °C**, all hydrocarbons are destroyed — leaving only **CO₂ and graphite**.

**Stage 5 — Migration and Accumulation**
- **Primary Migration:** Oil and gas are expelled *out* of the source rock due to compaction and pressure.
- **Secondary Migration:** Hydrocarbons move *upward* through permeable carrier beds (sandstone, limestone).
- **Trapping:** Hydrocarbons accumulate in **structural traps** (anticlines, fault traps) or **stratigraphic traps** beneath an impermeable **cap rock**.

### 2.2 The Four Types of Kerogen (Van Krevelen Classification)

| Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Origin | Hydrocarbon Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I (Algal/Sapropelic)** | High (>1.5) | Low (<0.1) | Algae, lacustrine deposits | **High oil yield** |
| **Type II (Marine/Planktonic)** | Moderate (1.0–1.5) | Low–Moderate | Marine plankton, spores | **Good oil + gas yield** |
| **Type III (Humic/Coaly)** | Low (<1.0) | High (>0.2) | Terrestrial plants, wood, coal | **Mostly gas (dry gas)** |
| **Type IV (Inert/Residual)** | Very Low | Variable | Oxidized/recycled organic matter | **Negligible hydrocarbons** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type I and Type II kerogens are the primary source materials for crude oil.** Type III is the primary source for **natural gas**, and Type IV is essentially barren.

### 2.3 Conditions Required for Petroleum Formation (The "Five Essentials")

For a complete petroleum system to form, the following geological elements must coexist:

1. **Source Rock** — A fine-grained, organic-rich sedimentary rock (typically shale or mudstone) with **TOC > 0.5 wt%**.
2. **Reservoir Rock** — A porous and permeable rock (e.g., sandstone, fractured limestone) to store hydrocarbons.
3. **Cap Rock** — An impermeable seal (e.g., shale, salt) that traps hydrocarbons.
4. **Trap** — A geological configuration (anticline, fault, unconformity) that concentrates hydrocarbons.
5. **Geological Time + Heat** — Sustained temperatures within the **oil/gas window** for millions of years.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = T_0 + G \times D$ | Subsurface temperature; $T_0$ = surface temp, $G$ = geothermal gradient, $D$ = depth | °C, °C/km, m |
| 2 | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Transformation Ratio; $S_1$ = free hydrocarbons, $S_2$ = remaining HC potential | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{TOC (wt%)} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Organic Carbon}}{\text{Total Mass of Rock}} \times 100$ | Total Organic Carbon content | wt% |
| 4 | $\text{Vitrinite Reflectance } (R_o) = \dfrac{I_{\text{sample}}}{I_{\text{standard}}}$ | Maturity indicator (oil window: $R_o = 0.6\% - 1.0\%$) | % |
| 5 | $\text{H/C Ratio} = \dfrac{n_H}{n_C}$ | Atomic hydrogen-to-carbon ratio of kerogen | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{O/C Ratio} = \dfrac{n_O}{n_C}$ | Atomic oxygen-to-carbon ratio of kerogen | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_t}$ | Porosity of reservoir rock; $V_p$ = pore volume, $V_t$ = total volume | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$ | Permeability (Darcy's Law); $Q$ = flow rate, $\mu$ = viscosity, $L$ = length, $A$ = area, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop | Darcy (D) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$)** is the single most important maturity parameter measured in petroleum exploration. Values between **0.6%–1.0%** indicate the **oil window**, **1.0%–1.35%** indicate the **wet gas window**, and **> 1.35%** indicates the **dry gas window**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding formation processes is critical in:
- **Exploration Geology:** Selecting drilling locations based on source rock maturity.
- **Reservoir Engineering:** Predicting hydrocarbon phase (oil vs. gas) before drilling.
- **Unconventional Resources:** Identifying tight oil, shale gas, and coalbed methane plays — all governed by the same formation principles.
- **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS):** Mature, depleted formations are being repurposed for CO₂ sequestration.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Subsurface Temperature from Geothermal Gradient

The temperature at any depth inside the Earth is calculated using the **linear geothermal gradient assumption**, which is valid for depths up to **~10 km** (above the Mohorovičić discontinuity in most basins).

**Starting Equation:**

$$
T = T_0 + (G \times D)
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Temperature at depth $D$ (in °C)
- $T_0$ = Mean annual surface temperature (in °C)
- $G$ = Geothermal gradient (in °C/km)
- $D$ = Depth below surface (in km)

**Step-by-Step Solution — Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A source rock in the Mumbai High Basin is located at a depth of **3,500 m**. The mean surface temperature is **27 °C** and the local geothermal gradient is **28 °C/km**. Determine whether the source rock lies within the **oil window (60–120 °C)**.

**Step 1: Convert the depth to kilometers.**

$$
D = 3{,}500 \text{ m} = 3.5 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values into the temperature equation.**

$$
T = T_0 + (G \times D)
$$

$$
T = 27 + (28 \times 3.5)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the product term.**

$$
28 \times 3.5 = 98
$$

**Step 4: Add the surface temperature.**

$$
T = 27 + 98 = 125 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with the oil window range.**

$$
\text{Oil Window} = 60 \text{ °C} - 120 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
125 \text{ °C} > 120 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

$$
T = 125 \text{ °C} \Rightarrow \text{The source rock lies in the WET GAS / GAS CONDENSATE window, NOT the oil window.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Conversion:** The problem is solved by simple algebraic substitution. Students often forget to convert **meters to kilometers** when the geothermal gradient is given in °C/km — this is a common KTU board exam pitfall.

### 3.2 Derivation: Transformation Ratio (TR) and Kerogen Maturation

The **Transformation Ratio (TR)** quantifies how much of the original hydrocarbon generation potential of a kerogen has been realized.

**Starting Definition:**

$$
\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}
$$

Where:
- $S_1$ = Mass of **free hydrocarbons** (bitumen + oil + gas) already generated per unit mass of rock (in mg HC / g rock)
- $S_2$ = Mass of **residual hydrocarbon potential** remaining in the kerogen (in mg HC / g rock)

**Analytical Steps for the Van Krevelen Interpretation:**

1. **Initial State (Immature Kerogen):** $S_1 \approx 0$, $S_2$ is large, hence:

$$
\text{TR} = \dfrac{0}{0 + S_2} = 0
$$

2. **Peak Oil Generation:** $S_1$ rises sharply, $S_2$ falls.

3. **End of Oil Window:** $S_2$ is very small, $S_1$ is large, hence:

$$
\text{TR} \rightarrow 1
$$

**Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** Rock-Eval pyrolysis of a shale sample yields $S_1 = 4.2$ mg/g and $S_2 = 7.8$ mg/g. Calculate the **Transformation Ratio (TR)**.

**Step 1: Substitute the given values.**

$$
\text{TR} = \dfrac{4.2}{4.2 + 7.8}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the denominator.**

$$
4.2 + 7.8 = 12.0
$$

**Step 3: Divide.**

$$
\text{TR} = \dfrac{4.2}{12.0} = 0.35
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

$$
\text{TR} = 0.35 \Rightarrow 35\% \text{ of the kerogen's hydrocarbon potential has been realized (early-to-mid oil window).}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Kerogen Types vs. Generation Potential

| Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Preferred Product | Best Geological Setting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | **Paraffinic Crude Oil** | Ancient lake beds (e.g., Green River Shale) |
| **Type II** | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | **Mixed Oil + Gas** | Marine shale (e.g., Kimmeridge Clay) |
| **Type III** | < 1.0 | > 0.2 | **Dry Natural Gas** | Coal-bearing strata (e.g., North Sea gas fields) |
| **Type IV** | Very Low | Variable | **None (Inert)** | Oxidized/recycled sediments |

### 3.4 Mathematical Relationship: Maturity vs. Depth (Lopatin's TTI Method — Conceptual)

The **Lopatin Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** method estimates the cumulative thermal exposure of a source rock. The simplified form is:

$$
\text{TTI} = \sum_{i} \Delta t_i \cdot 2^{n_i}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta t_i$ = Time (in millions of years) spent in the $i$-th temperature interval
- $n_i$ = An integer index representing the temperature range:
  - $n = 0$ for $T = 30$ °C – 60 °C
  - $n = 1$ for $T = 60$ °C – 90 °C
  - $n = 2$ for $T = 90$ °C – 120 °C
  - $n = 3$ for $T = 120$ °C – 150 °C
- The **threshold for oil generation** is typically $\text{TTI} \approx 15$.
- The **threshold for gas generation** is typically $\text{TTI} \approx 75$.

**Simplified Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A source rock spent 5 My in the $n = 2$ interval (90 °C – 120 °C) and 3 My in the $n = 3$ interval (120 °C – 150 °C). Calculate the TTI and interpret the result.

**Step 1: Calculate the contribution from the first interval.**

$$
\text{TTI}_1 = 5 \times 2^{2} = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the contribution from the second interval.**

$$
\text{TTI}_2 = 3 \times 2^{3} = 3 \times 8 = 24
$$

**Step 3: Sum the contributions.**

$$
\text{TTI} = 20 + 24 = 44
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

$$
\text{TTI} = 44 > 15 \text{ (oil threshold) and } < 75 \text{ (gas threshold)} \Rightarrow \text{Source rock is in the OIL WINDOW.}
$$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Kerogen Type I Algal] -->|Low Heat Diagenesis| K2[Kerogen Type II Marine]
    K2 -->|Oil Window 60 to 120 °C| OIL[Liquid Crude Oil]
    K2 -->|Gas Window 120 to 200 °C| GAS[Wet Gas and Condensate]
    OIL -->|Further Cracking > 200 °C| DG[Dry Methane Gas]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Conceptual Cross-Section of a Petroleum Trap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SURF[Earth Surface] --> SR[Source Rock Organic Rich Shale]
    SURF --> RR[Reservoir Rock Porous Sandstone]
    SURF --> CR[Cap Rock Impermeable Shale]
    SURF --> BR[Basement Rock Impermeable]

    SR -.->|Primary Migration| RR
    RR -->|Oil Saturated Zone| OZ[Oil Accumulation]
    RR -->|Gas Cap Above Oil| GZ[Free Gas Cap]
    RR -->|Water Saturated Zone| WZ[Formation Water Below]
    CR -->|Seals Hydrocarbons| RR

    classDef rockClass fill:#78716C,stroke:#1C1917,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef fluidClass fill:#DC2626,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef surfaceClass fill:#22C55E,stroke:#14532D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class SR,RR,CR,BR rockClass
    class OZ,GZ,WZ fluidClass
    class SURF surfaceClass
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The diagram is read top-to-bottom. Organic-rich shale at depth generates oil; oil migrates upward through the carrier bed into the porous sandstone; the impermeable cap rock prevents further escape, forming a **structural trap**. Gas (less dense) occupies the top of the trap, oil the middle, and water (heaviest) the bottom — a fundamental concept called **gravitational segregation**.

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Q:** Define *kerogen*. List the four types of kerogen and identify which type generates the most oil.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Kerogen** is the insoluble, complex organic macromolecular matter dispersed in sedimentary source rocks. It is the *precursor* from which oil and natural gas are generated upon maturation.
>
> The four types of kerogen are:
> 1. **Type I (Algal/Sapropelic)** — High H/C ratio (>1.5), low O/C ratio. Derived from algal and lacustrine organic matter. **Generates the highest oil yield.**
> 2. **Type II (Marine/Planktonic)** — Derived from marine plankton and spores. Good source of both oil and gas.
> 3. **Type III (Humic/Coaly)** — Derived from terrestrial woody plants. Predominantly gas-prone.
> 4. **Type IV (Inert/Residual)** — Highly oxidized or recycled organic matter. **No hydrocarbon generation potential.**
>
