### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **Question 3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S7 B.Tech Chemical Engineering, PECHT522]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **complete process of formation of oil and natural gas** starting from organic matter deposition to hydrocarbon accumulation in a reservoir rock. Discuss the role of **geothermal gradient** in determining the depth of the oil window. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **van Krevelen diagram** in detail. How are kerogen types (I, II, III, IV) classified based on H/C and O/C atomic ratios? Which kerogen type is most favorable for **liquid hydrocarbon generation** and why? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question 3A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The formation of oil and natural gas proceeds through **five sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Organic Matter Deposition:** Dead marine organisms (plankton, algae) accumulate on the seafloor under anoxic conditions.

**Stage 2 — Diagenesis:** Burial up to 1 km, temperature < 60 °C. Microbial activity converts organic matter into **kerogen**.

**Stage 3 — Catagenesis (Oil Window):** Burial 1.5–3.5 km, temperature **60 °C – 120 °C**. Kerogen undergoes thermal cracking to produce **liquid crude oil**. The relationship is given by:

$$
T_{\text{depth}} = T_0 + (G \times D)
$$

For a typical basin with $G = 28$ °C/km and $T_0 = 25$ °C, the oil window top is reached at:

$$
D = \dfrac{60 - 25}{28} \approx 1.25 \text{ km}
$$

The bottom of the oil window is at:

$$
D = \dfrac{120 - 25}{28} \approx 3.39 \text{ km}
$$

Thus, the oil window in such a basin lies between **1.25 km and 3.39 km**. A higher geothermal gradient (e.g., 40 °C/km in volcanic arcs) shifts the window *upward* (shallower depth); a lower gradient shifts it *downward*.

**Stage 4 — Metagenesis (Gas Window):** Temperature **120 °C – 225 °C**. Liquid oil is cracked into **wet and dry natural gas (methane)**.

**Stage 5 — Migration and Accumulation:** Primary migration expels oil from the source rock; secondary migration carries it upward through permeable carrier beds; hydrocarbons accumulate in a **structural or stratigraphic trap** sealed by an impermeable cap rock.

> *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for each stage (5 stages = 5 marks) + 2 marks for the geothermal gradient calculation and explanation.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **van Krevelen diagram** is a cross-plot of the **H/C atomic ratio (y-axis)** versus the **O/C atomic ratio (x-axis)** of kerogen, used to classify organic matter into Types I, II, III, and IV based on its elemental composition and thermal maturity.

| Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Origin | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | Algal/lacustrine | **Paraffinic oil** (high H, low O) |
| **Type II** | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Marine plankton | Mixed oil + gas |
| **Type III** | < 1.0 | > 0.2 | Terrestrial/woody | Dry gas |
| **Type IV** | Very Low | Variable | Oxidized | None |

As kerogen matures with depth, it loses hydrogen and oxygen (CO₂, H₂O, hydrocarbons are expelled), so its path on the van Krevelen diagram moves **downward and to the left**, approaching pure carbon (graphite) at the end of metagenesis.

**Type I kerogen is the most favorable for liquid hydrocarbon generation** because:
- It has the **highest initial H/C ratio** (>1.5), meaning abundant hydrogen is available to form C–H bonds in liquid hydrocarbons.
- It has a **very low O/C ratio** (<0.1), meaning minimal oxygen is wasted as CO₂ — more carbon is preserved for hydrocarbon formation.
- It generates **paraffinic (alkane-rich) crude oil** with minimal aromatic content.

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for van Krevelen diagram explanation + 2 marks for kerogen type classification + 3 marks for identifying Type I and justifying with chemical reasoning.)*

---

> **Question 3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Internal Choice Alternative]**
>
> **(a)** What is the **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)**? A source rock spent **8 million years in the temperature interval 60 °C – 90 °C** and **5 million years in the interval 90 °C – 120 °C**. Calculate the TTI and state whether the rock has reached the **oil window threshold (TTI = 15)**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concepts of **primary migration** and **secondary migration** in petroleum geology. What conditions are required for hydrocarbons to be expelled from a source rock? List the **four essential elements of a petroleum system**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question 3B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** is a numerical method (Lopatin's method) used to quantify the cumulative thermal exposure experienced by a source rock over geological time. It is the basis for predicting the maturity of kerogen and the timing of hydrocarbon generation.

The simplified TTI formula is:

$$
\text{TTI} = \sum_{i} \Delta t_i \cdot 2^{n_i}
$$

Where $n_i$ is the temperature index (0 for 30–60 °C, 1 for 60–90 °C, 2 for 90–120 °C, etc.).

**Step 1: Identify the temperature indices.**
- Interval 1: 60 °C – 90 °C → $n_1 = 1$, $\Delta t_1 = 8$ My
- Interval 2: 90 °C – 120 °C → $n_2 = 2$, $\Delta t_2 = 5$ My

**Step 2: Calculate the contribution from the first interval.**

$$
\text{TTI}_1 = 8 \times 2^{1} = 8 \times 2 = 16
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the contribution from the second interval.**

$$
\text{TTI}_2 = 5 \times 2^{2} = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**Step 4: Sum the contributions.**

$$
\text{TTI} = 16 + 20 = 36
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

$$
\text{TTI} = 36 > 15 \text{ (oil threshold)} \Rightarrow \text{The source rock HAS reached the oil window.}
$$

Since $36 < 75$ (gas threshold), the rock is in the **oil window**, not the gas window.

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for TTI definition + 1 mark for correct n-index identification + 2 marks for calculation + 2 marks for interpretation.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Primary Migration** is the **expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock** (typically a fine-grained shale) into adjacent permeable carrier beds. It is driven by:
- **Compaction of the source rock** under overburden pressure, which squeezes out pore fluids.
- **Generation-induced microfracturing** — newly formed hydrocarbons increase pore pressure, causing the source rock to fracture.
- **Thermal expansion** of fluids at elevated temperatures.
- **Osmosis and diffusion** through the kerogen network.

Conditions required for expulsion include: (i) sufficient hydrocarbon saturation in the source rock (typically > 20% of pore space), (ii) sufficient overpressure, and (iii) generation of oil with low viscosity.

**Secondary Migration** is the **upward movement of hydrocarbons** through permeable carrier beds (sandstone, fractured limestone) from the source rock to the **reservoir rock and trap**. It is driven by **buoyancy** (oil and gas are less dense than formation water) and continues until hydrocarbons are trapped by an **impermeable cap rock**.

**The Four Essential Elements of a Petroleum System (Source-Reservoir-Cap-Trap):**
1. **Source Rock** — Generates hydrocarbons (e.g., organic-rich shale with TOC > 0.5%).
2. **Reservoir Rock** — Stores hydrocarbons in its pore space (e.g., porous sandstone, fractured carbonate).
3. **Cap Rock** — Seals the reservoir and prevents hydrocarbon escape (e.g., shale, evaporite, salt).
4. **Trap** — The geological configuration that concentrates hydrocarbons (e.g., anticline, fault trap, salt dome, stratigraphic pinch-out).

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for primary migration + 2 marks for secondary migration + 3 marks for the four essential elements.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** The most common error is failing to convert **depth from meters to kilometers** when using the geothermal gradient equation. Always check units first.
> 2. **Kerogen Confusion:** Students often confuse **Type I and Type III** kerogen. Remember: **Type I = Oil, Type III = Gas.** Use the mnemonic **"I want Oil, III want Gas"** (Roman numeral III = G for Gas).
> 3. **Van Krevelen Axis Reversal:** The H/C ratio is on the **y-axis** and O/C ratio is on the **x-axis**. Reversing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **TTI Index Miscalculation:** The temperature index $n$ starts at 0 for the 30 °C – 60 °C interval, NOT 60 °C – 90 °C. Off-by-one errors here are heavily penalized.
> 5. **Mixing Migration Stages:** Many students conflate primary and secondary migration. Primary = *expulsion from source rock*; Secondary = *movement to trap*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Formation**

- ⭐ **Formation of oil and gas** is a *geological* process spanning **10–350 million years**.
- ⭐ The **GMA Model** (Generation → Migration → Accumulation) is the framework KTU expects in answers.
- ⭐ The **oil window** lies at **60 °C – 120 °C**; the **gas window** lies at **120 °C – 225 °C**.
- ⭐ **Geothermal gradient equation:** $T = T_0 + G \times D$ — convert depth to **km** before using.
- ⭐ **Kerogen Types:** **Type I = Oil (high H/C)**, **Type II = Oil + Gas (marine)**, **Type III = Gas (humic)**, **Type IV = Inert**.
- ⭐ **Van Krevelen Diagram:** Plots H/C (y-axis) vs. O/C (x-axis); maturation moves the path **down and to the left**.
- ⭐ **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$):** Oil window at **0.6% – 1.0%**, wet gas at **1.0% – 1.35%**, dry gas at **> 1.35%**.
- ⭐ **TTI Method (Lopatin):** Oil threshold $\text{TTI} = 15$, gas threshold $\text{TTI} = 75$.
- ⭐ **TOC (Total Organic Carbon)** must be **> 0.5 wt%** for a viable source rock.
- ⭐ **Primary Migration** = expulsion from source rock; **Secondary Migration** = upward movement to trap.
- ⭐ **Four Essentials of Petroleum System:** **Source Rock, Reservoir Rock, Cap Rock, Trap** — never forget one in a 7-mark answer.
- ⭐ **Biogenic methane** forms at low temperatures (diagenesis); **Thermogenic methane** forms at high temperatures (catagenesis/metagenesis).
- ⭐ **Hydrocarbon trap configuration** has gas on top, oil in the middle, water at the bottom (gravitational segregation).
- ⭐ **Over-maturation** above **225 °C** destroys all hydrocarbons, leaving only **graphite and CO₂**.
- ⭐ For numerical problems, always **show step-by-step substitution** — KTU examiners award partial marks for correct methodology even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geological Occurrence and Characteristics of Oil and Natural Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Geological Occurrence and Characteristics of Oil and Natural Gas # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Petroleum (Rock Oil)** is a naturally occurring, flammable, hydrocarbon-rich liquid found in geological formations beneath the Earth's surface. It consists predominantly of a complex mixture of **alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatic hydrocarbons**, with minor proportions of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and trace metals (Ni, V, Fe). **Natural Gas**, in strict KTU 2024 terminology, is a naturally occurring mixture of hydrocarbon and non-hydrocarbon gases found in porous permeable underground reservoirs, either in association with crude oil (**Associated Gas**) or independently (**Non-Associated Gas**). Its primary combustible component is **methane (CH₄)**, typically comprising **70–90 mol %** of the mixture. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** > - **Volume:** 1 Standard Cubic Meter (Sm³) at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and **101.325 kPa (14.7 psia)** > - **Density (Standard Air):** **1.225 kg/m³** > - **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** --- ## 1.2 The Petroleum System — A Five-Element Chain A working **petroleum system** is the integrated geological machinery required for a hydrocarbon accumulation to exist. It consists of **five essential elements** plus **two processes** (per Magoon & Dow, 1994 framework — universally accepted in KTU petroleum geology references): | # | Element | Engineering Function | |---|---------|----------------------| | 1 | **Source Rock** | Generates hydrocarbons from kerogen | | 2 | **Reservoir Rock** | Stores hydrocarbons in pores | | 3 | **Cap Rock (Seal)** | Prevents upward escape | | 4 | **Trap (Geometry)** | Concentrates hydrocarbons in a recoverable volume | | 5 | **Overburden Rock** | Provides thermal maturation pressure | | — | *Process 1:* **Generation & Migration** | Expulsion and movement of HC | | — | *Process 2:* **Accumulation & Preservation** | Trapping and sealing over geological time | --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Underground Battery" > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the Earth's subsurface as a giant **leaky battery system**: > - The **Source Rock** is the *chemical cell* — it slowly produces a charge (hydrocarbons) over millions of years. > - The **Migration Pathway** is the *wire* carrying the charge upward (because oil/gas are buoyant). > - The **Reservoir Rock** is the *capacitor* — a porous sponge that stores the energy. > - The **Cap Rock** is the *insulating plastic wrap* — it stops the charge from leaking to the surface. > - The **Trap** is the *terminal clip* — it concentrates the energy in a specific location. > - A well is simply drilling in to *tap the terminal* and release the stored energy. This mental model immediately clarifies **why** all five elements must coexist — remove any one, and you have **no accumulation**. --- ## 1.4 Origin of Petroleum — Two Competing Theories > [!NOTE] > KTU Module 1 mandates coverage of **both** the **Organic** and **Inorganic** theories of petroleum origin. Marks are frequently awarded for a clear comparative statement. ### A. Organic Theory (Dominant — Universally Accepted) - **Postulate:** Petroleum is derived from the buried remains of **marine and lacustrine organisms** (plankton, algae, bacteria) deposited in anoxic (oxygen-deficient) sedimentary basins. - **Process Pathway:** Organic matter → **Kerogen** (insoluble in organic solvents) → **Catagenesis** (thermal cracking) → **Crude oil & Gas** - **Evidence:** Optical activity of petroleum, presence of **porphyrins** (biomarkers derived from chlorophyll/hemoglobin), and the association of petroleum with sedimentary basins (not igneous/metamorphic). ### B. Inorganic Theory (Mendeleev's Carbide Theory — 1877) - **Postulate:** Hydrocarbons formed deep within the Earth from reactions of metal carbides with water: $$ \text{FeC}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 $$ followed by polymerization of acetylene into heavier hydrocarbons. - **Evidence cited:** Presence of hydrocarbons in some meteorites and in interstellar space; hydrocarbons in volcanic gases. - **Verdict (KTU expected):** The organic theory is now overwhelmingly supported by geochemical evidence. The inorganic theory explains only trace abiogenic methane and is not commercially significant. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students frequently write that "Petroleum comes ONLY from dead dinosaurs." This is **wrong** — the dominant source is **microscopic marine plankton and algae**, not large vertebrates. Marks are deducted for oversimplification. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Source Rocks and Kerogen — The Generative Engine A **source rock** is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically **shale, marl, or carbonate**) containing sufficient **Total Organic Carbon (TOC > 0.5 wt %)** and the right kerogen type to generate and expel hydrocarbons upon thermal maturation. ### 2.1.1 Kerogen Classification (Van Krevelen Diagram Logic) Kerogen is classified by its **H/C** and **O/C** atomic ratios into four types: | Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Origin | Hydrocarbon Yield | KTU Note | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **Type I** (Algal) | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | Lacustrine algae, botryococcus | **Excellent — predominantly oil** | Rare; highest oil-prone | | **Type II** (Planktonic) | 1.2 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Marine plankton, spores | **Very good — oil + gas** | Most common source globally | | **Type III** (Humic) | < 1.0 | > 0.2 | Terrestrial plant material (woody) | **Poor — predominantly gas** | Coal-bearing sequences | | **Type IV** (Inert) | < 0.5 | High | Oxidized/recycled organic matter | **None** | No HC potential | ### 2.1.2 The Three Stages of Kerogen Maturation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently asks:** "At what thermal maturity stage is oil generated?" The answer is the **"Oil Window."** 1. **Diagenesis** (T < 50 °C): Kerogen forms; mainly produces **biogenic methane**. 2. **Catagenesis** (50 °C < T < 150 °C): The **"Oil Window"** — Type I & II kerogen crack into liquid hydrocarbons. Peak oil generation occurs at **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) ≈ 0.6 – 1.0 %**. 3. **Metagenesis** (T > 150 °C): Only **dry gas** (methane) remains. Anything above **Ro > 2.0 %** is overmature — the kitchen is "burnt out." --- ## 2.2 Reservoir Rocks — The Porous Sponge A **reservoir rock** must possess two properties simultaneously: **porosity (storage)** and **permeability (flow)**. The dominant reservoir lithologies are: - **Clastic:** Sandstone, conglomerates - **Carbonate:** Limestone, dolomite (fractured/vuggy types dominate the Middle East giants) ### 2.2.1 Porosity (φ) — Storage Capacity $$\phi = \frac{V_{\text{pores}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$ - **Effective porosity** (φ_eff): interconnected pore volume only (this is what matters in production). - **Reservoir-quality rocks:** **10 % < φ < 30 %**. Carbonate reefs can exceed **40 %**. ### 2.2.2 Permeability (k) — Flow Capacity Darcy's Law (linear, horizontal flow): $$Q = \frac{k \, A \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L}$$ Solving for permeability: $$k = \frac{Q \, \mu \, L}{A \, \Delta P}$$ Where the SI unit is **m²** and the practical field unit is the **Darcy (D)**, where: $$1 \text{ Darcy} = 0.987 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$$ Typical values: **1 mD – 1000 mD** (1 mD = 0.001 D). --- ## 2.3 Cap Rock and Traps — The Sealing Mechanism ### 2.3.1 Cap Rock (Seal) A fine-grained, low-permeability rock (typically **shale, salt, or anhydrite**) with permeability **< 0.001 mD** that prevents vertical migration. Evaporite seals (salt) are the most effective due to their **plasticity** — they self-heal fractures. ### 2.3.2 Traps — The Two Major Families > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A *trap* is a geometric arrangement of rock types that concentrates hydrocarbons at the highest point of a permeable layer. Memorize both classifications — diagrams of trap types are favorite 7-mark questions. **A. Structural Traps** (formed by tectonic deformation) - Anticline (fold) trap — *most common* - Fault trap - Salt dome (piercement) trap **B. Stratigraphic Traps** (formed by depositional or erosional variation) - Lens/pinch-out trap - Unconformity trap - Reef (carbonate build-up) trap --- ## 2.4 Migration of Hydrocarbons | Stage | Mechanism | Driving Force | Path | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Migration** | Expulsion from source rock | Compaction pressure, kerogen cracking | Source → carrier bed | | **Secondary Migration** | Upward buoyant movement through permeable rock | Buoyancy (ρ_oil < ρ_water) + capillary pressure | Carrier bed → trap | | **Tertiary Migration** | Re-migration after trap failure | Trap breach, fault reactivation | Original trap → surface / secondary trap | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Buoyancy Force** driving secondary migration is governed by: > $$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V_{hc}$$ > where **ρ_w** = water density (≈ 1.02 g/cm³), **ρ_hc** = hydrocarbon density. This is **why oil and gas always migrate UPWARD** through tilted permeable beds until trapped. --- ## 2.5 Characteristics of Crude Oil ### 2.5.1 API Gravity — The Master Property $$^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ where **SG** is the specific gravity relative to water at **15.6 °C**. | Classification | API Gravity | Type | Example | |:---|:---:|:---|:---| | **Light Crude** | > 31.1° | High value, low viscosity | Brent, WTI | | **Medium Crude** | 22.3° – 31.1° | Moderate | — | | **Heavy Crude** | 10° – 22.3° | Low value, viscous | Maya (Mexico) | | **Extra-Heavy** | < 10° | Asphalt-like | Orinoco (Venezuela) | ### 2.5.2 Sulfur Content Classification - **Sweet crude:** **S < 0.5 wt %** (premium, low refining cost) - **Sour crude:** **S > 0.5 wt %** (requires desulfurization; H₂S risk) ### 2.5.3 Other Key Crude Properties - **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature at which oil will flow (°C). - **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which oil vapor ignites (**safety critical**). - **Viscosity (μ):** Resistance to flow; light crudes ~1 cP, heavy crudes > 10,000 cP. --- ## 2.6 Characteristics of Natural Gas ### 2.6.1 Composition - **Hydrocarbons:** CH₄ (70–90 %), C₂H₆ (5–15 %), C₃H₈ (2–5 %), butanes, pentanes+ (collectively the **NGLs**). - **Non-hydrocarbon impurities:** N₂, CO₂, H₂S ("sour gas" if H₂S > 4 ppm), He, H₂O vapor. ### 2.6.2 Heating Value $$HHV = \sum_{i} y_i \cdot HHV_i \quad \text{[MJ/Sm}^3\text{]}$$ Typical pure methane HHV ≈ **37.3 MJ/Sm³**. Pipeline-grade gas specification: **HHV > 36 MJ/Sm³** and **H₂S < 4 mg/Sm³**. ### 2.6.3 Compressibility Factor (Z) — Real Gas Correction $$Z = \frac{P V}{n R T}$$ For an **ideal gas**, Z = 1. For natural gas at reservoir conditions (high P, high T), Z can be **0.7 – 1.3**, typically obtained from the **Standing-Katz chart** or the **Dranchuk-Abou-Kassem (DAK) equation of state**. ### 2.6.4 Gas Specific Gravity $$\gamma_g = \frac{M_{\text{gas}}}{M_{\text{air}}} = \frac{M_{\text{gas}}}{28.97}$$ Typical natural gas: **γ_g = 0.55 – 0.75**. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are tested in KTU numericals.** Memorize units and standard conditions. | # | Property | Formula | Units (SI) | Standard Conditions | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | **API Gravity** | °API = 141.5/SG − 131.5 | dimensionless | 15.6 °C, 1 atm | | 2 | **Porosity** | φ = V_pores / V_total | % or fraction | — | | 3 | **Darcy's Law** | Q = k A ΔP / (μ L) | m³/s | — | | 4 | **Permeability** | k = Q μ L / (A ΔP) | m² or Darcy | — | | 5 | **Buoyancy Force** | F_b = (ρ_w − ρ_hc) g V_hc | N | — | | 6 | **Ideal Gas Law** | PV = nRT or PV = ZnRT | Pa, m³, mol, K | — | | 7 | **Real Gas Z-factor** | Z = P V / (n R T) | dimensionless | — | | 8 | **Gas Specific Gravity** | γ_g = M_gas / 28.97 | dimensionless | — | | 9 | **Heating Value (mix)** | HHV = Σ y_i HHV_i | MJ/Sm³ | STP | | 10 | **Formation Resistivity Factor** | F_R = a / φ^m | dimensionless | — | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These properties are the foundation of **reservoir volumetrics** (OOIP = 7758 × φ × (1−S_w) / B_o), **well test analysis**, and **pipeline design**. The Darcy equation alone underpins nearly every **flow assurance and production engineering** calculation in your B.Tech. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!NOTE] > All derivations below follow the **KTU 2024 numerical answer scheme** — every step is shown explicitly. Partial marks in KTU are awarded for correct intermediate steps, so do not skip any. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity The American Petroleum Institute (API) defined gravity relative to water in such a way that **liquids lighter than water yield positive API values** (unlike SG, where oil < 1). **Starting principle:** The hydrometer scale is calibrated so that pure water reads **10°** and a liquid with SG = 1.0 (water) is anchored at 10° on the API scale. The mathematical form is the **inverse-proportionality** between API and SG: $$\text{°API} \propto \frac{1}{SG}$$ **Step 1:** Introduce the proportionality constant to match the water anchor (SG = 1.0 → °API = 10): $$\text{°API} = \frac{C}{SG}$$ At SG = 1.0, °API = 10 ⟹ **C = 10 × 1.0 = 10**? No — this would give the wrong slope. KTU uses the **standard empirical form**: $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ **Step 2:** Verify the water anchor (SG = 1.0): $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10.0\ \text{°API}\ \checkmark$$ **Step 3:** Verify the half-scale reference. A liquid with SG = 0.8 should give: $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.8} - 131.5 = 176.875 - 131.5 = 45.375\ \text{°API}$$ **Step 4:** Convert °API back to SG (useful in KTU reverse problems): $$SG = \frac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5}$$ **Step 5:** Convert SG to density: $$\rho = SG \times \rho_{\text{water}}$$ where **ρ_water = 999 kg/m³ at 15.6 °C** (rounded to **1000 kg/m³** in most KTU problems unless specified). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style) **Problem:** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of **0.876** at 15.6 °C. Calculate (a) its API gravity, (b) its density in **kg/m³**, and (c) classify it. **Solution:** **Part (a) — API Gravity:** $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.876} - 131.5$$ $$= 161.53 - 131.5$$ $$= 30.03\ \text{°API}$$ **Part (b) — Density in kg/m³:** $$\rho = 0.876 \times 999\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 875.12\ \text{kg/m}^3$$ (Using 1000 kg/m³: ρ = 876 kg/m³ — KTU accepts both with proper unit mention.) **Part (c) — Classification:** Since **22.3° < 30.03° < 31.1°** falls in the borderline, by standard KTU classification: $$30.03\ \text{°API} \rightarrow \textbf{Medium Crude (just below light threshold)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in KTU valuation:** Students often confuse 31.1° as the boundary for "light" — it is **> 31.1°** that defines light crude. Equal to 31.1° is **medium**. Memorize: Light = strictly greater than 31.1. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Porosity from Core Plug Measurements **Scenario:** A cylindrical core plug of length **L = 7.62 cm** and diameter **d = 2.54 cm** is saturated with brine of density **1.05 g/cm³**. The **dry mass = 80.21 g**, and the **saturated mass = 95.43 g**. **Step 1 — Total Volume:** $$V_t = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi (2.54)^2}{4} \times 7.62$$ $$= 5.067 \times 7.62 = 38.61\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 2 — Pore Volume (Brine Volume):** $$V_p = \frac{m_{\text{sat}} - m_{\text{dry}}}{\rho_{\text{brine}}} = \frac{95.43 - 80.21}{1.05} = \frac{15.22}{1.05} = 14.50\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Porosity:** $$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_t} = \frac{14.50}{38.61} = 0.3755 = 37.55\%$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** This is a **high-quality reservoir rock** (> 20% porosity is good; 37.55% is excellent — likely a vuggy carbonate or unconsolidated sand). --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Permeability from Darcy's Law **Problem:** A horizontal core plug of **L = 5 cm** and **A = 4.91 cm²** (1-inch diameter) is subjected to a pressure differential of **ΔP = 200 kPa**. Oil of viscosity **μ = 1.5 cP** flows at a rate of **Q = 0.05 cm³/s**. Calculate permeability in Darcy and milli-Darcy. **Step 1 — Unit Conversion (Critical in KTU):** - **L = 5 cm = 5 × 10⁻² m** - **A = 4.91 cm² = 4.91 × 10⁻⁴ m²** - **ΔP = 200 kPa = 2 × 10⁵ Pa** - **μ = 1.5 cP = 1.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** - **Q = 0.05 cm³/s = 5 × 10⁻⁸ m³/s** **Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law:** $$k = \frac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$$ $$= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2})}{(4.91 \times 10^{-4}) \times (2 \times 10^5)}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$(5 \times 10^{-8})(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-2}) = 3.75 \times 10^{-12}$$ **Step 4 — Denominator:** $$(4.91 \times 10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{5}) = 0.0982$$ **Step 5 — Final k:** $$k = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0982} = 3.819 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Darcy:** $$k = \frac{3.819 \times 10^{-11}}{0.987 \times 10^{-12}} = 38.7\ \text{Darcy}$$ $$k = 38{,}700\ \text{mD}$$ **Interpretation:** A permeability of 38,700 mD is **extremely high** — characteristic of **fractured carbonates or vuggy reef reservoirs**. A "tight" reservoir would be < 0.1 mD; conventional sandstones are typically 10–500 mD. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Always show unit conversion **explicitly**. Marks are deducted when students use mixed cgs/SI units without conversion. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Hydrocarbons by Properties | Hydrocarbon | Molecular Formula | State at STP | Boiling Point (°C) | HHV (MJ/Sm³) | Liquid Density (kg/m³) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Methane | CH₄ | Gas | −161.5 | 37.3 | — | | Ethane | C₂H₆ | Gas | −88.6 | 64.6 | — | | Propane | C₃H₈ | Gas (liq under pressure) | −42.1 | 93.2 | 506 (liq) | | n-Butane | C₄H₁₀ | Gas | −0.5 | 121.4 | 580 (liq) | | n-Pentane | C₅H₁₂ | Liquid | 36.1 | 149.0 | 626 | | n-Hexane | C₆H₁₄ | Liquid | 68.7 | — | 660 | | n-Heptane | C₇H₁₆ | Liquid | 98.4 | — | 684 | > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in KTU Module 1?** Because natural gas is **not just CH₄**. The "dry" vs "wet" gas classification depends on the **C₅+ (pentanes-plus) content**: > - **Dry gas:** C₅+ < 0.1 gal/1000 scf (almost pure methane). > - **Wet gas:** C₅+ > 0.1 gal/1000 scf (commercially recoverable NGLs). > - **Condensate:** Liquid hydrocarbons recovered at the surface from wet gas (API > 50°). # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 The Petroleum System — Block Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > The mermaid block diagram below maps the **complete interaction topology** of a petroleum system, including the two process layers (generation/migration and accumulation/preservation). It corresponds to the **KTU Module 1 system-level summary** expected in 14-mark descriptive questions. ```mermaid flowchart TB OM[Organic Matter
Type I II III IV Kerogen] SR[Source Rock
Shale Marl Carbonate] CR[Cap Rock
Shale Salt Anhydrite] RR[Reservoir Rock
Sandstone Limestone Dolomite] TR[Trap Geometry
Anticline Fault Stratigraphic] SP[Surface Facilities
Wellhead Separator] PROC1[Process 1
Generation and Migration] PROC2[Process 2
Accumulation and Preservation] OM -->|deposition| SR SR -->|kerogen cracking at 50 to 150 C| PROC1 PROC1 -->|buoyancy driven upward flow| RR RR -->|laterally updip migration| TR CR -->|seals hydrocarbon column| TR TR -->|drilled by wellbore| PROC2 PROC2 -->|production to surface| SP SR -.thermal maturation.-> SR RR -.porosity and permeability.-> RR CR -.low permeability less than 0.001 mD.-> CR ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read this in the exam:** Trace the flow from **OM (top)** to **SP (bottom)**. The two **PROC** nodes are *process blocks* (not elements) — this matches the Magoon-Dow system definition and earns full marks in KTU Module 1 descriptive questions. --- ## 4.2 Hydrocarbon Migration Pathway — Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR SR[Source Rock
Shale] CB[Carrier Bed
Permeable Sand] TR1[Trap 1
Filled] TR2[Trap 2
Spill Point] SF[Surface Seep
Leakage] SR -->|primary migration
expulsion pressure| CB CB -->|secondary migration
buoyancy upward| TR1 TR1 -->|trap filled to spill| TR2 TR2 -->|breach or leakage| SF ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Spill Point** is the **highest point at which a trap can hold hydrocarbons** before leakage occurs. The trap geometry must close (form a dome) at a level **above the spill point** — this is a frequent 3-mark question. --- ## 4.3 Classification Tree — Kerogen and Hydrocarbon Type ```mermaid flowchart TD KER[Kerogen] KER --> T1[Type I Algal] KER --> T2[Type II Planktonic] KER --> T3[Type III Humic] KER --> T4[Type IV Inert] T1 --> OIL[Predominantly Oil] T2 --> OILGAS[Oil and Gas] T3 --> GAS[Predominantly Gas] T4 --> NONE[No Hydrocarbon Potential] OIL --> WIN[Oil Window Ro 0.6 to 1.0 percent] OILGAS --> WIN GAS --> GWIN[Gas Window Ro 1.0 to 2.0 percent] ``` --- ## 4.4 Trap Architecture — Structural vs Stratigraphic ```mermaid flowchart TB TRAP[Hydrocarbon Trap] TRAP --> STR[Structural Traps] TRAP --> STG[Stratigraphic Traps] STR --> ANTI[Anticline Fold] STR --> FAULT[Fault Trap] STR --> SALT[Salt Dome Piercement] STG --> LENS[Sandstone Lens Pinchout] STG --> UNC[Unconformity Trap] STG --> REEF[Reef Carbonate Buildup] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often classify a **salt dome** as a stratigraphic trap because salt is a sedimentary rock. **It is STRUCTURAL** — it is formed by *tectonic flow* of salt, not by deposition. The salt merely *intrudes* into other rocks post-deposition. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!NOTE] > All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Marks are distributed per KTU guidelines — 3 marks for direct definition, 14 marks for descriptive/numerical with internal choice (a) and (b) sub-parts. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define API gravity. A crude oil has a specific gravity of 0.92. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (1 mark):** API gravity is a measure of how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water, defined mathematically as °API = (141.5 / SG) − 131.5 at 15.6 °C. **Calculation (1.5 marks):** $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 = 153.80 - 131.5 = 22.3\ \text{°API}$$ **Classification (0.5 marks):** Since 22.3° API lies at the boundary of medium-heavy crude, it is classified as **heavy crude** (heavy: 10° < °API < 22.3°; medium: 22.3° ≤ °API < 31.1°). With **22.3° exactly**, by KTU convention, it is at the **upper limit of heavy / lower limit of medium** (commonly accepted as **medium crude** in valuation key). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between source rock and reservoir rock. List any two properties of a good cap rock.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Source Rock | Reservoir Rock | |:---|:---|:---| | Function | **Generates** hydrocarbons | **Stores** hydrocarbons | | Typical lithology | Shale, marl | Sandstone, carbonate | | Porosity | Low to moderate | High (> 10%) | | Permeability | Very low (seal-like) | High (> 1 mD typically) | | Organic content | TOC > 0.5% | TOC not relevant | **Properties of a good cap rock (1 mark each, any two):** 1. **Very low permeability** (k < 0.001 mD) to prevent vertical leakage. 2. **High capillary entry pressure** — must exceed the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column. 3. **Plasticity and ductility** (e.g., evaporite salts self-heal fractures). 4. **Lateral continuity and sufficient thickness** (typically > 10 m for shale, less for salt). 5. **Chemically inert** — does not react with hydrocarbons. --- ## PART B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question 3A [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain in detail the **organic theory of origin of petroleum**, highlighting the role of source rocks, kerogen, and the three stages of thermal maturation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical core sample has dimensions **L = 10 cm**, **d = 3.81 cm**, and dry mass = **150 g**. After saturation with brine (density 1.02 g/cm³), the saturated mass is **175.5 g**. Calculate the **porosity** and **grain density** of the sample. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question 3A #### Part (a) — Organic Theory (7 marks) **1. Origin of Organic Matter (2 marks):** - Petroleum originates primarily from **microscopic marine organisms** — plankton, algae, and bacteria — deposited in **anoxic sedimentary environments** (e.g., deep marine basins, stratified lakes). - Larger organisms (vertebrates) contribute minimally; the term "fossil fuels" is misleading at the species level. **2. Formation of Kerogen (2 marks):** - Upon burial, soft tissues of organic matter undergo **diagenesis** — microbial activity, dehydration, and condensation produce **kerogen**, a macromolecular insoluble solid. - Kerogen classification: **Type I (algal, oil-prone), Type II (planktonic, oil + gas), Type III (humic, gas-prone), Type IV (inert, no potential).** **3. Thermal Maturation Stages (3 marks):** | Stage | Temperature | Process | Product | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | **Diagenesis** | T < 50 °C | Microbial alteration, dewatering | Biogenic methane | | **Catagenesis** | 50 °C < T < 150 °C | Thermal cracking of kerogen (C–C bond breakage) | **Crude oil + wet gas (Oil Window: Ro 0.6–1.0%)** | | **Metagenesis** | T > 150 °C | Secondary cracking of oil to gas | **Dry gas only (Ro 1.0–2.0%)** | Above Ro > 2.0%, the source rock is **overmature** — no further hydrocarbon generation is possible. --- #### Part (b) — Porosity and Grain Density Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Total Volume of Core (1 mark):** $$V_t = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi (3.81)^2}{4} \times 10 = \frac{\pi \times 14.516}{4} \times 10 = 113.99\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 2 — Pore Volume (2 marks):** $$V_p = \frac{m_{\text{sat}} - m_{\text{dry}}}{\rho_{\text{brine}}} = \frac{175.5 - 150}{1.02} = \frac{25.5}{1.02} = 25.0\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Porosity (1 mark):** $$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_t} = \frac{25.0}{113.99} = 0.2193 = 21.93\%$$ **Step 4 — Grain Volume (1 mark):** $$V_g = V_t - V_p = 113.99 - 25.0 = 88.99\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 5 — Grain Density (2 marks):** $$\rho_g = \frac{m_{\text{dry}}}{V_g} = \frac{150}{88.99} = 1.686\ \text{g/cm}^3$$ **Interpretation:** Porosity of ~22% is a **good reservoir quality**. Grain density of 1.686 g/cm³ is below the quartz density (2.65 g/cm³), suggesting the presence of **clay minerals** (e.g., illite, kaolinite) or other low-density minerals like **calcite (2.71)** combined with clays. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget that **grain density ≠ bulk density**. Bulk density uses total volume (including pores); grain density uses only the solid matrix. The correct formula for **grain density** is `m_dry / V_grain`, NOT `m_dry / V_total`. Marks are deducted for this confusion. --- ### Question 3B — ALTERNATIVE (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the different **types of hydrocarbon traps** (structural and stratigraphic). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A core plug (**L = 7 cm, A = 5 cm²**) is tested in a Darcy apparatus. Oil of viscosity **μ = 2 cP** flows at **Q = 0.02 cm³/s** under a pressure drop of **ΔP = 150 kPa**. Calculate the **permeability in mD** and classify the reservoir quality. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question 3B #### Part (a) — Trap Types (7 marks) (Refer to Section 4.4 mermaid diagram for the classification tree; describe in words here.) **Structural Traps (4 marks):** 1. **Anticline Trap:** Upward-arching fold in reservoir rock capped by impermeable rock. Hydrocarbons migrate upward and accumulate at the crest. *Example: Ghawar Field, Saudi Arabia.* 2. **Fault Trap:** Impermeable fault plane juxtaposes reservoir rock against sealing rock, blocking lateral/vertical migration. *Example: many North Sea fields.* 3. **Salt Dome (Piercement) Trap:** Salt diapir intrudes upward, deforming overlying strata into a dome. Hydrocarbons accumulate in the dome-shaped reservoir around the salt. *Example: Spindletop, Texas.* **Stratigraphic Traps (3 marks):** 1. **Lens / Pinch-out Trap:** Sandstone lens or wedge-shaped reservoir thins to zero (pinch-out) into shale. *Example: many East Texas fields.* 2. **Unconformity Trap:** Erosion surface truncates reservoir rock, then is overlain by impermeable rock. *Example: Prudhoe Bay, Alaska.* 3. **Reef Trap:** Carbonate reef build-up in shale — porous reef core holds hydrocarbons. --- #### Part (b) — Permeability Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Unit Conversions (2 marks):** - L = 7 cm = 7 × 10⁻² m - A = 5 cm² = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m² - ΔP = 150 kPa = 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa - μ = 2 cP = 2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s - Q = 0.02 cm³/s = 2 × 10⁻⁸ m³/s **Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law (2 marks):** $$k = \frac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator (1 mark):** $$(2 \times 10^{-8})(2 \times 10^{-3})(7 \times 10^{-2}) = 2.8 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Denominator (1 mark):** $$(5 \times 10^{-4})(1.5 \times 10^5) = 75\ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 5 — k in m² (0.5 marks):** $$k = \frac{2.8 \times 10^{-12}}{75} = 3.733 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mD (0.5 marks):** $$k = \frac{3.733 \times 10^{-14}}{0.987 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.0378\ \text{Darcy} = 37.8\ \text{mD}$$ **Classification:** A permeability of **37.8 mD** is a **good reservoir** (typical conventional sandstone range is 10–500 mD). The reservoir is classified as a **conventional, producible reservoir** with no need for hydraulic fracturing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Warning — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** (cgs ↔ SI). Darcy's Law requires consistent SI units (Pa·s, m², Pa, m³/s). Marks lost silently. > 2. **Misclassifying "1 Darcy" — it equals 0.987 × 10⁻¹² m², NOT 10⁻¹² m².** The 0.987 factor is critical in KTU numericals. > 3. **Confusing "porosity" with "permeability"** — high porosity does not guarantee high permeability. Shale has 30% porosity but < 0.001 mD permeability. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Geological Occurrence and Characteristics of Oil & Gas** ### Core Concepts - **Petroleum origin:** Organic theory is dominant; **inorganic theory** (Mendeleev) is historically important but not commercially significant. - **Five elements of a petroleum system:** Source, Reservoir, Cap, Trap, Overburden + two processes (generation/migration & accumulation/preservation). - **Kerogen types:** **Type I (oil), Type II (oil + gas), Type III (gas), Type IV (none)**. Memorize via the **Van Krevelen H/C and O/C** ratio logic. - **Oil Window:** Catagenesis stage, **50 °C < T < 150 °C**, **Ro 0.6 – 1.0%** for peak oil generation. - **Migration:** Primary (compaction) and secondary (buoyancy). HC always migrates **upward** due to lower density than water. - **API Gravity:** **°API = 141.5 / SG − 131.5**. Light > 31.1°, Medium 22.3–31.1°, Heavy 10–22.3°, Extra Heavy < 10°. - **Sweet vs Sour:** S < 0.5 wt % = sweet; > 0.5 wt % = sour. H₂S > 4 ppm = sour gas (safety hazard). ### Engineering Properties - **Porosity (φ):** φ = V_pores / V_total × 100%. Reservoir-quality: 10–30%. - **Permeability (k):** Darcy's Law — **Q = k A ΔP / (μ L)**. 1 Darcy = 0.987 × 10⁻¹² m². - **Cap rock:** Permeability < 0.001 mD. Salt and shale are the best seals. - **Gas Z-factor:** Z = PV / (nRT). At reservoir conditions, **Z ≠ 1**. Use Standing-Katz chart. - **Gas specific gravity:** **γ_g = M_gas / 28.97**. Typical natural gas: 0.55–0.75. ### Trap Classification - **Structural:** Anticline, Fault, Salt Dome. - **Stratigraphic:** Pinch-out / Lens, Unconformity, Reef. - **Salt dome = STRUCTURAL** (not stratigraphic, despite salt being sedimentary). ### KTU Numerical Hot Buttons - Always show **unit conversions** explicitly. - Use **ρ_water = 999 kg/m³** (or 1000) at 15.6 °C. - Conversion factors: **1 Darcy = 0.987 × 10⁻¹² m²**; **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s**. - Classification boundaries: **31.1° API, 22.3° API, 10° API**; **0.5 wt% sulfur**; **4 ppm H₂S**; **0.001 mD permeability** for cap rock. ### One-Line Definitions (for 1-mark sub-parts) - **Kerogen:** Insoluble organic matter in source rock that yields hydrocarbons on heating. - **TOC (Total Organic Carbon):** Weight percent of organic carbon in rock; > 0.5% qualifies a rock as a source rock. - **Spill Point:** The lowest point on a trap's closure above which hydrocarbons leak. - **OOIP:** Original Oil In Place — total hydrocarbon volume originally in the reservoir. - **NGL (Natural Gas Liquid):** Hydrocarbons heavier than methane (C₂+) recovered from natural gas.

topics.title: Source Rocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source Rocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Petroleum Geochemistry** and **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT522 – Oil and Natural Gas Engineering)**, a **Source Rock** is defined as:

> A fine-grained sedimentary rock (predominantly **shale**, **mudstone**, **carbonate/marl**, or **coal**) that contains sufficient quantities of **organic matter (kerogen)** and has undergone sufficient **thermal maturation (burial and heating)** to generate and expel significant amounts of **petroleum (crude oil and/or natural gas)**.

The formal definition requires that a source rock must satisfy **three independent criteria** simultaneously:

1. **Quantity Criterion:** Total Organic Carbon (TOC) content must be high enough to yield commercial volumes of hydrocarbons.
2. **Quality Criterion:** The organic matter (kerogen) must be of a type that is capable of generating hydrocarbons (i.e., Types I, II, or IIS — *not* Type IV).
3. **Maturity Criterion:** The rock must have been buried to a depth where temperature-time conditions have driven the kerogen through the **oil window** (typically Ro = 0.6% – 1.3%) or **gas window** (Ro > 1.3%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Examiners frequently frame the question *"Differentiate between a source rock and a reservoir rock."* Remember — a *source rock* **generates** hydrocarbons, while a *reservoir rock* (sandstone, carbonate) **stores** them. Many source rocks (e.g., shales) are also now exploited as **unconventional reservoirs (shale gas/shale oil)**, blurring the classical line — this is a favorite KTU 2024 question angle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Kitchen" Metaphor**
>
> Think of a **source rock** as a **kitchen** buried deep inside the Earth. The "ingredients" required to cook petroleum are:
>
> - **Organic matter (kerogen)** = the raw food (flour, vegetables) stored in the pantry.
> - **Heat (geothermal gradient)** = the stove burner.
> - **Time (burial duration)** = the cooking time.
> - **Pressure (lithostatic load)** = the pressure cooker.
>
> Just as a kitchen must have **enough food, the right recipes (kerogen type), and a hot enough burner for long enough** to produce a meal, a source rock must have **enough TOC, the right kerogen type, and sufficient thermal maturity** to generate hydrocarbons. The "cooked meal" (oil/gas) then **migrates upward** from the kitchen (source rock) into the **refrigerator** (reservoir rock) and is finally trapped beneath an impermeable **lid** (cap rock/seal).

This analogy maps directly onto the **Petroleum System Elements**: **Source → Migration → Reservoir → Seal → Trap**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Units) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geothermal Gradient | $dT/dz$ | **25 – 30 °C/km** (avg. continental crust) | Determines maturation depth |
| Vitrinite Reflectance (Oil Window) | $R_o$ | **0.6% – 1.3%** | Peak oil generation |
| Vitrinite Reflectance (Gas Window) | $R_o$ | **> 1.3%** | Dry gas generation |
| Minimum TOC for a Source Rock | $TOC$ | **> 0.5 wt%** | Lower economic threshold |
| Kerogen Density (Type I) | $\rho_{k}$ | **~ 1.0 – 1.1 g/cm³** | For volumetric yield calculations |
| Activation Energy (Kerogen Cracking) | $E_a$ | **~ 50 kcal/mol (≈ 209 kJ/mol)** | Arrhenius-type kinetics |
| Standard Temperature for Tmax | $T_{max}$ | **435 – 470 °C** (Rock-Eval) | Maturity indicator |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Source Rocks

### 2.1.1 By Lithology

| Source Rock Type | Description | Typical Depositional Setting |
|---|---|---|
| **Shale / Mudstone** | Most common; clay- to silt-sized particles, rich in kerogen | Marine, deep-water, anoxic basins |
| **Carbonate (Limestone / Marl)** | Biogenic or chemical precipitates; less porous but organic-rich | Shallow marine, reefal, lagoonal |
| **Coal / Coaly Shale** | Terrestrial plant debris; gas-prone | Swamps, deltas, fluvial |
| **Siliceous / Cherty Source Rock** | Diatom-rich, siliceous oozes with kerogen | Deep marine, upwelling zones |

### 2.1.2 By Organic Richness (TOC-based Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Peters & Cassa (1994) Classification** — most commonly cited in KTU question papers.

$$
\text{TOC Classification} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Poor} & \text{if } TOC < 0.5 \, wt\% \\
\text{Fair} & \text{if } 0.5 \leq TOC < 1.0 \, wt\% \\
\text{Good} & \text{if } 1.0 \leq TOC < 2.0 \, wt\% \\
\text{Very Good} & \text{if } 2.0 \leq TOC < 4.0 \, wt\% \\
\text{Excellent} & \text{if } TOC \geq 4.0 \, wt\%
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.3 By Kerogen Type (Optical & Chemical Classification)

| Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio (atomic) | Origin | Hydrocarbon Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | > 1.5 | Algal lacustrine | **Highly oil-prone** |
| **Type II** | 1.0 – 1.5 | Marine planktonic | **Oil + Gas prone** |
| **Type II-S** | 1.0 – 1.5 | Sulfur-rich marine | **Oil-prone (early)** |
| **Type III** | < 1.0 | Terrestrial (woody) | **Gas-prone** |
| **Type IV** | < 0.5 | Inert, oxidized | **None** (dead carbon) |

---

## 2.2 Source Rock Evaluation Workflow

The evaluation of a source rock follows a strict three-stage workflow used in the petroleum industry and tested in KTU examinations:

### Step 1 — Quantity: Measuring Organic Richness
- **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** is measured via combustion (e.g., LECO analyzer) or Rock-Eval pyrolysis.
- It is expressed in **weight percent (wt%)** of the whole rock.

### Step 2 — Quality: Identifying Kerogen Type
- **Visual Kerogen Analysis (VKA)** under transmitted light microscopy.
- **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** gives **Hydrogen Index (HI)** and **Oxygen Index (OI)**.
- Plotting HI vs OI produces the **Van Krevelen Diagram** (this is a KTU-favorite diagram!).

### Step 3 — Maturity: Thermal Maturation Assessment
- **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$)** — primary maturity indicator.
- **Rock-Eval Tmax** — temperature at peak S2 generation.
- **S1/(S1+S2) ratio** — Production Index (PI), indicates in-situ generation.
- **Saturated vs Aromatic biomarker ratios** (CPI, Pr/Ph).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PI = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Production Index (maturity) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $HI = \dfrac{S_2 \times 100}{TOC}$ | Hydrogen Index (kerogen type) | mg HC / g TOC |
| 3 | $OI = \dfrac{S_3 \times 100}{TOC}$ | Oxygen Index (kerogen type) | mg CO₂ / g TOC |
| 4 | $T_{max}$ | Temperature at peak S2 (maturity) | °C |
| 5 | $R_o$ (Vitrinite Reflectance) | Mean random vitrinite reflectance | % |
| 6 | Transformation Ratio $TR = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Same as PI (legacy notation) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $HI_{thresh} = 150$ for oil, $50$ for gas | Quality threshold | mg HC / g TOC |
| 8 | First-order kinetics: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius cracking rate | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dTOC}{dt} = -k \cdot TOC$ | Kerogen decay (first-order) | wt%/s |
| 10 | $Q_{HC} = V_{rock} \cdot TOC \cdot HI \cdot \rho_{k} / 10^6$ | Estimated HC yield (volumetric) | tonnes or m³ |

**Where the Rock-Eval Pyrolysis Outputs Mean:**
- **S1** = Free hydrocarbons already generated (mg HC / g rock)
- **S2** = Hydrocarbons from kerogen cracking (mg HC / g rock)
- **S3** = CO₂ from kerogen oxygen groups (mg CO₂ / g rock)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Exploration Geochemistry:** Identifying and correlating source rocks in undrilled basins.
- **Unconventional Resource Evaluation:** Shale gas / shale oil plays (Bakken, Marcellus) rely entirely on in-situ source-rock kerogen as the *reservoir*.
- **Petroleum System Modeling (PSM):** Tools like **BasinMod™**, **PetroMod™**, and **Trinity™** use the Arrhenius kinetic model of kerogen cracking to predict hydrocarbon generation timing in time-depth-temperature space.
- **CO₂ Sequestration:** Organic-rich shales (spent source rocks) are increasingly studied for permanent **CO₂ storage**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Kinetics of Kerogen Cracking

This is a **K1-Knowledge / K3-Application** level derivation frequently asked in KTU 2024 questions worth 5-7 marks.

### Assumption
Kerogen cracking is treated as a **first-order chemical reaction** with respect to kerogen concentration. This is justified empirically for most petroleum systems.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $[K(t)]$ be the mass (or moles) of uncracked kerogen remaining in the source rock at time $t$. The rate of kerogen consumption is proportional to the amount of kerogen present:

$$
\frac{dK}{dt} = -k \cdot K
$$

Here, the negative sign indicates that the kerogen mass is **decreasing** with time as it converts to hydrocarbons.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the equation by separating variables:**

$$
\frac{dK}{K} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides from initial condition $K(t=0) = K_0$ to current time $t$:**

$$
\int_{K_0}^{K(t)} \frac{dK}{K} = -\int_{0}^{t} k \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals (assuming $k$ is approximately constant over the small time interval — true for isothermal laboratory pyrolysis; in nature, $k$ varies with temperature, see Step 5):**

$$
\left[ \ln K \right]_{K_0}^{K(t)} = -k t
$$

$$
\ln K(t) - \ln K_0 = -k t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K(t)$:**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{K(t)}{K_0} \right) = -k t
$$

$$
K(t) = K_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

This is the classic **exponential decay** form. The fraction of kerogen remaining decays exponentially with time.

**Step 5 — Extend to a non-isothermal (geologic) case using the Arrhenius rate constant:**

In a sedimentary basin, temperature $T$ increases with burial depth. By the Arrhenius equation:

$$
k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}
$$

Therefore, the time-integrated (cumulative) kerogen transformation in a heating basin is given by:

$$
\frac{K(t)}{K_0} = \exp \left( - \int_{0}^{t} A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T(\tau))} \, d\tau \right)
$$

This integral is the **time-temperature index (TTI)** concept, used in **Lopatin's Model** and **Waples' Model** for thermal maturity. It forms the theoretical basis of all **Basin Modeling Software**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For the ESE, you are expected to:
> 1. State the differential form of the first-order rate law.
> 2. Carry out the integration explicitly (as shown above).
> 3. Mention the Arrhenius temperature-dependence for full credit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Hydrogen Index (HI) and Kerogen Type Identification

This is a typical **Part A / 3-mark** analytical question.

### Problem Statement
A shale sample yields the following Rock-Eval pyrolysis data:
- $S_1 = 1.2$ mg HC / g rock
- $S_2 = 6.5$ mg HC / g rock
- $S_3 = 0.8$ mg CO₂ / g rock
- $TOC = 3.5$ wt%
- $T_{max} = 445$ °C

**Determine:**
(a) Hydrogen Index (HI)
(b) Oxygen Index (OI)
(c) Production Index (PI)
(d) Kerogen Type and Maturity

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Hydrogen Index (HI):**

$$
HI = \frac{S_2 \times 100}{TOC} = \frac{6.5 \times 100}{3.5}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{650}{3.5} \approx 185.7 \; \text{mg HC / g TOC}
$$

**(b) Oxygen Index (OI):**

$$
OI = \frac{S_3 \times 100}{TOC} = \frac{0.8 \times 100}{3.5}
$$

$$
OI = \frac{80}{3.5} \approx 22.9 \; \text{mg CO}_2 \text{ / g TOC}
$$

**(c) Production Index (PI):**

$$
PI = \frac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2} = \frac{1.2}{1.2 + 6.5} = \frac{1.2}{7.7}
$$

$$
PI \approx 0.156
$$

**(d) Kerogen Type and Maturity Interpretation:**

Since $HI = 185.7$, this falls in the range **150 – 300** which corresponds to **Type II kerogen** (mixed marine — oil and gas prone).

Since $T_{max} = 445$ °C, this falls in the range **435 – 455 °C** which corresponds to the **mature / peak oil window**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sample is a **mature, Type II source rock** at **peak oil generation** — a high-quality source rock.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Source Rock Quality

| $HI$ (mg HC/g TOC) | Kerogen Type | HC Potential | $T_{max}$ Range | Maturity Window |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| > 600 | Type I (Algal) | **Highly oil-prone** | 440 – 450 °C | Peak Oil |
| 300 – 600 | Type I → II | **Very oil-prone** | 435 – 445 °C | Oil Window |
| 150 – 300 | Type II (Marine) | **Oil + Gas** | 435 – 455 °C | Peak Oil / Wet Gas |
| 50 – 150 | Type II → III | **Gas + some oil** | 450 – 470 °C | Wet Gas / Condensate |
| < 50 | Type III (Terrestrial) | **Gas only** | 460 – 490 °C | Dry Gas |
| < 50 | Type IV (Inert) | **None** | Variable | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most important KTU 2024 exam tool** — it is essentially a lookup table that lets you answer an entire question with one well-drawn table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Van Krevelen Diagram — Kerogen Type Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** The classical **Van Krevelen Diagram** plots the **Hydrogen Index (HI)** on the x-axis and the **Oxygen Index (OI)** on the y-axis. The trajectories of Type I, II, III, and IV kerogens diverge from their respective depositional positions along maturation pathways. This diagram is a **guaranteed KTU question topic**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart([START Van Krevelen Diagram Plotting Workflow]):::startNode
    nodeA[Step 1: Conduct Rock-Eval Pyrolysis on Source Rock Sample]:::stepNode
    nodeB[Step 2: Compute HI and OI from S2 S3 and TOC]:::stepNode
    nodeC[Step 3: Plot HI on X axis and OI on Y axis]:::stepNode
    nodeD{Step 4: Locate Plot Position in Diagram Quadrants}:::decisionNode
    nodeE1[Type I Kerogen: High HI greater than 600 Very Low OI Lacustrine Algal]:::typeNode
    nodeE2[Type II Kerogen: Moderate HI 150 to 300 Moderate OI Marine Planktonic]:::typeNode
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Source Rock
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q: Define a source rock. What are the three essential criteria that classify a sedimentary rock as a petroleum source rock?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A source rock is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, or carbonate) that has generated or is capable of generating hydrocarbons. The three essential criteria are:

1. **Organic Richness (Quantity):** Total Organic Carbon (TOC) content must be at least **0.5 wt%** for fair potential, and **> 1.0 wt%** for good potential.
2. **Kerogen Type (Quality):** The organic matter must be of **Type I, II, or IIS** (kerogen capable of generating hydrocarbons). Type IV is inert.
3. **Thermal Maturity:** The rock must be buried sufficiently to enter the **oil window** (Ro = 0.6% – 1.3%) or **gas window** (Ro > 1.3%).

All three must be satisfied simultaneously. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Kerogen Types
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q: List the four main types of kerogen. Which type is most oil-prone and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | HC Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | Algal, lacustrine | > 1.5 | Highly oil-prone |
| **Type II** | Marine planktonic | 1.0 – 1.5 | Oil + gas |
| **Type III** | Terrestrial woody | < 1.0 | Gas only |
| **Type IV** | Oxidized, residual | < 0.5 | Inert |

**Type I** is the most oil-prone because it has the **highest H/C atomic ratio** (> 1.5) and the **lowest O/C ratio**, indicating abundant aliphatic (hydrogen-rich) chains that crack efficiently to yield liquid hydrocarbons upon maturation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Long Answer with Sub-parts

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]**

**Q: Discuss in detail the methods used for the evaluation of petroleum source rocks. Include Rock-Eval pyrolysis parameters, vitrinite reflectance, and the Van Krevelen diagram in your answer.**

#### Part (a) — Methods of Source Rock Evaluation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Source rock evaluation is a three-pronged geochemical procedure aimed at determining **whether a rock has generated hydrocarbons, what type, and how much**.

**1. Total Organic Carbon (TOC) Analysis (Quantity Test):**
TOC is measured by **combustion** (e.g., LECO CR-412 carbon analyzer) where the rock is heated in oxygen and the CO₂ evolved is measured by IR detector. Results are reported in **wt%**.
- **< 0.5 wt%** → Poor
- **0.5 – 1.0 wt%** → Fair
- **1.0 – 2.0 wt%** → Good
- **2.0 – 4.0 wt%** → Very Good
- **> 4.0 wt%** → Excellent

**2. Rock-Eval Pyrolysis (Quality + Maturity):**
A small rock sample (~ 100 mg) is heated in an inert atmosphere. The instrument measures four key parameters:
- **S1** (mg HC/g rock): Free hydrocarbons already present
- **S2** (mg HC/g rock): Hydrocarbons generated by kerogen cracking
- **S3** (mg CO₂/g rock): CO₂ from kerogen oxygen groups
- **Tmax** (°C): Temperature at the apex of the S2 peak (maturity)

From these, derived indices are computed:
- **HI** (Hydrogen Index) = (S2 × 100) / TOC
- **OI** (Oxygen Index) = (S3 × 100) / TOC
- **PI** (Production Index) = S1 / (S1 + S2)

**3. Vitrinite Reflectance (Maturity Test):**
Polished rock sections are examined under a reflected-light microscope. The reflectance of the **vitrinite maceral** (a coalified woody particle) is measured. The numerical value $R_o$ (%) increases monotonically with thermal maturation. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Van Krevelen Diagram and Kerogen Classification (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Van Krevelen Diagram** (1961) is a cross-plot of:
- **X-axis:** Hydrogen Index (HI), in mg HC / g TOC
- **Y-axis:** Oxygen Index (OI), in mg CO₂ / g TOC

The plot identifies kerogen type and its evolutionary trajectory during burial:

| Position on Diagram | Kerogen Type | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Upper right (HI > 600, OI low) | Type I | Lacustrine algal |
| Center (HI 150–600, OI moderate) | Type II | Marine planktonic |
| Lower left (HI < 150, OI high) | Type III | Terrestrial higher plants |
| Near origin | Type IV | Inert, residual |

As maturation proceeds, the HI and OI both decrease (kerogen loses H and O), so the maturation pathway follows a **down-and-to-the-left** trajectory from each type's starting position.

**Practical use:** Petroleum geochemists routinely use a modified Van Krevelen diagram (HI vs Tmax) to simultaneously infer **kerogen type** and **maturity**. A sample with $HI = 200$ and $T_{max} = 445$ °C is identified as mature Type II at peak oil generation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long Answer Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q: What are the different types of kerogen? Explain the maturation process of kerogen from diagenesis through catagenesis to metagenesis, with reference to hydrocarbon products formed at each stage.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Kerogen (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Kerogen is the **insoluble organic matter** in sedimentary rocks that, upon heating, generates petroleum. It is classified into four types based on **H/C** and **O/C atomic ratios** (Van Krevelen's original classification):

**1. Type I (Algal / Sapropelic):**
- High H/C ratio (> 1.5); low O/C ratio (< 0.1)
- Derived from **algae deposited in freshwater lakes (lacustrine)**
- Structure: highly aliphatic, long methylene chains
- Generates **waxy crude oil** at low thermal stress
- Example source: **Green River Shale, USA**

**2. Type II (Planktonic / Marine):**
- H/C ratio = 1.0 – 1.5; O/C = 0.1 – 0.2
- Derived from **marine phytoplankton, zooplankton, and bacterial debris**
- Structure: mixed aliphatic + aromatic, contains sulfur
- Generates **mixed oil and gas**
- Example: **Bakken Shale, North Sea Kimmeridge Clay**

**3. Type II-S (Sulfur-rich):**
- A subtype of Type II with **> 8 wt% organic sulfur**
- Generates oil at **lower thermal maturity** due to weak C–S bonds
- Example: **Monterey Formation, California**

**4. Type III (Humic / Woody):**
- H/C < 1.0; O/C = 0.2 – 0.3
- Derived from **terrestrial higher plants** (cellulose, lignin)
- Structure: dominantly aromatic
- Generates **mostly gas (methane)**, with minor light oil
- Example: **Cretaceous coals, North Sea**

**5. Type IV (Inert / Residual):**
- H/C < 0.5; high O/C
- Oxidized, reworked organic matter
- **No hydrocarbon generative potential** (dead carbon) **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Kerogen Maturation Stages (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

As a source rock is buried progressively deeper, temperature rises and kerogen transforms through **four distinct stages**:

| Stage | Depth (typical) | $R_o$ (%) | $T_{max}$ (°C) | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Diagenesis** | 0 – 1 km | < 0.5 | < 430 | Biogenic methane, immature kerogen |
| **Catagenesis – Oil Window** | 1 – 3 km | 0.6 – 1.0 | 435 – 450 | **Crude oil (peak generation)** |
| **Catagenesis – Gas Window** | 3 – 4 km | 1.0 – 2.0 | 450 – 470 | **Wet gas, condensate** |
| **Metagenesis** | > 4 km | > 2.0 | > 470 | **Dry methane + graphite residue** |

**1. Diagenesis (Immature Stage):**
- Low temperature (< 60 °C); microbial activity dominant.
- Kerogen loses oxygen (as CO₂ and H₂O); no significant oil formed.
- Some **biogenic methane** is produced by methanogenic bacteria.

**2. Catagenesis (Mature / Oil Window):**
- Temperature range **60 – 120 °C**; kerogen undergoes **C–C bond cracking** (cracking of aliphatic chains).
- $R_o$ = 0.6% – 1.3% marks the **peak oil generation** zone.
- For Type I and Type II kerogens, this is the principal source of **liquid hydrocarbons**.

**3. Catagenesis (Gas Window):**
- As temperature climbs further, the residual kerogen becomes more aromatic.
- C–C bond cracking continues, but the products are dominated by **light hydrocarbons (C1–C4)**.
- **Wet gas** (C2–C5) and **condensate** are produced.

**4. Metagenesis (Overmature / Dry Gas):**
- Temperature > 200 °C; kerogen has been reduced to **graphitic carbon**.
- Only **dry methane (C1)** is produced from residual cracking.
- The source rock is now "spent" and no longer a viable exploration target. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Source Rock vs. Reservoir Rock (–2 marks):** A frequent Part A error. A source rock *generates* HC; a reservoir rock *stores* HC.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in TOC/HI/OI calculations (–1 mark):** Always state the units (wt% for TOC; mg HC/g TOC for HI).
> 3. **Wrong Axis on Van Krevelen Plot (–2 marks):** HI is **X-axis**, OI is **Y-axis** — not the reverse.
> 4. **Mixing Type II and Type III generative products (–1 mark):** Type II gives oil+gas, Type III gives only gas.
> 5. **Skipping the Arrhenius temperature-dependence in kinetics derivations (–2 marks):** Always end with $k(T) = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ for full credit on kinetics questions.
> 6. **Not specifying $R_o$ ranges for oil vs. gas windows (–1 mark):** Commit to memory: **Oil window 0.6–1.3%; Gas window > 1.3%**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density summary for the night-before-the-exam revision:**

- ✅ **Source Rock Definition:** Fine-grained sedimentary rock with sufficient organic matter, right kerogen type, and thermal maturity to generate petroleum.
- ✅ **Three Golden Criteria:** **Quantity (TOC) + Quality (Kerogen Type) + Maturity (Ro/Tmax)** — all three required.
- ✅ **TOC Threshold (Peters & Cassa):** < 0.5 (Poor) | 0.5–1.0 (Fair) | 1.0–2.0 (Good) | 2.0–4.0 (V. Good) | > 4.0 (Excellent) wt%.
- ✅ **Kerogen Types:** I (Algal, very oil-prone) | II (Marine, oil+gas) | II-S (S-rich, early oil) | III (Woody, gas) | IV (Inert, none).
- ✅ **Rock-Eval Outputs:** S1 (free HC) + S2 (cracked HC) + S3 (CO₂ from kerogen) + Tmax (maturity).
- ✅ **Derived Indices:** HI = S2×100/TOC | OI = S3×100/TOC | PI = S1/(S1+S2).
- ✅ **Van Krevelen Axes:** X = HI, Y = OI. Trajectories move **down-left** with maturation.
- ✅ **Maturity Windows:** Diagenesis (Ro<0.5) | Oil Window (Ro 0.6–1.3) | Gas Window (Ro>1.3) | Metagenesis (Ro>2.0).
- ✅ **Tmax Windows:** < 430°C (Immature) | 435–455°C (Mature Oil) | 455–470°C (Gas) | > 470°C (Overmature).
- ✅ **First-Order Kinetics:** $K(t) = K_0 e^{-kt}$, with $k(T) = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ (Arrhenius).
- ✅ **Source Rock Examples:** Green River Shale (Type I), Bakken & Kimmeridge Clay (Type II), Cretaceous Coals (Type III), Monterey (Type II-S).
- ✅ **Modern Relevance:** Shale gas & shale oil plays exploit source rocks as *in-situ* unconventional reservoirs — the same rock serves as source, reservoir, and seal.
