topics.title: Reservoir Rocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reservoir Rocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reservoir Rock** is a subsurface, porous, and permeable rock formation that possesses the capability to store and transmit hydrocarbon fluids (crude oil, natural gas, and condensates) along with formation water, under reservoir conditions of pressure and temperature. In the framework of the KTU 2024 syllabus for Petroleum Engineering, reservoir rocks are predominantly **sedimentary in origin**, formed through long-term depositional, diagenetic, and compaction processes occurring within petroliferous basins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT 522):** *A reservoir rock is a rock that has both effective porosity (to store hydrocarbons) and effective permeability (to allow the flow of hydrocarbons) under in-situ subsurface conditions.*

The two commercially dominant classes of reservoir rocks worldwide are:

1. **Clastic (Siliciclastic) Reservoir Rocks** — Example: **Sandstone**.
2. **Non-Clastic (Chemical/Biochemical) Reservoir Rocks** — Example: **Carbonate Rocks** (Limestone and Dolomite).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sedimentary Rocks?**
> Igneous and metamorphic rocks are generally **non-reservoir rocks** in conventional hydrocarbon systems, except in naturally fractured basement plays (e.g., the weathered granitic basement of the Mumbai High field, India). This is because their crystalline, interlocking grain structure offers negligible primary porosity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** sitting in a bowl of cooking oil, vegetable water, and air bubbles.

- The **sponge itself** = the **Reservoir Rock** (the solid framework).
- The **tiny holes inside the sponge** = the **pore spaces (porosity)** that physically hold the fluids.
- The **ability of oil to drip out when squeezed** = the **permeability** of the rock.
- The **oil trapped inside the small holes that won't drip out** = the **residual oil / connate water saturation**.

In the Earth's crust, the "sponge" is the sandstone or carbonate rock buried kilometers below the surface, the "oil" is crude petroleum, and the "squeeze" is the natural subsurface pressure drive that pushes oil toward the production well.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** A good reservoir rock must satisfy the **"3-P Rule"**: **P**orosity (storage), **P**ermeability (flow), and **P**ressure (drive mechanism).

## 1.3 Fundamental Reservoir Rock Properties (Syllabus Snapshot)

| Property | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Porosity | $\phi$ | fraction (or %) | Volumetric storage capacity |
| Permeability | $k$ | $\text{m}^2$ (or Darcy/mD) | Fluid flow conductivity |
| Fluid Saturation | $S$ | fraction | Hydrocarbon fraction in pore space |
| Wettability | $\theta$ | degrees | Rock-fluid interfacial preference |
| Capillary Pressure | $P_c$ | Pa (or psi) | Trapping force in pores |
| Compressibility | $c$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Rock's response to pressure change |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Reservoir Rocks

Reservoir rocks are systematically classified based on their **mode of origin** and **textural-mineralogical characteristics**. The KTU Module 1 syllabus lays strong emphasis on this classification hierarchy.

### A. Clastic (Siliciclastic) Reservoir Rocks

Formed by the **mechanical accumulation and lithification** of weathered mineral grains (predominantly quartz and feldspar).

- **Sandstone:** The most prolific clastic reservoir rock globally. Composed of sand-sized grains (diameter **0.0625 mm to 2 mm**). Examples: Ghawar Field (Saudi Arabia), Prudhoe Bay (Alaska).
- **Siltstone:** Marginal reservoir quality (very low permeability).
- **Conglomerates & Breccias:** Coarse-grained; reservoir quality depends on matrix and cementation.

### B. Non-Clastic (Carbonate) Reservoir Rocks

Formed by **chemical/biochemical precipitation** or by accumulation of skeletal remains of marine organisms (biogenic origin).

- **Limestone ($\text{CaCO}_3$):** Composed chiefly of calcite. Oolitic, fossiliferous, and chalky varieties are common reservoirs.
- **Dolomite ($\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2$):** Formed by diagenetic replacement of limestone (dolomitization), which often *enhances* porosity.
- **Chalk:** Soft, micro-porous limestone; e.g., the **Ekofisk Field (North Sea)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Statistics for KTU Exam:** Approximately **60% of the world's oil and 40% of the world's gas reserves are held in carbonate reservoirs**, despite carbonates being less abundant than sandstones at the Earth's surface. The **Middle East's** super-giant fields are predominantly carbonate.

### C. Unconventional / Miscellaneous Reservoir Rocks

- **Fractured Basement & Igneous Rocks:** E.g., Mumbai High (India), Bach Ho (Vietnam).
- **Tight Sandstones & Shales** (in hydraulic fracturing plays).
- **Coal Bed Methane (CBM) Reservoirs.**

## 2.2 Essential Reservoir Rock Properties — Detailed Treatment

### 2.2.1 Porosity ($\phi$)

**Porosity** is defined as the ratio of the **pore volume (void space)** in a rock to its **total (bulk) volume**. It is a dimensionless quantity, expressed either as a decimal fraction or a percentage.

$$\phi = \frac{V_{\text{pore}}}{V_{\text{bulk}}} = \frac{V_{\text{bulk}} - V_{\text{grain}}}{V_{\text{bulk}}}$$

**Types of Porosity (Critical for KTU):**

1. **Total (Absolute) Porosity:** All void spaces, including isolated pores.
2. **Effective Porosity ($\phi_e$):** Only the *interconnected* pore spaces that contribute to fluid storage and flow. This is the **engineer's porosity**.
3. **Primary (Depositional) Porosity:** Formed during the original deposition of sediments (intergranular porosity in sandstones).
4. **Secondary (Post-Depositional) Porosity:** Created by diagenetic processes such as dissolution, fracturing, and dolomitization. Critical in carbonates.

### 2.2.2 Permeability ($k$)

**Permeability** is the ability of a porous medium to transmit fluids under a pressure gradient. The foundation equation is **Darcy's Law (1856)** for linear, horizontal, single-phase, steady-state flow:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Solving for permeability:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across length $L$ (Pa)
- $\mu$ = fluid dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = length of the porous medium (m)
- $k$ = permeability ($\text{m}^2$ in SI, or **Darcy** in CGS)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Darcy Unit:** Named after **Henry Darcy**, **1 Darcy** is the permeability that allows a fluid of **1 centipoise viscosity** to flow at **1 cm³/s** through a cross-section of **1 cm²** under a pressure gradient of **1 atm/cm**. In SI units, **1 Darcy ≈ 9.869 × 10⁻¹³ m²**. Reservoir rocks typically range from **0.1 mD to 1000 mD**.

### 2.2.3 Fluid Saturation ($S$)

**Saturation** of a given fluid phase is the fraction of the **pore volume** occupied by that fluid. For a hydrocarbon-bearing rock with three fluid phases (oil, water, gas), the saturation constraint is:

$$S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$$

- $S_o$ = Oil Saturation
- $S_w$ = Water Saturation (connate/formation water)
- $S_g$ = Gas Saturation

**Connate Water Saturation ($S_{wc}$):** The irreducible minimum water saturation, which is *always* present in hydrocarbon-bearing reservoirs. It occupies the smallest pores and grain surfaces.

### 2.2.4 Wettability ($\theta$)

**Wettability** is the preferential tendency of one fluid to adhere to a solid surface in the presence of another immiscible fluid, measured via the **contact angle** $\theta$ measured through the denser phase.

- $\theta < 90°$ → **Water-wet rock** (water preferentially spreads on the grain).
- $\theta > 90°$ → **Oil-wet rock** (oil preferentially spreads on the grain).
- $\theta \approx 90°$ → **Neutral / Intermediate wettability**.

### 2.2.5 Capillary Pressure ($P_c$)

The **capillary pressure** is the pressure difference across the interface between two immiscible fluids within a pore throat:

$$P_c = P_{\text{non-wetting}} - P_{\text{wetting}} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

- $\sigma$ = interfacial tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $r$ = pore throat radius (m)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Porosity | $\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b}$ | $V_p$ (m³), $V_b$ (m³) | $0 \le \phi \le 1$ |
| 2 | Darcy's Law | $Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$ | $Q$ (m³/s), $k$ (m²), $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Linear, single-phase flow |
| 3 | Permeability | $k = \dfrac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$ | Permeability (m² / D / mD) | Steady state |
| 4 | Relative Permeability | $k_{r,i} = \dfrac{k_i}{k_{\text{abs}}}$ | $k_i$ (m²), $k_{\text{abs}}$ (m²) | Multi-phase flow, $0 \le k_r \le 1$ |
| 5 | Saturation Constraint | $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$ | All dimensionless fractions | Hydrocarbon + water system |
| 6 | Capillary Pressure | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\sigma$ (N/m), $r$ (m) | Capillary tube model |
| 7 | Formation Resistivity Factor | $F = \dfrac{R_o}{R_w} = \dfrac{a}{\phi^m}$ | $R_o, R_w$ ($\Omega$m), $a,m$ are constants | Archie (1942) clean sand |
| 8 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \rho_{\text{ma}} (1 - \phi) + \rho_f \phi$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Used in log interpretation |
| 9 | Compressibility | $c_f = \dfrac{1}{V_p}\dfrac{dV_p}{dP}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Pore volume response |
| 10 | Horizontal Flow Rate | $q = \dfrac{0.00708 \, k \, h \, (P_e - P_w)}{\mu \, B \, \left[ \ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s \right]}$ | $q$ (STB/day), $h$ (ft), $s$ = skin | Radial flow to wellbore |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reservoir Simulation:** Engineers use the properties $k$, $\phi$, $S_w$, and $P_c$ as inputs to numerical reservoir simulators (Eclipse, CMG) to forecast production and optimize recovery.
- **Well Log Interpretation:** Porosity is computed from density, neutron, and sonic logs; $S_w$ is derived from resistivity logs using the **Archie Equation**.
- **Reserve Estimation (Volumetric Method):** Original Oil In Place (OOIP) is calculated as:

$$\text{OOIP} = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

- **Reservoir Rock Typing (RRT):** Modern workflows use **Flow Zone Indicator (FZI)** and **Winland's $r_{35}$** to classify rocks for hydraulic fracturing candidate selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving Effective Permeability from Darcy's Law

We begin with **Darcy's Law for linear single-phase flow** through a horizontal cylindrical core plug of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, under an applied pressure drop $\Delta P = (P_1 - P_2)$.

**Step 1 — State Darcy's Law in its standard form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{\mu \cdot L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the permeability term $k$ on the left-hand side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the concept of Effective Permeability. When *more than one* fluid phase is present (e.g., oil and water), the flow of oil is reduced because water occupies part of the pore space. Hence, the permeability to oil alone ($k_o$) is called *effective permeability to oil*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_o &= \frac{Q_o \cdot \mu_o \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Relative Permeability as the ratio of effective permeability to a phase divided by the absolute permeability of the rock (single-phase permeability, typically measured with air or brine):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{ro} &= \frac{k_o}{k_{\text{abs}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine Step 3 and Step 4 to obtain the working equation used in multi-phase flow analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_o &= \frac{k_{\text{abs}} \cdot k_{ro} \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu_o \cdot L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The relative permeability $k_{ro}$ is a *function of saturation*. As water saturation $S_w$ increases, $k_{ro}$ decreases monotonically, which explains why oil production declines sharply during the late stages of water flooding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Pressure from a Curved Interface

We derive the **Young-Laplace equation** for a fluid interface inside a capillary pore throat of radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Consider a spherical cap interface of radius $R$ between the non-wetting phase (e.g., oil) and the wetting phase (e.g., water). The pressure jump across the curved interface is $\Delta P = P_c$.**

**Step 2 — The mechanical work done in expanding the interface by a differential area $dA$ is balanced by the work done against the pressure jump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot dV &= \sigma \cdot dA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For a sphere of radius $R$: Volume $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$, Area $A = 4\pi R^2$. Differentiate both:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dV &= 4\pi R^2 \, dR \\
dA &= 8\pi R \, dR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the work balance equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot (4\pi R^2 \, dR) &= \sigma \cdot (8\pi R \, dR) \\
\Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 &= \sigma \cdot 8\pi R \\
\Delta P &= \frac{2\sigma}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Relate the radius of the spherical cap $R$ to the capillary tube radius $r$ and contact angle $\theta$ via geometry: $R = \dfrac{r}{\cos\theta}$. Substituting gives the working capillary pressure equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation explains why *smaller* pore throats create *higher* capillary pressures. In a reservoir, this means the **smaller the pore**, the **harder it is for oil to displace water**, and thus the *higher* the residual connate water saturation $S_{wc}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (3-Mark Conceptual Type):** A cylindrical core sample has length **$L = 10 \text{ cm}$**, diameter **$d = 2.5 \text{ cm}$**, and is subjected to a flow test with brine ($\mu = 1 \text{ cP}$) under a pressure differential of **$\Delta P = 2 \text{ atm}$**. The measured flow rate is **$Q = 0.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$**. Calculate the absolute permeability in **Darcies** and **milli-Darcies**.

**Step 1 — Convert inputs to consistent CGS units:**
- $L = 10 \text{ cm}$
- $A = \pi \cdot (1.25)^2 = 4.909 \text{ cm}^2$
- $\mu = 1 \text{ cP}$
- $\Delta P = 2 \text{ atm}$ over a length of $10 \text{ cm}$, i.e., $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{2 \text{ atm}}{10 \text{ cm}} = 0.2 \text{ atm/cm}$
- $Q = 0.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Equation in Darcy's units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P} \\
k &= \frac{(0.5)(1)(10)}{(4.909)(2)} \\
k &= \frac{5.0}{9.818} \\
k &= 0.5093 \text{ Darcy} \\
k &= 509.3 \text{ mD}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** A permeability of $\approx 500 \text{ mD}$ is **excellent reservoir quality**, typical of a well-sorted, unconsolidated, clean sandstone. For reference, tight gas sands have $k < 0.1 \text{ mD}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sandstone vs. Carbonate Reservoir Rocks

| Property | Sandstone (Clastic) | Carbonate (Non-Clastic) | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Origin | Mechanical deposition | Chemical/biochemical precipitation | Different diagenetic pathways |
| Typical Porosity | 15% – 30% (primary) | 5% – 25% (often secondary) | Carbonates more heterogeneous |
| Typical Permeability | 100 – 1000 mD | 1 – 100 mD (matrix); high if fractured | Carbonates often need acidizing |
| Pore Geometry | Intergranular (uniform) | Vuggy, intercrystalline, fracture | Carbonates highly variable |
| Cementation | Quartz, clay, calcite | Calcite, dolomite, anhydrite | Affects porosity-permeability link |
| Wettability Preference | Often strongly water-wet | Often neutral to oil-wet | Impacts EOR design |
| Global Reserve Share | ~40% of oil, ~60% of gas | ~60% of oil, ~40% of gas | Carbonates dominate Middle East |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reservoir Rock Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Core Samples] --> B[Routine Core Analysis RCA]
    A --> C[Special Core Analysis SCAL]
    B --> D[Porosity phi Measurement]
    B --> E[Permeability k Measurement]
    B --> F[Grain Density rho_ma]
    C --> G[Wettability theta]
    C --> H[Capillary Pressure Pc]
    C --> I[Relative Permeability k_r]
    D --> J[Reservoir Rock Typing]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Log Calibration]
    K --> L[Volumetric Reserve Estimation OOIP / OGIP]
    L --> M[Reservoir Simulation Model]
    M --> N[Production Forecasting and Field Development Plan]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The diagram traces the lifecycle of reservoir rock characterization. It begins with physical core acquisition, branches into routine (RCA) and special (SCAL) lab analyses, converges into rock typing, feeds into well-log calibration, and ultimately powers the volumetric reserve calculation and reservoir simulator used for production forecasting.

## 4.2 Schematic: Pore-Level Fluid Distribution in an Oil-Wet Reservoir

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Grain Surface] -->|Contact angle theta greater than 90 deg| OIL[Oil Film Coating Grain]
    WATER[Connate Water in Center of Pore] -->|Discontinuous phase| OIL
    PTH[Pore Throat Radius r] -->|Controls capillary pressure| IF[Oil Water Interface Curvature]
    IF -->|Young Laplace equation| PC[Capillary Pressure Pc]
    OIL -->|Oil occupies large pores preferentially| WET[Oil Wet System]
    WATER --> WET
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rock Property Interdependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Depositional Environment] --> P2[Primary Porosity phi_p]
    P1 --> P3[Mineral Composition]
    P3 --> P4[Cementation Type]
    P2 --> P5[Effective Porosity phi_e]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Absolute Permeability k_abs]
    P5 --> P7[Fluid Saturation S_o S_w S_g]
    P6 --> P8[Effective Permeability k_eff]
    P7 --> P8
    P7 --> P9[Relative Permeability k_r]
    P8 --> P9
    P6 --> P10[Capillary Pressure Pc Curve]
    P7 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Initial Reservoir Fluid Distribution]
    P11 --> P12[Hydrocarbon Recovery Factor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Notice the **chain of dependencies** — depositional environment sets porosity, mineralogy sets cementation, cementation and porosity jointly determine permeability, which together with saturation controls capillary behavior, which in turn controls how oil and water are distributed in the reservoir at discovery. This causal chain is the foundation of modern reservoir characterization.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a reservoir rock. List any four essential properties that a rock must possess to qualify as a hydrocarbon reservoir rock.**

**Model Answer:**

A **reservoir rock** is a subsurface rock formation that contains economically recoverable quantities of hydrocarbons in its pore space and permits the flow of these fluids under existing pressure gradients.

**Four essential properties are:**

1. **Effective Porosity ($\phi_e$):** Must be sufficient to store hydrocarbons in commercial volumes (typically $\phi_e > 5\%$).
2. **Effective Permeability ($k$):** Must be high enough to allow hydrocarbon flow to the wellbore (typically $k > 0.1 \text{ mD}$).
3. **Cap Rock / Seal Above:** The reservoir must be capped by an impermeable stratum (e.g., shale, salt) to prevent hydrocarbon migration to the surface.
4. **Hydrocarbon Saturation:** The pore space must contain a hydrocarbon saturation above the *critical saturation* (i.e., the minimum saturation at which the hydrocarbon phase becomes mobile).

> [!TIP]
> For full marks, students should additionally mention **trap geometry** (anticline, fault, stratigraphic) and **source rock maturity** as part of the complete petroleum system.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between primary porosity and secondary porosity with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Primary Porosity | Secondary Porosity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Formed during the original deposition of sediments | Formed by post-depositional diagenetic processes |
| **Process** | Intergranular pore space between sand grains | Dissolution, fracturing, dolomitization |
| **Occurrence** | Dominant in **sandstones** | Dominant in **carbonates** |
| **Example** | Intergranular porosity in clean quartz arenite | Vuggy porosity in leached limestone; oomoldic porosity |

Secondary porosity is *additive* to primary porosity; together they constitute the **total porosity** of the rock.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Examinations under the 2024 scheme allow **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternative questions (A or B) are provided; students must answer one. Each long question typically carries two 7-mark sub-parts.

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain **Darcy's Law** for linear flow of fluids through a porous medium. State the assumptions and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Darcy's Law states that the volumetric flow rate of a homogeneous fluid through a porous medium is directly proportional to the cross-sectional area and the pressure gradient, and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
A horizontal cylindrical core of length $L$ and cross-section $A$ is shown. Fluid enters at the upstream face at pressure $P_1$ and exits at the downstream face at $P_2$, with $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$. The direction of flow is indicated by an arrow labeled $Q$.

**Assumptions of Darcy's Law:**
1. **Laminar (viscous) flow:** Reynolds number $Re < 1$ (in the linear regime).
2. **Single-phase fluid:** Only one fluid occupies the pore space.
3. **Homogeneous and isotropic porous medium:** $k$ is constant in all directions.
4. **Steady-state flow:** $Q$ does not change with time.
5. **Incompressible fluid:** $\rho$ and $\mu$ are independent of $P$.
6. **No chemical reaction** between fluid and rock.
7. **Isothermal conditions** throughout the flow domain.

**Limitations:**
- **Fails at high flow rates** (turbulent regime, $Re > 1$); a non-Darcy (Forchheimer) correction term $\beta \rho v^2$ is added.
- **Not valid for non-Newtonian fluids** (heavy oil, polymers) without modification.
- **Not valid for two-phase flow**; relative permeability must be incorporated.
- **Anisotropic reservoirs** (shales, fractured rocks) require a tensor form of permeability.

---

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical sandstone core has dimensions: diameter $d = 2.54 \text{ cm}$, length $L = 5.0 \text{ cm}$, and grain volume $V_{\text{grain}} = 19.5 \text{ cm}^3$. The dry weight is $54.0 \text{ g}$ and the saturated weight (with brine of density $1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$) is $59.2 \text{ g}$. Calculate:
- (i) Bulk volume, pore volume, and porosity.
- (ii) Grain density.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute bulk volume** $V_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_b &= \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 \cdot L = \pi (1.27)^2 (5.0) \\
V_b &= \pi (1.6129)(5.0) \\
V_b &= 25.33 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute pore volume** $V_p$ from the difference in weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \frac{W_{\text{sat}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{\rho_{\text{brine}}} = \frac{59.2 - 54.0}{1.05} \\
V_p &= \frac{5.2}{1.05} = 4.952 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute porosity** $\phi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \frac{V_p}{V_b} = \frac{4.952}{25.33} \\
\phi &= 0.1955 = 19.55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute grain density** $\rho_{\text{ma}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{ma}} &= \frac{W_{\text{dry}}}{V_{\text{grain}}} = \frac{54.0}{19.5} \\
\rho_{\text{ma}} &= 2.769 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A grain density of $\approx 2.77 \text{ g/cm}^3$ is consistent with a **quartz-rich sandstone** (theoretical quartz density is $2.65 \text{ g/cm}^3$; the higher value indicates minor heavy minerals or carbonate cement).

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **types of reservoir rocks** with examples. Why are sedimentary rocks the dominant hydrocarbon reservoirs?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Clastic (Siliciclastic) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed by the mechanical deposition and lithification of mineral grains. **Sandstone** is the principal type, e.g., the **Rotliegendes Sandstone** of the Southern North Sea Gas Fields (e.g., the Groningen field, Netherlands). Sub-types include:
- Quartz arenite (clean, well-sorted, best reservoir quality).
- Arkose (feldspar-rich).
- Greywacke (matrix-rich, low permeability).

**2. Non-Clastic (Carbonate) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed by chemical/biochemical precipitation of $\text{CaCO}_3$ or by skeletal accumulation. Sub-types include:
- **Limestone** (e.g., the Arab-D carbonate reservoir, Ghawar Field, Saudi Arabia).
- **Dolomite** (e.g., the Mississippian dolomite reservoirs of the Permian Basin, USA).
- **Chalk** (e.g., Ekofisk Field, North Sea).

**3. Fractured Igneous / Metamorphic Basement Reservoirs:**
Examples: **Mumbai High Basement**, Vietnam's **Bach Ho Field (White Tiger)**. Hydrocarbons are stored in fractures and weathered zones.

**4. Unconventional Reservoirs:**
- **Shale Gas/Oil** (e.g., Barnett Shale, Marcellus Shale, USA).
- **Tight Gas Sandstones** (e.g., Cotton Valley, USA).
- **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** (e.g., Jharkhand & Raniganj basins, India).

**Why are sedimentary rocks dominant?**
- They possess **primary intergranular porosity** formed during deposition.
- They exhibit **high permeability** when well-sorted and uncemented.
- They are deposited in **sedimentary basins**, the very same geological settings where **source rocks** (organic-rich shales) and **reservoir-seal pairs** coexist.
- Hydrocarbons, being buoyant, **migrate upward** through sedimentary strata and accumulate in the highest available porous and permeable sedimentary unit sealed by an impermeable layer.

---

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Archie's Equation** for water saturation determination in clean sandstones. Given: $R_w = 0.05 \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$, $R_t = 4.0 \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$, $a = 0.81$, $m = 2$, $n = 2$, $\phi = 0.20$. Compute $S_w$.

**Model Solution:**

**Archie's Equations (1942):**

For a clean (shale-free) sandstone fully saturated with brine:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{R_o}{R_w} = \frac{a}{\phi^m}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a partially hydrocarbon-saturated rock, the **Resistivity Index** $I$ is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{R_t}{R_o} = \frac{1}{S_w^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining the two:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \left[ \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t} \right]^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi^m &= (0.20)^2 = 0.04 \\
a \cdot R_w &= 0.81 \times 0.05 = 0.0405 \\
S_w &= \left[ \frac{0.0405}{0.04 \times 4.0} \right]^{1/2} \\
S_w &= \left[ \frac{0.0405}{0.16} \right]^{1/2} \\
S_w &= (0.2531)^{0.5} \\
S_w &= 0.503 \quad \text{(or 50.3\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hydrocarbon Saturation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_h = 1 - S_w = 1 - 0.503 = 0.497 = 49.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often forget the **negative sign** in $\Delta P = (P_1 - P_2)$ and confuse the direction of flow.
> 2. **Unit conversion** is the most common error — always state which system (Darcy or SI) you are using.
> 3. In **Archie's equation**, the *cementation exponent* $m$ is **2 for sandstones** and **1.8–2.0 for carbonates**; using the wrong value can swing $S_w$ by 20–30%.
> 4. Many students write **$\phi^2$ as $\phi^2 = 0.4$** — this is wrong! $\phi = 0.20$, so $\phi^2 = 0.04$.
> 5. Forgetting to apply the $1/n$ root in the Archie saturation equation is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- **Reservoir Rock Definition:** A rock must have **porosity + permeability + hydrocarbon saturation + a sealing cap rock** to qualify.
- **Two main classes:** **Clastic (Sandstone, ~60% of global gas reserves)** and **Non-Clastic (Carbonate, ~60% of global oil reserves)**.
- **Porosity ($\phi$):** $V_p / V_b$; **effective** porosity (engineer's porosity) excludes isolated pores.
- **Permeability ($k$):** Measured in **Darcy (D)** or **milli-Darcy (mD)**; **1 D ≈ 9.87 × 10⁻¹³ m²**.
- **Darcy's Law (linear form):** $Q = \dfrac{k \, A \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L}$.
- **Saturation Constraint:** $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$.
- **Connate Water Saturation $S_{wc}$:** Irreducible water saturation in oil reservoirs; typically 10–40%.
- **Relative Permeability:** $k_r = k_{\text{eff}} / k_{\text{abs}}$; depends on $S_w$ / $S_g$.
- **Wettability:** Measured by contact angle $\theta$; **$\theta < 90°$** = water-wet, **$\theta > 90°$** = oil-wet.
- **Capillary Pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$; higher in smaller pores.
- **Archie's Equation:** $S_w = \left[ \dfrac{a R_w}{\phi^m R_t} \right]^{1/n}$; with $a=0.81, m=2, n=2$ for clean sandstones.
- **Sandstone vs. Carbonate:** Sandstones = primary porosity, homogeneous, water-wet, high $k$. Carbonates = secondary porosity, heterogeneous, often fractured and oil-wet.
- **Famous Reservoirs:** **Ghawar (Saudi, carbonate), Ghawar's Arab-D, Prudhoe Bay (Alaska, sandstone), Mumbai High (India, basement + carbonate), Ekofisk (North Sea, chalk).**
- **OOIP Formula:** $\text{OOIP} = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$ (in field units: STB).
- **Always state units** in numerical answers — examiners reward unit awareness in KTU scripts.
- **Common KTU traps:** (a) confusing total vs. effective porosity, (b) using $k$ in SI without the $9.87 \times 10^{-13}$ conversion factor, (c) forgetting the saturation sum constraint, (d) misapplying Archie's equation in shaly sands (use Waxman-Smits or Simandoux for shale correction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any reservoir rock problem, *always* begin by (1) listing given data with units, (2) stating the governing equation, (3) writing the substitution step *before* the arithmetic, and (4) boxing the final answer with units. This presentation style alone typically earns **1–2 extra marks** under the KTU valuation scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Caprocks: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved)**
> A **caprock** (also termed a *seal rock* or *roof rock*) is a low-permeability, ductile, and laterally continuous geologic stratum that overlies a hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir formation, preventing the vertical and lateral migration of oil, natural gas, and other formation fluids. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the caprock functions as the **upper closure element** of a structural or stratigraphic hydrocarbon trap.

A caprock must satisfy three simultaneous petrophysical criteria:
- **Ultra-low permeability** (typically $k < 0.001 \text{ mD}$ or $10^{-18} \text{ m}^2$)
- **High capillary entry pressure** exceeding the buoyant pressure of the underlying hydrocarbon column
- **Sufficient ductility and thickness** to remain intact under tectonic stress

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT522 – Module 1)**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus introduces caprocks as the second (and most critical) element of the **petroleum migration–accumulation–entrapment triad**, following source rock and preceding reservoir rock. Without an effective caprock, no commercial hydrocarbon accumulation can persist over geological time scales (10⁶–10⁸ years).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!TIP] **The "Pressure Cooker" Analogy**
> Imagine a pressure cooker on your kitchen stove. The water (hydrocarbons) inside boils, and steam (gas) pushes upward. The heavy, sealed metal lid on top — which refuses to let anything escape — is your **caprock**. Remove or crack that lid, and the entire contents escape into the atmosphere. Similarly, nature places oil and gas under enormous subsurface pressure, and a caprock is the geological "lid" that holds them in place for millions of years.

A more precise petroleum-engineering analogy is that of a **soda bottle cap**. The cap (caprock) holds pressurized gas (hydrocarbon) dissolved in liquid (formation water) beneath it. The seal works not because the cap is strong in the engineering sense, but because it is **impermeable at the molecular scale** — gas bubbles cannot squeeze through the microscopic pore throats.

### 1.3 Why Caprocks Matter in Oil & Gas Engineering

The role of the caprock extends beyond simple containment:
- **Trap integrity** — Determines whether a trap is commercially viable.
- **Column height control** — Sets the maximum hydrocarbon column a trap can hold before leakage.
- **Pressure compartmentalization** — Creates distinct pressure regimes in stacked reservoirs.
- **Seal against leakage pathways** — Works in tandem with faults to prevent fluid escape.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Misconception**
> A caprock is **not** the "hardest" or "most compacted" rock in the subsurface. Hardness and permeability are not the same property. A soft, ductile **evaporite** (rock salt) is one of the best caprocks, while a hard, brittle **basement granite** is typically a leaky basement rock, not a caprock. Seal capacity is governed by **pore-throat geometry and wettability**, not by mechanical strength.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Petrophysical Pillars of an Effective Caprock

Every competent caprock must simultaneously satisfy the following three engineering conditions:

**Pillar I — Sealing Capacity (Capillary Resistance)**
- The caprock must have a **capillary entry pressure ($P_{ce}$)** that exceeds the **buoyancy pressure ($P_b$)** of the trapped hydrocarbon column.
- This relationship is captured by the **Schowalter (1979) membrane seal model** and is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 1 examinations.

**Pillar II — Lateral Continuity (Geometric Closure)**
- The caprock must extend across the entire structural closure of the trap without pinching out, faulting through, or being absent over any part of the prospect.

**Pillar III — Mechanical Integrity (Geomechanical Resilience)**
- The caprock must deform plastically rather than brittlely under in-situ stresses, minimizing fracture development that would create leakage pathways.

### 2.2 Classification of Caprocks by Lithology

Caprocks are categorized by their primary rock-forming mineralogy, which directly controls their seal behavior:

| Lithological Class | Typical Permeability (mD) | Capillary Entry Pressure | Common Geological Setting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Evaporites** (Halite, Anhydrite, Gypsum) | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-9}$ | Extremely high | Salt basins, sabkha environments |
| **Shales & Mudstones** | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-7}$ | High to moderate | Marine and lacustrine basins |
| **Compacted Marls** | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-5}$ | Moderate | Carbonate platforms |
| **Tight Chalk / Carbonate** | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{-4}$ | Moderate to low | Deep marine settings |
| **Volcanic Tuffs (altered)** | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-6}$ | Variable | Rift basins |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 Module 1 question paper.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H$ | Buoyancy pressure of hydrocarbon column | Pa (or psi) |
| 2 | $P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$ | Capillary entry pressure (Laplace–Young) | Pa (or psi) |
| 3 | $H_{max} = \dfrac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$ | Maximum sealable hydrocarbon column height | m (or ft) |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{\phi^3}{c \cdot S^2 \cdot (1-\phi)^2} \cdot \text{const.}$ | Kozeny–Carman permeability (caprock $k$ must be $< 0.001$ mD) | m² (or mD) |
| 5 | $\text{SFI} = P_{ce} / P_b$ | Seal Failure Index (SFI $< 1$ indicates seal breach) | Dimensionless |

**Where the symbols denote:**
- $\rho_w$ = formation water density ≈ **1.0–1.1 g/cm³**
- $\rho_{hc}$ = hydrocarbon density ≈ **0.2–0.9 g/cm³** (oil/gas dependent)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ≈ **9.81 m/s²**
- $H$ = vertical hydrocarbon column height (m)
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension between hydrocarbon and water (**0.02–0.03 N/m** for oil-water, **0.05–0.07 N/m** for gas-water)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (typically 0°–30° for strongly water-wet caprocks)
- $r_{throat}$ = maximum connected pore-throat radius in the caprock (m)
- $\phi$ = porosity (fraction)
- $S$ = specific surface area (m²/m³)
- $c$ = Kozeny constant (≈ **2.5–5.0**)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The caprock concept underpins critical decisions in:
- **Exploration geology** — Determines prospect ranking and risking.
- **Reservoir engineering** — Defines the overburden pressure boundary condition.
- **Drilling engineering** — Caprocks are preferred locations for casing shoes and seal assembly zones.
- **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)** — The same seal criteria determine whether a depleted reservoir can securely store $CO_2$.
- **Nuclear waste isolation** — Salt formations (the ultimate caprock) are used as long-term waste repositories.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> The same petrophysical property — **low permeability with high capillary entry pressure** — that traps hydrocarbons also makes a caprock the ideal **permanent seal for subsurface waste injection** operations. The caprock concept is therefore not just academic; it directly governs modern subsurface engineering practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Sealable Hydrocarbon Column Height

This is the single most important derivation in Module 1. It quantifies **how much oil or gas a caprock of given seal capacity can hold** — the direct engineering question explored in KTU problems.

**Starting Premise (Physical Statement):**
A caprock seals a hydrocarbon column as long as the **buoyancy pressure pushing hydrocarbons upward** does not exceed the **capillary entry pressure of the seal**.

$$P_b \le P_{ce}$$

**Step 1: Express the Buoyancy Pressure**

The buoyancy force per unit area (pressure) on the hydrocarbon column arises from the density contrast between formation water and hydrocarbon, integrated over the column height.

$$P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H$$

*Explanation:* The pressure at the top of the hydrocarbon column equals the weight of the water column displaced by the hydrocarbon, minus the actual weight of the hydrocarbon. This net upward pressure is the buoyant force that drives leakage.

**Step 2: Express the Capillary Entry Pressure (Laplace–Young Equation)**

$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the minimum pressure differential required for the non-wetting phase (hydrocarbon) to displace the wetting phase (water) from the largest connected pore throat in the caprock. Larger pore throats → lower seal capacity.

**Step 3: Equate Buoyancy Pressure to Capillary Entry Pressure (Limit Case)**

For a perfect seal at maximum capacity, set $P_b = P_{ce}$:

$$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H_{max} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$$

**Step 4: Solve Algebraically for $H_{max}$**

$$H_{max} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

**Step 5: Final Form (Engineering Convention)**

$$\boxed{\,H_{max} = \dfrac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}\,}$$

*Final Interpretation:* The maximum column height a caprock can seal is directly proportional to (a) the capillary entry pressure of the caprock, (b) the interfacial tension, and (c) the contact angle; and inversely proportional to (d) the pore-throat radius and (e) the hydrocarbon-water density contrast.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Seal Failure Index (SFI)

To quickly diagnose whether a trap's seal is intact, reservoir engineers define a dimensionless index:

$$\text{SFI} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{ce}} = \dfrac{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H}{2 \gamma \cos\theta \, / \, r_{throat}}$$

*Interpretation:*
- If $\text{SFI} < 1$ → $P_b < P_{ce}$ → **Seal is intact** (hydrocarbons remain trapped).
- If $\text{SFI} = 1$ → **Critical threshold** (leakage begins).
- If $\text{SFI} > 1$ → **Seal breach** (caprock fails; hydrocarbons leak).

> [!TIP] **Numerical Memory Aid for KTU Exams**
> A typical good evaporite caprock can seal a gas column of $\approx 1000$ m. A typical shale caprock seals $\approx 100$ m of gas column. A tight carbonate seals only $\approx 10$ m. The order of magnitude difference reflects the pore-throat size hierarchy: **salt (Å) < shale (nm) < tight carbonate (µm)**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A gas reservoir is overlain by a shale caprock with the following properties:
- $r_{throat} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $\gamma = 0.050$ N/m (gas–water)
- $\theta = 30°$
- $\rho_w = 1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{gas} = 0.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Find:** The maximum gas column height the caprock can seal.

**Step 1: Compute Capillary Entry Pressure**

$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.050 \times \cos(30°)}{5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\cos(30°) = 0.866$$

$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.050 \times 0.866}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{0.0866}{5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$P_{ce} = 1.732 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 17.32 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute Density Contrast (convert to SI)**

$$\rho_w = 1.05 \times 1000 = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{gas} = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_w - \rho_{gas} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Compute Maximum Column Height**

$$H_{max} = \dfrac{1.732 \times 10^{7}}{850 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{1.732 \times 10^{7}}{8338.5}$$

$$H_{max} \approx 2077 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** This shale caprock can seal a gas column of approximately **2077 m** before capillary leakage occurs. This is a high-quality seal by petroleum industry standards.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Caprock Performance Indicators

| Parameter | Excellent Seal | Marginal Seal | Failed Seal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permeability $k$ | $< 10^{-6}$ mD | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-3}$ mD | $> 10^{-3}$ mD |
| Capillary entry $P_{ce}$ | $> 10$ MPa | 1–10 MPa | $< 1$ MPa |
| Pore-throat radius $r$ | $< 10$ nm | 10–100 nm | $> 100$ nm |
| Seal Failure Index SFI | $< 0.5$ | 0.5–1.0 | $> 1.0$ |
| Typical lithology | Halite, Anhydrite | Compacted shale | Sandstone, chalk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Role of Caprock in a Hydrocarbon Trap

The following architecture diagram maps the functional position of the caprock within a petroleum trap system.

```mermaid

    F --> G[Reservoir Rock]
    G --> H[Source Rock Below]
    H --> I[Basement Crystalline Rock]

    J[Capillary Pressure Pce] --> C
    K[Buoyancy Pressure Pb] --> D
    L[Hydrocarbon Column Height H] --> D

    M[Critical Condition] --> N{Pce greater than Pb}
    N -->|YES| O[SEAL INTACT - Trap Preserved]
    N -->|NO| P[SEAL BREACH - Leakage Occurs]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a caprock and state any two essential properties it must possess for effective hydrocarbon sealing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A caprock is a low-permeability geologic formation that overlies a hydrocarbon reservoir and prevents the upward and lateral migration of oil, gas, and formation fluids.
Two essential properties:
- (1) **Ultra-low permeability** ($k < 0.001$ mD) to physically block fluid flow.
- (2) **High capillary entry pressure** exceeding the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column to prevent capillary leakage.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Name the two best lithological classes of caprocks and justify why evaporites are considered the most effective seals.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The two best caprock classes are **(1) evaporites** (halite, anhydrite) and **(2) shales/mudstones**.
Evaporites are the most effective because:
- Halite (rock salt) has effectively **zero porosity** and **zero permeability** in undisturbed form.
- It is **highly ductile** and flows plastically under pressure, **self-sealing any fractures**.
- Its **capillary entry pressure is the highest** among all sedimentary rocks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

> **Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 – Module 1 Internal Choice]` **(a)** Explain in detail the three petrophysical conditions a caprock must satisfy to constitute an effective seal, with reference to the relevant physical parameters. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A sandstone reservoir is overlain by a shale caprock. The shale has a maximum connected pore-throat radius of $2 \times 10^{-8}$ m. Given the following data, calculate the maximum oil column height the shale can seal, and comment on the seal integrity. **(7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $\gamma_{oil-water} = 0.025$ N/m
- $\theta = 25°$ (water-wet system)
- $\rho_w = 1.04 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Actual oil column in the field: $H = 50$ m

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Petrophysical Conditions (7 Marks):**

1. **Sealing Capacity / Capillary Resistance:** The capillary entry pressure $P_{ce}$ of the caprock must exceed the buoyancy pressure $P_b$ of the trapped hydrocarbon column. This is quantified by:
$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat}} > P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H$$

2. **Lateral Continuity:** The caprock must extend continuously over the entire structural closure of the trap. Any pinching out, fault breach, or erosional window compromises the seal. The geometric closure is checked using seismic mapping.

3. **Mechanical Integrity:** The caprock must deform **plastically** rather than **brittlely** under in-situ stress. Ductile rocks (evaporites, shales) self-seal microfractures, while brittle caprocks fracture and leak.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute capillary entry pressure:
$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.025 \times \cos(25°)}{2 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\cos(25°) = 0.9063$$

$$P_{ce} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.025 \times 0.9063}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{0.04532}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.266 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute density contrast:
$$\rho_w - \rho_{oil} = (1.04 - 0.85) \times 1000 = 190 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Compute maximum column height:
$$H_{max} = \dfrac{2.266 \times 10^{6}}{190 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{2.266 \times 10^{6}}{1863.9}$$

$$H_{max} \approx 1215.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Seal integrity check:
$$H_{actual} = 50 \text{ m} \ll H_{max} = 1215.7 \text{ m}$$

Since the actual oil column is vastly smaller than the maximum sealable column, the **seal is highly competent and intact**. The Seal Failure Index:
$$\text{SFI} = \dfrac{50}{1215.7} = 0.041 \ll 1$$

**Conclusion:** The shale caprock provides a **highly efficient seal** with a large safety margin (only ~4% of seal capacity utilized).

---

### 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks)

> **Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 – Module 1 Internal Choice]` **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the position and role of the caprock in a structural hydrocarbon trap. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compare the sealing capacities of evaporite and shale caprocks. Explain why the depth of burial affects caprock performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Caprock Position and Role in a Structural Trap (7 Marks):**

In a structural trap (typically an anticline), the caprock is the **uppermost impermeable layer** that drapes over the reservoir rock, forming the top closure of the trap. Below the caprock, hydrocarbons (gas on top, oil below) accumulate within the porous reservoir rock, displacing formation water. The caprock's role is to prevent upward migration of these buoyant fluids over geological timescales.

*See Mermaid Diagram 4.1 above for a functional schematic of the trap architecture.*

**Key Functions:**
- Prevents vertical escape of hydrocarbons.
- Maintains the pressure regime of the reservoir.
- Holds the gas cap in place above the oil leg.
- Provides the seal necessary for commercial accumulation.

**(b) Comparative Analysis (7 Marks):**

| Property | Evaporite Caprock | Shale Caprock |
|---|---|---|
| Typical permeability | $10^{-6}$–$10^{-9}$ mD | $10^{-4}$–$10^{-7}$ mD |
| Capillary entry pressure | Very high ($>10$ MPa) | High (1–10 MPa) |
| Ductility | Excellent (plastic flow) | Moderate |
| Self-sealing ability | Outstanding (flows to fill fractures) | Limited |
| Seal failure under burial | Improves with depth (becomes more ductile) | Degrades if overcompacted and fractured |

**Effect of Depth of Burial:**
- **For shales:** With increasing depth, porosity decreases, but **permeability initially improves seal** until a threshold, after which **brittle failure and microfracturing** degrade seal quality. Overcompacted shales can leak.
- **For evaporites:** With increasing depth and pressure, halite becomes **more plastic and flows**, enhancing its seal capacity. This is why **deep salt is one of the best seals in petroleum systems** (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, North Sea Zechstein).
- Generally, very shallow caprocks are vulnerable to surface weathering and fracturing, while very deep caprocks may be at risk of ductile thinning or thermal cracking (especially for organic-rich shales generating gas).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in caprock questions that cost marks:
> 1. **Confusing trap and seal:** A "trap" is the entire geometric closure; the "seal" (caprock) is only its top element. Do not use these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Mixing up capillary entry pressure and buoyancy pressure** in the inequality direction. Remember: **$P_{ce}$ must be GREATER than $P_b$** for the seal to hold.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** (g/cm³ → kg/m³, mD → m²) in numerical problems. Most KTU students lose 1–2 marks here per question.
> 4. **Ignoring contact angle θ.** A strongly water-wet caprock has $\theta \approx 0°$, maximizing $\cos\theta$ and seal capacity. Always state this assumption.
> 5. **Treating all caprocks as "hard rocks".** Evaporite caprocks (rock salt) are the *opposite* of hard — they are soft and ductile. This is a frequent descriptive mistake in 3-mark definition questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Definition:** A caprock is the **impermeable upper seal** of a hydrocarbon trap.
- **Three criteria:** (1) Low permeability, (2) High capillary entry pressure, (3) Lateral continuity.
- **Best lithology:** Evaporites (halite > anhydrite) > Shales > Tight carbonates.
- **Key formula:** $H_{max} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{throat} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$
- **Seal Failure Index (SFI):** If SFI $< 1$ → seal holds; if SFI $> 1$ → seal fails.
- **Buoyancy driver:** Density contrast $(\rho_w - \rho_{hc})$ — larger contrast = stronger upward push = harder to seal.
- **Capillary entry pressure driver:** Smaller pore throats = higher $P_{ce}$ = better seal.
- **Analogy:** Caprock = pressure cooker lid / soda bottle cap.
- **Real-world hierarchy:** Salt (Å pores) seals > Shale (nm pores) seals > Carbonate (µm pores) seals.
- **Engineering applications:** Hydrocarbon trapping, $CO_2$ storage (CCS), nuclear waste disposal, underground gas storage.
- **Depth effect:** Evaporites improve with depth (more plastic); shales degrade past overcompaction threshold.

> **Module 1 Conceptual Closure:** The caprock is the *guardian* of the petroleum system. Without a competent caprock, source rocks generate hydrocarbons, migration occurs, but no accumulation survives. In every KTU Module 1 question on trap integrity, **start your reasoning with the caprock and the $P_{ce} > P_b$ condition**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Definition
In the context of **Oil and Natural Gas Engineering**, the term **"Characteristics"** of a hydrocarbon system refers to the comprehensive set of **physical, chemical, and thermodynamic properties** that define crude oil and natural gas. These properties dictate how the fluids behave under reservoir conditions, how they flow through porous media, and how they must be processed, transported, and refined in surface facilities.

The characteristics are broadly classified into:

1. **Crude Oil Characteristics** — physical and chemical properties of liquid hydrocarbons.
2. **Natural Gas Characteristics** — properties of gaseous hydrocarbons.
3. **Reservoir Rock Characteristics** — petrophysical properties governing storage and flow (porosity, permeability, saturation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary focus of this module is understanding **crude oil characteristics** (API gravity, viscosity, pour point, sulfur content, etc.) and **natural gas characteristics** (composition, specific gravity, heating value, compressibility factor). Reservoir rock properties are typically covered in detail in **Module 2**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of crude oil and natural gas like two siblings from the same family (hydrocarbons) but with very different personalities:

- **Crude oil** is like a **thick, lazy syrup** — it flows slowly, varies in color from golden honey to tar-black, and carries baggage like sulfur and wax.
- **Natural gas** is like an **invisible, hyperactive balloon** — it expands rapidly, is lighter than air, and carries tremendous energy in every cubic foot.

Just as a doctor checks **blood pressure, cholesterol, and sugar levels** to characterize a patient's health, a petroleum engineer measures **API gravity, viscosity, sulfur content, and heating value** to characterize a hydrocarbon reservoir.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **API Gravity (°API):** Always measured at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and **1 atm**.
> - **Standard Pressure:** **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 14.7 psi**.
> - **Standard Temperature:** **15.6 °C = 60 °F = 288.75 K**.
> - **API of Water:** **10 °API** (the dividing line between light and heavy oil).

---

### Why Characteristics Matter
Every downstream decision in petroleum engineering — from **drilling mud design** to **pipeline sizing** to **refinery configuration** — depends fundamentally on these characteristics. For example:
- High **viscosity** oil requires **steam injection** or **diluents** to flow.
- High **sulfur** ("sour") crude demands **special alloy pipelines** and **desulfurization units**.
- High **wax** content causes **pipeline plugging** in cold climates.
- High **H₂S** in natural gas requires **amine treating** and **safety protocols**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crude Oil Characteristics

#### A. API Gravity and Specific Gravity
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** of oil is the ratio of the density of oil to the density of water at **standard conditions (15.6 °C, 1 atm)**.
- **API Gravity** is an inverse scale developed by the American Petroleum Institute. Higher API means lighter (more valuable) oil.

#### B. Viscosity
- A measure of the **internal resistance of oil to flow**.
- Expressed in **centipoise (cP)** or **Pa·s** (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s).
- **Decreases** with increasing temperature.
- **Light crude**: 1–10 cP at reservoir conditions.
- **Heavy crude**: > 100 cP, sometimes reaching 10,000 cP.

#### C. Pour Point
- The **lowest temperature** at which the oil will flow under standard test conditions (ASTM D97).
- Critical for pipeline design in cold regions (Alaska, North Sea).

#### D. Flash Point and Fire Point
- **Flash Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil vapors ignite **momentarily** when exposed to a flame.
- **Fire Point**: Temperature at which oil vapors burn **continuously** for at least 5 seconds.
- Critical for **storage, transportation, and safety regulations**.

#### E. Sulfur Content
- Expressed as **weight % of sulfur** in the crude.
- **Sweet crude**: < 0.5 wt% S → premium pricing.
- **Sour crude**: > 0.5 wt% S → requires extra processing.

#### F. Wax Content
- Paraffin wax (C₁₈ to C₃₆+) precipitates at low temperatures.
- High wax → **flow assurance problems** in subsea pipelines.

#### G. Other Properties
- **Color**: Light amber → black (darker = heavier, more asphaltenes).
- **Odor**: Sweet crude has mild odor; sour crude has pungent H₂S smell.
- **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)**: Measure of volatility at 37.8 °C.
- **Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom)**: Indicates asphalt content.
- **Ash Content**: Mineral residue after combustion.

---

### 2.2 Natural Gas Characteristics

#### A. Composition
Natural gas is primarily a mixture of:
- **Methane (CH₄)**: 70–90 mol%
- **Ethane (C₂H₆)**: 0–20 mol%
- **Propane (C₃H₈), Butane (C₄H₁₀)**: small amounts (LPG components)
- **Inerts**: N₂, CO₂, H₂S, He, H₂O vapor

#### B. Specific Gravity of Gas
- Ratio of gas molar mass to air molar mass (**M_air = 28.97 g/mol**).
- Natural gas is **lighter than air** → disperses upward when leaked (safety advantage).

#### C. Heating Value (Calorific Value)
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)**: Heat released with water condensed.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**: Heat released with water as vapor.
- Typical natural gas HHV: **900–1200 BTU/scf**.

#### D. Compressibility Factor (Z)
- Correction factor in the real gas equation: **PV = ZnRT**.
- For ideal gas, **Z = 1**; for real natural gas, **Z < 1** at typical reservoir conditions.

#### E. Wobbe Index
$$ WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{SG_{gas}}} $$
- Indicates **interchangeability of fuel gases** in burners.

#### F. Sour vs. Sweet Gas
- **Sweet gas**: H₂S < 4 ppm and CO₂ < 2 mol%.
- **Sour gas**: H₂S > 4 ppm (must be treated to meet pipeline spec of < 4 ppm H₂S).

---

### 2.3 Reservoir Rock Characteristics (Brief Overview)

| Property | Definition | Typical Range |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| **Porosity (φ)** | Fraction of void space in rock | 5–30% (sandstone: 10–25%) |
| **Permeability (k)** | Ability to transmit fluids | 0.1 mD to >1 D |
| **Fluid Saturation (S)** | Fraction of pore volume occupied by a fluid | S_o + S_w + S_g = 1 |

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $SG_{oil} = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ at 15.6 °C | Specific gravity of oil | dimensionless |
| 2 | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$ | API Gravity | °API |
| 3 | $PV = ZnRT$ | Real Gas Equation | P in Pa, V in m³ |
| 4 | $SG_{gas} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | Gas specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $WI = \dfrac{HHV}{\sqrt{SG_{gas}}}$ | Wobbe Index | BTU/scf |
| 6 | $\rho_{oil} = 141.5 \times \dfrac{\rho_{water}}{131.5 + ^\circ API}$ | Density from API | lb/ft³ or kg/m³ |
| 7 | $1 \text{ cP} = 0.001 \text{ Pa·s}$ | Viscosity conversion | — |
| 8 | $1 \text{ Darcy} = 1000 \text{ mD}$ | Permeability conversion | — |
| 9 | $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$ | Saturation balance | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\mu \propto e^{E/RT}$ (Andrade equation approximation) | Viscosity-temperature | cP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the magic constant 141.5** in the API gravity formula. This is the most frequently tested equation in KTU board exams for this module.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Reservoir Engineering**: API gravity and viscosity determine primary recovery mechanisms.
- **Production Engineering**: Pour point and wax content dictate flow assurance strategies (insulation, chemical inhibitors, pigging frequency).
- **Refinery Design**: Sulfur content determines the type of hydrodesulfurization (HDS) unit needed.
- **Pipeline Engineering**: RVP and viscosity govern pumping requirements and pipeline sizing.
- **Gas Processing**: H₂S and CO₂ concentrations decide whether amine sweetening or membrane separation is required.
- **LNG Operations**: Heating value and Wobbe index govern liquefaction plant design and burner compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The **API gravity scale** is defined such that water has an API of **10 °API** at 60 °F. The mathematical relationship between API gravity and specific gravity is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Let the specific gravity of oil be $SG_{oil}$ and its density be $\rho_{oil}$.

$$SG_{oil} = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$$

**Step 2:** The API scale is defined to be **inversely proportional** to density (lighter oil = higher API). The empirical equation proposed by the API is:

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$$

**Step 3:** Verify for water:
- $SG_{water} = 1.0$ at 15.6 °C
- Substituting: $^\circ API_{water} = \dfrac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 10$ °API ✓

**Step 4:** For a typical light crude with $SG_{oil} = 0.85$:
- $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 \approx 35$ °API
- This is consistent with **Bonny Light crude (Nigeria)** at ~35 °API.

**Step 5:** Invert to find SG from API:
- $SG_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5}$

**Step 6:** Calculate density of oil in **kg/m³** (knowing $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ at 4 °C, but **999 kg/m³ at 15.6 °C**):

$$\rho_{oil} = SG_{oil} \times 1000 = \frac{141.5 \times 1000}{^\circ API + 131.5} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gas Density at Standard Conditions

**Step 1:** Start with ideal gas law modified for standard conditions:
$$\rho_{gas} = \frac{PM_{gas}}{ZRT}$$

**Step 2:** At **standard conditions (P_sc, T_sc)**, with Z ≈ 1 for low-pressure gas:
$$\rho_{gas,sc} = \frac{P_{sc} \cdot M_{gas}}{R \cdot T_{sc}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $P_{sc} = 101325$ Pa, $T_{sc} = 288.75$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$\rho_{gas,sc} = \frac{101325 \cdot M_{gas}}{8.314 \times 288.75}$$

$$\rho_{gas,sc} = \frac{101325 \cdot M_{gas}}{2400.6}$$

**Step 4:** For pure methane ($M = 16.04$ g/mol = 0.01604 kg/mol):
$$\rho_{CH_4,sc} = \frac{101325 \times 0.01604}{2400.6} = 0.677 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5:** This matches the well-known value of methane density at STP (~0.72 kg/m³ at 0 °C, 1 atm).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of 0.92. Calculate:
- (a) API gravity
- (b) Density in kg/m³
- (c) Classify it as light, medium, or heavy crude

**Solution:**

**(a) API Gravity:**
$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5$$

$$^\circ API = 153.80 - 131.5 = 22.3 \text{ °API}$$

**(b) Density in kg/m³:**
$$\rho_{oil} = 0.92 \times 1000 = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(c) Classification** (per KTU board convention):
- Light crude: API > 31.1
- Medium crude: 22 ≤ API ≤ 31.1
- Heavy crude: 10 < API < 22
- Extra-heavy: API ≤ 10

Since **22.3 °API** lies just above 22, this is a **medium crude oil**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Crude Oil vs. Natural Gas Property Comparison

| Property | Crude Oil | Natural Gas |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **State at STP** | Liquid | Gas |
| **Primary component** | Mixed hydrocarbons (C₅–C₆₀+) | Methane (C₁) dominant |
| **Density at STP** | 700–1000 kg/m³ | 0.6–0.9 kg/m³ |
| **Specific Gravity** | 0.7–1.0 (vs. water) | 0.5–0.8 (vs. air) |
| **Key quality metric** | API gravity (°API) | Heating value (BTU/scf) |
| **Flow behavior** | Viscous, controlled by viscosity | Compressible, controlled by Z-factor |
| **Main contaminants** | Sulfur, wax, asphaltenes | H₂S, CO₂, N₂, H₂O |
| **Storage** | Atmospheric tanks | Pressurized spheres / pipelines |
| **Standard spec** | ASTM D1298 (SG), D97 (pour point) | GPA 2145, ASTM D3588 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define API gravity. What is its significance in classifying crude oil?**

**Model Answer:**
API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity is a measure of how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water. It is mathematically defined as:

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$$

where $SG_{oil}$ is the specific gravity of oil at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and **1 atm**. Water has an API of 10 °API. Crude oils with higher API gravity are **lighter** and generally more valuable, as they yield more gasoline and light distillates upon refining. Crude is classified as:

- **Light crude**: °API > 31.1
- **Medium crude**: 22 ≤ °API ≤ 31.1
- **Heavy crude**: 10 < °API < 22
- **Extra-heavy crude**: °API ≤ 10

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for classification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between "sweet" and "sour" crude oil with respect to sulfur content.**

**Model Answer:**
The classification of crude oil as "sweet" or "sour" is based on its **sulfur content** (and H₂S level for gases):

| Parameter | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Sulfur content (wt%) | < 0.5% | > 0.5% |
| H₂S level | Negligible | Detectable (rotten egg smell) |
| Refining | Simple, cheaper | Requires HDS unit |
| Pipeline material | Carbon steel | Corrosion-resistant alloy |
| Market price | Premium | Discounted |
| Examples | Bonny Light, Brent | Arabian Heavy, Maya |

Sweet crude is preferred because low sulfur reduces refining costs, prevents **SO₂ air pollution**, and minimizes **pipeline corrosion**.

**(3 marks — 1 for each criterion, 1 for engineering significance)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **physical and chemical characteristics of crude oil**. Explain how API gravity, viscosity, pour point, and sulfur content affect crude oil production, transportation, and refining operations.

**(b) [7 marks]** A crude oil sample has an API gravity of **30 °API**. Calculate:
- (i) The specific gravity of the oil.
- (ii) The density in **kg/m³** and **lb/ft³**.
- (iii) The mass of **1 barrel** (1 bbl = 0.159 m³) of this oil.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Physical Characteristics of Crude Oil:**

1. **API Gravity**: A measure of lightness. Higher API = lighter oil = more valuable. Calculated as $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$. Affects refinery yield — light crude gives more gasoline; heavy crude gives more diesel and fuel oil residue.

2. **Viscosity**: Resistance to flow. Heavy oils (low API) have high viscosity (hundreds of cP), making them difficult to pump. Affects:
   - **Production**: Needs artificial lift (ESP, rod pump) or thermal EOR (steam flooding for heavy oil).
   - **Transportation**: Requires heating, diluents (e.g., condensate blending), or heated pipelines.
   - **Refining**: High-viscosity crude needs higher distillation temperatures.

3. **Pour Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil flows. Critical for:
   - Cold-region pipeline design (North Sea, Alaska).
   - Storage tank heating requirements.
   - Wax deposition control in subsea flowlines.

4. **Sulfur Content**:
   - Sweet (< 0.5%): Direct refining, low corrosion.
   - Sour (> 0.5%): Requires hydrodesulfurization (HDS), special alloys.
   - Environmental regulation: IMO 2020 cap of 0.5% sulfur in marine fuels.

**Chemical Characteristics:**

5. **Wax Content (Paraffin)**: Long-chain n-alkanes (C₁₈–C₆₀). Precipitates at low temperature → plugs pipelines. Controlled by chemical inhibitors (PPD) and pigging.

6. **Asphaltenes**: High-MW polyaromatic hydrocarbons. Cause emulsion stability and deposition in reservoirs and pipelines.

7. **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)**: Volatility measure. High-RVP crude loses light ends during storage → safety hazard (vapor cloud).

8. **Carbon Residue** (Conradson/Ramsbottom): Indicates asphalt content. High values affect coker unit operation.

**Impact Summary Table:**

| Property | Production | Transport | Refining |
|----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| High API (light) | Easy flow, high GOR | Lower viscosity, easy pumping | High gasoline yield |
| Low API (heavy) | Needs EOR, artificial lift | Heating/diluents needed | Residue processing, coking |
| High sulfur | Corrosion in wellbore | Sour-service alloys | HDS unit required |
| High wax | Deposition in tubing | Pigging, insulation | Wax hydrocracking |
| High pour point | Flow assurance issues | Heated pipelines | Dewaxing unit needed |

**(7 marks — 1 each for the 4 physical properties, 2 for chemical, 1 for impact summary)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $^\circ API = 30$

**(i) Specific Gravity:**
$$SG_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{30 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{161.5}$$

$$SG_{oil} = 0.8762$$

**(ii) Density:**

In kg/m³ (using $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ at 15.6 °C):
$$\rho_{oil} = SG \times 1000 = 0.8762 \times 1000 = 876.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

In lb/ft³ (using 1 kg/m³ = 0.0624 lb/ft³):
$$\rho_{oil} = 876.2 \times 0.0624 = 54.68 \text{ lb/ft}^3$$

**(iii) Mass of 1 barrel:**

Volume of 1 barrel: $V = 0.159 \text{ m}^3$

$$m = \rho \times V = 876.2 \times 0.159 = 139.32 \text{ kg}$$

Convert to lb: $m = 139.32 \times 2.205 = 307.2 \text{ lb}$

**(7 marks — 2 for SG, 3 for density, 2 for barrel mass)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **characteristics of natural gas**. Explain composition, specific gravity, compressibility factor, heating value, and Wobbe index with relevant equations. Distinguish between sweet and sour natural gas.

**(b) [7 marks]** A natural gas sample has the following composition (mol%):
- CH₄: 85%
- C₂H₆: 8%
- C₃H₈: 4%
- N₂: 2%
- CO₂: 1%

Calculate:
- (i) Average molecular weight of the gas mixture.
- (ii) Specific gravity relative to air (M_air = 28.97).
- (iii) Wobbe Index if HHV = 1000 BTU/scf.
- (iv) Classify the gas as sweet or sour.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**1. Composition of Natural Gas:**
Natural gas is a hydrocarbon gas mixture. Typical composition:
- **Methane (CH₄)**: 70–90 mol% (primary combustible).
- **Ethane (C₂H₆)**: 0–20 mol%.
- **Propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀)**: Trace amounts (recovered as LPG).
- **Non-hydrocarbon inerts**: N₂, CO₂, H₂S, He, H₂O vapor.

**2. Specific Gravity of Gas:**
$$SG_{gas} = \frac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$$

Natural gas is **lighter than air** (SG < 1), so it disperses upward when leaked — a major safety advantage over LPG (SG > 1).

**3. Compressibility Factor (Z):**
Real gas deviates from ideal at high P and low T:
$$PV = ZnRT$$
- Z < 1: Attractive forces dominate.
- Z > 1: Repulsive forces dominate.
- Determined experimentally or via **Standing-Katz chart**, or computed via **Peng-Robinson** or **Soave-Redlich-Kwong** EOS.

**4. Heating Value (Calorific Value):**
- **HHV (Higher Heating Value)**: Combustion heat including condensation of water vapor.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value)**: Excludes latent heat of water.
- Typical pipeline natural gas: HHV ≈ 1000–1100 BTU/scf, LHV ≈ 900–1000 BTU/scf.

**5. Wobbe Index:**
$$WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{SG_{gas}}}$$
- Indicator of **fuel interchangeability** in gas burners.
- Two gases with same WI give same heat output at constant pressure.

**6. Sweet vs. Sour Gas:**
- **Sweet gas**: H₂S < 4 ppm, CO₂ < 2 mol% — meets pipeline spec directly.
- **Sour gas**: H₂S > 4 ppm — requires **amine treating** (MEA, DEA, MDEA) for H₂S removal.
- Toxicity: H₂S is lethal at 700 ppm in 5 minutes.

**(7 marks — 1 each for 5 characteristics + 2 for sweet/sour discussion)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** y_CH₄ = 0.85, y_C₂H₆ = 0.08, y_C₃H₈ = 0.04, y_N₂ = 0.02, y_CO₂ = 0.01

**Molecular Weights:** M(CH₄) = 16.04, M(C₂H₆) = 30.07, M(C₃H₈) = 44.10, M(N₂) = 28.01, M(CO₂) = 44.01

**(i) Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{gas} = \sum y_i M_i$$

$$M_{gas} = (0.85 \times 16.04) + (0.08 \times 30.07) + (0.04 \times 44.10) + (0.02 \times 28.01) + (0.01 \times 44.01)$$

Computing each term:
- $0.85 \times 16.04 = 13.634$
- $0.08 \times 30.07 = 2.406$
- $0.04 \times 44.10 = 1.764$
- $0.02 \times 28.01 = 0.560$
- $0.01 \times 44.01 = 0.440$

$$M_{gas} = 13.634 + 2.406 + 1.764 + 0.560 + 0.440 = 18.804 \text{ g/mol}$$

**(ii) Specific Gravity:**
$$SG_{gas} = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{18.804}{28.97} = 0.649$$

Since SG < 1, the gas is **lighter than air** ✓.

**(iii) Wobbe Index:**
$$WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{SG_{gas}}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{0.649}} = \frac{1000}{0.8056} = 1241.3 \text{ BTU/scf}$$

**(iv) Sweet or Sour Classification:**
- H₂S content: **0 ppm** (not listed in composition).
- CO₂ content: **1 mol%** (< 2 mol%).

$$\therefore \text{The gas is classified as SWEET natural gas.}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for MW, 1 for SG, 2 for Wobbe, 2 for classification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting standard conditions**: Always state **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and **1 atm** when calculating SG or API. Students lose 1 mark for omitting this.
> 2. **Inverse API confusion**: Some students write $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} + 131.5$ (with **+** instead of **−**). Remember: water is 10 °API, NOT 263 °API. The minus sign is critical.
> 3. **Using $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ vs 999 kg/m³**: For API calculations, use 1000 kg/m³ (rounded) unless precision is asked.
> 4. **Confusing HHV and LHV**: HHV > LHV always. Wobbe Index uses **HHV**.
> 5. **Unit errors in barrel calculations**: 1 bbl = **0.159 m³** (not 0.158987, which is the exact value, but 0.159 is the KTU-accepted approximation).
> 6. **Molar mass of air**: Use **28.97 g/mol** (not 29). Examiners specifically test this decimal.
> 7. **Sweet/sour threshold for gas**: H₂S **4 ppm** (not 5, not 3). Commit this to memory.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **API Gravity Formula**: $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ — **the single most important equation** in Module 1.
- **Water API** = **10 °API** (reference benchmark).
- **Classification**: Light (>31.1), Medium (22–31.1), Heavy (<22), Extra-Heavy (≤10).
- **Crude Oil Properties to Memorize**: API gravity, viscosity, pour point, flash point, sulfur content, wax content, RVP, carbon residue, asphaltenes.
- **Sweet vs Sour**: Sweet < 0.5 wt% S; Sour > 0.5 wt% S.
- **Natural Gas Composition**: Methane-dominated; inerts include N₂, CO₂, H₂S.
- **Gas SG Formula**: $SG_{gas} = M_{gas} / 28.97$; **natural gas is lighter than air** (SG < 1).
- **Wobbe Index**: $WI = HHV / \sqrt{SG}$ — measures burner interchangeability.
- **Real Gas Law**: $PV = ZnRT$ — always used for natural gas.
- **Viscosity Units**: 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.
- **Permeability Units**: 1 Darcy = 1000 mD.
- **Saturation Balance**: $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$ — no void space is unoccupied.
- **Standard Conditions**: **15.6 °C, 1 atm** for oil; **15.6 °C, 1 atm** for gas (some standards use 0 °C, but KTU uses 15.6 °C).
- **Yellowcake and sulfur specs**: Pipeline H₂S limit is **4 ppm**.
- **Industrial Examples**: Bonny Light (35 °API, sweet), Arab Heavy (28 °API, sour), Maya (22 °API, sour), Alberta Bitumen (8 °API, extra-heavy).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
