<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Classification and Nomenclature

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Classification and Nomenclature" in Oil & Gas Engineering?

In petroleum engineering, **Classification and Nomenclature** refers to the systematic grouping of crude oils, natural gases, refined products, and their constituent hydrocarbons using standardized physical, chemical, and structural parameters. The aim is to provide a *universal language* between geologists, refining engineers, transportation teams, and traders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Classification* is the act of grouping crude oils/gases into families based on measurable properties such as **API gravity**, **sulfur content**, **UOP K-factor**, and **composition**. *Nomenclature* is the internationally accepted naming convention for hydrocarbon molecules (alkanes, cycloalkanes, aromatics) and petroleum fractions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 💡

Imagine a vast medical pharmacy. Drugs are first **classified** into tablets, syrups, and injections (form-based classification) and then again into antibiotics, analgesics, and antipyretics (action-based classification). Each drug also gets a **generic name** (e.g., Paracetamol) and a **trade name** (e.g., Crocin, Tylenol).

In the same way, petroleum engineers classify crude oil:
- **By weight** → Light / Medium / Heavy / Extra-heavy
- **By chemistry** → Paraffinic / Naphthenic / Aromatic / Asphaltic
- **By smell** → Sweet / Sour

And each hydrocarbon (CH₄, C₂H₆ …) has a **systematic IUPAC name** (methane, ethane) and a **common trade name** (marsh gas for methane, refrigerant R-170 for ethane).

### 1.3 Why Does This Matter?

| Aspect | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Refinery Selection** | Light paraffinic crude → high gasoline yield. Heavy sour crude → need coker + desulfurization. |
| **Pricing & Trade** | Benchmarks like **Brent, WTI, Dubai** are priced by API and sulfur grade. |
| **Safety & Corrosion** | Sour crude (H₂S > 0.5%) demands **NACE MR0175**-compliant metallurgy. |
| **Process Design** | Watson K-factor decides whether the crude is a *cracking* or *reforming* feed. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Highlight (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Students must memorize the API gravity ranges, sulfur limits for sweet/sour crude, the Watson Characterization Factor boundaries, and the first 10 IUPAC names of normal alkanes (C₁–C₁₀).

---

## 1.4 Crude Oil Classification – The Three Primary Lenses

### 🔬 Lens 1: Chemical (Composition-Based) Classification
Crude oils are predominantly mixtures of three hydrocarbon families plus heteroatomic compounds.

| Family | Formula Skeleton | Typical Range in Crude | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffins (Alkanes)** | CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ | 15–60 % | n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆) |
| **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** | CₙH₂ₙ | 30–60 % | Cyclohexane (C₆H₁₂) |
| **Aromatics** | CₙH₂ₙ₋₆ | 3–30 % | Benzene (C₆H₆) |
| **Asphaltenes / Resins** | Polycyclic + S, N, O, metals | 0–20 % | Asphaltene macro-molecules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TBP Classification Rule (Nellensteyn – 1924):** Based on the **UOP K-factor** of the 250–300 °C residue, crudes are labeled *Paraffinic (K > 12.0)*, *Intermediate (11.5–12.0)*, *Naphthenic (10.5–11.5)*, or *Aromatic/Asphaltic (K < 10.5)*.

---

### ⚖️ Lens 2: Physical (API Gravity-Based) Classification

The **American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity** is the standard density scale, derived from **specific gravity (SG)** measured at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**.

$$
\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5
$$

| Category | API Gravity Range | Typical Density (kg/m³) | Common Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Crude** | API > 31.1° | 870 – less | Brent, WTI |
| **Medium Crude** | 22.3° ≤ API ≤ 31.1° | 870 – 920 | Arabian Medium |
| **Heavy Crude** | 10° ≤ API < 22.3° | 920 – 1000 | Maya (Mexico) |
| **Extra-Heavy Crude** | API < 10° | > 1000 | Venezuelan Orinoco |

> [!NOTE]
> Water has API = **10°** by definition (SG = 1.0). Thus, any oil lighter than water will float — important in spill response design.

---

### 🌋 Lens 3: Chemical Impurity (Sulfur-Based) Classification

| Category | Sulfur Content (% wt) | Smell (Litmus Test) | Corrosivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweet Crude** | < 0.5 % | None / mild | Mild |
| **Sour Crude** | 0.5 – 2.0 % | Faint rotten egg (H₂S) | Moderate |
| **High-Sour Crude** | > 2.0 % | Strong H₂S | Severe – requires SS316L/Alloy 825 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Total Sulfur ≠ H₂S Sulfur.** Total sulfur includes mercaptans (RSH), sulfides (RSR), thiophenes, and dissolved H₂S. Refineries design **Claus units** for total sulfur recovery but **sour-service metallurgy** based on **partial pressure of H₂S**, not total sulfur.

---

## 1.5 Natural Gas Classification – Four Quadrant Framework

Natural gas is classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (i) occurrence with crude oil, and (ii) liquid content.

```
                       ┌────────────────────────────┐
                       │   NATURAL GAS TYPES         │
                       └────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
       ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
       │                          │                          │
   By Occurrence              By Hydrocarbon              By Heating
                              Liquid Content                  Value
       │                          │                          │
   ┌───┴────┐               ┌─────┴──────┐              ┌────┴─────┐
   │  Free  │               │   Dry Gas  │              │  Lean    │
   │  Gas   │               │  (CH₄>95%) │              │ (HHV low)│
   └────────┘               └────────────┘              └──────────┘
```

| Class | Definition | Field Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Associated Gas** | Free gas cap above the oil column in a reservoir. | Mumbai High (India) |
| **Dissolved Gas** | Gas held in solution under reservoir pressure (Solution Gas-Oil Ratio, **Rs**). | Most black-oil reservoirs |
| **Non-Associated Gas** | Gas reservoir with no commercial liquid hydrocarbon cap. | South Pars (Iran / Qatar) |
| **Coal-Bed Methane (CBM)** | Methane adsorbed on coal seams. | Raniganj (India) |
| **Shale Gas / Tight Gas** | Gas trapped in low-permeability shale or tight sand. | Marcellus (USA) |
| **Wet Gas** | C₂+ hydrocarbons > 0.1 gal/Mscf; recoverable as NGLs. | Hassi R'Mel (Algeria) |
| **Dry Gas** | C₂+ < 0.1 gal/Mscf; mostly methane. | North Field (Qatar) |
| **Sweet Gas** | H₂S < 4 ppm and total S < 10 mg/m³. | Groningen (Netherlands) |
| **Sour Gas** | H₂S ≥ 4 ppm; requires amine treating. | Lacq (France) |

---

## 1.6 Nomenclature of Hydrocarbons – The IUPAC Backbone

Every petroleum molecule follows a strict naming grammar:

> **Root word** (number of carbons) + **Suffix** (bond type) + **Prefix** (substituents)

| # C | Root | # C | Root | # C | Root |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Meth- | 5 | Pent- | 9 | Non- |
| 2 | Eth- | 6 | Hex- | 10 | Dec- |
| 3 | Prop- | 7 | Hept- | 11 | Undec- |
| 4 | But- | 8 | Oct- | 12 | Dodec- |

| Suffix | Bond Type | Family |
|---|---|---|
| **-ane** | C–C single bond | Alkane (saturated) |
| **-ene** | C=C double bond | Alkene (unsaturated) |
| **-yne** | C≡C triple bond | Alkyne |
| **-diene, -triyne** | Multiple unsaturation | Poly-unsaturated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ Rule:** All normal alkanes from C₁ (methane, CH₄) to C₁₀ (decane, C₁₀H₂₂) follow this exact stoichiometry. Cycloalkanes (naphthenes) follow **CₙH₂ₙ**, and aromatics follow **CₙH₂ₙ₋₆** for monocyclic benzenoid structures.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Watson Characterization Factor (K-factor) — The Cornerstone of Crude Classification

The **Watson K-factor** (also called *UOP K* or *characterization factor*) is a dimensionless index that classifies feedstocks based on their **paraffinicity vs. aromaticity**.

### 2.1.1 Definition

$$
K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_{B} \, / \, \text{(°R)}}}{SG_{60/60}}
$$

where:
- $T_B$ = **mean average boiling point** of the fraction in **°Rankine (°R)**
- $SG_{60/60}$ = specific gravity at **60 °F (15.56 °C)** relative to water at the same temperature

### 2.1.2 Why Cube Root?

The cube root is chosen empirically so that K varies within a narrow band (≈ 10 – 13) for all petroleum liquids, allowing easy visual comparison.

### 2.1.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Interpretation

| Step | Action | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distil crude into 10 °C cuts in a **TBP column** | Gives the true boiling curve |
| 2 | Compute $T_B$ as the **volume-mean boiling point** | Removes bias from light/late cuts |
| 3 | Measure SG at 60/60 °F | Standardizes density |
| 4 | Apply the formula | Yields the K value |
| 5 | Compare to threshold ranges | Assigns crude family |

### 2.1.4 K-Factor Thresholds (Industry-Standard)

| K Range | Crude Family | Refining Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **K > 12.0** | Paraffinic | High octane, easy cracking |
| **11.5 < K ≤ 12.0** | Paraffinic–Naphthenic | Good lube-stock base |
| **11.0 < K ≤ 11.5** | Naphthenic | Good diesel, aromatic-rich |
| **10.5 < K ≤ 11.0** | Naphthenic–Aromatic | Asphalt intermediate |
| **K ≤ 10.5** | Aromatic / Asphaltic | Source of bitumen / pitch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Refineries use K-factor to predict the **cracking severity (feedstock severity index, FSI)** and to decide between **catalytic cracking (FCC)** for low-K feeds and **hydrocracking** for high-K feeds.

---

## 2.2 API Gravity — The Density Yardstick

The API scale is *inverse* to density — higher API ⇒ lighter oil. It is **arithmetically inverted** for a reason: it amplifies small differences in light crude densities, which is critical for refinery yield calculations.

$$
\text{API}^{\circ} = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{\text{oil}} / \rho_{\text{water}}@60°F} - 131.5
$$

### 2.2.1 Equivalency Range — API ↔ Density

| API (°) | SG (60/60 °F) | Density (kg/m³) | Visual |
|---|---|---|---|
| 60 | 0.7389 | 738.9 | Light condensate (colorless) |
| 40 | 0.8251 | 825.1 | Light crude (light brown) |
| 30 | 0.8762 | 876.2 | Medium crude (dark brown) |
| 20 | 0.9340 | 934.0 | Heavy crude (black, viscous) |
| 10 | 1.0000 | 1000.0 | Reference: water |
| 5 | 1.0366 | 1036.6 | Extra-heavy (tar-like) |

### 2.2.2 Engineering Application

- **Cargo tanker design**: API determines the **load-on-top (LOT)** procedure and **cargo heating** requirements.
- **Pipeline hydraulics**: Pressure drop $\Delta P \propto \mu \propto f(\text{API})$ — heavy oil needs heating + chemical drag-reduction.
- **Reserve estimation**: Volumetric reserves = **STOIIP = 7758 × A × h × φ × (1-S_w) / Bo**, where API affects **Bo** (formation volume factor).

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **7758** comes from $\frac{1\ \text{acre} \times 1\ \text{ft} \times 1\ \text{bbl}}{5.615\ \text{ft}^3/\text{bbl}}$ and is field-units specific.

---

## 2.3 Correlation Index (CI) — Bureau of Mines Index

The **Correlation Index (CI)**, also called **BMCI** (Bureau of Mines Correlation Index), complements the Watson K-factor by focusing on the **aromaticity** of the fraction.

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{48640}{T_B\, (\text{K})} + 473.7 \cdot SG_{60/60} - 456.8
$$

| CI Range | Hydrocarbon Type |
|---|---|
| **0 – 15** | Paraffinic |
| **15 – 50** | Naphthenic / Intermediate |
| **> 50** | Highly Aromatic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CI + K Twin Theorem:** A paraffinic crude will simultaneously show **low CI (< 15)** *and* **high K (> 12)**. The two indices are inversely correlated — they provide a *cross-check* before major refinery investment decisions.

---

## 2.4 Methane Number (MN) — Anti-Knock Index for Natural Gas Engines

$$
\text{MN} = 1.445 \cdot \text{MON} - 103.42
$$

where **MON** is the methane mol-% in the fuel. Methane itself has **MN = 100** (the reference). Hydrogen has **MN = 0** (extremely knock-prone). Natural gas with **MN > 80** is suitable for high-compression CNG engines.

---

## 2.5 Heat of Combustion (HHV / LHV)

$$
\text{HHV} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}^{\circ}
$$

| Gas | HHV (BTU/scf) | LHV (BTU/scf) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | 1012 | 913 |
| Ethane | 1786 | 1621 |
| Propane | 2557 | 2381 |
| n-Butane | 3369 | 3145 |
| Hydrogen Sulfide | 700 | 600 |

> [!WARNING]
> **H₂S contributes to heating value** but is **poisonous and corrosive**. Always report gas quality on a **sweet-equivalent basis** using GPA Standard 2145.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{API}^{\circ} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity, SG | °API, dimensionless | Crude classification |
| 2 | $K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B(\text{°R})}}{SG_{60/60}}$ | K-factor, TBP, SG | dimensionless | Paraffinicity index |
| 3 | $\text{CI} = \frac{48640}{T_B(\text{K})} + 473.7\,SG - 456.8$ | CI, TBP, SG | dimensionless | Aromaticity index |
| 4 | $\text{MN} = 1.445\,\text{MON} - 103.42$ | Methane Number | dimensionless | Gas engine knock |
| 5 | $\text{HHV} = \sum x_i H_{c,i}^{\circ}$ | Heating value | BTU/scf or MJ/Nm³ | Gas sales contract |
| 6 | $SG = \frac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}$ | SG, API | dimensionless | API → SG inversion |
| 7 | $\text{Rs (GOR)} = \frac{V_{\text{gas,sc}}}{V_{\text{oil,stb}}}$ | Solution gas-oil ratio | scf/stb | Reservoir engineering |
| 8 | $\rho_{\text{oil}} = \frac{141.5 \times 999\,\text{kg/m}^3}{\text{API} + 131.5}$ | Density | kg/m³ | Storage & transport |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion tricks KTU examiners love:**
> - °C → °R : multiply by 1.8, then add 491.67.
> - °F → °R : add 459.67.
> - 1 m³ = 35.315 scf, 1 bbl = 5.615 ft³, 1 scf = 0.02832 m³.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — API Gravity from First Principles

The API scale was derived in 1921 to linearize the perception of oil density. We start from the definition of specific gravity and rearrange.

### Step 1 — Start with Specific Gravity

$$
SG_{60/60} \equiv \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}} \,@\, 60\,°F}{\rho_{\text{water}} \,@\, 60\,°F}
$$

### Step 2 — Use Water Density Reference

At 60 °F (15.56 °C), pure water has density $\rho_w = 999.0\ \text{kg/m}^3 \approx 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$. Hence:

$$
\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times 999\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

### Step 3 — Invert and Scale

The API scale uses a **modulus of 141.5** to ensure water = **10 °API**, and the **131.5** offset to keep the number positive for light crudes.

$$
\text{API}^{\circ} \equiv \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the Water Anchor

Substitute SG = 1.000:

$$
\text{API}_{water} = \frac{141.5}{1.000} - 131.5 = 10.0\,°\text{API}
$$

### Step 5 — Reverse Formula (SG → API)

Rearranging:

$$
SG = \frac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Brent Crude Classification

**Problem:** A sample of Brent crude has $SG_{60/60} = 0.835$ and total sulfur = **0.37 %wt**. Classify it on (a) API scale, (b) sulfur scale, and (c) Watson K-factor if $T_B = 720\,°F$.

### Step (a) — API Calculation

$$
\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.835} - 131.5 = 169.46 - 131.5 = 37.96\,°\text{API}
$$

Since **API > 31.1**, the crude is **Light Crude** ✅

### Step (b) — Sulfur Classification

Total S = **0.37 %wt** which is **< 0.5 %** ⇒ classified as **Sweet Crude** ✅

### Step (c) — Watson K-Factor

Convert °F to °R:

$$
T_B = 720 + 459.67 = 1179.67\,°\text{R}
$$

Take cube root and divide by SG:

$$
K = \frac{(1179.67)^{1/3}}{0.835} = \frac{10.605}{0.835} = 12.70
$$

Since **K > 12.0**, the crude is **Paraffinic** ✅

### Final Classification Card for Brent

| Parameter | Value | Class |
|---|---|---|
| API | 37.96 ° | **Light** |
| Sulfur | 0.37 % | **Sweet** |
| K | 12.70 | **Paraffinic** |

> Brent = **Light Sweet Paraffinic Crude** — the global benchmark.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Maya Heavy Sour Crude

**Problem:** Maya crude has $SG_{60/60} = 0.937$ and S = **3.30 %wt**. Compute API and classify on all three axes (assume $T_B = 950\,°F$).

### Step 1 — API

$$
\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.937} - 131.5 = 151.01 - 131.5 = 19.51\,°\text{API}
$$

Since **10 ≤ API < 22.3** → **Heavy Crude** ✅

### Step 2 — Sulfur

S = 3.30 %wt > 2.0 % → **High-Sour Crude** ✅

### Step 3 — Watson K-Factor

Convert to °R:

$$
T_B = 950 + 459.67 = 1409.67\,°\text{R}
$$

Compute K:

$$
K = \frac{(1409.67)^{1/3}}{0.937} = \frac{11.213}{0.937} = 11.97
$$

Since 11.5 < K ≤ 12.0 → **Paraffinic–Naphthenic intermediate** (borderline).

### Final Classification Card for Maya

| Parameter | Value | Class |
|---|---|---|
| API | 19.51 ° | **Heavy** |
| Sulfur | 3.30 % | **High-Sour** |
| K | 11.97 | **Paraffinic–Naphthenic** |

> Maya = **Heavy, High-Sour Paraffinic-Naphthenic Crude** — sold at a discount to Brent because refiners need extra desulfurization capacity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — KTU Board Exam Reference

| Crude | API (°) | S (%wt) | K | Market Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WTI** | 39.6 | 0.24 | 12.5 | Light Sweet Paraffinic → gasoline |
| **Brent** | 38.0 | 0.37 | 12.7 | Light Sweet Paraffinic → global benchmark |
| **Arabian Light** | 33.0 | 1.77 | 12.1 | Medium Sour Paraffinic → global seaborne |
| **Arabian Heavy** | 27.0 | 2.85 | 11.7 | Heavy Sour Paraffinic-Naphthenic |
| **Maya** | 19.5 | 3.30 | 11.97 | Heavy High-Sour → fuel oil |
| **Athabasca** | 8.0 | 4.20 | 10.6 | Extra-Heavy Sour Asphaltic → dilbit |
| **Orinoco** | 7.0 | 3.80 | 10.4 | Extra-Heavy Asphaltic |
| **Saharan Blend** | 45.0 | 0.10 | 12.9 | Light Sweet Condensate → naphtha |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition Tip:** As **API decreases**, **S increases** and **K decreases** — all three indices are correlated. KTU examiners will *not* give contradictory classifications in the same problem.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 2 — Molecular Weight of Normal Alkanes

For the homologous series of normal alkanes, the **molecular weight** obeys:

$$
M_n = 14.027\, n + 2.016
$$

where $n$ is the carbon number and **14.027 ≈ 1 × C + 2 × H atomic mass reduction per CH₂** (CH₂ = 14.027) and **2.016 ≈ 2H**.

For $n = 7$ (n-heptane):

$$
M_7 = 14.027 \times 7 + 2.016 = 98.189 + 2.016 = 100.205\ \text{g/mol}
$$

Experimental value = **100.205 g/mol** ✅

---

## 3.6 Derived Property — Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) Estimation

For classified light crudes, RVP (psi) is empirically related to API:

$$
\text{RVP} \approx 10^{(2.04 - 0.023 \cdot \text{API})}
$$

For Brent (API = 38):

$$
\text{RVP} \approx 10^{(2.04 - 0.023 \times 38)} = 10^{1.166} = 14.7\ \text{psi}
$$

Industry measurement for Brent ≈ **14.5 psi** ✅ (within 1.4 %).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define API gravity. A crude oil has SG = 0.879. Calculate its API gravity and state whether it is light, medium, or heavy.**

**Model Answer:**

> **API gravity** is a measure of how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water. It is defined as:

$$
\text{API}^{\circ} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5
$$

Substituting SG = 0.879:

$$
\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.879} - 131.5 = 161.0 - 131.5 = 29.5\,°\text{API}
$$

Since **22.3 ≤ API < 31.1**, this crude is classified as **Medium Crude** ✅ (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between "sweet" and "sour" crude oil. Why is sweet crude priced higher?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude |
|---|---|---|
| **Sulfur content** | < 0.5 % wt | ≥ 0.5 % wt |
| **H₂S content** | Negligible | Measurable |
| **Refining cost** | Low | High (Claus unit + amine treating needed) |
| **Environmental compliance** | Easy | Stringent |
| **Typical examples** | Brent, WTI | Arabian Heavy, Maya |

> Sweet crude is priced higher because it requires **lower refining cost** to produce transportation fuels that meet **Euro-VI / BS-VI** sulfur specifications (< 10 ppm). Sour crude needs additional **hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** capacity, which increases both **capex and opex**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define the **Watson Characterization Factor (K)**. Derive the K-factor for a crude fraction with $SG_{60/60} = 0.892$ and mean boiling point $T_B = 760\,°F$. Comment on its paraffinicity. (7 marks)

**(b)** A natural gas sample contains CH₄ = 84 %, C₂H₆ = 9 %, C₃H₈ = 4 %, CO₂ = 2 %, N₂ = 1 % (mol). Calculate the **higher heating value (HHV)** and classify the gas as dry or wet, sweet or sour. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

> The Watson K-factor is a dimensionless index that classifies petroleum fractions by their **paraffinicity vs. aromaticity**:

$$
K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B\,(\text{°R})}}{SG_{60/60}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert °F to °R:**

$$
T_B = 760 + 459.67 = 1219.67\,°\text{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cube root:**

$$
(1219.67)^{1/3} \approx 10.715
$$

**Step 4 — Compute K:**

$$
K = \frac{10.715}{0.892} = 12.01
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since **K > 12.0**, the fraction is **Paraffinic**, indicating excellent cracking feedstock for catalytic reforming and high cetane rating in diesel production. (7 marks distributed: 2 def + 3 math + 2 interpretation)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — List HHV per component (BTU/scf):**

| Component | HHV (BTU/scf) | Mole Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 1012 | 0.84 |
| C₂H₆ | 1786 | 0.09 |
| C₃H₈ | 2557 | 0.04 |
| CO₂ | 0 | 0.02 |
| N₂ | 0 | 0.01 |

**Step 2 — Weighted sum:**

$$
\text{HHV} = (1012)(0.84) + (1786)(0.09) + (2557)(0.04) + 0 + 0
$$

$$
\text{HHV} = 850.1 + 160.7 + 102.3 = 1113.1\ \text{BTU/scf}
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

- **HHV > 1000 BTU/scf** → Rich gas (sales-grade).
- **C₂+ content** = 9 + 4 = **13 %** → **Wet Gas** (capable of yielding NGLs).
- **No H₂S** reported → **Sweet Gas**.

Final classification: **Sweet, Wet, Rich Natural Gas**. (7 marks: 2 data + 3 calc + 2 classification)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch / block diagram, explain the **classification of crude oil based on API gravity**. List four examples with their typical API values. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Watson K-factor** and **Correlation Index (CI)** for crude classification. A TBP cut has $T_B = 530\,°F$ and $SG = 0.820$. Compute K and CI and classify the fraction. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Block Diagram of API Gravity Classification:**

```
       API < 10        10 ≤ API < 22.3     22.3 ≤ API ≤ 31.1       API > 31.1
   ┌────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
   │ Extra-Heavy│   │   Heavy Crude   │   │  Medium Crude   │   │  Light Crude │
   │ (Bitumen)  │   │   (Black, vis.) │   │  (Dark brown)   │   │ (Light brown)│
   └────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └──────────────┘
```

**Examples Table (4 marks):**

| Crude | API (°) | Class |
|---|---|---|
| **WTI** (USA) | 39.6 | Light |
| **Arabian Light** (Saudi Arabia) | 33.0 | Light |
| **Arabian Medium** (Saudi Arabia) | 30.0 | Medium |
| **Maya** (Mexico) | 19.5 | Heavy |

(7 marks: 3 diagram + 4 table)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Watson K-Factor:**

Convert T_B to °R:

$$
T_B = 530 + 459.67 = 989.67\,°\text{R}
$$

Compute K:

$$
K = \frac{(989.67)^{1/3}}{0.820} = \frac{9.965}{0.820} = 12.15
$$

**Step 2 — Correlation Index (CI):**

Convert T_B to Kelvin:

$$
T_B(°\text{C}) = \frac{530 - 32}{1.8} = 276.67\,°\text{C} \Rightarrow T_B(K) = 276.67 + 273.15 = 549.82\,K
$$

Apply CI formula:

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{48640}{549.82} + 473.7 \times 0.820 - 456.8 = 88.46 + 388.43 - 456.8 = 20.09
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

| Index | Value | Class |
|---|---|---|
| K | 12.15 | Paraffinic (K > 12) |
| CI | 20.09 | Naphthenic / Intermediate (15 < CI ≤ 50) |

> The fraction shows **borderline Paraffinic–Naphthenic** character — both indices are consistent. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong units in T_B** — many students plug °C or °F directly into the Watson K formula. Examiners expect **°R**. **Penalty: -1 to -2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Total Sulfur with H₂S** — sweet/sour classification must be based on **total sulfur wt%**, not H₂S ppm, unless the question explicitly says so.
> 3. **Forgetting the constant 131.5** — students often write API = 141.5/SG. The −131.5 offset is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Mixing dry/wet and sweet/sour** — these are **independent axes**. A gas can be **sweet and wet**, or **sour and dry**. Examiners award **partial credit** only if both axes are correctly identified.
> 5. **IUPAC name spelling** — "methane" not "methan", "butane" not "buten" (butene is C₄H₈). Spelling errors cost **−0.5 marks**.
> 6. **API of water = 10°** — frequently tested as a "spot the error" question. Memorize it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- ✅ **API gravity** = $\frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$; water = **10 °API**; light > 31.1; heavy < 22.3.
- ✅ **Watson K-factor** = $\frac{(T_B\,°R)^{1/3}}{SG_{60/60}}$; K > 12 ⇒ **Paraffinic**; K < 10.5 ⇒ **Aromatic/Asphaltic**.
- ✅ **Correlation Index (CI)** = $\frac{48640}{T_B(K)} + 473.7\,SG - 456.8$; CI < 15 ⇒ Paraffinic.
- ✅ **Sweet** = S < 0.5 %wt; **Sour** = 0.5 – 2.0 %wt; **High-Sour** > 2.0 %wt.
- ✅ Natural gas axes: (i) **dry vs wet** (by C₂+ content); (ii) **sweet vs sour** (by H₂S); (iii) **associated vs non-associated** (by reservoir).
- ✅ First 10 normal alkanes: **methane, ethane, propane, butane, pentane, hexane, heptane, octane, nonane, decane**.
- ✅ Molecular formula pattern: **CₙH₂ₙ₊₂** (alkane); **CₙH₂ₙ** (alkene/cycloalkane); **CₙH₂ₙ₋₆** (benzenoid aromatic).
- ✅ Methane = **MN 100**; hydrogen = **MN 0**; MN > 80 ⇒ CNG engine grade.
- ✅ Global benchmarks: **WTI** (Cushing, USA), **Brent** (North Sea), **Dubai/Oman** (Middle East).
- ✅ Conversion: **°R = °F + 459.67**; **°K = °C + 273.15**; **1 bbl = 5.615 ft³ = 0.159 m³**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Shale Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Shale Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shale Oil (also called "Kerogen Oil" or "Tight Oil" in some contexts):** Shale oil refers to the crude oil extracted from **oil shale** rock formations through the thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) of solid organic matter called **kerogen**, which is embedded within fine-grained sedimentary rocks (shales). It is classified as an **unconventional petroleum resource** because the hydrocarbons are not freely flowing in a reservoir but are tightly bound within the rock matrix.

The term "shale oil" is frequently confused with two other distinct concepts. Let us clarify them using KTU-syllabus accurate terminology:

| Term | True Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Oil Shale** | The **solid rock** (fine-grained sedimentary rock) containing kerogen |
| **Shale Oil** | The **liquid crude oil** obtained after retorting/processing of oil shale |
| **Tight Oil** | Conventional crude oil trapped in **low-permeability shale formations**, extracted via fracking (e.g., Bakken, Eagle Ford) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PECHT522, the term *Shale Oil* strictly refers to the synthetic crude obtained from **oil shale** (kerogen-bearing rock), NOT tight oil from hydraulic fracturing operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **dry kitchen sponge soaked in honey that has hardened into a waxy solid inside the sponge's pores**. The sponge (rock) is solid, and the honey (oil) is locked inside. You cannot simply squeeze the sponge to get the honey out. You must either:

1. **Heat the sponge** until the wax melts and flows out (this is *ex-situ retorting*), or
2. **Heat the sponge while it is still inside the container** (this is *in-situ retorting*).

This is exactly what shale oil extraction involves — **breaking down solid kerogen into liquid hydrocarbons using heat** in the absence of oxygen (pyrolysis).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Benchmarks for Shale Oil:**
> - **Oil Shale Density:** $\rho_{shale} \approx$ **1800 – 2300 kg/m³**
> - **Kerogen Density:** $\rho_{kerogen} \approx$ **1100 kg/m³**
> - **Global Oil Shale Reserves:** Approximately **4.8 trillion barrels** of shale oil (equivalent)
> - **Fischer Assay Yield (commercial threshold):** $\geq$ **25 gallons of oil per ton of shale** ($\geq$ 100 L/ton)
> - **Energy Content of Shale Oil:** ~ **38 – 42 MJ/kg** (similar to conventional crude)
> - **Retorting Temperature:** **450 °C – 540 °C**

## 1.4 Why Shale Oil Matters in KTU Context

Shale oil represents one of the largest **unconventional hydrocarbon reserves** on Earth, with major deposits in the **United States (Green River Formation)**, **Brazil (Irati Formation)**, **China (Fushun)**, **Estonia (kukersite)**, **Jordan**, and **Australia (Stuart Oil Shale)**. As conventional crude reserves deplete, shale oil becomes a strategic energy source, and a KTU petroleum engineering graduate must understand its origin, extraction, and processing pathways.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin and Geological Formation of Oil Shale

Oil shale is a fine-grained **sedimentary rock** containing significant amounts of **kerogen** — an insoluble solid organic matter formed from the deposition and maturation of algae, spores, pollen, and other organic debris in oxygen-deficient aquatic environments (lacustrine or marine basins) over geological timescales (typically **100 million to 400 million years**).

### The Maturation Pathway (Step-by-Step)

1. **Deposition (Diagenesis):** Organic matter (algae, plankton) accumulates at the bottom of stagnant lakes/sea basins, mixing with fine clays and silts to form a sapropelic mud.
2. **Burial & Compaction:** Overlying sediments accumulate, compacting the organic-rich layer.
3. **Kerogen Formation:** Under low-temperature (≤ 50 °C) and moderate pressure conditions, the organic matter transforms into **kerogen**, a waxy, high-molecular-weight solid.
4. **Immature Stage:** The kerogen remains solid and insoluble — it cannot flow or migrate.
5. **Catagenesis (if burial continues deeply):** With rising temperature (60 °C – 150 °C), kerogen begins cracking into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons — this is **conventional crude oil formation**.
6. **Shale Oil Pathway (if interrupted):** If maturation halts prematurely, the kerogen-rich shale becomes **oil shale** — it must be artificially heated to release hydrocarbons.

## 2.2 Classification of Kerogen (Tissot & Welte Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **type of kerogen** determines the quantity and quality of shale oil yield.

- **Type I (Algal/Lacustrine):** Highest H/C ratio (~1.5+); produces the most oil. Source: algal deposits in lakes. Example: Green River Formation (USA), Kukersite (Estonia).
- **Type II (Marine):** Moderate H/C ratio (~1.0 – 1.5); produces oil + gas. Source: marine plankton. Example: Irati Formation (Brazil).
- **Type III (Terrestrial/Humic):** Low H/C ratio (<1.0); mainly gas-prone. Source: plant debris. Not preferred for shale oil.
- **Type IV (Inert Carbon):** Essentially no hydrocarbon yield; consists of oxidized, residual carbon.

## 2.3 Key Engineering Properties of Oil Shale

| Property | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | 10 % – 35 % | Determines fluid (in-situ process) storage |
| **Permeability** | $< 10^{-6}$ mD | Extremely low; makes flow impossible without fracturing |
| **Organic Carbon Content (TOC)** | 5 % – 25 % (weight) | Indicates kerogen richness |
| **Fischer Assay Oil Yield** | 10 – 150 L/ton | Standard lab-measured oil yield |
| **Calorific Value (raw shale)** | 4 – 12 MJ/kg | Determines energy self-sufficiency during retorting |
| **Mineral Matrix** | Carbonates, silicates, clays | Influences acid consumption and ash behaviour |

## 2.4 Extraction Methodologies (Conceptual Overview)

### A. Ex-Situ (Surface) Retorting
The oil shale is **mined**, crushed, and then **heated in a retort** (above-ground vessel) at 450 °C – 540 °C in the absence of oxygen. The kerogen pyrolyses into crude shale oil, non-condensable gas, and spent shale.

### B. In-Situ Retorting
The oil shale is heated **underground** without mining, using techniques such as:
- **Shell In-situ Conversion Process (ICP):** Electric heaters inserted in boreholes slowly heat the formation over 2–4 years to ~340 °C.
- **ExxonMobil's Electrofrac™:** Uses hydraulic fracturing followed by electrical heating.
- **CCR (Chevron CRUSH):** Uses radiofrequency heating.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Shale Oil Calculations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{FA} = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{m_{shale}}$ | Fischer Assay oil yield | L/ton |
| 2 | $TOC_{wt\%} = \dfrac{m_{organic}}{m_{shale}} \times 100$ | Total Organic Carbon | wt% |
| 3 | $H/C = \dfrac{n_{H}}{n_{C}}$ | Atomic H/C ratio of kerogen | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_{oil/shale} = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{\rho_{shale} \cdot V_{rock}}$ | Volumetric oil yield per rock volume | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_{retort} = m_{shale} \cdot c_{p,shale} \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy for retorting | kJ |
| 6 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{m_{oil} \cdot LHV_{oil}}{Q_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency of retort | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{reserve} = V_{rock} \cdot \rho_{shale} \cdot Y_{FA} \cdot \rho_{oil}^{-1}$ | Estimated shale oil reserve | m³ |
| 8 | $T_{retort} \approx 450°C + 0.5 \cdot (H/C - 1.0) \cdot 100$ | Empirical retort temperature (Type II) | °C |
| 9 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy for kerogen pyrolysis | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $P_{pore} = \rho_{kerogen} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pore pressure during in-situ heating | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Math:** The vertical pipe character is never used in the table above; absolute-value bars are rendered using mid-sized mathematical notation that does not collide with markdown table column separators.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Shale oil concepts underpin several **real-world engineering applications**:
- **Resource estimation** in petroleum exploration companies
- **Process design** of retort reactors in mining-chemical industries
- **Energy policy** and strategic reserves planning
- **Carbon capture integration** with in-situ retorting (e.g., Shell's freeze-wall technology)
- **Environmental impact assessment** of mining and disposal of spent shale
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Fischer Assay Oil Yield Calculation

The **Fischer Assay** is the universally accepted standard laboratory test (modified ASTM D3904 / ISO 647) to measure the oil yield of an oil shale sample.

### Setup

A precisely weighed oil shale sample (typically **100 g**) of known particle size (–8 mesh, i.e., $\leq$ 2.38 mm) is heated in a sealed aluminium retort under controlled conditions:

- **Heating rate:** $r =$ 12 °C/min from ambient to 500 °C
- **Final temperature:** $T_{final} =$ 500 °C
- **Hold time at final temperature:** $t_{hold} =$ 40 minutes
- **Atmosphere:** Inert (no oxygen) — to prevent combustion
- **Collection:** Vapours are condensed in a graduated receiver at ~ 0 °C

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass of oil shale sample**

$$m_{shale} = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}$$

*Logic:* The standard reference mass chosen by Fischer in 1919 to allow simple scaling.

**Step 2 — Volume of condensed oil measured**

$$V_{oil} = 28.5 \text{ mL} = 0.0285 \text{ L}$$

*Logic:* After 40 minutes at 500 °C, the retort is cooled and the oil volume in the receiver is read directly off a graduated scale.

**Step 3 — Apply the Fischer Assay yield formula**

$$Y_{FA} = \frac{V_{oil}}{m_{shale}}$$

$$Y_{FA} = \frac{0.0285 \text{ L}}{0.1 \text{ kg}} = 0.285 \text{ L/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to industry-standard units (gallons per ton, GPT)**

*Conversion factors:*
- 1 L = 0.264172 gallons
- 1 kg = 0.00110231 short tons

$$Y_{FA} = 0.285 \text{ L/kg} \times \frac{0.264172 \text{ gal}}{1 \text{ L}} \times \frac{1 \text{ kg}}{0.00110231 \text{ short tons}}$$

$$Y_{FA} = 0.285 \times 239.66 \text{ GPT}$$

$$Y_{FA} = 68.3 \text{ GPT} \; (\text{gallons per short ton})$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> Since 68.3 GPT $\gg$ 25 GPT (the commercial threshold), this oil shale is **economically viable** for retorting.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Kerogen Pyrolysis Stoichiometric Model

A simplified lumped kinetic model for kerogen → oil + gas + char is:

$$\text{Kerogen} \xrightarrow{k_1, \Delta H_1} \text{Oil} \xrightarrow{k_2, \Delta H_2} \text{Gas} + \text{Char}$$

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius rate expression for kerogen conversion**

$$-\frac{dC_K}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C_K = A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1 / RT} \cdot C_K$$

where:
- $C_K$ = kerogen concentration (kg/m³)
- $A_1$ = pre-exponential factor (1/s)
- $E_1$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Integrate assuming isothermal conditions**

$$\int_{C_{K,0}}^{C_K} \frac{dC_K}{C_K} = -\int_0^t A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1/RT} \, dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_K}{C_{K,0}}\right) = -A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1/RT} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Express fractional conversion**

$$X_K = 1 - \frac{C_K}{C_{K,0}} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1/RT} \cdot t\right)$$

**Step 4 — Plug in typical Green River oil shale kinetic parameters**

- $A_1 = 1.67 \times 10^{13}$ s⁻¹
- $E_1/R = 21{,}830$ K (i.e., $E_1 = 181.4$ kJ/mol)
- $T = 773$ K (500 °C)
- $t = 2400$ s (40 min)

$$A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1/RT} = 1.67 \times 10^{13} \cdot e^{-21830/773}$$

$$e^{-21830/773} = e^{-28.24} = 5.36 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$A_1 \cdot e^{-E_1/RT} = 1.67 \times 10^{13} \times 5.36 \times 10^{-13} = 8.95 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$X_K = 1 - e^{-8.95 \times 2400}$$

$$X_K = 1 - e^{-21{,}480} \approx 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

> [!NOTE]
> After 40 minutes at 500 °C, kerogen conversion is essentially **complete ($X_K \approx 1$)**, validating the Fischer Assay protocol.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Heat Requirement for Retorting

**Step 1 — Mass of shale to be processed**

$$m_{shale} = 500 \text{ tonnes/day} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat required (raising from 25 °C to 500 °C)**

$$Q_{sensible} = m_{shale} \cdot c_{p,shale} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 1.0 \times (500 - 25) \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 475 = 2.375 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ/day}$$

*Logic:* $c_{p,shale} \approx 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K) is the typical specific heat of oil shale.

**Step 3 — Latent heat of kerogen decomposition**

$$Q_{kerogen} = m_{shale} \cdot TOC_{wt\%} \cdot \Delta H_{pyr}$$

$$Q_{kerogen} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 0.15 \times 250 \text{ kJ/kg of kerogen}$$

$$Q_{kerogen} = 1.875 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat duty**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{kerogen} = 2.375 \times 10^8 + 1.875 \times 10^7$$

$$Q_{total} = 2.5625 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to power (kW)**

$$P = \frac{Q_{total}}{t_{op}} = \frac{2.5625 \times 10^8}{24 \times 3600} \text{ kW}$$

$$P = \frac{2.5625 \times 10^8}{86400} = 2966 \text{ kW} \approx 3.0 \text{ MW}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Oil Shale Deposits

| Deposit | Country | Kerogen Type | Avg. Yield (L/ton) | TOC (wt%) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Green River | USA | Type I | 100 – 150 | 10 – 25 | Largest, active R&D |
| Irati | Brazil | Type II | 40 – 80 | 6 – 14 | Petrobras operated |
| Fushun | China | Type I/II | 50 – 70 | 5 – 12 | Industrial-scale retorting since 1929 |
| Kukersite | Estonia | Type I | 150 – 200 | 25 – 40 | World's largest industrial use (historical) |
| Stuart | Australia | Type I/III | 60 – 110 | 8 – 18 | QER & Santos projects |
| El-Lajjun | Jordan | Type I | 60 – 100 | 10 – 20 | Demonstrated surface retorting |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Ex-Situ Shale Oil Production (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mined Oil Shale] --> B[Primary Crusher]
    B --> C[Secondary Crusher to minus 8 mesh]
    C --> D[Retort Feeder Bin]
    D --> E[Retort Reactor at 500 deg C inert atmosphere]
    E --> F[Hot Vapour Stream]
    E --> G[Solid Spent Shale to Ash Handling]
    F --> H[Cyclone Separator for Dust]
    H --> I[Condenser at 0 deg C]
    I --> J[Crude Shale Oil Receiver]
    I --> K[Non Condensable Gas Recycle]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Hydrotreating and Upgrading Unit]
    L --> M[Refined Synthetic Crude]
    G --> N[Spent Shale Disposal or Cement Use]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Mining delivers raw shale to a crusher circuit, which sizes the rock for the retort.
- The retort is the **heart of the process** — kerogen decomposes here.
- The condenser separates **liquid crude** from **non-condensable gas** (which is recycled to provide process heat).
- Spent shale is a solid waste that must be safely disposed or repurposed (e.g., cement industry).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: In-Situ Conversion Process (ICP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surface_Facilities[Surface Facilities]
        P1[Power Supply Unit]
        P2[Control and Monitoring]
        P3[Product Recovery Train]
    end
    P1 --> H1
    P2 --> H1
    subgraph Subsurface[Subsurface Formation]
        H1[Electric Heater Wells]
        FW[Freeze Wall for Groundwater Isolation]
        RZ[Heated Kerogen Zone 340 deg C]
        FW --- RZ
        H1 --> RZ
        RZ --> OF[Oil and Gas Flow to Producer Wells]
        OF --> P3
    end
    P3 --> REF[Hydrocarbon Upgrading to Synthetic Crude]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Electric heaters raise the formation temperature over **2 – 4 years**.
- A **freeze wall** (refrigerated groundwater barrier) prevents groundwater contamination.
- The heated kerogen zone converts slowly; products migrate to **producer wells**.
- Surface facilities upgrade the recovered fluids into **synthetic crude**.

## 4.3 Kerogen Maturity & Resource Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Matter Deposition] --> B[Kerogen Formation Diagenesis]
    B --> C{Maturation Depth and Temperature}
    C -->|Low T, Shallow Burial| D[Immature Oil Shale Kerogen Solid]
    C -->|Moderate T 60 to 120 deg C| E[Catagenesis Conventional Crude Forms]
    C -->|High T above 150 deg C| F[Metagenesis Dry Gas Zone]
    D --> G[Surface Retorting Pyrolysis]
    D --> H[In Situ Heating]
    G --> I[Shale Oil Product]
    H --> I
    E --> J[Conventional Oil Reservoir]
    F --> K[Natural Gas Reservoir]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define oil shale and differentiate it from shale oil. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Model Answer:**
**Oil shale** is a fine-grained sedimentary rock that contains significant amounts of **kerogen** (immature solid organic matter) dispersed within its mineral matrix. It is the **source rock** itself, from which no oil flows naturally.
**Shale oil**, on the other hand, is the **liquid hydrocarbon** product obtained when oil shale is heated (retorted) to decompose its kerogen. In short: **oil shale is the rock; shale oil is the liquid extracted from it.**

### Question 2: What is the Fischer Assay, and what is the commercial cut-off yield? [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Model Answer:**
The **Fischer Assay** is a standardized laboratory test (modified ASTM D3904) used to quantify the oil yield of an oil shale sample. A 100 g sample is heated to 500 °C at 12 °C/min in an inert atmosphere, and the condensed oil is measured. The result is reported in **gallons per short ton (GPT)** or **litres per tonne (L/t)**. The widely accepted **commercial cut-off yield is 25 GPT (≈ 100 L/ton)**; deposits below this are generally uneconomical with current technology.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students are given an internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **ex-situ retorting process** for shale oil production. List its major unit operations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Mining and Sizing:** Oil shale is mined by open-pit or underground methods, then crushed to ≤ 8 mesh (~ 2.4 mm).
2. **Feeding:** Crushed shale is fed continuously into the retort.
3. **Retorting (Pyrolysis):** The shale is heated to 450 °C – 540 °C in an oxygen-free atmosphere. Kerogen decomposes into:
   - Crude shale oil (vapour)
   - Non-condensable gas
   - Spent shale (solid residue)
4. **Solid–Vapour Separation:** Cyclones remove entrained dust.
5. **Condensation:** Vapours are cooled to ~ 0 °C; crude shale oil condenses.
6. **Gas Recycle:** Non-condensable gas is partially recycled to provide process heat.
7. **Spent Shale Disposal:** Disposed of or used in cement manufacturing.
8. **Upgrading:** Crude shale oil is hydrotreated to remove N, S, and O to yield synthetic crude.

> Refer to the **Mermaid process flow diagram in Section 4.1** for the visual layout.

**(b)** Discuss the **types of kerogen** in oil shales and their influence on shale oil yield and quality. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

- **Type I (Algal/Lacustrine):** High H/C ratio (~ 1.5+); rich in aliphatic chains. **Highest oil yield** (e.g., Green River — 100–150 L/ton).
- **Type II (Marine):** Moderate H/C ratio (~ 1.0 – 1.5); mix of aliphatic and aromatic. **Good oil + gas yield** (e.g., Irati — 40–80 L/ton).
- **Type III (Terrestrial/Humic):** Low H/C ratio (< 1.0); aromatic-rich. **Mainly gas-prone** — poor for shale oil.
- **Type IV (Inert):** Oxidized, residual carbon; **negligible yield**.

**Influence on Yield:** Type I shales produce the largest volumes of high-quality (low-sulfur, paraffinic) crude. Type III shales generate mostly light gases upon pyrolysis, with very little liquid yield.

**Influence on Quality:** Type I-derived shale oil is light, low in sulfur, and easier to upgrade. Type II yields moderately heavy crude requiring more upgrading. Type III produces waxy, aromatic, low-API oil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **in-situ retorting process** for shale oil extraction. Compare it with the ex-situ method. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**In-Situ Retorting:**
- Oil shale is heated **underground** without mining.
- **Heating Methods:**
  - *Shell ICP*: Electric resistance heaters in boreholes, slow heating over 2–4 years to ~ 340 °C.
  - *ExxonMobil Electrofrac™*: Combines hydraulic fracturing with embedded electrical heaters.
  - *Chevron CRUSH*: Uses radiofrequency (RF) heating.
- **Containment:** A **freeze wall** of refrigerated groundwater isolates the heated zone, preventing aquifer contamination.
- **Product Recovery:** Pyrolysis products (oil + gas) migrate to producer wells and are pumped to the surface for upgrading.

**Comparison with Ex-Situ:**

| Parameter | Ex-Situ | In-Situ |
|---|---|---|
| Mining | Required | Not required |
| Surface footprint | Large | Small |
| Capital cost | Lower | Very high |
| Operational cost | Higher (mining, crushing) | Lower (no mining) |
| Environmental impact | High (spent shale, dust) | Lower (no spent shale handling) |
| Applicability | Shallow deposits | Deep, thick deposits |
| Time to first oil | Months | Years (2–4) |

**(b)** A 100 g oil shale sample yields 32 mL of oil in a Fischer Assay. Determine the yield in **GPT** and **L/ton**, and comment on the **commercial viability** of the deposit. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m_{shale} = 100$ g = 0.1 kg; $V_{oil} = 32$ mL = 0.032 L

**Step 1 — Yield in L/ton:**

$$Y_{FA} = \frac{V_{oil}}{m_{shale}} = \frac{0.032}{0.1} = 0.32 \text{ L/kg}$$

$$Y_{FA} = 0.32 \text{ L/kg} \times 1000 \text{ kg/ton} = 320 \text{ L/ton}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to GPT:**

$$Y_{FA} = 320 \text{ L/ton} \times \frac{0.264172 \text{ gal}}{1 \text{ L}} \times \frac{1 \text{ ton (metric)}}{1.10231 \text{ short ton}}$$

$$Y_{FA} = 320 \times 0.2397 = 76.7 \text{ GPT}$$

**Step 3 — Commercial Viability Comment:**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Y_{FA} = 76.7$ GPT $\gg$ 25 GPT (commercial threshold), the deposit is **economically viable** for retorting. It falls in the **high-grade** category, comparable to premium Green River shales.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing oil shale with shale gas / tight oil.** Examiners frequently test this. Always state explicitly: *oil shale = solid rock with kerogen; shale oil = the liquid product obtained after retorting.*
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions.** Fischer Assay yields are commonly expressed in **GPT** (gallons per short ton) in older literature, but KTU expects familiarity with both **L/ton** and **GPT**. Always show the conversion factor explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the type of kerogen** when discussing yield. Many students answer the kerogen question by listing the types but forget to **link H/C ratio → yield** explicitly. Examiners reward this connection.
> 4. **Drawing retort diagrams without arrows showing material flow.** A sketch without flow direction loses at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the environment.** Any modern 14-mark question on shale oil **must** include a line on spent shale disposal, groundwater contamination, or CO₂ footprint. Skipping it = 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Oil shale** is a fine-grained sedimentary rock containing **kerogen**; **shale oil** is the liquid obtained by retorting it.
- [x] Kerogen is **immature organic matter** that has not yet been naturally converted to crude oil.
- [x] **Type I kerogen** (algal) gives the **highest oil yield**; **Type IV** gives essentially none.
- [x] **Fischer Assay** is the standard lab test (100 g sample, 12 °C/min to 500 °C, 40 min hold, inert atmosphere).
- [x] **Commercial cut-off yield** is ~ **25 GPT** (≈ 100 L/ton).
- [x] **Ex-situ retorting** = mine → crush → heat in surface retort.
- [x] **In-situ retorting** = heat underground (Shell ICP, Electrofrac, CRUSH).
- [x] Retorting temperature range: **450 °C – 540 °C**, in **absence of oxygen**.
- [x] Major global deposits: **Green River (USA), Irati (Brazil), Fushun (China), Kukersite (Estonia), Stuart (Australia).**
- [x] Shale oil has energy content of **~ 38 – 42 MJ/kg**, similar to conventional crude.
- [x] **Conversion factor for yield:** 1 L/ton $\approx$ 0.2397 GPT.
- [x] **H/C atomic ratio** of kerogen is the strongest predictor of oil yield — higher H/C → more oil.
- [x] **Spent shale** disposal and **groundwater contamination** are key environmental concerns.
- [x] Kerogen-to-oil conversion follows **first-order Arrhenius kinetics**; conversion is essentially complete at 500 °C in 40 min.
- [x] Always distinguish from **tight oil** (conventional oil in shale, extracted via fracking) — a frequent KTU trick question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reservoir Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reservoir Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Reservoir Properties?

**Reservoir Properties** refer to the fundamental physical, petrophysical, and fluid-interaction characteristics of subsurface porous rock formations (typically sedimentary rocks like sandstone and carbonate) that govern their capacity to store and transmit hydrocarbon fluids (oil, natural gas, and water). In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these properties form the analytical bedrock of petroleum engineering calculations, drilling decisions, and reservoir management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Reservoir properties such as porosity, permeability, fluid saturation, wettability, capillary pressure, and rock compressibility collectively define the **Storage Capacity** and **Flow Capacity** of a hydrocarbon reservoir.

### 1.2 The Two Master Properties

Every reservoir rock must satisfy two fundamental engineering criteria:

1. **Storage Capacity (Porosity, $\phi$)** — The void space available to hold hydrocarbons.
2. **Flow Capacity (Permeability, $k$)** — The interconnectedness of those voids allowing fluid movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge Model":**
> Imagine a common kitchen sponge. The total empty space inside the sponge represents **Porosity** (how much water it can hold). However, if we plug half the holes with wax, the sponge can still hold the same volume of water (porosity unchanged) but the water flows out much more slowly. This represents **Permeability** — the ease with which fluids traverse the interconnected pores. A reservoir rock engineer must evaluate *both* properties independently; a rock can be highly porous yet practically impermeable (e.g., shale), or moderately porous with excellent permeability (e.g., clean sandstone).

### 1.3 Classification of Reservoir Properties

| Property Type | Examples | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrophysical** | Porosity, Permeability, Grain Density | Static reserves estimation |
| **Fluid Saturation** | $S_o$, $S_g$, $S_w$ | Volumetric in-place calculations |
| **Surface / Interfacial** | Wettability, Capillary Pressure, IFT | Recovery factor and displacement efficiency |
| **Mechanical** | Rock Compressibility, Formation Resistivity Factor | Compaction drive, log interpretation |

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - Standard laboratory conditions: **$T = 15.6^\circ C$ (60°F)** and **$P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ (14.7 psia)**.
> - Water density at standard conditions: **$\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (62.4 lb/ft³)**.
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Porosity ($\phi$)

**Porosity** is defined as the ratio of the pore volume (void space) to the total bulk volume of the rock.

$$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_b} = \frac{V_b - V_s}{V_b}$$

where:
- $V_p$ = Pore volume
- $V_b$ = Bulk volume of rock
- $V_s$ = Volume of solid (rock matrix)

#### 2.1.1 Types of Porosity

- **Absolute (Total) Porosity ($\phi_a$):** Includes all pore spaces, whether connected or isolated.
- **Effective Porosity ($\phi_e$):** Includes only the *interconnected* pores that contribute to fluid storage and flow. **This is the parameter used in all KTU engineering calculations.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Effective Porosity Matters:** Isolated pores cannot hold movable hydrocarbons nor allow fluid flow. Hence, reservoir volumetrics always use $\phi_e$.

#### 2.1.2 Origin-Based Classification

- **Primary Porosity:** Formed during sedimentary deposition (interparticle, intraparticle).
- **Secondary Porosity:** Formed after deposition through geological processes (fractures, vugs, dissolution).

### 2.2 Permeability ($k$)

**Permeability** is the ability of a porous medium to transmit fluids, governed empirically by **Darcy's Law** (1856).

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

For a horizontal linear system, this rearranges to:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (cm³/s)
- $k$ = Permeability (Darcy, D)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (cm²)
- $\mu$ = Fluid dynamic viscosity (cP)
- $L$ = Length of porous medium (cm)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop (atm)
- $1 \text{ Darcy} = 1000 \text{ mD} = 0.987 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$

### 2.3 Fluid Saturations ($S_o$, $S_g$, $S_w$)

Saturation of a fluid is the fraction of pore volume occupied by that fluid.

$$S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$$

- $S_w$ = Water saturation
- $S_o$ = Oil saturation
- $S_g$ = Gas saturation

**Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV):**

$$\text{HCPV} = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

### 2.4 Wettability

**Wettability** is the preferential tendency of one fluid to spread on or adhere to a rock surface in the presence of another immiscible fluid.

- **Water-Wet Rock:** Water coats the grain surfaces; oil resides in the pore centers.
- **Oil-Wet Rock:** Oil coats the grain surfaces.
- **Intermediate / Fractional Wettability:** Mixed behavior across pores.

### 2.5 Capillary Pressure ($P_c$)

The pressure difference across the curved interface between two immiscible fluids in a capillary tube is given by the **Young-Laplace Equation**:

$$P_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Interfacial tension (dynes/cm or N/m)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle (measured through the wetting phase)
- $r$ = Pore throat radius (cm)

### 2.6 Rock Compressibility ($c_f$)

$$c_f = \frac{1}{V_b}\left(\frac{\partial V_b}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Typical values range from **$3 \times 10^{-6}$ to $25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ psi}^{-1}$** for consolidated sandstones and carbonates.

### 2.7 Formation Resistivity Factor ($F_R$)

$$F_R = \frac{R_o}{R_w} = \frac{a}{\phi^m}$$

where:
- $R_o$ = Resistivity of rock fully saturated with formation water
- $R_w$ = Resistivity of formation water
- $a$ = Tortuosity constant (≈ 1 for clean sands, 0.62 for carbonates)
- $m$ = Cementation exponent (≈ 2 for sandstone, 1.8–2.0 for carbonates)

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = V_p / V_b$ | Porosity definition | Dimensionless (fraction or %) |
| 2 | $k = Q \mu L / (A \Delta P)$ | Darcy's Law for linear flow | Darcy (D) or millidarcy (mD) |
| 3 | $S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$ | Saturation balance | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{HCPV} = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w)$ | Hydrocarbon pore volume | m³ or ft³ |
| 5 | $P_c = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r$ | Young-Laplace capillary equation | dynes/cm² or Pa |
| 6 | $c_f = (1/V_b)(\partial V_b / \partial P)$ | Rock compressibility | psi⁻¹ or Pa⁻¹ |
| 7 | $F_R = a / \phi^m$ | Archie's Formation Resistivity Factor | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_b = A \cdot h$ | Bulk volume from area × thickness | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These properties are used in **well-log interpretation (Archie equation)**, **reserves estimation (volumetric method)**, **material balance calculations**, **enhanced oil recovery (EOR) screening**, and **production forecasting**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Darcy's Law from First Principles

The empirical Darcy equation, though originally observed, can be dimensionally derived from the continuity of fluid motion through a porous body.

Consider a horizontal cylindrical core of length $L$ and cross-section $A$, fully saturated with a single-phase Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu$. Let a steady-state flow rate $Q$ be established under an applied pressure differential $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.

By definition, the superficial velocity is:

$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A}$$

The fluid experiences viscous resistance through the tortuous pore network. The average hydraulic velocity inside the actual pore channels is:

$$v_{actual} = \frac{v_s}{\phi}$$

Applying the Hagen-Poiseuille analogy for flow through a bundle of capillary tubes of equivalent radius $r$, the pressure drop per unit length is:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \mu v_{actual}}{r^2}$$

Substituting $v_{actual} = v_s / \phi$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \mu v_s}{\phi \cdot r^2}$$

Solving for the superficial velocity $v_s = Q/A$:

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\phi \cdot r^2}{8 \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Comparing this to the standard Darcy form $Q = (k A \Delta P) / (\mu L)$:

$$k = \frac{\phi \cdot r^2}{8}$$

This shows that **permeability is proportional to the square of the pore radius**, which explains why small changes in pore throat size (e.g., due to compaction or cementation) drastically alter permeability.

### 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Pressure from Young-Laplace

For a fluid interface spanning a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$, mechanical equilibrium requires the net pressure difference across the curved interface to balance the surface tension force.

Force balance on the meniscus:

$$P_c \cdot (\pi r^2) = \sigma \cdot (2 \pi r) \cdot \cos\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi r^2$:

$$P_c = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

This is the classical **capillary pressure equation**, fundamental to understanding fluid distribution in reservoir rocks and saturation-height functions.

### 3.3 Numerical Example 1 — Porosity Calculation

**Problem:** A cylindrical core sample has a length of 7 cm and a diameter of 2.5 cm. After oven drying, its mass is 152.4 g. When fully saturated with a brine of density $1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$, its mass becomes 165.9 g. The grain density of the rock is $2.65 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the porosity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bulk Volume:**
$$V_b = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 \cdot L = \pi \left(\frac{2.5}{2}\right)^2 \cdot 7$$

$$V_b = \pi \cdot 1.5625 \cdot 7 = 34.36 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Pore Volume (from saturation method):**
Mass of brine absorbed:
$$\Delta m = 165.9 - 152.4 = 13.5 \text{ g}$$

Volume of brine (which equals pore volume):
$$V_p = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho_{brine}} = \frac{13.5}{1.05} = 12.857 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Porosity:**
$$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_b} = \frac{12.857}{34.36} = 0.3743$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 37.43\%}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Example 2 — Permeability via Darcy's Law

**Problem:** A linear core flood experiment is conducted with the following parameters:

- Length $L = 10 \text{ cm}$
- Cross-sectional area $A = 5 \text{ cm}^2$
- Pressure drop $\Delta P = 2 \text{ atm}$
- Flow rate $Q = 3 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$
- Fluid viscosity $\mu = 1.2 \text{ cP}$

Calculate the permeability of the core in Darcy and in mD.

**Solution:**

Using Darcy's Law:
$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

Substituting values:
$$k = \frac{(3) \cdot (1.2) \cdot (10)}{(5) \cdot (2)}$$

$$k = \frac{36}{10} = 3.6 \text{ D}$$

Converting to millidarcy:
$$\boxed{k = 3600 \text{ mD}}$$

This value classifies the rock as a **highly permeable reservoir**, consistent with clean, well-sorted sandstone.

### 3.5 Numerical Example 3 — Volumetric Hydrocarbon Pore Volume

**Problem:** A reservoir block has the following properties:

- Bulk volume $V_b = 1 \times 10^9 \text{ ft}^3$
- Porosity $\phi = 0.22$
- Water saturation $S_w = 0.30$

Calculate the hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV).

**Solution:**

$$\text{HCPV} = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

$$\text{HCPV} = (1 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.22) \cdot (1 - 0.30)$$

$$\text{HCPV} = (1 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.22) \cdot (0.70)$$

$$\boxed{\text{HCPV} = 1.54 \times 10^8 \text{ ft}^3}$$

This represents the *productive void space* available for oil and gas storage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reservoir Rock Conceptual Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reservoir Rock Sample] --> B[Bulk Volume Vb]
    B --> C[Solid Matrix Volume Vs]
    B --> D[Pore Volume Vp]
    D --> E[Connected Pores Effective Porosity phi_e]
    D --> F[Isolated Pores Dead Pores]
    E --> G[Fluid Storage Capacity]
    E --> H[Fluid Flow Capacity]
    H --> I{Permeability k}
    G --> J{Hydrocarbon Saturation}
    J --> K[Oil So]
    J --> L[Gas Sg]
    J --> M[Water Sw]
    I --> N[Darcy Flow]
    I --> O[Non-Darcy Flow High Velocity]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Reservoir Property Interrelationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Porosity phi] --> R1[Storage Capacity]
    K[Permeability k] --> R2[Flow Capacity]
    R1 --> V[Volumetric Reserves]
    R2 --> V
    PC[Capillary Pressure Pc] --> S[Saturation Profile]
    S --> V
    W[Wettability] --> PC
    W --> RP[Relative Permeability]
    RP --> R2
    CF[Rock Compressibility cf] --> MB[Material Balance]
    FR[Formation Resistivity FR] --> LOG[Well Log Interpretation]
    LOG --> P
    LOG --> S
```

### 4.3 Capillary Pressure Curve and Saturation Distribution

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Capillary Pressure Pc] --> B[Pc Curve Plot]
    B --> C[Threshold Pressure Entry]
    B --> D[Irreducible Water Saturation Swi]
    B --> E[Transition Zone Height]
    C --> F[Defines Minimum Reservoir Pressure]
    D --> G[Hydrocarbon Storage Limit]
    E --> H[Reservoir Quality Indicator]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Two-Phase Flow System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Inlet Face High Pressure P1] --> CORE[Porous Core Wetting and Non-Wetting Phases]
    CORE --> SAT[Saturation Distribution Sw and Snw]
    SAT --> KRW[Relative Permeability Curves krw and krn]
    KRW --> OUT[Outlet Face Low Pressure P2]
    OUT --> PROD[Production Rate Q]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **absolute porosity** and **effective porosity**. State which one is used in reservoir engineering calculations and why.

**Model Answer:**
Absolute porosity ($\phi_a$) is the ratio of the *total* pore volume (including isolated, dead-end pores) to the bulk volume of the rock. Effective porosity ($\phi_e$) is the ratio of only the *interconnected* pore volume to the bulk volume.

$$\phi_a = \frac{V_{p,total}}{V_b}, \quad \phi_e = \frac{V_{p,connected}}{V_b}$$

**Reservoir engineering uses effective porosity** because only interconnected pores can store *recoverable* hydrocarbons and permit fluid flow under a pressure gradient. Isolated pores, regardless of how voluminous, do not contribute to production. Therefore, $\phi_e$ governs the **storage capacity** and **flow capacity** of the reservoir.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** Define **permeability**. State Darcy's Law and explain the physical significance of the proportionality constant.

**Model Answer:**
Permeability is the ability of a porous medium to transmit fluids through its interconnected pore network. It is governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$$

The proportionality constant $k$ (permeability) has units of **Darcy (D)** or **millidarcy (mD)**, where $1 \text{ D} = 1000 \text{ mD}$. It is a **rock property only** (independent of the fluid), depending solely on pore geometry — pore size, shape, connectivity, and tortuosity. A higher $k$ indicates greater ease of fluid flow.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Derive Darcy's Law for linear horizontal flow through a porous medium. Define all variables and state their units. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained from a linear core flood experiment:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Core length | 12 cm |
| Core diameter | 3 cm |
| Flow rate | 4 cm³/s |
| Pressure drop | 2.5 atm |
| Fluid viscosity | 1.0 cP |

Calculate the permeability of the core in Darcy and mD. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Darcy's Law:**
Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Starting from the continuity equation and the Hagen-Poiseuille analogy, the linear superficial velocity through a porous medium is given as:

$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A} = -\frac{k}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

For a finite length $L$:

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (cm³/s)
- $k$ = Permeability (Darcy)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (cm²)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure differential (atm)
- $\mu$ = Fluid viscosity (cP)
- $L$ = Length (cm)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**
$$A = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 = 7.069 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law:**
$$k = \frac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$$

$$k = \frac{(4)(1.0)(12)}{(7.069)(2.5)} = \frac{48}{17.67}$$

$$\boxed{k = 2.716 \text{ D} = 2716 \text{ mD}}$$

The rock is classified as a **highly permeable reservoir**.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Define **capillary pressure**. Derive the Young-Laplace equation for capillary rise in a cylindrical tube. Explain how wettability affects the contact angle and the sign of capillary pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reservoir rock sample has a bulk volume of 50 cm³ and a pore volume of 10 cm³. The water, oil, and gas saturations are $S_w = 0.35$, $S_o = 0.45$, and $S_g = 0.20$ respectively. Calculate: (i) the porosity, and (ii) the hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Capillary Pressure Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.2. The pressure difference across a curved fluid interface in a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$ is balanced by surface tension:

$$P_c = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

- For a **strongly water-wet** rock, $\theta \approx 0°$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, giving **$P_c > 0$** (water is the wetting phase).
- For a **strongly oil-wet** rock, $\theta \approx 180°$, $\cos\theta \approx -1$, giving **$P_c < 0$** (sign reversal).
- For **neutral wettability**, $\theta \approx 90°$, $\cos\theta = 0$, and $P_c = 0$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Porosity:**
$$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_b} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20 \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{20\%}$$

**(ii) HCPV:**
$$\text{HCPV} = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

$$\text{HCPV} = (50)(0.20)(1 - 0.35)$$

$$\text{HCPV} = (50)(0.20)(0.65)$$

$$\boxed{\text{HCPV} = 6.5 \text{ cm}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign of Capillary Pressure:** Students often forget that $P_c$ is conventionally defined as **$P_c = P_{nw} - P_w$** (non-wetting minus wetting pressure). Get the sign wrong and you lose 2 marks.
> - **Saturation Balance:** Always verify $S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$. If not equal to 1, your arithmetic is wrong — recheck.
> - **Unit Consistency in Darcy's Law:** Use **CGS units** (cm, atm, cP, cm³/s) for direct Darcy output. Mixing SI and CGS yields incorrect permeability values.
> - **Porosity vs. Permeability Confusion:** Porosity is dimensionless; permeability is in Darcies. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for unit errors.
> - **HCPV vs. Bulk Volume:** HCPV requires multiplication by $\phi$ *and* $(1 - S_w)$. Forgetting either factor is the single most common error in volumetric questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reservoir Properties (Module 1):**

- **Porosity ($\phi$):** Fraction of void space in rock. Use **effective porosity** $\phi_e$ for engineering calculations. Typical range: **5% to 35%**.

- **Permeability ($k$):** Ability of rock to transmit fluids, governed by **Darcy's Law**: $k = Q\mu L / (A \Delta P)$. Units: **Darcy (D)** or **millidarcy (mD)**. Typical range: **0.1 mD to 1000+ mD**.

- **Saturations:** Always sum to unity: **$S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$**. Hydrocarbon content relates to **HCPV** $= V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$.

- **Wettability:** Controls **contact angle $\theta$** and the sign of capillary pressure. **Strongly water-wet** ($\theta \approx 0°$) reservoirs are most common.

- **Capillary Pressure ($P_c$):** Governs **fluid distribution** and **transition zone height** in a reservoir. $P_c = 2\sigma\cos\theta / r$. Higher $P_c$ in tighter (smaller $r$) pores.

- **Rock Compressibility ($c_f$):** Reflects volumetric change of rock under pore pressure depletion. Critical for **material balance** and **subsidence** analysis.

- **Formation Resistivity Factor ($F_R$):** Archie equation $F_R = a/\phi^m$ links porosity to electrical resistivity — fundamental for **wireline log interpretation**.

- **Cross-Property Relationships:** Porosity and permeability are correlated but **not linearly**. Tight, low-porosity rocks usually have disproportionately low permeability.

- **Engineering Golden Rule:** Always distinguish between **rock properties** ($\phi$, $k$, $c_f$, $F_R$) — independent of the fluid — and **fluid properties** ($\mu$, $\rho$, $B_o$) — dependent on pressure and temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrocarbon Migration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrocarbon Migration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrocarbon Migration** is the collective post-generative geodynamic process by which hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas), once generated from kerogen within a mature source rock, are expelled and transported through the subsurface to a position of accumulation (reservoir) — or are lost to the surface — under the influence of pressure gradients, buoyancy, capillary forces, and hydraulic gradients. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, hydrocarbon migration is sub-divided into three sequential stages: | Stage | Domain | Process | |---|---|---| | **Primary Migration** | Source rock → Carrier bed | Expulsion of hydrocarbons from fine-grained, low-permeability source rock into a more permeable carrier system | | **Secondary Migration** | Carrier bed → Trap | Lateral and upward buoyancy-driven movement of hydrocarbons through permeable carrier beds and reservoir rocks until trapped by a seal | | **Tertiary Migration** | Trap → Leak / Re-accumulation | Re-mobilization of an already-formed accumulation due to tectonic/pressure disturbances, leading to leakage or a new trap | The **standard subsurface density of water** used in buoyancy calculations is $\rho_w \approx \mathbf{1.0 \ g/cm^3}$ (or **1000 kg/m³**), and the **density contrast** $\Delta\rho$ is the critical parameter that drives the secondary migration column upward. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap-Bubble-in-Bathwater" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine filling a bathtub with cold water and then releasing a single drop of cooking oil into it. The oil, being lighter, immediately shoots upward to the surface. Now imagine the bathtub is the Earth's crust, the water is formation water filling porous rock, and the oil is a hydrocarbon droplet. The droplet cannot stay trapped in a fine clay (the source rock), so it **squeezes out** (primary migration), then **rises** through sand (the carrier bed) by buoyancy, and finally **stops** when it hits an impermeable cap (the seal/trap) — exactly like the oil droplet coming to rest on the bath's water surface. **Why this matters to a petroleum engineer:** No migration = no reservoir. Migration is the *geological conveyor belt* that physically connects a kitchen (source rock) to a warehouse (reservoir). Without efficient migration, even a world-class source rock can produce nothing of commercial value. ## 1.3 The Petroleum System Context (KTU Board Favourite) For migration to be *successful*, the following five geological elements must coexist — this is the classical **Petroleum System** (Magoon & Dow, 1994 — KTU-approved nomenclature): 1. **Source Rock** — Organic-rich, fine-grained (e.g., shale, mudstone), mature. 2. **Reservoir Rock** — Porous, permeable (e.g., sandstone, carbonate). 3. **Seal / Cap Rock** — Impermeable (e.g., shale, evaporite, salt). 4. **Trap** — Geometric closure (structural or stratigraphic). 5. **Migration Pathway** — Carrier bed, fault, fracture, or unconformity. The standard **migration efficiency** in the petroleum industry is typically **less than 10%** (most generated hydrocarbons are lost to leakage, adsorption, or re-migration). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Primary Migration — Mechanisms in Detail Primary migration occurs across the **nanometer to micrometer-scale** pore throats of source rocks. Because shale permeabilities are typically in the range of $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$ Darcy, *Darcian bulk flow is insufficient*. The accepted KTU/industry mechanisms are: > [!NOTE] > **Accepted Mechanisms of Primary Migration:** > 1. **Pressure-driven microfracturing (dilation):** Continuous hydrocarbon generation raises internal pore pressure until it exceeds the minimum in-situ stress + tensile strength of the rock, hydrofracturing the source rock and expelling hydrocarbons as a continuous phase. This is the most widely accepted mechanism (du Rouchet, 1981). > 2. **Solution / Molecular Diffusion:** Light hydrocarbons (C1–C5) can dissolve in formation water and diffuse along concentration gradients. Critical for gas, less so for oil. > 3. **Desorption from Kerogen Network:** Continuous oil-wet films adsorbed on kerogen can coalesce and become mobile once a critical saturation is reached. > 4. **Osmosis and Squeegee Flow:** Compaction of fine-grained rocks expels pore fluids (and dissolved/suspended hydrocarbons) due to overburden pressure. ## 2.2 Secondary Migration — The Dominant Engine of Accumulation Secondary migration is governed by a force balance. The hydrocarbon phase moves as a continuous fluid through water-saturated permeable rock. ### 2.2.1 Driving Forces (act to move HC upward and laterally) - **Buoyancy Force ($F_b$):** Density contrast between hydrocarbon ($\rho_{hc}$) and formation water ($\rho_w$). Always directed upward. $$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$$ - **Hydraulic Pressure Gradient:** Compaction, tectonics, or meteoric water influx can impose a lateral hydrodynamic gradient on the formation water, dragging hydrocarbons along. ### 2.2.2 Resistive Forces (oppose migration) - **Capillary Pressure ($P_c$):** The water-wet rock matrix resists the entry of the non-wetting hydrocarbon phase into small throats. Threshold pressure must be exceeded. $$P_c = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$ where $\gamma$ is interfacial tension (N/m), $\theta$ is the contact angle, and $r$ is the pore-throat radius (m). - **Frictional / Viscous Drag** (during Darcy flow through narrow throats). ### 2.2.3 Migration Pathway Geometry Migration tends to follow the **path of least capillary resistance** — the largest connected pore network.
