This is why migration pathways are *vertical-to-inclined* through high-permeability streaks and not random. ## 2.3 Tertiary Migration Triggered by: - Seal failure (overpressure, fault reactivation) - Tilting of the trap (geomorphic/spill-point changes) - Biodegradation, de-asphaltening, or gas injection from deeper sources ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Key Variable | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_b = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Buoyancy force on HC blob | $\Delta\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m³ | | 2 | $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary entry pressure | $P_c$ in Pa, $r$ in m | | 3 | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho} \cdot (\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$ | Critical HC column height to enter pore throat | $h$ in m | | 4 | $Q = -\dfrac{kA}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | **Darcy's Law** (volumetric flow rate during migration) | $k$ in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s | | 5 | $v = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Darcy velocity of HC phase | m/s | | 6 | $t = \dfrac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 k \Delta P}$ | Characteristic migration time (Welte-Duddy estimate) | seconds | | 7 | $z_{max} = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r g (\rho_w - \rho_{hc})}$ | Maximum capillary seal height (cap-rock column) | m | | 8 | $\Delta\rho = \rho_w - \rho_{hc}$ | Density contrast (driving buoyancy) | kg/m³ | | 9 | $S_{hc}^{crit} \approx 0.10 - 0.20$ | Critical HC saturation to begin bulk flow | fraction (dimensionless) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip (KTU):** Always state the standard values — for crude oil $\rho_{hc} \approx 0.7\text{–}0.9 \ g/cm^3$ and for natural gas $\rho_{hc} \approx 0.0007\text{–}0.3 \ g/cm^3$ (pressure dependent). This gives $\Delta\rho$ of **0.1–1.0 g/cm³**, the parameter examiners love to plug into buoyancy equations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The same physics is exploited in: - **CO₂ sequestration** — predicting migration and trapping in saline aquifers. - **Underground gas storage** — designing seals to prevent leakage. - **Nuclear waste isolation** — selecting cap rocks. - **Environmental remediation** — modelling NAPLs (non-aqueous phase liquids) in groundwater. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buoyancy Force on a Hydrocarbon Column Consider a vertical hydrocarbon column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ sitting in a water-saturated pore space. **Step 1:** The hydrostatic pressure exerted by the water column immediately below the hydrocarbon is: $$P_{water} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 2:** The hydrostatic pressure exerted by the hydrocarbon column at the same depth is: $$P_{hc} = \rho_{hc} \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 3:** The buoyancy pressure (the net upward force per unit area) is the difference: $$\Delta P = P_{water} - P_{hc} = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 4:** Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$ to get the total upward buoyant force: $$F_b = \Delta P \cdot A = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A$$ **Step 5:** Since volume $V = h \cdot A$, this simplifies to the canonical form: $$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$$ > **Interpretation:** The buoyancy is directly proportional to the density contrast $\Delta\rho$ and the volume of the hydrocarbon. This is why gas (very low $\rho_{hc}$) experiences a *much stronger* upward drive than crude oil. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Hydrocarbon Column Height for Pore Entry A hydrocarbon blob can only enter a water-wet pore throat when its buoyancy pressure exceeds the capillary entry (threshold) pressure of the throat. **Step 1:** Capillary entry pressure for a cylindrical throat of radius $r$ is given by the Young-Laplace relation: $$P_c = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$ **Step 2:** Equating the buoyancy pressure (from Derivation 1, with $V = A \cdot h$, so $\Delta P_{buoy} = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h$) to the capillary pressure: $$\Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$ **Step 3:** Solving for the critical column height $h$: $$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho}$$ **Step 4:** More generally, if the hydrocarbon must traverse two throats of differing radii $r_1 > r_2$ (from coarse to fine), the column must first satisfy entry at the smaller throat. The differential form used in KTU problems is: $$h = \frac{2\gamma}{r_2 g \Delta\rho}\bigl(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2\bigr)$$ **Step 5:** This equation is the *fundamental seal-capacity test* — engineers compute the theoretical maximum column a cap rock can hold before leaking. ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > **Given:** $\gamma = 0.025 \ N/m$ (oil-water), $\theta = 30°$, $r = 1 \times 10^{-6} \ m$ (1 micron), $\Delta\rho = 200 \ kg/m^3$ (oil vs water), $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$. > > **Find:** Critical oil column height $h$. **Step 1 — Compute numerator:** $$2 \gamma \cos\theta = 2 \times 0.025 \times \cos 30°$$ $$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$ $$2 \times 0.025 \times 0.8660 = 0.04330 \ N/m$$ **Step 2 — Compute denominator:** $$r \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 9.81 \times 200$$ $$= 1.962 \times 10^{-3} \ N/m^3 \cdot m^2 / m = 1.962 \times 10^{-3} \ N/m$$ **Step 3 — Final division:** $$h = \frac{0.04330}{1.962 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$h \approx 22.07 \ m$$ **Engineering conclusion:** A seal with 1-micron throats can hold a column of oil up to **~22 m** before capillary failure (leakage). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Migration Velocity from Darcy's Law **Step 1:** Darcy's Law in one dimension: $$Q = -\frac{k A}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$ **Step 2:** The Darcy velocity (apparent velocity) is $v = Q/A$: $$v = -\frac{k}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$ **Step 3:** For secondary migration driven by buoyancy through a column of height $h$ over a horizontal distance $L$, the equivalent pressure gradient is: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h}{L}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting: $$v = \frac{k \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 5:** For a typical carrier bed: $k = 10^{-13} \ m^2$ (100 mD), $\Delta\rho = 200 \ kg/m^3$, $h = 50 \ m$, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3} \ Pa\cdot s$, $L = 5 \ km = 5000 \ m$: $$v = \frac{(10^{-13})(200)(9.81)(50)}{(10^{-3})(5000)}$$ $$v = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-10}}{5} = 1.962 \times 10^{-10} \ m/s$$ **Step 6:** Convert to realistic geological time (per million years): $$v = 1.962 \times 10^{-10} \times (3.156 \times 10^{13}) \approx 6.19 \times 10^{3} \ m/Myr$$ $$v \approx 6.2 \ km/Myr$$ **Engineering conclusion:** Hydrocarbons can travel **kilometres in 1 million years** — geologically *very fast*. This is why long-distance migration is observed in real basins. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Primary Migration | Secondary Migration | Tertiary Migration | |---|---|---|---| | **Medium** | Source rock (shale) | Carrier / reservoir rock | Existing reservoir / seal breach | | **Permeability** | Nano-Darcy to micro-Darcy | Milli-Darcy to Darcy | Variable | | **Dominant driving force** | Compaction pressure, microfracturing | Buoyancy ($\Delta\rho g h$) | Tectonic disturbance, seal failure | | **Dominant resistance** | Capillary, adsorption | Capillary pressure of throats | Capillary, structural | | **Phase** | Continuous, dissolved, gaseous | Continuous phase (oil/gas) | Continuous phase | | **Typical scale** | Metres | 100 m – 100 km | 1 – 100 km | | **Time scale** | $10^5$ – $10^6$ yrs | $10^4$ – $10^6$ yrs | Episodic | | **Direction** | Outward from source | Upward and updip | Upward / lateral re-migration | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrocarbon Migration System — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of hydrocarbons from generation to accumulation, including the dominant physics at each node. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[KEROGEN MATURATION]:::start --> B[OIL and GAS GENERATION
in source rock] B --> C[PRIMARY MIGRATION
expulsion from source] C --> D[CARRIER BED
permeable pathway] D --> E[SECONDARY MIGRATION
buoyancy driven updip flow] E --> F[TRAP IDENTIFICATION
structural or stratigraphic closure] F --> G[SEAL TEST
capillary pressure check] G -->|Seal holds| H[ACCUMULATION
commercial reservoir] G -->|Seal fails| I[LEAKAGE
tertiary migration or seep] I --> J[RE MIGRATION
to shallower trap or surface] H --> K[PRESERVATION
geological time] K --> L[RESERVOIR FOR PRODUCTION] B -.Pressure overbuild.-> C C -.Buoyancy plus capillary.-> D D -.Darcy flow.-> E E -.Capillary threshold.-> F F -.Seal capacity check.-> G classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Force-Balance Schematic on a Hydrocarbon Droplet ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HC["HYDROCARBON DROPLET"] droplet[ρ_hc] end subgraph DRIVE["DRIVING FORCES"] buoy[BUOYANCY F_b
Δρ g V] hyd[HYDRAULIC GRADIENT] end subgraph RESIST["RESISTIVE FORCES"] cap[CAPILLARY P_c
2γ cosθ / r] visc[VISCOUS DRAG
from Darcy friction] end buoy --> droplet hyd --> droplet cap -. opposes .-> droplet visc -. opposes .-> droplet ``` ## 4.3 Migration Pathway Cross-Section (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SURF["SURFACE"] s1[Top of section] end subgraph SEAL["CAP ROCK - SHALE - IMPERMEABLE"] s2[Seal layer
seals the trap] end subgraph RES["RESERVOIR ROCK - SAND - POROUS"] r1[Reservoir layer
stores hydrocarbons] end subgraph CAR["CARRIER BED - SANDSTONE - PERMEABLE"] c1[Carrier bed
conduit for migration] end subgraph SRC["SOURCE ROCK - SHALE - ORGANIC RICH"] so1[Source rock
generates hydrocarbons] end s1 --> SEAL SEAL --> RES RES --> CAR CAR --> SRC SRC --> BAS[Basement rock] so1 -.primary migration.-> c1 c1 -.buoyancy updip.-> r1 r1 -.trapped under.-> s2 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Generation begins in the source rock (bottom), then hydrocarbons squeeze out and rise through the carrier bed. At the top of the carrier bed, they accumulate under the impermeable seal within the porous reservoir rock. Migration is halted laterally where the reservoir pinches out or is faulted. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] > **Differentiate between primary and secondary migration of hydrocarbons.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Primary Migration | Secondary Migration | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into the carrier bed | Movement of hydrocarbons through the carrier/reservoir rock to the trap | | **Medium** | Source rock (shale, low permeability) | Carrier/reservoir rock (sandstone, carbonate; high permeability) | | **Main driving force** | Compaction pressure, microfracturing | Buoyancy ($\Delta\rho g V$) and hydraulic gradient | | **Direction** | Outward from source | Upward and updip | | **Scale** | Metres | Kilometres | | **Phase behaviour** | Solution, diffusion, continuous phase | Predominantly continuous phase | > **Marks distribution (typical):** 1 mark — definition of each; 1 mark — driving force distinction; 1 mark — medium / scale difference. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **Define buoyancy force in the context of hydrocarbon migration and state the controlling equation.** **Model Answer:** Buoyancy is the upward force experienced by a hydrocarbon phase immersed in denser formation water within a porous medium. It arises from the density contrast $\Delta\rho = \rho_w - \rho_{hc}$ and the gravitational acceleration $g$. It is the **primary driving force for secondary migration** in the subsurface. $$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$$ Equivalently, expressed as a buoyancy *pressure* on a column of height $h$: $$\Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$ > **Marks distribution:** 1 mark — concept; 1 mark — equation; 1 mark — significance (drives updip migration). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **(a)** Explain in detail the various mechanisms of primary migration of hydrocarbons. State the role of microfracturing with a neat schematic. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A hydrocarbon reservoir is overlain by a cap rock whose pore-throat radius is $r = 5 \times 10^{-7} \ m$. The oil–water interfacial tension is $\gamma = 0.030 \ N/m$ and the contact angle is $\theta = 35°$. Formation water density is $1030 \ kg/m^3$ and oil density is $830 \ kg/m^3$. Estimate the **maximum oil column height** that the seal can hold without capillary leakage. Take $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$. **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution — Mechanisms of Primary Migration (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Primary migration is the expulsion of hydrocarbons from a fine-grained, low-permeability source rock into a more permeable carrier bed. It is the *bridge* between generation and secondary migration. **Mechanism 1 — Pressure-driven Microfracturing (3 marks):** As kerogen matures, it converts to hydrocarbons with a substantial volume increase. The resulting pore-pressure build-up eventually exceeds the minimum in-situ (least principal) stress. The rock fails in tension, creating microfractures through which hydrocarbons flow as a *continuous phase*. This is considered the most viable mechanism because Darcy flow through nano-Darcy shale is orders of magnitude too slow. The fractures self-seal as soon as pressure drops, allowing pulses of expulsion. **Mechanism 2 — Solution / Molecular Diffusion (1.5 marks):** Light hydrocarbons (C1–C5) are soluble in formation water at elevated temperatures and pressures. They diffuse along concentration gradients from high-potential source rocks to lower-potential carrier beds. Particularly important for gas migration. **Mechanism 3 — Desorption and Wettability Change (1 mark):** As kerogen transforms, its surface properties shift from oil-wet to water-wet, releasing previously adsorbed hydrocarbons. Coalescence of oil-wet films at a critical saturation of $S_{hc}^{crit} \approx 10\text{–}20\%$ enables bulk flow. **Mechanism 4 — Squeegee / Compaction Flow (0.5 mark):** Continued burial compacts shale and expels pore fluids (with dissolved/suspended hydrocarbons) toward the basin margins. > **Schematic (descriptive):** Source rock is shown as horizontal shale beds, with vertical hairline microfractures emanating from overpressured zones toward the carrier bed interface. Hydrocarbons are shown exiting through the fractures. --- #### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Capillary Column Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the controlling equation (1 mark):** $$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc})}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark):** $$\gamma = 0.030 \ N/m, \quad \theta = 35°, \quad r = 5 \times 10^{-7} \ m$$ $$\rho_w = 1030 \ kg/m^3, \quad \rho_{hc} = 830 \ kg/m^3, \quad g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute density contrast (1 mark):** $$\Delta\rho = 1030 - 830 = 200 \ kg/m^3$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\cos\theta$ (1 mark):** $$\cos 35° \approx 0.8192$$ **Step 5 — Compute the numerator (1 mark):** $$2\gamma\cos\theta = 2 \times 0.030 \times 0.8192 = 0.04915 \ N/m$$ **Step 6 — Compute the denominator (1 mark):** $$r \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 9.81 \times 200$$ $$= 9.81 \times 10^{-4} = 9.81 \times 10^{-4} \ N/m$$ **Step 7 — Final computation (1 mark):** $$h = \frac{0.04915}{9.81 \times 10^{-4}} = 50.10 \ m$$ **Engineering conclusion:** The cap rock can retain an oil column of up to **~50 m** before capillary leakage occurs. If the discovered column exceeds this threshold, the trap is **leaking** and not a viable commercial reservoir at that closure height. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] > **(a)** With the help of a force-balance diagram, explain the forces acting on a hydrocarbon phase during secondary migration. Discuss why buoyancy is considered the dominant driving force. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Estimate the **Darcy velocity** of oil migrating through a carrier bed given: $k = 100 \ mD$, $\mu_o = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ Pa\cdot s$, $\Delta\rho = 250 \ kg/m^3$, $h = 80 \ m$ (vertical HC column), $L = 4 \ km$ (lateral migration distance), $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$. Also compute the **time required** to traverse the 4 km path. State your result in years. **(7 marks)** --- #### (a) Model Solution — Force-Balance on a Hydrocarbon Phase (7 marks) **Three force categories act on a hydrocarbon phase during secondary migration:** **(i) Buoyancy Force $F_b$ — Driving (2 marks):** Resulting from density contrast with formation water. Always directed *vertically upward* in the absence of tilt. Acts on every element of the hydrocarbon column. $$F_b = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot V = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) g V$$ **(ii) Capillary Pressure $P_c$ — Resistive (2 marks):** Water-wet grains resist entry of the non-wetting hydrocarbon phase into narrow throats. The hydrocarbon must overcome the threshold capillary pressure. $$P_c = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{r}$$ If $P_c$ is too high (small pores, high interfacial tension, oil-wet), the migration stalls. **(iii) Viscous Drag — Resistive (1.5 marks):** As the hydrocarbon phase flows through narrow pores, viscous friction at the oil–water interface dissipates energy. Quantified by Darcy's law with effective permeability to oil. **(iv) Hydrodynamic Drag — Driving or Resistive (1.5 marks):** Moving formation water (from compaction, meteoric recharge) can either *assist* migration (down-current) or *retard* it (counter-current). This is especially important in foreland basins. > **Conclusion (Bonus 0.5 mark):** Buoyancy is dominant because (a) it is always present in any dipping or layered system, (b) it scales with $\Delta\rho$ which is naturally large (0.1–1.0 g/cm³), and (c) it acts on the *entire* column, unlike capillary pressure which is a localized threshold. --- #### (b) Model Solution — Darcy Velocity and Migration Time (7 marks) **Step 1 — Convert permeability to SI (1 mark):** $$k = 100 \ mD = 100 \times 9.869 \times 10^{-16} \ m^2 \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-14} \ m^2$$ **Step 2 — Write the Darcy velocity equation (1 mark):** $$v = \frac{k \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute (1 mark):** $$v = \frac{(9.87 \times 10^{-14}) \times 250 \times 9.81 \times 80}{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4000}$$ **Step 4 — Compute numerator (1 mark):** $$9.87 \times 10^{-14} \times 250 \times 9.81 \times 80$$ $$= 9.87 \times 10^{-14} \times 196200 = 1.937 \times 10^{-8}$$ **Step 5 — Compute denominator (1 mark):** $$2 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000 = 8$$ **Step 6 — Final velocity (0.5 mark):** $$v = \frac{1.937 \times 10^{-8}}{8} = 2.42 \times 10^{-9} \ m/s$$ **Step 7 — Migration time (1.5 marks):** $$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{4000}{2.42 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.65 \times 10^{12} \ s$$ Convert to years (1 year $= 3.156 \times 10^7 \ s$): $$t = \frac{1.65 \times 10^{12}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \approx 5.23 \times 10^{4} \ years$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 52{,}300 \ years \approx 52.3 \ kilo-years}$$ > **Conclusion:** The migration is geologically instantaneous (<< 1 Myr), confirming that secondary migration is *not* a time-limiting step in basin evolution. --- ## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students LOSE marks (KTU Board pattern):** > 1. **Mixing up phases:** Confusing *Darcy velocity* (apparent) with *interstitial velocity* (actual) — actual = Darcy / porosity. > 2. **Unit mismatches in Darcy's Law:** Permeability in mD must be converted to m² (multiply by $9.869 \times 10^{-16}$). Skipping this yields an answer off by ~$10^{15}$ — a guaranteed zero. > 3. **Forgetting contact angle sign:** Capillary pressure depends on $\cos\theta$ — for $\theta > 90°$ (oil-wet), $\cos\theta$ is negative and the equation inverts. > 4. **No mention of critical saturation:** Any answer about primary migration that omits the *need for a critical hydrocarbon saturation* is incomplete. > 5. **Confusing tertiary migration with leakage:** Tertiary migration specifically means *re-migration to a new trap*; simple leakage to the surface is not tertiary migration. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrocarbon Migration — Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - ☐ Migration has **three stages**: Primary (source → carrier), Secondary (carrier → trap), Tertiary (re-migration). > - ☐ **Primary migration** is dominated by **pressure-driven microfracturing**; secondary by **buoyancy**. > - ☐ **Buoyancy** $\Rightarrow F_b = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot V$ — the lighter the HC (gas > oil), the stronger the upward drive. > - ☐ **Capillary pressure** $P_c = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r$ is the *resistive* threshold that the buoyancy must overcome. > - ☐ **Maximum seal column** $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (r g \Delta\rho)$ — the seal-capacity equation. > - ☐ **Darcy's law** $Q = -kA/\mu \cdot dP/dx$ governs the *rate* of migration; bulk permeability is the key. > - ☐ Critical HC saturation for bulk flow: $S_{hc}^{crit} \approx 0.10\text{–}0.20$. > - ☐ **Migration efficiency** is typically **< 10%** — most generated hydrocarbons are lost. > - ☐ Migration follows the **path of least capillary resistance** — large, connected pore throats. > - ☐ **Standard densities** to memorize: $\rho_w \approx 1.0 \ g/cm^3$; $\rho_{oil} \approx 0.7\text{–}0.9 \ g/cm^3$; $\rho_{gas} \approx 0.0007\text{–}0.3 \ g/cm^3$. > - ☐ Geological migration time is **fast** (kyr to Myr) — *not* a rate-limiting step. > - ☐ Hydrocarbons accumulate where **buoyancy < capillary entry pressure** of the seal.

topics.title: Petroleum Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Exploration** is the systematic, multi-disciplinary scientific and engineering search for hydrocarbon accumulations (crude oil, natural gas, and condensate) within the Earth's subsurface. It encompasses the complete chain of activities — from regional geological reconnaissance to detailed reservoir delineation — aimed at locating commercially viable petroleum traps.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PECHT522), petroleum exploration is formally defined as:

> The sequential application of geological, geophysical, geochemical, and petrophysical methods to identify, evaluate, and delineate subsurface geological structures and strata that may host economic accumulations of hydrocarbons, culminating in the drilling of exploratory wells to confirm their presence.

The exploration phase precedes **appraisal**, **development**, and **production** in the upstream oil and gas value chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT522 Module 1):** Petroleum Exploration is the foundational pillar of the course. The student is expected to understand *why* each exploration method is deployed, *what* geological parameter it measures, and *how* data integration leads to a drillable prospect.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to find a specific book inside a massive, multi-storey library that is buried underground. You cannot see the books directly. Here is how you would proceed:

1. **First**, you would consult a city map to find areas where libraries are typically built (avoiding floodplains, marshy lands) — this is analogous to **Geological Surveying** (basin analysis, surface geology).
2. **Next**, you would send sound waves (claps, echoes) into the ground and listen for differences in how the sound bounces back — this is analogous to **Geophysical Methods** (seismic, gravity, magnetic surveys).
3. **Then**, you would analyze the soil chemistry for traces of paper or ink seeping upward — this is analogous to **Geochemical Exploration** (soil gas surveying, microseepage analysis).
4. **Finally**, you would drill a small narrow hole and lower a microphone/camera to confirm whether books are actually present on a specific shelf — this is analogous to **Exploratory Drilling and Well Logging**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Petroleum exploration is a *funneling process* — each stage progressively reduces the search area from thousands of square kilometers (basin scale) down to a few hectares (drillable prospect). The cost and data-density of each stage rise dramatically as the area shrinks.

---

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics and Constants

The following **bold constants and metrics** are universally cited in petroleum exploration literature and form the bedrock of quantitative calculations:

- **World's proven oil reserves (2024 est.):** ~**1.5 trillion barrels (≈ 240 Gt)**.
- **Average exploration success rate (wildcat wells):** ~**1 in 10 (≈ 10%)** globally; in mature basins it can be **1 in 3**.
- **Typical exploration cycle:** **5 to 10 years** from basin entry to commercial discovery.
- **Average wildcat well depth:** **2,000 – 4,000 m**, though ultra-deep wells exceed **10,000 m**.
- **Standard 3-D seismic survey bin size:** **25 m × 25 m** (high-resolution) to **50 m × 50 m** (regional).
- **Density contrast used in gravity surveys:** Δρ ≈ **0.1 to 0.5 g/cm³** between porous sedimentary rock and dense basement/caprock.
- **Seismic P-wave velocity in sandstone:** **2,000 – 4,500 m/s**; in shale: **1,800 – 3,500 m/s**; in salt: **4,000 – 5,500 m/s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter for KTU exams:** Numerical problems in petroleum exploration often hinge on volumetric reserve estimation, seismic travel-time calculations, and gravity anomaly computations. These constants form the boundary conditions for such problems.

---

## 1.4 The Exploration Funnel — A Strategic Overview

The petroleum exploration workflow is best visualized as a **risk-reward funnel**. Each successive stage involves higher capital expenditure but progressively lower geological risk.

| Stage | Activity | Spatial Scale | Approx. Cost (USD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Basin Reconnaissance | Regional geology, remote sensing | 10⁴ – 10⁵ km² | 0.1 – 1 M |
| 2. Geophysical Surveys | Gravity, magnetic, 2-D/3-D seismic | 10² – 10³ km² | 1 – 50 M |
| 3. Prospect Generation | Seismic interpretation, mapping | 1 – 100 km² | 0.5 – 5 M |
| 4. Exploratory Drilling | Wildcat/stratigraphic wells | < 1 km² | 10 – 100 M |
| 5. Discovery & Appraisal | Confirmation wells, DST, logging | well-scale | 10 – 50 M |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cognitive Level Mapping:** This topic aligns with **CO1 (Remember & Understand)** of PECHT522. Expect questions at Bloom's Levels 1–3 (Recall, Comprehension, and basic Application).

---

## 1.5 Why Petroleum Exploration is Technically Distinctive

Petroleum exploration differs fundamentally from mineral (hard-rock) exploration due to:

1. **Fluid migration dynamics** — hydrocarbons migrate *upward* from mature source rocks through carrier beds until trapped by impermeable seals.
2. **Trap dependence** — economic accumulations require *four* elements to coexist in space and time: **source rock, reservoir rock, cap rock, and trap geometry** (the petroleum system concept).
3. **Indirect detection** — hydrocarbons themselves are not directly sensed; explorers detect **structural closures, stratigraphic variations, and fluid-induced anomalies**.
4. **Irreversible cost commitment** — once a well is drilled and abandoned as dry, the capital is sunk; hence the emphasis on reducing uncertainty *before* drilling.

This positions petroleum exploration as a **decision-making science under geological uncertainty**, governed by probability theory and risk economics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Exploration Workflow (Detailed Breakdown)

### **Stage 1 — Basin Reconnaissance (Desk Study)**

- **Objective:** Identify prospective sedimentary basins worthy of detailed survey.
- **Activities:** Compilation of regional geology, plate-tectonic analysis, stratigraphic literature review, satellite imagery interpretation (Landsat, Sentinel-2), aeromagnetic data screening.
- **Output:** A ranked list of basins ranked by hydrocarbon potential using the **Petroleum System Analysis** framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroleum System:** A natural system encompassing a pod of active source rock and all genetically related oil and gas accumulations. It requires the presence of **source rock, reservoir rock, seal rock, and overburden rock**, plus the geological processes of **trap formation, generation, migration, and accumulation** acting in concert.

---

### **Stage 2 — Reconnaissance Geophysical Surveys (Non-Seismic)**

#### (a) Gravity Survey
- **Physical Principle:** Density contrast (Δρ) between subsurface rock bodies produces measurable variations in the Earth's gravitational field.
- **Measured Quantity:** Acceleration in **mGal** (1 mGal = 10⁻⁵ m/s²).
- **Instrument:** **Lacoste & Romberg gravimeter** (modern field instrument).
- **Application:** Mapping basement topography, locating salt domes, delineating basin thickness.
- **Bouguer Anomaly Formula:** The free-air, Bouguer, and terrain-corrected anomaly is computed as:

$$
g_{\text{Bouguer}} = g_{\text{observed}} - g_{\text{theoretical}} + g_{\text{Free-Air}} - g_{\text{Bouguer Slab}} + g_{\text{Terrain}}
$$

- **Why it works:** Salt domes have density ≈ **2.2 g/cm³** (low); surrounding sediments ≈ **2.5 g/cm³**; basement ≈ **2.8 g/cm³**. This produces a **negative Bouguer anomaly** over salt structures.

#### (b) Magnetic Survey
- **Physical Principle:** Magnetization contrast (susceptibility κ) in rocks produces magnetic anomalies.
- **Measured Quantity:** Total magnetic intensity in **nanoTesla (nT)**.
- **Application:** Defining basement depth, identifying igneous intrusions, regional tectonic mapping.
- **Key Insight:** Sedimentary basins are generally **non-magnetic**; magnetic anomalies typically arise from **igneous basement** or **volcanic intrusions**.

#### (c) Geochemical Survey
- **Physical Principle:** Hydrocarbons microseep vertically through fractures and permeable strata, leaving detectable traces in soils and sediments.
- **Measured Quantities:** Light hydrocarbon gas concentrations (C₁–C₅), helium, radon, and δ¹³C isotope ratios.
- **Application:** Direct evidence of an active petroleum system at depth — even before drilling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Gravity + Magnetic + Geochemical are deployed before seismic:** They are orders of magnitude cheaper than seismic (gravity/magnetic surveys cost ~USD 100–500 per line-km; seismic 2-D costs ~USD 5,000–20,000 per line-km). They rapidly screen vast areas, and only the most prospective blocks are advanced to seismic.

---

### **Stage 3 — Detailed Seismic Survey (The Workhorse Method)**

- **Objective:** Image subsurface structural and stratigraphic geometries using **elastic wave propagation**.
- **Principle:** A seismic source (vibroseis trucks, dynamite, air guns offshore) generates acoustic pulses; reflections and refractions from acoustic impedance contrasts are recorded by geophones/hydrophones.
- **Key Equation:** Acoustic Impedance $Z = \rho \cdot v$, where $\rho$ is density and $v$ is seismic velocity.
- **Reflection Coefficient:** $R = (Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_1 + Z_2)$
- **Output:** A 2-D line or 3-D volume (time-domain) that, after migration and time-to-depth conversion, yields structural maps of horizons.

| Seismic Survey Type | Purpose | Typical Bin/Line Spacing |
|---|---|---|
| 2-D Seismic | Regional framework | 1–4 km line spacing |
| 3-D Seismic | Prospect delineation | 12.5 × 25 m bins |
| 4-D Seismic (3-D + time) | Reservoir monitoring | Re-acquired 3-D surveys |
| Vertical Seismic Profile (VSP) | Near-wellbore velocity calibration | Within a single well |

---

### **Stage 4 — Prospect Generation and Ranking**

- **Definition of a Prospect:** A mapped subsurface feature (structural or stratigraphic) that, if drilled, has a reasonable chance of containing hydrocarbons.
- **Risk Elements (independent probabilities):**
  1. **P(source):** Probability that mature source rock exists.
  2. **P(reservoir):** Probability that reservoir-quality rock exists.
  3. **P(trap):** Probability that a viable trap geometry exists.
  4. **P(seal):** Probability that an effective seal exists.
  5. **P(timing):** Probability that trap formation preceded hydrocarbon migration.
- **Geological Probability of Success (POS):**

$$
\text{POS}_{\text{geo}} = P(\text{source}) \times P(\text{reservoir}) \times P(\text{trap}) \times P(\text{seal}) \times P(\text{timing})
$$

- **Expected Monetary Value (EMV):**

$$
\text{EMV} = [\text{POS} \times (\text{Revenue} - \text{Cost}_{\text{success}})] - [(1 - \text{POS}) \times \text{Cost}_{\text{dry hole}}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EMV matters:** Operators use EMV to rank competing prospects and decide which to drill. A prospect with high POS but small reserves may have a lower EMV than a lower-POS but high-reserve-volume prospect.

---

### **Stage 5 — Exploratory Drilling and Logging**

- **Wildcat Well:** A well drilled in an unproven area — the highest-risk exploration activity.
- **Appraisal Well:** Drilled to delineate a discovered accumulation.
- **Well Logging:** The continuous recording of physical, chemical, and electrical properties of subsurface formations using tools lowered into the borehole.
- **Common Logs:**
  - **Gamma Ray (GR):** Measures natural radioactivity — distinguishes shale (high GR) from sand/carbonate (low GR).
  - **Resistivity (Deep/Medium/Shallow):** Measures resistivity — hydrocarbon-bearing zones show **high resistivity** because hydrocarbons are non-conductive.
  - **Density (RHOB):** Measures bulk density via gamma-gamma scattering — identifies porous zones (low density).
  - **Neutron Porosity (NPHI):** Measures hydrogen index — high in porous fluid-filled formations.
  - **Sonic (DT):** Measures P-wave travel time per unit length — used for porosity and seismic calibration.

| Log | Measures | Hydrocarbon Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma Ray | Natural radioactivity (API units) | Low GR → clean reservoir |
| Resistivity | Electrical resistivity (Ω·m) | High resistivity → HC presence |
| Density | Bulk density (g/cm³) | Low ρ → porous rock |
| Neutron | Hydrogen index (porosity) | Combined with density → lithology & fluid |
| Sonic | Slowness (μs/ft) | Used to compute porosity and synthetic seismograms |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** These formulas recur frequently in KTU Module 1 examination questions. Memorize the variable definitions and units to avoid unnecessary mark loss.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot v$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $v$ in m/s | $Z$ in kg/(m²·s) or Rayl |
| 2 | Reflection Coefficient | $R = (Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_1 + Z_2)$ | dimensionless | $R < 0.1$ generally weak; $R > 0.3$ strong |
| 3 | Transmission Coefficient | $T = 1 - R$ (lossless) | dimensionless | Energy balance: $R + T = 1$ |
| 4 | Two-way Travel Time | $TWT = 2d / v_{\text{avg}}$ | $d$ depth, $v$ velocity | Used to convert time to depth |
| 5 | Bouguer Anomaly | $\Delta g_{\text{B}} = 2\pi G \rho h$ | $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}$ N·m²/kg² | For an infinite slab of thickness $h$ |
| 6 | Magnetic Anomaly (dipole approx.) | $\Delta T \approx \kappa F / r^3$ | $\kappa$ susceptibility, $F$ field, $r$ distance | Used for depth-to-basement estimates |
| 7 | Geological POS | $\text{POS} = \prod P_i$ | independent probabilities | Product of 5 risk elements |
| 8 | EMV | $\text{EMV} = P_s V_s - P_f V_f$ | $V_s$ success NPV, $V_f$ failure cost | Decision-making metric |
| 9 | Volumetric Oil in Place (OOIP) | $N = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_o$ | $A$ acres, $h$ ft, $\phi$ fraction, $S_w$ fraction, $B_o$ bbl/STB | 7758 = 43560 ft²/acre × 1/5.615 ft³/bbl |
| 10 | Volumetric Gas in Place (OGIP) | $G = 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_g$ | $A$ acres, $h$ ft, $\phi$, $S_w$, $B_g$ | $G$ in scf |
| 11 | Recovery Factor (general) | $RF = (\text{Recoverable}) / (\text{OOIP or OGIP})$ | fraction | Typically 0.10 – 0.40 for oil; 0.60 – 0.85 for gas |
| 12 | Seismic Resolution (vertical) | $R_v = v / (4 f)$ | $v$ velocity, $f$ dominant frequency | Tuning thickness limit |
| 13 | Seismic Resolution (horizontal/Fresnel) | $R_h = \sqrt{(\lambda z / 2)}$ | $\lambda$ wavelength, $z$ depth | After migration ≈ $v / (2 f)$ |
| 14 | Snell's Law | $\sin \theta_1 / v_1 = \sin \theta_2 / v_2$ | angles in degrees/rad | Critical angle: $\sin \theta_c = v_1 / v_2$ |
| 15 | Gardner's Relation | $\rho = 0.31 \cdot v_p^{0.25}$ | $v_p$ in ft/s, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | Empirical; used in synthetic seismograms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students frequently confuse **Acoustic Impedance** with **Reflection Coefficient**. Acoustic Impedance is a *rock property*; Reflection Coefficient is a *boundary property*. They have entirely different physical units and meanings.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of These Methods

Each exploration technique addresses a specific decision-making question:

- **Geological mapping** answers: *"Where are the sedimentary basins?"*
- **Gravity / magnetic surveys** answer: *"Where is the basement, and are there any structural highs or salt bodies?"*
- **Geochemical surveys** answer: *"Is there evidence of a working petroleum system?"*
- **Seismic surveys** answer: *"Where exactly should we drill?"*
- **Well logging** answers: *"Did we hit hydrocarbons, and if so, how much?"*

In modern **integrated exploration workflows**, these datasets are jointly inverted and interpreted in **shared earth models** (e.g., Petrel, GOCAD) to reduce subsurface uncertainty — a practice known as **Multi-Disciplinary Integration (MDI)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient

This derivation underpins seismic exploration.

**Step 1: Define Acoustic Impedance.**

Acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is defined as the product of its bulk density $\rho$ and the P-wave velocity $v_p$ of seismic waves through it.

$$
Z = \rho \cdot v_p
$$

This expresses the resistance of the medium to the propagation of a pressure wave. Dimensionally, $[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^3$ and $[v_p] = \text{m/s}$, so $[Z] = \text{kg}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$.

**Step 2: Set up the boundary conditions at an interface.**

Consider a planar interface separating two elastic media with acoustic impedances $Z_1$ (upper) and $Z_2$ (lower). An incident plane P-wave of amplitude $A_i$ strikes the interface and produces a reflected wave $A_r$ and a transmitted wave $A_t$.

**Step 3: Apply continuity of displacement and stress.**

For an interface between two welded elastic media, both the particle displacement and the normal stress must be continuous. Combining these boundary conditions yields:

$$
\frac{A_r}{A_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} = R \quad \text{(Reflection Coefficient)}
$$

$$
\frac{A_t}{A_i} = \frac{2 Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} = T \quad \text{(Transmission Coefficient, pressure-normalized)}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the sign and magnitude.**

- If $Z_2 > Z_1$ (e.g., soft shale over hard limestone): $R > 0$ — reflected wave has the **same polarity** as the incident wave.
- If $Z_2 < Z_1$ (e.g., hard limestone over soft shale): $R < 0$ — polarity reversal (a "hard kick" produces a trough, depending on SEG convention).

The magnitude $\vert R \vert$ ranges from 0 (no contrast, full transmission) to 1 (perfect acoustic mismatch, full reflection).

**Step 5: Verify energy balance.**

Energy (which scales as amplitude squared divided by impedance) is conserved:

$$
R^2 \cdot \frac{Z_1}{Z_1} + T^2 \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = 1
$$

(For normal incidence, the energy reflection + transmission = 1 holds.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A standard 7-mark sub-question in the exam asks: *"Derive the reflection coefficient for a normally incident P-wave at a horizontal interface."* This derivation, framed as above, fetches full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Way Travel Time and Depth Conversion

Seismic data is acquired in **time**, but drilling decisions require **depth**. Conversion is therefore essential.

**Step 1: Define one-way travel time for a single homogeneous layer.**

For a layer of thickness $d$ with constant P-wave velocity $v$, the one-way travel time is:

$$
t_1 = \frac{d}{v}
$$

**Step 2: Apply to a normally reflected wave.**

For a reflection from the base of the layer, the seismic wave travels *down* through the layer and then *up* back through it. The total travel distance is $2d$. Thus the **two-way travel time (TWT)** is:

$$
t_{\text{TWT}} = 2 \cdot \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2 d}{v}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for depth.**

Rearranging for $d$:

$$
d = \frac{v \cdot t_{\text{TWT}}}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Extend to a multi-layer case.**

If the subsurface consists of $n$ horizontal layers with thicknesses $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n$ and interval velocities $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$, the total two-way time to the $n$-th reflector is:

$$
t_{\text{TWT}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2 d_i}{v_i} = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{d_i}{v_i}
$$

The corresponding depth is then:

$$
z_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot \Delta t_i
$$

where $\Delta t_i$ is the two-way travel time through layer $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-to-depth conversion** is among the most error-prone operations in exploration. Errors of even 5–10% in velocity can translate to **hundreds of meters of depth uncertainty** at typical reservoir depths (3000 m), with major consequences for drilling cost and well placement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Estimate of Original Oil in Place (OOIP)

This derivation is the **gold standard** of petroleum engineering calculations and is universally tested.

**Step 1: Start from a physical volume of reservoir rock.**

Consider a reservoir that occupies a surface area $A$ and has an average (net) thickness $h$. The bulk rock volume is:

$$
V_{\text{bulk}} = A \cdot h
$$

**Step 2: Apply porosity to get pore volume.**

Only the pore space — fraction $\phi$ of bulk volume — contains fluids (oil, water, gas). The pore volume is:

$$
V_{\text{pore}} = A \cdot h \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 3: Subtract water to get hydrocarbon pore volume.**

A fraction $S_w$ of the pore space is occupied by irreducible (connate) water. The remaining fraction $(1 - S_w)$ is occupied by hydrocarbons:

$$
V_{\text{HC}} = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)
$$

**Step 4: Convert from reservoir conditions to stock-tank conditions.**

Hydrocarbons exist at reservoir pressure and temperature as a single phase (or with dissolved gas). To express oil volume at surface (stock-tank) conditions, divide by the **Oil Formation Volume Factor** $B_o$ (typically **1.2 – 1.5 reservoir bbl/STB**):

$$
N = \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

**Step 5: Apply unit conversion factors for field units.**

In oilfield units (acres for area, feet for thickness, barrels for volume), the conversion factors are:

- **1 acre = 43,560 ft²**
- **1 ft³ = 7.481 gallons = 0.1781 bbl**, so **1 bbl = 5.615 ft³**.

Inserting these constants:

$$
N \, [\text{STB}] = \frac{A \, [\text{acres}] \cdot h \, [\text{ft}] \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o \, [\text{bbl/STB}]} \cdot \frac{43{,}560 \, \text{ft}^2/\text{acre}}{5.615 \, \text{ft}^3/\text{bbl}}
$$

$$
N = \frac{43{,}560}{5.615} \cdot \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

The numerical constant $\frac{43{,}560}{5.615} = 7758$. Hence the standard oilfield formula:

$$
\boxed{N = 7758 \cdot \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o} \quad \text{[STB]}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Favorites:** A typical 7-mark Part B sub-question provides $A, h, \phi, S_w, B_o$ and asks for OOIP. Always state assumptions (uniform reservoir, average properties) and report the answer in stock-tank barrels (STB) or 10⁶ STB (MMSTB).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Volumetric Problem)

**Problem:** A reservoir has the following properties:
- Area $A = 1500$ acres
- Net pay thickness $h = 50$ ft
- Average porosity $\phi = 0.20$
- Average water saturation $S_w = 0.30$
- Oil formation volume factor $B_o = 1.30$ bbl/STB

**Estimate the Original Oil in Place (OOIP).**

**Solution:**

Step 1: Substitute into the OOIP formula:

$$
N = 7758 \cdot \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

Step 2: Plug in the numerical values:

$$
N = 7758 \cdot \frac{1500 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.20 \cdot (1 - 0.30)}{1.30}
$$

Step 3: Compute the numerator (excluding 7758):

$$
1500 \cdot 50 = 75{,}000
$$

$$
75{,}000 \cdot 0.20 = 15{,}000
$$

$$
15{,}000 \cdot 0.70 = 10{,}500
$$

Step 4: Divide by $B_o = 1.30$:

$$
\frac{10{,}500}{1.30} = 8{,}076.92
$$

Step 5: Multiply by 7758:

$$
N = 7758 \cdot 8{,}076.92 = 62{,}660{,}538 \, \text{STB} \approx 62.66 \, \text{MMSTB}
$$

**Answer:** OOIP $\approx$ **62.66 million stock-tank barrels**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table of Exploration Methods

| Method | Measures | Cost (relative) | Penetration Depth | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Geological Surface Mapping | Rock type, structure, fossils | Very Low | Surface only | Basin reconnaissance |
| Satellite Remote Sensing | Lineaments, surface features | Very Low | Surface only | Regional structural framework |
| Gravity Survey | Density contrast | Low | Up to basement (~10 km) | Basement mapping, salt domes |
| Magnetic Survey | Magnetic susceptibility | Low | Up to Curie isotherm (~30 km) | Basement topography, volcanics |
| Geochemical Survey | Hydrocarbon microseepage | Low | Indirect (surface) | Active petroleum system confirmation |
| 2-D Seismic | Acoustic impedance contrast | Medium | 5–10 km | Regional framework, lead identification |
| 3-D Seismic | Acoustic impedance contrast | High | 5–10 km | Prospect delineation, well placement |
| CSEM (Marine) | Resistivity | Very High | 1–5 km | Direct hydrocarbon indication (offshore) |
| Exploratory Well | Direct rock/fluid | Extreme | Engineered depth | Final confirmation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The exploration industry has progressively moved from *direct geological observation* (cheap, shallow) to *indirect geophysical inference* (expensive, deep) and finally to *direct physical sampling* (most expensive, deepest). Each step demands increasing integration of multidisciplinary data to manage risk.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Petroleum Exploration Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Basin Reconnaissance Desk Study] --> stepB[Regional Geological Mapping Remote Sensing Literature Review]
    stepB --> stepC{Non-Seismic Reconnaissance}
    stepC --> stepD[Gravity Survey Density Mapping]
    stepC --> stepE[Magnetic Survey Basement Structure]
    stepC --> stepF[Geochemical Survey Microseepage Analysis]
    stepD --> stepG[Integration Risk Mapping]
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[2-D Seismic Survey Regional Framework]
    stepH --> stepI[Prospect Identification Mapping Leads]
    stepI --> stepJ{Commercial Prospect?}
    stepJ -- No --> stepK[Drop or Defer]
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepL[3-D Seismic Survey Detailed Imaging]
    stepL --> stepM[Prospect Ranking EMV Calculation]
    stepM --> stepN[Drill Wildcat Exploratory Well]
    stepN --> stepO{Hydrocarbon Discovery?}
    stepO -- No --> stepP[Plug and Abandon Plug Dry Hole]
    stepO -- Yes --> stepQ[Discovery Well Logging and Testing]
    stepQ --> stepR[Appraisal Drilling Reservoir Delineation]
    stepR --> stepS[Move to Development and Production Phase]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The chart reads top-down, mirroring the chronological sequence of exploration activities. Branching points (the diamonds `{}`) represent **Go / No-Go decision gates**, where capital is committed or withheld based on geological and economic criteria.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Subsurface Petroleum System Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph surfaceBlock[Surface]
        surfA[Land Surface or Seafloor]
    end

    subgraph overburdenBlock[Overburden Layer]
        ob1[Impermeable Sedimentary Layers]
    end

    subgraph reservoirBlock[Reservoir Unit]
        res1[Porous Permeable Sandstone or Carbonate]
    end

    subgraph trapBlock[Trap Geometry]
        trap1[Structural or Stratigraphic Closure]
    end

    subgraph sourceBlock[Source Rock]
        src1[Organic-Rich Shale or Marl]
    end

    subgraph sealBlock[Seal Rock]
        seal1[Impermeable Shale or Evaporite Cap]
    end

    surfA --> ob1
    ob1 --> trap1
    trap1 --> res1
    seal1 -.- trap1
    src1 -. Hydrocarbon Migration Upward .-> trap1
    ob1 -. Generates Pressure Compaction .-> src1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Subsurface layout convention:** The diagram represents a vertical cross-section. The **source rock** (typically deep, mature, organic-rich) generates hydrocarbons that **migrate upward** (dashed arrow) into the **reservoir rock** within a structural or stratigraphic **trap**, sealed from above by an impermeable **cap rock**. The **overburden** provides thermal maturation conditions.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    queryStart[New Exploration Block Identified] --> q1{Stage of Evaluation?}
    q1 -- Regional Screening --> m1[Apply Gravity Magnetic and Geochemical Surveys]
    q1 -- Prospect Delineation --> m2[Apply 2D and 3D Seismic Surveys]
    q1 -- Pre Drill Risk Reduction --> m3[Apply CSEM or Seismic Inversion]
    q1 -- Well Planning --> m4[Apply Well Logging and VSP]

    m1 --> r1[Identify Leads and Basement Features]
    m2 --> r2[Map Closure and Reservoir Geometry]
    m3 --> r3[Confirm Direct Hydrocarbon Indicators]
    m4 --> r4[Quantify Porosity Saturation and Pay]

    r1 --> merge1[Integrated Subsurface Model]
    r2 --> merge1
    r3 --> merge1
    r4 --> merge1

    merge1 --> finalDecision[Drill or Drop Decision EMV Based]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision logic explanation:** Each exploration method is matched to a specific stage of decision-making — from regional screening (gravity/magnetic) to direct hydrocarbon indication (CSEM) to reservoir quantification (logs). The integrated model synthesizes all outputs.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Data Flow from Acquisition to Drilling Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataSourceA[Field Acquisition Gravity Magnetic Seismic Logs] --> procA[Data Processing Filtering Stacking Migration]
    procA --> interpA[Geological and Geophysical Interpretation]
    interpA --> modelA[Static Reservoir Model Structural Maps and Property Cubes]
    modelA --> riskA[Volumetric and Risk Analysis OOIP POS EMV]
    riskA --> econA[Economic Analysis CAPEX OPEX NPV]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU exams:** When a question asks *"Describe the data flow in a modern exploration project"* or *"Explain how an exploration decision is made,"* this exact pipeline is what examiners expect. The keywords to include are: **acquisition → processing → interpretation → modeling → volumetric analysis → economic analysis → decision**.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

> **Define the term "Petroleum System" and list its essential elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A petroleum system is a natural system that encompasses a pod of active source rock and all genetically related oil and gas accumulations. It includes all the geological elements and processes that are essential for a hydrocarbon accumulation to exist.

**Essential elements:**

1. **Source rock** — organic-rich rock capable of generating hydrocarbons.
2. **Reservoir rock** — porous and permeable rock capable of storing hydrocarbons.
3. **Seal rock (cap rock)** — impermeable rock that prevents upward escape of hydrocarbons.
4. **Overburden rock** — sedimentary layers providing pressure and temperature for maturation.

**Essential processes:**

5. **Trap formation**
6. **Generation-migration-accumulation**

*(All six points must appear for full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

> **Differentiate between 2-D and 3-D seismic surveys.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 2-D Seismic | 3-D Seismic |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Single lines of receivers | Dense grid of receivers |
| Coverage | Linear cross-section | Full subsurface volume |
| Data density | Sparse (1–4 km line spacing) | Dense (12.5–25 m bin spacing) |
| Cost | Low to moderate | High |
| Use | Regional framework, lead identification | Prospect delineation, well placement, reservoir characterization |
| Migration | 2-D migration | 3-D migration (handles side-swipe properly) |

*(Award 1 mark for each correctly identified difference with brief justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides an internal choice between two alternatives. The pattern is typically **sub-question (a) for 7 marks** and **sub-question (b) for 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the various stages of petroleum exploration, from basin reconnaissance to exploratory drilling. Highlight the role of geophysical methods at each stage.

**Model Answer:**

Petroleum exploration follows a sequential, funnel-like workflow that progressively narrows the search area while increasing capital commitment and reducing geological uncertainty.

**Stage 1 — Basin Reconnaissance (1 mark):**
This stage involves regional geological studies, satellite imagery interpretation, and literature surveys. It identifies prospective sedimentary basins based on tectonic setting, sediment thickness, and the presence of a working petroleum system.

**Stage 2 — Non-Seismic Geophysical Surveys (2 marks):**
Gravity and magnetic surveys are deployed to map basement topography and detect subsurface density/magnetic contrasts. Gravity surveys are particularly effective for locating salt domes (low density), whereas magnetic surveys delineate basement structure and igneous intrusions. Geochemical surveys complement these by detecting hydrocarbon microseepage at the surface, providing direct evidence of an active petroleum system.

**Stage 3 — Seismic Surveys (2 marks):**
Two-dimensional (2-D) seismic surveys are first acquired to establish the regional structural framework. Once leads are identified, three-dimensional (3-D) seismic surveys provide high-resolution volumetric images of prospects. The acoustic impedance contrast at formation boundaries produces reflections that, after migration and time-to-depth conversion, define trap geometry, reservoir distribution, and seal integrity.

**Stage 4 — Prospect Generation and Ranking (1 mark):**
Geoscientists integrate all datasets to map closures, estimate reservoir thickness, and compute the **Geological Probability of Success (POS)** and **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**. Only prospects with acceptable risk-reward profiles are advanced.

**Stage 5 — Exploratory Drilling (1 mark):**
A wildcat well is drilled to test the prospect. Well logging and formation testing confirm the presence or absence of hydrocarbons. Successful discoveries advance to appraisal and development; dry holes are plugged and abandoned.

*(Marks allocated as indicated; diagrams earn additional credit.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reservoir has the following properties:
- Area = 2000 acres
- Net pay thickness = 40 ft
- Porosity = 0.18
- Water saturation = 0.25
- Oil formation volume factor = 1.25 bbl/STB

Calculate the **Original Oil in Place (OOIP)**. If the recovery factor is **30%**, compute the **recoverable reserves**. Comment on the significance of recovery factor in field development planning.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the OOIP formula (1 mark):**

$$
N = 7758 \cdot \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values (1 mark):**

$$
N = 7758 \cdot \frac{2000 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.18 \cdot (1 - 0.25)}{1.25}
$$

**Step 3: Compute step by step (3 marks):**

$$
N = 7758 \cdot \frac{2000 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.75}{1.25}
$$

$$
= 7758 \cdot \frac{2000 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.135}{1.25}
$$

$$
= 7758 \cdot \frac{10{,}800}{1.25}
$$

$$
= 7758 \cdot 8{,}640
$$

$$
= 67{,}029{,}120 \, \text{STB} \approx 67.03 \, \text{MMSTB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute recoverable reserves (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Recoverable Reserves} = RF \times N = 0.30 \times 67.03 \, \text{MMSTB} = 20.11 \, \text{MMSTB}
$$

**Step 5: Comment on recovery factor (1 mark):**

The recovery factor depends on reservoir drive mechanism (solution gas, water drive, gas cap), reservoir heterogeneity, fluid properties, and production strategy. A low RF indicates that enhanced oil recovery (EOR) methods — such as water flooding, gas injection, or chemical flooding — may substantially increase ultimate recovery. Field development planning must balance RF against CAPEX, OPEX, and oil price scenarios to optimize net present value (NPV).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **seismic reflection method** in petroleum exploration. Derive the expression for **reflection coefficient** in terms of acoustic impedances of the two media.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (4 marks):**
The seismic reflection method is based on the propagation of elastic waves through the Earth. A seismic source (e.g., dynamite, vibroseis, air gun) generates acoustic pulses that travel downward. When these waves encounter a boundary between two rock layers with contrasting acoustic impedances, a portion of the energy is reflected back to the surface, where it is recorded by an array of geophones (or hydrophones offshore). The two-way travel time, amplitude, and shape of the reflected wave contain information about the depth, geometry, and physical properties of the reflecting horizon.

Modern processing of the recorded data — including **stacking, migration, and time-to-depth conversion** — produces 2-D lines or 3-D volumes that image subsurface structure. By interpreting these images, geoscientists identify **structural traps** (anticlines, faults, salt domes) and **stratigraphic traps** (channels, reefs, unconformities) that may host hydrocarbon accumulations.

**Derivation of Reflection Coefficient (3 marks):**

Let two media have densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$, and P-wave velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$, respectively. The acoustic impedances are:

$$
Z_1 = \rho_1 v_1 \qquad Z_2 = \rho_2 v_2
$$

Applying the boundary conditions of continuity of normal displacement and normal stress at a planar interface for a normally incident P-wave, the reflection coefficient (ratio of reflected to incident amplitude) is:

$$
R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}
$$

The corresponding transmission coefficient (pressure-normalized) is:

$$
T = \frac{2 Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}
$$

At normal incidence, energy conservation requires $R^2 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} T^2 = 1$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **gravity, magnetic, and seismic geophysical methods** in terms of physical principle, measurable quantity, application, and cost. Discuss why seismic is generally the most decisive method despite its higher cost.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Gravity | Magnetic | Seismic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Principle** | Density contrast (Δρ) | Magnetic susceptibility (κ) | Acoustic impedance contrast (ΔZ) |
| **Measured Quantity** | Acceleration (mGal) | Total field intensity (nT) | Two-way travel time & amplitude |
| **Typical Magnitude** | 0.01 – 1 mGal | 10 – 1000 nT | Reflection at ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻¹ of source amplitude |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Low (km scale) | Low to moderate | High (m scale after migration) |
| **Penetration Depth** | Up to basement | Up to Curie isotherm | 5 – 10 km typical |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High |
| **Best Application** | Basement mapping, salt domes | Igneous bodies, basement | Structural & stratigraphic imaging |
| **Limitations** | Non-unique interpretation | Non-unique, deep sources | Expensive, time-consuming |

**Why seismic is most decisive (2 marks):**

Despite its higher cost, seismic is the most decisive exploration method because:

1. **Highest resolution:** Seismic wavelengths are typically 30–100 m, yielding vertical and lateral resolution far superior to gravity or magnetic methods.
2. **Direct imaging of structure:** Seismic reflections map geological horizons directly, allowing 3-D visualization of traps, faults, and reservoir geometries.
3. **Quantitative reservoir characterization:** Amplitude, phase, and AVO (Amplitude Versus Offset) analysis provide insights into fluid content and lithology — features inaccessible to gravity/magnetic methods.
4. **Reduced drilling risk:** Detailed seismic imaging dramatically reduces the chance of a dry hole, justifying the high cost when balanced against drilling expenditure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Petroleum Exploration questions (PECHT522):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in OOIP Calculations:** Forgetting to use **7758** as the conversion constant when using oilfield units (acres, feet). Marks are deducted if the student uses SI units but reports the answer in barrels inconsistently.
> 2. **Reflection Coefficient Sign Errors:** Forgetting that $R > 0$ means same polarity and $R < 0$ means polarity reversal. Examiners often test this explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing Acoustic Impedance with Reflection Coefficient:** Impedance is a rock property; reflection coefficient is a boundary property. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping Diagrams in Long Answers:** A 14-mark answer on exploration stages without a flowchart or block diagram typically loses **2–3 marks** versus a diagram-decorated answer.
> 5. **Omitting Definitions in Short Answers:** Even in 3-mark Part A questions, examiners expect at least **one sentence of formal definition** before listing points.
> 6. **Ignoring the B_o Term:** In OOIP problems, students sometimes write $N = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$ without dividing by $B_o$. This is the most common single mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Petroleum Exploration (PECHT522 Module 1):**

- **Petroleum exploration** is a *funneling* process: from basin scale down to drillable prospect.
- The **petroleum system** consists of **source rock, reservoir rock, cap rock, overburden rock**, plus the processes of **generation, migration, accumulation, and trap formation**.
- **Exploration stages:** (1) Basin reconnaissance → (2) Non-seismic (gravity, magnetic, geochemical) → (3) Seismic (2-D then 3-D) → (4) Prospect ranking (POS, EMV) → (5) Exploratory drilling & logging.
- **Gravity surveys** detect **density contrasts**; measured in **mGal**; useful for salt domes and basement mapping.
- **Magnetic surveys** detect **susceptibility contrasts**; measured in **nT**; useful for igneous intrusions and basement structure.
- **Geochemical surveys** detect **hydrocarbon microseepage**; provide direct evidence of a working petroleum system.
- **Seismic surveys** exploit **acoustic impedance contrasts** ($Z = \rho \cdot v$); reflections are governed by $R = (Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_1 + Z_2)$.
- **Two-way travel time** $TWT = 2d / v$; **depth conversion** uses interval velocities from sonic logs or stacking velocities.
- **Well logging** tools: Gamma Ray (lithology), Resistivity (HC indicator), Density (porosity), Neutron (porosity), Sonic (velocity).
- **OOIP formula (oilfield units):** $N = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_o$.
- **OGIP formula (oilfield units):** $G = 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_g$.
- **Geological POS** is the **product** of independent risk probabilities for source, reservoir, trap, seal, and timing.
- **EMV** = (POS × Success NPV) − (Failure probability × Dry hole cost).
- **Vertical seismic resolution** $\approx v / (4f)$; **horizontal resolution** (post-migration) $\approx v / (2f)$.
- **Snell's Law** governs refraction at dipping interfaces; the **critical angle** $\sin \theta_c = v_1 / v_2$.
- Always state **assumptions, units, and final interpretation** in any numerical answer.
- **Modern exploration** is increasingly *data-integrated*, leveraging machine learning, 4-D seismic, and CSEM for direct hydrocarbon indication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Natural Gas: Associated Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conventional Natural Gas: Associated Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Associated Gas (AG)** is a classification of **conventional natural gas** that occurs in hydrocarbon reservoirs in **physical coexistence with crude oil**. It may exist in two distinct physical states within the same reservoir:

1. **Dissolved (Solution) Gas** — gas molecules held in molecular solution within the liquid crude oil phase under the high pressure of the reservoir.
2. **Free Gas Cap Gas** — gas occupying the pore space immediately above the liquid oil column in the same structural trap.

Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT522)**, associated gas is therefore **not a standalone reservoir fluid**; its recovery, composition, and economics are intrinsically coupled to the simultaneous production of crude oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of natural gas into *Associated*, *Non-Associated*, and *Dissolved* gas is the foundational taxonomy under Module 2. Associated gas is the gas co-produced with crude oil, irrespective of whether it was originally dissolved or existed as a free cap.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soda Bottle" Model

Imagine a sealed plastic soda bottle (the **reservoir**) under high pressure:

- The **liquid soda** = crude oil containing dissolved sugar and CO₂.
- The **headspace CO₂** at the top = free gas cap.
- The **dissolved CO₂** in the liquid = solution gas.

When you slowly open the cap (analogous to **production / pressure decline**):
- The internal pressure drops.
- The dissolved CO₂ begins to **bubble out of solution** (this is exactly the **bubble-point phenomenon** in oil reservoirs).
- The headspace gas escapes first (gas cap production), followed by the liberated dissolved gas.

This analogy captures the *thermodynamic essence* of associated gas: gas and oil are not independent phases — they are in **dynamic phase equilibrium** dictated by reservoir pressure, temperature, and fluid composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Production Metrics** used universally in the petroleum industry for associated gas:
> - **scf** (standard cubic feet) at **14.7 psia and 60 °F**
> - **bbl** (barrel) = **42 US gallons** = **0.158987 m³**
> - **STB** (stock tank barrel) = volume of oil measured at surface separator conditions
> - **GOR (Gas-Oil Ratio)** expressed in **scf/STB**

### 1.3 Distinguishing Features of Associated Gas

| Property | Associated Gas | Non-Associated Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Reservoir fluid | Gas + oil in same trap | Gas only |
| Initial phase state | Dissolved and/or free | Single gaseous phase |
| Typical methane content | **60 – 80 %** (lower) | **85 – 95 %** (higher) |
| C₂⁺ (ethane and heavier) content | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| H₂S and CO₂ variability | Common | Variable |
| Primary driver of recovery | Oil production schedule | Gas production schedule |
| Re-injection requirement | Often mandated (conservation) | Rare |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks to "distinguish associated from non-associated gas," the *phase equilibrium coupling* with oil and the *re-injection economics* are the two highest-weightage points.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Basis — Phase Equilibrium

Associated gas behavior is governed by the **Pressure–Volume–Temperature (PVT) phase behavior** of hydrocarbon mixtures. A reservoir containing oil + dissolved gas + free gas is a multi-phase system described by:

- **Bubble-point pressure ($P_b$):** the pressure at which the first bubble of gas is liberated from the oil as pressure declines.
- **Dew-point pressure ($P_d$):** relevant for gas-condensate systems (not the primary concern for associated gas in oil reservoirs, but conceptually important).
- **Retrograde behavior:** in gas-condensate reservoirs, liquids drop out of the gas phase as pressure falls below $P_d$ — a phenomenon that, while not the focus here, demonstrates that hydrocarbon phase behavior is non-trivial.

### 2.2 The Three Pressure Regimes of an Oil Reservoir

A producing oil reservoir containing associated gas passes through three thermodynamic stages as reservoir pressure ($P_r$) declines:

1. **$P_r > P_b$ (Above Bubble Point):** All gas remains dissolved in oil. **No free gas** exists in the pores. Producing GOR equals initial solution GOR.
2. **$P_r = P_b$ (At Bubble Point):** The first gas bubble nucleates. The oil is *saturated* — it holds the maximum dissolved gas it can retain at that temperature.
3. **$P_r < P_b$ (Below Bubble Point):** Gas evolves continuously from the oil. The liberated gas may migrate upward to form a **secondary gas cap** or flow directly to the wellbore.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters (KTU focus):** Reservoir engineering calculations of oil in-place, gas cap size, and recovery factor are all predicated on identifying which regime the reservoir is in. Above the bubble point, the system behaves as a single liquid phase; below, two phases coexist and relative permeability governs flow.

## 2.2.1 Key Operational Definitions

- **Initial Solution Gas-Oil Ratio ($R_{si}$):** Volume of gas dissolved (at STP) per unit volume of stock-tank oil originally in the reservoir.
- **Current Solution Gas-Oil Ratio ($R_s$):** Same definition, but evaluated at current reservoir pressure.
- **Instantaneous Producing Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR or $R_p$):** Ratio of gas volume to oil volume produced during a given production interval.
- **Cumulative GOR ($R_{pc}$):** Same ratio evaluated over cumulative production.
- **Gas Cap Ratio (m):** Ratio of initial gas cap volume to initial oil volume (both in reservoir conditions).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solution GOR | $R_s$ | From PVT lab or correlation (e.g., Standing) | scf/STB | Decreases as $P_r$ falls below $P_b$ |
| 2 | Producing GOR | $R_p$ | $\dfrac{\text{Cumulative gas produced (scf)}}{\text{Cumulative oil produced (STB)}}$ | scf/STB | Field-measured |
| 3 | Oil Formation Volume Factor | $B_o$ | $B_o = 0.9759 + 12 \times 10^{-5}(1.4 R_s + 60 T)^{0.5} \cdot \ldots$ (Standing) | rb/STB | Reservoir bbl per STB; $B_o \geq 1$ |
| 4 | Gas Formation Volume Factor | $B_g$ | $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{Z T}{P}$ | rcf/scf | Reservoir ft³ per scf |
| 5 | Two-Phase Formation Vol. Factor | $B_t$ | $B_t = B_o + (R_{si} - R_s) B_g$ | rb/STB | Used below bubble point |
| 6 | Gas compressibility factor | $Z$ | From Standing–Katz chart or EOS | dimensionless | Function of $P_{pr}, T_{pr}$ |
| 7 | Stock-tank gas-oil ratio | $R_{st}$ | Measured in lab differential liberation | scf/STB | Often $\approx R_{si}$ |
| 8 | Total surface gas from oil | $G_{tot}$ | $G_{tot} = N \cdot R_{si}$ | scf | $N$ = initial oil in place (STB) |
| 9 | Gas-cap gas in place | $G_{cap}$ | $G_{cap} = m \cdot N \cdot B_{oi} / B_{gi}$ | scf | Requires gas cap ratio $m$ |
| 10 | Total initial gas in place | $G_{total}$ | $G_{total} = N R_{si} + \dfrac{m N B_{oi}}{B_{gi}}$ | scf | Combined dissolved + free cap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion Reminders** (must commit to memory for KTU numericals):
> - **1 STB = 5.615 ft³** (reservoir volume conversion)
> - **1 MMscf = 10⁶ scf**
> - **1 Bcf = 10⁹ scf**
> - **1 Tcf = 10¹² scf**
> - **°R = °F + 459.67**
> - **psia = psig + 14.7**

## 2.4 The Real-World Engineering Utility

The associated gas framework is not merely academic — it underpins:

- **Reservoir management decisions:** Whether to produce gas cap gas immediately or reinject it for pressure maintenance.
- **Surface facility design:** Sizing of separators, gas processing trains, NGL recovery units.
- **Flaring minimization policy:** Global frameworks (e.g., World Bank "Zero Routine Flaring by 2030") target associated gas that is currently flared.
- **Carbon accounting:** Methane release from associated gas venting is a major greenhouse concern.
- **Gas-to-wire / CNG / LNG / GTL:** Conversion pathways that monetize stranded associated gas.
- **Petroleum economics:** Gas prices are typically lower than oil on energy-equivalent basis, making gas reinjection vs. sale a critical economic decision.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gas Formation Volume Factor ($B_g$) from Real Gas Law

The gas formation volume factor converts gas volume from surface (standard) conditions to reservoir conditions. It is the most fundamental identity in gas PVT.

**Starting point — Real Gas Equation of State:**

$$PV = ZnRT$$

Apply this to a fixed mass of gas at two states:
- **State 1 — Reservoir conditions:** $P_r, T_r, V_r, Z_r$
- **State 2 — Surface standard conditions:** $P_{sc}, T_{sc}, V_{sc}, Z_{sc} \approx 1.0$

**Step 1: Equate moles (same mass of gas).**

$$\dfrac{P_r V_r}{Z_r R T_r} = \dfrac{P_{sc} V_{sc}}{Z_{sc} R T_{sc}}$$

**Step 2: Cancel gas constant $R$ on both sides.**

$$\dfrac{P_r V_r}{Z_r T_r} = \dfrac{P_{sc} V_{sc}}{Z_{sc} T_{sc}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the ratio $V_r / V_{sc} = B_g$.**

$$B_g = \dfrac{V_r}{V_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z_r T_r \, P_{sc}}{Z_{sc} T_{sc} \, P_r}$$

**Step 4: Insert field-unit constants.**

- $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia
- $T_{sc} = 520$ °R
- $Z_{sc} \approx 1.0$ for ideal behavior at low standard pressure

$$B_g = \dfrac{Z_r T_r \times 14.7}{1.0 \times 520 \times P_r}$$

**Step 5: Simplify numerically.**

$$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{Z_r T_r}{P_r}$$

This yields $B_g$ in units of **rcf/scf** (reservoir cubic feet per standard cubic foot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $B_g$ tells us the volume "shrinkage" or "expansion" between reservoir and surface. For a high-pressure gas ($P_r \approx 4000$ psia, $T_r \approx 200$ °F, $Z \approx 0.85$):
>
> $$B_g \approx 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{0.85 \times 660}{4000} \approx 0.00397 \text{ rcf/scf}$$
>
> i.e., **~252 scf of surface gas come from 1 rcf of reservoir gas** — the enormous expansion that makes gas reservoirs so productive.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Initial Gas In Place in an Oil Reservoir

For a reservoir containing **both** dissolved gas and a free gas cap, the total initial gas in place combines two contributions.

**Step 1: Define initial oil in place in reservoir barrels.**

$$N \cdot B_{oi}$$

where $N$ is the stock-tank oil initially in place (STB) and $B_{oi}$ is the initial oil formation volume factor (rb/STB). This is the actual hydrocarbon pore volume occupied by the oil plus its dissolved gas.

**Step 2: Volume of free gas in the gas cap (in reservoir barrels).**

$$V_{gc} = m \cdot N \cdot B_{oi}$$

where $m$ is the **gas cap ratio** (dimensionless), defined as:

$$m = \dfrac{\text{Initial gas cap volume (rb)}}{\text{Initial oil zone volume (rb)}}$$

**Step 3: Convert gas cap volume from rb to scf using $B_{gi}$.**

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{V_{gc}}{B_{gi}} = \dfrac{m N B_{oi}}{B_{gi}}$$

**Step 4: Dissolved gas in oil (in scf).**

$$G_{diss} = N \cdot R_{si}$$

**Step 5: Sum the two contributions to obtain total initial gas in place.**

$$G_{total} = N R_{si} + \dfrac{m N B_{oi}}{B_{gi}}$$

**Step 6: Factor out $N$ for compactness.**

$$\boxed{G_{total} = N \left[ R_{si} + \dfrac{m B_{oi}}{B_{gi}} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** If $m = 0$ (undersaturated oil reservoir with no gas cap), this collapses to $G_{total} = N R_{si}$ — all gas is dissolved. If $R_{si} = 0$ (hypothetical), all gas is in the cap. The bracketed term is sometimes called the **total gas solubility factor** of the system.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Standing's Correlation for Bubble-Point Pressure

Standing (1947) developed an empirical correlation widely used for black-oil systems. The bubble-point pressure $P_b$ (psia) is:

$$P_b = 18.2 \left( R_s / \gamma_g \right)^{0.83} \cdot 10^{(0.00091 T - 0.0125 \gamma_{API})}$$

**Where:**
- $R_s$ = solution GOR (scf/STB)
- $\gamma_g$ = gas specific gravity (air = 1)
- $T$ = temperature (°F)
- $\gamma_{API}$ = oil API gravity

**Derivation Logic (Re-arrangement from Standing's original graphical fit):**

1. Standing observed that for many crude oils, $P_b$ correlates strongly with the molar concentration of light hydrocarbons dissolved in the oil.
2. He proposed that the **mole fraction of gas in the liquid phase** $\approx R_s / (\gamma_g \cdot \text{const})$ is the principal correlating parameter.
3. He then plotted $\log P_b$ against the combined term $(R_s/\gamma_g)$ and $T$ for hundreds of crude samples and found a near-linear trend, leading to the form above.
4. The $\gamma_{API}$ term corrects for oil composition — lighter oils (high API) hold more gas, so $P_b$ is higher for them.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are unlikely to be asked to derive Standing's correlation step-by-step. However, you **must** be able to compute $P_b$ given $R_s$, $\gamma_g$, $T$, and $\gamma_{API}$. Memorize the formula and the constant **18.2**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Surface GOR vs. Instantaneous GOR

The **instantaneous producing GOR** at any time during depletion can be expressed as:

$$R_p = R_s + \dfrac{k_{rg}}{k_{ro}} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_o B_o}{\mu_g B_g} + \text{(gas cap contribution)}$$

**Logic of each term:**

- $R_s$: gas dissolved in oil, liberated at surface → minimum possible GOR.
- Second term: **free gas flowing** from the oil zone (below bubble point). The ratio $k_{rg}/k_{ro}$ is the **relative permeability ratio**; this term rises rapidly as oil saturation drops.
- Third term (if present): free gas flowing from a **gas cap** in a commingled completion.

When $P_r > P_b$, the second and third terms vanish and $R_p = R_s$. As $P_r$ falls below $P_b$, $R_p$ rises monotonically — the classic **rising GOR signature** of a depleting oil reservoir.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Governing Equation | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Calculate gas-cap gas in place | $G_{cap} = m N B_{oi} / B_{gi}$ | Hydrocarbon pore volume known |
| Bubble-point pressure estimate | Standing's correlation | Black-oil; $T < 250$ °F |
| Two-phase FVF below $P_b$ | $B_t = B_o + (R_{si} - R_s) B_g$ | Differential liberation data |
| Total dissolved gas at discovery | $G_{diss} = N R_{si}$ | Volumetric reservoir |
| Material balance gas drive | Havlena–Odeh straight line | $F = N E_o + G E_g$ |
| Separator gas-oil ratio | Field measurement | One or more stage separation |
