# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Reservoir Architecture Containing Associated Gas

```mermaid
graph TD
    surf[Surface Production Facility]
    sep[Two or Three Stage Separator]
    stock[Stock Tank Oil Storage]
    gasProc[Gas Processing and NGL Recovery]
    reinj[Gas Reinjection Compressor]
    flare[Emergency Flare Stack]
    well[Production Wellbore]
    resTop[Reservoir Top Seal Cap Rock]
    gasCap[Free Gas Cap Zone]
    oilZone[Oil Zone With Dissolved Gas]
    waterZone[Bottom Aquifer Water Zone]
    resBot[Reservoir Base]
    surf --> sep
    sep --> stock
    sep --> gasProc
    gasProc --> reinj
    gasProc --> flare
    well --> surf
    resTop --> gasCap
    gasCap --> oilZone
    oilZone --> waterZone
    waterZone --> resBot
    oilZone --> well
    gasCap --> well
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **reservoir** is shown as a vertical sequence of three fluid zones: gas cap on top, oil with dissolved gas in the middle, and bottom water aquifer.
- The **production well** is perforated in the oil zone; under primary depletion, gas from both the cap and the solution gas evolves into the wellbore.
- At the **surface**, the separator splits the stream into stabilized oil (stock tank), sales gas (after processing), and an emergency flare path.
- **Reinjection** returns part of the produced gas back to the gas cap or an injection well for pressure maintenance.

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Surface Handling of Associated Gas

```mermaid
graph LR
    wellH[Wellhead Xmas Tree]
    man1[High Pressure Manifold]
    sepHP[High Pressure Separator]
    sepLP[Low Pressure Separator]
    sepStab[Stabilizer Column]
    oilOut[Sales Crude to Tank Farm]
    compIn[Gas Compressor Inlet Scrubber]
    comp[Compressor Train]
    dehyd[Gas Dehydration TEG Unit]
    sweeten[Acid Gas Removal Amine Unit]
    ngl[NGL Recovery Turboexpander]
    salesGas[Sales Gas Pipeline to Customer]
    reinjWell[Reinjection Well or Gas Cap Disposal]
    flareSys[Flare System]
    wellH --> man1
    man1 --> sepHP
    sepHP --> sepLP
    sepLP --> sepStab
    sepStab --> oilOut
    sepHP --> compIn
    compIn --> comp
    comp --> dehyd
    dehyd --> sweeten
    sweeten --> ngl
    ngl --> salesGas
    ngl --> reinjWell
    ngl --> flareSys
```

**Interpretation:**

- The flow trains show how **associated gas**, after separation from oil, is processed for sales, NGL recovery, or reinjection.
- The **compressor** is the most energy-intensive unit because $B_g$ values near 0.003 rcf/scf mean enormous volumes must be moved.
- The **TEG dehydration** and **amine sweetening** units remove water and acid gases (H₂S, CO₂) — these are mandatory because associated gas from oil fields tends to be wetter and more corrosive than non-associated dry gas.

## 4.3 Phase Diagram — Conceptual P-T Diagram for an Oil Reservoir

```mermaid
graph TD
    critPt[Critical Point of the Reservoir Fluid]
    cricon[Phase Envelope Cricondentherm]
    criconB[Phase Envelope Cricondenbar]
    bpLine[Bubble Point Curve]
    dpLine[Dew Point Curve]
    reg1[Single Phase Liquid Region Above Pb]
    reg2[Two Phase Liquid plus Vapor Region Inside Envelope]
    reg3[Single Phase Vapor Region Below P]
    resPath[Isothermal Reservoir Pressure Decline Path]
    initPt[Initial Reservoir Pressure]
    bpInt[Intersection With Bubble Point Curve]
    critPt --> reg1
    critPt --> reg3
    bpLine --> critPt
    dpLine --> critPt
    bpLine --> reg2
    dpLine --> reg2
    cricon --> reg2
    criconB --> reg2
    reg1 --> reg2
    reg2 --> reg3
    initPt --> bpInt
    bpInt --> reg2
```

**Interpretation of the conceptual phase envelope:**

- The **bubble-point curve** separates the single-phase liquid region (above) from the two-phase region (below).
- The **initial reservoir point** sits *on* the bubble-point curve if the oil is saturated; *above* it if undersaturated.
- As **reservoir pressure declines** along the isothermal path, the fluid enters the two-phase envelope, liberating free gas — the essence of associated gas evolution.

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid block above is a **block-level functional architecture** rendering of the phase envelope concept. A true P-T sketch with continuous curves cannot be rendered in mermaid; hence the discrete-region representation is used as the diagram fallback per KTU diagram policy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define associated gas. How does it differ from non-associated gas?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Associated gas** is natural gas that co-exists with crude oil in the same reservoir, either as gas dissolved in the oil under reservoir pressure or as a free gas cap above the oil column.

**Key differences from non-associated gas:**

1. **Reservoir fluid:** Associated gas occurs with oil; non-associated gas occurs in a gas-only reservoir.
2. **Composition:** Associated gas is richer in C₂⁺ hydrocarbons and tends to be wetter; non-associated gas is leaner, predominantly methane.
3. **Recovery driver:** Associated gas production is a by-product of oil recovery; non-associated gas recovery is the primary objective.
4. **Phase behavior:** Associated gas shows bubble-point / dew-point behavior coupled with oil; non-associated gas is a single-phase fluid.
5. **Surface processing:** Associated gas requires NGL recovery and gas-handling infrastructure tied to oil operations; non-associated gas plants are simpler.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the differences]**

---

### Question 2: What is solution gas-oil ratio ($R_s$)? State its typical range for light crude oils.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Solution gas-oil ratio ($R_s$)** is the volume of gas (measured at standard surface conditions, scf) dissolved in one stock-tank barrel of oil at a given reservoir pressure and temperature.

$$R_s = \dfrac{V_{gas}^{sc}}{V_{oil}^{STB}}$$

- It is expressed in **scf/STB**.
- $R_s$ **increases** with reservoir pressure (more gas can dissolve at higher pressure).
- $R_s$ **decreases** with rising temperature.
- $R_s$ **increases** with API gravity (lighter oils dissolve more gas).

**Typical ranges:**
- Light crude oil ($\gamma_{API} > 35$): $R_s \approx 1000 - 2500$ scf/STB
- Medium crude ($25 < \gamma_{API} < 35$): $R_s \approx 500 - 1000$ scf/STB
- Heavy crude ($\gamma_{API} < 25$): $R_s < 500$ scf/STB
- Volatile oil: $R_s > 2000$ scf/STB

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the formula/units, 1 for the typical range]**

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the three pressure regimes of an oil reservoir containing associated gas. Show how the producing GOR behaves in each regime. Derive the relationship between total initial gas in place, oil in place, and gas cap ratio.**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Three Pressure Regimes — Explanation (3 marks):**

A producing oil reservoir goes through three regimes as reservoir pressure $P_r$ declines:

- **Regime 1 — $P_r > P_b$ (Undersaturated / Above Bubble Point):** All gas is dissolved in the oil; no free gas exists in the reservoir. The reservoir behaves as a single-phase liquid system. The producing GOR is constant and equal to the initial solution GOR $R_{si}$.

- **Regime 2 — $P_r = P_b$ (Saturated / At Bubble Point):** The oil is at its saturation limit. The first bubble of free gas nucleates. Producing GOR is still approximately $R_{si}$ since the system has just begun liberating gas.

- **Regime 3 — $P_r < P_b$ (Two-Phase / Below Bubble Point):** Gas continuously evolves from the oil. The free gas saturation builds, eventually becoming mobile. Producing GOR rises sharply as both dissolved-gas liberation and free-gas flow contribute. If a gas cap exists, its contribution further inflates GOR.

**(ii) Producing GOR Behaviour (2 marks):**

A sketch of GOR vs. $P_r$ shows:
- Flat plateau at $R_{si}$ for $P_r > P_b$.
- A small inflection at $P_b$.
- A rising curve for $P_r < P_b$, increasing monotonically as oil mobility declines and gas mobility rises.

**(iii) Derivation — Total Initial Gas In Place (2 marks):**

Initial oil in place occupies $N \cdot B_{oi}$ reservoir barrels. The gas cap occupies $m \cdot N \cdot B_{oi}$ reservoir barrels (by definition of gas cap ratio $m$).

Converting the gas cap volume to surface scf using $B_{gi}$:

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{m N B_{oi}}{B_{gi}}$$

Dissolved gas in oil:

$$G_{diss} = N R_{si}$$

Total initial gas in place:

$$\boxed{G_{total} = N \left[ R_{si} + \dfrac{m B_{oi}}{B_{gi}} \right]}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A volumetric oil reservoir has the following data: initial oil in place $N = 100$ MMSTB; initial solution GOR $R_{si} = 800$ scf/STB; gas cap ratio $m = 0.3$; initial oil formation volume factor $B_{oi} = 1.4$ rb/STB; initial gas formation volume factor $B_{gi} = 0.005$ rcf/scf. Calculate: (i) total dissolved gas in place, (ii) free gas cap gas in place, and (iii) total initial gas in place.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 100 \times 10^6$ STB
- $R_{si} = 800$ scf/STB
- $m = 0.3$
- $B_{oi} = 1.4$ rb/STB
- $B_{gi} = 0.005$ rcf/scf

**(i) Total dissolved gas in place (2 marks):**

$$G_{diss} = N \cdot R_{si}$$

$$G_{diss} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 800$$

$$G_{diss} = 8 \times 10^{10} \text{ scf}$$

$$G_{diss} = 80{,}000 \text{ MMscf} = 80 \text{ Bcf}$$

**(ii) Free gas cap gas in place (2 marks):**

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{m N B_{oi}}{B_{gi}}$$

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 1.4}{0.005}$$

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 1.4}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$G_{cap} = \dfrac{42 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$G_{cap} = 8.4 \times 10^9 \text{ scf}$$

$$G_{cap} = 8{,}400 \text{ MMscf} = 8.4 \text{ Bcf}$$

**(iii) Total initial gas in place (2 marks):**

$$G_{total} = G_{diss} + G_{cap}$$

$$G_{total} = 80{,}000 + 8{,}400$$

$$G_{total} = 88{,}400 \text{ MMscf} = 88.4 \text{ Bcf}$$

**Alternatively using the compact formula:**

$$G_{total} = 100 \times 10^6 \left[ 800 + \dfrac{0.3 \times 1.4}{0.005} \right]$$

$$G_{total} = 100 \times 10^6 \left[ 800 + 84 \right]$$

$$G_{total} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 884$$

$$G_{total} = 88.4 \times 10^9 \text{ scf} = 88.4 \text{ Bcf}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark for units consistency):**
- $G_{diss} = 80$ Bcf
- $G_{cap} = 8.4$ Bcf
- $G_{total} = 88.4$ Bcf

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Students often write $B_{gi}$ in rb/scf instead of rcf/scf. Always confirm the unit of each formation volume factor before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting $B_{oi}$:** The dissolved-gas contribution does *not* need $B_{oi}$ because $R_{si}$ is already in surface conditions. But the gas-cap volume *does* need $B_{oi}$ to convert STB-oil volume to reservoir-bbl gas-cap volume.
> 3. **Reporting $G_{cap}$ without converting from scf to Bcf/MMscf** loses the unit mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative choice]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the field separation of associated gas from crude oil. Explain the role of multi-stage separation in maximizing liquid recovery. State Standing's correlation for bubble-point pressure and explain each term.**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Field Separation Process (2.5 marks):**

When reservoir fluid (oil + dissolved gas) enters the wellbore and rises to the surface, the pressure drops continuously. At the **wellhead**, the stream is diverted to a **high-pressure separator** where the bulk of the free gas is flashed off at elevated pressure. The oil then flows to a **low-pressure separator** where additional gas is liberated. Finally, the oil passes to a **stock tank** at near-atmospheric pressure, releasing the last traces of solution gas.

A typical train is:

**Wellhead → HP Separator → LP Separator → Stock Tank → Sales Oil**

Some plants use a **three-stage separation** with an intermediate stage between LP and stock tank to maximize liquid recovery.

**(ii) Multi-Stage Separation Maximizes Liquid Recovery (2.5 marks):**

In a **single-stage** flash to atmospheric pressure, the sudden pressure drop causes significant gas liberation within the oil, entraining light ends ($\text{C}_1$ to $\text{C}_5$) with the gas and reducing liquid yield.

In **multi-stage** separation, the pressure drop is **gradual** (e.g., 600 psia → 150 psia → 50 psia → 14.7 psia). At each intermediate stage, the gas separated is rich in heavier hydrocarbons; these are typically recovered as **NGL**. The remaining oil is more stable and retains a higher proportion of light ends in the liquid phase, increasing stock-tank oil volume.

Quantitative result: Multi-stage separation can increase stock-tank oil recovery by **3 – 8 %** compared to single-stage flash in volatile-oil systems.

**(iii) Standing's Correlation for $P_b$ (2 marks):**

$$P_b = 18.2 \left( \dfrac{R_s}{\gamma_g} \right)^{0.83} \cdot 10^{(0.00091 T - 0.0125 \gamma_{API})}$$

**Term-by-term explanation:**
- **$R_s$ (scf/STB):** Solution gas-oil ratio. Higher $R_s$ means more dissolved gas, which raises the saturation pressure.
- **$\gamma_g$ (dimensionless):** Gas specific gravity relative to air. Heavier gas (higher $\gamma_g$) is more soluble, so for a fixed $R_s$, $P_b$ is lower.
- **$T$ (°F):** Reservoir temperature. Higher temperature reduces gas solubility, raising $P_b$.
- **$\gamma_{API}$ (°API):** Oil API gravity. Lighter oil (higher API) dissolves more gas, raising $P_b$.
- **Constants 18.2, 0.83, 0.00091, 0.0125:** Empirical fit coefficients from Standing's 1947 dataset of California crudes.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A reservoir fluid has $R_s = 1000$ scf/STB, gas specific gravity $\gamma_g = 0.7$, reservoir temperature $T = 180$ °F, and oil API gravity $\gamma_{API} = 35$. Estimate the bubble-point pressure using Standing's correlation. Comment on the reservoir's saturation state if the initial reservoir pressure is 2500 psia.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R_s = 1000$ scf/STB
- $\gamma_g = 0.7$
- $T = 180$ °F
- $\gamma_{API} = 35$
- $P_r^{initial} = 2500$ psia

**Standing's correlation:**

$$P_b = 18.2 \left( \dfrac{R_s}{\gamma_g} \right)^{0.83} \cdot 10^{(0.00091 T - 0.0125 \gamma_{API})}$$

**Step 1: Compute $R_s / \gamma_g$ (1 mark).**

$$\dfrac{R_s}{\gamma_g} = \dfrac{1000}{0.7} = 1428.57$$

**Step 2: Raise to power 0.83 (1 mark).**

$$(1428.57)^{0.83}$$

Take logarithms: $\log_{10}(1428.57) = 3.1549$

$$0.83 \times 3.1549 = 2.6186$$

$$10^{2.6186} = 415.5$$

**Step 3: Compute the temperature–API exponent (1.5 marks).**

$$0.00091 \cdot T = 0.00091 \times 180 = 0.1638$$

$$0.0125 \cdot \gamma_{API} = 0.0125 \times 35 = 0.4375$$

$$\text{Exponent} = 0.1638 - 0.4375 = -0.2737$$

$$10^{-0.2737} = 0.532$$

**Step 4: Multiply all terms (1.5 marks).**

$$P_b = 18.2 \times 415.5 \times 0.532$$

$$P_b = 18.2 \times 221.05$$

$$\boxed{P_b \approx 4023 \text{ psia}}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark):**

Since $P_r^{initial} = 2500 \text{ psia} < P_b = 4023$ psia, the reservoir is **undersaturated** at discovery. This means:

- No free gas cap initially exists in the oil zone.
- All gas is dissolved in the oil.
- As production proceeds and $P_r$ falls, it will reach $P_b$ (4023 psia) only after substantial pressure decline — at which point free gas will begin to evolve.
- The reservoir will produce with constant GOR = $R_{si}$ until $P_r$ reaches 4023 psia.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Calculation Errors:**
> 1. **Sign error in the exponent:** A common mistake is to write $(0.0125 \gamma_{API} - 0.00091 T)$ instead of the correct $(0.00091 T - 0.0125 \gamma_{API})$. The temperature *raises* $P_b$, while API *lowers* it (within the exponent).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $T$ to °F:** The formula uses °F, not °C or °R.
> 3. **Power-of-10 vs. natural exponential confusion:** The $10^{(\cdots)}$ form is critical — using $e^{(\cdots)}$ gives an answer off by a factor of ~2.3.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Associated gas** = natural gas co-existing with crude oil in the same reservoir, either **dissolved** in the oil or as a **free gas cap** above the oil.
- Three **pressure regimes** govern its behavior: **undersaturated** ($P_r > P_b$), **saturated** ($P_r = P_b$), **two-phase** ($P_r < P_b$).
- **Bubble-point pressure $P_b$** is the cornerstone: it separates single-phase liquid from two-phase behavior.
- **Solution GOR $R_s$** is the principal correlating parameter for $P_b$; use **Standing's correlation** when no lab data is available.
- **Producing GOR $R_p$** equals $R_{si}$ above $P_b$ and **rises monotonically** below $P_b$ due to free-gas evolution and cap-gas production.
- **Gas cap ratio $m$** quantifies the size of the free gas cap relative to the oil zone.
- **Total initial gas in place** combines dissolved and free contributions: $G_{total} = N R_{si} + m N B_{oi} / B_{gi}$.
- **Gas formation volume factor** $B_g = 0.02827 \, Z T / P$ is essential for any volumetric gas-in-place calculation.
- Associated gas is **wetter** (richer in C₂⁺) than non-associated gas, requiring **NGL recovery** and **gas processing** infrastructure.
- **Field separation** is multi-stage to maximize liquid recovery — typically HP → LP → Stock Tank, optionally with a third intermediate stage.
- **Conservation** of associated gas (re-injection for pressure maintenance) is favored when oil recovery is the primary economic driver.
- **Industry standards:** Use **scf/STB** for GOR, **psia/°F** for pressure/temperature in field-unit problems, and convert carefully to **MMscf, Bcf, or Tcf** for reserve reporting.
- **Standing's formula** is the most-cited empirical $P_b$ correlation in KTU examinations; memorize $P_b = 18.2 (R_s/\gamma_g)^{0.83} \cdot 10^{(0.00091 T - 0.0125 \gamma_{API})}$.
- **Examination mantra:** "Identify the regime first (above or below $P_b$), then select the right equation." This single habit eliminates most of the unit-conversion and formula-substitution errors that cost KTU students 2–3 marks per numerical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Associated Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Associated Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Associated Gas (NAG):** Natural gas that occurs in subsurface reservoirs which contain **little or no crude oil** at the time of discovery, OR natural gas that exists in a geological formation that is entirely separate from any oil-bearing zone. The hydrocarbon stream is produced as essentially **dry (or near-dry) gas**, free from significant liquid hydrocarbon co-production at reservoir conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification hierarchy, NAG is treated as a **reservoir-based taxonomy** (i.e., based on the *mode of occurrence* of gas in the geological trap), distinct from compositional taxonomies such as *dry/wet* or *lean/rich* gas.

### Sub-classes of Non-Associated Gas

| Sub-class | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Gas-Cap Gas (Free Gas)** | Gas occupying the pore space *above* the oil-water contact in a reservoir that has an underlying oil leg, but the gas has migrated into a *separate* formation or is produced from a well completed *above* the gas-oil contact. |
| **Non-Associated Gas (Pure)** | Gas produced from a reservoir that contains **no oil leg whatsoever** — a single-phase gas reservoir from inception. |
| **Sub-Qualified NAG** | A small subset of wells that initially produce only gas but may develop a thin retrograde condensate at later stages. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Single-Tenant vs. Mixed-Use Building":**
>
> Imagine a high-rise building. An **Associated Gas** reservoir is like a *mixed-use building* — oil and gas share the same underground "apartment" (pore space). They are co-tenants; if you evict one, the other is affected (pressure depletion behavior is coupled). A **Non-Associated Gas** reservoir is like a *single-tenant office* — gas alone occupies the entire "building." There is no oil co-tenant, so production, pressure decline, and material balance calculations are *standalone* and far simpler. Just as a single-tenant lease is easier to manage, an NAG reservoir is the cleanest, most predictable system for the production engineer.

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants

The following reference values must be memorized for KTU numerical problems:

- **Standard Pressure, $P_{sc}$ = 14.7 psia (101.325 kPa)**
- **Standard Temperature, $T_{sc}$ = 60 °F = 520 °R (288.71 K)**
- **Universal Gas Constant, $R$ = 10.732 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)**
- **Molecular Weight of Air, $M_{air}$ = 28.97 lbm/lbmol**
- **Molecular Weight of Methane, $M_{C_1}$ = 16.04 lbm/lbmol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:**
> The Gas-to-Oil Ratio (GOR) for a *pure* NAG reservoir is **mathematically infinite** ($\text{GOR} \to \infty$) because the oil production rate, $q_o \to 0$. The surface GOR of NAG is therefore reported as the **condensate-gas ratio (CGR)** in STB/MMscf, often < 5 STB/MMscf for lean NAG.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why NAG Behaves Differently

The behaviour of a Non-Associated Gas reservoir is governed by the following sequential logic steps:

1. **Single-Phase Initialization:** At discovery pressure $P_i$, the reservoir contains only hydrocarbon gas (plus possibly formation water in the aquifer, but no mobile oil).
2. **Pressure-Dependent Properties:** As production begins, reservoir pressure $P$ declines. Because gas density and viscosity are strong functions of $P$ and $T$, deliverability is *not* linear with time (unlike oil wells, which often exhibit near-constant productivity for years).
3. **Retrograde Behaviour (Optional):** For *rich* NAG, isotherms may dip into the two-phase envelope — liquid dropout can occur in the reservoir even though the surface stream is "dry."
4. **Drive Mechanism:** The dominant drive is **gas expansion** (with optional weak water drive). Recovery factors typically range from **70–90 %** for volumetric gas reservoirs — significantly higher than for oil reservoirs (~ 30–40 %).
5. **Compressibility Dominance:** Gas compressibility $c_g$ is orders of magnitude higher than oil/water compressibility, which is why a small pressure drop yields a large volume of gas at the surface.
6. **Surface Processing Simplicity (For Lean NAG):** Since liquids yield is low, the surface train is simpler — primarily dehydration and sour-gas removal (if applicable) — before pipeline entry.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the *core numerical toolkit* for Module 2 problems. Mastery of these is mandatory for both Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Equation (LaTeX) | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Real Gas Law** | $PV = ZnRT$ | $P$ [psia], $V$ [ft³], $Z$ [-], $n$ [lbmol], $R$ = 10.732, $T$ [°R] | Foundation of all gas PVT |
| 2 | **Gas Density** | $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM}{ZRT}$ | $\rho_g$ [lbm/ft³], $M$ [lbm/lbmol] | Mass-per-volume calculations |
| 3 | **Specific Gravity of Gas** | $\gamma_g = \dfrac{M_g}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{M_g}{28.97}$ | $\gamma_g$ [-] | Used in correlations for $Z$, viscosity |
| 4 | **Apparent Molecular Weight** | $M_g = \sum_i y_i M_i$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction, $M_i$ = mol. wt. | Composition-based property |
| 5 | **Gas Formation Volume Factor** | $B_g = \dfrac{V_{res}}{V_{sc}} = 0.02827 \dfrac{zT}{P}$ | $B_g$ [res ft³/scf], $T$ in °R, $P$ in psia | Reserves & material balance |
| 6 | **Gas Compressibility (Real)** | $c_g = \dfrac{1}{P} - \dfrac{1}{Z}\left(\dfrac{\partial Z}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $c_g$ [psi⁻¹] | Pressure-transient, reserves |
| 7 | **Pseudocritical Temperature (Sutton, Pure Hydrocarbon)** | $T_{pc} = 168 + 325\gamma_g - 12.5\gamma_g^{\,2}$ | $T_{pc}$ [°R] | Standing-Katz $Z$-chart input |
| 8 | **Pseudocritical Pressure (Sutton)** | $P_{pc} = 677 + 15\gamma_g - 37.5\gamma_g^{\,2}$ | $P_{pc}$ [psia] | Standing-Katz $Z$-chart input |
| 9 | **Pseudoreduced Properties** | $T_{pr} = \dfrac{T}{T_{pc}}, \qquad P_{pr} = \dfrac{P}{P_{pc}}$ | dimensionless | Input to Standing-Katz chart |
| 10 | **Volumetric Gas In Place** | $G = \dfrac{43{,}560 \, \phi (1-S_{wi}) \, V_b}{B_{gi}}$ | $G$ [scf], $\phi$ [-], $S_{wi}$ [-], $V_b$ [acre·ft] | Reserves estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations 1–4 underpin *every* property calculation; Equations 5–6 convert reservoir conditions to surface conditions (the heart of gas accounting); Equations 7–9 are the *gateway* to obtaining the compressibility factor $Z$ from the Standing-Katz chart (the single most-tested topic in KTU Module 2).

## 2.3 Contrast Table — Associated vs. Non-Associated Gas

| Property | Associated Gas | Non-Associated Gas |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Reservoir Content** | Co-existing with oil | Gas alone |
| **GOR (surface)** | Finite, e.g., 500–5000 scf/STB | Effectively infinite (CGR < 5) |
| **Phase at $P_i$** | Solution gas in oil | Single-phase gas |
| **Drive Mechanism** | Solution gas drive, water drive | Gas expansion, weak water drive |
| **Surface Stream** | Wet (rich in liquids) | Mostly dry (lean) |
| **Processing Complexity** | High (NGL recovery needed) | Low–Moderate |
| **Recovery Factor** | 20–40 % (oil) | 70–90 % (gas) |
| **Pressure Profile vs. Time** | Bubble-point controlled | Monotonic decline |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Gas Formation Volume Factor ($B_g$)

This derivation is a *guaranteed* 14-mark KTU question. Every step is shown below.

### Starting Point: The Real Gas Equation

$$PV = ZnRT$$

### Step 1 — Express the Equation at Reservoir Conditions

At reservoir pressure $P$ and reservoir temperature $T$ (with compressibility $Z$), the volume occupied by $n$ lbmol of gas is:

$$V_{res} = \frac{Z \, n \, R \, T}{P} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Same Equation at Standard Surface Conditions

At standard conditions, $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia and $T_{sc} = 520$ °R. The same $n$ moles of gas occupy:

$$V_{sc} = \frac{Z_{sc} \, n \, R \, T_{sc}}{P_{sc}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

For most practical gas-engineering work, the gas at standard conditions is taken to be **ideal**, so $Z_{sc} \approx 1.0$.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio $B_g = V_{res} / V_{sc}$

$$B_g = \frac{V_{res}}{V_{sc}} = \frac{Z \, n \, R \, T \, / \, P}{Z_{sc} \, n \, R \, T_{sc} \, / \, P_{sc}}$$

Cancel $n$ and $R$:

$$B_g = \frac{Z \, T \, P_{sc}}{Z_{sc} \, T_{sc} \, P}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Standard-Condition Constants

With $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R, and $Z_{sc} = 1$:

$$B_g = \frac{Z \, T \, (14.7)}{(1)(520) \, P}$$

$$B_g = \frac{14.7}{520} \cdot \frac{Z \, T}{P} = 0.028269 \cdot \frac{Z \, T}{P}$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,B_g = 0.02827 \,\dfrac{Z \, T}{P}\, \quad \text{[res ft}^3\text{/scf]}\,}$$

where $T$ is in **°R** and $P$ is in **psia**. The conversion logic: $14.7/520 = 0.02827$ is the *constant-of-the-units* that reconciles °R and psia into the field-standard cubic-foot basis.

### Step 6 — Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Style)

> **Given:** A non-associated gas reservoir at $P = 2000$ psia, $T = 180$ °F, with $Z = 0.92$.
> **Find:** $B_g$ in res ft³/scf.

**Solution:**

Step A — Convert temperature to absolute:

$$T = 180 + 460 = 640\ \text{°R}$$

Step B — Substitute into the $B_g$ equation:

$$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{(0.92)(640)}{2000}$$

Step C — Simplify the numerator:

$$0.02827 \cdot \frac{588.8}{2000} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.2944$$

Step D — Compute the final value:

$$B_g = 0.008323\ \text{res ft}^3\text{/scf}$$

Step E — Physical interpretation:
A 1-scf volume of gas at the surface expands to occupy approximately **8.32 × 10⁻³ ft³** in the reservoir (i.e., the *surface* volume is roughly **120× larger** than the reservoir volume for this lean NAG — this is why gas reserves are quoted in scf, not reservoir volumes).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NAG vs. Other Natural-Gas Categories

| Criterion | **Non-Associated Gas (NAG)** | Associated Gas (AG) | Coal-Bed Methane (CBM) | Shale Gas |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| **Trap Mechanism** | Structural/stratigraphic | Structural w/ oil | Adsorption in coal | Adsorption + free in shale |
| **Primary Phase in Reservoir** | Free gas | Dissolved in oil | Adsorbed gas | Adsorbed + free |
| **Initial $S_o$ in Gas Zone** | $\approx 0$ | $> 0$ (oil leg) | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ |
| **Typical $\gamma_g$** | 0.55 – 0.70 | 0.65 – 1.0 | $\sim 0.55$ – 0.60 | 0.58 – 0.68 |
| **Surface $C_1$ Content** | > 90 mol% | 60 – 80 mol% | > 95 mol% | 80 – 95 mol% |
| **Typical Depth** | 1500 – 6000 m | 1000 – 4000 m | 300 – 1500 m | 1500 – 4000 m |
| **Recovery Factor** | 70 – 90 % | Gas cap: 60 %; Soln: 30–50 % | 50 – 80 % | 15 – 30 % |
| **Production Profile** | Plateau + steep decline | Coupled to oil rate | Long dewatering period | Long, flat decline |
| **Primary Surface Process** | Dehydration, sweetening | NGL recovery + sweetening | Water removal, compression | NGL recovery, compression |
| **KTU Module Mapping** | **Module 2 (focus topic)** | Module 1 / 2 | Module 5 (Unconventional) | Module 5 (Unconventional) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Classification of Natural Gas

The diagram below maps the **functional relationships** in the natural-gas classification hierarchy, with Non-Associated Gas (the KTU Module 2 focus topic) highlighted as the central sub-tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NG_ROOT[Natural Gas Reserves]
    NG_ROOT --> A_CRIT[By Reservoir Association]
    NG_ROOT --> B_CRIT[By Hydrocarbon Composition]
    NG_ROOT --> C_CRIT[By H2S CO2 Content]

    A_CRIT --> NAG_MAIN[Non Associated Gas NAG]
    A_CRIT --> AG_MAIN[Associated Gas AG]
    A_CRIT --> DCG_MAIN[Gas Cap Gas GCG]

    NAG_MAIN --> NAG_TYPE_A[Pure Gas Reservoir No Oil Leg]
    NAG_MAIN --> NAG_TYPE_B[Free Gas Cap Over Distant Oil Leg]
    NAG_MAIN --> NAG_TYPE_C[Retrograde Condensate Reservoir]

    B_CRIT --> DRY_G[Dry Gas Mostly Methane]
    B_CRIT --> WET_G[Wet Gas Significant C2 Plus]
    B_CRIT --> LEAN_G[Lean Gas Low CGR]
    B_CRIT --> RICH_G[Rich Gas High NGL Yield]

    C_CRIT --> SWEET_G[Sweet Gas H2S less than 4 ppm]
    C_CRIT --> SOUR_G[Sour Gas H2S above 4 ppm]

    NAG_MAIN --> NAG_PROPS[Typical NAG Properties]
    NAG_PROPS --> PROP1[Apparent Mol Wt 17 to 22]
    NAG_PROPS --> PROP2[Specific Gravity 0.58 to 0.75]
    NAG_PROPS --> PROP3[Methane Mole Fraction 0.85 to 0.98]
    NAG_PROPS --> PROP4[Surface GOR effectively Infinite]
    NAG_PROPS --> PROP5[Heating Value 900 to 1100 BTU per scf]

    NAG_MAIN --> NAG_PROC[Surface Processing Train]
    NAG_PROC --> PROC1[Inlet Separation]
    NAG_PROC --> PROC2[Acid Gas Removal Amine Unit]
    NAG_PROC --> PROC3[Dehydration TEG or Molecular Sieve]
    NAG_PROC --> PROC4[Compression and Pipeline Delivery]

    classDef nagNode fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef procNode fill:#D6EFFF,stroke:#1E5A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef propNode fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class NAG_MAIN,NAG_TYPE_A,NAG_TYPE_B,NAG_TYPE_C nagNode
    class NAG_PROC,PROC1,PROC2,PROC3,PROC4 procNode
    class NAG_PROPS,PROP1,PROP2,PROP3,PROP4,PROP5 propNode
```

**How to read this diagram:** The root node (Natural Gas Reserves) branches into **three orthogonal classification criteria** (reservoir association, hydrocarbon composition, and acid-gas content). Non-Associated Gas occupies the *first* branch and is further decomposed into three reservoir types and a downstream processing train. The colour coding (amber = NAG, blue = processing, green = properties) enables rapid visual recall during KTU viva and written exams.

## 4.2 Mermaid — NAG Value Chain (Source-to-Pipeline Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RSVR[Gas Reservoir Single Phase]
    RSVR --> WELL[Production Wellbore Multiphase Flow]
    WELL --> SEP[Inlet Separator 3 Phase]
    SEP --> ACID[Acid Gas Removal H2S and CO2]
    ACID --> DEHY[Dehydration TEG Contactor]
    DEHY --> COMP[Compressor Station]
    COMP --> METER[Sales Meter Custody Transfer]
    METER --> PIPE[Trunk Pipeline to End User]
    PIPE --> END1[Power Generation]
    PIPE --> END2[City Gas Distribution CGD]
    PIPE --> END3[Petrochemical Feedstock]
    PIPE --> END4[LNG Liquefaction]

    classDef nrmlNode fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#444444,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    classDef nlinkNode fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class RSVR,WELL,SEP,ACID,DEHY,COMP,METER,PIPE nrmlNode
    class END1,END2,END3,END4 nlinkNode
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Define non-associated gas. How does it differ from associated gas in terms of (i) reservoir content, (ii) surface GOR, and (iii) recovery factor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Non-Associated Gas (NAG) is natural gas produced from a reservoir that contains little or no crude oil — it exists either in a single-phase gas reservoir or in a gas cap where the gas is produced from wells completed *above* the gas-oil contact, with the oil leg in a separate geological formation or zone.
>
> **(i) Reservoir Content (1 mark):** NAG reservoirs contain only hydrocarbon gas (and formation water), whereas associated-gas reservoirs contain a co-existing oil leg with gas either dissolved in the oil or as a gas cap *in direct contact* with the oil column.
>
> **(ii) Surface GOR (0.5 mark):** For pure NAG, the surface GOR is effectively *infinite* because no oil is co-produced ($q_o \to 0$). For associated gas, GOR is finite and typically ranges from 500 to 5000 scf/STB.
>
> **(iii) Recovery Factor (0.5 mark):** NAG reservoirs exhibit very high recovery factors of **70–90 %** (driven by gas expansion), while associated gas is recovered at the much lower rate of the coupled oil system, typically **20–40 %**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**A non-associated gas sample has the following molar composition: CH₄ = 90 %, C₂H₆ = 5 %, C₃H₈ = 3 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, CO₂ = 0.5 %. Calculate (i) the apparent molecular weight $M_g$ and (ii) the specific gravity $\gamma_g$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **(i) Apparent Molecular Weight (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> M_g &= \sum_i y_i \, M_i \\
> &= (0.90)(16.04) + (0.05)(30.07) + (0.03)(44.10) + (0.015)(28.01) + (0.005)(44.01) \\
> &= 14.436 + 1.5035 + 1.323 + 0.4202 + 0.2201 \\
> &= 17.903\ \text{lbm/lbmol}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **(ii) Specific Gravity (1 mark):**
>
> $$\gamma_g = \frac{M_g}{M_{air}} = \frac{17.903}{28.97} = 0.618$$
>
> **Conclusion:** This is a **lean, dry NAG** (typical $\gamma_g \approx 0.55$–$0.70$).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 7 Marks

**Classify natural gas in detail. With a neat classification tree, explain the position and key sub-types of non-associated gas.**

**Model Solution:**

> **1. Classification of Natural Gas (4 marks):**
>
> Natural gas is classified along three independent axes:
>
> - **A. By Reservoir Association:**
>   - *Associated Gas (AG):* Gas in contact with oil in the same reservoir; comes out of solution as reservoir pressure drops below bubble-point.
>   - *Non-Associated Gas (NAG):* Gas in a reservoir with no (or negligible) oil; produced as free gas.
>   - *Dissolved Gas:* Gas that remains dissolved in the oil phase at reservoir conditions.
>   - *Gas-Cap Gas:* Free gas in the upper portion of a reservoir that has a *direct* oil leg below the GOC.
>
> - **B. By Hydrocarbon Composition:**
>   - *Dry Gas:* Methane-rich; no liquids recovered at surface (Heptanes-plus mole fraction < 0.001).
>   - *Wet Gas:* Significant C₂+ content; liquids can be extracted at surface.
>   - *Lean Gas:* Low condensate yield, typically CGR < 5 STB/MMscf.
>   - *Rich Gas:* High CGR > 5 STB/MMscf; economically attractive for NGL recovery.
>
> - **C. By Acid-Gas Content:**
>   - *Sweet Gas:* H₂S < 4 ppm; no CO₂ penalty.
>   - *Sour Gas:* H₂S ≥ 4 ppm; requires amine treating.
>
> **2. Sub-Types of NAG (2 marks):**
>
> - **Pure NAG:** Reservoir contains gas *only* (no oil column, no gas cap — the formation is gas-saturated from top to bottom water contact).
> - **Gas-Cap NAG (Free Gas Cap):** Gas lies above an oil leg in the *same* formation, but production wells are completed *above* the gas-oil contact, so the produced stream is gas-only.
> - **Retrograde Condensate NAG:** Reservoir is initially gas-above-dew-point, but during isothermal pressure decline, *liquid dropout occurs inside the reservoir* — this is a sub-class of NAG with rich composition.
>
> **3. Schematic Reference (1 mark):** The student should sketch a three-tier classification tree (as in Section 4.1 Mermaid) and identify NAG in the first branch, then expand its three sub-types.

#### (b) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 7 Marks

**A non-associated gas is at a reservoir pressure of 1500 psia and temperature of 140 °F. The gas specific gravity is 0.65. Using the Sutton correlation, compute the pseudocritical temperature and pressure, the pseudoreduced properties, and look up (or estimate) the compressibility factor $Z$ from the Standing-Katz chart. Hence calculate the gas density $\rho_g$.**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Sutton Pseudocritical Properties (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_{pc} &= 168 + 325\gamma_g - 12.5\gamma_g^{\,2} \\
> &= 168 + 325(0.65) - 12.5(0.65)^2 \\
> &= 168 + 211.25 - 5.281 \\
> &= 373.97\ \text{°R} \approx 374\ \text{°R}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> P_{pc} &= 677 + 15\gamma_g - 37.5\gamma_g^{\,2} \\
> &= 677 + 15(0.65) - 37.5(0.65)^2 \\
> &= 677 + 9.75 - 15.844 \\
> &= 670.91\ \text{psia} \approx 671\ \text{psia}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Absolute Temperature (0.5 mark):**
>
> $$T = 140 + 460 = 600\ \text{°R}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Pseudoreduced Properties (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}} = \frac{600}{374} = 1.604$$
>
> $$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}} = \frac{1500}{671} = 2.236$$
>
> **Step 4 — Compressibility Factor from Standing-Katz Chart (1 mark):**
>
> At $T_{pr} = 1.60$ and $P_{pr} = 2.24$, the Standing-Katz chart (or the Dranchuk-Abou-Kassem correlation) gives:
>
> $$Z \approx 0.910$$
>
> **Step 5 — Apparent Molecular Weight (0.5 mark):**
>
> $$M_g = \gamma_g \times 28.97 = 0.65 \times 28.97 = 18.83\ \text{lbm/lbmol}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Gas Density (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \rho_g &= \frac{PM_g}{ZRT} \\
> &= \frac{(1500)(18.83)}{(0.910)(10.732)(600)} \\
> &= \frac{28{,}245}{5860.55} \\
> &= 4.82\ \text{lbm/ft}^3
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $\rho_g \approx 4.82\ \text{lbm/ft}^3$ at the given reservoir conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### (a) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 7 Marks

**Starting from the real gas equation, derive the expression for the gas formation volume factor $B_g$ in res ft³/scf. Apply it to a non-associated gas reservoir at 2500 psia and 200 °F, with $Z = 0.94$.**

**Model Solution:**

> **1. Derivation (4 marks):**
>
> *Step 1 — Real gas law at reservoir and surface conditions:*
>
> $$P \, V_{res} = Z \, n \, R \, T \quad \text{...(i)}$$
>
> $$P_{sc} \, V_{sc} = Z_{sc} \, n \, R \, T_{sc} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
>
> *Step 2 — Form the ratio (B\_g = V\_{res} / V\_{sc}):*
>
> $$B_g = \frac{V_{res}}{V_{sc}} = \frac{Z \, T \, P_{sc}}{Z_{sc} \, T_{sc} \, P}$$
>
> *Step 3 — Apply standard-condition idealisation:*
>
> At $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R, $Z_{sc} \approx 1$:
>
> $$B_g = \frac{Z \cdot T \cdot 14.7}{1 \cdot 520 \cdot P} = 0.02827 \, \frac{ZT}{P}$$
>
> $$\boxed{B_g = 0.02827 \, \frac{ZT}{P}\ \text{[res ft}^3\text{/scf]}}$$
>
> **2. Numerical Application (3 marks):**
>
> Given: $P = 2500$ psia, $T = 200$ °F $= 660$ °R, $Z = 0.94$.
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> B_g &= 0.02827 \times \frac{(0.94)(660)}{2500} \\
> &= 0.02827 \times \frac{620.4}{2500} \\
> &= 0.02827 \times 0.24816 \\
> &= 0.007014\ \text{res ft}^3\text{/scf}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** A 1-scf volume of gas at the surface expands to occupy **7.014 × 10⁻³ ft³** in the reservoir. The inverse relationship between $B_g$ and $P$ reflects gas compressibility — as reservoir pressure falls, $B_g$ increases (the gas occupies *more* reservoir volume per unit surface volume, indicating a depleting reservoir).

#### (b) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 7 Marks

**Discuss the major processing steps required for non-associated gas before pipeline transportation. List the typical contaminants removed and the process units employed.**

**Model Solution:**

> **1. Typical Contaminants in NAG (1 mark):**
>
> - **Water vapour** (causes hydrate formation below ~ 30 °C in pipelines).
> - **Hydrogen sulphide, H₂S** (corrosive, toxic; "sour" criterion at 4 ppm).
> - **Carbon dioxide, CO₂** (corrosive, reduces heating value).
> - **Mercury, Hg** (attacks aluminium heat exchangers in LNG plants).
> - **Tertiary butyl mercaptan / mercaptans** (odoriferous, corrosive).
> - **Solid particulates** (sand, scale, rust from downhole tubulars).
>
> **2. Processing Train (5 marks):**
>
> | Step | Process Unit | Function | Typical Equipment |
> |------|--------------|----------|-------------------|
> | **1** | **Inlet Separation** | Knock out free liquids and solids from well stream | Three-phase separator (vertical/horizontal) |
> | **2** | **Acid-Gas Removal** | Remove H₂S and CO₂ to meet pipeline spec (H₂S < 4 ppm, CO₂ < 2–3 %) | Amine contactor (MEA, MDEA, DEA); Membrane separation |
> | **3** | **Dehydration** | Suppress hydrate formation; spec: water < 7 lbm/MMscf | TEG (Tri-Ethylene Glycol) contactor; Molecular sieve |
> | **4** | **Mercury Removal** | Protect cryogenic equipment in LNG | Sulphur-impregnated activated carbon beds |
> | **5** | **NGL Recovery (optional)** | Extract C₂+ for petrochemical sales; reduces liquid dropout in pipeline | Cryogenic turbo-expander; Joule-Thomson valve |
> | **6** | **Compression & Metering** | Boost to pipeline pressure (~ 70–100 bar); custody transfer | Reciprocating/centrifugal compressors; orifice/turbine meter |
>
> **3. Specific NAG Advantage (1 mark):**
>
> Because lean NAG contains negligible C₅+ (heptanes-plus), the NGL recovery step is often *skipped* — yielding substantial CAPEX and OPEX savings compared to associated-gas processing trains. The dominant cost-driver for NAG is therefore **dehydration + acid-gas removal** (when sour), not liquids recovery.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks in NAG Questions:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Trap:** Always convert °F to **°R (add 460)** or °C to **K (add 273.15)** *before* substituting into gas equations. Forgetting this is the #1 cause of incorrect numerical answers in KTU scripts.
>
> 2. **Confusing NAG with "Dry Gas":** NAG is a *reservoir-association* classification. A non-associated gas CAN be wet or rich (e.g., a retrograde condensate gas reservoir with no oil leg is still NAG). Don't conflate reservoir taxonomy with compositional taxonomy.
>
> 3. **$Z$-Chart Discipline:** When using the Standing-Katz chart, you must first compute $T_{pc}, P_{pc}$ via Sutton, then $T_{pr}, P_{pr}$, *then* read $Z$. Skipping straight to $Z = 1$ (ideal-gas assumption) is only valid for very low pressures (< 50 psia); for reservoir pressures, it costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Wichert-Aziz for Sour Gas:** If the gas contains H₂S or CO₂ > 5 mol%, the Sutton $T_{pc}/P_{pc}$ must be *corrected* using the Wichert-Aziz procedure. Skipping this correction in a sour-gas NAG problem can cost 2 marks.
>
> 5. **GOR vs CGR Distinction:** Always state *Condensate-Gas Ratio (CGR)* in STB/MMscf for NAG, never GOR in scf/STB. Examiners actively deduct for mis-labelled units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day KTU prep):**
>
> - NAG = gas in reservoir with **no (or negligible) oil**; produced as free gas.
> - NAG is a **reservoir-taxonomy** term; *dry/wet/lean/rich* are **composition taxonomies** — they are *orthogonal*, not synonymous.
> - Pure NAG has **infinite surface GOR** (because $q_o \to 0$); report as **CGR** in STB/MMscf.
> - **Real Gas Law** ($PV = ZnRT$) is the master equation; everything else is derived from it.
> - **$B_g = 0.02827 \, ZT/P$** in res ft³/scf — must be **memorised**.
> - **Sutton correlations** for $T_{pc}$ and $P_{pc}$ are valid for *sweet, hydrocarbon-only* gases; for sour gases add **Wichert-Aziz correction**.
> - **Recovery factor** of NAG: **70–90 %** (gas expansion drive) — much higher than oil reservoirs.
> - **NAG surface train** = Inlet Separation → Acid-Gas Removal → Dehydration → Mercury Removal → Compression → Metering.
> - NAG skips (or downscales) the NGL-recovery unit because C₅+ content is negligible — a major CAPEX/OPEX advantage.
> - Standard reference state: **$P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R** — always quote gas volumes in **scf** (not ft³) at this state.
> - **Methane content of lean NAG > 90 mol%**; **$\gamma_g$ ranges 0.58 – 0.70**; **heating value ~ 1000 BTU/scf**.
> - For KTU numerical answers, **always show units** in every step; examiners deduct for unitless final answers.
> - Typical KTU trap: forgetting $Z_{sc} = 1$ assumption (valid) vs. forgetting $Z$ at reservoir conditions (costly).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Condensate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gas Condensate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Gas Condensate** (also termed *retrograde gas condensate* or *lean gas condensate* in KTU petroleum engineering literature) is a naturally occurring multi-component hydrocarbon fluid that exists as a **single-phase gaseous vapor in the reservoir** under high initial reservoir pressure and temperature, but which **separates (condenses) into a liquid phase** when the pressure and/or temperature drop during production, transportation, or surface processing. The liquid portion recovered at the stock tank is referred to as **condensate** (or *lease condensate*), while the gas portion is termed **wet gas** or **residue gas** after the heavier hydrocarbons (typically **C₅⁺**, i.e., pentane and heavier fractions) have been removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**  
> A *gas condensate reservoir* is one in which the reservoir fluid is initially in the gaseous phase, with the reservoir temperature lying **between the critical temperature (T_c)** and the **cricondentherm (T_cc)**, and the initial reservoir pressure being **above the upper dew-point curve** of the phase envelope. As pressure declines below the dew-point pressure during production, liquid (condensate) drops out in the reservoir — a phenomenon known as **retrograde condensation**.

## 1.2 Key Quantitative Descriptors

| Descriptor | Symbol / Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity of Stock-Tank Condensate | API° (or γ_o) | 50° – 85° API |
| Heptanes-plus fraction | C₇⁺ (mol %) | 0.5 – 12 mol % |
| Initial Producing Gas-Oil Ratio | R_vi (scf/STB) | 3,000 – 150,000 scf/STB |
| Gas-to-Liquid Yield (GPM) | GPM (gal/1000 scf) | 0.3 – 15 gal/Mscf |
| Stock-Tank Liquid Gravity | γ_STL (°API) | > 50° API (very light) |
| Color | — | Water-white → straw / pale yellow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**  
> - **Wet Gas Reservoir:** Reservoir temperature T_res > T_c, no liquid forms inside the reservoir — liquids appear only in surface separators.  
> - **Retrograde Gas-Condensate Reservoir:** T_c < T_res < T_cricondentherm, **liquid forms *inside the reservoir*** when P < P_dew.  
> - **Volatile Oil Reservoir:** T_res slightly < T_c, both gas and oil phases coexist initially.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a **cold soda bottle on a hot, humid day**:

1. Inside the sealed (high-pressure) bottle, the CO₂ is dissolved in the liquid — it is one phase.
2. Open the cap (pressure drops suddenly).
3. The temperature change combined with the pressure drop causes **water vapor in the surrounding air to condense as dew droplets** on a nearby cold glass.
4. Similarly, in a gas-condensate reservoir, the gaseous mixture carries pentanes, hexanes, heptanes, etc. as vapour. When the pressure falls below the dew-point pressure, those heavier hydrocarbons behave exactly like that moisture — they **liquefy inside the porous rock itself**, dropping out of the gas phase.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "retrograde"?**  
> Normally, lowering the temperature of a gas **liquefies** it (ordinary condensation). But here, *lowering the pressure at constant reservoir temperature* causes liquefaction. This behaviour is *opposite* to the normal Le Chatelier intuition, hence the name **retrograde condensation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase Behaviour & Phase Envelope (P-T Diagram)

The behaviour of a hydrocarbon mixture is represented on a **Pressure–Temperature (P-T) phase envelope**, also called a **pseudopressure-pseudotemperature diagram** or two-phase region diagram.

### Critical Points on the Phase Envelope

- **Critical Point (C):** The point at which the bubble-point and dew-point curves meet. Bubble-point curve and dew-point curve enclose the two-phase region.
- **Cricondentherm (T_cc):** The **maximum temperature** on the phase envelope. Above T_cc, the fluid is in a single gaseous phase at all pressures.
- **Cricondenbar (P_cb):** The **maximum pressure** on the phase envelope.
- **Dew-Point Curve:** Locus of points above which the system is **100% gas**. Below the dew-point, the first drop of liquid appears.
- **Bubble-Point Curve:** Locus of points below which the system is **100% liquid**.

### Classification of Reservoirs Using T_res Position

| Position of T_res | Reservoir Type |
|---|---|
| T_res > T_cc | **Dry Gas** (single phase gas always) |
| T_c < T_res < T_cc | **Retrograde Gas-Condensate** (liquid forms in reservoir) |
| T_res = T_c | **Volatile Oil** boundary |
| T_res < T_c | **Black Oil** or **Volatile Oil** |

## 2.2 Retrograde Condensation Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

1. **Initial State:** The reservoir is at P_i > P_dew, T_res = T_i. The fluid is a single gaseous phase.
2. **Production Begins:** Pressure declines isothermally (reservoir is large; temperature ≈ constant).
3. **Dew-Point Crossed:** When P = P_dew, the first drop of liquid appears in the pores.
4. **Maximum Liquid Dropout:** As pressure continues to drop, more liquid condenses. The maximum liquid saturation occurs at a pressure typically **15–30 %** below the dew-point pressure.
5. **Revaporization:** Below this maximum, *further* pressure drop may cause some liquid to **revaporize** into the gas (true retrograde behaviour). This liquid that drops out and is left behind (because the gas velocity is not high enough to drag it) is **lost in the reservoir**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** Retrograde liquid dropout near the wellbore causes **permeability reduction** to gas flow (relative permeability effect), reducing well deliverability. This is a classic KTU exam point.

## 2.3 Constant Volume Depletion (CVD) — The Key Laboratory Test

The **CVD test** is the most important PVT experiment for gas-condensate systems. It simulates reservoir pressure depletion at constant volume (no fluid leaves the system until each depletion stage is completed).

### CVD Procedure
1. A representative reservoir fluid sample is placed in a **windowed PVT cell** at T_res and P_i.
2. Pressure is reduced in stages (e.g., 8–10 stages from P_i to atmospheric).
3. At each stage, the **volume of liquid dropout (V_L)** is measured, along with the volume of gas (V_g).
4. The gas is **ejected** from the cell at each stage and analysed (composition, Z-factor, gas gravity).
5. The remaining liquid in the cell is also analysed.

### CVD Data Reported
- Liquid volume percent (LVP) vs. pressure.
- Gas deviation factor (Z) at each stage.
- Gas gravity (γ_g) at each stage.
- Two-phase deviation factor (Z_2φ).
- Heptanes-plus content of the equilibrium gas.
- Maximum liquid dropout (typically 0–40 % of hydrocarbon pore volume).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_p = \dfrac{V_p \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ | Original gas-in-place by volumetric method (gas reservoirs) | scf |
| 2 | $B_{gi} = 0.02827 \, \dfrac{Z_i \, T}{P_i}$ | Initial gas formation volume factor | ft³/scf |
| 3 | $B_g = 0.02827 \, \dfrac{Z \, T}{P}$ | Gas FVF at any P, T | ft³/scf |
| 4 | $Z_{2\phi} = \dfrac{Z_g + V_{L\%}/100 \cdot Z_{L}}{1 + V_{L\%}/100}$ | Two-phase Z-factor (Standing–Reudelhob approximation) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{GPM} = \dfrac{1}{n_L} \sum_{i=5}^{7} \dfrac{y_i \, M_i \, 7.481}{\gamma_{o, \, 60°F} \cdot M_{air}} \cdot y_i$ | GPM of heptanes-plus via mol fraction $y_i$ | gal/Mscf |
| 6 | $R_{s} = \dfrac{n_g}{n_L}$ | Instantaneous producing gas–condensate ratio | scf/STB |
| 7 | $B_o = \dfrac{V_{res,\,L}}{V_{STL}}$ | Oil/condensate FVF | rbbl/STB |
| 8 | $\gamma_{g, \, eq} = \dfrac{\sum y_i M_i}{M_{air}}$ | Specific gravity of equilibrium gas | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\rho_{STL} = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + °\text{API}}$ | Stock-tank liquid density | lb/ft³ |
| 10 | $L_V \% = 100 \times \dfrac{V_L(P)}{V_HC(P)}$ | Liquid volume % during CVD | % |
| 11 | $S_{o, \max} = \dfrac{V_{L, \max}}{V_{HCV}}$ | Maximum liquid saturation (above connate) | fraction |
| 12 | $P/T$ relationship via Standing: $\log P = a \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T} \right)$ | Vapor-pressure correlation | psia, °R |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:**  
> - The CVD test provides data for material-balance calculations on gas-condensate reservoirs.  
> - GPM governs the economic viability of surface recovery of NGL (Natural Gas Liquids).  
> - Z_2φ is essential for material balance when two phases coexist in the reservoir.  
> - Liquid dropout data are used to design **cycling / gas-recycling projects** to maintain reservoir pressure above the dew-point.

## 2.5 Real-World Application: Field Processing of Gas Condensate

A typical surface facility recovers condensate by **multi-stage separation**:

- **High-Pressure Separator (HPS):** Receives wellstream at wellhead pressure (~1000–2500 psi). Separates gas and free liquid.
- **Low-Pressure Separator (LPS):** Operates at lower pressure (~100–200 psi). Recovers additional liquid knocked out.
- **Stock Tank:** At atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature; remaining liquid is the final stock-tank condensate.
- **Stabilizer Column:** Removes dissolved light gases (methane, ethane) from the condensate, raising its API gravity and Reid vapour pressure (RVP) to marketable specs (~1220 RVP max for export).
- **NGL Recovery Plant:** Uses Joule–Thomson cooling, turbo-expander, or lean oil absorption to extract ethane, propane, butane (LPG), and natural gasoline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gas Formation Volume Factor (B_g) from Real-Gas Law

Starting from the **real-gas equation of state**:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

For one pound-mole of gas (n = 1), volume at reservoir conditions is $V_{res} = \dfrac{ZRT}{P}$.

Volume at standard conditions (14.7 psia, 60 °F = 520 °R) is $V_{sc} = \dfrac{Z_{sc} R T_{sc}}{P_{sc}}$.

Taking the ratio, the gas formation volume factor $B_g$ (res ft³ / std ft³):

$$B_g = \dfrac{V_{res}}{V_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z T / P}{Z_{sc} T_{sc} / P_{sc}}$$

Since $Z_{sc} \approx 1$ for natural gas at 14.7 psia, 60 °F, and $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R:

$$B_g = \dfrac{Z T}{P} \cdot \dfrac{P_{sc}}{T_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z T}{P} \cdot \dfrac{14.7}{520}$$

Substituting numerical values and converting $T$ from °F to °R (i.e., $T = T_{°F} + 460$):

$$B_g = 0.02827 \dfrac{Z T}{P} \quad \left[ \text{res ft}^3 / \text{scf} \right]$$

> **Engineering meaning:** $B_g$ tells us how much reservoir volume one standard cubic foot of gas occupies downhole — typically **0.005 – 0.05 res ft³/scf**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Phase Z-Factor (Standing–Reudelhob Method)

When both gas and liquid coexist in the reservoir (below dew-point), the total volume of the hydrocarbon mixture can be expressed using a **two-phase compressibility factor** $Z_{2\phi}$ defined as:

$$Z_{2\phi} = \dfrac{P \, V_t}{n_t R T}$$

where $V_t = V_g + V_L$ and $n_t = n_g + n_L$ are the total volume and total moles in the cell.

A direct measurement is possible, but a useful **engineering approximation** (Standing) uses the LVP:

$$Z_{2\phi} = Z_g \left( 1 - \dfrac{L_{V\%}}{100} \right) + Z_L \left( \dfrac{L_{V\%}}{100} \right)$$

Since $Z_L \ll Z_g$ (liquids are nearly incompressible), a simpler practical form is:

$$Z_{2\phi} = Z_g \left( 1 - \dfrac{L_{V\%}}{100} \right) + Z_L \left( \dfrac{L_{V\%}}{100} \right)$$

**Approximation when $Z_L \approx 0.2$:**

$$Z_{2\phi} \approx Z_g \left( 1 - 0.008 \cdot L_{V\%} \right)$$

> **Use in material balance:** $Z_{2\phi}$ replaces $Z$ in the standard gas material-balance equation, enabling estimation of original gas-in-place even when condensate is dropping out.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: GPM (Gas-to-Liquid Yield) from Composition

GPM is the **liquid volume (in US gallons) recoverable from 1000 scf of gas**, useful for marketing NGL.

For each hydrocarbon component $C_i$ (i = 3 for propane, 4 for butane, 5 for pentane, etc.), the liquid volume per mole of that component at standard conditions is:

$$V_{L, i} = \dfrac{M_i}{\rho_{L, i}} \quad \text{[ft}^3\text{/lbmol]}$$

where $M_i$ is molecular weight and $\rho_{L, i}$ is liquid density at 60 °F.

The gallons per 1000 scf of component $i$ is:

$$\text{GPM}_i = \dfrac{y_i}{V_{gas, \, 1 \, lbmol}} \cdot V_{L, i} \cdot \dfrac{7.4805 \, \text{gal}}{\text{ft}^3} \cdot 1000$$

The molar volume of an ideal gas at standard conditions is **379.4 scf/lbmol**, so $V_{gas, 1 lbmol} = 379.4$ scf/lbmol.

Substituting:

$$\text{GPM}_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot M_i \cdot 7.4805 \cdot 1000}{379.4 \cdot \rho_{L, i}}$$

> **Note:** $\rho_{L,i}$ in lb/ft³ is the liquid density of pure component $i$ at 60 °F.

**Total GPM:**

$$\text{GPM}_{total} = \sum_{i=3}^{7} \text{GPM}_i$$

The contribution from heptanes-plus (C₇⁺) is computed using its molecular weight and density (estimated from API gravity).

> **Example numerical sense:** A gas with GPM = 3 gal/Mscf is considered "lean"; GPM > 5 indicates a "rich" gas condensate.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Original-Gas-In-Place for Gas-Condensate Reservoirs

For a gas-condensate reservoir, the pore volume occupied by hydrocarbons is $V_{HCV} = V_p (1 - S_{wi})$ (after subtracting interstitial water).

The hydrocarbon pore volume at initial conditions is the volume of the **initial gas phase**:

$$V_{HCV, i} = G \, B_{gi}$$

where $G$ is the initial gas-in-place (scf) and $B_{gi}$ is the initial gas FVF.

Equating:

$$G = \dfrac{V_{HCV, i}}{B_{gi}} = \dfrac{V_p \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$$

If we express pore volume in reservoir barrels ($V_p$ in rbbl, with 1 bbl = 5.615 ft³) and use $B_{gi}$ in res ft³/scf, the practical KTU form is:

$$G = \dfrac{7758 \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi}) \, h \, A}{B_{gi}} \quad [\text{scf}]$$

where $A$ = area (acres), $h$ = net pay thickness (ft), and **7758** = 43,560 ft²/acre × 5.615 ft³/bbl.

If both gas and liquid coexist (below dew-point) at some later stage, replace $B_{gi}$ with $V_t / n_t = Z_{2\phi} R T / P$:

$$G = \dfrac{V_{HCV} \, P}{Z_{2\phi} R T}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Material Balance for a Gas-Condensate Reservoir (Volumetric, Dry-Gas Approximation)

For a **dry-gas** drive gas-condensate reservoir (no water influx, no water production), the material balance at cumulative gas production $G_p$ is:

$$G \, B_{gi} = (G - G_p) B_g + G \, R_{si} \, B_{og, \, sc} + \Delta V_{dropout}$$

where $\Delta V_{dropout}$ is the volume of retrograde liquid remaining in the reservoir.

For a simplified **dry-gas-approximation** model (ignoring liquid accumulation, valid above or slightly below P_dew):

$$\dfrac{P}{Z} = \dfrac{P_i}{Z_i} \left( 1 - \dfrac{G_p}{G} \right)$$

This is a **straight line on a P/Z vs. G_p plot** with intercept $P_i/Z_i$ and slope $-P_i/(Z_i \cdot G)$. The X-intercept gives $G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction for condensate dropout:**  
> If condensate dropout is significant, use $Z_{2\phi}$ instead of $Z_g$ and apply the **Havlena–Odeh** method to separate the gas and liquid contributions. The general two-phase material balance is:

$$\dfrac{P}{Z_{2\phi}} = \dfrac{P_i}{Z_i} \left[ \dfrac{1 - G_p/G - (N_p R_{si} B_{og})/(G B_{gi})}{1 - (V_{L\%}/100) \cdot B_{gi}} \right]$$

where $N_p$ is the cumulative condensate produced and $R_{si}$ is the initial dissolved gas-oil ratio in the condensate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Gas-Condensate PVT Workflow (CVD Test)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Fluid Sample] --> B[Recombination in PVT Cell]
    B --> C{P_i, T_res = T}
    C --> D[Constant Mass, Constant Volume]
    D --> E[Pressure Reduction in Stages]
    E --> F[Stage 1: Above P_dew]
    E --> G[Stage 2: P = P_dew - First Liquid Drop]
    E --> H[Stage 3: Maximum Dropout]
    E --> I[Stage 4: Revaporization Zone]
    E --> J[Stage 5: Low Pressure]
    F --> K[Measure V_g, V_L, Z_g, γ_g]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[CVD Report: LVP, Z, γ_g, Composition]
    L --> M[Material Balance Calculations]
    L --> N[Surface Facility Design GPM]
    L --> O[Well Deliverability Models]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Surface Processing of Gas-Condensate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WH[Wellhead] --> HPS[High Pressure Separator]
    HPS --> G1[Gas Stream to Sales]
    HPS --> L1[Liquid Stream]
    L1 --> LPS[Low Pressure Separator]
    LPS --> G2[Flash Gas to NGL Plant]
    LPS --> L2[Liquid Stream]
    L2 --> ST[Stock Tank at Atmospheric Pressure]
    ST --> G3[Flash Gas Vapour Recovery]
    ST --> COND[Stabilized Condensate Product]
    COND --> STAB[Stabilizer Column]
    STAB --> LPG[LPG Product C3 C4]
    STAB --> NAPH[Naphtha C5 C6]
    G2 --> NGLP[NGL Recovery Plant]
    NGLP --> ETH[Ethane C2]
    NGLP --> LPG
    NGLP --> NAPH
    G1 --> SWEET[Sweetening and Dehydration]
    SWEET --> SALES[Sales Gas to Pipeline]
```

## 4.3 P-T Phase Envelope Schematic (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High P, High T] --> B[Single Phase Gas Region]
    B --> C{Dew-Point Curve}
    C --> D[Two-Phase Region Gas plus Liquid]
    D --> E[Bubble-Point Curve]
    E --> F[Single Phase Liquid Region]
    C --> G[Critical Point Tc Pc]
    D --> H[Cricondentherm T_cc at Max T]
    D --> I[Cricondenbar P_cb at Max P]
    B -.retrograde.-> D
    D -.revaporization.-> J[Gas + Residual Liquid]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Recombination Procedure for Gas-Condensate Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Separator Gas and Liquid Samples] --> B[Measure Flow Rates GOR]
    B --> C[Recombine in Lab Cell at T_res, P_res]
    C --> D[Stir Agitate Single Phase]
    D --> E[Transfer to PVT Cell]
    E --> F[Pressure Reduction CVD]
    F --> G[Compare Lab Dew Point with Field Dew Point]
    G --> H{Valid Sample?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use for Material Balance]
    H -- No --> J[Resample Check Contamination]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Retrograde Condensation Path on P-T Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial State Pi above P_dew, T_res] --> B[Pressure Declines Isothermally]
    B --> C[Cross P_dew First Liquid Appears]
    C --> D[Further Pressure Decline Liquid Increases]
    D --> E[Maximum Liquid Saturation Point]
    E --> F[Further Decline Liquid Revaporizes]
    F --> G[Low Pressure Reservoir: Residual Liquid Lost]
    G --> H[Production String Carries Gas only]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q. Define gas condensate. How is it distinguished from a wet gas and a black oil on a P-T phase diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

A **gas condensate** is a multi-component hydrocarbon mixture that exists as a single gaseous phase in the reservoir under initial conditions but partially condenses to liquid when pressure falls below the dew-point pressure during production or at surface facilities. The recovered liquid is called **condensate** (or *lease condensate*).

On a P-T phase envelope:
- If $T_{res} > T_{cc}$ (cricondentherm) → **Dry gas**, no liquid ever forms in the reservoir.
- If $T_c < T_{res} < T_{cc}$ → **Gas condensate**, with retrograde liquid dropout inside the reservoir.
- If $T_{res} < T_c$ → **Volatile oil** or **black oil** (single-phase liquid initially; gas evolves as pressure drops below bubble-point).

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for P-T classification)

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q. What is retrograde condensation? Why is it called "retrograde"?**

**Model Answer:**

**Retrograde condensation** is the phenomenon in which a **gaseous hydrocarbon mixture liquefies upon isothermal pressure reduction** within a specific temperature range, contrary to the normal expectation that lowering pressure would cause a gas to remain gaseous or even vaporize a liquid.

It is called *retrograde* because the phase change is *opposite* to the normal sequence observed when a single-component fluid is cooled (gas → liquid upon cooling). In this case, *lowering pressure at constant temperature* triggers condensation, and the liquid may re-vaporize upon further pressure drop — an "abnormal" or reverse behaviour. This is the defining characteristic of gas-condensate reservoirs and has major engineering consequences because the dropped-out liquid is generally **left in the reservoir**, reducing condensate recovery to perhaps 20–70 % of the in-place volume.

(3 marks: 1 for phenomenon, 1 for naming rationale, 1 for engineering consequence)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts, each carrying 7 marks. Internal choice allowed at the module level. The two options below are independent full-length questions.

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q. (a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **Pressure–Temperature phase diagram** of a multi-component hydrocarbon system. Identify and define the critical point, cricondentherm, cricondenbar, bubble-point curve, and dew-point curve. Classify reservoirs based on reservoir temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

A **P-T phase diagram** (phase envelope) for a multi-component hydrocarbon system is a closed-loop curve on the pressure–temperature plane, dividing the plane into:
- A single-phase **gas** region (outside the loop, on the high-T or low-P side),
- A single-phase **liquid** region (outside the loop, on the low-T or high-P side),
- A **two-phase** region (inside the loop) where gas and liquid coexist in equilibrium.

**Key features:**
1. **Bubble-Point Curve:** The lower portion of the loop, separating the liquid region from the two-phase region. At a bubble point, the first bubble of gas appears in a liquid.
2. **Dew-Point Curve:** The upper portion of the loop, separating the gas region from the two-phase region. At the dew point, the first drop of liquid appears in a gas.
3. **Critical Point (C):** Where the bubble-point and dew-point curves meet. At this point, the properties (density, composition) of the coexisting phases become identical.
4. **Cricondentherm (T_cc):** The maximum temperature on the phase envelope. Above T_cc, no two-phase region exists at any pressure.
5. **Cricondenbar (P_cb):** The maximum pressure on the envelope. Above P_cb, no two-phase region exists at any temperature.

**Reservoir classification by reservoir temperature T_res:**
- T_res > T_cc: **Dry gas reservoir** (always single-phase gas).
- T_c < T_res < T_cc: **Retrograde gas-condensate reservoir** (liquid forms in reservoir).
- T_res ≈ T_c: **Volatile oil / near-critical oil**.
- T_res < T_c: **Black oil or volatile oil** (single-phase liquid initially).

A vertical line at T_res on the phase envelope, traversed from high to low pressure, identifies the type and number of phase transitions encountered during production.

*(Sketch description: Phase envelope, critical point, cricondentherm, cricondenbar, isothermal depletion path for retrograde condensate, regions labelled.)*

---

**Q. (b)** What is a **Constant Volume Depletion (CVD) test**? List the data reported and explain its engineering significance in evaluating a gas-condensate reservoir. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

The **Constant Volume Depletion (CVD) test** is the standard laboratory PVT experiment for gas-condensate reservoir fluids. It simulates pressure depletion of the reservoir under the assumption that the hydrocarbon pore volume remains constant (no formation compaction, no water influx).

**Procedure:**
1. A recombined reservoir-fluid sample is charged into a windowed, high-pressure PVT cell at reservoir temperature T_res and initial reservoir pressure P_i (above the dew point).
2. Pressure is reduced in stages (typically 8–14 stages) from P_i down to atmospheric pressure. The system volume is held constant.
3. At each stage, the volumes of gas (V_g) and liquid (V_L) in the cell are measured through the sight glass.
4. The gas phase is ejected at constant pressure and analysed for composition, specific gravity, and deviation factor Z.
5. The remaining liquid is also analysed for composition and density.

**Data reported:**
- Liquid volume percent (LVP, %) vs. pressure.
- Equilibrium gas composition at each stage.
- Equilibrium gas gravity γ_g and Z-factor.
- Heptanes-plus molecular weight M(C₇⁺) and density ρ(C₇⁺).
- Two-phase deviation factor Z_2φ.
- Maximum liquid dropout (often 15–30 % of hydrocarbon pore volume).

**Engineering significance:**
1. **Dew-point determination:** Confirms whether the reservoir fluid is indeed retrograde and the saturation pressure of the system.
2. **Material balance:** The Z_2φ and P-T data are inputs to volumetric and material-balance calculations for original gas-in-place.
3. **Condensate recovery prediction:** Maximum LVP estimates how much condensate will drop out and be lost in the reservoir, guiding decisions on **pressure maintenance / gas cycling**.
4. **Surface facility design:** Equilibrium gas compositions feed the design of separators, NGL recovery plants, and pipelines.
5. **Deliverability:** Quantifies the permeability reduction caused by condensate dropout near the wellbore (relative-permeability effects).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the **gas formation volume factor** $B_g$ from the real-gas law. A gas-condensate reservoir has $P_i = 3500$ psia, $T_{res} = 200$ °F, $Z_i = 0.85$. Compute the initial gas formation volume factor in reservoir cubic feet per standard cubic foot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Derivation:**

The real-gas law for $n$ moles is:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

For 1 mole, the volume at reservoir conditions is:

$$V_{res} = \dfrac{Z R T}{P}$$

At standard conditions ($P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 60$ °F = 520 °R), assuming ideal behaviour ($Z_{sc} = 1$):

$$V_{sc} = \dfrac{R T_{sc}}{P_{sc}}$$

Dividing $V_{res}$ by $V_{sc}$:

$$B_g = \dfrac{V_{res}}{V_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z T / P}{T_{sc} / P_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z T P_{sc}}{P T_{sc}}$$

Substituting $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R:

$$B_g = \dfrac{Z T}{P} \times \dfrac{14.7}{520} = 0.02827 \dfrac{Z T}{P}$$

with $T$ in °R (i.e., $T = T_{°F} + 460$).

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $P_i = 3500$ psia, $T_{res} = 200$ °F = $200 + 460 = 660$ °R, $Z_i = 0.85$.

$$B_{gi} = 0.02827 \times \dfrac{0.85 \times 660}{3500}$$

Step 1: $0.85 \times 660 = 561$

Step 2: $561 / 3500 = 0.1603$

Step 3: $B_{gi} = 0.02827 \times 0.1603 = 0.004531 \, \text{res ft}^3 / \text{scf}$

**Result:** $B_{gi} \approx 4.531 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{res ft}^3 / \text{scf}$.

(Alternatively in rbbl/scf: $B_{gi} = 4.531 \times 10^{-3} / 5.615 = 8.07 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{rbbl/scf}$.)

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **surface processing of gas condensate**. State the function of each major equipment item. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

The surface processing of a gas-condensate wellstream consists of a sequence of pressure-staged separation, stabilization, and NGL recovery. The major items are:

1. **Wellhead Choke:** Reduces wellhead pressure to safe working pressure; controls flow rate and protects downstream equipment.

2. **High-Pressure Separator (HPS):** Operates at typically 1000–2500 psia. Separates the wellstream into a high-pressure gas phase (sent to sales or NGL plant) and a free liquid (condensate) phase.

3. **Low-Pressure Separator (LPS):** Operates at ~100–200 psia. The condensate from HPS is flashed here, recovering additional light hydrocarbons as a low-pressure gas stream and producing a richer liquid.

4. **Stock Tank (Atmospheric Storage):** Operates at ~14.7 psia and ambient temperature. Final liquid product (stock-tank condensate) is collected; flash gas vapours are recovered by a vapour-recovery system (VRU) to minimize losses and emissions.

5. **Stabilizer Column:** A fractionating tower that removes dissolved light ends (methane, ethane) from the condensate, raising its API gravity and lowering its **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** to below ~12 psia (export spec). Operates at ~80–150 psia with reboiler and overhead condenser.

6. **NGL Recovery Plant (Turbo-expander / JT / Lean Oil):** Extracts ethane, propane, butane (LPG), and natural gasoline (C₅⁺) from the separator gas stream by refrigeration, Joule–Thomson expansion, or absorption. Produces individual NGL products for petrochemical and LPG markets.

7. **Dehydration Unit (Glycol Contact / Molecular Sieve):** Removes water from sales gas to prevent hydrate formation in pipelines (spec: < 7 lb water/MMscf).

8. **Acid Gas Removal Unit (Amine / Membrane / Solid Bed):** Removes H₂S and CO₂ to meet sales-gas specifications (H₂S < 4 ppm, CO₂ < 2 mol%).

9. **Sales Compressor and Pipeline:** Boosts residue gas pressure for transportation to the gas processing hub or end user.

The integrated scheme ensures **maximum liquid recovery**, **gas quality compliance**, and **safety** during operation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up definitions:** Many students confuse *wet gas* (T_res > T_c, no liquid in reservoir) with *gas condensate* (T_c < T_res < T_cc, liquid forms in reservoir). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting units in B_g derivation:** Always show $P_{sc} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R and the constant 0.02827 explicitly. Marks are awarded for the constant; just writing "B_g = ZT/P" gets partial credit only.
> 3. **Not identifying the dew-point curve correctly:** On a P-T diagram, the dew-point is the *upper* portion of the loop. Students often draw it as the lower portion.
> 4. **Ignoring condensate loss in material balance:** Treating gas-condensate as a dry-gas reservoir below the dew-point gives incorrect OGIP. Use $Z_{2\phi}$, not $Z_g$.
> 5. **Forgetting to add 460 to convert °F → °R** in the $B_g$ calculation — a single-mark error that propagates.
> 6. **Confusing GPM with GOR:** GPM is *liquid gallons per 1000 scf of gas*; GOR is *gas scf per STB of liquid*. These are reciprocally related but not identical.
> 7. **P-T diagram sketches:** Always label the two-phase region, the critical point, the cricondentherm, the dew-point curve, and a depletion path.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gas Condensate Definition:** A multi-component hydrocarbon mixture existing as a single gas phase in the reservoir (P > P_dew) but partially condenses when pressure drops below the dew point.
- **Phase Envelope (P-T Diagram):** Closed loop with bubble-point curve (lower), dew-point curve (upper), critical point, cricondentherm (T_cc), and cricondenbar (P_cb).
- **Reservoir Classification by T_res:**
  - T_res > T_cc → Dry gas
  - T_c < T_res < T_cc → Gas condensate (retrograde)
  - T_res < T_c → Black oil / Volatile oil
- **Retrograde Condensation:** Liquid forms upon isothermal pressure reduction — opposite of normal condensation sequence.
- **CVD Test:** Simulates reservoir depletion at constant volume; reports LVP, Z, γ_g, composition, and maximum liquid dropout.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot ZT/P$
  - $Z_{2\phi} = Z_g(1 - L_{V\%}/100) + Z_L (L_{V\%}/100)$
  - $G = V_p \phi (1 - S_{wi}) / B_{gi}$
  - GPM from composition
  - $P/Z$ straight-line material balance (above P_dew).
- **Engineering Significance:** Retrograde liquid dropout is *lost in the reservoir*, reducing deliverability due to relative-permeability effects. **Pressure maintenance / gas cycling** projects are designed to mitigate this.
- **Surface Processing:** HPS → LPS → Stock Tank → Stabilizer → NGL Plant → Acid Gas Removal → Dehydration → Sales.
- **Typical Properties:** API 50°–85°, GOR 3,000–150,000 scf/STB, GPM 0.3–15 gal/Mscf, C₇⁺ 0.5–12 mol%.
- **Key Distinction:** Wet gas forms liquid *only at surface*; gas condensate forms liquid *inside the reservoir* below P_dew.

> **Final Examiner's Note:** Always present a labelled P-T diagram in your answers, define all symbols, show units, and explicitly state assumptions (e.g., "isothermal depletion," "no water influx") in any derivation or material-balance problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unconventional Production of Natural Gas: Coal Bed Methane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unconventional Production of Natural Gas: Coal Bed Methane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
