## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
**Define shale gas. How is it different from conventional natural gas in terms of storage mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Shale gas is natural gas (mostly methane) trapped within fine-grained, organic-rich shale formations where the shale acts simultaneously as source rock, reservoir rock, and cap rock.

**Storage mechanism (1 mark):**
Gas in shale exists in three states:
1. **Free gas** in micro-porosity and natural fractures
2. **Adsorbed gas** on the surface of kerogen and clay particles
3. **Dissolved gas** within the organic matter (kerogen/bitumen)

**Difference from conventional (1 mark):**
Conventional gas exists only as **free gas in porous, permeable reservoir rock** (sandstone/carbonate) sealed by a separate impermeable cap rock. In shale gas, the source, reservoir, and seal are the **same rock unit**, and a significant portion of gas is **adsorbed**, not free.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
**List the key petrophysical properties used to characterize tight gas sands.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **In-situ matrix permeability (k) ≤ 0.1 mD** — the defining property; air permeability measured at overburden conditions (1 mark)
2. **Porosity (φ)** — typically 5–12%, often reduced by diagenetic cementation (1 mark)
3. **Pore-throat size and distribution** — controlled by grain sorting and cementation; affects capillary pressure and relative permeability (0.5 mark)
4. **Water saturation (S_w)** — controls gas pore volume; high S_w reduces producible gas (0.5 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Shale Gas Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the geological and petrophysical characteristics of shale gas reservoirs. Discuss the storage mechanisms and recovery mechanisms involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the Langmuir isotherm equation for gas adsorption in shale and apply it to estimate the adsorbed gas content for a shale formation with the following data: (7 marks)
- Langmuir Volume $V_L = 120$ SCF/ton
- Langmuir Pressure $P_L = 600$ psia
- Initial Reservoir Pressure $P_i = 3500$ psia
- Bulk Density $\rho_b = 155$ lb/ft³
- Drainage Area $A = 320$ acres
- Net Pay $h = 60$ ft

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Geological Characteristics (3 marks):**
   - Shale is fine-grained (clay + silt sized) with very low matrix permeability
   - High Total Organic Carbon (TOC) content (2–10%) — provides source for hydrocarbon generation
   - Mature in the gas window: vitrinite reflectance $R_o$ = 1.1% to 3.0%
   - Brittle mineral content (quartz + carbonate) high to enable hydraulic fracturing
   - Low porosity (2–10%) with predominantly nano-scale pores

2. **Petrophysical Properties (2 marks):**
   - Permeability in nanodarcy to microdarcy range
   - Bimodal pore size distribution
   - High irreducible water saturation
   - Low effective porosity

3. **Storage Mechanisms (1 mark):**
   - Free gas in microfractures and inter-particle pores
   - Adsorbed gas on kerogen surface
   - Dissolved gas in kerogen

4. **Recovery Mechanisms (1 mark):**
   - Hydraulic fracturing creates extensive fracture network
   - Horizontal wells maximize reservoir contact
   - Pressure depletion releases adsorbed gas progressively (desorption dominates in late life)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from equilibrium condition where rate of adsorption equals rate of desorption on a homogeneous surface:

$$
k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta
$$

Solving for $\theta$:
$$
\theta = \dfrac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}
$$

Using $V = V_L \theta$ and defining $P_L = k_d / k_a$:
$$
V = \dfrac{V_L P}{P_L + P}
$$

**Application (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Langmuir ratio at initial pressure:**
$$
\dfrac{P_i}{P_L + P_i} = \dfrac{3500}{600 + 3500} = \dfrac{3500}{4100} = 0.8537
$$

**Step 2 — Adsorbed gas content per unit mass:**
$$
G_{ads,\text{per ton}} = V_L \cdot \dfrac{P_i}{P_L + P_i} = 120 \times 0.8537 = 102.44 \text{ SCF/ton}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert bulk density to lb/acre-ft:**
$$
\rho_b = 155 \text{ lb/ft}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert area:**
$$
A = 320 \text{ acres} \times 43560 \text{ ft}^2/\text{acre} = 1.394 \times 10^7 \text{ ft}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Reservoir bulk volume:**
$$
V_{rock} = A \cdot h = 1.394 \times 10^7 \times 60 = 8.36 \times 10^8 \text{ ft}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Total mass of rock:**
$$
m_{rock} = V_{rock} \cdot \rho_b = 8.36 \times 10^8 \times 155 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} \text{ lb}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert mass to tons:**
$$
m_{tons} = \dfrac{1.296 \times 10^{11}}{2000} = 6.48 \times 10^7 \text{ tons}
$$

**Step 8 — Total adsorbed gas in place:**
$$
G_{ads} = 6.48 \times 10^7 \times 102.44 = 6.64 \times 10^9 \text{ SCF} = 6.64 \text{ BCF}
$$

**Final Answer:** Adsorbed gas in place = **6.64 BCF** (or **6.64 × 10⁹ SCF**)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tight Gas Sands Focus (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the geological characteristics, storage mechanisms, and production technologies used for tight gas sands versus shale gas reservoirs. (7 marks)

**(b)** A tight gas sand reservoir has the following volumetric data. Calculate the gas initially in place (GIIP) using the volumetric method: (7 marks)
- Drainage Area $A = 640$ acres
- Net Pay Thickness $h = 40$ ft
- Porosity $\phi = 0.10$
- Irreducible Water Saturation $S_{wi} = 0.35$
- Gas Formation Volume Factor $B_{gi} = 0.0048$ res bbl/SCF
- Recovery Factor expected = 40%

**Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Tight Gas Sand | Shale Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Rock Type | Sandstone (quartz-rich) | Shale (clay-rich, organic) |
| Permeability | 0.001 – 0.1 mD | nano- to micro-darcy |
| Porosity | 5 – 12% | 2 – 10% |
| Source Rock | Separate (deeper formation) | Same formation (self-sourced) |
| Storage | Free gas only | Free + Adsorbed + Dissolved |
| Cap Rock | Required (shale above) | Self-sealing |
| Well Type | Vertical + Hydraulic Frac | Horizontal + Multi-stage Frac |
| Production Decline | Moderate | Very steep initially, long tail |

**Production Technologies (2 marks):**
- **Tight Gas:** Massive hydraulic fracturing (MHF), often single-stage in vertical wells; some horizontal applications.
- **Shale Gas:** Horizontal drilling with 10–40 stages of slickwater fracturing per well; advanced microseismic monitoring.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert area to ft²:**
$$
A = 640 \times 43560 = 2.788 \times 10^7 \text{ ft}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate bulk rock volume:**
$$
V_{rock} = A \cdot h = 2.788 \times 10^7 \times 40 = 1.115 \times 10^9 \text{ ft}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Pore volume:**
$$
V_{pore} = V_{rock} \cdot \phi = 1.115 \times 10^9 \times 0.10 = 1.115 \times 10^8 \text{ ft}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Hydrocarbon pore volume:**
$$
V_{HCPV} = V_{pore} \cdot (1 - S_{wi}) = 1.115 \times 10^8 \times (1 - 0.35)
$$

$$
V_{HCPV} = 1.115 \times 10^8 \times 0.65 = 7.25 \times 10^7 \text{ ft}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Convert reservoir volume to surface volume using $B_{gi}$:**

Note: $B_{gi}$ in field units is often expressed as res cf/SCF or res bbl/SCF. Here, $B_{gi} = 0.0048$ res ft³/SCF (assuming bbl was a typo, or convert if needed). Using $B_{gi} = 0.0048$ res ft³/SCF:

$$
G_{ip} = \dfrac{V_{HCPV}}{B_{gi}} = \dfrac{7.25 \times 10^7}{0.0048}
$$

$$
G_{ip} = 1.51 \times 10^{10} \text{ SCF} = 15.1 \text{ BCF}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate recoverable reserves:**
$$
G_{rec} = G_{ip} \times RF = 15.1 \times 0.40 = 6.04 \text{ BCF}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Gas Initially In Place (GIIP) = 15.1 BCF** (or 1.51 × 10¹⁰ SCF)
- **Recoverable Reserves = 6.04 BCF**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The single biggest mark-loss area. Always convert acres to ft² (× 43,560), ft to m if SI is required, and pay attention to $B_{gi}$ units (res bbl/SCF vs res ft³/SCF — they differ by 5.615).
> 2. **Forgetting $S_{wi}$:** Many students compute GIIP without subtracting irreducible water, leading to 30–40% overestimation.
> 3. **Langmuir units:** $V_L$ is per unit mass (per ton or per kg). Multiply by total rock mass, not rock volume.
> 4. **Permeability SI conversion:** 1 mD ≠ 1 m². Use 1 mD = 9.869 × 10⁻¹⁶ m².
> 5. **Mixing shale and tight equations:** Don't add the adsorbed gas term for tight gas sands — they have negligible adsorption.
> 6. **Recovery Factor confusion:** In shale gas, RF is often 5–25% (NOT 70–90% as in conventional). Examiners test this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Shale gas** has permeability in **nano- to micro-darcy** range; tight gas sands have **0.001–0.1 mD**.
- [x] Shale acts as **source + reservoir + cap** simultaneously; tight gas has a separate cap rock.
- [x] Three storage mechanisms in shale: **Free + Adsorbed + Dissolved**; tight gas = essentially **free gas only**.
- [x] **Langmuir Isotherm:** $V = V_L P / (P_L + P)$ — fundamental for adsorbed gas calculations.
- [x] **Shale Gas GIP** = Free gas component + Adsorbed gas component.
- [x] **Recovery Factor:** Conventional = 70–90%, Tight = 30–60%, Shale = 5–25%.
- [x] **Production enablers:** Horizontal wells + multi-stage hydraulic fracturing.
- [x] **Fracture conductivity dimensionless number** $D_F = k_F w_F / (k_m x_f)$ is critical for frac design.
- [x] **Volumetric GIIP formula** for tight gas: $G_{ip} = A h \phi (1-S_{wi}) / B_{gi}$.
- [x] **Standard threshold:** Tight reservoir = permeability ≤ **0.1 mD** (at overburden).
- [x] **Conversion must-knows:** 1 acre = 43,560 ft²; 1 mD = 9.869 × 10⁻¹⁶ m²; 1 BCF = 10⁹ SCF.
- [x] **Decline behavior:** Shale wells show hyperbolic decline with **b > 1** and very steep early drop.
- [x] **TOC threshold** for commercial shale gas: usually **> 2 wt%**.
- [x] **Vitrinite reflectance** $R_o$ range for gas window: **1.1% – 3.0%**.
- [x] **Brittleness** of shale is essential for fracability — high quartz/carbonate content preferred.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coal Bed Methane: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coal Bed Methane: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)**, also referred to as **Coal Seam Gas (CSG)** or **Coalbed Natural Gas**, is defined as a **naturally occurring unconventional gas** (predominantly **methane, CH₄**) that is generated, stored, and adsorbed within the **micropores and cleat systems** of coal seams. It exists primarily in the **adsorbed state** on the internal surface area of coal matrix, and is liberated only when the reservoir pressure is reduced below the desorption pressure during dewatering.

The gas is a part of the broader family of **Unconventional Gas Resources** which also includes:
- Shale Gas
- Tight Gas
- Methane Hydrates
- Deep Coal Seam Gas

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Coal Bed Methane (CBM) is positioned as a *bridge fuel* in the energy transition curriculum, since it is cleaner than coal but produced from coal seams. Students must clearly differentiate CBM from **Conventional Natural Gas** and **Biogenic vs. Thermogenic** origin.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **dry kitchen sponge dropped in a bowl of water**. The water clings *to the inside walls* of the sponge — it is not flowing freely, yet it is *trapped inside* the solid structure. If you **squeeze the sponge**, water starts dripping out.

**Coal acts exactly like that sponge**:
- The **micropores and cleats** of coal = the sponge's internal pores.
- The **methane molecules** = the water molecules clinging to the pore walls.
- **Reducing reservoir pressure (dewatering)** = squeezing the sponge.
- The **released gas** flowing to the wellbore = the dripping water.

This **adsorption** mechanism is what makes CBM fundamentally different from conventional gas, which exists as **free gas in pore spaces** of sandstone/carbonate reservoirs.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | **CH₄** | Same as conventional methane |
| Molar mass | **16.04 g/mol** | |
| Density (STP) | **0.717 kg/m³** | |
| Heating value | **~ 37 MJ/m³** | Slightly lower than pipeline gas |
| Methane purity in CBM | **> 90 %** (typical) | Rest is N₂, CO₂, heavier HC |
| Adsorption capacity (dry coal) | **5 – 30 cm³/g** | At standard conditions |
| Typical in-situ pressure | **1 – 20 MPa** | Hydrostatic to lithostatic |
| Coal rank for CBM | **Bituminous to Anthracite** | Higher rank → higher gas content |

> [!NOTE]
> A commonly cited **standard metric** in KTU 2024 curriculum: **1 tonne of coal ≈ 200 – 400 m³ of methane** at standard conditions, depending on rank, depth, and thermal maturity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Generation of Coal Bed Methane

The methane stored in coal seams is generated through **two distinct geological processes**:

#### A. Biogenic Methane (Early Stage)
- Generated by **microbial action** (methanogenic archaea bacteria) on coal during the **early stages of coalification** (peat to lignite stage).
- Occurs at **low temperatures** (< 50 °C) and shallow depths.
- Produces **isotopically light methane** (δ¹³C < −60 ‰ PDB).
- Generally **richer in CH₄** (up to 99 %).

#### B. Thermogenic Methane (Late Stage)
- Generated by **thermal cracking (pyrolysis)** of coal kerogen during progressive burial and heating.
- Temperature range: **50 °C – 200 °C**, depths > 1 km.
- Produces **isotopically heavier methane** (δ¹³C between −60 ‰ to −20 ‰ PDB).
- Associated with higher rank coals (**bituminous to anthracite**).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "*Bio = Baby* (low T, shallow), *Thermo = Teen* (high T, deep)*". Most economically exploitable CBM in India (e.g., Jharkhand, Raniganj) is **thermogenic** in origin.

### 2.2 Storage Mechanism in Coal

Unlike conventional reservoirs, coal stores gas in **three different modes**:

| Storage Mode | Description | Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Adsorbed gas** | CH₄ molecules adhere to the **internal microporous surface** of coal matrix via van der Waals forces | **~ 95 – 98 %** |
| **Free gas** | CH₄ present in **cleats (natural fractures)** and macropores as compressed gas | **~ 1 – 5 %** |
| **Dissolved gas** | CH₄ dissolved in **formation water** within the coal seam | **< 1 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **massive internal surface area** of coal (often 100 – 300 m²/g, equivalent to a football field per gram) is what enables such high adsorption storage. This is the *defining feature* of CBM and the reason CBM is classified as an **unconventional gas resource**.

### 2.3 Coal Structure – Matrix and Cleat System

Coal is a **dual-porosity** system:

1. **Coal Matrix (Microporous):**
   - Contains the **primary porosity** (micro- and mesopores, 0.5 – 50 nm).
   - Responsible for **gas storage (adsorption)**.
   - Has extremely **low permeability** (nano-Darcy range).

2. **Cleat System (Naturally Fractured):**
   - Two perpendicular sets: **Face Cleats** (continuous) and **Butt Cleats** (discontinuous).
   - Provides the **secondary porosity and permeability** (milli-Darcy range).
   - Acts as the **flow pathway** for gas and water.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{ad} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$ | **Langmuir Isotherm Equation**: $V_{ad}$ = adsorbed gas volume (cm³/g); $V_L$ = Langmuir volume (cm³/g); $P$ = reservoir pressure (MPa); $P_L$ = Langmuir pressure (MPa) |
| 2 | $G_c = V_{ad} \cdot \rho_c \cdot A \cdot h$ | **Gas-in-Place** estimation: $G_c$ = total gas content (m³); $\rho_c$ = coal density (g/cm³); $A$ = area (m²); $h$ = thickness (m) |
| 3 | $G_s = \dfrac{V_{ad} \cdot 1000}{M_c}$ | Gas content per tonne of coal: $G_s$ in m³/t, $M_c$ = mass of coal (kg) |
| 4 | $V_{des} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_L \cdot (P_i - P_d)}{(P_L + P_i)(P_L + P_d)}$ | **Desorbed gas volume** between initial pressure $P_i$ and desorption pressure $P_d$ |
| 5 | $R_f = \dfrac{k_{cleat}}{\phi_{cleat} \cdot \mu \cdot c_t}$ | Reservoir flow indicator for CBM wells (cleat permeability / fluid mobility) |
| 6 | $\rho_{CBM} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{CH_4}}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$ | Real-gas density of CBM at reservoir P, T |
| 7 | $C_{tot} = C_m + C_f + C_d + C_w$ | **Material balance** for CBM: matrix + free + dissolved + water-dissolved |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Langmuir Isotherm** is the most tested formula in KTU exams for CBM problems. Always remember: at $P = P_L$, the gas adsorbed is exactly $V_L / 2$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of CBM Engineering

- **Cleaner energy source** (CH₄ burns 2x cleaner than coal).
- **Reduces coal mine explosions** by pre-draining methane.
- **Energy security**: India's CBM reserves estimated at **~ 4.6 TCM (Trillion Cubic Meters)**.
- **Carbon capture potential**: CO₂ can be sequestered in unmineable coal seams (Enhanced CBM / ECBM).
- **Rural electrification** in coal-bearing states (Jharkhand, West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

The **Langmuir Isotherm** is the fundamental equation describing gas adsorption on coal. It is derived by considering dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption rates on a homogeneous surface.

**Step 1: Define the rate of adsorption**

The rate at which gas molecules strike and adhere to the coal surface is proportional to:
- The **gas pressure** $P$ (more pressure = more collisions).
- The **fraction of vacant surface sites** $(1 - \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the fraction of surface covered.

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

Where $k_a$ is the adsorption rate constant.

**Step 2: Define the rate of desorption**

The desorption rate is proportional only to the **fraction of occupied sites** $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

Where $k_d$ is the desorption rate constant.

**Step 3: Apply dynamic equilibrium at steady state**

At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption:

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta$**

Rearranging:

$$k_a \cdot P - k_a \cdot P \cdot \theta = k_d \cdot \theta$$

$$k_a \cdot P = \theta \cdot (k_d + k_a \cdot P)$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \cdot P}{k_d + k_a \cdot P}$$

**Step 5: Introduce the Langmuir pressure**

Define **Langmuir pressure** $P_L = \dfrac{k_d}{k_a}$ (the pressure at which half the surface is covered, $\theta = 0.5$):

$$\theta = \dfrac{P}{P_L + P}$$

**Step 6: Convert fractional coverage to gas volume**

The volume of gas adsorbed $V_{ad}$ is proportional to $\theta$. At infinite pressure, the surface becomes fully covered ($\theta = 1$), corresponding to the **Langmuir volume** $V_L$:

$$V_{ad} = V_L \cdot \theta = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

This is the final **Langmuir Isotherm Equation** used universally in CBM engineering.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Gas-in-Place Calculation for a CBM Block

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark type):**
A CBM reservoir block has the following data:
- Area $A = 2 \text{ km}^2 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Average coal thickness $h = 8 \text{ m}$
- Coal bulk density $\rho_c = 1.35 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1350 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Langmuir volume $V_L = 25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$
- Langmuir pressure $P_L = 3.5 \text{ MPa}$
- Initial reservoir pressure $P_i = 5.0 \text{ MPa}$

**Calculate the total Gas-in-Place (GIP) for the block.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the Langmuir equation to get the in-situ adsorbed gas content**

$$V_{ad} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}$$

$$V_{ad} = \dfrac{25 \cdot 5.0}{3.5 + 5.0} = \dfrac{125}{8.5}$$

$$V_{ad} = 14.706 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 2: Convert this gas content to volume per cubic meter of coal**

Since 1 m³ of coal weighs $\rho_c = 1350$ kg $= 1.35 \times 10^6$ g:

$$V_{ad,vol} = 14.706 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g} \times 1.35 \times 10^6 \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$V_{ad,vol} = 1.9853 \times 10^7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^3$$

Converting to m³:

$$V_{ad,vol} = 19.853 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of CH}_4 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ of coal}$$

**Step 3: Compute the total coal volume in the block**

$$V_{coal} = A \times h = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 \times 8 \text{ m} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate the total Gas-in-Place (GIP)**

$$GIP = V_{ad,vol} \times V_{coal} = 19.853 \times 1.6 \times 10^7$$

$$GIP = 3.1765 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{GIP \approx 3.18 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3 \;\; (\approx 318 \text{ MMSCM})}$$

**Step 5: Add a free-gas correction (5 %) for cleat-stored gas**

$$GIP_{total} = 3.1765 \times 10^8 \times 1.05 \approx 3.335 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CBM vs. Conventional Natural Gas

| Parameter | Coal Bed Methane (CBM) | Conventional Natural Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Storage mechanism | **Adsorbed** on coal micropores | **Free gas** in pore spaces |
| Reservoir rock | Coal (organic-rich) | Sandstone / Carbonate |
| Porosity | High (10 – 20 %), microporous | Moderate (5 – 30 %) |
| Permeability | Very low (µD – mD via cleats) | Moderate to high (mD – D) |
| Drive mechanism | **Pressure depletion + dewatering** | Pressure depletion, water drive, etc. |
| Production technique | Hydraulic fracturing, cavity completion | Conventional drilling |
| Initial water production | **Very high** (must dewater first) | Low |
| Gas-to-water ratio | Low (initially) | High |
| Recovery factor | **50 – 80 %** | 60 – 90 % |
| Exploration cost | Higher (deep wells, fracturing) | Lower (standard wells) |
| Environmental note | Lower CO₂ per MJ than coal | Cleanest fossil fuel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Formation Pathway and Geological Stages of CBM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Organic Matter Accumulation] --> B[Peat Formation]
    B --> C[Burial and Compaction]
    C --> D[Biogenic Methane Generation at Low T]
    D --> E1[Lignite Stage - Shallow]
    C --> F[Progressive Burial and Heating]
    F --> G[Thermogenic Cracking Begins T > 50C]
    G --> H[Bituminous Coal - Peak Gas Generation]
    H --> I[Anthracite Stage - High Rank]
    E1 --> J[Coal Seam with Adsorbed CH4]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Reservoir Pressure Trap]
    K --> L[Drilling and Dewatering]
    L --> M[Pressure Drop Below Desorption Pressure]
    M --> N[Methane Desorption and Production]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CBM Production System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reservoir[Underground Coal Seam]
        M1[Coal Matrix - Adsorbed CH4] -->|Desorption| F1[Cleat System Fractures]
        F1 -->|Free gas + water| W1[Production Wellbore]
    end
    W1 --> S1[Surface Separation Unit]
    S1 --> W2[Water Handling and Disposal]
    S1 --> G1[Gas Gathering Pipeline]
    G1 --> P1[Compressor Station]
    P1 --> T1[Processing Plant - CO2 H2S Removal]
    T1 --> M2[Metering and Sales Pipeline]
    W2 --> R1[Re-injection Well or Treatment]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Coal Matrix and Cleat System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Matrix Block - Microporous] -->|Connected by| B[Face Cleat - Primary Fracture]
    A -->|Intersected by| C[Butt Cleat - Secondary Fracture]
    B --> D[CH4 Adsorbed on Internal Surface of A]
    C --> E[Free Water and Gas in Cleats]
    D -->|On pressure drop| F[Desorbed CH4 Migrates to Cleats]
    F --> E
    E -->|Flows to| G[Wellbore]
```

### 4.4 CBM Production Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exploration and Prospect Identification] --> B[Pilot Well Drilling]
    B --> C[Well Testing and Core Analysis]
    C --> D{Commercially Viable?}
    D -->|No| E[Abandon or Re-evaluate]
    D -->|Yes| F[Field Development Plan]
    F --> G[Multi-Well Drilling Pattern]
    G --> H[Dewatering Phase - High Water Cut]
    H --> I[Gas Desorption Begins]
    I --> J[Stable Gas Production - Peak]
    J --> K[Decline Phase - Reservoir Pressure Drops]
    K --> L[Abandonment and Site Restoration]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Coal Bed Methane (CBM). List any two distinguishing features that separate it from conventional natural gas.**

**Model Answer:**
Coal Bed Methane (CBM) is an unconventional natural gas composed predominantly of methane (CH₄), which is generated and stored within coal seams. Unlike conventional natural gas, it is held primarily by **adsorption** on the internal microporous surface of the coal matrix rather than existing as free gas in the pore spaces of reservoir rocks.

**Two distinguishing features:**
1. **Storage mechanism:** CBM is stored in *adsorbed state* (95 – 98 %) on coal micropores, whereas conventional gas exists as *free gas* in sandstone/carbonate pore spaces.
2. **Production technique:** CBM production requires *dewatering* of the coal seam to reduce reservoir pressure below desorption pressure, which is not needed for conventional gas reservoirs.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for distinguishing features.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between biogenic and thermogenic methane generation in coal seams.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Biogenic Methane | Thermogenic Methane |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Microbial (methanogens) action | Thermal cracking (pyrolysis) of coal kerogen |
| **Temperature** | < 50 °C | 50 °C – 200 °C |
| **Coal rank** | Lignite to sub-bituminous | Bituminous to anthracite |
| **Depth** | Shallow (< 1 km) | Deep (> 1 km) |
| **Isotopic signature** | δ¹³C < −60 ‰ | δ¹³C between −60 ‰ and −20 ‰ |
| **Time of generation** | Early stage of coalification | Late stage of coalification |

**(3 marks)** — Mechanism (1), Conditions (1), Difference in isotopic/rank signature (1).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **geological formation of Coal Bed Methane**, highlighting the role of coalification, biogenic and thermogenic processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CBM reservoir has the following data:
- Langmuir volume $V_L$ = 30 cm³/g
- Langmuir pressure $P_L$ = 4.0 MPa
- Initial reservoir pressure $P_i$ = 6.5 MPa
- Desorption pressure $P_d$ = 1.5 MPa
- Coal density $\rho_c$ = 1.4 g/cm³
- Reservoir area = 5 km², average thickness = 10 m

Calculate the **total desorbable gas-in-place** and the **recoverable gas volume at standard conditions**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The geological formation of CBM involves a sequence of **organic matter accumulation, burial, coalification, and gas generation**, summarized as follows:

1. **Accumulation of Plant Material:** In swampy, anaerobic environments (e.g., Gondwana basins of India), plant debris accumulates over geological time to form **peat**.
2. **Burial and Compaction:** Successive layers of sediment bury the peat, compacting it and progressively increasing temperature and pressure.
3. **Coalification Stages:** Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite, with increasing carbon content and heating value.
4. **Biogenic Methane Generation:** At shallow depths and low temperatures (< 50 °C), anaerobic bacteria (*methanogens*) break down coal organic matter to produce **biogenic CH₄** during early coalification.
5. **Thermogenic Methane Generation:** At greater depths and elevated temperatures (50 – 200 °C), **thermal cracking** of the kerogen and coal macerals produces **thermogenic CH₄**.
6. **Storage:** The generated methane is **adsorbed** onto the internal surface area of the coal matrix (microporous structure) under high reservoir pressure.
7. **Trap Formation:** The coal seam itself acts as both **source rock and reservoir rock**, sealed by impermeable strata (e.g., shales, sandstones) above and below.

*A neat diagram should show the vertical column: peat → lignite → bituminous → anthracite, with temperature window markers for biogenic and thermogenic zones.*

**(7 marks)** — Stages (3), Biogenic vs. Thermogenic (2), Storage and trap (2).

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1: Apply the Langmuir equation at initial pressure**

$$V_{ad,i} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i} = \dfrac{30 \times 6.5}{4.0 + 6.5} = \dfrac{195}{10.5} = 18.571 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Langmuir equation at desorption pressure**

$$V_{ad,d} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_d}{P_L + P_d} = \dfrac{30 \times 1.5}{4.0 + 1.5} = \dfrac{45}{5.5} = 8.182 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the desorbable gas volume per gram**

$$V_{des} = V_{ad,i} - V_{ad,d} = 18.571 - 8.182 = 10.389 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 4: Compute total coal mass in the reservoir**

$$V_{coal} = A \times h = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 \times 10 \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3$$

$$M_{coal} = V_{coal} \times \rho_c = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3 \times 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ g/m}^3 = 7.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Total desorbable gas-in-place**

$$G_{des} = V_{des} \times M_{coal} = 10.389 \times 7.0 \times 10^{13} = 7.2723 \times 10^{14} \text{ cm}^3$$

Converting to m³:

$$\boxed{G_{des} \approx 7.27 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3 \; (\approx 727 \text{ MMSCM})}$$

**Step 6: Recoverable gas at standard conditions (using recovery factor 70 %)**

$$G_{rec} = 0.70 \times 7.27 \times 10^8 = 5.09 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{G_{rec} \approx 5.09 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3 \; (\approx 509 \text{ MMSCM})}$$

**(7 marks)** — Langmuir at $P_i$ (1), Langmuir at $P_d$ (1), Desorbable volume per gram (1), Coal mass (2), GIP and recovery (2).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **storage mechanism of methane in coal seams**, with reference to the **dual-porosity model** (matrix + cleat system). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** with **Conventional Natural Gas** under the headings: storage mechanism, reservoir rock, permeability, production technique, and recovery factor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

Coal seams are characterized by a **dual-porosity storage system**:

**1. Coal Matrix (Primary Porosity):**
- Composed of micro- and mesopores (0.5 – 50 nm).
- **High internal surface area** (100 – 300 m²/g).
- **Stores 95 – 98 % of the total gas** by **physical adsorption** (van der Waals forces).
- Acts as the **source of gas** during production.

**2. Cleat System (Secondary Porosity):**
- Natural fractures comprising **face cleats** (continuous, dominant) and **butt cleats** (discontinuous, perpendicular).
- **Stores only 2 – 5 % of total gas** as **free gas** and **formation water**.
- Provides the **permeability pathway** (milli-Darcy range) for gas and water to flow to the wellbore.

**Production Flow Mechanism:**
1. Reservoir pressure is reduced (via dewatering) below the critical desorption pressure.
2. Methane **desorbs** from the coal matrix micropores.
3. Gas diffuses through the matrix to the cleat system (**Fick's Law of Diffusion**).
4. Gas flows through the cleats (Darcy flow) to the wellbore.

*A neat sketch should depict: matrix blocks surrounded by an orthogonal cleat network, with arrows showing desorption and flow directions.*

**(7 marks)** — Matrix description (2), Cleat description (2), Storage fractions (1), Production flow mechanism (2).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Parameter | CBM | Conventional Natural Gas |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage mechanism** | Adsorbed on coal micropores (95 %+) | Free gas in sandstone/carbonate pores |
| **Reservoir rock** | Coal seams (organic-rich) | Sandstone, limestone, dolomite |
| **Permeability** | Very low (µD – mD via cleats) | Moderate to high (mD – Darcies) |
| **Production technique** | Dewatering + pressure depletion, sometimes hydraulic fracturing | Standard drilling; pressure depletion, water drive, gas cap drive |
| **Recovery factor** | 50 – 80 % | 60 – 90 % |
| **Initial water production** | Very high (must pump out water) | Low |
| **Gas-water contact** | Absent (gas is adsorbed) | Present |
| **Environmental aspect** | Reduces mine methane; lower GHG vs coal | Cleanest fossil fuel |

**(7 marks)** — 5 parameters × 1 mark each + 2 marks for additional valid comparison (water production / environmental).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "adsorbed" with "absorbed".** Adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon; absorption is bulk uptake. CBM uses **adsorption**.
> 2. **Always state units** for gas content (cm³/g or m³/t). Missing units cost easy marks.
> 3. **Langmuir isotherm applicability:** It assumes a *monolayer* and *homogeneous* surface — students often forget to mention this limitation.
> 4. **Recovery factor of CBM is *not* the same** as conventional gas; do not quote 60 – 90 % generically.
> 5. **Dewatering must be mentioned** in any CBM production question — examiners specifically look for this keyword.
> 6. **Biogenic vs Thermogenic confusion:** Biogenic = *bacterial, low-T*; Thermogenic = *thermal cracking, high-T*. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks easily.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **CBM = CH₄ stored in coal seams** by **adsorption** (95 – 98 %).
- **Unconventional gas**; coal acts as both **source and reservoir**.
- **Two origins:** *Biogenic* (low-T, bacterial) and *Thermogenic* (high-T, pyrolysis).
- **Dual porosity:** Matrix (storage, nano-Darcy) + Cleats (flow, milli-Darcy).
- **Langmuir Isotherm** $V_{ad} = \dfrac{V_L P}{P_L + P}$ — the **most important equation** for CBM.
- **Desorption pressure** is the key: production requires reservoir pressure to drop below it.
- **Production lifecycle:** Drilling → Dewatering → Desorption → Peak gas → Decline → Abandonment.
- **Indian CBM blocks:** Raniganj, Jharia, Bokaro, Sohagpur, RG (East) — total reserves ~ 4.6 TCM.
- **Key terms to remember:** Cleat, Adsorption, Langmuir volume/pressure, Desorption pressure, Dewatering, ECBM, Recovery factor, δ¹³C isotope.
- **Environmental bonus:** CBM is a **cleaner bridge fuel**; can enable **CO₂ sequestration (ECBM)** in unmineable seams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Status
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Present Status # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Present Status of Oil and Natural Gas** refers to the comprehensive global and national scenario of hydrocarbon resources, encompassing the **proved reserves**, **annual production**, **consumption patterns**, **geographic distribution**, and the **strategic outlook** of crude oil and natural gas in the world energy mix at a given point in time. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus framework for *PECHT522*, "Present Status" is treated as a descriptive-cum-analytical module that quantifies the **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio**, identifies the **leading hydrocarbon economies**, and establishes the techno-economic position of fossil fuels vis-à-vis renewable energy transition. ### 1.1.1 Defining the Core Vectors The "Present Status" is characterized by **five measurable vectors** as per industry-standard reports (BP Statistical Review, OPEC Annual Statistical Bulletin, EIA International Energy Outlook): 1. **Proved Reserves** – the estimated quantities of crude oil and natural gas that geological and engineering data demonstrate with reasonable certainty to be recoverable in future years from known reservoirs under existing economic and operating conditions. 2. **Production Rate** – the annual volume of hydrocarbons extracted, expressed in **Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent (MTOE)** or **Million Barrels per Day (MMbpd)** for oil, and **Billion Cubic Metres (BCM)** or **Trillion Cubic Feet (TCF)** for gas. 3. **Consumption Pattern** – the geographic and sector-wise utilization of hydrocarbons (transport, power, industry, domestic). 4. **Reserve Life (R/P Ratio)** – the static remaining life of a reserve at current production levels, expressed in **years**. 5. **Energy Mix Share** – the percentage contribution of oil and gas in the global primary energy consumption basket. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module expects students to demonstrate awareness of India's ONGC, OIL, and private/JV operators; OPEC's market influence; and the rise of **shale gas/tight oil** (USA leadership) as a game-changer in 21st-century hydrocarbon dynamics. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the world's oil and gas as a **giant bank account**: - **Reserves** = the *total balance* sitting in the account. - **Annual Production** = the *annual withdrawal* (salary-like debit). - **R/P Ratio** = the *number of years* the account will last if you keep withdrawing at the same pace and make NO new deposits. > Just as a financially aware person checks "How much money do I have? How long will it last?", the **Present Status** is essentially a **balance sheet of the planet's hydrocarbon wealth** — answering *"How much oil and gas is left, who owns it, who is using it, and for how much longer?"* A simple real-world analogy: think of a **cluster of water tanks** in a building. - The **biggest tanks** (Saudi Arabia, Russia, USA, Iran, Canada) hold the maximum reserves. - The **most frequently opened taps** (USA, China, India) consume the most. - Some tanks are **leaky** (high decline-rate mature fields like Mexico's Cantarell), while others are **slowly refilling** through new discoveries (Guyana, Namibia, Mozambique gas discoveries). --- ## 1.3 Key Global Metrics at a Glance | Parameter | Approximate Global Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Total Proved Oil Reserves | **~1,700** | Billion Barrels | | Total Proved Gas Reserves | **~7,500** | Trillion Cubic Metres (TCM) | | Global Oil Production | **~95–102** | MMbpd | | Global Gas Production | **~4,000** | BCM | | Oil R/P Ratio | **~50+** | Years | | Gas R/P Ratio | **~50+** | Years | | Share of Fossil Fuels in Primary Energy | **~80%** | Percentage | > [!IMPORTANT] > These are **order-of-magnitude indicators** consistent with publicly available annual reviews. Exact numbers fluctuate year-on-year and vary slightly between BP, OPEC, and EIA publications. --- ## 1.4 Energy Hierarchy in the Hydrocarbon Value Chain The present status must be visualized in three concentric layers: 1. **Top of the Pyramid – Producers & Owners:** OPEC+ (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, Iran, Kuwait), Russia, USA, Canada. 2. **Middle – Major Consumers & Refiners:** USA, China, India, EU, Japan. 3. **Base – Strategic & Trade Hubs:** Singapore, Rotterdam, Houston (Socar trading), Fujairah. This layered view helps explain **geopolitical pricing leverage**, **energy security concerns**, and **the rationale behind India's hydrocarbon diplomacy** with Russia, Gulf nations, and Africa. --- ## 1.5 India's Strategic Position (Asymmetric Profile) > [!NOTE] > India is the **world's third-largest consumer** of crude oil but imports **>85%** of its crude requirement. This single statistic defines India's "Present Status" — a country of **high demand, low indigenous supply**. - **Crude oil import dependence:** ~85% - **Natural gas import dependence:** ~50% (rising as LNG infrastructure expands) - **Major suppliers to India:** Russia (now #1 after recent shifts), Iraq, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Nigeria - **Domestic upstream players:** ONGC, Oil India Limited (OIL), Reliance Industries (KG-D6 block), Vedanta (Cairn Rajasthan) - **Strategic initiative:** **SAUBHAGYA**, **PNG/CNG expansion**, **CBM (Coal Bed Methane)** policy rounds --- ## 1.6 Section Summary (Concept Lock-in) In one sentence: **The Present Status of Oil and Natural Gas is a snapshot of the planet's hydrocarbon reserve geography, production-consumption dynamics, and strategic positioning of major players — pivotal for understanding energy security, pricing, and the transition roadmap.** # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Five Analytical Pillars of "Present Status" The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to interpret "Present Status" through **five structured analytical pillars**. Each pillar is dissected below with logical sub-steps. ### Pillar 1 — Global Reserve Geography (Who has what?) **Step 1:** Oil reserves are geographically **concentrated**, not evenly distributed. - **Middle East** holds nearly **~50%** of global proved oil reserves. - **Venezuela** (Orinoco Belt, ultra-heavy crude) is technically #1 by reserves but #12 by production due to economic/technological constraints. - **North America** (USA + Canada) holds **~20%** of global oil, with Canada dominating via **oil sands (Athabasca)**. **Step 2:** Natural gas reserves are slightly more geographically dispersed. - **Russia** holds the largest gas reserves (~24%). - **Iran and Qatar** together hold the **South Pars / North Dome** field — the world's largest gas accumulation. - **USA's shale revolution** unlocked unconventional gas in the **Marcellus, Barnett, Haynesville** shales. **Step 3:** Why does this matter? - Reserves determine **long-term pricing leverage**. - Concentration in politically sensitive regions creates **supply vulnerability** for importing nations. --- ### Pillar 2 — Production Dynamics (Who is producing how much?) **Step 1:** Top oil producers (approximate ranking, recent years): 1. **USA** — leading global producer, ~12–13 MMbpd, dominantly **tight oil** (Bakken, Eagle Ford, Permian Basin). 2. **Saudi Arabia** — ~10–11 MMbpd, anchored by **Ghawar** field (world's largest conventional onshore field). 3. **Russia** — ~9–10 MMbpd, with West Siberian mature fields. 4. **Canada** — ~5–6 MMbpd, oil-sands heavy. 5. **Iraq, China, Iran, UAE** — each 3–5 MMbpd. **Step 2:** Top gas producers: 1. **USA** — dominant, ~24 TCM/year, largely shale. 2. **Russia** — ~22 TCM/year, with **Yamburg, Urengoy, Medvezhye**. 3. **Iran, Qatar, Canada, China, Norway** — significant. **Step 3:** India's production: - Crude: ~30 MMT/year (~0.6 MMbpd) — negligible relative to consumption. - Gas: ~32 BCM/year — modest. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Production determines **OPEC+ quotas**, **spot market liquidity**, and the **geopolitical bargaining power** of nations. --- ### Pillar 3 — Consumption Pattern & Demand Centers **Step 1:** Global oil consumption is dominated by **transportation (~65%)** — the most "hard-to-abate" sector. - USA, China, India are the top three consumers. - India is now the **fastest-growing petroleum products market** (diesel, gasoline, ATF). **Step 2:** Global gas consumption is dominated by **power generation, industrial heat, and city gas distribution (CGD)**. - Industrial use: fertilizers, petrochemicals, refining. - CGD: PNG to households, CNG to vehicles. **Step 3:** The **demand-side asymmetry** is the structural feature: - Developed world = plateauing demand (efficiency gains, EVs). - Developing world = rising demand (industrialization, mobility). - Result: **Asia-Pacific demand growth offsets OECD decline.** --- ### Pillar 4 — The R/P (Reserve-to-Production) Ratio The **single most important KTU-question-friendly metric.** **Step 4.1:** Formula derivation: $$ R/P \text{ Ratio (years)} = \frac{\text{Proved Reserves at end of year}}{\text{Annual Production in that year}} $$ **Step 4.2:** Interpretation: - Global oil R/P ~ **50+ years**. - Global gas R/P ~ **50+ years**. - But this is **static** — assumes no new discoveries, no growth in production, and current technology. - The **dynamic R/P** (accounting for new discoveries, technology) gives a different picture. **Step 4.3:** Strategic significance: - Low R/P → **exploration urgency** (e.g., India ~15–20 years for oil). - High R/P → **strategic optionality** (e.g., Saudi Arabia ~70+ years). --- ### Pillar 5 — Unconventional Hydrocarbons & the Transition Era **Step 1:** **Shale gas/tight oil** has rewritten global rankings since 2010. - USA's shale boom transformed it from a net importer to the **largest producer**. - China's, Argentina's (Vaca Muerta), and Saudi Arabia's shale potential is being actively developed. **Step 2:** **CBM (Coal Bed Methane)** and **Gas Hydrates** are frontier resources. - India has CBM blocks in Jharkhand, West Bengal. - India's **National Gas Hydrate Programme (NGHP)** is exploring the Krishna-Godavari, Mahanadi, and Andaman basins. **Step 3:** **The Transition Paradox:** - Hydrocarbons still supply **~80%** of primary energy. - Yet, IEA Net-Zero pathways project a decline. - Present Status sits in this **tension zone** — hydrocarbons remain king, but renewables are rising. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Proved Reserves (1P) | Volumes recoverable with **>90% probability** under current tech & economics | MMbbl (oil), BCM (gas) | Reserve booking | | 2 | Probable Reserves (2P) | 1P + reserves with **>50% probability** | Same | Reserve estimation | | 3 | Possible Reserves (3P) | 2P + reserves with **>10% probability** | Same | Speculative cases | | 4 | R/P Ratio | $\dfrac{R}{P}$ | years | Reserve life indicator | | 5 | Oil Recovery Factor (RF) | $\dfrac{N_p}{OOIP}$ | fraction (or %) | Field maturity | | 6 | OOIP (Original Oil In Place) | $V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_{wc})$ / B₀ | MMbbl | Reservoir estimation | | 7 | OGIP (Original Gas In Place) | $V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_{wc})$ | TCF | Gas reservoir | | 8 | Energy Equivalence | 1 MTOE $\approx$ 7.33 oil bbl $\approx$ 1,100 Nm³ gas | unitless | Cross-fuel accounting | | 9 | Hubbert Peak Model | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = k \cdot P \cdot (1 - P/URR)$ | MMbpd/year | Production forecasting | | 10 | Decline Curve (Exponential) | $q = q_i \cdot e^{-Dt}$ | bpd | Production analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **Proved Reserves** with **OOIP**. Remember: *OOIP* is the total oil-in-place in the reservoir rock; *Proved Reserves* is the *recoverable fraction* = OOIP × Recovery Factor. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sector | How "Present Status" Data is Used | |---|---| | **Exploration Companies** | Identifying underexplored basins for licensing rounds | | **Refiners (IOCs)** | Crude sourcing strategy, hedging decisions | | **Geopolitics / Diplomacy** | Energy security treaties, OPEC negotiations | | **Climate Policy** | Setting realistic transition timelines | | **Financial Markets** | Valuing E&P stocks, project financing | | **Academia (KTU)** | Quantitative assessment of national energy security | --- ## 2.4 Section Summary (Lock the Logic) The "Present Status" is not a static data-dump — it is a **structured diagnostic** that uses five analytical lenses (Reserves, Production, Consumption, R/P, Unconventionals) to map the global hydrocarbon landscape and to inform national-level strategic decisions. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: The R/P Ratio (Reserve Life) Formula > The R/P ratio is the **simplest and most asked** quantitative topic in this module. **Given:** - Proved Reserves at year-end, $R$ (in MMbbl or BCM). - Annual production in that year, $P$ (in MMbpd or MMCM/year). **Step 1:** Express both quantities in **consistent units**. If $P$ is given in MMbpd, convert to MMbbl/year: $$ P_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{daily}} \times 365 \text{ days} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the definition — "How many years will reserves last if I keep producing at this rate?" $$ \text{Reserve Life (years)} = \dfrac{R_{\text{year-end}}}{P_{\text{annual}}} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for a sample (numerical evaluation): > **Sample:** A country has $R = 700$ MMbbl of proved oil reserves, and produces $P = 1.0$ MMbpd. Compute the R/P ratio. Convert $P$ to annual: $$ P_{\text{annual}} = 1.0 \text{ MMbpd} \times 365 = 365 \text{ MMbbl/year} $$ Apply formula: $$ R/P = \dfrac{700 \text{ MMbbl}}{365 \text{ MMbbl/year}} = 1.92 \text{ years} $$ > [!NOTE] > A 1.92-year R/P is extremely low — typical of a heavily producing, low-reserve region. India's R/P ratio for oil is **~15 years**, much lower than the global average of ~50 years, reflecting the **import-dependence dilemma**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Original Oil In Place (OOIP) — Volumetric Method This derivation is the **KTU board-favorite** for Part B questions. **Step 1:** Start with the **rock volume**. $$ V_b = A \times h $$ where $A$ = reservoir area (acres or m²), $h$ = average reservoir thickness (ft or m). **Step 2:** Multiply by **porosity** ($\phi$) — fraction of rock that is void. $$ V_p = V_b \times \phi $$ **Step 3:** Multiply by **hydrocarbon saturation** $(1 - S_{wc})$ — fraction of pore space occupied by oil. $$ V_{oil \text{ in pore}} = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc}) $$ **Step 4:** Convert **surface volume** using **formation volume factor** $B_{oi}$ (rbbl/stb). $$ OOIP = \dfrac{V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc})}{B_{oi}} $$ **Worked Example:** > A reservoir has $A = 1{,}000$ acres, $h = 50$ ft, $\phi = 0.20$, $S_{wc} = 0.25$, $B_{oi} = 1.25$ rbbl/stb. **Step (a):** Compute bulk volume in acre-ft: $$ V_b = 1000 \times 50 = 50{,}000 \text{ acre-ft} $$ **Step (b):** Convert to reservoir barrels. Use $1$ acre-ft $= 7758$ bbl. $$ V_b = 50{,}000 \times 7758 = 387{,}900{,}000 \text{ bbl} $$ **Step (c):** Apply porosity and saturation: $$ \text{Oil volume} = 387.9 \times 10^6 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.25) $$ $$ = 387.9 \times 10^6 \times 0.20 \times 0.75 $$ $$ = 58.185 \times 10^6 \text{ rbbl} $$ **Step (d):** Convert to stock-tank barrels: $$ OOIP = \dfrac{58.185 \times 10^6}{1.25} = 46.548 \times 10^6 \text{ stb} $$ $$ \boxed{OOIP \approx 46.55 \text{ MMbbl}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Recovery Factor (RF) **Step 1:** Definition — fraction of OOIP that is economically recoverable. $$ RF = \dfrac{N_p}{OOIP} $$ where $N_p$ = cumulative oil produced to date (or estimated ultimate recovery, EUR). **Step 2:** For **waterflood (secondary recovery)** in sandstone reservoirs, the empirical **API correlation**: $$ RF = 0.114 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc})}{\mu_{ob}}\right)^{0.2156} \cdot \left(\dfrac{k \cdot S_{wc}}{\mu_{ob}}\right)^{0.1305} $$ > **NOTE:** This empirical form is for illustration; the KTU expects conceptual awareness, not memorization of constants. **Step 3:** Typical RF ranges: | Reservoir Type | Typical RF | |---|---| | Light oil, strong aquifer | 30–50% | | Heavy oil (cold production) | 5–15% | | Thermal EOR (heavy oil) | 25–40% | | Shale/tight (primary) | 5–15% | **Step 4:** Sample application: > Given $OOIP = 46.55$ MMbbl (from §3.2), and $RF = 0.35$, compute **recoverable reserves**. $$ N_p = OOIP \times RF = 46.55 \times 0.35 = 16.29 \text{ MMbbl} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Hubbert Peak (Logistic Model) **Step 1:** Hubbert proposed that annual production follows a **logistic (S-curve)**. $$ P(t) = \dfrac{2 \cdot P_{\max}}{1 + \cosh\left[k \cdot (t - t_{\max})\right]} $$ Equivalently, the **differential form**: $$ \dfrac{dP}{dt} = a \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P}{URR}\right) $$ where $P$ = annual production, $URR$ = ultimately recoverable resources, $a$ = a constant. **Step 2:** Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = 0$ (peak condition): $$ P = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad P = URR \quad (\text{boundary cases}) $$ The **production peak** occurs when cumulative production $C(t) = URR / 2$. **Step 3:** Interpretation: - Hubbert's model **predicted** a peak in US oil production around 1970 — and it was correct. - Application to global oil: estimates of the global peak range from **passed (some analysts)** to **2030–2040 (most)**. --- ## 3.5 Energy Unit Conversion (Cross-Fuel Accounting) **Step 1:** Use the standard petroleum equivalences: $$ 1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 10{,}034 \text{ Mcal} $$ **Step 2:** Cross-fuel conversion examples: $$ 1 \text{ MTOE of oil} \approx 7.33 \text{ MMbbl of oil} \approx 1{,}100 \text{ MMNm}^3 \text{ of gas} $$ **Step 3:** Sample: A country consumes 100 MTOE of gas. What is the equivalent oil volume? $$ V_{oil\,eq} = \dfrac{100}{7.33 \times (1/7.33)} \approx \dfrac{100 \times 1{,}100}{1{,}000} \approx 110 \text{ MMbbl oil eq.} $$ > Or more directly: $$ \text{Gas volume} = 100 \text{ MTOE} \times 1{,}100 \text{ MNm}^3/\text{MTOE} = 1.1 \times 10^5 \text{ MNm}^3 = 110 \text{ BCM} $$ --- ## 3.6 The Reserve Pyramid (Visual Hierarchy) To internalize the **uncertainty layering** of reserves: $$ \boxed{\text{Possible (3P)} \supset \text{Probable (2P)} \supset \text{Proved (1P)}} $$ - **1P**: Conservative, used by financial analysts for **SEC-compliant reporting**. - **2P**: Most common in upstream economics. - **3P**: Used for **internal portfolio screening** by majors (ExxonMobil, Shell, BP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks "Define proved reserves", always state the **probability threshold (P90 = 90%)** and the **economic-operational condition** ("under current prices and technology"). Half-marks are routinely lost for missing these qualifiers. --- ## 3.7 Section Summary The derivations above equip you to solve numerical problems on: - R/P ratio calculation (most common) - OOIP via volumetric method (board favorite) - Recovery Factor estimation - Energy unit conversion - Hubbert peak conceptual interpretation # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Global Hydrocarbon Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between reserves, production, consumption, and geopolitics** that define the "Present Status". ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Underground Reservoirs

Solar Wind EV Adoption] J --> F K[Discovery and Exploration

Government SPR] --> E subgraph TopProducers direction LR N1[USA Tight Oil and Shale] N2[Saudi Arabia Conventional] N3[Russia West Siberia] N4[Canada Oil Sands] end B --> N1 B --> N2 B --> N3 B --> N4 subgraph TopConsumers direction LR C1[USA Transport Heavy] C2[China Industry and Power] C3[India Mobility and Industry] end F --> C1 F --> C2 F --> C3 ``` --- ## 4.2 Reserves-Production-Consumption Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR Reserves[Proved Reserves
Static and Dynamic] --> RPRatio[RP Ratio Calculation] Production[Annual Production
MMbpd or BCM] --> RPRatio RPRatio --> LifeIndex[Reserve Life Index Years] Reserves --> Tech[Technology and EOR] Tech --> Production Reserves --> Discovery[New Discoveries and Appraisals] Discovery --> Reserves Production --> Market[Global Supply Demand] Consumption[Sectoral Consumption
Transport Industry Power] --> Market Market --> Price[Crude and Gas Price USD per bbl MMBtu] Price --> Investment[E and P Investment Decisions] Investment --> Discovery Investment --> Tech ``` --- ## 4.3 Reserve Classification Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD P3[Possible Reserves 3P
P10 Confidence

High Demand Low Domestic Supply] India --> Demand[Demand Side
Third Largest Consumer] Demand --> OilDemand[Oil Demand Transport Heavy] Demand --> GasDemand[Gas Demand Power CGD Fertilizer] India --> Supply[Supply Side
Import Dependence Above 85 Percent Oil] Supply --> Imports[Import Sources
Russia Iraq Saudi UAE Nigeria] Supply --> Domestic[Domestic Upstream
ONGC OIL Reliance Vedanta] India --> Policy[Policy and Strategic Initiatives] Policy --> Policy1[Open Acreage Licensing Policy OALP] Policy --> Policy2[Discovered Small Field Policy DSF] Policy --> Policy3[City Gas Distribution CGD Bidding Rounds] Policy --> Policy4[Strategic Petroleum Reserve SPR Phase 1 and 2] India --> Future[Future Vectors] Future --> F1[Shale Exploration Policy] Future --> F2[CBM and Gas Hydrates NGHP] Future --> F3[Ethanol Blending E20] Future --> F4[Green Hydrogen and Renewable Push] ``` --- ## 4.5 OPEC+ Influence Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR Members[OPEC Plus Member Nations
Saudi UAE Iraq Kuwait Iran Russia] --> Quota[Production Quota Allocation] Quota --> Supply[Aggregate Supply Control] Supply --> Price[Global Crude Price Benchmark Brent WTI] Price --> Revenue[Member Nation Revenue] Revenue --> Members Price --> NonMembers[Non OPEC Producers
USA Canada Brazil Guyana] NonMembers --> Supply External[External Shocks
Geopolitical Conflict Pandemic Demand Shocks] --> Price ``` --- ## 4.6 Section Summary These five schematics collectively visualize: 1. The **end-to-end hydrocarbon value chain** in present-day terms. 2. The **feedback loops** that govern reserve life and price. 3. The **uncertainty layering** in reserves classification. 4. The **structural energy security challenge** for India. 5. The **OPEC+ pricing influence cycle**. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define the term "Proved Reserves" as used in petroleum engineering. State its probability threshold.** **Model Answer:** Proved Reserves (1P) are those quantities of oil and natural gas that, upon analysis of geological and engineering data, can be estimated with **reasonable certainty** to be economically recoverable in future years from known reservoirs under existing economic and operating conditions. - **Probability threshold:** **P90** — meaning there is at least a **90% probability** that the actual recoverable volume will equal or exceed the estimated proved reserves. - Reported on a **stock-tank barrel basis** for oil at standard surface conditions. - Audited under **SEC guidelines** for listed companies. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Award only **partial credit (1.5/3)** if the student omits the probability qualifier or the "economic recoverability" clause. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio? Why is it important for a country like India?** **Model Answer:** The R/P ratio is defined as: $$ R/P \text{ (years)} = \dfrac{\text{Proved Reserves at year-end}}{\text{Annual Production in that year}} $$ It represents the **static reserve life** — the number of years a country can sustain production at current rates without new discoveries. **Importance for India:** - India's oil R/P ratio is **~15 years** (vs. global ~50 years), signalling **acute import dependence**. - Drives policy on **exploration licensing, EOR, strategic reserves, and diplomatic crude sourcing**. - Influences **national energy security planning** under India's NEP framework. --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **global present status of oil and natural gas**, covering the top producers, reserve concentration, and consumption centers. Highlight the role of OPEC+. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Calculate the **Original Oil In Place (OOIP)** for a sandstone reservoir having the following data: - Area = 2,000 acres - Average thickness = 40 ft - Porosity = 0.22 - Connate water saturation = 0.20 - Oil formation volume factor = 1.30 rbbl/stb If the expected **Recovery Factor** is 35%, compute the **recoverable oil reserves**. *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Global Present Status (7 Marks) **1. Reserves Concentration (2 marks):** - Middle East holds ~50% of global proved oil reserves. - Russia, Iran, Qatar hold ~75% of global gas reserves. - Venezuela tops the chart technically but is constrained economically. **2. Top Oil Producers (2 marks):** - **USA** (~12–13 MMbpd), **Saudi Arabia** (~10 MMbpd), **Russia** (~9–10 MMbpd). - USA's rise is **tight-oil driven** (Permian, Bakken). - Saudi Arabia's dominance rests on **Ghawar field**. **3. Consumption Centers (1.5 marks):** - USA, China, India are the top three consumers. - Asia-Pacific is the **demand growth engine** of the next two decades. **4. OPEC+ Role (1.5 marks):** - OPEC+ controls ~50% of global supply via **production quotas**. - The cartel balances **revenue maximization** against **market share defense** (vs. US shale). - Influences **Brent/WTI benchmarks** through coordinated supply adjustments. --- #### Part (b) — OOIP and Recoverable Reserves (7 Marks) **Step 1: Bulk Volume** $$ V_b = A \times h = 2000 \text{ acres} \times 40 \text{ ft} = 80{,}000 \text{ acre-ft} $$ **Step 2: Convert to Barrels** (1 acre-ft = 7,758 bbl) $$ V_b = 80{,}000 \times 7758 = 6.2064 \times 10^8 \text{ bbl} = 620.64 \times 10^6 \text{ bbl} $$ **Step 3: Apply Porosity and Saturation** $$ \text{HC Pore Volume} = V_b \times \phi \times (1 - S_{wc}) $$ $$ = 620.64 \times 10^6 \times 0.22 \times (1 - 0.20) $$ $$ = 620.64 \times 10^6 \times 0.22 \times 0.80 $$ $$ = 109.23 \times 10^6 \text{ rbbl} $$ **Step 4: Convert to Stock-Tank Barrels using $B_{oi}$** $$ OOIP = \dfrac{109.23 \times 10^6}{1.30} = 84.02 \times 10^6 \text{ stb} $$ $$ \boxed{OOIP \approx 84.02 \text{ MMbbl}} $$ **Step 5: Apply Recovery Factor** $$ \text{Recoverable Reserves} = OOIP \times RF = 84.02 \times 0.35 = 29.41 \text{ MMbbl} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Recoverable Reserves} \approx 29.41 \text{ MMbbl}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes:** > 1. Forgetting the unit conversion **1 acre-ft = 7,758 bbl** — leading to answers 4 orders of magnitude wrong. > 2. Using **0.22 × 0.20** instead of **0.22 × 0.80** (confusing oil saturation with water saturation). > 3. Dividing by $B_{oi}$ instead of multiplying (B₀ > 1, so it reduces surface volume). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss the **present status of India's hydrocarbon sector**, with emphasis on reserves, production, consumption, import dependence, and recent policy reforms. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What are **unconventional hydrocarbon resources**? Explain the rise of shale gas and tight oil, and assess their impact on the global present status. *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B #### Part (a) — India's Hydrocarbon Present Status (7 Marks) **1. Reserves Profile (1.5 marks):** - Proved oil reserves: ~600 MMT (~4.4 Giga-bbl) — small relative to demand. - Proved gas reserves: ~1.4 TCM. **2. Production Status (1.5 marks):** - Crude: ~30 MMT/year — declining from peak fields. - Gas: ~32 BCM/year — incl. CBM contributions. **3. Consumption & Import Dependence (2 marks):** - Oil demand: ~250 MMT/year → import ~85%. - Gas demand: ~60 BCM/year → import ~50% (rising). - Russia has become **#1 supplier** post recent geopolitical shifts. **4. Policy Reforms (2 marks):** - **OALP** (Open Acreage Licensing Policy) — flexible exploration blocks. - **DSF** (Discovered Small Field) — monetization of marginal fields. - **HELP** (Hydrocarbon Exploration and Licensing Policy) — single uniform licensing. - **CGD rounds** — 100% PNG/CNG coverage by ~2030. - **Strategic Petroleum Reserve** — Phase 1 (5.33 MMT at Visakhapatnam, Mangaluru, Padur) operational. --- #### Part (b) — Unconventional Resources & Their Global Impact (7 Marks) **1. Definition (1.5 marks):** - Unconventional hydrocarbons are produced from **low-permeability reservoirs** requiring stimulation (fracturing, horizontal drilling, thermal injection). - Categories: **shale gas/tight oil, CBM, oil sands, gas hydrates**. **2. Shale Revolution in the USA (2.5 marks):** - Fracking + horizontal drilling enabled production from **Bakken, Eagle Ford, Permian Basin**. - USA transformed from **net importer to #1 producer in less than a decade**. - Tight oil now represents ~60% of US crude production. **3. Global Impact (2 marks):** - Reduced **OPEC's market share** — cartel shifted strategy toward defending price levels rather than volumes. - Lowered global crude prices in 2014–16, 2020. - Inspired shale exploration in **China, Argentina (Vaca Muerta), Saudi Arabia**. - Reshaped **geopolitics** — US energy independence altered Middle East strategy. **4. India's Position (1 mark):** - India has **assessed shale potential** in Cambay, KG, Cauvery, Assam-Arakan basins. - **Shale policy** under evolution; first exploration rounds being designed. - CBM blocks operational in **Raniganj, Jharia, Bokaro**. - **NGHP expeditions** drilling gas hydrates offshore. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **shale gas** (source rock = reservoir) with **tight gas** (low-perm sandstone/carbonate). Both need fracking but have different geology. Award marks only if the distinction is implicit or stated. --- ## 5.3 ADDITIONAL SHORT-TYPE QUESTIONS (For Quick Practice) | # | Question (2 marks) | One-line Answer | |---|---|---| | 1 | Name the world's largest producing oil field. | **Ghawar**, Saudi Arabia | | 2 | What is OPEC's main lever for price control? | **Production quota allocation** | | 3 | India's crude oil import dependence is approximately? | **~85%** | | 4 | Define Recovery Factor. | **RF = N_p / OOIP** | | 5 | The R/P ratio for global oil is approximately? | **~50 years** | | 6 | India's Strategic Petroleum Reserve Phase 1 capacity? | **~5.33 MMT** | | 7 | Name one major gas field of Russia. | **Urengoy / Yamburg / Medvezhye** | | 8 | What does SEC-compliant reserve booking require? | **P90 probability**, current economic conditions | | 9 | What is the formula for OOIP volumetric method? | **OOIP = (A·h·φ·(1-S_wc)) / B_oi** | | 10 | Largest gas field in the world? | **South Pars / North Dome** (Iran-Qatar) | --- ## 5.4 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on "Present Status" questions:** > > 1. **Citing outdated data**: Using pre-2010 figures (USA was NOT the top producer before shale boom). Always contextualize the year. > 2. **Confusing India-specific numbers**: India's R/P ratio is **NOT** the same as global. State the value with year context. > 3. **Missing the "Probable + Possible" qualifier**: When asked about reserves categories, **list 1P/2P/3P** with their P90/P50/P10 thresholds. > 4. **Forgetting units in calculations**: Numerical answers without units = **half credit**. > 5. **Ignoring OPEC+**: A "Present Status" answer without OPEC is **incomplete** (will cost ~1–1.5 marks). > 6. **Not mentioning unconventionals**: Post-2010, shale revolution is a **must-mention** topic. --- ## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Proved Reserves (1P)** → P90 confidence, economically recoverable, SEC-audited. - ✅ **R/P Ratio** = $R \div P$ → static reserve life, ~50 years globally, ~15 years for India. - ✅ **OOIP Formula** = $\frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc})}{B_{oi}}$ — memorize the structure. - ✅ **Recovery Factor** = $N_p / OOIP$ → 30–40% typical, <15% for heavy/tight. - ✅ **Global Top Producers (Oil)** → USA, Saudi Arabia, Russia, Canada, Iraq. - ✅ **Global Top Producers (Gas)** → USA, Russia, Iran, Qatar, Canada. - ✅ **Reserve Concentration** → Middle East ~50% oil; Russia + Iran + Qatar ~75% gas. - ✅ **OPEC+** → controls ~50% of supply via production quotas; influences Brent/WTI benchmarks. - ✅ **India's Dilemma** → 3rd largest consumer, <15% self-sufficient in oil, <50% in gas. - ✅ **India's R/P (oil)** ~15 years → strategic urgency for exploration and EOR. - ✅ **Shale Revolution** → USA's tight-oil boom post-2010, transformed global rankings. - ✅ **Unconventionals** → shale gas/tight oil, CBM, gas hydrates, oil sands. - ✅ **Hubbert Peak** → logistic S-curve model, predicts mid-point of production peak. - ✅ **Energy Unit** → 1 MTOE ≈ 7.33 MMbbl oil ≈ 1,100 MMNm³ gas. - ✅ **Strategic Reserves** → India's SPR Phase 1 = 5.33 MMT (3 locations). - ✅ **India's Reforms** → OALP, DSF, HELP, CGD rounds, ethanol blending E20. ### 🧠 Three Things to Say in Every "Present Status" Answer 1. **State the global picture**: reserves concentration, top producers, top consumers. 2. **Mention India specifically**: import dependence, R/P ratio, policy reforms. 3. **Highlight unconventionals**: shale revolution, CBM, gas hydrates — show awareness of recent shifts. ### 🔑 Mnemonics - **Top 5 Oil Reserves (by % share)**: **V**enezuela, **S**audi **A**rabia, **C**anada, **I**ran, **I**raq → **"VSA CII"** (think "V's A CII"). - **Top Gas Reserves**: **R**ussia, **I**ran, **Q**atar, **T**urkmenistan, **U**SA → **"RIQTU"**. - **Reserve Categories**: **1P** (P90), **2P** (P50), **3P** (P10) → "**90-50-10**". --- > **End of Notes — Present Status | Module 2 | PECHT522** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 Aligned*

topics.title: Formation and Properties of Coal Bed Methane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formation and Properties of Coal Bed Methane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)**, also termed **Coal Seam Gas (CSG)** in Australia or **Coal Mine Methane (CMM)** in mining contexts, is an **unconventional natural gas** resource composed predominantly of **methane (CH₄)** that is generated, stored, and produced from **bituminous and sub-bituminous coal seams** rather than from conventional sandstone or carbonate reservoirs.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus conventions for PECHT522, CBM is classified as a **self-sourced, continuous-type unconventional gas accumulation** where the coal acts as both the **source rock** and the **reservoir rock**, distinguishing it fundamentally from conventional gas plays where source and reservoir are separate lithologic units.

### Standard Composition of CBM

| Component | Typical Mole % |
|---|---|
| **Methane (CH₄)** | **80 – 95 %** |
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | 0 – 10 % |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 0 – 10 % |
| Ethane (C₂H₆) | Traces – 2 % |
| Heavy Hydrocarbons (C₃+) | Traces |
| Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | Traces (rare) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Wet Sponge Wrung Out"**
> Imagine a **kitchen sponge** soaked in water. The water sticks to the sponge's internal surfaces. You cannot squeeze it out easily until you press harder — only when the squeezing force exceeds the **"stickiness threshold"** does the water flow freely.
>
> A coal seam behaves like this sponge: Methane molecules are **physically adsorbed** onto the immense internal surface area of coal's microporous matrix. Production begins only when the **reservoir pressure drops below the critical desorption pressure**, just like wringing a sponge.

The **standard metric** for surface area in coal is **200 – 300 m²/g**, making a 1-tonne block of coal possess an internal surface equivalent to **50 football fields**.

## 1.3 Why CBM is a Unique Unconventional Resource

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinguishing Features:**
> 1. Coal is **both source and reservoir** (no migration needed).
> 2. Gas storage is **primarily by adsorption** (not free gas as in conventional plays).
> 3. Coal seams have **dual porosity**: microporous matrix + naturally fractured cleat system.
> 4. Gas must be **desorbed first**, then diffuses through the matrix, then flows through cleats.
> 5. Production requires **dewatering** before gas flows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Coalification (Coal Genesis)

The formation of CBM begins with **coalification** — the progressive transformation of plant debris into coal under heat and pressure. Each rank produces a different quantity and quality of methane.

| Stage (Coal Rank) | Fixed Carbon (%) | Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro %) | Methane Generation Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peat** | < 30 | 0.20 – 0.30 | Negligible |
| **Lignite** | 30 – 40 | 0.30 – 0.40 | Very Low |
| **Sub-bituminous** | 40 – 50 | 0.40 – 0.60 | Low |
| **Bituminous (HV)** | 50 – 60 | 0.60 – 0.80 | Moderate |
| **Bituminous (MV)** | 60 – 70 | 0.80 – 1.20 | High |
| **Bituminous (LV)** | 70 – 80 | 1.20 – 1.50 | Very High |
| **Semi-anthracite** | 80 – 90 | 1.50 – 2.00 | High (declining) |
| **Anthracite** | 90 – 98 | > 2.00 | Low – Dry gas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sweet Spot for CBM Exploration:** Bituminous coals with **Ro ≈ 0.8 – 1.2 %** typically offer the **best balance** of gas content and permeability.

## 2.2 Mechanisms of Methane Generation

### (a) Thermogenic Pathway
- Occurs during burial and heating of coal (**Ro > 0.6 %**).
- Methane is liberated by **cracking of kerogen** and coal macerals.
- Reaction summary:

$$\text{Kerogen} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{heat}} \text{Hydrocarbons} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CH}_4 \uparrow$$

### (b) Biogenic Pathway
- Occurs at **low temperatures (≤ 56 °C)** via methanogenic archaea.
- Two sub-types:
  - **Primary Biogenic** — formed during early peat stage.
  - **Secondary Biogenic** — formed when meteoric water introduces microbes into mature seams via groundwater recharge.

$$\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{methanogens}} \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{methanogens}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

## 2.3 Storage Mechanisms of Methane in Coal

Methane in a coal seam exists in **three simultaneous states**:

| Storage Mode | % of Total Gas | Location | Release Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adsorbed** | **85 – 95 %** | Internal microporous surface | **Pressure decline (desorption)** |
| **Free Gas** | 5 – 12 % | Cleats and natural fractures | Pressure-driven Darcy flow |
| **Dissolved** | 1 – 3 % | Formation water in cleats | Exsolution on depressurization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption** is governed by weak **van der Waals forces** between CH₄ molecules and the carbonaceous coal surface. The dominant model is the **Langmuir Isotherm**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are high-priority for KTU board examinations on this topic.**

### A. Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

$$V(P) = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V(P)$ | Gas content at pressure $P$ | scf/ton or cc/g |
| $V_L$ | Langmuir volume (max sorption capacity) | scf/ton or cc/g |
| $P_L$ | Langmuir pressure (pressure at 0.5 $V_L$) | psia or kPa |
| $P$ | Reservoir pressure | psia or kPa |

### B. Critical Desorption Pressure

$$P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot C_g}{V_L - C_g}$$

Where $C_g$ is the **in-situ gas content** measured from canister desorption tests.

> A coal seam produces gas **without compression work only when** $P_{reservoir} > P_{cd}$.

### C. Gas-in-Place (GIP) per unit area of coal

$$GIP = 1.359 \times 10^{-5} \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot C_g$$

| Variable | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $h$ | Net coal thickness (ft) |
| $\rho_c$ | Coal bulk density (g/cm³) |
| $C_g$ | Gas content (scf/ton) |
| $GIP$ | Gas in place (scf per acre) |

### D. Diffusivity (Fickian Transport Through Matrix)

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

Where $D$ is the **diffusion coefficient** (typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ m²/s for coal).

### E. Cleat Porosity – Permeability Relation

$$k = \frac{\phi_c \cdot b^2}{12 \cdot \tau}$$

Where $b$ is the **aperture (face-cleat spacing)**, $\phi_c$ is **cleat porosity**, $\tau$ is **tortuosity**.

### F. Coal Density – Ash Correction

$$\rho_{coal,daf} = \frac{\rho_{coal,raw} - A/100}{1 - A/100}$$

Where $A$ is the ash fraction (wt%). "daf" = dry, ash-free.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Reservoir Engineering:** Designing dewatering schedules and predicting gas breakthrough time.
- **Drilling Engineering:** Selecting underbalanced drilling muds to prevent formation damage.
- **Coal Mine Safety:** Pre-methane drainage to avoid explosions (CMM extraction).
- **Environmental Engineering:** Carbon sequestration via CO₂ injection into coal (ECBM — Enhanced CBM).
- **Production Engineering:** Designing artificial lift systems for water disposal during dewatering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (Rigorous Kinetic Approach)

The Langmuir isotherm can be derived from **first-order kinetic arguments** describing dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption at the coal surface.

### Step 1 — Define the Rates

Let $\theta$ be the **fractional surface coverage** of the coal by methane molecules.
- **Rate of adsorption** is proportional to the gas pressure $P$ and the **fraction of vacant sites** $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

- **Rate of desorption** is proportional to the **fraction of occupied sites** $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

Where $k_a$ (adsorption rate constant) and $k_d$ (desorption rate constant) are temperature-dependent Arrhenius parameters.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition

At thermodynamic equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta \left( k_d + k_a P \right)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \frac{P}{\dfrac{k_d}{k_a} + P}$$

### Step 4 — Define Langmuir Pressure

Let $P_L \equiv \dfrac{k_d}{k_a}$. Substituting:

$$\theta = \frac{P}{P_L + P}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Coverage to Gas Content

If $V_L$ is the **maximum sorption capacity** (corresponding to monolayer coverage $\theta = 1$), then the actual gas content is $V = \theta \cdot V_L$. Hence:

$$\boxed{V(P) = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}}$$

This is the **Langmuir isotherm** universally used in CBM reservoir characterization.

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Desorption Pressure

Starting from the Langmuir isotherm, the **in-situ gas content** $C_g$ corresponds to the reservoir pressure $P_i$:

$$C_g = \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}$$

Solving for $P_i$ (the reservoir pressure at which the coal holds $C_g$):

$$C_g (P_L + P_i) = V_L P_i$$

$$C_g P_L + C_g P_i = V_L P_i$$

$$C_g P_L = V_L P_i - C_g P_i = P_i (V_L - C_g)$$

$$P_i = \frac{C_g P_L}{V_L - C_g}$$

This reservoir pressure at which desorption **begins** is termed the **Critical Desorption Pressure (P_cd)**. As long as $P_i > P_{cd}$ during depletion, gas desorbs spontaneously without requiring artificial pressure support.

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = \frac{C_g \cdot P_L}{V_L - C_g}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Gas-in-Place (GIP)

Starting from the definition of gas content per unit mass, for a coal volume of area $A$ and thickness $h$:

$$\text{Volume of coal} = A \cdot h$$

$$\text{Mass of coal} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho_c$$

$$\text{Gas stored} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot C_g$$

For $A$ = 1 acre = 43,560 ft², $h$ in ft, $\rho_c$ in g/cm³, $C_g$ in scf/ton, and applying unit conversions (1 acre-ft of coal × density × gas content), the empirical **Mavor & Pratt** form gives:

$$\boxed{GIP = 1.359 \times 10^{-5} \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot C_g \;\; (\text{scf/acre})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **industry-standard metric** for evaluating CBM prospect acreage and is directly referenced in **SEC reserves reporting**.
