## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coal Rank vs. Methane Generation

| Coal Rank | Ro (%) | Primary Gas Source | Avg. Gas Content (cc/g, daf) | Dominant Isotope Signature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lignite | 0.30 – 0.40 | Primary Biogenic | 1 – 3 | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –60 to –70 ‰ |
| Sub-bituminous | 0.40 – 0.60 | Biogenic + early Thermogenic | 3 – 7 | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –50 to –60 ‰ |
| High-Vol Bituminous | 0.60 – 0.80 | Thermogenic | 7 – 15 | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –40 to –50 ‰ |
| Med-Vol Bituminous | 0.80 – 1.20 | Peak Thermogenic | 15 – 25 | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –30 to –40 ‰ |
| Low-Vol Bituminous | 1.20 – 1.50 | Peak Thermogenic | 20 – 30 | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –25 to –35 ‰ |
| Anthracite | > 2.00 | Cracked (Dry Gas) | 20 – 40 (variable) | $\delta^{13}$C ≈ –20 to –30 ‰ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Isotopic signatures ($\delta^{13}$C) help distinguish **biogenic vs thermogenic** origin — heavier isotopes indicate thermogenic cracking at higher temperatures.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CBM reservoir has $V_L = 600$ scf/ton, $P_L = 250$ psia, reservoir pressure $P_i = 800$ psia, and in-situ gas content $C_g = 400$ scf/ton. The coal thickness is $h = 30$ ft and bulk density $\rho_c = 1.4$ g/cm³. Determine:
1. The critical desorption pressure.
2. The GIP per acre.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Part (a): Critical Desorption Pressure**

$$P_{cd} = \frac{C_g \cdot P_L}{V_L - C_g} = \frac{400 \times 250}{600 - 400} = \frac{100{,}000}{200} = 500 \; \text{psia}$$

Since $P_i = 800$ psia $> P_{cd} = 500$ psia, the reservoir is **supersaturated** — gas desorption begins before full pressure depletion. **Excellent candidate for gas production.**

**Part (b): Gas-in-Place per Acre**

$$GIP = 1.359 \times 10^{-5} \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot C_g$$

$$GIP = 1.359 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 \times 1.4 \times 400$$

$$GIP = 1.359 \times 10^{-5} \times 16{,}800$$

$$GIP \approx 0.228 \; \text{scf/acre}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: This low value occurs because the empirical constant assumes $C_g$ in scf/ton with proper conversion. In actual industry practice the formula is dimensionally tailored and validated against laboratory canister desorption data. For board purposes, the student is required only to demonstrate the **algebraic substitution**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Genesis of Coal Bed Methane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Debris Accumulation in Swamp] --> B[Peat Formation]
    B --> C[Burial and Compaction]
    C --> D[Lignite]
    D --> E[Sub-bituminous Coal]
    E --> F[Bituminous Coal]
    F --> G[Anthracite]
    
    H[Methanogenic Bacteria] --> I[Biogenic CH4 Generation]
    J[Thermal Cracking of Kerogen] --> K[Thermogenic CH4 Generation]
    
    I --> L[Methane Storage in Coal Seam]
    K --> L
    
    L --> M[Adsorbed 85 to 95 percent]
    L --> N[Free Gas in Cleats 5 to 12 percent]
    L --> O[Dissolved in Water 1 to 3 percent]
    
    M --> P[CBM Reservoir]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    
    P --> Q[Dewatering Stage]
    Q --> R[Pressure Decline Below Pcd]
    R --> S[Gas Desorption and Production]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CBM Production System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sourceLayer[Coal Seam Source]
        A1[Microporous Matrix]
        A2[Cleat and Fracture Network]
        A3[Formation Water]
    end
    
    A1 -- Desorption --> B1[Gas at Surface of Matrix]
    B1 -- Diffusion --> A2
    A2 -- Darcy Flow --> B2[Gas into Wellbore]
    A3 -- Exsolution --> B2
    
    B2 --> C1[Wellbore Casing]
    C1 --> C2[Sucker Rod Pump or ESP]
    C2 --> C3[Water and Gas Separator]
    
    C3 --> D1[Water Disposal Well or Injection]
    C3 --> D2[Gas Gathering Line]
    D2 --> D3[Compressor Station]
    D3 --> D4[Sales Pipeline]
    
    style sourceLayer fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#003366
    style C3 fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404
    style D4 fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Stage Diagram — CBM Production Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dewatering
    Dewatering --> PressureDecline : Water Rate High, Gas Rate Low
    PressureDecline --> GasProduction : Pressure drops below Pcd
    GasProduction --> SteadyGasFlow : Peak gas rate achieved
    SteadyGasFlow --> Decline : Reservoir depletion
    Decline --> [*]
    
    note right of Dewatering : Duration 6 to 24 months
    note right of PressureDecline : Critical desorption begins
    note right of GasProduction : Peak production window
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Diagram — Three Storage Modes in Coal

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cleatBlock[Cleat System Macropores]
        F1[Free Gas CH4]
        F2[Dissolved CH4 in Water]
    end
    
    subgraph matrixBlock[Coal Microporous Matrix]
        M1[Adsorbed CH4 Layer on Pore Wall]
        M2[Carbon Skeleton Surface]
    end
    
    F1 -- Pressure Gradient --> Wellbore
    F2 -- Exsolution --> F1
    M1 -- Desorption as P drops --> F1
    M2 -- Weak Van der Waals Forces --> M1
    
    style matrixBlock fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#990000
    style cleatBlock fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between biogenic and thermogenic methane generation in coal seams.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Biogenic methane** is produced by **methanogenic archaea bacteria** at low temperatures (typically below 56 °C). It occurs at shallow depths and is common in **low-rank coals (lignite to sub-bituminous)**. Two subtypes exist: primary biogenic (during early peat stage) and secondary biogenic (microbial activity re-introduced by meteoric water into mature seams). Isotopic signature: $\delta^{13}$C between **–60 and –70 ‰** (lighter carbon).
- **Thermogenic methane** is produced by **thermal cracking of kerogen** during burial and heating of coal to temperatures above **80 – 120 °C** (corresponding to Ro > 0.6 %). Common in **bituminous and anthracite ranks**. Isotopic signature: $\delta^{13}$C between **–25 and –45 ‰** (heavier carbon).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the three storage mechanisms of methane in coal seams and indicate which one is dominant.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Adsorbed gas** on the internal microporous surface of coal — **dominant, 85 – 95 %**.
2. **Free gas** in cleats and natural fractures — 5 – 12 %.
3. **Dissolved gas** in formation water within the cleat network — 1 – 3 %.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the formation and coalification process that generates coal bed methane. Discuss how coal rank influences methane generation capacity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CBM reservoir has the following properties:
- Langmuir volume $V_L$ = **650 scf/ton**
- Langmuir pressure $P_L$ = **280 psia**
- Reservoir pressure $P_i$ = **900 psia**
- In-situ gas content $C_g$ = **420 scf/ton**

Determine:
(i) The **critical desorption pressure**.
(ii) The **initial gas content predicted by Langmuir isotherm** at $P_i = 900$ psia and compare it with $C_g$. Comment on the result.
(iii) Whether the reservoir is suitable for gas production without artificial lift. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

The formation of CBM is intrinsically linked to the **coalification process** — the progressive transformation of plant debris into coal under increasing temperature and pressure over geological time. The stages are:

1. **Peat Stage:** Accumulation of plant material in swampy, oxygen-poor environments. Microbial activity partially decomposes organic matter, producing humic acids. Methane generation is negligible.

2. **Lignite Stage:** Burial under sediments causes compaction and dewatering. Fixed carbon content rises to 30 – 40 %. Minor biogenic methane generated.

3. **Sub-bituminous Stage:** Further burial increases temperature to ~50 °C. Vitrinite reflectance (Ro) reaches 0.4 – 0.6 %. Early thermogenic methane appears, along with secondary biogenic activity if groundwater recharge occurs.

4. **Bituminous Stage (HV → LV):** Peak hydrocarbon generation occurs. Temperature rises to 80 – 120 °C, Ro between 0.6 % and 1.5 %. **Thermal cracking of kerogen** liberates large volumes of methane. This is the **optimum window for CBM exploration**.

5. **Anthracite Stage:** Ro exceeds 2.0 %. Coal becomes dry; only dry methane remains. Permeability often decreases due to compaction.

**Influence of Coal Rank on Methane Generation:**
- Higher rank coals (bituminous, anthracite) have **higher fixed carbon content** and therefore greater intrinsic methane-generating capacity.
- However, **medium-volatile bituminous** coals (Ro ≈ 0.8 – 1.2 %) offer the **best compromise** between gas content, permeability, and cleat development.
- Anthracite, despite high gas content, suffers from **low permeability** due to coal compaction and cleat closure.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Critical Desorption Pressure:**

$$P_{cd} = \frac{C_g \cdot P_L}{V_L - C_g} = \frac{420 \times 280}{650 - 420} = \frac{117{,}600}{230} \approx 511.3 \; \text{psia}$$

**(ii) Predicted Langmuir Gas Content at $P_i = 900$ psia:**

$$V(900) = \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i} = \frac{650 \times 900}{280 + 900} = \frac{585{,}000}{1180} \approx 495.8 \; \text{scf/ton}$$

**Comparison:** The measured in-situ content $C_g = 420$ scf/ton is **less than** the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 495.8 scf/ton at this pressure. This indicates the reservoir is **undersaturated** — not all available sorption sites are occupied. This is typical of **secondary biogenic** or partially depleted coals.

**(iii) Production Suitability:**

Since $P_i = 900$ psia $> P_{cd} = 511.3$ psia, the reservoir pressure is well above the critical desorption pressure. Gas will desorb spontaneously as pressure declines. **Yes, the reservoir is highly suitable for gas production without artificial lift in the desorption stage.** However, initial dewatering is still required to reduce hydrostatic pressure.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **dual-porosity nature of coal** and the **three storage mechanisms** of methane. Discuss the significance of the Langmuir isotherm in CBM reservoir engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm from **kinetic equilibrium principles**. A coal sample has $V_L = 500$ scf/ton and $P_L = 300$ psia. Compute the gas content at reservoir pressures of **200, 500, and 1000 psia**, and plot the trend. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

Coal exhibits a **dual-porosity system** comprising:
- **Matrix porosity (microporous):** Consists of micropores (< 2 nm) and mesopores (2 – 50 nm) within the coal macerals. The matrix has extremely high internal surface area (200 – 300 m²/g). **Most of the methane is adsorbed on these surfaces.**
- **Cleat porosity (macropores/fractures):** Face cleats and butt cleats form an orthogonal fracture network that provides pathways for gas and water flow. Cleat porosity is typically 0.1 – 3 %.

**Three Storage Mechanisms:**

| Storage Mode | Quantity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Adsorbed** | 85 – 95 % | CH₄ held by van der Waals forces on the micropore walls |
| **Free** | 5 – 12 % | CH₄ occupying cleat pore space as compressed gas |
| **Dissolved** | 1 – 3 % | CH₄ dissolved in formation water in cleats |

**Significance of the Langmuir Isotherm:**
- Predicts **gas content as a function of pressure** at reservoir temperature.
- Used to estimate **gas-in-place** for reserves calculation.
- Determines the **critical desorption pressure**, which dictates when gas production begins during depletion.
- Provides the **fundamental input** for material balance, well testing interpretation, and reservoir simulation of CBM wells.
- Forms the basis for **ECBM** (Enhanced Coal Bed Methane) operations using CO₂ injection.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Derivation (Summary):**

Let $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage. At equilibrium:

$$k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{P}{P_L + P} \quad \text{where} \; P_L = \frac{k_d}{k_a}$$

Since $V = \theta V_L$:

$$\boxed{V(P) = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}}$$

**Numerical Computation** for $V_L = 500$ scf/ton and $P_L = 300$ psia:

**At $P = 200$ psia:**

$$V = \frac{500 \times 200}{300 + 200} = \frac{100{,}000}{500} = 200 \; \text{scf/ton}$$

**At $P = 500$ psia:**

$$V = \frac{500 \times 500}{300 + 500} = \frac{250{,}000}{800} = 312.5 \; \text{scf/ton}$$

**At $P = 1000$ psia:**

$$V = \frac{500 \times 1000}{300 + 1000} = \frac{500{,}000}{1300} \approx 384.6 \; \text{scf/ton}$$

**Trend:** As pressure rises, gas content asymptotically approaches $V_L = 500$ scf/ton. The relationship is **non-linear and saturating**.

| Pressure (psia) | Gas Content (scf/ton) |
|---|---|
| 200 | 200 |
| 500 | 312.5 |
| 1000 | 384.6 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Students frequently mix **scf/ton** with **cc/g** without converting. Always state units explicitly in the formula sheet.
> 2. **Langmuir Isotherm misuse:** Many students apply $V_L$ as a "maximum pressure" — it is actually the **maximum gas content**, not pressure.
> 3. **Critical Desorption Pressure calculation:** A common error is swapping $C_g$ and $V_L$ in the denominator. Always place the **larger quantity in the numerator and subtract the smaller one**.
> 4. **Forgetting dewatering:** In 14-mark answers on CBM production, students often skip the **dewatering stage** before gas production. Examiners specifically award marks for mentioning this critical step.
> 5. **Coal Rank identification:** Confusing **Ro %** ranges with rank boundaries results in partial marking. Memorize the table in Section 2.1.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Coal Bed Methane**

- **CBM** is unconventional gas primarily composed of **methane (CH₄)**, stored predominantly by **adsorption** (85 – 95 %) on the micropore walls of coal.
- Coal acts as **both source and reservoir** rock — a defining characteristic of CBM.
- **Two genetic pathways:** **Thermogenic** (high T, high rank) and **Biogenic** (low T, microbial).
- **Three storage modes:** **Adsorbed → Free → Dissolved** (in decreasing order of volume).
- **Dual porosity system:** **Matrix (microporous)** for storage + **Cleats (fractures)** for flow.
- **Langmuir Isotherm:** $V = \dfrac{V_L P}{P_L + P}$ — the central equation of CBM engineering.
- **Critical Desorption Pressure** marks the onset of gas flow during depletion.
- **Sweet Spot:** Medium-volatile bituminous coal with Ro = 0.8 – 1.2 % and adequate cleat permeability.
- **Production sequence:** **Dewatering → Pressure Decline → Gas Desorption → Production → Decline**.
- **Reservoir properties to characterize:** $V_L$, $P_L$, $C_g$, cleat permeability, reservoir pressure, sorption time, and gas saturation.
- **Industry use of CBM:** Power generation, city gas distribution, feedstock for chemicals, and pre-mine methane drainage for safety.
- **Environmental link:** **CO₂-ECBM** — injecting CO₂ displaces CH₄ and sequesters carbon simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Gas Hydrate: Concepts and Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Natural Gas Hydrate: Concepts and Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Gas Hydrate (NGH)** is a non-stoichiometric, crystalline, ice-like clathrate compound formed when light hydrocarbon guest molecules (such as CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, CO₂, H₂S, and N₂) become physically trapped inside hydrogen-bonded cages (host lattices) built from water molecules under conditions of **low temperature** and **elevated pressure**.

In strict IUPAC and petroleum engineering terminology, NGH belongs to the class of **clathrate hydrates** (or *gas hydrates*), where the water (H₂O) molecules self-assemble through **hydrogen bonding** into polyhedral cavities, and the gas molecules (the "guests") occupy these cavities through weak **van der Waals (dispersion) interactions**, without forming any chemical (covalent or ionic) bond with the host lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the three crystallographic structures of hydrates — **Structure I (sI)**, **Structure II (sII)**, and **Structure H (sH)** — and the relation between hydrate stability, pressure, temperature, and gas composition.

### 1.2 Thermodynamic Boundary Conditions for Natural Existence

| Parameter | Typical Range | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **0 °C to 25 °C** | Perennially available in deep seabed / permafrost |
| Pressure | **1 MPa to 100 MPa** | Corresponds to seawater depths > 500 m |
| Depth on Earth | **> 500 m seabed** or **> 200 m permafrost** | Stability Zone (GHSZ) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice Hotel for Gas Molecules"

Imagine a multi-storey, all-suite **ice hotel** built by tiny construction workers (water molecules) that snap together using Velcro strips (hydrogen bonds). The hotel is constructed to be spacious and empty, but every now and then a wealthy traveller (a methane or ethane gas molecule) checks in and stays locked inside a room.

- The **ice hotel's structure** is held together by the Velcro (H-bonds) of the workers — *not* by the traveller.
- The traveller (gas) does **not** chemically modify the hotel. It just sits there, kept snug by the surrounding walls.
- The hotel will **disintegrate** if it gets too warm (H-bonds break) or if the room becomes empty (pressure drops and gas escapes).
- Sometimes the hotel is so overstuffed with travellers that it clogs the entire pipeline — this is what causes **hydrate plugs** in subsea flowlines!

This is exactly what happens in a real Natural Gas Hydrate: water plays the role of the construction crew, and methane (or other light hydrocarbons) plays the role of the traveller. There is no covalent bond between them — only physical containment.

> [!TIP]
> **For Examiners:** The most important conceptual distinction students must internalize is that gas hydrates are **physical inclusion compounds**, not chemical compounds. Do not write that the gas "reacts with" water; it is *encaged* by water.

### 1.4 Historical Context & Real-World Occurrence

- First observed by **Sir Humphry Davy in 1810** (chlorine hydrate).
- The first **natural** occurrence was confirmed in the **Messoyakha gas field, Siberia (1960s)**, establishing the geological reality of in-situ gas hydrates.
- The global **carbon储量** sequestered in hydrate form is estimated to be **2× to 10× larger than all conventional fossil fuel reserves combined**, making NGH a strategic future energy resource.
- Industrial hazards include flowline plugging in offshore platforms (e.g., the **Deepwater Horizon** incident) and gas processing trains.
- Promising future applications: **Hydrogen storage (H₂ hydrates)**, **CO₂ sequestration** (replacing CH₄ in sI cages), and **desalination** of seawater.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Architecture of a Clathrate Hydrate

The water (host) molecules assemble into **pentagonal dodecahedra** (5¹² cages) and other polyhedra made of **pentagonal and hexagonal faces**. Each water molecule is shared among multiple cages, so the cage framework has long-range crystalline order.

A unit cell is characterized by:

- **Type I (sI):** Cubic, space group *Pm3̄n*, 2 small (5¹²) + 6 large (5¹² 6²) cages, 46 H₂O molecules per unit cell. Ideal cavity size: 5.1 Å (small), 5.86 Å (large).
- **Type II (sII):** Cubic, space group *Fd3̄m*, 16 small (5¹²) + 8 large (5¹² 6⁴) cages, 136 H₂O molecules per unit cell. Ideal cavity size: 5.1 Å (small), 6.69 Å (large).
- **Type H (sH):** Hexagonal, space group *P6/mmc*, 3 small (5¹²) + 2 medium (4³ 5⁶ 6³) + 1 large (5¹² 6⁸) cages, 34 H₂O molecules per unit cell. Large cavity: 8.87 Å.

## 2.2 Why Three Structures? — A Logical Breakdown

| Step | Logical Reasoning |
|---|---|
| **Step 1: Cage Geometry** | The 5¹² cage (a pentagonal dodecahedron) is the smallest, most stable building block. It can host only the **smallest guests** like CH₄, H₂S, N₂, CO₂. |
| **Step 2: Stabilization by Guest Size** | A cage that contains no guest collapses (since water cages are thermodynamically metastable in pure form). The guest's van der Waals interaction with the cage walls **lattice-stabilizes** the structure. |
| **Step 3: Size Match Dictates Structure** | If guests are small (e.g., CH₄, C₂H₆), sI forms. If guests include propane/iso-butane, sII forms. If guests are huge (e.g., neohexane, methylcyclohexane) with a small help-gas (CH₄, H₂S), sH forms. |
| **Step 4: Pressure–Temperature Driver** | Higher pressure and lower temperature favor fuller cage occupancy. The hydrate formation curve on a P–T diagram separates the **hydrate-stable region** (above the line, i.e., high P, low T) from the dissociated region. |

## 2.3 The Hydration Number (n)

The **hydration number** *n* is the **molar ratio of water molecules to guest gas molecules** in the unit cell.

$$
n = \frac{N_{\text{water}}}{N_{\text{guest}}}
$$

For a **fully occupied** unit cell:

- **sI:** $n = 46 / 8 = 5.75$
- **sII:** $n = 136 / 24 = 5.67$
- **sH:** $n = 34 / 6 = 5.67$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, hydrate cages are **never 100% occupied** due to the Boltzmann distribution of guest molecules. Industrial methane hydrates typically show *n* ≈ **6.0 to 6.4**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Typical Use / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydration number** | $n = N_{\text{water}} / N_{\text{guest}}$ | Stoichiometry of hydrates (dimensionless) |
| 2 | **Clausius–Clapeyron for hydrate equilibrium** | $\dfrac{d(\ln P)}{d(1/T)} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR}$ | P–T phase boundary slope |
| 3 | **Pressure prediction (Katz correlation)** | $\ln P = \beta + \dfrac{\gamma}{T}$ where $\beta, \gamma$ are gas-specific | Quick gas-specific P–T estimates |
| 4 | **Cage-occupancy (Langmuir)** | $\theta_{ij} = \dfrac{C_{ij} \, f_i}{1 + \sum_k C_{kj} \, f_k}$ | Statistical thermo of guest filling |
| 5 | **Volume ratio of hydrate to water (solid)** | $V_{\text{hydrate}} / V_{\text{water}} \approx 1.25$ to $1.30$ | Volume expansion upon formation |
| 6 | **Heat released during formation** | $\Delta H_{\text{form}} \approx 55$ to $75$ kJ/mol of gas | Exothermic nature; pipeline insulation issue |
| 7 | **Gas storage capacity (volume STP)** | $V_{\text{STP}} / V_{\text{hydrate}} \approx 160$ to 180 | 1 m³ hydrate → ~170 m³ gas at STP |
| 8 | **Inhibition shift (ΔT) — Hammerschmidt** | $\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot m}{M}$ where $K \approx 2335$ for methanol, $m$ = wt%, $M$ = mol wt | Thermodynamic inhibitor dosage |
| 9 | **Hydrate number for CH₄** | $n_{\text{CH}_4} \approx 6.0$ (real) vs 5.75 (ideal) | Engineering design parameter |
| 10 | **Stability condition (simplified)** | $T < T_{\text{eq}}(P)$ AND $P > P_{\text{eq}}(T)$ | Defines GHSZ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol Conflict Note:** When writing `|x|` (absolute value) inside a markdown table, use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking column syntax. Example: write $\vert dP/dT \vert$ instead of $\vert dP/dT \vert$ — the LaTeX is preserved.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Flow Assurance in Subsea Pipelines:** Predicting hydrate formation P–T curves prevents multimillion-dollar flowline blockages.
2. **Energy Resource Assessment:** Methane hydrates in the Indian **KG Basin (Krishna-Godavari)** and **Mahanadi Basin** are active exploration targets under India's **National Gas Hydrate Programme (NGHP)**.
3. **Carbon Capture & Sequestration (CCS):** Injecting CO₂ into marine hydrate-bearing sediments can liberate CH₄ ("CH₄–CO₂ swap"), achieving simultaneous energy recovery and carbon storage.
4. **Hydrogen Economy:** H₂ hydrates (sII) are being researched as a safe, high-density H₂ carrier.
5. **Cold Storage / Desalination:** Hydrate-based desalination uses the salt-excluding property of clathrate cages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation for Hydrate Equilibrium

The hydrate ⇌ water + gas equilibrium can be written in general as:

$$
G \cdot n H_2O_{\text{(cage)}} \;\rightleftharpoons\; G_{\text{(free)}} + n H_2O_{\text{(liquid or ice)}}
$$

For this two-phase (or pseudo two-phase) equilibrium, the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation** applies:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{\text{dis}}}
$$

where $\Delta H_{\text{dis}}$ is the molar enthalpy of dissociation and $\Delta V_{\text{dis}}$ is the molar volume change on dissociation.

### Step 1: Assume $\Delta V$ and $\Delta H$ are approximately constant with T

For narrow T-ranges of interest (0–25 °C), the molar volume change $\Delta V \approx V_{\text{gas}} - V_{\text{water}} \approx V_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{zRT}{P}$ (since $V_{\text{water}} \ll V_{\text{gas}}$).

### Step 2: Substitute $V_{\text{gas}} = zRT/P$ into the equation

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{T} \cdot \frac{P}{zRT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR} \cdot \frac{P}{T^2}
$$

### Step 3: Cross-multiply and separate variables

$$
\frac{dP}{P} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

Since $dT/T^2 = -d(1/T)$,

$$
\frac{d(\ln P)}{d(1/T)} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate from $(P_1, T_1)$ to $(P_2, T_2)$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Express in the Katz form

Setting $P_1 = 1$ atm, $T_1 = T^*$ (reference temperature), and absorbing constants into $\beta$:

$$
\ln P \;=\; \beta \;+\; \frac{\gamma}{T} \quad\text{where}\quad \gamma = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **working engineering equation** that the KTU examiner expects you to quote for any gas-specific P–T hydrate problem. The constants $\beta$ and $\gamma$ are tabulated in the **Carson & Katz (1942)** charts for methane, ethane, propane, CO₂, H₂S, and N₂.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A natural gas mixture containing 90% CH₄ and 10% C₂H₆ is held in a pipeline at **10 °C**. Using the simplified Katz correlation:

- For CH₄: $\ln P_{\text{CH}_4} = 14.7175 - \dfrac{1886.79}{T(K)}$
- For C₂H₆: $\ln P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 17.5106 - \dfrac{3104.20}{T(K)}$

Estimate the **hydrate formation pressure** of the mixture using the simple **Katz mixing rule**:

$$
P_{\text{mix}} = \sum_i y_i \, P_i^{\text{pure}}(T)
$$

### Solution — Step 1: Convert temperature

$$
T = 10^\circ C = 283.15\ K
$$

### Step 2: Compute pure-component hydrate pressures

$$
\ln P_{\text{CH}_4} = 14.7175 - \frac{1886.79}{283.15} = 14.7175 - 6.6635 = 8.0540
$$

$$
P_{\text{CH}_4} = e^{8.0540} = 3151\ \text{psia} \approx 21.7\ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\ln P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 17.5106 - \frac{3104.20}{283.15} = 17.5106 - 10.9616 = 6.5490
$$

$$
P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = e^{6.5490} = 700.2\ \text{psia} \approx 4.83\ \text{MPa}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Katz mixing rule

$$
P_{\text{mix}} = 0.90 \times 3151 + 0.10 \times 700.2
$$

$$
P_{\text{mix}} = 2835.9 + 70.02 = 2905.9\ \text{psia} \approx 20.0\ \text{MPa}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Always use **consistent units** (psia ↔ K, OR MPa ↔ K) in Clausius-type equations. Mixing units is the single most common KTU valuation mistake.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Hydrate Structure from Gas Composition

| Guest Gas | Molecular Diameter (Å) | Preferred Structure | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 4.36 | sI (and sII as help-gas) | Fits 5¹² cage; balances sI energy |
| C₂H₆ | 5.5 | sI | Occupies 5¹² 6² large cage of sI |
| C₃H₈ | 6.28 | sII | Too big for sI; fits 5¹² 6⁴ of sII |
| iso-C₄H₁₀ | 6.5 | sII | Forms sII with CH₄ as co-guest |
| n-C₄H₁₀ | 7.1 | sH (with help-gas) | Forms sH with CH₄/H₂S |
| CO₂ | 5.12 | sI | Similar to CH₄ |
| H₂S | 4.58 | sI | Strong stabilizer; reduces P at given T |
| N₂ | 4.10 | sI (or sII) | Smaller than CH₄ |
| Neohexane | 9.0 | sH | Requires sH's huge 5¹² 6⁸ cage |
| H₂ | 2.72 | sII (under high P) | Needs small cages; tight fit |

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Derivation of Hydration Number from X-ray Data

For a single unit cell of **sI** with full occupancy:
- Number of small cages: 2
- Number of large cages: 6
- Number of H₂O molecules: 46
- Total guest capacity: 8 molecules

$$
n_{\text{sI}} = \frac{N_{\text{water}}}{N_{\text{guest, max}}} = \frac{46}{2 + 6} = \frac{46}{8} = 5.75
$$

For a **partially occupied** real hydrate where the small-cage occupancy is $\theta_S$ and the large-cage occupancy is $\theta_L$:

$$
n_{\text{real}} = \frac{46}{2\theta_S + 6\theta_L}
$$

For typical CH₄ hydrates with $\theta_S \approx 0.87$ and $\theta_L \approx 0.98$:

$$
n_{\text{real}} = \frac{46}{2(0.87) + 6(0.98)} = \frac{46}{1.74 + 5.88} = \frac{46}{7.62} \approx 6.04
$$

This matches the experimentally measured hydration number for methane hydrate of **n ≈ 6.0**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Process of Natural Gas Hydrate Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Gas and Liquid Water] --> B[Contact at Low T and High P]
    B --> C{Nucleation of Water Cages}
    C --> D[5-to-the-12 Pentagonal Dodecahedra Form]
    D --> E[Guest Molecules Trapped in Cages]
    E --> F{Which Structure Forms}
    F -->|Small guests: CH4 CO2 H2S| G[Structure I - sI]
    F -->|Medium guests: C3H8 iC4| H[Structure II - sII]
    F -->|Large plus small help-gas| I[Structure H - sH]
    G --> J[Crystalline Clathrate Solid]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Dissociation Triggered}
    K -->|T rises| L[Gas Released, Water Returns]
    K -->|P drops| L
    K -->|Inhibitor injected| L
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Process Architecture for Hydrate Management in Subsea Flowlines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Reservoir]
        R1[High P, High T Hydrocarbon Fluid]
    end
    subgraph S2[Subsea Flowline]
        R1 --> R2[Multiphase Flow Pipe]
        R2 --> R3[Joule-Thomson Cooling]
    end
    subgraph S3[Hydrate Risk Window]
        R3 --> R4{P-T Enters Hydrate Region}
        R4 -->|Yes| R5[Exothermic Crystallization Begins]
        R4 -->|No| R6[Safe Single-Phase Flow]
    end
    subgraph S4[Mitigation Block]
        R5 --> R7[Options: 1 Methanol MEG Inhibition 2 Dehydration 3 Insulation 4 Low-Dosage Kinetic Inhibitors]
    end
    subgraph S5[Topside]
        R7 --> R8[Offshore Platform Separator]
        R6 --> R8
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Unit Cell Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clathrate Hydrate Unit Cell Families] --> B[Structure I - sI]
    A --> C[Structure II - sII]
    A --> D[Structure H - sH]
    B --> B1[Cubic Pm3n]
    B --> B2[46 H2O per Cell]
    B --> B3[2 Small Cages 5-12]
    B --> B4[6 Large Cages 5-12 6-2]
    C --> C1[Cubic Fd3m]
    C --> C2[136 H2O per Cell]
    C --> C3[16 Small Cages 5-12]
    C --> C4[8 Large Cages 5-12 6-4]
    D --> D1[Hexagonal P6 over mmc]
    D --> D2[34 H2O per Cell]
    D --> D3[3 Small Cages 5-12]
    D --> D4[2 Medium Cages 4-3 5-6 6-3]
    D --> D5[1 Large Cage 5-12 6-8]
```

## 4.4 ASCII-Text Schematic of an sI 5¹² Cage (Pentagonal Dodecahedron)

```
        .--""--.
      .'        '.
    .'   * O *    '.      (O = pentagonal face)
   /                 \
  |   O       O       |   Each vertex = 1 H2O molecule
  |       O           |   Each face = pentagon
   \   O       O     /
    '.    O       .'
      '.         .'
        '-....-'
```

> [!NOTE]
> A 5¹² cage has 20 vertices (water molecules) and 12 pentagonal faces. The cavity diameter is approximately **5.1 Å**, ideal for **CH₄** (4.36 Å).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**"Define Natural Gas Hydrate. Why is it called a 'clathrate' compound rather than a 'chemical' compound?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Natural gas hydrate is a crystalline, ice-like, non-stoichiometric inclusion compound in which light hydrocarbon molecules (e.g., CH₄, C₂H₆) are physically trapped inside hydrogen-bonded cages of water molecules, formed under conditions of low temperature and high pressure.
- **Why clathrate (1 mark):** The term "clathrate" denotes a *cage-like enclosure*; the gas molecules are physically held within the water lattice by weak **van der Waals forces**, not by covalent or ionic bonds. Hence the composition is *non-stoichiometric* (variable hydration number).
- **Key distinction (1 mark):** Unlike a true chemical compound, the host (water) and guest (gas) are not bonded — they can be separated by simple depressurization or warming, releasing the gas intact.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**"List the three known clathrate hydrate structures and state one distinguishing property of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **sI (Structure I):** Cubic, 46 H₂O per unit cell, formed by gases like **CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, H₂S** (small to medium guests).
- **sII (Structure II):** Cubic, 136 H₂O per unit cell, formed by gases like **C₃H₈ and iso-butane** plus a help gas (e.g., CH₄). Has larger 5¹²6⁴ cavities.
- **sH (Structure H):** Hexagonal, 34 H₂O per unit cell, requires a **large guest** (e.g., methylcyclohexane) plus a small **help gas** (CH₄, H₂S). Has the largest 5¹²6⁸ cavity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the three structures' cavity counts** — this is the most common 2-mark deduction in the KTU answer script.
> 2. **Always state the units** (MPa, psia, °C, K) and convert between them carefully; partial marking is strict.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse "hydration number" with "stoichiometry of a chemical compound"** — emphasize *non-stoichiometric* nature.
> 4. **State assumptions** (ideal gas, constant $\Delta H$) before applying the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, or the examiner will deduct 1 mark.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Model]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the three crystallographic structures of natural gas hydrates (sI, sII, sH). Tabulate the number of small, medium, and large cages per unit cell, the number of water molecules per unit cell, and the type of guest gas for which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas of the following composition (mol %): CH₄ = 0.85, C₂H₆ = 0.10, C₃H₈ = 0.05 is processed at 7 °C. Using the Katz correlation:

- CH₄: $\ln P = 14.7175 - \dfrac{1886.79}{T(K)}$
- C₂H₆: $\ln P = 17.5106 - \dfrac{3104.20}{T(K)}$
- C₃H₈: $\ln P = 18.9414 - \dfrac{3837.90}{T(K)}$

Estimate the **hydrate formation pressure** of the mixture using the Katz mixing rule. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

| Feature | sI | sII | sH |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crystal System | Cubic | Cubic | Hexagonal |
| Space Group | Pm3̄n | Fd3̄m | P6/mmc |
| H₂O per Unit Cell | 46 | 136 | 34 |
| Small Cages (5¹²) | 2 | 16 | 3 |
| Medium Cages (4³ 5⁶ 6³) | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| Large Cages | 6 (5¹² 6²) | 8 (5¹² 6⁴) | 1 (5¹² 6⁸) |
| Small Cavity Diameter | 5.1 Å | 5.1 Å | 5.1 Å |
| Large Cavity Diameter | 5.86 Å | 6.69 Å | 8.87 Å |
| Typical Guests | CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, H₂S | C₃H₈, iso-C₄H₁₀, with help gas | n-C₅, methylcyclohexane + CH₄/H₂S |
| Hydration Number (ideal) | 5.75 | 5.67 | 5.67 |

> (Refer to the **Mermaid Block Diagram in Section 4.3** for the visual schematic of the unit cell architecture.)

**Marking key:** 1 mark for naming each structure correctly (3 marks total). 2 marks for the table. 2 marks for stating typical guests.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert temperature:**
$T = 7^\circ C = 280.15\ K$

**Step 2 — Pure-component hydrate pressures:**

$$
\ln P_{\text{CH}_4} = 14.7175 - \frac{1886.79}{280.15} = 14.7175 - 6.7339 = 7.9836
$$
$$
P_{\text{CH}_4} = e^{7.9836} \approx 2930\ \text{psia}
$$

$$
\ln P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 17.5106 - \frac{3104.20}{280.15} = 17.5106 - 11.0800 = 6.4306
$$
$$
P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = e^{6.4306} \approx 621.5\ \text{psia}
$$

$$
\ln P_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_8} = 18.9414 - \frac{3837.90}{280.15} = 18.9414 - 13.7000 = 5.2414
$$
$$
P_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_8} = e^{5.2414} \approx 189.0\ \text{psia}
$$

**Step 3 — Katz mixing rule:**

$$
P_{\text{mix}} = 0.85(2930) + 0.10(621.5) + 0.05(189.0)
$$

$$
P_{\text{mix}} = 2490.5 + 62.15 + 9.45 = 2562.1\ \text{psia}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{mix}} \approx 2562\ \text{psia} \approx 17.66\ \text{MPa}}
$$

**Marking key:** 2 marks for unit conversion + formula. 2 marks for partial pressures. 2 marks for the mixing rule. 1 mark for final answer with units.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** for hydrate equilibrium, clearly stating the assumptions. Discuss the physical significance of the slope $dP/dT$ and explain why methane hydrates become more stable at higher pressures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **hydration number**? For an sI methane hydrate, given that small-cage occupancy $\theta_S = 0.87$ and large-cage occupancy $\theta_L = 0.98$, compute the **actual hydration number**. Comment on why real hydrates have higher hydration numbers than the ideal 5.75. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The hydrate–water–gas equilibrium: $G \cdot n H_2O_{\text{(solid)}} \rightleftharpoons G_{\text{(gas)}} + n H_2O_{\text{(liquid)}}$

Applying the standard Clausius–Clapeyron relation:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{\text{dis}}}
$$

**Assumption 1:** $\Delta V_{\text{dis}} \approx V_{\text{gas}}$ (water/solid volume is negligible compared to gas).

**Assumption 2:** Treat gas as ideal: $V_{\text{gas}} = zRT/P$.

**Assumption 3:** $\Delta H_{\text{dis}}$ and $z$ are approximately constant over the T-range.

**Substitution:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{T} \cdot \frac{P}{zRT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{dis}}}{zR} \cdot \frac{P}{T^2}
$$

**Separation of variables and integration** (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$
\ln P = \beta + \frac{\gamma}{T}
$$

**Physical significance:** The slope $dP/dT$ is **always positive** for hydrate equilibrium, because $\Delta H_{\text{dis}} > 0$ (endothermic dissociation) and $\Delta V_{\text{dis}} > 0$ (gas volume > solid volume). Thus, increasing T requires a higher P to maintain hydrate stability.

**Why methane hydrates are more stable at higher P:** Higher pressure drives more methane molecules into the available cages (raising cage occupancy $\theta$), which in turn increases the lattice stabilization energy, making the hydrate thermodynamically preferred. The 5¹² and 5¹² 6² cages have a maximum number of guests; high P ensures that this capacity is reached.

**Marking key:** 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation, 1 mark for slope interpretation, 1 mark for stability discussion.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The hydration number *n* is the **molar ratio of water molecules to gas molecules** in a hydrate unit cell:

$$
n = \frac{N_{\text{water}}}{N_{\text{guest}}}
$$

For an sI unit cell with 46 water molecules and 8 available cage sites, the **ideal** $n = 46/8 = 5.75$.

**Actual computation (3 marks):** With $\theta_S = 0.87$ and $\theta_L = 0.98$:

- Number of CH₄ in small cages: $2 \times 0.87 = 1.74$
- Number of CH₄ in large cages: $6 \times 0.98 = 5.88$
- Total CH₄: $1.74 + 5.88 = 7.62$

$$
n_{\text{real}} = \frac{46}{7.62} = 6.04
$$

**Comment (2 marks):** Real hydrates have **higher** hydration numbers than the ideal 5.75 because:
- Cage occupancy is **never 100%** (Boltzmann energy distribution).
- Statistical thermodynamics and steric factors prevent every cage from being filled.
- The small 5¹² cages in particular are often less occupied because they are more constrained.

Hence, $n_{\text{real}} > n_{\text{ideal}}$.

**Marking key:** 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for arithmetic, 2 marks for physical comment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU):**

- ✅ A natural gas hydrate is a **clathrate inclusion compound** — *physical*, not chemical.
- ✅ Three structures: **sI (cubic, 46 H₂O), sII (cubic, 136 H₂O), sH (hexagonal, 34 H₂O)**.
- ✅ Cage notation: **5¹²** = 12 pentagonal faces; **5¹² 6²** = 12 pentagons + 2 hexagons; **5¹² 6⁴** = 12 pentagons + 4 hexagons; **5¹² 6⁸** = 12 pentagons + 8 hexagons.
- ✅ **Hydration number** $n$ = $N_{\text{water}} / N_{\text{guest}}$; ideal values: 5.75 (sI), 5.67 (sII), 5.67 (sH).
- ✅ **Methane → sI**; **Propane / iso-butane → sII**; **Large molecules (e.g., methylcyclohexane) + small help-gas → sH**.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron form:** $\ln P = \beta + \gamma / T$ (Katz form).
- ✅ **Hammerschmidt equation for inhibition:** $\Delta T = (K \cdot m) / M$, with $K = 2335$ for methanol.
- ✅ **Typical storage capacity:** 1 m³ of methane hydrate releases ~ **170 m³ of methane gas at STP**.
- ✅ **Formation is exothermic:** $\Delta H \approx 55$–$75$ kJ/mol of gas.
- ✅ **Volume expansion:** Hydrate occupies ~ 25–30% more volume than the original water.
- ✅ **Real-world P–T conditions:** Seabed > 500 m (high P, ~ 2–4 °C) or permafrost > 200 m depth.
- ✅ **Katz mixing rule:** $P_{\text{mix}} = \sum y_i P_i^{\text{pure}}(T)$ — *additive in mole fraction*.
- ✅ **Key industry standard:** ISO 13703 / API RP 17 for hydrate management in subsea systems.
- ✅ **Strategic importance:** NGHP (India), Methane Hydrate R&D Act, climate change implications.
- ✅ **Industrial prevention methods:** (1) **Thermodynamic inhibition** (methanol, MEG), (2) **Low-dos hydrate inhibitors (LDHI)** — kinetic + anti-agglomerant, (3) **Dehydration** of gas, (4) **Insulation** to keep T above hydrate curve, (5) **Pressure control / dead-leg management**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation and Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation and Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Petroleum Engineering, **Evaluation** refers to the systematic quantitative determination of the hydrocarbon volumes originally in place (STOIIP, GIIP), the recoverable reserves, and the current hydrocarbon pore volume within a reservoir. **Prediction** refers to the forward-looking estimation of future reservoir performance — production rates, pressure decline, water cut evolution, gas-oil ratio behavior, and ultimate recovery — as a function of time, based on mathematical models calibrated to historical data.

Together, **Evaluation and Prediction** form the analytical backbone of reservoir engineering decision-making: how much oil/gas exists, how much can be recovered, and how fast it can be produced under various development scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PECHT522 / Module 2):**
> This topic integrates the static description of the reservoir (porosity, saturation, fluid contacts) with the dynamic description (drive mechanisms, decline behavior, material balance). It is the bridge between geological/geophysical interpretation and production engineering execution.

## 1.2 Sub-Components of Evaluation and Prediction

| Sub-Component | Core Question Answered | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Evaluation** | *How much hydrocarbon is in the ground?* | STOIIP / GIIP (stock-tank barrels / scf) |
| **Material Balance Evaluation** | *Is the reservoir behaving as expected?* | Original hydrocarbon in place, drive indices, aquifer influx |
| **Decline Curve Analysis (DCA)** | *How will rate change with time?* | Forecast of future rate, cumulative production, EUR |
| **Reservoir Simulation / Performance Prediction** | *What is the optimum development plan?* | Field-level production profiles under different scenarios |
| **Well Test Interpretation (Transient Analysis)** | *What are the local reservoir properties?* | Permeability, skin, fracture half-length, drainage area |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank with a Hole"**
> 
> Imagine a large overhead water tank with an unknown volume of water (the *original oil in place*). You open a small hole at the bottom (the *production well*) and observe how fast the water level drops over time (the *production rate* and *pressure decline*). 
> 
> - **Volumetric Evaluation** is like measuring the tank's cross-sectional area and water height from the outside, then multiplying them to estimate the water volume.
> - **Material Balance** is like tracking every drop that has left the tank and the water that has evaporated or seeped in through the walls, then solving for the original water volume by conservation.
> - **Decline Curve Analysis** is like watching the flow rate at the hole decay over time and extrapolating the trend into the future to predict when the tank will be empty.
> 
> The tank analogy, while simplified, captures the essence of all three approaches: the unknown is the original hydrocarbon volume, the observables are pressure, rate, and cumulative production, and the equations are all statements of mass conservation.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

The following metrics, all expressed in field units unless otherwise noted, are the fundamental output of any evaluation/prediction workflow:

- **STOIIP** — Stock-Tank Oil Initially In Place (bbl or MMbbl)
- **GIIP** — Gas Initially In Place (scf or Bscf)
- **OOIP** — Original Oil In Place (reservoir-condition bbl, rb)
- **OGIP** — Original Gas In Place (reservoir-condition scf, res ft³)
- **RF** — Recovery Factor (fraction or %)
- **EUR** — Estimated Ultimate Recovery (bbl or scf)
- **q, q_o, q_g, q_w** — Total, oil, gas, water surface flow rates (STB/d, Mscf/d, STB/d)
- **G_p, N_p, W_p** — Cumulative gas, oil, water produced
- **p, p_i, p_bar** — Current, initial, average reservoir pressure
- **B_o, B_g, B_w** — Oil, gas, water formation volume factors (rb/STB, rb/scf, rb/STB)
- **R_s, R_si, R_p** — Solution gas-oil ratio (scf/STB), initial, producing
- **c_f, c_w, c_o** — Formation (rock), water, oil compressibilities (psi⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard reference conditions for stock-tank volumes are **14.7 psia and 60 °F** (US field units) — the KTU 2024 syllabus and most board questions adopt this convention. The symbol **scf** (standard cubic feet) refers to gas volume measured at these reference conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Evaluation and Prediction

### Pillar 1 — Volumetric Method (Static Evaluation)

The volumetric method computes the hydrocarbon pore volume from a **geometric model** of the reservoir and the **average petrophysical properties** of the rock and fluids.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. Define the **gross rock volume (GRV)** of the reservoir above the hydrocarbon-water contact (or below the gas-oil contact for gas reservoirs). GRV is derived from structural maps (top of reservoir × base of reservoir).
2. Subtract the volume of rock that is below the **net pay cutoff** (typically V_shale < some threshold, ϕ > some threshold) → Net Rock Volume (NRV).
3. Multiply NRV by the **average porosity ϕ** to obtain the **pore volume (PV)**.
4. Multiply PV by **(1 − S_wi)** to obtain the **hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV)**.
5. Divide HCPV by the **formation volume factor (B_o or B_g)** to convert from reservoir conditions to surface (stock-tank) conditions.

### Pillar 2 — Material Balance Method (Dynamic Evaluation)

The material balance equation (MBE) is a **zero-dimensional statement of mass conservation** for the entire reservoir. It equates the total volume of fluids originally in the reservoir (expanded by pressure depletion) plus any water influx, to the cumulative production plus the current fluid volumes remaining.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. Write the cumulative expansion of oil, free gas, connate water, and rock as the reservoir pressure drops from p_i to p.
2. Add cumulative water influx W_e from an adjoining aquifer.
3. Equate this total expansion to cumulative hydrocarbon production and current remaining hydrocarbon volume at pressure p.
4. Solve for one unknown (commonly OOIP, N, or aquifer model parameters), using either analytical or graphical (Havlena-Odeh) methods.
5. Compute drive indices (DDI, SDI, WDI) to determine the dominant recovery mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the MBE work?** It is a direct consequence of the conservation of mass: nothing is created or destroyed, only re-distributed between the reservoir, the producing wells, and any connected aquifer. It is a closed-system balance, hence "zero-dimensional."

### Pillar 3 — Decline Curve Analysis (Empirical Prediction)

DCA uses **historical rate-time data** from individual wells or the entire field, fits a known decline model (exponential, hyperbolic, harmonic, or modified hyperbolic), and extrapolates into the future.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. Plot q vs t (rate-time) or q vs N_p (rate-cumulative) on appropriate axes.
2. Identify the decline model that best fits the data.
3. Determine the **decline constant D** and the **decline exponent b**.
4. Extrapolate to the **economic limit rate** q_el to obtain the **estimated ultimate recovery (EUR)**.
5. Use the resulting rate-time forecast for economic evaluation, facility design, and reserves booking.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for this topic. Memorize the symbols and the units. Every KTU board question on this topic can be solved using a combination of these equations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric STOIIP | $N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_{wi})}{B_{oi}}$ | $A$ = acres, $h$ = ft, $\phi$ = fraction, $S_{wi}$ = fraction, $B_{oi}$ = rb/STB, $N$ in **STB**. Constant 7758 converts acre·ft → bbl. |
| 2 | Volumetric GIIP | $G = \dfrac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ | $B_{gi}$ in **res ft³/scf**, $G$ in **scf**. Constant 43560 converts acre·ft → ft³. |
| 3 | Gas Formation Volume Factor | $B_g = \dfrac{0.00504 \cdot z \cdot T}{p}$ | $T$ in °R, $p$ in psia, $z$ = compressibility factor, $B_g$ in **res ft³/scf**. |
| 4 | Oil Formation Volume Factor (Standing) | $B_o = 0.9759 + 0.00012 \, F$ | $F$ is the Standing correlation parameter (see any standard text). |
| 5 | Recovery Factor | $RF = \dfrac{N_p}{N} = \dfrac{EUR}{STOIIP}$ | Dimensionless fraction, often reported as %. |
| 6 | Volumetric Gas Material Balance | $\dfrac{p}{z} = \dfrac{p_i}{z_i} \left(1 - \dfrac{G_p}{G}\right)$ | **p/z vs G_p** is a straight line for volumetric gas reservoirs. |
| 7 | Generalized MBE (oil reservoir) | $N(B_t - B_{ti}) + N \, m \, B_{ti} \left(\dfrac{B_g}{B_{gi}} - 1\right) + (1+m) N B_{ti} \left(\dfrac{c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1-S_{wi}}\right) \Delta p = N_p B_o + (G_p - N_p R_s) B_g + W_p B_w - W_e$ | Combined (oil + free gas + cap gas + rock + water expansion) form. |
| 8 | Two-Phase Formation Volume Factor | $B_t = B_o + (R_{si} - R_s) B_g$ | rb/STB. |
| 9 | Solution GOR Ratio (m) | $m = \dfrac{GB_{gi}}{NB_{oi}}$ | Cap gas to oil volume ratio, dimensionless. |
| 10 | Drive Index — Solution Gas (SDI) | $SDI = \dfrac{N(R_{si}-R_s)B_g}{N B_t + \dots}$ (from MBE) | Indicates contribution of solution gas drive. |
| 11 | Exponential Decline (rate) | $q(t) = q_i \cdot e^{-D t}$ | $D$ in **1/time** (e.g., day⁻¹, month⁻¹, year⁻¹). |
| 12 | Hyperbolic Decline (rate) | $q(t) = \dfrac{q_i}{(1 + b D_i t)^{1/b}}$ | $0 \le b \le 1$. As $b \to 0$ → exponential; $b = 1$ → harmonic. |
| 13 | Harmonic Decline (rate) | $q(t) = \dfrac{q_i}{1 + D_i t}$ | Special case of hyperbolic with $b = 1$. |
| 14 | Exponential — Cumulative | $N_p(t) = \dfrac{q_i - q(t)}{D}$ | Only valid for exponential. |
| 15 | Hyperbolic — Cumulative | $N_p(t) = \dfrac{q_i^{b}}{D_i (1-b)} \left[ q_i^{1-b} - q(t)^{1-b} \right]$ | For $b \ne 1$. |
| 16 | Instantaneous Water Cut | $WC = \dfrac{q_w}{q_o + q_w}$ | Fraction. |
| 17 | Gas-Oil Ratio (producing) | $R_p = \dfrac{G_p}{N_p}$ | scf/STB. |
| 18 | Productivity Index | $J = \dfrac{q}{\overline{p}_r - p_{wf}}$ | STB/d/psi. |
| 19 | Darcy's Law (radial) | $q = \dfrac{0.00708 k h (p_e - p_{wf})}{\mu \left[ \ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s \right]}$ | US field units, $k$ in md, $q$ in STB/d, $\mu$ in cp. |
| 20 | Dimensionless Time | $t_D = \dfrac{0.000264 k t}{\phi \mu c_t r_w^2}$ | Used in transient well-test analysis. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Volumetric methods** are the *only* feasible evaluation approach in the **exploration and early appraisal phase**, when production data is absent. They are also the basis of reserves reporting under the **PRMS (Petroleum Resources Management System)** framework adopted by SPE, WPC, AAPG, and SPEE.
- **Material balance** is critical for **reservoir surveillance** during the production phase. It can detect drive mechanism, aquifer strength, and even identify infill drilling opportunities.
- **Decline curve analysis** is the workhorse for **producing-company reserves reports**, SEC filings, and **bankable-engineering reports** for project finance. It is the most frequently used technique for forecasting production in mature fields.
- The integration of all three methods (volumetric + material balance + DCA) provides **reconciliation** — a cornerstone of reliable reserves estimation. Reserves are typically classified as **1P (proved)**, **2P (proved + probable)**, and **3P (proved + probable + possible)** based on the level of certainty.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often test the **integration** of these methods rather than a single equation in isolation. A typical 14-mark question may give volumetric input data *and* a production history and ask the student to estimate STOIIP, drive mechanism, and forecast one-year-ahead production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric STOIIP

We start from the definition of hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV):

$$
HCPV = V_{rock} \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})
$$

where $V_{rock}$ is the bulk rock volume, $\phi$ is porosity (fraction), and $S_{wi}$ is the irreducible (initial) water saturation (fraction).

The volume of oil at surface conditions is obtained by converting the reservoir-condition oil volume to stock-tank conditions using the oil formation volume factor $B_{oi}$:

$$
N = \dfrac{HCPV}{B_{oi}}
$$

For $A$ in **acres** and $h$ in **feet**, the bulk rock volume is:

$$
V_{rock} = A \cdot h \quad \text{[acre·ft]}
$$

Since $1 \text{ acre·ft} = 7758 \text{ bbl}$, we get:

$$
N = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot 7758 \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{oi}} \quad \text{[STB]}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ acre} = 43560 \text{ ft}^2$. Therefore $1 \text{ acre·ft} = 43560 \text{ ft}^3 = 43560 \times 7.4805 \text{ gal} = 7758.37 \text{ bbl}$. The constant 7758 is the rounded conversion.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Generalized Material Balance Equation

We begin with the principle of **conservation of mass (or volume, for incompressible fluids)**: the cumulative expansion of all fluids in the reservoir plus any external water influx must equal the cumulative production.

**Step 1 — Define cumulative expansion terms:**

- **Oil expansion** (oil + dissolved gas liberated):
$$
\Delta V_{oil} = N (B_o - B_{oi})
$$
- **Free gas cap expansion** (cap gas, includes liberated solution gas from the oil leg):
$$
\Delta V_{cap} = N m B_{oi} \left( \dfrac{B_g}{B_{gi}} - 1 \right) + N (R_{si} - R_s) B_g
$$
where the first term is expansion of the original free gas and the second is solution gas coming out of the oil leg.
- **Formation (rock) and connate water expansion** (combined):
$$
\Delta V_{rock+w} = (1+m) N B_{oi} \cdot \dfrac{c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1 - S_{wi}} \cdot \Delta p
$$

**Step 2 — Define cumulative production terms:**

- **Oil production** (surface volume × formation volume factor to convert to reservoir volume):
$$
V_{N_p} = N_p B_o
$$
- **Free gas production** (gas that was in the gas cap and solution gas that came out and was produced):
$$
V_{G_p} = G_p B_g
$$
- **Produced water volume:**
$$
V_{W_p} = W_p B_w
$$
- **Water influx from aquifer** (reduces the net expansion needed):
$$
V_{W_e} = W_e
$$

**Step 3 — Equate expansion to production (mass conservation):**

$$
\Delta V_{oil} + \Delta V_{cap} + \Delta V_{rock+w} + W_e = V_{N_p} + (G_p - N_p R_s) B_g + W_p B_w
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange (the standard form is):**

$$
N (B_t - B_{ti}) + N m B_{ti} \left( \dfrac{B_g}{B_{gi}} - 1 \right) + (1+m) N B_{ti} \dfrac{c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1 - S_{wi}} \Delta p = N_p B_o + (G_p - N_p R_s) B_g + W_p B_w - W_e
$$

where $B_t = B_o + (R_{si} - R_s) B_g$ is the **total (two-phase) formation volume factor**.

> **Conversion Logic:** The term $(G_p - N_p R_s) B_g$ represents the *net* free gas produced: total gas produced $G_p$ minus the gas that was in solution in the produced oil $N_p R_s$, all converted to reservoir volume via $B_g$. The negative $W_e$ term appears on the right because water influx acts as a *source* (not a production), so it appears on the same side as production when we view the equation as "expansion + influx = production."

**Step 5 — Special case: Volumetric undersaturated oil reservoir (no gas cap, no aquifer, $B_t \approx B_o$ since $R_s = R_{si}$):**

$$
N = \dfrac{N_p B_o}{B_o - B_{oi} + (R_{si} - R_s) B_g + B_{oi}(1+m) \dfrac{c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1 - S_{wi}} \Delta p}
$$

For the simplest case ($m=0$, $W_e=0$, $R_s = R_{si}$):

$$
N = \dfrac{N_p B_o}{B_o - B_{oi}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the famous "Havlena-Odeh straight line" form. If we plot $N_p B_o$ on the y-axis and $(B_o - B_{oi})$ on the x-axis, we get a straight line through the origin with slope $N$. Deviations from linearity reveal the presence of an aquifer or other drive mechanisms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exponential Decline (Arps Model, b = 0)

The Arps decline equations describe how production rate $q$ declines with time $t$. The instantaneous decline rate is defined as:

$$
D = -\dfrac{1}{q} \dfrac{dq}{dt}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume D is constant** (the definition of exponential decline):

$$
-\dfrac{1}{q} \dfrac{dq}{dt} = D = \text{const}
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$
\dfrac{dq}{q} = -D \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $q_i$ at $t = 0$ to $q$ at time $t$:**

$$
\int_{q_i}^{q} \dfrac{dq'}{q'} = -D \int_0^t dt'
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{q}{q_i}\right) = -Dt
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for q(t):**

$$
q(t) = q_i \, e^{-Dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive cumulative production by integrating rate over time:**

$$
N_p(t) = \int_0^t q(t') \, dt' = \int_0^t q_i \, e^{-Dt'} \, dt'
$$

$$
N_p(t) = q_i \left[ \dfrac{-1}{D} e^{-Dt'} \right]_0^t = \dfrac{q_i}{D} \left( 1 - e^{-Dt} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate time using $q(t) = q_i e^{-Dt}$:**

$$
e^{-Dt} = \dfrac{q(t)}{q_i}
$$

$$
N_p(t) = \dfrac{q_i - q(t)}{D}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the cleanest, most-tested form on the KTU board. Given $q_i$, $D$, and an economic limit $q_{el}$, the EUR is:
> $$
> EUR = N_p(t_{el}) = \dfrac{q_i - q_{el}}{D}
> $$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — p/z Material Balance for Volumetric Gas Reservoir

For a dry-gas reservoir with no water influx, the MBE reduces to a single fluid (gas). The instantaneous gas volume balance is:

$$
G B_{gi} = (G - G_p) B_g
$$

**Step 1 — Expand using $B_g = 0.00504 z T / p$:**

$$
G \cdot \dfrac{0.00504 z_i T}{p_i} = (G - G_p) \cdot \dfrac{0.00504 z T}{p}
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $0.00504 T$ on both sides:**

$$
G \cdot \dfrac{z_i}{p_i} = (G - G_p) \cdot \dfrac{z}{p}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p/z$ as a function of $G_p$:**

$$
\dfrac{p}{z} = \dfrac{p_i}{z_i} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{G_p}{G} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the linear form:** A plot of $p/z$ vs $G_p$ is a **straight line** that:
- Has y-intercept $p_i / z_i$ (initial reservoir gas in place related intercept).
- Crosses the x-axis at $G_p = G$ (i.e., when $p/z = 0$, all original gas has been produced — in principle).
- Allows direct determination of **GIIP** from the slope-intercept relationship.

> **Conversion Logic:** Strictly, the straight line does not extrapolate exactly to $G_p = G$ at $p/z = 0$ due to the residual abandonment pressure and the non-ideal gas behavior at low pressures. Real-world forecasts truncate the line at the **abandonment pressure** $p_{ab}$ (typically 200–500 psia depending on well depth and compression economics).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following example is a representative KTU 14-mark style problem solved step-by-step. The numbers are realistic and the solution methodology is the same as expected in a KTU End Semester Examination.

**Problem Statement (Question):**
A volumetric oil reservoir has the following average properties: area $A = 2000$ acres, average thickness $h = 50$ ft, porosity $\phi = 0.20$, initial water saturation $S_{wi} = 0.25$, initial oil formation volume factor $B_{oi} = 1.30$ rb/STB. The reservoir is producing at an exponential decline with initial rate $q_i = 5000$ STB/d and decline constant $D = 0.10$ /year. Determine:
- (a) STOIIP using the volumetric method.
- (b) Production rate after 5 years.
- (c) Cumulative oil produced in 5 years.
- (d) Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR) at an economic limit of 50 STB/d.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — STOIIP:**

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{oi}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \times 2000 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1.30}
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
7758 \times 2000 = 15{,}516{,}000
$$

$$
15{,}516{,}000 \times 50 = 775{,}800{,}000
$$

$$
775{,}800{,}000 \times 0.20 = 155{,}160{,}000
$$

$$
155{,}160{,}000 \times 0.75 = 116{,}370{,}000
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{116{,}370{,}000}{1.30} = 89{,}515{,}384.6 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 89.52 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor 7758 is the acre·ft → bbl conversion, used in US field units which are the KTU convention. The factor 0.75 represents $(1 - S_{wi}) = (1 - 0.25)$.

**Part (b) — Rate after 5 years:**

$$
q(5) = q_i \, e^{-Dt} = 5000 \times e^{-0.10 \times 5} = 5000 \times e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
e^{-0.5} = 0.60653
$$

$$
q(5) = 5000 \times 0.60653 = 3032.65 \text{ STB/d}
$$

$$
\boxed{q(5) \approx 3033 \text{ STB/d}}
$$

**Part (c) — Cumulative production in 5 years:**

$$
N_p(5) = \dfrac{q_i - q(5)}{D} = \dfrac{5000 - 3032.65}{0.10} = \dfrac{1967.35}{0.10} = 19{,}673.5 \text{ STB/year unit}
$$

Note: $D$ is in /year but $q$ is in STB/day, so we must convert. Since $D = 0.10$/year corresponds to a daily basis, $D_{day} = 0.10/365.25 = 2.738 \times 10^{-4}$ /day. The correct unit-consistent computation is:

$$
N_p(5) = \int_0^{5 \text{ yr}} q(t) \, dt = \int_0^{1826.25 \text{ d}} 5000 \, e^{-(0.10/365.25) t} \, dt
$$

Alternatively, use the form $N_p = (q_i - q)/D$ *with consistent units*:

If $D = 0.10$/year and we work in years, then $q_i$ must be in STB/year: $q_i = 5000 \times 365.25 = 1{,}826{,}250$ STB/year.

$$
N_p(5) = \dfrac{1{,}826{,}250 - (3032.65 \times 365.25)}{0.10}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1{,}826{,}250 - 1{,}107{,}680}{0.10} = \dfrac{718{,}570}{0.10} = 7{,}185{,}700 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_p(5) \approx 7.19 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**Part (d) — EUR at $q_{el} = 50$ STB/d:**

Convert to annual: $q_{el} = 50 \times 365.25 = 18{,}262.5$ STB/year.

$$
EUR = \dfrac{q_i - q_{el}}{D} = \dfrac{1{,}826{,}250 - 18{,}262.5}{0.10} = \dfrac{1{,}807{,}987.5}{0.10} = 18{,}079{,}875 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{EUR \approx 18.08 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**Recovery Factor:**

$$
RF = \dfrac{EUR}{N} = \dfrac{18{,}079{,}875}{89{,}515{,}385} = 0.2020 = 20.20\%
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The unit consistency in exponential decline is the most common pitfall. Always keep $q$ and $D$ in the same time unit. A 1-year decline is rarely 1 day, so the conversion $365.25$ days/year is the rigorous factor used in petroleum engineering.
