# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style**

**Q1. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Define STOIIP. Write the volumetric formula for STOIIP and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**
**STOIIP** stands for **Stock-Tank Oil Initially In Place**, the total volume of crude oil (in stock-tank barrels) originally present in the reservoir before any production. The volumetric formula is:

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \, A \, h \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{oi}}
$$

where: $N$ = STOIIP in **STB**, $A$ = reservoir area in **acres**, $h$ = average thickness in **feet**, $\phi$ = average porosity (fraction), $S_{wi}$ = initial water saturation (fraction), $B_{oi}$ = initial oil formation volume factor in **rb/STB**, and $7758$ is the **acre·ft → barrel** conversion constant.

**(Examiner Note:** Marks awarded for correct definition (1), correct formula (1), and explanation of all terms with units (1).)**

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between exponential decline and hyperbolic decline in Arps model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Exponential Decline | Hyperbolic Decline |
|---|---|---|
| Decline exponent $b$ | $b = 0$ | $0 < b < 1$ |
| Decline rate $D$ | Constant | Varies with time ($D = D_i / (1 + b D_i t)$) |
| Rate vs time | $q = q_i e^{-Dt}$ | $q = q_i / (1 + b D_i t)^{1/b}$ |
| Rate vs cumulative | Straight line on $q$ vs $N_p$ | Curved on $q$ vs $N_p$ |
| Realism | Approximation, valid for low $b$ | More realistic for solution-gas-drive reservoirs |
| Use in KTU exam | Most common in numerical problems | Requires more careful integration |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Students must answer one full question from the choice given.

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

> **Q. (A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 2 Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the volumetric equation for STOIIP starting from the definition of hydrocarbon pore volume. A reservoir has $A = 1500$ acres, $h = 40$ ft, $\phi = 0.18$, $S_{wi} = 0.30$, and $B_{oi} = 1.25$ rb/STB. Calculate the STOIIP.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With reference to the material balance equation, explain the various drive mechanisms in an oil reservoir. How is the **drive index** defined and what is its physical significance?

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must show:
1. $HCPV = V_{rock} \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})$
2. $N = HCPV / B_{oi}$
3. $V_{rock} = A \cdot h$ (acre·ft)
4. $1$ acre·ft $= 7758$ bbl
5. Final formula: $N = 7758 A h \phi (1 - S_{wi}) / B_{oi}$

Numerical computation:

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \times 1500 \times 40 \times 0.18 \times (1 - 0.30)}{1.25}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{7758 \times 1500 \times 40 \times 0.18 \times 0.70}{1.25}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{7758 \times 1500 \times 40 \times 0.126}{1.25}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{7758 \times 7560}{1.25} = \dfrac{58{,}650{,}480}{1.25} = 46{,}920{,}384 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 46.92 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**(b)** **Drive Mechanisms and Drive Index:**

The generalized MBE (Section 3.2) can be rewritten by dividing each term by the total expansion:

$$
1 = DI_{DD} + DI_{SD} + DI_{GCD} + DI_{CD} + DI_{WD}
$$

where each DI represents the **fractional contribution** of one drive mechanism to total production. Specifically:

- **DI_{DD}** — Dissolved Gas (Solution Gas) Drive Index: $DI_{SD} = N (R_{si} - R_s) B_g / \text{Total Expansion}$
- **DI_{GCD}** — Gas Cap Drive Index: $DI_{GCD} = N m B_{ti} (B_g / B_{gi} - 1) / \text{Total Expansion}$
- **DI_{WD}** — Water Drive Index: $DI_{WD} = W_e / \text{Total Expansion}$
- **DI_{CD}** — Compaction (Rock + Connate Water) Drive Index: small contribution from $c_f$ and $c_w$.

**Physical Significance:** The drive index quantifies the relative importance of each drive mechanism. A water drive index near 1 indicates a strong aquifer-dominated reservoir (high recovery, often 35–60% RF). A solution gas drive index near 1 indicates a "depletion drive" reservoir (lower recovery, typically 5–30% RF). The drive index plot evolves with time and pressure and is a key surveillance tool.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Drive Mechanism Questions:**
> Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by (1) omitting the unit-bearing form of the formula, (2) failing to list all five drive mechanisms (the 4 listed above plus **compaction drive**), or (3) not connecting each DI to the **expected recovery factor** for that mechanism. Always include the link: water drive → high RF; solution gas drive → moderate RF; gas cap drive → moderate-high RF (with proper management); compaction drive → minor contribution.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **Q. (B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Module 2 Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] An oil well produces at $q_i = 4000$ STB/day initially and declines exponentially with $D = 0.15$ /year. Calculate: (i) the rate at the end of 3 years, (ii) cumulative production in 3 years, and (iii) the EUR when the economic limit is 100 STB/day.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the p/z method for estimating GIIP in a volumetric gas reservoir. Sketch the characteristic p/z vs cumulative gas production plot and explain how GIIP and abandonment pressure are obtained from the plot.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Use $D = 0.15$/year with consistent units.

**Step 1 — Convert $q_i$ to annual basis:** $q_i = 4000 \times 365.25 = 1{,}461{,}000$ STB/year.

**Step 2 — Compute rate at $t = 3$ years:**

$$
q(3) = q_i e^{-Dt} = 1{,}461{,}000 \times e^{-0.15 \times 3} = 1{,}461{,}000 \times e^{-0.45}
$$

$$
e^{-0.45} = 0.6376
$$

$$
q(3) = 1{,}461{,}000 \times 0.6376 = 931{,}580 \text{ STB/year} = \dfrac{931{,}580}{365.25} \text{ STB/d}
$$

$$
\boxed{q(3) \approx 2551 \text{ STB/day}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative production in 3 years:**

$$
N_p(3) = \dfrac{q_i - q(3)}{D} = \dfrac{1{,}461{,}000 - 931{,}580}{0.15} = \dfrac{529{,}420}{0.15}
$$

$$
N_p(3) = 3{,}529{,}467 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_p(3) \approx 3.53 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**Step 4 — EUR at $q_{el} = 100$ STB/day:**

$q_{el}$ annual = $100 \times 365.25 = 36{,}525$ STB/year.

$$
EUR = \dfrac{1{,}461{,}000 - 36{,}525}{0.15} = \dfrac{1{,}424{,}475}{0.15} = 9{,}496{,}500 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{EUR \approx 9.50 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**(b) p/z Method for GIIP:**

The p/z material balance equation for a volumetric gas reservoir is (Section 3.4):

$$
\dfrac{p}{z} = \dfrac{p_i}{z_i} \left( 1 - \dfrac{G_p}{G} \right)
$$

**Characteristic p/z Plot:**
The plot of $p/z$ (y-axis) vs $G_p$ (x-axis) is a **straight line** with:
- y-intercept $= p_i / z_i$ at $G_p = 0$
- x-intercept $= G$ at $p/z = 0$ (i.e., the OGIP)

**Methodology:**
1. Collect reservoir pressure $p$, gas deviation factor $z$, and cumulative gas production $G_p$ at several time points.
2. Compute $p/z$ for each data point.
3. Plot $p/z$ vs $G_p$ and perform a linear regression.
4. Extrapolate the line to the **abandonment pressure** $p_{ab}$ (typically 200–500 psia for gas wells; corresponds to the minimum wellhead pressure at which the well can still produce against gathering-system back-pressure).
5. The cumulative production at $p_{ab}/z_{ab}$ is the **EUR** in scf.
6. The x-intercept of the line is **GIIP** (scf).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — p/z Method:**
> Students typically lose marks by: (1) forgetting to convert $p$ to **absolute** (psia, not psig), (2) using surface pressure instead of **bottom-hole** (or volumetric average reservoir) pressure, (3) ignoring the **abandonment pressure cutoff** when reporting EUR, and (4) not specifying the **linearity of the plot** as a diagnostic for volumetric behavior — a curved p/z plot indicates water influx (reservoir is **not volumetric**).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Evaluation and Prediction**

- [x] **STOIIP** is the stock-tank barrels of oil originally in place; **GIIP** is the scf of gas originally in place.
- [x] The volumetric constant **7758 acre·ft → bbl** and **43560 acre·ft → ft³** must be memorized.
- [x] $B_o$ (rb/STB) > 1 because oil shrinks when dissolved gas comes out at surface.
- [x] $B_g$ (res ft³/scf) is computed as $0.00504 \, z T / p$ with $T$ in **°R** and $p$ in **psia**.
- [x] $S_{wi}$ is the initial (irreducible) water saturation; $(1 - S_{wi})$ is the hydrocarbon saturation.
- [x] **Material balance** is a mass-conservation statement; it works in reservoir-condition volumes.
- [x] The four drive mechanisms are: **solution gas, gas cap, water drive, compaction drive** (with **injection** as a 5th artificial mechanism).
- [x] **Drive indices** sum to unity and quantify fractional contribution of each mechanism.
- [x] **Arps decline** has three forms: **exponential ($b=0$), hyperbolic ($0<b<1$), harmonic ($b=1$)**.
- [x] Exponential decline is the **most tested** on the KTU board; formula: $q = q_i e^{-Dt}$, $N_p = (q_i - q)/D$.
- [x] **Unit consistency** in DCA: $D$, $q$, and $t$ must all be in the **same time unit**.
- [x] **p/z method** applies only to **volumetric gas reservoirs** (no water influx); a curved plot = water influx.
- [x] **GIIP** is read from the x-intercept of the p/z line; **EUR** is read at the abandonment p/z.
- [x] **RF** = EUR / OOIP; typical ranges: water drive 35–60%, solution gas 5–30%, gas cap 20–40%, compaction <5%.
- [x] **PRMS reserves categories**: 1P (proved), 2P (probable), 3P (possible); commercial vs. sub-commercial: **Reserves** vs. **Contingent Resources** vs. **Prospective Resources**.
- [x] **PRMS project status**: On Production, Approved for Development, Justified for Development.
- [x] KTU 14-mark questions on this topic often integrate **two sub-questions** — typically a numerical part (volumetric or DCA) + a descriptive part (drive mechanism or p/z method).
- [x] Always show the **arithmetic in steps**; the KTU board examiner allocates partial credit per step.
- [x] The "hockey stick" deviation in p/z plots is the diagnostic fingerprint of **water influx** in gas reservoirs.
- [x] For hyperbolic decline, the **modified hyperbolic** model (with terminal $b$-value cutoff) is used to avoid the non-physical infinite EUR at $b \geq 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Techniques** in petroleum engineering refer to the integrated suite of engineering methodologies, mechanical systems, and operational procedures employed to bring hydrocarbons from the reservoir to the surface economically and safely, after the well has been drilled. These techniques encompass well completion design, artificial lift systems, well stimulation, sand control, workover operations, and production system optimization through nodal analysis.

In the context of KTU PECHT522 (Module 2), **Production Techniques** is defined as the discipline that bridges **reservoir engineering** (static fluid behavior underground) with **surface facilities engineering** (processing and export), using the **wellbore** as the connecting conduit.

The principal objectives are:

1. **Maximize hydrocarbon recovery factor** while respecting reservoir management constraints.
2. **Maintain wellbore productivity** through stimulation and sand control.
3. **Lift fluids to surface** when reservoir pressure is insufficient (artificial lift).
4. **Ensure well integrity** through proper completion, workover, and intervention strategies.
5. **Optimize flow rate** using nodal analysis to balance inflow and outflow performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw in a Thick Milkshake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a thick milkshake sitting at the bottom of a tall glass with a narrow straw. Initially, the milkshake may ooze up on its own (analogous to **natural flow** driven by reservoir pressure). But as the level drops and the shake thickens (declining reservoir pressure, increasing water cut, or heavy oil), you must **suck harder, use a wider straw, or warm the shake to make it flow** — exactly analogous to **artificial lift, well stimulation, and improved completions** used by production engineers.

- **Reservoir** → The milkshake
- **Wellbore** → The straw
- **Reservoir pressure** → Initial suction
- **Artificial lift (rod pump, ESP, gas lift)** → Your lungs sucking harder
- **Acidizing/fracturing** → Warming/thinning the shake to flow easier
- **Sand control (gravel pack, screens)** → Filtering chunks that block the straw

## 1.3 Production Life Cycle — Where Production Techniques Fit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Production techniques operate between the **drilling phase** (Module 1) and the **surface processing phase** (later modules). The sequence of events is:
>
> **Drill well → Run casing/cement → Perforate (or complete open-hole) → Stimulate if needed → Install lift mechanism (if required) → Produce → Monitor → Workover when problems occur → Eventually plug and abandon.**

The **Production Engineer** is responsible for everything that happens to the well **after** the drilling rig releases it, until the well is abandoned.

## 1.4 Classification of Production Techniques

Production techniques can be broadly categorized as follows (this is the **KTU Module 2 syllabus map**):

| S.No | Production Technique | Primary Purpose | KTU Module Coverage |
|------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Well Completion Methods | Establish conduit between reservoir & wellbore | High |
| 2 | Artificial Lift Systems | Lift fluids when reservoir pressure insufficient | High |
| 3 | Well Stimulation | Restore/enhance near-wellbore permeability | High |
| 4 | Sand Control | Prevent sand production that erodes equipment | Medium |
| 5 | Workover Operations | Restore or replace failed downhole equipment | Medium |
| 6 | Nodal Analysis | Optimize production rate across the system | High |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key physical constants and standard field units used throughout production engineering:
> - **g** = 9.81 m/s² (acceleration due to gravity)
> - **γ_g** = Gas specific gravity (air = 1.0), typically **0.55–0.75** for natural gas
> - **γ_o** = Oil specific gravity (water = 1.0), typically **0.7–0.95** for crude
> - **P_wh / P_wf** = Wellhead / Bottomhole flowing pressure (**psia** or **bar**)
> - **q_o** = Oil production rate (**STB/day** or **m³/day**)
> - **J** = Productivity Index (**STB/day/psi**)
> - **BHP** = Bottomhole pressure
> - **GOR** = Gas-Oil Ratio (**SCF/STB**)
> - **WOR** = Water-Oil Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Well Completion Methods

Well completion is the **process of preparing a newly drilled well for production or injection**. The choice of completion determines future intervention options, productivity, and cost.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Completions

#### A. Open-Hole Completion

The production casing is set **above the producing zone**, and the reservoir is left open to the wellbore.

**Why used:** Simple, cheap, used in **competent formations** (e.g., carbonates) that don't slough.

**How:** Drill past pay zone, set casing above, then produce directly through open hole.

**Pros:** No formation damage from cement/perforation; full reservoir exposure.
**Cons:** Cannot isolate water/gas zones; bad for unconsolidated sands; difficult to stimulate selectively.

#### B. Cased-Hole Completion (Most Common)

The reservoir section is cased and **cemented**, then **perforated** to establish communication.

**Steps:**
1. Run production casing across pay zone.
2. Cement casing in place.
3. Perforate casing and cement using shaped-charge explosive guns.
4. Establish flow through perforation tunnels.

**Perforation Density:** Typically **4–12 shots per foot (SPF)** with **60°–120° phasing**.

#### C. Liner Completion

A **liner** (casing string that does not extend to surface) is hung from the previous casing string with a **liner hanger**. Used in **deep wells** to save cost.

#### D. Horizontal / Multilateral Completion

The well is drilled horizontally (or multi-branched) to expose more reservoir area and improve productivity in thin pay zones or low-permeability reservoirs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perforation Penetration vs. Productivity:**
> Perforation tunnels ideally should extend **beyond the damaged zone** around the wellbore (typically 6–18 inches). If tunnels stay within the damaged zone, productivity is severely impaired.

### 2.1.2 Productivity Index (J) — Foundation of Production Engineering

The **Productivity Index (J)** is the single most important metric in production engineering. It links flow rate to pressure drawdown.

$$J = \frac{q_o}{P_r - P_wf}$$

where:
- $q_o$ = Oil production rate (STB/day)
- $P_r$ = Average reservoir pressure (psia)
- $P_wf$ = Flowing bottomhole pressure (psia)
- $J$ = Productivity Index (STB/day/psi)

**Physical meaning:** $J$ tells you **how many stock-tank barrels of oil you get per day for every psi of drawdown**. A high $J$ means a productive well; a low $J$ means damaged or low-permeability formation.

### 2.1.3 Darcy's Radial Flow Equation

For radial flow into a wellbore, the productivity index relates to reservoir properties:

$$J = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \left[ \ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s \right]}$$

where:
- $k$ = Permeability (md)
- $h$ = Net pay thickness (ft)
- $\mu$ = Fluid viscosity (cp)
- $B$ = Formation volume factor (RB/STB)
- $r_e$ = External drainage radius (ft)
- $r_w$ = Wellbore radius (ft)
- $s$ = Skin factor (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Skin Factor (s):**
> - $s = 0$: Ideal undamaged well
> - $s > 0$: Formation damage (positive skin)
> - $s < 0$: Stimulated well (negative skin) — produced by acidizing or fracturing

## 2.2 Inflow Performance Relationship (IPR)

The IPR curve describes how flow rate changes with bottomhole flowing pressure.

### 2.2.1 Linear IPR (Single-Phase Flow, Above Bubble Point)

For slightly compressible fluids above bubble point, IPR is a straight line:

$$q = J(P_r - P_wf)$$

### 2.2.2 Vogel's IPR (Two-Phase Flow, Solution Gas Drive)

When pressure falls below bubble point, free gas comes out of solution, and the relationship becomes **non-linear**. Vogel's empirical equation is used:

$$\frac{q_o}{q_{max}} = 1 - 0.2 \left(\frac{P_wf}{P_r}\right) - 0.8 \left(\frac{P_wf}{P_r}\right)^2$$

where:
- $q_{max}$ = Maximum (absolute open flow) rate at $P_wf = 0$
- $q_{max} = \dfrac{q_o}{1 - 0.2(P_wf/P_r) - 0.8(P_wf/P_r)^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standing's Modification (1971)** for reservoirs with known bubble-point pressure $P_b$:
> - If $P_wf \geq P_b$: Use linear IPR
> - If $P_wf < P_b$: Use composite IPR (linear above $P_b$, Vogel below $P_b$)

### 2.2.3 Fetkovich's IPR

A more general IPR model using **deliverability equation** based on back-pressure testing:

$$q = C(P_r^2 - P_wf^2)^n$$

where $C$ is the deliverability constant and $n$ is the back-pressure exponent (typically **0.5–1.0**).

## 2.3 Vertical Lift Performance (VLP) / Tubing Performance

The VLP curve relates flow rate to required bottomhole pressure to lift fluids up the tubing. It depends on:

1. **Hydrostatic pressure** of fluid column (heaviest component)
2. **Frictional pressure losses** in tubing
3. **Kinetic energy changes** (minor)

The general energy balance from reservoir to separator:

$$P_wf = P_{wh} + \Delta P_{hydrostatic} + \Delta P_{friction} - \Delta P_{kinetic}$$

For a single-phase liquid column (simplified):

$$P_wf = P_{wh} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Converting to field units (Beggs-Brill style gradient correlation):

$$P_{wf} = P_{wh} + \frac{\rho_m \cdot g \cdot h}{144 \cdot g_c}$$

where $\rho_m$ is mixture density in lb/ft³.

## 2.4 Nodal Analysis — The Heart of Production Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nodal analysis** is the procedure of choosing a node (a division point) in the production system and analyzing pressure losses on either side. The **operating point** is where the IPR curve (reservoir) intersects the VLP curve (wellbore + surface) at the chosen node — typically at the **sandface** or **bottomhole**.

**Operating Point:** $q_{IPR}(P_{wf}) = q_{VLP}(P_{wf})$ at the sandface.

By varying artificial lift parameters, tubing diameter, choke size, etc., we can shift VLP curve to maximize the intersection flow rate.

## 2.5 Artificial Lift Methods

When $P_wf$ approaches $P_r$ (low drawdown) or when reservoir pressure drops below hydrostatic head, **artificial lift** is required.

### 2.5.1 Sucker Rod Pumping (SRP) — Most Common Worldwide

**Principle:** A **surface pumping unit** (beam pump, "horsehead") drives a string of **sucker rods** up and down inside the tubing. A **bottomhole pump** (typically a traveling-barrel reciprocating pump) lifts fluid.

**Components:**
- Pumping unit (beam, crank, walking beam)
- Polished rod
- Sucker rods (steel rods connecting surface to downhole pump)
- Standing valve (fixed at pump inlet)
- Traveling valve (moves with plunger)
- Barrel and plunger

**Capacity Equation:**

$$Q = \frac{1440 \cdot A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot E_v}{B_o}$$

where:
- $A_p$ = Plunger cross-sectional area (in²)
- $S$ = Stroke length (in)
- $N$ = Pumping speed (spm)
- $E_v$ = Volumetric efficiency (typically **70–90%**)
- $B_o$ = Oil formation volume factor

### 2.5.2 Electrical Submersible Pump (ESP)

**Principle:** A **multi-stage centrifugal pump** is lowered on the bottom of the production tubing, driven by a **submersible electric motor**. Power is supplied via **armored cable** from surface.

**Components (top to bottom):** Motor seal → Motor → Intake section → Pump (multistage) → Gas separator (optional) → Discharge head

**Affinity Laws (ESP performance):**

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

$$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

where:
- $Q$ = Flow rate
- $H$ = Head (developed by pump)
- $P$ = Power
- $N$ = Rotational speed

**Horsepower Required:**

$$HP = \frac{Q \cdot H \cdot \rho}{550 \cdot E_p}$$

where $E_p$ is pump efficiency (typically **40–65%**).

**Range:** ESPs can handle **200 to 60,000+ BPD** and lift to **15,000+ ft** — best for **high-volume, moderate-depth** wells.

> [!WARNING]
> **ESP Pitfall:** ESPs are extremely sensitive to **free gas** (>10% by volume at intake) and **sand** (>50 ppm). Gas locks and erosion are common failure modes.

### 2.5.3 Gas Lift

**Principle:** Compressed gas is injected down the **casing-tubing annulus** through a **gas lift valve** (orifice) into the tubing. The injected gas **aerates** the fluid column, **reducing hydrostatic gradient**, allowing formation pressure to push fluids up.

**Two Main Categories:**
1. **Continuous Gas Lift:** Steady injection — best for high-volume wells.
2. **Intermittent Gas Lift:** Periodic slug lifting — used for low-pressure wells.

**Gas Lift Valve Mechanics:** Unloading sequence uses a series of valves (operating valves, orifice valves) at progressively deeper depths to start the well.

**Pressure-Operated Valve Equation (basic):**

$$P_{op} = P_c \cdot A_v - P_t \cdot A_b$$

where:
- $P_{op}$ = Valve opening pressure (psia)
- $P_c$ = Casing (gas injection) pressure
- $P_t$ = Tubing pressure
- $A_v$, $A_b$ = Valve and bellows areas

**Advantages:** Handles sand and gas well, no moving parts downhole, suitable for offshore/deviated wells.
**Disadvantages:** Requires compression facility (capital intensive).

### 2.5.4 Hydraulic Pumping

**Principle:** A **power oil** (clean crude) is pumped from surface down a string, drives a **bottomhole hydraulic motor** (or jet pump), which then lifts the produced fluids.

**Types:** Jet Pump (no moving parts) and Piston Pump.

### 2.5.5 Progressive Cavity Pump (PCP)

**Principle:** A **rotor** (helical steel shaft) turns inside an **elastomer stator**, creating sealed cavities that progressively move fluid upward.

**Best for:** **Heavy oil**, viscous fluids, sand-laden wells. Limited by rotor-stator wear.

### 2.5.6 Selection Criteria for Artificial Lift Method

| Lift Method | Depth (ft) | Volume (BPD) | Fluid Type | Solids Tolerance |
|-------------|-----------|--------------|------------|------------------|
| SRP | Up to 12,000 | 100–5,000 | Light-medium oil | Low |
| ESP | Up to 15,000 | 200–60,000 | Light oil, low gas | Very low |
| Gas Lift | Up to 15,000+ | 500–30,000 | Any, high gas OK | Good |
| Hydraulic | Up to 15,000 | 200–15,000 | Any | Good |
| PCP | Up to 6,000 | 50–10,000 | Heavy oil | Excellent |

## 2.6 Well Stimulation

Stimulation aims to **overcome formation damage** (positive skin) or **bypass near-wellbore damage** to improve productivity.

### 2.6.1 Acidizing

**Principle:** Inject acid to **dissolve minerals** in the near-wellbore region, restoring or enhancing permeability.

**Common Acid Systems:**

| Acid Type | Formula | Concentration | Use |
|-----------|---------|---------------|-----|
| HCl (Mud Acid) | HCl | 7.5–15% | Carbonate formations |
| HF | HF | 1.5–6% | Sandstone (with HCl preflush) |
| Organic (Acetic, Formic) | CH₃COOH, HCOOH | 5–10% | High-temperature, sensitive wells |

**Matrix Acidizing:** Acid is injected **below fracturing pressure** — dissolves pores slowly. Best for damage removal.
**Fracture Acidizing:** Acid is injected **above fracturing pressure** — creates fractures that are etched (won't fully close in carbonate). Creates high-conductivity flow path.

### 2.6.2 Hydraulic Fracturing

**Principle:** Inject fluid **above formation breakdown pressure** to create a conductive fracture, then **prop the fracture open** with sand/proppant.

**Frac Fluid Stages (typical slick-water frac):**
1. **Pad fluid** (no proppant) — initiates fracture
2. **Slurry stages** (proppant-laden fluid) — packs fracture
3. **Flush** (clean fluid) — displaces proppant

**Proppant Types:** Silica sand (cheap), Resin-coated sand (better), Ceramics (high strength, deep wells).

**Fracture Geometry Models:**

**PKN Model** (Perkins-Kern-Nordgren — for long fractures):

$$w(x) = 0.31 \left( \frac{q \cdot \mu \cdot x}{E'} \right)^{1/4}$$

**KGD Model** (Khristianovich-Geertsma-de Klerk — for short, wide fractures):

$$w = 0.29 \left( \frac{q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{E'} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Dimensionless Fracture Toughness** controls propagation behavior in PKN/KGD.

> [!NOTE]
> **$E'$** is the **plane-strain modulus**: $E' = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}$, where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

## 2.7 Sand Control

Produced sand causes **erosion, plugging, and equipment failure**. Methods to control sand:

1. **Gravel Pack:** Place sized gravel around screen; sand bridges on gravel, not on screen.
2. **Frac Pack:** Combination of fracturing + gravel packing — best for high-rate wells.
3. **Standalone Screens:** Slotted liners, wire-wrapped screens, premium mesh.
4. **Consolidation:** Chemically bond sand grains using resin.

**Saucier Criterion (for gravel pack design):**

$$D_{50,\ gravel} = 5 \text{ to } 6 \times D_{50,\ sand}$$

## 2.8 Workover Operations

A **workover** is any major intervention to restore or replace downhole equipment. Performed using a **workover rig**.

**Common Reasons:**
- Replace failed artificial lift equipment
- Recomplete to new zone
- Sidetrack to bypass obstruction
- Stimulate existing well
- Repair casing

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Exam**

| Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Productivity Index | $J = q_o / (P_r - P_wf)$ | STB/day/psi | Linear IPR |
| Darcy Radial J | $J = \dfrac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \left[\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s\right]}$ | STB/day/psi | J from reservoir props |
| Vogel's IPR | $\dfrac{q_o}{q_{max}} = 1 - 0.2(P_{wf}/P_r) - 0.8(P_{wf}/P_r)^2$ | dimensionless | Two-phase flow |
| Fetkovich IPR | $q = C(P_r^2 - P_{wf}^2)^n$ | STB/day | Gas wells (back-pressure) |
| SRP Capacity | $Q = \dfrac{1440 \cdot A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot E_v}{B_o}$ | BPD | Sucker rod design |
| ESP Affinity (Q) | $Q_1/Q_2 = N_1/N_2$ | – | Speed change |
| ESP Affinity (H) | $H_1/H_2 = (N_1/N_2)^2$ | – | Speed change |
| ESP Affinity (HP) | $HP_1/HP_2 = (N_1/N_2)^3$ | – | Speed change |
| ESP Horsepower | $HP = \dfrac{Q \cdot H \cdot \rho}{550 \cdot E_p}$ | HP | Pump selection |
| PKN Width | $w = 0.31(q \mu x / E')^{1/4}$ | inches | Long fracture |
| KGD Width | $w = 0.29(q \mu L / E')^{1/4}$ | inches | Short fracture |
| Saucier Criterion | $D_{50,g} = 5\text{-}6 \times D_{50,s}$ | inches | Gravel pack design |
| Hydrostatic BH | $P_{wf} = P_{wh} + \rho g h / (144 g_c)$ | psia | VLP gradient |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Productivity Index from Darcy's Radial Flow Equation

**Objective:** Derive the steady-state radial flow equation and identify the **Productivity Index**.

**Starting Point — Darcy's Law in Radial Coordinates:**

For steady-state radial flow of a slightly compressible fluid toward a well, Darcy's law in radial form states:

$$q = \frac{2\pi k h (P_e - P_wf)}{\mu B \ln(r_e / r_w)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify terms:**
- $q$ = flow rate (RB/day, reservoir barrels)
- $k$ = permeability (md)
- $h$ = pay thickness (ft)
- $P_e$ = external (reservoir) pressure at $r_e$ (psia)
- $P_wf$ = bottomhole flowing pressure at $r_w$ (psia)
- $\mu$ = viscosity (cp)
- $B$ = formation volume factor (RB/STB)
- $r_e$, $r_w$ = external and wellbore radii (ft)

**Step 2 — Define Productivity Index (J) by inspection:**

Comparing the equation with $q = J \cdot (P_e - P_wf)$, we identify:

$$J = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \ln(r_e / r_w)}$$

**Step 3 — Add the Skin Factor:**

In real wells, the near-wellbore region may be altered (damaged or stimulated). This is captured by adding an **additional pressure drop** in the skin region:

$$\Delta P_{skin} = \frac{q \mu B}{2\pi k h} \cdot s$$

Total pressure drop:

$$P_e - P_wf = \frac{q \mu B}{2\pi k h} \left[ \ln(r_e/r_w) + s \right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for J:**

$$J = \frac{q}{P_e - P_wf} = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \left[\ln(r_e/r_w) + s\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Van Everdingen-Hurst Modification (Infinite-Acting Reservoir):**

For transient (pseudo-steady state) flow, the constant 0.75 replaces the simple log term:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \left[\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s\right]}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:**
- As $k$ (permeability) ↑, $J$ ↑ → better productivity.
- As $\mu$ (viscosity) ↑, $J$ ↓ → heavy oil is harder to produce.
- As $s$ (skin) ↑, $J$ ↓ → damage hurts productivity.
- Acidizing/fracturing makes $s$ **negative** → $J$ increases.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vogel's IPR Equation — Origin and Usage

**Empirical basis:** Vogel (1968) developed an empirical IPR for **solution-gas drive reservoirs** producing below bubble point, based on numerical simulation of numerous reservoirs.

**The Final Equation:**

$$\frac{q_o}{q_{max}} = 1 - 0.2 \left(\frac{P_wf}{P_r}\right) - 0.8 \left(\frac{P_wf}{P_r}\right)^2$$

**Worked Example:**

**Given:** $P_r = 2500$ psia, $P_wf = 1500$ psia, $q_o = 500$ STB/day

**Find:** $q_{max}$ (absolute open flow potential)

**Step 1 — Compute pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_wf}{P_r} = \frac{1500}{2500} = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute RHS of Vogel's equation:**

$$RHS = 1 - 0.2(0.6) - 0.8(0.6)^2$$

$$\boxed{RHS = 1 - 0.12 - 0.288 = 0.592}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $q_{max}$:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{q_o}{0.592} = \frac{500}{0.592}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 844.6 \text{ STB/day}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute productivity at different $P_wf$:**

At $P_wf = 1000$ psia (ratio = 0.4):

$$q_o = q_{max} \left[ 1 - 0.2(0.4) - 0.8(0.4)^2 \right]$$

$$q_o = 844.6 \cdot [1 - 0.08 - 0.128] = 844.6 \cdot 0.792$$

$$q_o = 668.9 \text{ STB/day}$$

**Interpretation:** As $P_wf$ decreases (more drawdown), flow rate increases non-linearly, but rate of increase diminishes due to relative permeability effects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sucker Rod Pump Capacity

**Objective:** Derive the volumetric displacement equation for SRP.

**Step 1 — Plunger displaces volume per stroke:**

$$V_{stroke} = A_p \cdot S$$

where $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4}$ (plunger area, in²) and $S$ = stroke length (in).

**Step 2 — Total displacement per day (1440 minutes):**

$$V_{day} = A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot 1440$$

where $N$ = pumping speed in strokes per minute (spm).

**Step 3 — Account for volumetric efficiency $E_v$:**

$$V_{day, eff} = A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot 1440 \cdot E_v$$

$E_v$ accounts for **slippage** (fluid leaking past plunger) and **pump fillage**.

**Step 4 — Convert to surface conditions using $B_o$:**

Surface volume = subsurface volume / $B_o$ (since $B_o$ gives RB per STB, and we want STB):

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{1440 \cdot A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot E_v}{B_o}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

**Given:** $D_p = 1.5$ in, $S = 64$ in, $N = 10$ spm, $E_v = 0.85$, $B_o = 1.25$ RB/STB

**Compute:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi (1.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.25}{4} = 1.767 \text{ in}^2$$

$$Q = \frac{1440 \cdot 1.767 \cdot 64 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.85}{1.25}$$

$$Q = \frac{1{,}383{,}716}{1.25}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 1{,}106{,}973 \text{ in}^3/\text{day}}$$

Convert in³/day to BPD: $1 \text{ BPD} = 5.614 \text{ ft}^3/\text{day} = 5.614 \times 1728 \text{ in}^3/\text{day} = 9699 \text{ in}^3/\text{day}$

$$Q = \frac{1{,}106{,}973}{9699} = 114.1 \text{ BPD}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ESP Affinity Laws

**Foundation:** Pump similarity laws derived from dimensional analysis.

**Step 1 — Capacity (Q) is proportional to impeller tip speed:**

Since $v_{tip} = \pi D N$, and $Q = v_{tip} \cdot A = f(N)$, we have:

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

**Step 2 — Head (H) is proportional to velocity squared (energy per unit mass):**

$$H \propto v^2 \propto N^2$$

$$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Power (P) is force × velocity, hence proportional to $N^3$:**

$$P = \rho g Q H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

A pump operates at **3500 rpm** producing **5000 BPD** at **800 ft head** with **80 HP**.

If frequency is increased to **4000 rpm**:

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{4000}{3500} = 1.143$$

$$Q_2 = 5000 \cdot 1.143 = 5714 \text{ BPD}$$

$$H_2 = 800 \cdot (1.143)^2 = 800 \cdot 1.306 = 1045 \text{ ft}$$

$$P_2 = 80 \cdot (1.143)^3 = 80 \cdot 1.493 = 119.4 \text{ HP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** A small 14% increase in speed gives 14% more flow, 31% more head, but **49% more power**. Power demand grows rapidly — important for motor sizing.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Hydraulic Fracture Width — PKN Model

**Objective:** Derive the PKN fracture width expression.

**Step 1 — Elasticity of rock:** For an elliptical crack of half-width $w$ and length $x$, the pressure required to open it is given by Sneddon's solution:

$$\Delta P = \frac{G w}{(1-\nu) x}$$

where $G$ = shear modulus.

**Step 2 — Continuity:** Fluid leak-off into walls is neglected (PKN assumes height containment). The fluid continuity equation is:

$$q = 2 \int_0^x \frac{\partial A}{\partial t} dx + q_L$$

**Step 3 — Combining (Carter's leak-off model + PKN elasticity):**

After integration for constant injection rate $q$, the maximum width at the wellbore is:

$$w_0 = 2.31 \left( \frac{q \mu x}{E'} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 — Plane-strain modulus:**

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2}$$

**Final PKN Width Equation:**

$$\boxed{w(x) = 0.31 \left( \frac{q \mu x}{E'} \right)^{1/4}}$$

(Note: The factor 0.31 arises after unit conversion to inches when $q$ is in bbl/min, $\mu$ in cp, $x$ in ft, $E'$ in psi.)

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

**Given:** $q = 20$ bbl/min, $\mu = 1$ cp, $x = 500$ ft, $E = 4 \times 10^6$ psi, $\nu = 0.25$

**Compute $E'$:**

$$E' = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{1 - 0.0625} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{0.9375} = 4.267 \times 10^6 \text{ psi}$$

**Compute width:**

$$w = 0.31 \left( \frac{20 \cdot 1 \cdot 500}{4.267 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/4}$$

$$w = 0.31 \left( \frac{10000}{4.267 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/4} = 0.31 \cdot (2.343 \times 10^{-3})^{1/4}$$

$$(2.343 \times 10^{-3})^{1/4} = 0.2196$$

$$\boxed{w = 0.31 \cdot 0.2196 = 0.0681 \text{ inches} \approx 0.07 \text{ inches}}$$

This is a typical fracture width — about the thickness of 5 sheets of paper!

---

## 3.6 Derivations Summary Table

| # | Derivation | Key Output | Use Case |
|---|------------|------------|----------|
| 1 | Darcy → J | $J = \dfrac{2\pi k h}{\mu B[\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s]}$ | Well test analysis, J from reservoir props |
| 2 | Vogel IPR | $q_o = q_{max}[1 - 0.2 P_{wf}/P_r - 0.8 (P_{wf}/P_r)^2]$ | Two-phase flow forecasting |
| 3 | SRP Capacity | $Q = 1440 A_p S N E_v / B_o$ | Pump design |
| 4 | ESP Affinity | $Q \propto N, H \propto N^2, P \propto N^3$ | Variable speed ESP |
| 5 | PKN Width | $w = 0.31 (q \mu x / E')^{1/4}$ | Frac design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Production System (Reservoir to Sales Point)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir: Hydrocarbon-bearing formation] --> B[Wellbore: Tubing and casing]
    B --> C[Bottomhole Completion: Perforations / Open Hole / Sand Control]
    C --> D[Artificial Lift or Natural Flow]
    D --> E[Wellhead Christmas Tree: Choke and master valves]
    E --> F[Flowline to Manifold]
    F --> G[Separation Train: Oil, Gas, Water]
    G --> H[Oil Treatment: Dehydration and Desalting]
    H --> I[Gas Treatment: Compression and Sweetening]
    I --> J[Sales Point: Export Pipeline or Tanker Loading]
    G --> K[Water Treatment: Re-injection or Disposal]
    A --> L[Nodal Analysis Point]
    B --> L
    C --> L
    L --> M[Operating Point: IPR intersects VLP]
    M --> N[Production Optimization Decision]
    N --> D
```

**Description:** This block diagram traces hydrocarbon flow from the reservoir, through the wellbore and artificial lift, via the Christmas Tree, to surface separation and treatment. The **Nodal Analysis** loop continuously evaluates operating conditions and may trigger changes to artificial lift parameters to optimize production.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting Artificial Lift Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need Artificial Lift?] --> Q1{Reservoir Pressure Below Hydrostatic Head?}
    Q1 -- No --> NaturalFlow[Continue Natural Flow]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Fluid Viscosity cp}
    Q2 -- Less than 50 --> Q3{Gas Liquid Ratio}
    Q2 -- Greater than 50 --> Q4[Select Progressive Cavity Pump PCP]
    Q3 -- Less than 500 --> Q5{Total Solids Content}
    Q3 -- Greater than 500 --> Q6[Select Gas Lift Continuous]
    Q5 -- Less than 50 ppm --> Q7{Required Rate BPD}
    Q5 -- Greater than 50 ppm --> Q8[Select Hydraulic Pumping or PCP]
    Q7 -- Less than 5000 --> Q9[Select Sucker Rod Pumping SRP]
    Q7 -- Greater than 5000 --> Q10[Select Electrical Submersible Pump ESP]
```

**Description:** This decision tree helps a production engineer select the most appropriate artificial lift method based on reservoir pressure, fluid properties, gas-oil ratio, solids content, and required production rate.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Fracturing Treatment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Step1[Step 1: Pre-frac Diagnostic - Well Testing - Caliper Log] --> Step2
    Step2[Step 2: Perforate Pay Zone - 4 to 8 SPF - Deep Penetration Charges] --> Step3
    Step3[Step 3: Step Rate Test - Determine Breakdown Pressure - Fracture Gradient] --> Step4
    Step4[Step 4: Minifrac or Diagnostic Injection - Calibrate Frac Model] --> Step5
    Step5[Step 5: Pad Stage - Initiate Fracture - No Proppant - Slick Water or Linear Gel] --> Step6
    Step6[Step 6: Slurry Stages - Inject Proppant-Laden Fluid - Increasing Concentration] --> Step7
    Step7[Step 7: Flush Stage - Clean Fluid - Displace Proppant to Tip] --> Step8
    Step8[Step 8: Shut-in - Pressure Falloff - Closure Analysis] --> Step9
    Step9[Step 9: Flowback and Clean-up - Recover Frac Fluid - Establish Production]
```

**Description:** This sequence outlines the typical steps in a hydraulic fracturing operation, from pre-frac diagnostics through clean-up. Each step is critical — skipping diagnostic injection, for example, often leads to screenouts and treatment failures.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Sucker Rod Pump (SRP) Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface: Beam Pumping Unit - Horsehead - Walking Beam - Pitman Arms - Crank] --> B
    B[Polished Rod - Connects to Bridle - Seals Stuffing Box] --> C
    C[Sucker Rod String - Steel Rods 0.5 to 1.125 inch Diameter] --> D
    D[Bottomhole Pump Assembly: - Traveling Valve moves with plunger - Standing Valve fixed - Barrel houses plunger] --> E
    E[Production Fluid Enters Through Standing Valve - Lifted per Stroke - Through Traveling Valve into Tubing]
```

**Description:** Shows the path of mechanical power transmission in SRP from surface prime mover down through the rod string to the bottomhole pump, with fluid entry paths shown.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Nodal Analysis Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Node Location - Typically Sandface] --> B
    B[Gather Reservoir Data: - Pressure - Permeability - PVT] --> C
    C[Build IPR Curve - Darcy or Vogel] --> D
    D[Gather Wellbore and Surface Data: - Tubing Diameter - Depth - Pwh - Choke Size] --> E
    E[Build VLP Curve - Beggs-Brill or Hagedorn-Brown] --> F
    F[Plot IPR and VLP on P versus q Diagram] --> G
    G[Identify Operating Point - Intersection of IPR and VLP] --> H
    H{Sensitivity Analysis}
    H -- Change Tubing Diameter --> I[New VLP - New Operating Point]
    H -- Change Choke --> I
    H -- Install Artificial Lift --> I
    I --> J[Recommend Design Changes to Optimize Production Rate]
```

**Description:** This is the conceptual workflow of nodal analysis, the systematic method used to optimize production by analyzing pressure losses across the entire system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
