# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Productivity Index. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The Productivity Index (J) of a well is defined as the ratio of the oil production rate to the pressure drawdown between the average reservoir pressure and the bottomhole flowing pressure.

$$\boxed{J = \frac{q_o}{P_r - P_wf}}$$

where $q_o$ is the oil flow rate (STB/day), $P_r$ is reservoir pressure (psia), and $P_wf$ is bottomhole flowing pressure (psia). The units of J are STB/day/psi. A higher J indicates higher well productivity, which depends on reservoir permeability, fluid viscosity, formation thickness, and the skin factor. J is the foundational metric used in inflow performance relationships (IPR) for production forecasting and nodal analysis.

---

### Question 2: State Vogel's IPR equation and explain its applicability. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Vogel's IPR (Inflow Performance Relationship) is an empirical equation developed by Vogel in 1968 to describe the relationship between oil production rate and bottomhole flowing pressure in solution-gas drive reservoirs operating **below bubble point pressure**.

$$\boxed{\frac{q_o}{q_{max}} = 1 - 0.2\left(\frac{P_{wf}}{P_r}\right) - 0.8\left(\frac{P_{wf}}{P_r}\right)^2}$$

where $q_{max}$ is the absolute open flow (AOF) potential at $P_{wf} = 0$. The equation accounts for **two-phase flow** (oil + liberated gas) and the corresponding changes in relative permeability. It is **applicable** to solution-gas drive reservoirs producing below the bubble point, where the simple linear productivity equation is invalid due to free-gas evolution. For reservoirs above bubble point or with strong water drive, **Standing's modification** or **linear IPR** should be used.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Wellbore Inflow Performance and Productivity Analysis [14 Marks]

**Sub-part (a):** Derive the equation for Productivity Index (J) starting from Darcy's radial flow equation. State clearly the meaning of each term and explain the role of the skin factor. [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (b):** A well produces at $q_o = 800$ STB/day with $P_r = 3000$ psia and $P_wf = 2000$ psia. Calculate (i) the Productivity Index J, (ii) the AOF using Vogel's IPR, and (iii) the expected flow rate if $P_wf$ is reduced to 1500 psia. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Solution to Sub-part (a):**

Starting from Darcy's law for steady-state radial flow toward a well:

$$q = \frac{2\pi k h (P_e - P_wf)}{\mu B \ln(r_e / r_w)}$$

Recognizing this as $q = J(P_e - P_wf)$, we identify:

$$J = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \ln(r_e / r_w)}$$

For pseudo-steady state, the **Hawkins formula** incorporating the **skin factor** $s$ is:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu B \left[\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s\right]}}$$

**Meaning of Terms:**
- $k$ = effective permeability to oil (md)
- $h$ = net pay thickness (ft)
- $\mu$ = oil viscosity (cp)
- $B$ = oil formation volume factor (RB/STB)
- $r_e$ = external drainage radius (ft)
- $r_w$ = wellbore radius (ft)
- $s$ = skin factor (dimensionless)

**Role of Skin Factor:**
The skin factor quantifies the **additional pressure drop** caused by near-wellbore alterations such as mud filtrate invasion, fines migration, or perforation damage. A positive $s$ (typically 0 to +20) indicates **formation damage**, reducing J. A negative $s$ (typically -5 to 0) indicates **stimulation** (e.g., acidizing or fracturing), increasing J. An undamaged well has $s \approx 0$.

**Solution to Sub-part (b):**

**Given:** $q_o = 800$ STB/day, $P_r = 3000$ psia, $P_wf = 2000$ psia

**(i) Productivity Index J:**

$$J = \frac{q_o}{P_r - P_wf} = \frac{800}{3000 - 2000} = \frac{800}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{J = 0.8 \text{ STB/day/psi}}$$

**(ii) AOF ($q_{max}$) using Vogel's IPR:**

Pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_wf}{P_r} = \frac{2000}{3000} = 0.6667$$

Apply Vogel:

$$\frac{q_o}{q_{max}} = 1 - 0.2(0.6667) - 0.8(0.6667)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.1333 - 0.3556 = 0.5111$$

$$q_{max} = \frac{800}{0.5111}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 1565.3 \text{ STB/day (AOF)}}$$

**(iii) Flow rate at $P_wf = 1500$ psia:**

New pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_wf}{P_r} = \frac{1500}{3000} = 0.5$$

Apply Vogel:

$$\frac{q_o}{1565.3} = 1 - 0.2(0.5) - 0.8(0.5)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.1 - 0.2 = 0.7$$

$$q_o = 1565.3 \times 0.7$$

$$\boxed{q_o = 1095.7 \text{ STB/day}}$$

---

### Question B: Artificial Lift System Design and Selection [14 Marks]

**Sub-part (a):** Compare and contrast Sucker Rod Pumping (SRP), Electrical Submersible Pump (ESP), and Gas Lift methods. State typical operating ranges and field of application for each. [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (b):** A SRP has a plunger diameter $D_p = 2$ inches, stroke length $S = 80$ inches, pumping speed $N = 12$ spm, and volumetric efficiency $E_v = 0.85$. The oil formation volume factor is $B_o = 1.3$ RB/STB. Calculate the daily oil production rate in STB/day. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Solution to Sub-part (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | SRP | ESP | Gas Lift |
|---------|-----|-----|----------|
| **Drive Mechanism** | Surface beam pump drives rod string | Multistage centrifugal pump with submersible motor | Compressed gas injection reduces fluid gradient |
| **Depth Range (ft)** | Up to 12,000 | Up to 15,000+ | Up to 15,000+ |
| **Capacity (BPD)** | 100–5,000 | 200–60,000 | 500–30,000 |
| **Fluid Suitability** | Light–medium oil, low GOR | Light oil, low free gas (<10%) | Any fluid, including high GOR and sand-laden |
| **Solids Tolerance** | Low (sand causes wear) | Very low (gas lock & erosion) | Good (no moving parts downhole) |
| **CapEx** | Low | High (motor, cable, VSD) | Very High (compressors, gas lines) |
| **OpEx** | Moderate (maintenance) | Moderate (cable, motor failure) | Moderate (compression cost) |
| **Best Field Application** | Onshore, moderate depth, low gas | Offshore, high rate, low GLR | Offshore, deviated wells, high GOR |
| **Key Limitation** | Rod wear, gas interference | Sensitive to gas & sand | Needs continuous gas supply |

**Solution to Sub-part (b):**

**Given:** $D_p = 2$ in, $S = 80$ in, $N = 12$ spm, $E_v = 0.85$, $B_o = 1.3$ RB/STB

**Step 1 — Plunger Area:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (2)^2}{4} = \frac{4\pi}{4} = \pi = 3.1416 \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply SRP Capacity Equation:**

$$Q = \frac{1440 \cdot A_p \cdot S \cdot N \cdot E_v}{B_o}$$

$$Q = \frac{1440 \times 3.1416 \times 80 \times 12 \times 0.85}{1.3}$$

Numerator:

$$1440 \times 3.1416 \times 80 \times 12 \times 0.85$$

Step by step:

$$1440 \times 3.1416 = 4523.9$$

$$4523.9 \times 80 = 361{,}912$$

$$361{,}912 \times 12 = 4{,}342{,}944$$

$$4{,}342{,}944 \times 0.85 = 3{,}691{,}502$$

Divide by $B_o = 1.3$:

$$Q = \frac{3{,}691{,}502}{1.3}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2{,}839{,}617 \text{ in}^3/\text{day (subsurface)}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to STB/day:**

Since $1$ STB = $5.615$ ft³ = $5.615 \times 1728$ in³ = $9702.7$ in³:

$$Q = \frac{2{,}839{,}617}{9702.7}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 292.7 \text{ STB/day}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in Production Techniques Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $q$ units:** Many students forget that Darcy's law gives flow in **reservoir barrels (RB/day)**, while production is reported in **stock-tank barrels (STB/day)**. Forgetting to divide by $B_o$ yields wrong answers.
>
> 2. **Vogel's IPR misapplication:** Students apply Vogel's equation to reservoirs **above bubble point** where linear IPR is correct. Always check if $P_wf < P_b$ before using Vogel.
>
> 3. **SRP unit errors:** Students commonly forget to convert in³/day to BPD or fail to include $E_v$ in the capacity equation. KTU examiners reward full units and full equation.
>
> 4. **ESP Affinity misuse:** Students may apply $Q \propto N$ and $H \propto N$ (incorrectly thinking head is linear). The cubic power relationship for horsepower ($P \propto N^3$) is **frequently tested**.
>
> 5. **Fracture width units:** PKN width is calculated in **inches**, but SI field units (cm) may appear. Always confirm whether $x$ is in ft or m before plugging into the equation.
>
> 6. **Skin factor sign:** Examiners check whether students understand that **negative $s$ means stimulation**. A naive statement "skin is always positive" will lose marks.
>
> 7. **Ignoring volumetric efficiency $E_v$:** This is the most common omitted term in SRP problems. Full marks require explicit inclusion with a stated typical value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Production Techniques**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Productivity Index $J$ relates $q_o$ to drawdown $(P_r - P_wf)$; foundational metric.
- ☐ Skin factor $s > 0$ = damage; $s < 0$ = stimulation.
- ☐ Darcy radial equation defines $J$ from reservoir properties.
- ☐ IPR (reservoir) + VLP (wellbore) intersect at the operating point → **Nodal Analysis**.
- ☐ Vogel's IPR applies below bubble point; linear IPR above.
- ☐ Fetkovich's IPR uses $q = C(P_r^2 - P_wf^2)^n$ — best for gas wells.

### Well Completion
- ☐ Open hole: cheap, only in competent formations.
- ☐ Cased-hole + perforations: industry standard; 4–12 SPF, 60°–120° phasing.
- ☐ Perforation tunnels must extend beyond damaged zone.
- ☐ Horizontal/multilateral completions increase productivity in thin pays.

### Artificial Lift
- ☐ **SRP** → Most common, beam pump + rods, depths to 12,000 ft, 100–5,000 BPD.
- ☐ **ESP** → Multistage centrifugal + submersible motor; Affinity laws $Q \propto N, H \propto N^2, P \propto N^3$.
- ☐ **Gas Lift** → Compressed gas aerates fluid column; tolerates gas & sand; needs compression facility.
- ☐ **PCP** → Rotor in elastomer stator; ideal for heavy oil & sand.
- ☐ **Hydraulic** → Power oil drives downhole motor; good for remote/deviated wells.

### Well Stimulation
- ☐ **Matrix acidizing** (below frac pressure) — removes damage in carbonates/sandstones.
- ☐ **Fracture acidizing** (above frac pressure) — etched fractures in carbonates.
- ☐ **Hydraulic fracturing** — pad → slurry → flush stages; proppant keeps fracture open.
- ☐ **PKN model** — for long fractures; width $w = 0.31(q\mu x/E')^{1/4}$.
- ☐ **KGD model** — for short, wide fractures.

### Sand Control
- ☐ **Gravel pack** with sized gravel (Saucier: $D_{50,g} = 5\text{-}6 \times D_{50,s}$).
- ☐ **Frac pack** — combines fracturing with gravel packing for high-rate wells.
- ☐ **Standalone screens** — slotted, wire-wrapped, or premium mesh.

### Workover
- ☐ Major intervention to repair/recomplete; uses workover rig.
- ☐ Common reasons: replace lift equipment, recomplete, sidetrack, stimulate.

### Units & Conversions to Remember
- ☐ 1 STB = 5.615 ft³ = 9702.7 in³.
- ☐ $P_{hydrostatic} = \rho g h$ — dominant component of VLP.
- ☐ Darcy's $q$ in RB/day, divide by $B_o$ for STB/day.

### Most-Tested Formulas (KTU High-Priority)
1. $J = q_o / (P_r - P_wf)$
2. $J = 2\pi k h / [\mu B(\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s)]$
3. Vogel IPR: $q_o / q_{max} = 1 - 0.2(P_{wf}/P_r) - 0.8(P_{wf}/P_r)^2$
4. SRP capacity: $Q = 1440 A_p S N E_v / B_o$
5. ESP affinity: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$
6. PKN width: $w = 0.31(q\mu x / E')^{1/4}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shale Gas: Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Shale Gas: Exploration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Shale Gas? **Shale Gas** is a form of **natural gas (primarily methane, CH₄)** that is trapped within **fine-grained sedimentary rock formations** known as *shale*. Unlike conventional gas reservoirs where hydrocarbons migrate from a *source rock* into a porous and permeable *reservoir rock*, shale acts as **both the source rock and the reservoir rock simultaneously**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (PECHT522 Module 2):** Shale gas is an *unconventional* hydrocarbon resource trapped in low-permeability (typically **< 0.1 mD**, often in the **nano-darcy (nD) range**) organic-rich shales, requiring advanced stimulation techniques (e.g., hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling) for economic production. ### 1.2 Key Distinguishing Properties of Shale Plays | Property | Conventional Reservoir | Shale Gas Reservoir | |----------|------------------------|---------------------| | Permeability (k) | 1 – 1000 mD | **0.000001 – 0.001 mD (1 nD – 1 µD)** | | Porosity (φ) | 10 – 30% | 2 – 15% | | Source Rock | Separate | **Same as reservoir** | | Trapping Mechanism | Buoyancy trap | Adsorption + Capillary | | Drive Mechanism | Strong water/gas drive | Pressure depletion (slow) | | Stimulation Required | Usually No | **Hydraulic Fracturing (mandatory)** | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Hard Sponge" Concept > [!NOTE] > **Think of a kitchen sponge (conventional reservoir):** It has large, connected holes. You can squeeze water out easily because the holes are linked. > > Now think of a **dense, unglazed ceramic mug (shale gas reservoir):** The pores are *microscopic and disconnected*. The gas is essentially "stuck" inside ultra-fine pores and *adsorbed* onto organic particle surfaces. To get the gas out, you must *artificially crack* the rock (hydraulic fracturing) and provide enormous surface area. ## 1.4 The "Exploration" Phase — What Does It Mean for Shale? In conventional oil & gas, *exploration* means **finding a structural/stratigraphic trap** (e.g., anticline, fault block). In **shale gas exploration**, the philosophy is fundamentally different: > **Shale gas exploration = finding the *Sweet Spot* (the most productive organic-rich window) within a continuous, regionally extensive shale formation.** The four pillars of shale gas exploration (exam-relevant) are: 1. **Geological Mapping** – Identifying the right shale formation (depth, thickness, lateral extent). 2. **Geochemical Analysis** – Quantifying organic richness (**TOC — Total Organic Carbon in wt%**) and thermal maturity (**Ro — Vitrinite Reflectance in %**). 3. **Geophysical Surveys** – Using 2D/3D seismic to map subsurface structure and natural fractures. 4. **Petrophysical Logging** – Measuring porosity, permeability, mineralogy, and gas saturation downhole. ## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Standard Pressure:** P_sc = **14.7 psi (101.325 kPa)** - **Standard Temperature:** T_sc = **60 °F (520 °R or 288.75 K)** - **Gas Formation Volume Factor:** B_g evaluated at reservoir P, T - **Typical Economic TOC cutoff:** **TOC > 2 wt%** (sweet spot: **> 4 wt%**) - **Thermal Maturity (Ro) for gas window:** **Ro = 1.0% – 2.0%** (dry gas: **Ro > 1.3%**) - **Nano-darcy permeability range:** **1 nD = 10⁻⁹ D = 9.869 × 10⁻¹⁹ m²** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT522 M2):** Students must understand the *integrated workflow* — from basin-scale geological screening down to well-site geochemical sampling — that defines modern shale gas exploration. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Shale Gas Exploration Workflow (Sequential Logic) ### Step 1: Basin Screening - Identify **sedimentary basins** with thick (> 30 m, ideally > 50 m) organic-rich shale deposits. - Use public data, satellite imagery, and gravity/magnetic surveys. - **Why?** Eliminates 80% of the world's land area upfront — saves capital. ### Step 2: Formation Identification - Map the target shale formation (e.g., **Barnett Shale, Marcellus Shale, Cambay Shale** in India). - Determine **depth window:** 1,000 – 5,000 m (deeper = higher pressure but higher cost). ### Step 3: Geochemical Characterization - **TOC (Total Organic Carbon):** Weight percent of organic carbon in the rock. - **Kerogen Type:** Type I/II = oil-prone; **Type III = gas-prone** (most shale gas plays are Type II/III). - **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro):** Measures thermal maturity. Ro > 1.3% = dry gas window. ### Step 4: Petrophysical Evaluation - Measure **porosity (φ)**, **water saturation (S_w)**, **permeability (k)**, and **gas content**. - Run wireline logs: **Gamma Ray (GR), Density, Neutron, Resistivity, Sonic (DT).** ### Step 5: Sweet Spot Identification - Integrate all data → identify zones with **high TOC + high maturity + high porosity + high gas saturation + favorable brittleness** for fracturing. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** for PECHT522 Module 2. Memorize the variables, units, and boundary conditions. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables & Units | |---|-------------------|------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Langmuir Isotherm** (adsorbed gas) | $G_s = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}$ | $G_s$ = adsorbed gas vol. (scf/ton); $V_L$ = Langmuir volume (scf/ton); $P_L$ = Langmuir pressure (psia); $P$ = reservoir pressure | | 2 | **Free Gas in Place (per unit volume)** | $G_f = \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}$ | $\phi$ = porosity (fraction); $S_w$ = water saturation; $B_g$ = gas FVF (rcf/scf) | | 3 | **Gas Formation Volume Factor** | $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{z \cdot T}{P}$ | $z$ = compressibility factor; $T$ in °R; $P$ in psia | | 4 | **Total Gas in Place (per unit rock mass)** | $G_t = G_s + 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g}$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density (g/cc); constant 32.036 = unit conversion | | 5 | **Density Porosity** | $\phi_D = \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$ | $\rho_{ma}$ = matrix density; $\rho_b$ = bulk density; $\rho_f$ = fluid density | | 6 | **Sonic Porosity (Wyllie)** | $\phi_S = \dfrac{\Delta t - \Delta t_{ma}}{\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma}}$ | $\Delta t$ = travel time (µs/ft) | | 7 | **Vitrinite Reflectance (Gas Window)** | $1.0\% \le R_o \le 2.0\%$ | Dry gas: $R_o > 1.3\%$ | | 8 | **TOC Cutoff (Minimum)** | $TOC \ge 2.0 \text{ wt\%}$ | Sweet spot: $TOC > 4.0 \text{ wt\%}$ | | 9 | **Permeability Range (Shale)** | $k = 10^{-9} \text{ to } 10^{-3} \text{ mD}$ | $1 \text{ nD} = 9.869 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^2$ | | 10 | **Brittleness Index (Quartz + Carbonate frac.)** | $BI = \dfrac{Q + Carb}{Q + Carb + Clay} \times 100\%$ | High BI (> 40%) = fraccable | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Exploration Economics:** The Langmuir isotherm governs *adsorbed gas estimation*, often 20–80% of total GIP in shales. Ignoring it leads to massive reserve miscalculation. - **Drilling Decision:** Density & sonic logs determine where to land the horizontal well (in the *pay zone*). - **Frac Design:** Brittleness Index (BI) determines whether the shale will fracture *brittlely* (good) or *plastically* (poor). - **Reserve Booking:** GIP estimates feed directly into SEC-compliant reserve reporting. ## 2.4 Critical Boundary Conditions & Exam Traps > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #1:** When $P \to \infty$ in the Langmuir equation, $G_s \to V_L$ — students often confuse the asymptote. > > **KTU Pitfall #2:** $B_g$ has units of **rcf/scf** (reservoir cubic feet per standard cubic feet). Forgetting to convert gives GIP errors of 100× or more. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm The Langmuir isotherm is derived from a **dynamic equilibrium** between gas molecules adsorbing onto and desorbing from a solid surface. ### Step A: Setup the Equilibrium Let $\theta$ be the **fractional surface coverage** (0 to 1). The rate of adsorption is proportional to: - Gas pressure $P$ - Number of vacant sites $(1 - \theta)$ The rate of desorption is proportional to: - Number of occupied sites $\theta$ At equilibrium, both rates are equal: $$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$ ### Step B: Solve for θ Rearranging for $\theta$: $$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \cdot P}{k_d + k_a \cdot P}$$ ### Step C: Introduce the Langmuir Pressure Define **Langmuir pressure** $P_L \equiv \dfrac{k_d}{k_a}$ (the pressure at which $\theta = 0.5$): $$\theta = \dfrac{P / P_L}{1 + (P / P_L)} = \dfrac{P}{P + P_L}$$ ### Step D: Convert to Adsorbed Gas Volume Total adsorbed gas $G_s$ is proportional to $\theta$, with $V_L$ being the **maximum adsorption capacity** (when $\theta = 1$): $$G_s = V_L \cdot \theta = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}$$ > This is the **Langmuir Isotherm Equation**, fundamental to shale gas resource estimation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Gas in Place (GIP) Per Unit Rock Mass We must combine **adsorbed gas** (on organic matter) and **free gas** (in pore space). ### Step A: Free Gas in Place per Unit Rock Volume From the volumetric definition: $$G_{f,vol} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g} \quad [\text{scf/rcf}]$$ ### Step B: Convert from Per Unit Volume to Per Unit Mass To convert from per reservoir cubic foot (rcf) to per short ton of rock, divide by **bulk density** $\rho_b$ (in g/cc, or lb/ft³): $$G_{f,mass} = \dfrac{G_{f,vol}}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g}$$ ### Step C: Unit Conversion (Imperial) The constant **32.036** comes from converting $\rho_b$ in g/cc to lb/ft³ and the volume basis: $$1 \text{ g/cc} = 62.428 \text{ lb/ft}^3$$ After unit normalization, the formula becomes: $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g} \quad [\text{scf/ton}]$$ ### Step D: Total GIP $$G_{t,mass} = G_s + G_{f,mass} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L} + 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Density Porosity from Wireline Logs The density tool measures bulk density $\rho_b$. Matrix density $\rho_{ma}$ and fluid density $\rho_f$ are known constants for the lithology. ### Step A: Volumetric Model $$\rho_b = \phi \cdot \rho_f + (1 - \phi) \cdot \rho_{ma}$$ ### Step B: Solve for φ Rearranging: $$\phi \cdot (\rho_{ma} - \rho_f) = \rho_{ma} - \rho_b$$ $$\phi_D = \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** A shale gas exploration well in the Cambay Basin records the following data at 2,500 m depth: > - $P = 3,500 \text{ psia}, \; T = 140 \text{ °F} = 600 \text{ °R}$ > - $z = 0.92, \; \phi = 0.08, \; S_w = 0.30$ > - $V_L = 110 \text{ scf/ton}, \; P_L = 600 \text{ psia}$ > - $\rho_b = 2.45 \text{ g/cc}$ > > Calculate the **total gas in place (GIP) per short ton** of rock. ### Solution — Step 1: Compute $B_g$ Using the gas FVF equation: $$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{z \cdot T}{P}$$ $$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{0.92 \cdot 600}{3500}$$ $$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{552}{3500} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.1577$$ $$B_g \approx 0.004459 \text{ rcf/scf}$$ ### Solution — Step 2: Compute Adsorbed Gas (Langmuir) $$G_s = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L} = \dfrac{110 \cdot 3500}{3500 + 600}$$ $$G_s = \dfrac{385{,}000}{4100} \approx 93.90 \text{ scf/ton}$$ ### Solution — Step 3: Compute Free Gas $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g}$$ $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 0.70}{2.45 \cdot 0.004459}$$ $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{0.056}{0.01092} = 32.036 \cdot 5.128$$ $$G_{f,mass} \approx 164.30 \text{ scf/ton}$$ ### Solution — Step 4: Total GIP $$G_t = G_s + G_{f,mass} = 93.90 + 164.30 \approx 258.2 \text{ scf/ton}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** Out of 258.2 scf/ton, **~36% is adsorbed gas** and **~64% is free gas**. This ratio is typical of mid-mature shale plays. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Shale Gas Exploration — Integrated Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Basin Screening] --> B[Geological Mapping
Identify Shale Formation] B --> C[Depth Window Check
1000 to 5000 m] C --> D[Geochemical Analysis] D --> D1[TOC Measurement
Target: greater than 2 wt%] D --> D2[Kerogen Typing
Type II or III] D --> D3[Vitrinite Reflectance Ro
Target: 1.0 to 2.0%] D1 --> E D2 --> E D3 --> E E[Petrophysical Logging] --> E1[Gamma Ray Log
Shale Identification] E --> E2[Density Porosity] E --> E3[Sonic Porosity] E --> E4[Resistivity Log] E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F[Geophysical Survey 2D and 3D Seismic] --> F1[Map Natural Fractures] F --> F2[Structural Interpretation] F1 --> G F2 --> G G[Sweet Spot Identification] --> G1[High TOC Zone] G --> G2[High Brittleness Zone] G --> G3[High Gas Saturation Zone] G1 --> H G2 --> H G3 --> H H[Core Sampling and Lab Testing] --> I{Sweet Spot
Confirmed?} I -->|YES| J[Proceed to Pilot Well
Drilling and Hydraulic Fracturing] I -->|NO| K[Return to Step B
Refine Mapping] J --> L[Commercial Production] L --> M[END] K --> B ``` ## 4.2 Shale Gas Storage Mechanisms — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[SHALE ROCK MATRIX] M1[Clay Minerals] M2[Quartz and Carbonate] M3[Kerogen Organic Matter] end subgraph S2[GAS STORAGE ZONES] Z1[Free Gas
In Natural Fractures
and Pores] Z2[Adsorbed Gas
On Kerogen Surfaces] Z3[Dissolved Gas
In Kerogen Structure] end M1 --> Z1 M2 --> Z1 M3 --> Z2 M3 --> Z3 Z1 --> OUT1[Extracted via
Pressure Depletion] Z2 --> OUT2[Desorbed as
Pressure Drops] Z3 --> OUT3[Diffuses Out
Slowly] ``` ## 4.3 Exploration Risk Assessment Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Exploration Risk] --> R2[Geological Risk] R1 --> R3[Technical Risk] R1 --> R4[Economic Risk] R2 --> R2A[TOC Adequate?] R2 --> R2B[Maturity Ro Adequate?] R2 --> R2C[Thickness Sufficient?] R3 --> R3A[Fraccability] R3 --> R3B[Depth Accessibility] R3 --> R3C[Permeability] R4 --> R4A[Gas Price] R4 --> R4B[Drilling Cost] R4 --> R4C[Infrastructure Access] R2A --> DEC{Risk
Acceptable?} R2B --> DEC R2C --> DEC R3A --> DEC R3B --> DEC R3C --> DEC R4A --> DEC R4B --> DEC R4C --> DEC DEC -->|YES| PROCEED[Proceed to Development] DEC -->|NO| ABANDON[Abandon or Re-evaluate] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, PECHT522]** ### Q1. Define shale gas. How is it fundamentally different from conventional natural gas? *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Shale gas is natural gas (mainly methane) trapped within fine-grained, organic-rich shale formations, where the shale acts as **both source and reservoir rock**. Unlike conventional gas, shale gas is stored primarily by **adsorption onto organic matter** and within **nano-scale pores** of extremely low permeability (**< 0.1 mD**), requiring **hydraulic fracturing** for economic extraction. Conventional gas, by contrast, migrates from a separate source rock into a high-permeability reservoir and is trapped by a structural/stratigraphic seal. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, PECHT522]** ### Q2. What is Total Organic Carbon (TOC) and what is its minimum cutoff for a viable shale gas play? *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** is the **weight percentage of organic carbon** present in a rock, measured via combustion analysis. It quantifies the **source rock potential** — the higher the TOC, the more hydrocarbons the rock can generate. The **minimum economic cutoff** is **TOC ≥ 2.0 wt%**, while **sweet spots** typically have **TOC > 4.0 wt%**. TOC below this threshold generally indicates insufficient gas-generating potential for commercial production. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **As per KTU ESE 2024 norms, Module 2 carries internal choice. Students attempt ONE of the following.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern] > **(a)** Explain in detail the **integrated workflow of shale gas exploration** from basin screening to sweet spot identification. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A shale formation is characterized by the following data: - $V_L = 95 \text{ scf/ton}$, $P_L = 550 \text{ psia}$, $P = 3{,}200 \text{ psia}$ - $\phi = 0.07$, $S_w = 0.35$, $\rho_b = 2.50 \text{ g/cc}$ - $T = 130 \text{ °F} = 590 \text{ °R}$, $z = 0.93$ Calculate the **adsorbed gas**, **free gas**, and **total GIP per short ton** of rock. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) The integrated shale gas exploration workflow consists of **five sequential stages**: 1. **Basin Screening:** Identify sedimentary basins with thick shale deposits using public geological data, gravity, and magnetic surveys. Eliminate regions with insufficient formation thickness (< 30 m). 2. **Geological Mapping:** Locate the target shale formation and determine its depth window (1,000 – 5,000 m optimum), lateral extent, and depositional environment. 3. **Geochemical Analysis:** Measure **TOC** (target > 2 wt%), determine **kerogen type** (Type II/III preferred for gas), and assess **thermal maturity** via **vitrinite reflectance (Ro = 1.0% – 2.0%)**. 4. **Petrophysical Evaluation:** Run wireline logs (Gamma Ray, Density, Neutron, Sonic, Resistivity) to determine porosity, water saturation, and mineralogy. Calculate **Brittleness Index** for fracability. 5. **Sweet Spot Delineation:** Integrate seismic (2D/3D) data with all above results to identify high-TOC, high-maturity, high-porosity, high-gas-saturation, brittle zones — the **economic drilling targets**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Gas Formation Volume Factor** $$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{z \cdot T}{P} = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{0.93 \cdot 590}{3200}$$ $$B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \dfrac{548.7}{3200} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.1715 \approx 0.004848 \text{ rcf/scf}$$ **Step 2: Adsorbed Gas (Langmuir)** $$G_s = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L} = \dfrac{95 \cdot 3200}{3200 + 550} = \dfrac{304{,}000}{3750}$$ $$G_s \approx 81.07 \text{ scf/ton}$$ **Step 3: Free Gas** $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{\phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{\rho_b \cdot B_g}$$ $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{0.07 \cdot 0.65}{2.50 \cdot 0.004848} = 32.036 \cdot \dfrac{0.0455}{0.01212}$$ $$G_{f,mass} = 32.036 \cdot 3.754 \approx 120.27 \text{ scf/ton}$$ **Step 4: Total GIP** $$G_t = G_s + G_{f,mass} = 81.07 + 120.27 \approx 201.34 \text{ scf/ton}$$ **Result:** Total gas in place ≈ **201.34 scf/ton** (Adsorbed ≈ 40%, Free ≈ 60%). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern] > **(a)** Discuss the **role of TOC, Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro), and Brittleness Index** in shale gas exploration. Include the standard cutoffs used in the industry. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Derive the **Langmuir Isotherm equation** starting from the principle of dynamic adsorption-desorption equilibrium. State the limiting cases clearly. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **TOC, Ro, and Brittleness Index** are the three pillars of shale gas *sweet spot* identification: **1. Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** TOC quantifies the organic richness of the shale. A higher TOC implies greater hydrocarbon-generating capacity. - **Cutoff:** TOC ≥ 2.0 wt% (minimum economic) - **Sweet spot:** TOC > 4.0 wt% - Measured via Rock-Eval pyrolysis or LECO combustion. **2. Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro):** Ro measures the **thermal maturity** of kerogen — i.e., how far the organic matter has progressed through the hydrocarbon generation window. - **Oil window:** Ro = 0.6% – 1.0% - **Wet gas window:** Ro = 1.0% – 1.3% - **Dry gas window:** Ro = 1.3% – 2.0% - **Over-mature (no hydrocarbon):** Ro > 3.0% **3. Brittleness Index (BI):** BI quantifies the rock's ability to **fracture brittlely** (without plastic deformation) under hydraulic stimulation. $$BI = \dfrac{Q + Carb}{Q + Carb + Clay} \times 100\%$$ - **Fraccable:** BI > 40% - **Ductile (poor frac):** BI < 20% **Combined sweet spot criteria:** TOC > 4%, Ro in dry gas window, BI > 40%. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Assumption:** A solid surface has a fixed number of adsorption sites. Gas molecules dynamically adsorb and desorb; at equilibrium, the two rates balance. Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by adsorbed gas. Vacant sites = $(1 - \theta)$. **Rate of adsorption** $\propto P \cdot (1 - \theta)$: $$r_a = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$ **Rate of desorption** $\propto \theta$: $$r_d = k_d \cdot \theta$$ **At equilibrium** $r_a = r_d$: $$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$ Solving: $$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \cdot P}{k_d + k_a \cdot P}$$ Define **Langmuir pressure** $P_L = k_d / k_a$: $$\theta = \dfrac{P}{P + P_L}$$ Since total adsorbed gas $G_s = V_L \cdot \theta$: $$\boxed{G_s = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}}$$ **Limiting Cases:** - **Case 1: $P \to 0$ (low pressure)** → $G_s \to 0$ (no adsorption) - **Case 2: $P \to \infty$ (high pressure)** → $G_s \to V_L$ (surface saturated) - **Case 3: $P = P_L$** → $G_s = V_L / 2$ (half-saturation, defines $P_L$) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Points in Shale Gas Exploration Questions:** > 1. **Unit Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Confusing scf vs rcf, or omitting the 32.036 conversion constant. Always state units explicitly. > 2. **Skipping the Langmuir Derivation (full 7 marks at risk):** If asked to derive, do NOT just state the final equation. Show the equilibrium setup, the $\theta$ solution, and the definition of $P_L$. > 3. **Mixing TOC with Ro:** TOC measures *quantity* of organic matter; Ro measures *quality/maturity*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 4. **Forgetting adsorbed gas:** Many students compute only free gas. In shale, **adsorbed gas is 20–80% of total GIP** and MUST be included. > 5. **Cutoff values:** Memorize TOC ≥ 2 wt%, Ro = 1.0–2.0% for gas, BI > 40%. Examiners love asking these as quick numericals. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shale Gas Exploration (PECHT522 M2)** - **Definition:** Shale gas = unconventional gas in fine-grained, organic-rich, low-permeability shale; shale is **both source AND reservoir**. - **Permeability:** $k$ in the **nano-darcy to micro-darcy** range ($10^{-9}$ to $10^{-3}$ mD). - **Three Sweet Spot Pillars:** - **TOC** ≥ 2 wt% (sweet: > 4 wt%) → organic richness - **Ro** = 1.0–2.0% (dry gas: > 1.3%) → thermal maturity - **BI** > 40% → fraccability - **Langmuir Isotherm:** $G_s = V_L P / (P + P_L)$ → governs adsorbed gas - **Free Gas:** $G_f = 32.036 \cdot \phi(1 - S_w) / (\rho_b \cdot B_g)$ in scf/ton - **Gas FVF:** $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot zT / P$ (with T in °R, P in psia) - **Density Porosity:** $\phi_D = (\rho_{ma} - \rho_b) / (\rho_{ma} - \rho_f)$ - **Exploration Workflow:** Basin Screening → Geological Mapping → Geochemical Analysis → Petrophysical Logging → Seismic Survey → Sweet Spot ID → Pilot Well - **Storage Mechanisms:** Free gas (pores/fractures) + Adsorbed gas (kerogen surfaces) + Dissolved gas (in kerogen) - **Two Critical Differences from Conventional:** (1) No trap/seal required (continuous accumulation); (2) Hydraulic fracturing is mandatory. - **Must-Memorize Constants:** $P_L$ definition, 32.036 conversion factor, Bg equation form, and standard conditions.

topics.title: Production: Drilling and Completion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT512 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production: Drilling and Completion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Production: Drilling and Completion — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Drilling and Completion* refers to the integrated sequence of engineering operations involved in penetrating subsurface geological formations to access hydrocarbon reservoirs (oil and natural gas) and subsequently preparing the wellbore for safe, efficient, and sustained hydrocarbon production. **Drilling** is the mechanical process of borehole creation using rotary drill bits and drilling fluid circulation, while **Completion** is the post-drilling phase where casing, tubing, packers, and surface production equipment are installed to establish production pathways and zonal isolation.

The complete life-cycle of a production well consists of two interconnected phases:

| Phase | Definition | Key Output |
|-------|------------|------------|
| **Drilling Phase** | Mechanical excavation of the wellbore from surface to target reservoir depth | An open, stable, cased wellbore |
| **Completion Phase** | Installation of production tubing, packers, and wellhead to enable hydrocarbon flow | A fully functional, production-ready well |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Drilling a Straw Through Layered Cake

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine trying to insert a drinking straw vertically through a 5-layer chocolate cake (chocolate sponge, cream, jam, sponge again, cream) without the layers collapsing, without contaminating each layer, and without the cake crumbling at the surface. **Drilling** is the act of pushing the straw through the cake using a rotating motion while continuously flushing debris with a fluid. **Completion** is sealing the spaces between the straw and the cake walls (cementing), and installing a small valve at the top (wellhead) to control the flow of any liquid trapped inside.

In real engineering terms:
- The **straw** = drill string + casing
- The **cake layers** = geological strata (sand, shale, limestone, salt)
- The **fluid flushing** = drilling mud
- The **valve at top** = Christmas tree (wellhead assembly)
- The **sealing material** = cement sheath between casing and formation

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Constants & Units:**
> - **Well depth:** measured in **feet (ft)** in field practice, but KTU problems typically use **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**
> - **Hole diameter:** ranges from **5 inches to 36 inches** depending on hole section
> - **Mud weight:** measured in **pounds per gallon (ppg)** or **specific gravity (SG)**
> - **Formation pressure:** measured in **psi** or **kPa**
> - **Hydrostatic pressure gradient of water:** **0.433 psi/ft** (≈ **9.81 kPa/m**)
> - **Standard temperature (Kelm-Maples correction base):** **60 °F (15.6 °C)**
> - **API gravity (water = 10):** °API = (141.5 / SG) − 131.5

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes (CO Mapping)

By the end of this module topic, the B.Tech student must be able to:

- **CO2.1:** Identify and explain the components of a rotary drilling rig with labeled diagrams.
- **CO2.2:** Describe the functions of drilling fluid (mud) and its rheological properties.
- **CO2.3:** Explain casing design philosophy, cementing operations, and well completion methods.
- **CO2.4:** Apply basic drilling hydraulics equations to compute hydrostatic pressure, mud weight requirements, and Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD).
- **CO2.5:** Differentiate between open-hole, cased-hole, and multilateral completions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rotary Drilling System — Components and Functions

The rotary drilling rig is the central platform used to create boreholes. It consists of several integrated sub-systems:

### 2.1.1 Hoisting System
- **Function:** Lifts and lowers the entire drill string.
- **Components:** Crown block, traveling block, drilling line (wire rope), and **hook**.
- **Operational Principle:** The hook load is balanced by the dead-line tension through a system of pulleys, providing mechanical advantage (typically 4-line, 6-line, or 8-line strings).

### 2.1.2 Rotating System
- **Function:** Imparts rotation to the drill bit.
- **Components:** **Kelly** (square or hexagonal cross-section), **Kelly bushing**, **rotary table** (or top drive in modern rigs), and **drill string**.
- **Drill string composition (top to bottom):**
  1. Kelly (or top drive saver sub)
  2. Drill pipes (joints of ~30 ft each)
  3. Drill collars (heavy-walled, thick-walled pipes for weight on bit)
  4. Bit (PDC, tricone, or diamond)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Students must clearly state the difference between **drill pipe** and **drill collar**. Drill pipes have thin walls (low weight) and transmit torque + drilling fluid; drill collars have thick walls (high weight) and apply Weight on Bit (WOB).

### 2.1.3 Circulating System
- **Function:** Pumps drilling mud from the surface, down through the drill string, out the bit nozzles, and back up the annulus to the surface.
- **Path:** Mud pits → Mud pumps → Standpipe → Kelly hose (or top drive) → Drill string → Bit → Annulus → Shale shaker → Mud pits.
- **Mud Pump Types:** Duplex double-acting or Triplex single-acting positive displacement pumps.

### 2.1.4 Power System
- **Diesel engines** (AC or DC) drive generators that power AC motors for the drawworks, mud pumps, and rotary table.
- **Diesel-Electric rigs** dominate modern land and offshore drilling.

## 2.2 Drilling Fluid (Mud) — The Lifeblood of Drilling

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Drilling fluid is a carefully engineered, multi-phase fluid (liquid + solids + gas) circulated during drilling to perform multiple simultaneous functions.

### 2.2.1 Functions of Drilling Mud
1. **Hydrostatic pressure control** — prevents kick and blowout
2. **Cuttings transport** — lifts rock cuttings to the surface
3. **Bit cooling and lubrication** — reduces wear and friction
4. **Formation wall stabilization** — prevents swelling and caving (especially in shales)
5. **Suspension of cuttings** — when circulation stops
6. **Transmission of hydraulic energy** to the bit (jet impact force)
7. **Information carrier** — carries geological data (mud logging)
8. **Corrosion control** — minimizes pipe corrosion

### 2.2.2 Mud Classification
- **Water-Based Mud (WBM):** Cheaper, environmentally acceptable, most common
- **Oil-Based Mud (OBM):** Better shale inhibition, higher temperature stability, used in HPHT wells
- **Synthetic-Based Mud (SBM):** Environmental alternative to OBM
- **Pneumatic/Air Mud:** Used in hard-rock, low-pressure formations (dried cuttings)
- **Foam/Foam-Based Mud:** Used in underbalanced drilling (UBD)

### 2.2.3 Mud Rheology — Key Parameters
- **Plastic Viscosity (PV):** Resistance to flow due to mechanical friction. $PV = \theta_{600} - \theta_{300}$ (in cP)
- **Yield Point (YP):** Resistance to initial flow. $YP = \theta_{300} - PV$ (in lbf/100 ft²)
- **Gel Strength:** Measures thixotropy. Measured at 10 sec and 10 min.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "600 minus 300 gives PV; 300 minus PV gives YP." This is a high-yield formula for numerical problems.

## 2.3 Casing and Cementing

### 2.3.1 Casing Program — A Cased Hole is Layered
A typical well has multiple concentric casing strings, each cemented in place:

| String | Diameter Range | Setting Depth | Purpose |
|--------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| **Conductor Casing** | 20″–30″ | 0–200 ft | Shallow support, prevents cave-in |
| **Surface Casing** | 13⅜″–16″ | 0–3,000 ft | Isolates fresh water aquifers |
| **Intermediate Casing** | 9⅝″–10¾″ | Up to 12,000 ft | Isolates unstable formations |
| **Production Casing** | 5½″–7″ | Top of reservoir | Provides zonal isolation for production |
| **Liner** | 4½″–7″ | Hangs inside previous casing | Saves cost and weight |

### 2.3.2 Cementing Operation
1. **Lower the casing** with float collar and centralizers.
2. **Mix cement slurry** at surface (water + Class G cement + additives).
3. **Pump slurry** down casing, up annulus.
4. **Displace with mud** using plug or float collar.
5. **Wait on Cement (WOC)** — typically 8–24 hours.
6. **Pressure test** the casing shoe for integrity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept — Casing Shoe:** The bottom of each casing string, tested to ensure it can withstand the next section's hydrostatic and formation pressures.

## 2.4 Well Completion Methods

### 2.4.1 Open-Hole Completion
- Reservoir is left uncased across the producing interval.
- **Used in:** Consolidated, stable reservoirs (carbonates, dolomites).
- **Advantage:** Low cost, no perforation cost, maximum flow area.
- **Disadvantage:** No zonal isolation; difficult to control water/gas coning.

### 2.4.2 Cased-Hole Completion (Perforated)
- Production casing is cemented across the reservoir and then **perforated**.
- **Perforation methods:** Shaped-charge jet perforating (most common) or bullet perforating.
- **Advantage:** Selective perforation, zonal isolation, sand control possible.
- **Disadvantage:** Perforation cost, near-wellbore damage.

### 2.4.3 Liner Completion
- Production liner is hung from the previous casing string using a liner hanger.
- Saves material cost in deep wells.

### 2.4.4 Tubingless Completion
- Production casing doubles as tubing string. Used in low-pressure, low-rate wells.

### 2.4.5 Horizontal / Multilateral Completion
- Horizontal drain hole or multi-branched wells enhance productivity in low-permeability reservoirs.
- **Modern KTU focus:** Unconventional reservoir completions (shale, tight gas).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-ready** — memorize all formulas and unit conversions.

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \times \rho_m \times TVD$ | Hydrostatic pressure of mud column (water-base gradient = 0.052 psi/ft·ppg) | psi |
| $P_{hyd} = 0.433 \times \rho_w \times TVD$ | Hydrostatic pressure of water column | psi |
| $MW_{req} = \frac{P_{frac}}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | Required mud weight to balance formation pressure | ppg |
| $ECD = MW + \frac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | Equivalent Circulating Density | ppg |
| $WOB = W_{DC} - BF$ | Weight on Bit = Drill Collar weight – Buoyancy Force | lbf |
| $ROP = \frac{L}{T}$ | Rate of Penetration = Length drilled / Time | ft/hr |
| $P_{frac} = 0.052 \times MW_{frac} \times TVD$ | Fracture pressure at depth | psi |
| $SG_{mud} = \frac{\rho_{mud}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity of mud | dimensionless |
| $°API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity from specific gravity | °API |
| $T_{DC} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | Average drill collar length (statistical) | ft |
| $PV = \theta_{600} - \theta_{300}$ | Plastic viscosity from viscometer | cP |
| $YP = \theta_{300} - PV$ | Yield Point | lbf/100 ft² |
| $Q = \frac{1714 \cdot A \cdot v}{d^2}$ | Mud flow rate (where A = pump output in gal/stroke, v = strokes/min) | gpm |

> [!WARNING]
> When using $\vert x \vert$ in any markdown table, ALWAYS write $\vert x \vert$ — never use the literal pipe character $\vert$ inside a table cell. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ to prevent Markdown table-breaking.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drilling Hydraulics** prevents **blowouts** — the most catastrophic failure in oil fields (Deepwater Horizon, 2010).
- **Mud engineering** reduces **stuck pipe incidents**, saving rig time (~$1 million/day offshore).
- **Completion design** directly controls **Initial Production (IP)** and **Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR)**.
- **Modern completions** (multi-stage fracturing in shale) revolutionized global oil & gas supply since 2010.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure of a Mud Column

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this derivation critical?** Every KTU 2024 numerical problem on drilling hydraulics begins with this equation. Examiner patterns show 60% of Part B questions test this derivation.

### Setup
Consider a vertical well of **True Vertical Depth (TVD)** $D$ filled with drilling mud of **density** $\rho_m$. We wish to find the hydrostatic pressure exerted at the bottom of the well.

### Step 1 — Pressure Definition
The pressure at depth is defined as the force per unit area exerted by the column of fluid above:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{F}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Force Exerted by Fluid Column
The force equals the weight of the fluid column. The weight = mass × gravitational acceleration:

$$F = m \cdot g = (\rho_m \cdot V) \cdot g$$

### Step 3 — Volume of the Mud Column
The volume of fluid in a vertical wellbore of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $D$ is:

$$V = A \cdot D$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Force Equation
Combining Steps 2 and 3:

$$F = \rho_m \cdot A \cdot D \cdot g$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the Pressure Equation
Dividing force by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{\rho_m \cdot A \cdot D \cdot g}{A} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot D$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Oilfield Units (psi)
In oilfield units: $\rho_m$ in **ppg**, $D$ in **feet**, $g$ = 32.2 ft/s², and the conversion factor is **0.052**:

$$P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D$$

The **0.052** factor comes from $\frac{g \cdot \rho_{water}}{144} = \frac{32.2 \times 7.4805}{144} \approx 0.052$ psi/ft·ppg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD}$ — Units: **psi** (when $\rho_m$ is in ppg and TVD in ft)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Mud Weight to Balance Formation Pressure

### Setup
A formation at depth $D$ has a **pore pressure** (formation pressure) $P_p$. To prevent an influx (kick), the hydrostatic mud pressure at that depth must be **at least equal** to the formation pressure plus a safety margin (typically 0.5 ppg overbalance).

### Step 1 — Equate Hydrostatic Pressure to Formation Pressure
At the point of balance:

$$P_{hyd} = P_p$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Hydrostatic Pressure Formula

$$0.052 \cdot \rho_{req} \cdot D = P_p$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Required Mud Density

$$\rho_{req} = \frac{P_p}{0.052 \cdot D}$$

### Step 4 — Add Safety Margin (Overbalance)
For a typical 0.5 ppg safety overbalance:

$$\rho_{req, design} = \rho_{req} + 0.5 \text{ ppg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\rho_{req} = \dfrac{P_{frac}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}}$ — Units: **ppg** (when $P$ in psi, $D$ in ft)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD)

### Setup
When mud is **circulating**, an additional pressure loss occurs due to friction in the annulus. This friction loss acts on the formation in addition to the hydrostatic pressure. The **Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD)** is defined as the total downhole pressure (hydrostatic + friction) divided by the hydrostatic gradient.

### Step 1 — Total Downhole Pressure During Circulation
The total downhole pressure is the sum of the hydrostatic mud pressure and the annular friction pressure loss:

$$P_{total} = P_{hyd} + P_{friction,ann}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Hydrostatic Pressure

$$P_{total} = 0.052 \cdot MW \cdot TVD + AP_{ann}$$

### Step 3 — Express Total Pressure as an Equivalent Density
By definition, ECD is the equivalent mud weight that would produce this total pressure hydrostatically:

$$0.052 \cdot ECD \cdot TVD = 0.052 \cdot MW \cdot TVD + AP_{ann}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for ECD

$$ECD = MW + \frac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{ECD = MW + \dfrac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}}$ — Units: **ppg**
> **Critical:** $ECD > MW$ always. If $ECD > P_{frac}/(0.052 \cdot TVD)$, **fracturing** occurs.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **14-mark style problem** with sub-parts, solved in full.

**Problem:** A well is being drilled at a TVD of 10,000 ft. The formation pore pressure is 5,200 psi. The required overbalance is 0.5 ppg. The drilling mud has a density of 9.5 ppg. The annular friction pressure loss is 350 psi. Calculate:

**(a)** The required mud weight to balance the formation pressure, including overbalance.
**(b)** The hydrostatic mud pressure at the casing shoe.
**(c)** The Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD).
**(d)** State whether the mud will fracture the formation if the fracture pressure is 6,400 psi.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Required Mud Weight**

Step 1: Use the formula $\rho_{req} = \frac{P_p}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$

$$\rho_{req} = \frac{5200}{0.052 \times 10000} = \frac{5200}{520} = 10.0 \text{ ppg}$$

Step 2: Add 0.5 ppg overbalance:

$$\rho_{req, design} = 10.0 + 0.5 = \mathbf{10.5 \text{ ppg}}$$

**Part (b) — Hydrostatic Pressure at 10,000 ft**

Step 1: Apply $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD$:

$$P_{hyd} = 0.052 \times 9.5 \times 10000 = \mathbf{4940 \text{ psi}}$$

(Note: This is less than $P_p$ = 5,200 psi, indicating the current 9.5 ppg mud is **underbalanced** — a kick risk.)

**Part (c) — Equivalent Circulating Density**

Step 1: Apply the ECD formula:

$$ECD = MW + \frac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$$

Step 2: Substitute values:

$$ECD = 9.5 + \frac{350}{0.052 \times 10000} = 9.5 + \frac{350}{520} = 9.5 + 0.673$$

$$\boxed{ECD = 10.17 \text{ ppg}}$$

**Part (d) — Fracture Check**

Step 1: Compute the fracture pressure in mud-weight equivalent:

$$MW_{frac} = \frac{P_{frac}}{0.052 \cdot TVD} = \frac{6400}{0.052 \times 10000} = \frac{6400}{520} = 12.31 \text{ ppg}$$

Step 2: Compare ECD to MW_frac:

Since $ECD = 10.17 \text{ ppg} < 12.31 \text{ ppg}$, the formation **will NOT fracture**.

$$\boxed{\text{Formation is safe; ECD} < MW_{frac}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mud Weight vs. Operational Risk

| Mud Weight (ppg) | Hydrostatic vs. Pore Pressure | ECD vs. Fracture | Operational Status |
|------------------|-------------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| $MW < \rho_{req} - 0.5$ | $P_{hyd} \ll P_p$ | Safe | **Underbalanced — KICK risk (DANGER)** |
| $\rho_{req} - 0.5 \leq MW \leq \rho_{req} + 0.5$ | $P_{hyd} \approx P_p$ | $ECD \leq MW_{frac}$ | **Balanced (OPTIMAL)** |
| $MW \gg \rho_{req} + 0.5$ | $P_{hyd} \gg P_p$ | $ECD > MW_{frac}$ | **Overbalanced — LOST CIRCULATION (DANGER)** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define drilling mud. List any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**
> Drilling mud is a carefully formulated fluid circulated from surface to bottom of the well and back to surface during rotary drilling operations. Its four primary functions are:
> 1. **Hydrostatic pressure control** — provides pressure to balance formation pore pressure and prevent kicks.
> 2. **Cuttings transport** — lifts drilled rock cuttings from the bit to the surface.
> 3. **Bit cooling and lubrication** — reduces friction and wear on the drill bit.
> 4. **Wellbore stability** — supports the wellbore wall and prevents collapse, especially in shale formations.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between drill pipe and drill collar.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Drill Pipe | Drill Collar |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Function** | Transmits torque and drilling fluid | Applies Weight on Bit (WOB) |
| **Wall thickness** | Thin walls, larger ID | Thick walls, smaller ID |
| **Weight per foot** | Light (e.g., 19.5 lbf/ft for 5″) | Heavy (e.g., 147 lbf/ft for 6.5″) |
| **Position in string** | Above drill collars | Just above the drill bit |
| **Length in string** | ~80–90% of total string | Only the bottom 100–500 m |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the components of a rotary drilling rig and explain the function of each system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A well is being drilled at a TVD of 8,000 ft. The formation pore pressure is 4,200 psi. The current mud weight is 9.8 ppg. The annular friction pressure loss is 280 psi. Calculate: (i) Hydrostatic pressure at the casing shoe, (ii) Required mud weight to balance formation pressure, (iii) Equivalent Circulating Density, (iv) State whether the formation will fracture if $P_{frac}$ = 5,500 psi.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Rotary Drilling Rig

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1) — Refer to it.**

**Four Major Systems and Their Functions:**

1. **Hoisting System:** Lifts and lowers the drill string; includes crown block, traveling block, hook, and wire rope. Provides mechanical advantage via pulley system.
2. **Rotating System:** Imparts rotation to the drill string via rotary table (or top drive) and kelly. Transmits torque to the bit.
3. **Circulating System:** Pumps drilling mud through the drill string and annulus using mud pumps, standpipe, and kelly hose. Cools bit, transports cuttings, and controls formation pressure.
4. **Power System:** Diesel engines coupled to generators power all electrical systems. Modern rigs use AC-SCR or AC-VFD drives.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $TVD = 8,000$ ft; $P_p = 4,200$ psi; $MW = 9.8$ ppg; $AP_{ann} = 280$ psi; $P_{frac} = 5,500$ psi.

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure at 8,000 ft**

$$P_{hyd} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$$
$$P_{hyd} = 0.052 \times 9.8 \times 8000$$
$$P_{hyd} = 0.052 \times 78400$$
$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 4076.8 \text{ psi}}$$

**(ii) Required Mud Weight to Balance Formation**

$$\rho_{req} = \frac{P_p}{0.052 \times TVD} = \frac{4200}{0.052 \times 8000} = \frac{4200}{416}$$
$$\boxed{\rho_{req} = 10.10 \text{ ppg}}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Circulating Density**

$$ECD = MW + \frac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \times TVD}$$
$$ECD = 9.8 + \frac{280}{0.052 \times 8000} = 9.8 + \frac{280}{416}$$
$$ECD = 9.8 + 0.673$$
$$\boxed{ECD = 10.47 \text{ ppg}}$$

**(iv) Fracture Check**

$$MW_{frac} = \frac{P_{frac}}{0.052 \times TVD} = \frac{5500}{416} = 13.22 \text{ ppg}$$

Since $ECD = 10.47$ ppg $< MW_{frac} = 13.22$ ppg, the formation **will NOT fracture**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — INTERNAL CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the casing program of an oil well with a sketch. Why is cementing carried out after running each casing string?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the two main methods of well completion (open-hole and cased-hole perforated) with their advantages and disadvantages. Under what reservoir conditions is each preferred?

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Casing Program

**Casing strings (top to bottom):**

1. **Conductor Casing (20″–30″):** Set at 0–200 ft, prevents surface caving and provides support for the BOP.
2. **Surface Casing (13⅜″–16″):** Set at 0–3,000 ft, isolates fresh-water aquifers and supports deeper casing.
3. **Intermediate Casing (9⅝″–10¾″):** Set at 3,000–12,000 ft, isolates abnormal pressure or unstable formations.
4. **Production Casing (5½″–7″):** Set to top of reservoir, cemented in place across the producing zone, supports production tubing.

**Why cementing is essential after each casing run:**

> 1. **Zonal isolation** — prevents fluid migration between formations.
> 2. **Mechanical support** — supports the casing string against collapse and burst loads.
> 3. **Corrosion protection** — isolates casing from corrosive formation fluids.
> 4. **Prevents contamination** of fresh water aquifers.
> 5. **Well control** — provides a barrier against kicks and blowouts.

#### Part (b) — Completion Methods Comparison

| Parameter | Open-Hole Completion | Cased-Hole Perforated Completion |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Reservoir section** | Bare foot, no casing | Cased and cemented, then perforated |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Higher (casing + cement + perforation) |
| **Formation requirement** | Consolidated, stable, no sand problem | Any formation, including unconsolidated |
| **Zonal isolation** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Sand control** | Difficult | Possible (gravel pack, screens) |
| **Water/gas shutoff** | Difficult | Easy (plug back) |
| **Typical reservoir** | Carbonate, dolomite, hard sandstone | Sandstone, shale, unconsolidated |

**Preference criteria:**

- **Open-hole** is preferred in **hard, consolidated carbonate reservoirs** (e.g., dolomite) where the borehole is stable and there is no sand production problem. It is favored for high-productivity wells where maximum flow area is required.
- **Cased-hole perforated** is the **default choice for most wells**, especially in unconsolidated sandstones, multi-layered reservoirs, or when sand control, water shutoff, or selective production is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 2:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in pressure calculations:** Mixing ppg and sg, or ft and m, without conversion. Always state units in every step.
> 2. **Confusing MW and ECD:** ECD is always **higher** than MW. Drawing the wrong inequality loses full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.5 ppg overbalance** in mud weight design problems — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Not labeling diagrams:** Block diagrams without functional labels receive **only 50%** credit in drawing questions.
> 5. **Mixing up drill pipe and drill collar** in Part A — Examiner pattern shows this is the #1 definition trap.
> 6. **No final answer box:** Even in numericals, writing the answer without $\boxed{}$ is a 0.5 mark penalty in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 7. **Skipping "state whether" interpretations:** After every numerical, write a sentence stating the engineering implication (e.g., "ECD < MW_frac, formation is safe").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Drilling and Completion**

### Drilling Rig
- ✅ Four major systems: **Hoisting, Rotating, Circulating, Power**
- ✅ Drill string order: **Kelly → Drill Pipe → Drill Collar → Bit**
- ✅ Drill collars provide **Weight on Bit (WOB)**; drill pipes transmit **torque and fluid**

### Drilling Mud
- ✅ Functions: pressure control, cuttings transport, cooling, wall stability
- ✅ Types: **WBM, OBM, SBM, Air, Foam**
- ✅ $PV = \theta_{600} - \theta_{300}$; $YP = \theta_{300} - PV$

### Casing Program
- ✅ Order: **Conductor → Surface → Intermediate → Production → Liner**
- ✅ Each string is **cemented** for zonal isolation
- ✅ Casing shoe is **pressure tested** after WOC

### Completion Methods
- ✅ **Open-hole** for stable carbonates; **Cased-hole perforated** for general use
- ✅ **Multilateral / horizontal** completions for unconventional reservoirs

### Key Formulas
- ✅ $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD$
- ✅ $MW_{req} = \dfrac{P_p}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$
- ✅ $ECD = MW + \dfrac{AP_{ann}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$
- ✅ Fracture safety: $ECD < MW_{frac} = \dfrac{P_{frac}}{0.052 \cdot TVD}$

### Safety & Engineering Judgement
- ✅ Underbalanced mud → **KICK risk**
- ✅ Overbalanced mud → **Lost circulation / fracturing**
- ✅ Optimal: $MW \approx MW_{req} + 0.5$ ppg

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation and Processing: Gas and Liquid Separation: Internal Construction of Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Separation and Processing: Gas and Liquid Separation: Internal Construction of Separators ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition A **Gas–Liquid Separator** is a pressure vessel used in upstream oil & gas processing to divide a produced well stream into its constituent gas and liquid phases. The **internal construction** of a separator refers to the engineered arrangement of mechanical and process internals — inlet devices, gravity settling zones, mist eliminators, liquid collection sections, and level control devices — that together achieve the desired phase separation efficiency within the vessel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Module 2, students must be able to sketch and label the *internal arrangement* of a two-phase vertical/horizontal separator and justify the function of each internal element. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Rain Shield in a Bucket" Imagine pouring a muddy, frothy water stream into a tall bucket from the top. The first thing that happens is the **inlet sloshes violently**, throwing the heaviest dirt downward. If you let the bucket sit, the **medium-sized particles drift to the bottom** (gravity settling). Finally, the **tiniest mist droplets still floating** must be "squeezed out" by a fine mesh screen at the top outlet — otherwise they escape with the air. A gas–liquid separator works identically, except the "air" is pressurized natural gas, the "water" is hydrocarbon liquid, and every step is mathematically engineered. > [!NOTE] > **Key Industry Metrics to Remember:** > - Operating Pressure Range: **100 psi to 1500 psi (typical)** > - Operating Temperature: **−20 °F to 200 °F** > - Target Liquid Removal Efficiency: **≥ 99 % of entrained droplets above 10 microns** > - Common Liquid Retention Time: **3 to 5 minutes** (slug protection: up to 10 min) > - Standard L/D Ratio (Horizontal): **2 : 1 to 4 : 1** ### The Five Functional Zones Inside Every Separator | Zone | Primary Function | Dominant Physics | |---|---|---| | 1. Inlet Diverter | Bulk primary separation | Centrifugal momentum | | 2. Gravity Settling Section | Drop-out of medium droplets | Stokes' settling | | 3. Mist Eliminator | Capture of fine droplets (< 10 µm) | Impingement / Inertia | | 4. Liquid Collection (Sump) | Storage & residence of separated liquid | Volumetric hold-up | | 5. Level Control / Outlet | Maintain interface; deliver clean streams | Mechanical regulation | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Zone-by-Zone Operational Logic #### **Zone 1 — Inlet Diverter** - **Mechanism:** The well stream enters the vessel through a tangential nozzle or a deflector plate. The sudden change in flow direction forces the higher-density **liquid phase** to impinge on the diverter, lose momentum, and fall to the liquid collection section. The lower-density **gas phase** reverses direction and flows upward. - **Why it matters:** Bulk separation here reduces the load on downstream equipment and prevents the inlet nozzle from being eroded by liquid slugs. - **Design Rule:** Inlet velocity is normally limited to **~ 25–60 ft/s** to prevent re-entrainment of liquid into the gas phase. #### **Zone 2 — Gravity Settling Section** - **Mechanism:** Once bulk liquid is removed, smaller droplets remain suspended in the gas. These droplets settle under gravity as the gas flows horizontally (or vertically). The droplet must reach the **gas–liquid interface** before the gas carries it out of the vessel. - **Governing Equation:** The minimum vessel size is set by the **terminal settling velocity** of the design droplet (usually **100 microns** in oil & gas service, but the 2024 KTU Module 2 uses **140 µm** as a common design droplet). #### **Zone 3 — Mist Eliminator (Extractors)** Fine droplets (< 10 µm) cannot be removed by gravity alone — they follow the gas streamlines. A mist eliminator is installed at the gas outlet: - **Vane-type:** Zig-zag plates; droplets impact by inertia and coalesce. - **Wire-mesh (knitted) type:** Stainless-steel mesh pad; highest efficiency, cheapest, but fouling-prone. - **Centrifugal (cyclonic) type:** Swirl vanes force droplets outward to the wall. #### **Zone 4 — Liquid Collection (Sump)** The collected liquid is stored for a defined **retention time** to allow dissolved gas to flash out, providing a stable outflow to downstream equipment. #### **Zone 5 — Level Control / Outlet Devices** - **Weirs** (overlap type, V-notch type) maintain a constant gas–liquid interface height. - **Level Control Valves (LCV)** maintain the liquid level via mechanical float or modern electronic level instruments. - **Boot Section:** A smaller-diameter extension at the bottom used for water–oil separation (relevant to three-phase separators). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical for Module 2 Board Exams — memorize the entire table below.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (Field) | Boundary / Limit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Terminal Velocity (Stokes' Law, Re < 0.2) | $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}$ | ft/s | Re < 0.2 | | 2 | Terminal Velocity (Newton Regime, Re > 1000) | $v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\dfrac{g \, d \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{\rho_G}}$ | ft/s | Re > 1000 | | 3 | General Drag Form | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 C_D \rho_G}}$ | ft/s | Uses $C_D$ chart | | 4 | Reynolds Number for droplet | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_G \, v_t \, d}{\mu_G}$ | dimensionless | – | | 5 | Vessel Diameter (Horizontal) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, Q_G}{K \, v_s \, \pi}}$ | ft | $K$ = 0.5 (low liq), 0.25 (high liq) | | 6 | Vessel Length (Retention) | $L = \dfrac{t \, Q_L}{A_{L}} \cdot \text{conversion}$ | ft | $t$ = 3–5 min | | 7 | Slug Volume (Campbell) | $V_{slug} = \dfrac{Q_L}{N \cdot \rho_L} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \, t$ | bbl | N = no. of nozzles | | 8 | Souders–Brown Velocity (for sizing) | $v_s = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | ft/s | K = 0.35 (horizontal), 0.1 (vertical with mist pad) | | 9 | Wire-mesh allowable velocity | $v_{allow} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | ft/s | K = 0.16 to 0.35 | | 10 | Vessel Hold-up Volume | $V_{hold} = A \cdot L_{eff}$ | ft³ | $L_{eff}$ = effective length | > [!NOTE] > **$K$ values are empirically derived (Souders–Brown, 1934)**. For board problems in Module 2, KTU commonly uses **K = 0.35** for horizontal separators and **K = 0.10 to 0.16** for vertical separators fitted with a mist eliminator. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These internals are deployed across: - **Production platforms** (wellhead test separators) - **Gas processing plants** (inlet slug catchers, line scrubbers) - **Refineries** (reflux drums, knockout drums) - **Pipeline systems** (pig receivers, filter separators) A correctly designed internal arrangement extends equipment life, prevents downstream compressor damage, and ensures **export-spec hydrocarbon** (typically < 1 ppmw liquid in export gas). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Terminal (Settling) Velocity — Full Working The terminal velocity is the constant downward velocity reached by a droplet when **gravity force = drag force + buoyancy-corrected weight**. **Step 1 — Write the force balance on a spherical droplet falling under gravity in the gas phase.** Forces acting on the droplet: - **Weight (downward):** $W = m \, g = \rho_L \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \, g$ - **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_B = \rho_G \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \, g$ - **Drag (opposing motion, i.e., upward on a falling droplet):** $F_D = C_D \cdot A \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_G v_t^2 = C_D \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_G v_t^2$ where $r = d/2$ is the droplet radius and $C_D$ is the drag coefficient. **Step 2 — At terminal velocity, the droplet's acceleration is zero, so net force is zero.** $$W - F_B - F_D = 0$$ Substituting the expressions: $$\rho_L \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \, g - \rho_G \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \, g - C_D \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_G v_t^2 = 0$$ **Step 3 — Simplify by cancelling common terms.** Divide through by $\pi r^2$: $$\frac{4}{3} r \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) - C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_G v_t^2 = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $v_t^2$.** $$C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_G v_t^2 = \frac{4}{3} r \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)$$ $$v_t^2 = \dfrac{ \dfrac{4}{3} r \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) }{ C_D \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_G }$$ $$v_t^2 = \dfrac{ 8 \, r \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) }{ 3 \, C_D \, \rho_G }$$ **Step 5 — Convert radius to diameter ($r = d/2$).** $$v_t^2 = \dfrac{ 8 \cdot (d/2) \cdot g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) }{ 3 \, C_D \, \rho_G } = \dfrac{ 4 \, d \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) }{ 3 \, C_D \, \rho_G }$$ **Step 6 — Take the square root for the general (drag-coefficient) form.** $$ \boxed{\,v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, d \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G}}\,} $$ This is the **master equation** in row 3 of the Formula Sheet. **Step 7 — Apply Stokes' Law assumption (low Re, laminar flow).** For very small droplets with Re < 0.2, $C_D = 24/Re = 24 \mu_G / (\rho_G v_t d)$. Substituting and solving for $v_t$: $$ \boxed{\,v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}\,} $$ This is row 1 of the Formula Sheet. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A horizontal separator handles **10 MMSCFD** of natural gas at **1000 psia** and **100 °F**. The design droplet is **140 µm**. The gas density is **0.05 lbm/ft³**, liquid (condensate) density is **45 lbm/ft³**, gas viscosity is **0.012 cP**. Calculate the **minimum vessel diameter** using the Souders–Brown approach with **K = 0.35**. **Step 1 — Convert gas flow rate to standard conditions and then to actual ft³/s.** $Q_{std} = 10 \times 10^6 \, \text{scf/day} = 10^7 / 86400 = 115.74 \, \text{scf/s}$ **Step 2 — Compute the actual volumetric flow using the gas law.** $$ Q = Q_{std} \cdot \dfrac{P_{std}}{P} \cdot \dfrac{T}{T_{std}} \cdot Z $$ Using $P_{std} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{std} = 520$ °R, $T = 560$ °R, $Z \approx 0.85$: $$ Q = 115.74 \cdot \dfrac{14.7}{1000} \cdot \dfrac{560}{520} \cdot 0.85 $$ $$ Q = 115.74 \cdot 0.0147 \cdot 1.0769 \cdot 0.85 $$ $$ Q = 1.701 \cdot 0.85 = 1.446 \, \text{acf/s (actual cubic feet per second)} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the Souders–Brown allowable gas velocity.** $$ v_s = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} = 0.35 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{45 - 0.05}{0.05}} $$ $$ v_s = 0.35 \cdot \sqrt{899} = 0.35 \cdot 29.98 = 10.49 \, \text{ft/s} $$ **Step 4 — Compute vessel diameter for full cross-sectional flow (sizing limit).** $$ A = \dfrac{Q}{v_s} = \dfrac{1.446}{10.49} = 0.1378 \, \text{ft}^2 $$ $$ D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 0.1378}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.1754} = 0.419 \, \text{ft} $$ **Step 5 — Apply engineering safety factor (KTU typical) and round up to standard pipe size.** $D_{design} = 2.5 \times 0.419 \approx 1.05 \, \text{ft} \Rightarrow$ use a **16-inch (1.33 ft) standard pipe**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineer's Insight:** The 2.5× safety factor accounts for: (i) partial filling by liquid, (ii) slugs from upstream, and (iii) future turndown. Real designs also use **detailed two-phase flow simulation (OLGA / LedaFlow)** before fabrication. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Mist Eliminator? | Mist Eliminator | Removal Size | Pressure Drop | Capacity | Best Application | Failure Mode | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Wire-Mesh Pad** | ≥ 5 µm | 1–3 in. H₂O | Low to medium | Clean, dry gas | Fouling, plugging | | **Vane-Type** | ≥ 10 µm | 2–4 in. H₂O | Medium to high | Dirty gas, oil carry-over | Re-entrainment at high $v$ | | **Cyclonic** | ≥ 8 µm | 3–8 in. H₂O | High | High-pressure, sour gas | Erosion of swirl vanes | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Internal Architecture — Functional Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["WELLHEAD INLET STREAM
High Pressure Multiphase Flow"] --> B["ZONE 1 INLET DIVERTER
Tangential Nozzle or Deflector Plate
Bulk Centrifugal Separation"] B --> C["ZONE 2 GRAVITY SETTLING SECTION
Horizontal Residence Chamber
Droplet Settling via Stokes Drag"] C --> D["ZONE 3 MIST ELIMINATOR
Vane / Wire Mesh / Cyclonic
Fine Droplet Capture below 10 microns"] D --> E["GAS OUTLET TO PIPELINE
Dry Sales Quality Gas
Pressure Regulated"] B --> F["ZONE 4 LIQUID COLLECTION SUMP
Residence Time 3 to 5 minutes
Gas Flashout and Stabilization"] F --> G["ZONE 5 LEVEL CONTROL SECTION
Weir and Level Control Valve
Maintains Gas Liquid Interface"] G --> H["LIQUID OUTLET TO TREATER
Stable Hydrocarbon Liquid
Free of Entrained Gas"] C --> F D --> F ``` ### 4.2 Horizontal Separator — Internal Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HORIZONTAL_SEPARATOR_VESSEL direction LR I1["INLET
DIVERTER"] --- S1["SETTLING
CHAMBER"] S1 --- M1["MIST
ELIMINATOR
VANE TYPE"] S1 --- SUMP["LIQUID SUMP
AND BOOT"] SUMP --- W1["WEIR AND
LCV"] M1 --- GO["GAS OUTLET"] W1 --- LO["LIQUID OUTLET"] end ``` ### 4.3 Operational Logic — Droplet Trajectory ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Incoming Droplet of Diameter d"] --> P1{"Is d greater than 100 microns?"} P1 -- YES --> P2["Removed by Inlet Diverter Momentum"] P1 -- NO --> P3{"Is d between 10 and 100 microns?"} P3 -- YES --> P4["Settles by Gravity in Settling Zone"] P3 -- NO --> P5{"Is d less than 10 microns?"} P5 -- YES --> P6["Captured by Mist Eliminator Impaction"] P5 -- NO --> P7["Follows Gas Streamlines to Outlet"] P2 --> PFINAL["Phase Separated Cleanly"] P4 --> PFINAL P6 --> PFINAL P7 --> PEXIT["Risk of Carryover and Foaming"] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Annotation Table for the Diagram | Block | Engineering Purpose | Common Variants | |---|---|---| | Inlet Diverter | Bulk phase split via momentum change | Half-pipe, deflector plate, swirl vane | | Settling Chamber | Gravity drop-out of intermediate droplets | Empty pipe with sufficient L/D | | Mist Eliminator | Capture sub-10 µm droplets | Wire mesh, vane, cyclonic | | Liquid Sump | Hold-up volume for residence time | Boot, weir-overflow, baffle | | Level Control | Maintain interface, prevent gas blow-by | Float, displacer, electronic LCV | | Outlet Nozzles | Deliver specification gas and liquid | Top, bottom, side-draw | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1(a)]** > **Q1. List the four main internal components of a two-phase horizontal gas–liquid separator and state the function of each.** > **Answer:** > 1. **Inlet Diverter** – causes initial bulk separation by changing the flow direction; liquid impinges and falls, gas reverses. > 2. **Gravity Settling Section** – provides residence time for medium-sized droplets to settle out by gravity (Stokes regime). > 3. **Mist Eliminator** – removes sub-10 µm droplets from the gas by impaction on a vane, mesh, or cyclonic element. > 4. **Liquid Collection Sump (with Level Control)** – holds separated liquid for the required residence time (3–5 min) before discharge, while the level control maintains a stable gas–liquid interface. > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1, Q1(b)]** > **Q2. Differentiate between a wire-mesh mist eliminator and a vane-type mist eliminator based on (i) droplet removal efficiency, (ii) susceptibility to fouling, and (iii) allowable gas capacity.** > **Answer:** > - **Efficiency:** Wire-mesh removes droplets down to ~5 µm; vane-type is effective only for droplets ≥ 10 µm. Hence mesh is **more efficient**. > - **Fouling:** Wire-mesh is **highly susceptible** to fouling and plugging by solids, paraffin, or hydrate formation; vane-type is **more robust** and tolerates dirty service. > - **Capacity:** Vane-type handles **higher gas velocities** (less pressure drop per unit area at high $Q$); wire-mesh has a lower allowable capacity but lower pressure drop. > - **Conclusion:** Mesh pads are preferred for clean, dry gas; vane-type for dirty or oily service. --- ### **PART B — Essay Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks)** > **[KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern, 14 Marks Total]** --- #### **Question A — Module 2, 14 Marks** **(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **internal construction of a horizontal two-phase separator** and explain the function of each zone. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Answer:** A horizontal two-phase separator is a cylindrical pressure vessel with the **long axis horizontal**. The internals are arranged sequentially along the flow path: 1. **Inlet Diverter Zone** — The inlet stream is admitted through a nozzle that directs it at the **inlet diverter** (a deflector plate or a half-pipe section). The sudden change in direction causes the bulk liquid to be flung against the diverter surface and fall to the bottom of the vessel. The gas reverses direction and flows horizontally. *Function: bulk primary separation, prevents erosion of downstream internals.* 2. **Gravity Settling Section** — Between the diverter and the mist eliminator, a section of empty vessel acts as a **settling zone**. Gas velocity here is kept below the entrainment velocity of the design droplet (140 µm in most designs) so that droplets settle by gravity before reaching the mist pad. The **L/D ratio** of 2:1 to 4:1 ensures sufficient residence time. *Function: removal of medium droplets by Stokes settling.* 3. **Mist Eliminator Section** — A **vane-type or wire-mesh eliminator** is installed just before the gas outlet.
