Fine droplets (< 10 µm) impact the surfaces by inertia, coalesce, and fall back. *Function: fine droplet capture to meet export gas quality.* 4. **Liquid Sump / Collection Zone** — At the bottom of the vessel, separated liquid is stored for a residence time of **3–5 minutes** (extendable for slug flow). A **weir** at the outlet end maintains a constant liquid level, and a **Level Control Valve (LCV)** discharges liquid. *Function: liquid hold-up, gas flash-out, and stable liquid delivery.* 5. **Outlet Zone** — Clean dry gas exits the top; stabilized liquid exits the bottom through the LCV. > [!NOTE] > **Sketch to draw on the answer sheet:** A horizontal cylinder, inlet nozzle on the left → diverter plate → empty settling chamber → mist pad near the right end → gas outlet at the top right; liquid sump at the bottom with weir and LCV at the right bottom. Label all zones, indicate gas flow arrows (left to right, then up) and liquid flow arrows (downward into sump). **(b)** A horizontal separator is to be sized for a gas flow of **8 MMSCFD** at **800 psia** and **120 °F**. The design droplet diameter is **140 µm**, gas density = **0.06 lbm/ft³**, liquid (oil) density = **50 lbm/ft³**, and the **Souders–Brown constant K = 0.35**. Using the Souders–Brown correlation, calculate the **minimum vessel diameter**. Apply a 2.5× safety factor and suggest a standard pipe size. **\[7 Marks\]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Standard volumetric flow rate.** $Q_{std} = 8 \times 10^6 / 86400 = 92.59 \, \text{scf/s}$ **Step 2 — Convert to actual conditions using real gas law.** $P_{std} = 14.7$ psia, $T_{std} = 520$ °R, $T = 580$ °R, $Z = 0.88$ (assumed): $$ Q_{act} = 92.59 \times \dfrac{14.7}{800} \times \dfrac{580}{520} \times 0.88 $$ $$ Q_{act} = 92.59 \times 0.018375 \times 1.1154 \times 0.88 $$ $$ Q_{act} = 92.59 \times 0.01804 = 1.670 \, \text{acf/s} $$ **Step 3 — Souders–Brown gas velocity.** $$ v_s = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} = 0.35 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{50 - 0.06}{0.06}} $$ $$ v_s = 0.35 \cdot \sqrt{832.3} = 0.35 \cdot 28.85 = 10.10 \, \text{ft/s} $$ **Step 4 — Vessel cross-sectional area.** $$ A = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{v_s} = \dfrac{1.670}{10.10} = 0.1653 \, \text{ft}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Minimum theoretical diameter.** $$ D_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 0.1653}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.2105} = 0.459 \, \text{ft} \approx 5.5 \, \text{in.} $$ **Step 6 — Apply 2.5× safety factor and round up to standard pipe size.** $D_{design} = 2.5 \times 5.5 = 13.75 \, \text{in.} \Rightarrow$ choose a **16-inch (16 in. ID) standard pipe** (closest commercial size). **Final Answer:** Use a **16-inch horizontal separator**. --- #### **Question B — Module 2, 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of three different types of mist eliminators** (wire-mesh, vane, and centrifugal) used in gas–liquid separators. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Answer:** All mist eliminators rely on the principle that **when a gas stream carrying droplets is forced to change direction, the inertia of the droplets causes them to deviate from the gas streamlines and impact a surface**, where they coalesce and fall back as liquid. 1. **Wire-Mesh (Knitted) Mist Eliminator:** - Constructed from multiple layers of fine stainless-steel wire knitted into a thick pad. - Gas flows through the tortuous passages; droplets impact the wire surfaces and coalesce into larger droplets that drain downward. - *Removal efficiency:* Excellent — removes droplets down to **5 µm**. - *Pressure drop:* Low (~ 1–3 in. H₂O). - *Drawback:* Prone to **fouling** by solids, scale, or hydrate; cannot tolerate slug flow. 2. **Vane-Type Mist Eliminator:** - A series of corrugated or zig-zag plates installed vertically across the gas flow path. - Gas is forced to change direction sharply around the vanes; droplets, having higher inertia, impact the vane surfaces and are collected. - *Removal efficiency:* Moderate — effective for droplets **≥ 10 µm**. - *Pressure drop:* Moderate (~ 2–4 in. H₂O). - *Advantage:* Handles **higher gas capacity**, more robust for dirty service. 3. **Centrifugal (Cyclonic) Mist Eliminator:** - Gas enters a cylindrical body tangentially, creating a **swirling vortex**. - Centrifugal force throws droplets outward against the vessel wall, where they coalesce and drain. - *Removal efficiency:* High for droplets **≥ 8 µm**. - *Pressure drop:* Higher (~ 3–8 in. H₂O). - *Advantage:* Compact, no moving parts, suitable for **high-pressure sour service**. **Selection criterion:** Choice depends on gas cleanliness, droplet size, allowable pressure drop, and capital cost. **(b)** A vertical separator is to handle **5 MMSCFD** of gas and **500 bbl/day** of liquid at **500 psia** and **100 °F**. Liquid density = **55 lbm/ft³**, gas density = **0.04 lbm/ft³**, design droplet = **100 µm**, gas viscosity = **0.013 cP**. Compute the **terminal settling velocity** using **Stokes' Law** and check if the droplet qualifies for the laminar regime (Re < 0.2). **\[7 Marks\]** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert droplet diameter to feet.** $d = 100 \, \mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 3.281 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{ft}$ **Step 2 — Convert gas viscosity to lbm/(ft·s).** $\mu_G = 0.013 \, \text{cP} = 0.013 \times 6.72 \times 10^{-4} = 8.736 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{lbm/(ft·s)}$ **Step 3 — Apply Stokes' Law.** $$ v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G} $$ Using $g = 32.2 \, \text{ft/s}^2$: $$ v_t = \dfrac{32.2 \times (3.281 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (55 - 0.04)}{18 \times 8.736 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ v_t = \dfrac{32.2 \times 1.077 \times 10^{-7} \times 54.96}{1.572 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ v_t = \dfrac{1.905 \times 10^{-4}}{1.572 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.21 \, \text{ft/s} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Reynolds number to verify Stokes' validity.** $$ Re = \dfrac{\rho_G \, v_t \, d}{\mu_G} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 1.21 \times 3.281 \times 10^{-4}}{8.736 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ Re = \dfrac{1.588 \times 10^{-5}}{8.736 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.82 $$ **Step 5 — Validate.** $Re = 1.82 > 0.2$, so the droplet is **not in the Stokes (laminar) regime**. **Correction (Newton Regime):** Use the Newton equation: $$ v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\dfrac{g \, d (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{\rho_G}} = 1.74 \sqrt{\dfrac{32.2 \times 3.281 \times 10^{-4} \times 54.96}{0.04}} $$ $$ v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{14.53} = 1.74 \times 3.81 = 6.63 \, \text{ft/s} $$ **Final Answer:** Stokes' Law gives **$v_t = 1.21$ ft/s** but is **invalid (Re = 1.82)**. The corrected terminal velocity using the Newton regime is **$v_t \approx 6.63$ ft/s**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to check the Reynolds number after applying Stokes' Law** — this is the single most common mistake. Always compute Re and verify Re < 0.2; otherwise switch to the Newton regime. > 2. **Using $g = 9.81$ in FPS calculations** — if your density and viscosity are in imperial units, use $g = 32.2 \, \text{ft/s}^2$. Mixing units costs 2 marks instantly. > 3. **Skipping the safety factor in diameter calculation** — KTU almost always expects a 1.5×–2.5× engineering margin and a **standard pipe size** rounding. > 4. **Drawing the separator without labelled arrows** — a sketch without gas/liquid flow arrows and zone labels will lose up to 3 marks in the diagram question. > 5. **Mixing up $K$ values for horizontal vs. vertical separators** — the question stem must be read carefully; the examiner will not grant partial marks for the wrong geometry. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - **Inlet Diverter** = bulk separation by **momentum / centrifugal change** of direction. - **Settling Section** = gravity drop-out governed by **Stokes' Law** ($v_t \propto d^2$). - **Mist Eliminator** = captures **< 10 µm** droplets; three types: **Mesh, Vane, Cyclonic**. - **Liquid Sump** retention time = **3 to 5 minutes** (10 minutes for slug service). - **Level Control** is achieved with a **weir + LCV** (or float in older designs). - **Master formula:** $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 C_D \rho_G}}$ is universally valid; Stokes' and Newton forms are special cases. - **Sizing standard:** Souders–Brown with **K = 0.35 (horizontal)**, **K = 0.10–0.16 (vertical with mesh pad)**. - **Always apply 1.5×–2.5× safety factor** and round up to a **commercial pipe size (16 in., 20 in., 24 in., 30 in., 36 in., etc.)**. - **Standard L/D ratio for horizontal separators:** 2:1 to 4:1. - **Design droplet for KTU Module 2 board problems:** typically **100 µm, 140 µm, or 200 µm**. - **Re-check regime after computing $v_t$** — Stokes' Law requires $Re < 0.2$; otherwise use Newton regime. - **Mist eliminator selection rule of thumb:** Clean gas → mesh; Dirty/oily gas → vane; High-pressure sour → cyclonic. - **Two-phase separator =** gas + liquid only; **Three-phase separator adds a water boot** and uses specific gravity differences for oil–water separation.

topics.title: Types of Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Separators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of upstream and midstream oil and gas processing, an **oil and gas separator** is a pressure vessel employed for the purpose of separating the wellhead multiphase fluid stream into its constituent phases — typically natural gas, crude oil (hydrocarbon liquids), and free water (produced water) — by exploiting the thermodynamic immiscibility and density differential between the phases under controlled pressure and temperature conditions.

The classification of separators can be made on the basis of:

1. **Geometric Configuration (Shape & Orientation)** — Horizontal, Vertical, Spherical, and Multiphase Cyclonic.
2. **Number of Phases Handled** — Two-Phase Separators and Three-Phase Separators.
3. **Functional Role in the Process Train** — Primary (Production) Separator, Secondary (Intermediate) Separator, Test Separator, Scrubber, Knock-out Drum, and Filter / Coalescer Separator.
4. **Operating Pressure Regime** — High-Pressure (HP) Separator and Low-Pressure (LP) Separator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Students must be able to draw and label a generic oil-gas separator, classify separator types based on geometry, phases, and function, and justify the selection of a given separator type for a given upstream process duty.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a **salad dressing bottle** containing oil, water, and vinegar that you have shaken vigorously. If you let it stand on the kitchen counter for a few minutes, the heavier water and vinegar layer settles at the bottom, the lighter oil layer floats on top, and any tiny air bubbles (representing dissolved gas) escape to the top opening.

A **production separator does exactly the same thing** — but on an industrial scale, inside a thick steel pressure vessel, operating at high pressure and designed for thousands of barrels of fluid per day.

The only differences are:
- The **bottle stands upright and is open** (atmospheric) — the **separator is closed and pressurized** so that the natural gas remains in a usable liquid or dense gaseous state.
- The **settling in a bottle takes minutes by gravity alone** — the **separator uses gravity + baffles + mist eliminators + residence time** to accelerate separation.
- The **bottle separates two phases** — many **production separators separate three phases simultaneously** (gas at top, oil in the middle, water at the bottom).

Think of the separator as the **"referee" of the wellstream**: it sorts the chaotic multi-phase mixture from the wellhead into clean, well-defined streams so that each downstream unit (gas compressor, oil dehydration unit, water treatment plant) can process only what it is designed for.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Separator Design:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**
> - Standard temperature: **$T_{std} = 288.71 \text{ K (15.56 °C)}$**
> - Air molar mass: **$M_{air} = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$**
> - Universal gas constant: **$R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$**
> - Universal gas constant in field units: **$R = 10.732 \text{ psia·ft}^3/(\text{lbmol·°R)}$**

---

## 1.3 The Fundamental Physical Principle

All mechanical separators (as opposed to membrane or chemical separators) operate on the principle of **gravitational settling** governed by **Stokes' Law / Newton's Law regime**, which states that the **terminal settling velocity** of a dispersed liquid droplet (or solid particle) in a continuous gas phase is a function of:

- The **density difference** $(\rho_L - \rho_G)$ between the liquid droplet and the gas medium,
- The **gravitational acceleration** $g$, and
- The **droplet diameter squared** $d_p^2$.

This is why a separator must provide:
1. Sufficient **residence time** (fluid retention time within the vessel),
2. Adequate **cross-sectional area** for separation to occur, and
3. A **quiescent flow region** to avoid re-entrainment of separated droplets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Zones Inside a Generic Separator

A production separator is conceptually divided into **four functional zones**, regardless of its external shape:

| Zone | Function | Physical Phenomenon Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 1 — Primary Separation (Inlet) Section** | Sudden deceleration and bulk phase disengagement using an inlet diverter. | **Momentum Redistribution** — sudden drop in velocity causes bulk liquid to fall out of gas. |
| **Zone 2 — Secondary Separation (Gravity) Section** | Coalescence of smaller liquid droplets suspended in the gas phase. | **Gravitational Settling** governed by Stokes / transition regime. |
| **Zone 3 — Mist Extraction (Coalescing) Section** | Capture of entrained fine droplets (< 10 μm) that bypass gravity section. | **Inertial Impaction + Coalescence** using vane packs, wire mesh, or filter cartridges. |
| **Zone 4 — Liquid Collection (Settling) Section** | Liquid-liquid separation (oil from water) and surge volume. | **Density Difference + Residence Time** for oil-water emulsion break. |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Separators — Comprehensive Logic Breakdown

### A. By Geometric Configuration

**1. Horizontal Separator**
- Cylindrical vessel with axis horizontal, closed by two hemispherical or elliptical heads.
- **Advantages:** Large liquid surface area → good liquid-liquid separation; handles high GOR and foaming crudes; smaller footprint per unit volume; more residence time.
- **Disadvantages:** Larger weld seam area → higher cost in very large sizes; harder to remove solids; uneven liquid level distribution along length.
- **Typical Use:** Onshore production trains, offshore platforms, high-GOR wells, three-phase service with water removal.

**2. Vertical Separator**
- Cylindrical vessel with axis vertical, closed by two hemispherical heads.
- **Advantages:** Handles solids/sand better (solids fall to bottom); smaller plan area footprint; easier level control; handles slugs better.
- **Disadvantages:** Less efficient gas-liquid separation at very high GOR; larger and costlier for same capacity; larger total vessel height.
- **Typical Use:** Wells with sand/scale production, low GOR wells, test separators.

**3. Spherical Separator**
- Spherical pressure vessel.
- **Advantages:** Cheapest per unit volume for a given pressure rating; less surface area; lower foundation loads.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited liquid capacity; difficult internals installation; phase control (level) is critical.
- **Typical Use:** High-pressure well testing, gas field high-pressure early-stage separation.

**4. Multiphase Cyclonic Separator (Inline / Compact)**
- Uses swirl-inducing geometry (tangential inlet, hydrocyclone) to generate centripetal acceleration that replaces gravity.
- **Advantages:** No moving parts; very compact; subsea-friendly.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited turn-down ratio; sensitive to flow variations.
- **Typical Use:** Subsea manifolds, FPSO topsides where space and weight are at a premium.

### B. By Number of Phases

- **Two-Phase Separator:** Produces a gas stream and a combined liquid stream (oil + water together). Liquid stream is then routed to a free-water knockout / heater-treater downstream.
- **Three-Phase Separator:** Produces three distinct outlet streams — gas at top, oil in middle, water at bottom — using an interface-level controller and a weir / baffle.

### C. By Functional Role

- **Primary (Production) Separator:** First-stage separation immediately downstream of the wellhead choke; typically operates at high pressure.
- **Secondary (Intermediate / Low-Pressure) Separator:** Receives liquid from the primary separator after a pressure let-down stage; flashes dissolved gas.
- **Test Separator:** A smaller, parallel separator used to test individual wells' productivity and to take stabilized PVT samples.
- **Scrubber:** A small vertical vessel that removes only liquid droplets from gas (gas-liquid two-phase only, minimal liquid retention).
- **Knock-out Drum:** Similar to a scrubber; placed downstream of equipment to capture entrained liquid slugs.
- **Filter / Coalescer Separator:** Uses replaceable cartridges for very fine droplet removal (down to 1 μm).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Critical)

The following table is the **mandatory formula set** for any numerical problem on separator design. All quantities must be in **consistent SI units** unless otherwise stated.

| # | Formula | Equation | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (settling droplet) | $\displaystyle Re = \frac{\rho_G \, u_t \, d_p}{\mu_G}$ | dimensionless | Determines Stokes / Transition / Newton regime. |
| 2 | Stokes' Law Terminal Velocity (laminar, $Re \le 0.2$) | $\displaystyle u_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}$ | m/s | Settling velocity of fine droplets. |
| 3 | Intermediate (Transition) Regime — empirical correlation | $\displaystyle u_t = \frac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)^{0.71}}{\rho_G^{0.29} \, \mu_G^{0.43}}$ | m/s | Settling velocity in transition region ($0.2 < Re < 500$). |
| 4 | Newton's Law Terminal Velocity (turbulent, $Re > 500$) | $\displaystyle u_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{g \, d_p \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{\rho_G}}$ | m/s | Settling velocity of large droplets. |
| 5 | Required Vessel Cross-Sectional Area for Gas Capacity | $\displaystyle A = \frac{Q_G}{u_t}$ | m² | Minimum cross-section so droplets can fall. |
| 6 | Vessel Diameter (for vertical separator) | $\displaystyle D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}}$ | m | Diameter of cylindrical shell. |
| 7 | Vessel Length (for horizontal separator) | $\displaystyle L = \frac{Q_G}{u_t \, D}$ | m | Length for required retention. |
| 8 | Liquid Retention Time (Slenderness check) | $\displaystyle t_{ret} = \frac{V_{liq}}{Q_L}$ | s or min | Must be ≥ 1 min (water) / 3 min (oil) for 3-phase. |
| 9 | Separator Surge Volume | $\displaystyle V_{liq} = Q_L \cdot t_{ret}$ | m³ | Liquid hold-up required in vessel. |
| 10 | Gas Capacity Equation (Souders-Brown based) | $\displaystyle u_t = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | m/s | Empirical sizing velocity, $K$ from experience. |
| 11 | Pressure-Volume-Temperature Real Gas | $\displaystyle PV = ZnRT$ | varies | Real gas density / flow calculations. |
| 12 | Gas Density (Ideal) | $\displaystyle \rho_G = \frac{PM_G}{ZRT}$ | kg/m³ | Required in settling equations. |
| 13 | Liquid Density (oil API conversion) | $\displaystyle \rho_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}API + 131.5} \times 1000$ | kg/m³ | Used in density difference. |
| 14 | Gas Specific Gravity | $\displaystyle \gamma_g = \frac{M_G}{M_{air}}$ | dimensionless | Used in $M_G = \gamma_g \cdot 28.97$ g/mol. |
| 15 | Slug Flow Critical Velocity (for surge) | $\displaystyle u_{slug} = C \sqrt{g D}$ | m/s | Used in slug catcher design. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the formula table above, the absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) and conditions (e.g., $Re \le 0.2$) use the LaTeX $\vert$ / $\le$ operators — never a vertical pipe `|` character — so that the markdown table structure is preserved.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection of separator type** directly impacts the **CAPEX and OPEX** of a production facility. A horizontal separator typically has **30–50 % lower installation cost** than a vertical separator of the same gas-handling capacity on a skidded onshore facility.
- **Three-phase separation efficiency** is critical for **crude oil export specifications** (BS&W — Base Sediment & Water must be ≤ 1 % for export).
- **Sizing error** of even 10 % in cross-sectional area can lead to **liquid carry-over into the gas line**, causing hydrate formation, compressor damage, and unplanned shutdowns.
- **Subsea separation** (using multiphase cyclonic separators) reduces the **flowline back-pressure**, increasing well productivity by 10–25 % in deepwater fields.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

A liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ falling under gravity through a quiescent gas phase experiences a **force balance** between gravity (downward), buoyancy (upward), and drag (upward, opposing motion).

### Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the droplet.

- **Gravitational Force (Weight):**
$$F_g = m_p \, g = \rho_L \, V_p \, g = \rho_L \, \left( \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \right) g$$

- **Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle):**
$$F_b = \rho_G \, V_p \, g = \rho_G \, \left( \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \right) g$$

- **Drag Force (Newtonian drag on a sphere):**
$$F_D = C_D \, \left( \frac{1}{2} \rho_G u_t^2 \right) A_p = C_D \, \left( \frac{1}{2} \rho_G u_t^2 \right) \left( \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \right)$$

where $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient** (dimensionless), $u_t$ is the **terminal velocity** (m/s), and $A_p$ is the droplet projected area (m²).

### Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition at terminal velocity.

At terminal velocity, the net force on the droplet is zero, meaning the droplet has reached constant velocity (no further acceleration). The downward force equals the sum of the upward forces:

$$F_g = F_b + F_D$$

Substituting:

$$\rho_L \left( \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \right) g = \rho_G \left( \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \right) g + C_D \left( \frac{1}{2} \rho_G u_t^2 \right) \left( \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Simplify algebraically.

Cancel $\pi$ throughout. Multiply through by $\dfrac{6}{d_p^3}$:

$$\rho_L \, g = \rho_G \, g + \frac{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G \, u_t^2}{4 \, d_p}$$

Rearrange to isolate $u_t^2$:

$$\rho_L \, g - \rho_G \, g = \frac{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G \, u_t^2}{4 \, d_p}$$

$$g (\rho_L - \rho_G) = \frac{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G \, u_t^2}{4 \, d_p}$$

$$u_t^2 = \frac{4 \, d_p \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G}$$

$$\boxed{u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_G}}}$$

This is the **general settling velocity equation**, valid for any drag regime.

### Step 4 — Apply the Stokes' regime drag coefficient.

For **laminar flow** around a small sphere ($Re \le 0.2$), the drag coefficient is:

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24 \, \mu_G}{\rho_G \, u_t \, d_p}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $C_D$ and simplify.

$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{3 \left( \dfrac{24 \, \mu_G}{\rho_G \, u_t \, d_p}\right) \rho_G}}$$

$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) \, \rho_G \, u_t}{72 \, \mu_G \, \rho_G}}$$

$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) \, u_t}{72 \, \mu_G}}$$

$$u_t^2 = \frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G) \, u_t}{72 \, \mu_G}$$

Divide both sides by $u_t$ (since $u_t \neq 0$):

$$u_t = \frac{4 \, d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{72 \, \mu_G} = \frac{d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}$$

$$\boxed{u_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}}$$

This is the **Stokes' Law terminal velocity** — Equation (2) in the formula sheet.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sizing a Vertical Separator)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A vertical two-phase oil-gas separator must be sized for the following duty at standard conditions:
> - Gas flow rate: $Q_G = 0.50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (at separator operating conditions)
> - Oil density: $\rho_L = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Gas density: $\rho_G = 8.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Gas viscosity: $\mu_G = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
> - Liquid droplet diameter to be removed: $d_p = 150 \text{ μm} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Find:**
1. The terminal settling velocity $u_t$ of the droplet (use Stokes' Law).
2. The minimum cross-sectional area $A$ of the separator.
3. The required vessel diameter $D$.

### Solution — Step 1: Verify the regime (Reynolds Number).

$$Re = \frac{\rho_G \, u_t \, d_p}{\mu_G}$$

Since we do not yet know $u_t$, we first compute using Stokes' Law, then verify.

### Solution — Step 2: Calculate terminal velocity using Stokes' Law.

$$u_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}$$

Substitute the values:

$$u_t = \frac{(9.81) \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (850 - 8.0)}{18 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-5})}$$

Compute numerator piece by piece:
- $d_p^2 = (150 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 22500 \times 10^{-12} = 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$
- $g \cdot d_p^2 = 9.81 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} = 2.207 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$
- $\rho_L - \rho_G = 850 - 8.0 = 842 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Numerator $= 2.207 \times 10^{-7} \times 842 = 1.858 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s}^2\text{)}$

Denominator:
- $18 \times \mu_G = 18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$

Therefore:
$$u_t = \frac{1.858 \times 10^{-4}}{2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.860 \text{ m/s}$$

### Solution — Step 3: Verify Reynolds Number.

$$Re = \frac{(8.0) \times (0.860) \times (150 \times 10^{-6})}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{8.0 \times 0.860 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.032 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 86.0$$

Since $Re = 86 > 0.2$, the **Stokes' regime assumption is invalid**. The droplet actually settles in the **transition regime**.

### Solution — Step 4: Recompute using the transition regime correlation.

$$u_t = \frac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)^{0.71}}{\rho_G^{0.29} \, \mu_G^{0.43}}$$

Compute each term:
- $g^{0.71} = (9.81)^{0.71}$. Using logarithms: $0.71 \log(9.81) = 0.71 \times 0.9917 = 0.7041$, so $g^{0.71} = 10^{0.7041} = 5.062 \text{ m}^{0.71}/\text{s}^{1.42}$
- $d_p^{1.14} = (1.5 \times 10^{-4})^{1.14} = 10^{-3.7038} = 1.978 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{1.14}$
- $(\rho_L - \rho_G)^{0.71} = (842)^{0.71}$. $0.71 \log(842) = 0.71 \times 2.9253 = 2.077$, so $(842)^{0.71} = 10^{2.077} = 119.5$
- $\rho_G^{0.29} = (8.0)^{0.29}$. $0.29 \log(8.0) = 0.29 \times 0.9031 = 0.2619$, so $\rho_G^{0.29} = 10^{0.2619} = 1.829$
- $\mu_G^{0.43} = (1.2 \times 10^{-5})^{0.43}$. $0.43 \log(1.2 \times 10^{-5}) = 0.43 \times (-4.9208) = -2.1159$, so $\mu_G^{0.43} = 10^{-2.1159} = 7.67 \times 10^{-3}$

Substitute:
$$u_t = \frac{0.153 \times 5.062 \times (1.978 \times 10^{-4}) \times 119.5}{1.829 \times 7.67 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator:
- $0.153 \times 5.062 = 0.7745$
- $0.7745 \times 1.978 \times 10^{-4} = 1.532 \times 10^{-4}$
- $1.532 \times 10^{-4} \times 119.5 = 1.831 \times 10^{-2}$

Denominator:
- $1.829 \times 7.67 \times 10^{-3} = 1.403 \times 10^{-2}$

$$u_t = \frac{1.831 \times 10^{-2}}{1.403 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.305 \text{ m/s}$$

### Solution — Step 5: Cross-check Reynolds Number with new $u_t$.

$$Re = \frac{8.0 \times 1.305 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.566 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 130.5$$

The Reynolds Number $Re = 130.5$ lies in the transition regime ($0.2 < Re < 500$), so the calculation is self-consistent.

### Solution — Step 6: Calculate minimum cross-sectional area.

$$A = \frac{Q_G}{u_t} = \frac{0.50}{1.305} = 0.383 \text{ m}^2$$

### Solution — Step 7: Calculate vessel diameter.

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.383}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.532}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.4877} = 0.698 \text{ m} \approx 0.70 \text{ m}$$

In practice, the next standard pipe/vessel size above 0.70 m is chosen, say **$D = 0.76 \text{ m (30 inches)}$**, and a design margin of 20 % is added.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Separator Selection

| Design Criterion | Vertical Separator | Horizontal Separator | Spherical Separator | Cyclonic Separator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slug / Sand Handling** | **Excellent** (falls to bottom) | Moderate (collects at one end) | Poor (limited liquid space) | Poor |
| **Foaming Crude Handling** | Moderate | **Excellent** (large surface) | Poor | Poor |
| **Three-Phase (Oil/Water/Gas)** | Moderate | **Excellent** | Poor | N/A |
| **Footprint Area** | Small (vertical) | Large (horizontal) | Small | **Very Small** |
| **Height** | **Tall** | Short | Short | Short |
| **Cost per Unit Capacity** | Moderate (for low GOR) | **Low** (for high GOR) | **Lowest** (for HP gas) | High (specialized) |
| **Liquid Capacity (Surge)** | Small | **Large** | **Smallest** | Negligible |
| **Sand / Scale Cleanout** | **Easiest** | Difficult (requires entry) | Difficult | N/A |
| **Typical Operating Pressure** | Up to 1000 psi | Up to 1500 psi | Up to 3000+ psi | Process-line |
| **Best Fit for** | Low-to-medium GOR wells, sand-laden | High GOR, foaming, 3-phase | HP gas wells, test trains | Subsea, space-constrained |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow Architecture of Oil & Gas Separators

The following block-level Mermaid diagram maps the complete taxonomy of separator types studied in Module 2, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of any reserved keywords; all node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any inline markdown formatting that would corrupt the diagram.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SEPARATOR CLASSIFICATION TAXONOMY] --> B[BY GEOMETRY]
    A --> C[BY PHASE COUNT]
    A --> D[BY FUNCTION]
    A --> E[BY PRESSURE REGIME]

    B --> B1[VERTICAL SEPARATOR]
    B --> B2[HORIZONTAL SEPARATOR]
    B --> B3[SPHERICAL SEPARATOR]
    B --> B4[CYCLONIC SEPARATOR]

    C --> C1[TWO PHASE SEPARATOR]
    C --> C2[THREE PHASE SEPARATOR]

    D --> D1[PRIMARY PRODUCTION SEP]
    D --> D2[SECONDARY INTERMEDIATE SEP]
    D --> D3[TEST SEPARATOR]
    D --> D4[SCRUBBER]
    D --> D5[KNOCKOUT DRUM]
    D --> D6[FILTER COALESCER SEP]

    E --> E1[HIGH PRESSURE SEP]
    E --> E2[LOW PRESSURE SEP]

    B1 --> F1[BEST FOR SAND SLUGS LOW GOR]
    B2 --> F2[BEST FOR HIGH GOR FOAMING 3PHASE]
    B3 --> F3[BEST FOR HP GAS TESTING]
    B4 --> F4[BEST FOR SUBSEA COMPACT]

    C1 --> G1[GAS OUTLET AND COMBINED LIQUID OUTLET]
    C2 --> G2[GAS OUTLET OIL OUTLET WATER OUTLET]
```

---

## 4.2 Internal Functional Zones of a Generic Horizontal Three-Phase Separator

The following block-level architecture flow diagram maps the four internal functional zones of a generic horizontal three-phase oil-gas-water separator, together with the corresponding inlet/outlet streams. This is the **canonical labeled cross-section** a KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce in a 14-mark drawing question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[INLET STREAM: OIL GAS WATER MULTIPHASE] --> Z1
    Z1[ZONE 1 INLET DIVERTER PRIMARY SEP] --> Z2
    Z2[ZONE 2 GRAVITY SETTLING COALESCENCE] --> Z3
    Z3[ZONE 3 MIST EXTRACTOR WIRE MESH OR VANE PACK] --> Z4
    Z4[ZONE 4 LIQUID COLLECTION OIL WATER LEVEL CONTROL] --> OUT

    Z1 --> GOUT[GAS OUTLET TOP]
    Z4 --> OOUT[OIL OUTLET MIDDLE WEIR]
    Z4 --> WOUT[WATER OUTLET BOTTOM]
    OUT[STABILIZED EMULSION TO TREATER]
```

---

## 4.3 Process Train Placement of Separators in an Oil & Gas Production Facility

The following block-level schematic illustrates how multiple separator types are placed in series in a typical upstream process train, from the wellhead choke to the export point. This is critical for the KTU Module 2 question on "process flow and separator selection."

```mermaid
graph LR
    WH[WELLHEAD CHOKE] --> P1
    P1[PRIMARY HIGH PRESSURE SEPARATOR] --> P2
    P2[SECONDARY LOW PRESSURE SEPARATOR] --> P3
    P3[TEST SEPARATOR PARALLEL] --> P4
    P4[HEAT TREATER STABILIZER] --> P5
    P5[CRUDE OIL EXPORT TANK]

    P1 --> G1[GAS TO COMPRESSOR DEHYDRATION]
    P2 --> G2[GAS TO LOW PRESSURE GAS PLANT]
    P3 --> TS[TEST DATA PVT SAMPLES]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Mermaid diagrams above:**
> - The **first diagram (taxonomy)** is the visual answer a student should produce when the KTU question asks: *"Classify oil and gas separators with a neat sketch."*
> - The **second diagram (functional zones)** is the answer when the question asks: *"Draw a labeled sketch of a horizontal three-phase separator and explain the function of each section."*
> - The **third diagram (process train)** is the answer when the question asks: *"With a process flow diagram, explain the role of separators in a production facility."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a two-phase and a three-phase separator with one example application each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **two-phase separator** separates the wellstream into **two** outlet streams — a gas phase and a combined liquid phase (oil + water together). It is used, for example, as a **gas scrubber downstream of a compressor suction** where water knockout is handled separately.

A **three-phase separator** separates the wellstream into **three** distinct outlet streams — gas at the top, oil in the middle, and free water at the bottom — using a level controller, an interface-level probe, and a weir / baffle. It is used, for example, as a **primary production separator on an offshore platform** where BS&W export specification must be met.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of a horizontal separator over a vertical separator.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher gas-handling capacity** per unit vessel volume due to larger interfacial area for liquid dropout.
2. **Better suited for foaming crude oils** because of the larger liquid surface area exposed for gas disengagement.
3. **Superior three-phase separation efficiency** — the long axis provides residence time for oil-water separation by gravity.
*(Acceptable alternatives: lower height → easier installation, lower cost per unit capacity at high GOR.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note to the student:** As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question of 14 marks in Module 2 offers an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With a neat labeled sketch, explain the **functional zones of a horizontal three-phase oil-gas separator**. State the principle on which each zone operates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A horizontal three-phase oil-gas separator is internally divided into four functional zones, traversed by the wellstream from inlet to outlet:

**Zone 1 — Inlet Diverter (Primary Separation Section):**
- **Location:** At the inlet nozzle, immediately inside the vessel.
- **Component:** Inlet diverter (splash plate, half-pipe, or cyclone inlet).
- **Operating Principle:** **Sudden momentum change (bulk phase disengagement).** As the high-velocity multiphase stream impinges on the diverter, its kinetic energy is dissipated, and the bulk liquid phase (oil + water) falls by gravity to the liquid collection section, while the gas phase reverses direction and flows upward/along the vessel.
- **Function:** Removes 70–80 % of the bulk free liquid in the first 1–2 vessel diameters.

**Zone 2 — Gravity Settling (Coalescence) Section:**
- **Location:** The bulk of the upper gas space between the inlet and the mist extractor.
- **Component:** Often equipped with a calming baffle / vane coalescer to reduce turbulence.
- **Operating Principle:** **Gravitational settling per Stokes' Law / transition regime.** Smaller liquid droplets entrained in the gas phase fall under gravity at their terminal settling velocity $u_t$ given by Stokes' Law.
- **Function:** Removes 15–20 % of remaining entrained droplets (typically 100–500 μm diameter).

**Zone 3 — Mist Extractor (Coalescing Section):**
- **Location:** Near the gas outlet nozzle at the top of the vessel.
- **Component:** Wire-mesh pad, vane pack, or filter cartridge.
- **Operating Principle:** **Inertial impaction and coalescence.** Gas carrying fine droplets (< 10 μm) is forced to change direction; droplets due to inertia impinge on the mesh/vanes, coalesce into large drops, and fall back into the liquid section.
- **Function:** Achieves gas outlet liquid content < 0.005 gal/MMscf (gas quality spec).

**Zone 4 — Liquid Collection (Oil-Water Separation) Section:**
- **Location:** Lower portion of the vessel.
- **Component:** Weir, baffle, interface level controller.
- **Operating Principle:** **Density difference + residence time.** Water (denser, $\rho_W \approx 1020 \text{ kg/m}^3$) settles to the bottom; oil (lighter, $\rho_o \approx 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$) floats on top of the water.
- **Function:** Provides three distinct outlet streams — gas (top), oil (middle via weir), water (bottom).

> **Diagram to be drawn by the student:** Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram (rendered as a labeled cross-sectional sketch in the answer booklet).

**(b)** A vertical two-phase separator handles $0.30 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ of gas at operating conditions. The oil density is $870 \text{ kg/m}^3$, gas density is $6.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$, gas viscosity is $1.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$, and the droplet to be removed has diameter $200 \text{ μm}$. Calculate the **terminal settling velocity** (use Stokes' Law), the **minimum vessel cross-sectional area**, and the **minimum vessel diameter**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law.**

$$u_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \, \mu_G}$$

$$u_t = \frac{(9.81) \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (870 - 6.5)}{18 \times (1.4 \times 10^{-5})}$$

- $d_p^2 = (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$
- $g \cdot d_p^2 = 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 3.924 \times 10^{-7}$
- $\rho_L - \rho_G = 863.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Numerator $= 3.924 \times 10^{-7} \times 863.5 = 3.388 \times 10^{-4}$
- Denominator $= 18 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-5} = 2.52 \times 10^{-4}$

$$u_t = \frac{3.388 \times 10^{-4}}{2.52 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.345 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Reynolds Number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho_G \, u_t \, d_p}{\mu_G} = \frac{6.5 \times 1.345 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.749 \times 10^{-3}}{1.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 124.9$$

Since $Re > 0.2$, the **Stokes' assumption is borderline invalid**; the transition regime would yield a slightly higher $u_t$ (≈ 1.55 m/s), but for the purpose of the KTU exam question, the Stokes' result is accepted as the conservative lower bound.

**Step 3 — Calculate cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{Q_G}{u_t} = \frac{0.30}{1.345} = 0.223 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate vessel diameter.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.223}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.2839} = 0.533 \text{ m}$$

**Final Result (with 20 % design margin):**

$$D_{design} = 1.20 \times 0.533 = 0.640 \text{ m}$$

Rounded to the next standard size: **$D = 0.66 \text{ m (26 inches)}$**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Classify **oil and gas separators** based on **(i) geometry, (ii) number of phases handled, and (iii) functional role in the process train**. Give one application of each major type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Classification Basis | Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Geometry** | Horizontal | Three-phase separation on offshore platform |
| | Vertical | Sand-laden well on onshore battery |
| | Spherical | High-pressure gas well test separator |
| | Cyclonic | Subsea multiphase flowline |
| **(ii) Number of Phases** | Two-phase | Gas scrubber after compressor |
| | Three-phase | Production train oil dehydration |
| **(iii) Functional Role** | Primary Production Sep | First-stage separation after choke |
| | Secondary (LP) Sep | Flash gas recovery downstream of HP sep |
| | Test Separator | Individual well productivity testing |
| | Scrubber | Final gas polishing before export |
| | Knockout Drum | Liquid slug protection on gas line |
| | Filter/Coalescer | Compressor suction protection (1 μm spec) |

**(b)** A horizontal separator handles $2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ std m}^3/\text{day}$ of gas at $P = 50 \text{ bar}$ and $T = 50 \text{ °C}$, with $\gamma_g = 0.65$. The oil density is $880 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and the gas viscosity at operating conditions is $1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$. Using the Souders-Brown equation with $K = 0.107 \text{ m/s}$ (a typical value for scrubber service), determine the **required cross-sectional area and diameter** of the vessel. State any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate gas density at operating conditions.**

Molecular weight of gas:
$$M_G = \gamma_g \times M_{air} = 0.65 \times 28.97 = 18.83 \text{ g/mol} = 0.01883 \text{ kg/mol}$$

Operating conditions: $P = 50 \text{ bar} = 50 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $T = 50 + 273.15 = 323.15 \text{ K}$. Assume $Z \approx 0.95$ (typical for natural gas at 50 bar, 50 °C).

$$\rho_G = \frac{PM_G}{ZRT} = \frac{(50 \times 10^5) \times 0.01883}{0.95 \times 8.314 \times 323.15}$$

Compute denominator:
$$0.95 \times 8.314 \times 323.15 = 2552.5 \text{ J/mol}$$

Compute numerator:
$$50 \times 10^5 \times 0.01883 = 9.415 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa·kg/mol}$$

$$\rho_G = \frac{9.415 \times 10^4}{2552.5} = 36.88 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate actual gas volumetric flow rate at operating conditions.**

Standard volumetric flow rate: $Q_{std} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{86400} = 2.315 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (at std conditions of 1.01325 bar, 288.71 K).

Using real gas law for volumetric conversion:

$$Q_{op} = Q_{std} \times \frac{P_{std}}{P_{op}} \times \frac{T_{op}}{T_{std}} \times \frac{Z_{op}}{Z_{std}}$$

Assuming $Z_{std} = 1.0$:

$$Q_{op} = 2.315 \times \frac{1.01325 \times 10^5}{50 \times 10^5} \times \frac{323.15}{288.71} \times \frac{0.95}{1.0}$$

$$Q_{op} = 2.315 \times 0.02027 \times 1.1193 \times 0.95 = 0.04997 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Souders-Brown equation.**

$$u_t = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}} = 0.107 \times \sqrt{\frac{880 - 36.88}{36.88}}$$

$$u_t = 0.107 \times \sqrt{\frac{843.12}{36.88}} = 0.107 \times \sqrt{22.86} = 0.107 \times 4.781 = 0.5116 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{Q_{op}}{u_t} = \frac{0.04997}{0.5116} = 0.0977 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Vessel diameter.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.0977}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.1244} = 0.353 \text{ m}$$

**Assumptions made:**
- $Z_{op} = 0.95$ at 50 bar, 50 °C.
- $K = 0.107 \text{ m/s}$ is appropriate for scrubber/mist-eliminator service per GPSA.
- Negligible dissolved gas in oil at separator pressure.
- Steady-state, single-phase gas flow assumption (oil fraction handled by separate liquid section).

**Final Result (with 20 % design margin):** $D_{design} = 0.42 \text{ m} \approx 16 \text{ inches standard size}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on "Types of Separators" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using Stokes' Law without verifying the regime** — Always compute the Reynolds Number after finding $u_t$. If $Re > 0.2$, you MUST use the transition regime correlation. A 2-mark penalty is standard for this oversight.
> 2. **Confusing $M_{air}$ (28.97 g/mol) with $M_G$ directly** — Many students substitute 28.97 g/mol for the gas molecular weight. The correct value is $M_G = \gamma_g \times 28.97$ g/mol.
> 3. **Forgetting the design margin** — Board examiners expect 15–25 % margin on diameter for surge, slugs, and fouling. A raw calculated diameter without margin loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer** — Always state numerical answers with explicit SI units (m, m², m/s).
> 5. **Mislabeling the four functional zones** in the diagram — The order MUST be **Inlet → Gravity → Mist Extractor → Liquid Collection** (or equivalent labeling). A reversed order loses full marks for the sketch.
> 6. **In 3-mark questions, failing to give an application example** — KTU's 3-mark questions often carry a hidden 1 mark for "real-world example" or "industrial relevance." Always include one.
> 7. **For two-phase vs three-phase confusion** — A "two-phase" separator can still have gas and oil outlets (oil + water combined as the "liquid" outlet) — students often wrongly call this three-phase.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A separator is a pressure vessel that uses gravity (and/or centrifugal/inertial forces) to split a multiphase wellstream into gas, oil, and water streams.
- **Four functional zones inside any production separator:**
  1. **Inlet Diverter** — bulk momentum separation.
  2. **Gravity Section** — droplet settling per Stokes / transition regime.
  3. **Mist Extractor** — fine droplet capture by impaction/coalescence.
  4. **Liquid Collection** — oil-water level/interface control.
- **Classification axes:** Geometry (Vertical / Horizontal / Spherical / Cyclonic), Phase count (Two / Three), Function (Primary / Secondary / Test / Scrubber / KO Drum / Filter), Pressure (HP / LP).
- **Key selection rule of thumb:**
  - **Sand / Slug-prone wells → Vertical.**
  - **High GOR / Foaming / 3-phase → Horizontal.**
  - **HP gas testing / Small volume → Spherical.**
  - **Subsea / Weight-critical → Cyclonic.**
- **Stokes' Law** governs fine droplet settling: $u_t = \dfrac{g d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}$ — valid only when $Re \le 0.2$.
- **Transition regime** (the most common in practice): $u_t = \dfrac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} \, (\rho_L - \rho_G)^{0.71}}{\rho_G^{0.29} \, \mu_G^{0.43}}$ — valid for $0.2 < Re < 500$.
- **Cross-sectional area** of a vertical separator: $A = Q_G / u_t$.
- **Diameter:** $D = \sqrt{4A / \pi}$.
- **Length of horizontal separator:** $L = Q_G / (u_t \cdot D)$.
- **Liquid retention time** should be **≥ 1 min for water** and **≥ 3 min for oil** in a three-phase separator.
- **Gas density from real gas law:** $\rho_G = P M_G / (Z R T)$.
- **Oil density from API gravity:** $\rho_{oil} = 141.5 / (^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5) \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Souders-Brown $K$ factor** for separator sizing: scrubber $K \approx 0.107 \text{ m/s}$; production separator $K \approx 0.076 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Always add a 15–25 % design margin** on calculated diameter for surges, slugs, and fouling.
- **For 14-mark drawing questions**, label: inlet, inlet diverter, gravity section, mist extractor, gas outlet, oil outlet (with weir), water outlet, level/interface controllers, and pressure safety valve (PSV).
- **For process-flow questions**, always show separators in series: Wellhead → Primary HP Sep → Secondary LP Sep → Test Sep → Treater → Storage; and parallel gas streams to Gas Plant / Compressor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of oil and natural gas production engineering, **separation** refers to the process of partitioning a multi-phase wellstream (typically oil, gas, and water) into its constituent phases using mechanical equipment called **separators**. The efficiency of this separation is governed by a complex interplay of fluid properties, thermodynamic conditions, equipment design parameters, and operational variables.

**Factors Affecting Separation** are the physical, chemical, thermodynamic, and mechanical parameters that directly influence the degree of phase disengagement achieved in a separator vessel. These factors collectively determine the quality of separated phases, residence time requirements, vessel sizing, and ultimately, the downstream processing economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Separation efficiency in primary production equipment is a function of **gravity settling (Stokes' Law regime)**, **retention time**, **fluid properties**, **operating conditions**, and **mechanical design**, all of which must be optimized to meet export/inlet specifications for downstream units.

The primary engineering objective is to achieve **equilibrium separation** where:
- **Gas phase** meets pipeline dew-point specifications (typically $< 0.1$ gallon of liquid per $1000 \text{ scf}$ of gas)
- **Oil phase** meets BS&W (Basic Sediment and Water) specification (typically $< 1\%$ for export)
- **Water phase** meets oil-in-water specification for disposal/reinjection

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **salad dressing bottle** containing oil, vinegar, and herbs. When you **stop shaking** it, the liquids begin to settle naturally:
- The **heavier** vinegar sinks to the bottom
- The **lighter** oil floats to the top
- The **floating particles** (herbs) settle according to their size and density

This natural settling is **gravity separation** — the same principle used in industrial oil-gas separators.

However, several real-world factors complicate this simple analogy:
- **Temperature** changes (like warming the bottle) alter the densities
- **Smaller droplets** (like finely crushed herbs) settle much slower
- **Foam and emulsions** (like shaking too vigorously) resist separation
- **Flow rate** (pouring too fast) creates turbulence that re-mixes the fluids

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industry Analogy:** Think of a separator like a **multi-story parking garage** for fluids. Each "level" (or internal compartment) holds a different phase. The "traffic flow" (wellstream) enters at one level and exits at another based on density, just like cars park on different floors based on size/priority.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants and metrics are central to separation engineering:

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravitational acceleration** ($g$) | $\mathbf{9.80665}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| **Gas Constant** ($R$) | $\mathbf{8.314}$ | $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ |
| **API Gravity (typical crude)** | $\mathbf{25 - 40}$ | °API |
| **BS&W Specification (export)** | $\mathbf{< 1.0}$ | \% volume |
| **Salt Content (export)** | $\mathbf{< 50}$ | $\text{PTB}$ (pounds per thousand barrels) |
| **Gas Dew-Point** | $\mathbf{< 0.1}$ | $\text{gal/1000 scf}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **PTB** stands for **Pounds per Thousand Barrels** — a standard unit for salt content in crude oil export specifications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Separation

The factors influencing separator performance can be systematically classified into **four major categories**:

#### A. Fluid Properties (Intrinsic)
1. **Density Difference** ($\Delta \rho = \rho_L - \rho_G$)
   - The driving force for gravity separation
   - Greater density difference = faster settling
   - Example: Oil ($\rho \approx 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$) vs Gas ($\rho \approx 50 \text{ kg/m}^3$)

2. **Viscosity** ($\mu$)
   - Higher viscosity increases drag force on droplets
   - Affects residence time requirements
   - Strongly temperature-dependent

3. **Surface Tension** ($\sigma$)
   - Influences droplet coalescence behavior
   - Affects foam formation
   - Typically $\mathbf{20 - 40 \text{ mN/m}}$ for oil-water systems

4. **Foaming Tendency**
   - Crude oils contain surfactants (asphaltenes, resins)
   - Foam formation reduces effective separation volume
   - Foamy crudes require **3× larger** separator vessels

5. **Solid Content (Sand/Silt)**
   - Causes erosion of internals
   - Reduces effective volume
   - Requires sand jetting systems

#### B. Operating Conditions (Extrinsic)
1. **Pressure** ($P$)
   - Higher pressure increases gas density → reduces $\Delta \rho$
   - Affects gas-oil ratio (GOR)
   - Influences solubility of light ends in oil

2. **Temperature** ($T$)
   - Reduces viscosity → improves separation
   - Decreases density difference (negative effect)
   - Optimal temperature balances these effects

3. **Flow Rate / Throughput** ($Q$)
   - Higher flow rates reduce residence time
   - Increases turbulence → re-entrainment
   - Determines vessel diameter (gas capacity)

4. **Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR)**
   - High GOR requires multi-stage separation
   - Affects gas handling capacity

#### C. Droplet Characteristics
1. **Droplet Size Distribution** ($d_p$)
   - Governed by **Stokes' Law**
   - Larger droplets settle faster
   - Typical range: $\mathbf{100 - 500 \text{ μm}}$ in separators

2. **Droplet Concentration**
   - Affects coalescence efficiency
   - Influences Reynolds number of settling zone

#### D. Mechanical Design Parameters
1. **Residence Time** ($\tau$)
2. **Vessel Geometry** (L/D ratio, baffle design)
3. **Inlet Device Design** (cyclonic, vane, deflector)
4. **Vane/Baffle Configuration**
5. **Liquid Level Control**
6. **Weir Heights and Boot Design**

### 2.2 The Why and How Behind Each Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Principle:** Separation occurs when the **buoyancy force** acting on a dispersed droplet overcomes the **drag force** resisting its motion. Every factor above influences this force balance.

- **Why density difference matters:** Buoyancy force $\propto \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{droplet}$
- **How viscosity affects settling:** Higher $\mu$ means greater viscous drag, requiring more time
- **Why temperature helps:** Reduces $\mu$ exponentially (Arrhenius-type relationship)
- **How pressure complicates:** Compresses gas, making it denser, reducing $\Delta \rho$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The following formulas are **frequently tested** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Master the bold terms.

| # | Formula | Equation | Variables | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)** | $v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu}$ | $d_p$ = droplet diameter; $\rho_L, \rho_G$ = liquid/gas densities; $\mu$ = viscosity | **Fundamental equation** governing gravity separation |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (Droplet) | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_G \cdot v_t \cdot d_p}{\mu_G}$ | $\rho_G$ = gas density; $v_t$ = terminal velocity | Determines flow regime (laminar/transitional/turbulent) |
| 3 | **Residence Time** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{liquid}}{Q_{liquid}}$ | $V_L$ = liquid holding volume; $Q_L$ = liquid flow rate | Time available for phase disengagement |
| 4 | Gas Capacity (Souders-Brown) | $v_{max} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | $K$ = Souders-Brown coefficient (ft/s) | Maximum allowable gas velocity to prevent liquid re-entrainment |
| 5 | Separator Diameter (Gas Cap) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_g}{\pi \cdot v_{max}}}$ | $Q_g$ = gas volumetric flow rate | Vessel sizing for gas capacity |
| 6 | Hold-Up Time (Liquid) | $t_h = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot h_L}{4 \cdot Q_L}$ | $h_L$ = liquid level height | Total time liquid stays in vessel |
| 7 | Gas Density (Ideal Gas) | $\rho_G = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$ | $P$ = pressure; $M$ = molar mass; $Z$ = compressibility | Density used in Stokes' Law |
| 8 | API Gravity Conversion | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ | $\rho_{oil}$ in $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Oil density in field units |
| 9 | Viscosity-Temperature (Andrade) | $\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$ | $A, B$ = empirical constants | Predicts viscosity at operating T |
| 10 | Separation Efficiency (overall) | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $C$ = concentration of dispersed phase | Performance metric for separators |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Bar Pipe Rule:** In exam answers, write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX, **never** using single `|` which breaks markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These factors and formulas are not merely academic — they are the **backbone of**:

1. **Offshore Platform Design:** Multi-stage separation trains on FPSOs
2. **Gas Plant Operations:** Inlet separators for gas processing
3. **Crude Stabilization:** Heater-treaters for water removal
4. **Produced Water Treatment:** Enhanced gravity separators
5. **Flow Assurance:** Slug catchers and test separators
6. **Refinery Operations:** Distillation column feed drums

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Context:** A properly designed 3-phase separator can reduce downstream chemical treatment costs by **40-60%** and prevent costly equipment failures in pipelines, pumps, and compressors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stokes' Law for Terminal Settling Velocity

**Problem Setup:** Consider a spherical liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_L$ falling under gravity through a continuous gas phase of density $\rho_G$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu_G$.

#### Step 1: Identify Forces Acting on the Droplet

Three forces act on the falling droplet in the vertical direction:
- **Gravity (downward):** $F_g = m \cdot g = \rho_L \cdot V_d \cdot g = \rho_L \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$
- **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \rho_G \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$
- **Viscous Drag (upward, opposing motion):** $F_d = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G v^2}{2} \cdot A_p = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G v^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^3$

where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (dimensionless) and $A_p$ is the projected area.

#### Step 2: Apply Equilibrium Condition (Steady State)

At **terminal velocity**, the net force equals zero (acceleration = 0). The droplet no longer accelerates because gravitational pull balances drag and buoyancy.

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

#### Step 3: Write the Force Balance Equation

Substituting the expressions from Step 1:

$$\rho_L \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g = \rho_G \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g + C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G v^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^3$$

#### Step 4: Simplify the Equation

The factor $\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$ appears on both gravity and buoyancy terms, and $\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^3$ on the drag term. Cancel common terms strategically:

$$\rho_L \cdot g - \rho_G \cdot g = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G v^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^3}{\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3}$$

$$(\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G v^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{6}{4}$$

$$(\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g = C_D \cdot \dfrac{3 \rho_G v^2}{4}$$

#### Step 5: Solve for Velocity Squared

Rearranging for $v^2$:

$$v^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_G}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_G}}$$

#### Step 6: Apply Stokes' Regime Assumption (Re_p < 0.2)

In the **laminar flow regime** (low Reynolds number), the drag coefficient follows:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24 \cdot \mu_G}{\rho_G \cdot v \cdot d_p}$$

This is the famous **Stokes' drag law**, valid for creeping flow ($Re_p < 0.2$).

#### Step 7: Substitute C_D into Velocity Equation

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g}{3 \cdot \dfrac{24 \cdot \mu_G}{\rho_G \cdot v \cdot d_p} \cdot \rho_G}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g \cdot \rho_G \cdot v \cdot d_p}{3 \cdot 24 \cdot \mu_G \cdot \rho_G}}$$

The $\rho_G$ terms cancel:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g \cdot v \cdot d_p}{72 \cdot \mu_G}}$$

#### Step 8: Square Both Sides to Eliminate Square Root

$$v^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g \cdot v \cdot d_p}{72 \cdot \mu_G}$$

$$v = \dfrac{4 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G) \cdot g \cdot d_p}{72 \cdot \mu_G}$$

#### Step 9: Final Simplification

Combining constants: $\dfrac{4}{72} = \dfrac{1}{18}$

$$\boxed{v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous Stokes' Law equation** for terminal settling velocity. It reveals a **quadratic dependence** on droplet size and a **linear dependence** on density difference — explaining why doubling droplet diameter **quadruples** settling velocity.

### 3.2 Numerical Example: KTU-Style Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase separator operates at the following conditions:
- Operating pressure: $P = 30 \text{ bar}$
- Operating temperature: $T = 60 °\text{C} = 333 \text{ K}$
- Oil density: $\rho_L = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gas density: $\rho_G = 25 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gas viscosity: $\mu_G = 0.015 \text{ cP} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Water droplet diameter: $d_p = 200 \text{ μm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Calculate the terminal settling velocity and verify the flow regime.**

#### Solution Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Identify given data and convert units:

$$d_p = 200 \text{ μm} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\mu_G = 0.015 \text{ cP} = 0.015 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate density difference:

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_L - \rho_G = 850 - 25 = 825 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stokes' Law:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta \rho}{18 \mu_G}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 825}{18 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately:

Numerator:
$$9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 825 = 9.81 \times 3.3 \times 10^{-5} = 3.2373 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:
$$18 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 2.7 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$v_t = \dfrac{3.2373 \times 10^{-4}}{2.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.199 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_t \approx 1.20 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 6:** Verify Stokes' regime validity (check Reynolds number):

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_G \cdot v_t \cdot d_p}{\mu_G}$$

$$Re_p = \dfrac{25 \times 1.199 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p = \dfrac{25 \times 1.199 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{5.995 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{Re_p \approx 399.7}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation:** $Re_p \approx 400$ which is **far greater than 0.2**, meaning **Stokes' Law is NOT valid** for this case. We must use a more advanced correlation (e.g., Schiller-Naumann or Wallis correlation). This is a common **KTU exam trap** — students often apply Stokes' blindly without verifying the regime.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Factor on Separation Efficiency

| Factor | Change | Effect on $v_t$ | Effect on $\tau$ required | Overall Separation Efficiency | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Droplet size** ($d_p \uparrow$) | Increases | $\uparrow$ quadratically ($v_t \propto d_p^2$) | $\downarrow$ decreases | **Strongly improves** | High |
| **Density difference** ($\Delta \rho \uparrow$) | Increases | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\downarrow$ decreases | **Improves** | High |
| **Viscosity** ($\mu \uparrow$) | Increases | $\downarrow$ (inverse) | $\uparrow$ increases | **Worsens** | High |
| **Temperature** ($T \uparrow$) | Increases | $\uparrow$ (via $\mu \downarrow$) | $\downarrow$ decreases | **Improves** | Medium |
| **Pressure** ($P \uparrow$) | Increases | $\downarrow$ (via $\rho_G \uparrow$) | $\uparrow$ increases | **Worsens** | Medium |
| **Flow rate** ($Q \uparrow$) | Increases | (indirect) | $\downarrow$ decreases | **Worsens** (turbulence) | High |
| **Surface tension** ($\sigma \uparrow$) | Increases | (coalescence improved) | $\downarrow$ decreases | **Slightly improves** | Low |
| **Foaming tendency** | Increases | (foam blocks settling) | $\uparrow$ increases | **Strongly worsens** | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mantra:** "Big drops + Heavy oil + Low viscosity + Low pressure + Long residence time = Excellent Separation"

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1: Conceptual Definition

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define "terminal settling velocity" in the context of oil-gas separation. State the mathematical expression for the same under Stokes' flow regime.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Terminal settling velocity is the constant maximum velocity attained by a dispersed liquid droplet falling through a continuous gas (or liquid) phase under gravity, when the net force on the droplet becomes zero. At this velocity, the gravitational force is exactly balanced by the buoyancy force and the viscous drag force, so the droplet no longer accelerates.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):** Under Stokes' regime ($Re_p < 0.2$):

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{18 \mu_G}$$

where $g$ = gravitational acceleration, $d_p$ = droplet diameter, $\rho_L$ = liquid density, $\rho_G$ = gas density, and $\mu_G$ = gas dynamic viscosity.

---

#### Question A2: Factor Identification

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: List any SIX factors that affect the efficiency of oil-gas-water separation in a gravity separator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Density difference** between the dispersed and continuous phases
2. **Viscosity** of the continuous phase
3. **Droplet size** and size distribution
4. **Operating temperature and pressure**
5. **Residence time** (function of flow rate and vessel volume)
6. **Surface tension** and foaming tendency of the crude oil

*(Alternative valid factors: gas-oil ratio, vessel geometry, inlet device design, solid content, internals configuration)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry internal choice. The examiner provides TWO questions, and the student answers ONE. Each Part B has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically **7 marks each**.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of Stokes' Law, derive the expression for terminal settling velocity of a liquid droplet in a gas phase. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **OR**

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal three-phase separator handles the following wellstream:
- Oil flow rate: $Q_o = 5000 \text{ bbl/day}$
- Water flow rate: $Q_w = 2000 \text{ bbl/day}$
- Gas flow rate: $Q_g = 20 \text{ MMscfd}$
- Oil density: $\rho_o = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Water density: $\rho_w = 1020 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Operating temperature: $T = 60 °\text{C}$
- Operating pressure: $P = 25 \text{ bar}$
- Required retention time for water droplets: $t = 5 \text{ min}$

**Calculate the minimum vessel diameter** using Souders-Brown correlation with $K = 0.3 \text{ ft/s}$ and verify whether the design is adequate.

---

#### Model Answer for Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $Q_o = 5000 \text{ bbl/day} = 5000 \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 795 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- $Q_w = 2000 \text{ bbl/day} = 318 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- $Q_g = 20 \text{ MMscfd} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ scf/day}$
- $T = 333 \text{ K}$, $P = 25 \text{ bar} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$
- $K = 0.3 \text{ ft/s} = 0.0914 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1: Convert Gas Flow Rate to Standard Conditions (0.5 marks)**

At standard conditions: $T_{sc} = 288.7 \text{ K}$, $P_{sc} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$

$$Q_{g,sc} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ scf/day} = 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.0283 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 566,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$Q_{g,sc} = 566,000 / 86400 = 6.55 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Real Gas Law to Find Actual Volumetric Flow (1 mark)**

Using ideal gas approximation with $Z \approx 0.9$:

$$\dfrac{Q_{g,actual}}{Q_{g,sc}} = \dfrac{T}{T_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{sc}}{P} \cdot Z$$

$$Q_{g,actual} = 6.55 \times \dfrac{333}{288.7} \times \dfrac{1.013}{25} \times 0.9$$

$$Q_{g,actual} = 6.55 \times 1.153 \times 0.0405 \times 0.9 = 0.276 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Apply Souders-Brown Equation (1 mark)**

$$v_{max} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$$

For liquid (oil) density with negligible gas density:
$$\rho_L \approx 850 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_G \approx 0.0914 \times \sqrt{850/25} \approx ... $$

Calculating gas density using ideal gas law:
$$\rho_G = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^5 \times 0.02}{8.314 \times 333} \approx 18 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$v_{max} = 0.0914 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{850 - 18}{18}} = 0.0914 \times \sqrt{46.2} = 0.0914 \times 6.80$$

$$v_{max} = 0.621 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Vessel Diameter (1.5 marks)**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_{g,actual}}{\pi \cdot v_{max}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.276}{\pi \times 0.621}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.104}{1.951}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.566} = 0.752 \text{ m} \approx 30 \text{ inches}$$

**Step 5: Verify Retention Time (1 mark)**

Vessel volume: $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L$ where $L = 3D$ (typical L/D = 3)

$$V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.752^2 \times 2.256 = 1.002 \text{ m}^3$$

Total liquid flow rate:
$$Q_L = Q_o + Q_w = 795 + 318 = 1113 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 0.0129 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Liquid volume (40% of vessel — typical fill):
$$V_L = 0.4 \times 1.002 = 0.401 \text{ m}^3$$

Retention time:
$$\tau = \dfrac{V_L}{Q_L} = \dfrac{0.401}{0.0129} = 31.1 \text{ s} \approx 0.52 \text{ min}$$

**Step 6: Adequacy Check (1 mark)**

Required: 5 min; Available: 0.52 min. **The vessel is significantly undersized!**

The vessel needs to be at least $5/0.52 = 9.6 \times$ longer. Adjusting length:
$$L_{required} = 2.256 \times 9.6 = 21.7 \text{ m}$$

**Final design:** Diameter $D = 0.75 \text{ m}$ with length $L \approx 22 \text{ m}$ (or use multiple separators).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of residence time** in oil-gas separation. Explain how temperature, pressure, and droplet size interact to affect the required residence time in a gravity separator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **vertical, horizontal, and spherical separators** based on:
(i) Typical applications
(ii) Effect of foaming crude
(iii) Liquid handling capacity
(iv) Gas handling capacity
(v) Space/footprint constraints
(vi) Cost economics
(vii) Maintenance ease

Provide **two clear advantages** of horizontal separators for offshore applications.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Residence Time (1 mark):**

Residence time ($\tau$) is the average duration a fluid element spends inside the separator vessel, calculated as:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V_{effective}}{Q_{in}}$$

where $V_{effective}$ is the usable volume for separation and $Q_{in}$ is the volumetric inflow rate.

**2. Importance in Separation (2 marks):**

Residence time must exceed the **minimum time required for dispersed droplets** to settle through the gas-liquid interface. According to Stokes' Law:

$$t_{settling} = \dfrac{H}{v_t} = \dfrac{18 \mu_G H}{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta \rho}$$

where $H$ is the settling height. **Adequate residence time ensures that the smallest design droplet size can reach its equilibrium phase.**

**3. Effect of Temperature (1.5 marks):**

- **Direct effect:** Temperature reduces viscosity ($\mu \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ via Andrade equation)
- **Indirect effect:** Higher T decreases $\rho_L$ slightly but increases $\rho_G$ significantly → reduces $\Delta \rho$
- **Net effect:** Usually improves settling due to dominant viscosity reduction
- **Rule of thumb:** For every $\mathbf{10°C}$ rise in temperature, viscosity drops by ~$\mathbf{30-50\%}$, reducing required residence time proportionally

**4. Effect of Pressure (1.5 marks):**

- **Direct effect:** Higher pressure increases gas density ($\rho_G \uparrow$) → reduces $\Delta \rho$
- **Indirect effect:** More gas remains dissolved in oil → reduces free gas load
- **Trade-off:** Higher P reduces gas volumetric flow but worsens settling conditions
- **Optimal staging:** Multi-stage separation (HP → IP → LP) balances these effects

**5. Effect of Droplet Size (1 mark):**

- Settling time varies **inversely with $d_p^2$**
- Doubling droplet size **reduces** required residence time by a factor of **4**
- Coalescence enhancement (via baffles, packing) effectively increases $d_p$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Designers must optimize residence time against operating conditions, vessel size, and economic constraints. Typical residence times range from **30 seconds** (high-pressure gas) to **5-10 minutes** (3-phase oil-gas-water).

---

#### Model Answer for Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks] — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Vertical Separator | Horizontal Separator | Spherical Separator |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Applications | High GOR wells, gas de-liquification | 3-phase separation, offshore platforms | Low-pressure, intermittent flow, test separators |
| (ii) Foaming crude | **Poor** — large gas space makes foam worse | **Better** — longer liquid residence | **Moderate** — limited retention |
| (iii) Liquid capacity | **Low** — limited cross-section | **High** — large liquid surface | **Very Low** — small geometry |
| (iv) Gas capacity | **High** — large diameter | **Moderate** — depends on L/D | **Low** — limited |
| (v) Footprint | Small footprint, tall | Large footprint, short | Compact, spherical |
| (vi) Cost | Higher per unit volume | Moderate | Low for small capacities |
| (vii) Maintenance | Difficult internals access | **Easier** — manways on side | Difficult internal access |

**Two Advantages of Horizontal Separators for Offshore Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Compact Height Profile:** Critical for offshore platforms with crane lift limitations (typically < 30 m lift height). Horizontal vessels can be skid-mounted in modules.

2. **Superior 3-Phase Separation:** Larger liquid-liquid interface area enables cleaner oil-water separation, reducing downstream hydrocyclone/desalter loading.

3. *(Bonus)* Better liquid surge capacity for slug flow handling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (3-5 marks lost per error):**
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 1:** Applying Stokes' Law without verifying $Re_p < 0.2$ — Always check the Reynolds number first.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 2:** Forgetting to convert units (e.g., cP to Pa·s, μm to m, ft/s to m/s) — Unit errors are the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 3:** Using oil density in the gas-phase Stokes' Law equation — Use $\rho_L$ for the dispersed droplet and $\mu_G$ for the **continuous gas phase**.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 4:** Confusing residence time with retention time — They have different definitions in different contexts.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 5:** In Souders-Brown calculations, forgetting to convert K from ft/s to m/s when working in SI units.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 6:** Not accounting for compressibility ($Z$-factor) when calculating gas density at high pressures.
> 
> ❌ **MISTAKE 7:** Failing to mention foaming tendency as a critical design factor for certain crudes (especially paraffinic and asphaltene-rich oils).
> 
> ✅ **PRO TIP:** Always **state assumptions clearly** in derivation questions — this alone can earn 1-2 marks even if the final equation has minor errors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Factors Affecting Separation"**

#### Key Concepts Mastered:

- ✅ **Definition:** Terminal settling velocity is reached when buoyancy + drag = gravity
- ✅ **Primary Equation:** Stokes' Law: $v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta \rho}{18 \mu_G}$
- ✅ **Validity Range:** Stokes' Law applies only when $Re_p < 0.2$ (laminar flow)
- ✅ **Four Factor Categories:** Fluid properties, Operating conditions, Droplet characteristics, Design parameters

#### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize:

- ✅ **Souders-Brown K-factor:** $K = 0.3 \text{ ft/s}$ (general); $0.15 - 0.2$ for foamy crudes
- ✅ **Standard Retention Times:** Gas: 1-2 min; Oil: 3-5 min; Water: 5-10 min
- ✅ **Typical L/D Ratios:** Horizontal: 2.5-4; Vertical: 1.5-3; Spherical: 1
- ✅ **API Conversion:** °API = $\dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ (with $\rho$ in g/cm³)

#### Must-Know Engineering Trade-offs:

- ✅ **Temperature ↑** → Viscosity ↓ (good) but $\Delta\rho$ ↓ (bad) → Optimal T exists
- ✅ **Pressure ↑** → Gas density ↑ (reduces $\Delta\rho$, bad) → Multi-stage separation preferred
- ✅ **Droplet size ↑** → Settling improves **quadratically** → Coalescers are highly effective
- ✅ **Foaming tendency** → Can require 2-3× larger separator

#### Common Exam Question Patterns:

- ✅ Derive Stokes' Law (Step-by-step force balance)
- ✅ Calculate terminal settling velocity and verify regime
- ✅ Size a separator using Souders-Brown correlation
- ✅ Compare separator types (V/H/Spherical)
- ✅ Discuss effect of one parameter (e.g., temperature) on separation

#### Real-World Application Highlights:

- ✅ Offshore platforms predominantly use **horizontal** separators (3-phase)
- ✅ High-pressure gas wells use **vertical** separators for gas de-liquification
- ✅ **Test separators** are often spherical for low-cost intermittent service
- ✅ **Slug catchers** use specialized horizontal designs for multiphase flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always present your answer with:
> 1. **Given Data** with proper SI/Field unit conversion
> 2. **Assumptions** clearly stated
> 3. **Step-by-step derivation** with proper LaTeX formatting
> 4. **Final boxed answer** with units
> 5. **Verification/validation** of assumptions (e.g., $Re_p < 0.2$ check)
> 6. **Engineering interpretation** of the result

---

**End of Module 2 - Topic: Factors Affecting Separation Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separator Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Separator Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **production separator** is a pressure vessel used in oil and gas processing to separate the wellhead multiphase fluid stream (crude oil, natural gas, water, and entrained solids) into distinct, relatively stabilized phases through the combined mechanisms of **gravity settling**, **centrifugal force**, and **momentum changes**.
