**Separator Design** refers to the systematic engineering methodology of determining the principal vessel dimensions — namely, the **diameter (D)** and the **length/seam-to-seam height (L or H)** — using fundamental principles of fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, and process engineering, while satisfying KTU 2024 prescribed design codes such as **ASME Section VIII Div. 1** for pressure vessel integrity and **API Spec 12J** for oil and gas separation equipment specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT522 / Module 2):**
> Students must master three distinct design philosophies:
> 1. **Sizing by Gas Capacity (Souders–Brown Equation)** — governs minimum vessel diameter.
> 2. **Sizing by Liquid Retention Time** — governs the liquid-phase residence length/height.
> 3. **Sizing for Liquid–Liquid (Oil–Water) Separation** — governs baffle spacing and weir design.
> All three criteria must be checked, and the **largest governing dimension** is selected for the final vessel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall glass of muddy pond water left undisturbed on a windowsill. Within minutes, the heavy sand grains drop to the bottom, then the silt, and finally the clearer water rises to the top. A **production separator is essentially a giant, pressurized, engineered version of this windowsill** — but operating in seconds rather than hours and handling explosive hydrocarbons safely.

**Real-world analogy — the salad spinner analogy:**
- The wellstream enters a **primary inlet diverter** (like a salad spinner's lid), throwing the liquid *outward* against the wall (momentum separation).
- The **gravity settling section** is the calm body of the spinner where heavier water droplets fall out of the gas phase (gravity separation).
- The **mist extractor** at the top is like a fine mesh strainer catching the last tiny droplets (coalescence).
- The **liquid compartment** at the bottom is the collection bowl that further separates oil from water using a weir (liquid–liquid separation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Vessel diameters are typically expressed in **metres (m)** or **inches (in)** — 1 in = 0.0254 m.
> - Operating pressures are expressed in **kPa (gauge)**, **psig**, or **bar** — 1 bar = 100 kPa ≈ 14.504 psig.
> - The design temperature is the **MDMT (Minimum Design Metal Temperature)** + **29 °C (50 °F)** per ASME Sec. VIII UG-20.
> - The critical physical constants used are: **g = 9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration) and **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant).

---

### 1.3 Functional Role of a Separator in the Production Train

A separator performs **four simultaneous functions** that the KTU examiner expects you to enumerate clearly:

| Stage | Function | Physical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Primary Separation** | Bulk removal of free liquid from the gas | Momentum change via inlet diverter |
| 2. **Secondary Separation** | Removal of entrained mist from gas | Gravity settling |
| 3. **Coalescing** | Final droplet removal (typically >10 µm) | Vane/wire-mesh demister pad |
| 4. **Liquid–Liquid Split** | Separation of oil from produced water | Density difference + residence time |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Governing Design Philosophies

A correctly designed separator must simultaneously satisfy **two** independent sizing criteria. If either is violated, the vessel will fail in service (either gas will carry over liquid, called *liquid carry-over*, or liquid will entrain gas, called *gas carry-under*).

#### Philosophy A — Gas Capacity Sizing (Minimum Diameter)

The gas phase rises through the vessel at a velocity that must remain **below the "terminal settling velocity"** of the smallest liquid droplet we wish to capture. This terminal velocity is governed by **Stokes' Law** in the laminar regime (Re < 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Souders–Brown Equation** is the empirical KTU-standard formula:
> $$v_g = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$
> where the empirical constant **K** is the *Souders–Brown coefficient*, dependent on pressure and service.

**K values for design (KTI 2024 reference, API 12J):**

| Operating Pressure (psig) | K (ft/s) | K (m/s) |
|---|---|---|
| < 100 | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.107 – 0.137 |
| 100 – 500 | 0.30 – 0.40 | 0.091 – 0.122 |
| 500 – 1000 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.076 – 0.107 |
| > 1000 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.061 – 0.091 |

The required cross-sectional area for gas flow is then:

$$A_{min} = \frac{Q_g}{v_g}$$

For a **vertical separator**: $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \Rightarrow D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{min}}{\pi}}$

For a **horizontal separator**: $A = D \times L_{eff}$

#### Philosophy B — Liquid Retention Time Sizing (Length / Height)

Liquid droplets need **finite residence time** to coalesce and settle. KTU standard residence times are:

| Service | Retention Time (min) |
|---|---|
| Oil–Gas separation (high pressure) | 1 – 3 |
| Oil–Gas separation (low pressure / stock tank) | 3 – 5 |
| Oil–Water separation (treaters) | 5 – 10 (sometimes 20) |
| Three-phase (oil + gas + water) | 3 – 5 |
| Glycol / amine flash drums | 5 – 10 |

The required liquid-holdup volume is:

$$V_{liq} = Q_{liq} \times t_{ret}$$

For a **vertical separator**: $V_{liq} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times h_{liq}$, where $h_{liq}$ is the **low–low level (LLL) to high–high level (HHL)** distance, typically **D/2 to D**.

For a **horizontal separator**: $V_{liq}$ is split between oil and water compartments separated by an **interface weir**; residence times are checked independently for oil leg and water leg.

---

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any separator-design problem in the KTU 2024 examination. Memorize every entry.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stokes' Law settling velocity** | $v_t = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Valid for Re < 2 (laminar flow) |
| 2 | **Souders–Brown gas velocity** | $v_g = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | m/s | Empirical; K from table above |
| 3 | **Minimum cross-sectional area** | $A_{min} = \dfrac{Q_g}{v_g}$ | m² | $Q_g$ in m³/s |
| 4 | **Min. diameter (vertical)** | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q_g}{\pi \, v_g}}$ | m | — |
| 5 | **Liquid holdup volume** | $V_{liq} = Q_{liq} \times t_{ret}$ | m³ | $Q_{liq}$ in m³/s, $t_{ret}$ in s |
| 6 | **Vertical liquid height** | $h_{liq} = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{(\pi/4) D^2}$ | m | Check $h_{liq} \leq D$ |
| 7 | **Horizontal liquid volume (100% full half)** | $V_{liq} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | m³ | For half-full assumption |
| 8 | **Horizontal seam-to-seam length** | $L_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{(\pi/4) D^2} + L_{dry}$ | m | $L_{dry}$ ≈ 0.5 D for demister allowance |
| 9 | **Gas capacity (horizontal)** | $Q_g = 0.371 \, D^2 \, K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | ft³/s | Empirical; also given in m³/s |
| 10 | **Reynolds number check (drop)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_V \, d \, v_t}{\mu_V}$ | dimensionless | Must be < 2 for Stokes' Law |
| 11 | **Density of gas (ideal)** | $\rho_V = \dfrac{P M}{Z R T}$ | kg/m³ | M = mol. wt, Z = compressibility |
| 12 | **Real-world utility** | Controls quality of sales gas & crude | — | Specs: BS&W < 1%, salt < 50 PTB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention for Differences:**
> In every formula above, the term $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$ represents the **density difference** between the dispersed liquid phase and the continuous gas phase. **Always** subtract the *lighter* (gas) density from the *heavier* (liquid) density. If this term is negative, you have a sign error — *stop and recheck your phase identification*.

---

### 2.3 The Logic Behind the Sizing Approach

**Step 1 — Material balance first:** Before any sizing equation, perform a **flash calculation** at separator pressure and temperature to find $Q_g$ (gas volumetric flow at separator conditions) and $Q_{liq}$ (liquid volumetric flow). This is KTU's most common first-step trap — students skip the flash and plug in wrong numbers.

**Step 2 — Apply gas capacity criterion:** Calculate $v_g$ using Souders–Brown with the appropriate K. Find $D_{min}$ (vertical) or the cross-sectional constraint (horizontal).

**Step 3 — Apply liquid retention criterion:** Calculate $V_{liq}$ from $Q_{liq}$ and $t_{ret}$. Find $h_{liq}$ (vertical) or $L$ (horizontal).

**Step 4 — Select the larger dimension:** The final vessel must be sized to satisfy **both** criteria. The *larger* of the two required dimensions is the governing one.

**Step 5 — Apply mechanical constraints:** Check that the diameter matches a **standard pipe size** (e.g., 16", 20", 24", 30", 36", 42", 48" O.D.) and the length matches a **standard fabrication length** (multiples of 5 ft or 2 m).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

A liquid droplet of diameter *d* falling under gravity through a gas experiences three forces: gravity (downward), buoyancy (upward), and drag (opposite to motion).

**Force balance on the droplet at terminal velocity** (zero net acceleration, since inertial forces equal the differential weight):

$$F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} - F_{drag} = 0$$

**Step 1: Express gravity and buoyancy forces.**

The droplet mass is $m = \rho_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3$.

The displaced gas mass is $m_{displaced} = \rho_V \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3$.

Therefore, the net gravitational (effective) weight is:

$$F_{net} = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_L - \rho_V) \, g$$

**Step 2: Express the viscous drag force in the laminar regime (Stokes' drag).**

For a sphere in laminar flow (Re < 2):

$$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_V \, d \, v_t$$

**Step 3: Equate and solve for $v_t$.**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g = 3 \pi \mu_V \, d \, v_t$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi \, d$:

$$\frac{1}{6} d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g = 3 \mu_V \, v_t$$

Solving for $v_t$:

$$v_t = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{18 \, \mu_V}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Domain:** This derivation assumes Re < 2 (laminar), which corresponds to droplets typically < 100 µm. For larger droplets, the **Newton regime** (turbulent settling) applies with $F_{drag} = \frac{\pi}{8} C_D \rho_V d^2 v_t^2$, leading to a different (faster) terminal velocity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Vessel Diameter (Vertical Separator)

**Given:**
- Gas volumetric flow rate at operating conditions, $Q_g$ (m³/s)
- Souders–Brown coefficient, $K$ (m/s)
- Liquid density, $\rho_L$ (kg/m³)
- Gas density, $\rho_V$ (kg/m³)

**Find:** Minimum vessel diameter, $D_{min}$.

**Step 1: Calculate the allowable superficial gas velocity.**

$$v_g = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

**Step 2: Equate volumetric flow rate to velocity times area.**

$$Q_g = v_g \cdot A_{min}$$

Solving for $A_{min}$:

$$A_{min} = \frac{Q_g}{v_g}$$

**Step 3: Relate area to vessel diameter.**

For a circular cross-section:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Step 4: Solve for $D_{min}$.**

$$\frac{\pi}{4} D_{min}^2 = \frac{Q_g}{v_g}$$

$$D_{min}^2 = \frac{4 Q_g}{\pi \, v_g}$$

$$\boxed{D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, Q_g}{\pi \, v_g}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem Statement [KTU 2024 Practice Problem]:**
> A vertical two-phase oil-gas separator operates at 7 bar (gauge) and 50 °C. The gas flow rate at operating conditions is $Q_g = 0.20$ m³/s, and the liquid flow rate is $Q_{liq} = 0.005$ m³/s. The oil density is $\rho_L = 850$ kg/m³, the gas density is $\rho_V = 8.5$ kg/m³, and the recommended K-value is **0.1 m/s**. The required liquid retention time is **3 minutes**. Determine the minimum vessel diameter and the seam-to-seam height.

#### Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the gas capacity velocity using Souders–Brown.**

$$v_g = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$v_g = 0.1 \times \sqrt{\frac{850 - 8.5}{8.5}}$$

$$v_g = 0.1 \times \sqrt{\frac{841.5}{8.5}}$$

$$v_g = 0.1 \times \sqrt{99.0}$$

$$v_g = 0.1 \times 9.95$$

$$v_g = 0.995 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the minimum cross-sectional area.**

$$A_{min} = \frac{Q_g}{v_g} = \frac{0.20}{0.995} = 0.201 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the minimum diameter.**

$$D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.201}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.256} = 0.506 \text{ m}$$

Round up to nearest standard size: $D = 0.60$ m (≈ 24 in).

**Step 4: Calculate the required liquid holdup volume.**

Convert retention time to seconds:

$$t_{ret} = 3 \text{ min} = 180 \text{ s}$$

$$V_{liq} = Q_{liq} \times t_{ret} = 0.005 \times 180 = 0.90 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Calculate the required liquid height.**

$$h_{liq} = \frac{V_{liq}}{(\pi/4) D^2} = \frac{0.90}{(\pi/4) \times 0.60^2}$$

$$h_{liq} = \frac{0.90}{0.2827} = 3.18 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the seam-to-seam height.**

For a vertical separator, the typical layout (bottom to top) is:
- Bottom dead space (NLL) ≈ 0.30 m
- Liquid level ($h_{liq}$) = 3.18 m
- Vapor space above LLL ≈ D/2 = 0.30 m
- Mist extractor allowance ≈ 0.30 m
- Top dead space ≈ 0.20 m

$$H_{ss} = 0.30 + 3.18 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 4.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7: Sanity check — does $h_{liq} \leq D$?**

The standard rule is $h_{liq} \leq D$ for vertical separators. Here, $h_{liq} = 3.18$ m, but $D = 0.60$ m. **The criterion is violated**, meaning we have an *under-designed* vessel. We must increase the diameter.

**Step 8: Iterate — solve simultaneously for the diameter that satisfies both criteria.**

We need $h_{liq} = D/2$ as a starting assumption, so:

$$V_{liq} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times \frac{D}{2} = \frac{\pi D^3}{8}$$

$$D^3 = \frac{8 \times V_{liq}}{\pi} = \frac{8 \times 0.90}{\pi} = 2.292$$

$$D = \sqrt[3]{2.292} = 1.318 \text{ m}$$

Round up to standard: $D = 1.40$ m (≈ 54 in).

Recheck gas capacity at this larger diameter:

$$v_g = \frac{Q_g}{A} = \frac{0.20}{(\pi/4) \times 1.40^2} = \frac{0.20}{1.539} = 0.130 \text{ m/s}$$

This is well below the allowable 0.995 m/s, so the gas-capacity criterion is comfortably met. The **liquid retention criterion governs** the design.

**Final Design:**
- $D = 1.40$ m
- $H_{ss} \approx 5.0$ m (rounded for fabrication)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Criterion Governs?

| Scenario | Governing Criterion | Design Action |
|---|---|---|
| High gas flow, low liquid flow | **Gas capacity** | Increase $D$ to lower $v_g$ |
| Low gas flow, high liquid flow | **Liquid retention** | Increase $H$ (vertical) or $L$ (horizontal) |
| High pressure (> 1000 psi) | **Gas capacity** (K is small) | Use larger $D$ |
| Three-phase (oil + gas + water) | **Liquid retention** (water leg) | Add interface weir, design for water retention |
| Viscous oil (µ > 10 cP) | **Liquid retention** | Increase $t_{ret}$ to 5–10 min |
| Foamy crude | **Both** — use K reduced by 50% | Conservative design, add defoamer plate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Three-Phase Horizontal Separator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[INLET WELSTREAM Oil Gas Water] --> nodeB[INLET DIVERTER Primary Momentum Separation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[LIQUID COLLECTION SECTION Gravity Settling Chamber]
    nodeC --> nodeD[OIL WEIR Overflow to Oil Outlet]
    nodeC --> nodeE[INTERFACE WEIR Adjustable Level Control]
    nodeE --> nodeF[WATER OUTLET to Produced Water Treatment]
    nodeD --> nodeG[OIL OUTLET to Sales Crude Tank]
    nodeB --> nodeH[MIST EXTRACTOR Vane or Wire Mesh Demister Pad]
    nodeH --> nodeI[GAS OUTLET to Gas Processing Train]
    nodeA --> nodeJ[SAFETY RELIEF VALVE ASME UV Code]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[FLARE SYSTEM Emergency Venting]
    nodeC --> nodeL[INSTRUMENTATION LLT LLL HLL HHL LTG LT]
    nodeL --> nodeM[DCS CONTROL SYSTEM PLC and SCADA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node Identifier Convention:** All node IDs above follow the alphanumeric pattern `nodeA`–`nodeM`. Reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are *not* used as node IDs. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside double-quotes, ensuring flawless Mermaid compilation.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram of the Separator Design Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[STEP 1 PROCESS INLET DATA P T Qg Qliq] --> fl[STEP 2 FLASH CALCULATION Find rho_L rho_V]
    fl --> gc[STEP 3 GAS CAPACITY CHECK Compute v_g via Souders Brown]
    gc --> dmin[STEP 4 COMPUTE D_min from Gas Capacity]
    dmin --> lr[STEP 5 LIQUID RETENTION CHECK Compute V_liq from Qliq and t_ret]
    lr --> hmin[STEP 6 COMPUTE H_min or L_min from Liquid Volume]
    hmin --> sel[STEP 7 SELECT LARGER DIMENSION Governing Criterion]
    sel --> std[STEP 8 ROUND UP TO STANDARD SIZE ASME API 12J]
    std --> chk[STEP 9 VALIDATE Re lt 2 and h_liq lt D]
    chk -->|Pass| out[STEP 10 FINAL VESSEL D and H or L]
    chk -->|Fail| it[STEP 11 ITERATE OR ADD TRAYS]
    it --> gc
```

### 4.3 Verbal Schematic of a Vertical Two-Phase Separator (Cross-Sectional View)

```
          ╔══════════════════════╗    ← Head (ellipsoidal, ASME F&D)
          ║                      ║
          ║   MIST EXTRACTOR     ║    ← Wire-mesh or vane pad
          ║   ┌──────────────┐   ║
          ║   │ VAPOR SPACE  │   ║
          ║   │   (gas)      │   ║
          ║   └──────────────┘   ║
          ║   ┌──────────────┐   ║    ← Gas outlet nozzle
          ║   │  LIQUID      │   ║
          ║   │  LEVEL       │   ║    ← HLL (high-high level)
          ║   │  (oil)       │   ║
          ║   │              │   ║
          ║   │   h_liq ≈ D/2│   ║    ← LLL (low-low level)
          ║   └──────────────┘   ║
          ║   ┌──────────────┐   ║
          ║   │ BOTTOM SUMP  │   ║
          ║   └──────────────┘   ║
          ╚══════════════════════╝
                INLET (tangential)     LIQUID OUTLET (bottom)
```

**Annotations:**
- **Inlet diverter** is a deflector plate that forces the inlet jet to make two 90° turns, using *momentum change* to throw free liquid to the wall.
- **Vapor space** between LLL and mist pad is sized for **gas residence time of 3–5 seconds** at operating conditions.
- **Bottom sump** collects sand and sludge; it must be **at least 150 mm** deep and is drained through a separate nozzle.
- **Liquid outlet** has a **level-control valve (LCV)** maintaining LLL setpoint; failure of LCV can cause the vessel to fill with liquid and *flood the gas outlet* — a common KTU-exam scenario.

### 4.4 Verbal Schematic of a Horizontal Three-Phase Separator (Longitudinal View)

```
INLET ──► ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ ──► GAS OUTLET
          │ INLET│  GRAVITY      │ DEMISTER │ MIST     │            │
          │ DIVERTER  SETTLING   │  PAD     │ EXTRACTOR│            │
          │ ┌──┐  ┌────────────┐  │ ┌──────┐ │          │            │
          │ │  │  │ OIL LEG    │  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ │  │  │┌──────────┐│  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ │  │  ││ INTERFACE││  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ │  │  ││  WEIR    ││  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ │  │  │└──────────┘│  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ │  │  │ WATER LEG │  │ │      │ │          │            │
          │ └──┘  └────────────┘  │ └──────┘ │          │            │
          └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ▲                              ▲
              WATER OUTLET                    OIL OUTLET
           (bottom, under weir)            (top, over weir)
```

**Annotations:**
- The **interface weir** is a *physical* plate that sets the oil/water interface height. It is **adjustable** (slide gate) to allow field tuning.
- The **oil leg retention time** (3–5 min) and **water leg retention time** (5–10 min) are calculated *independently* and use different cross-sectional areas.
- The **mist extractor** is located at the gas outlet end, after a minimum of **one vessel diameter** of straight settling length to allow droplet growth.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define a production separator. List the four stages of separation achieved inside a two-phase horizontal production separator.*

**Model Answer:**

A **production separator** is a pressure vessel used to separate the wellhead multiphase stream into its constituent gas and liquid phases using gravity, momentum, and coalescence mechanisms.

**Four stages of separation inside a horizontal two-phase separator:**

1. **Primary (Inlet) Separation** — Bulk liquid removal via an inlet diverter that uses sudden momentum change.
2. **Secondary (Gravity) Separation** — Smaller droplets settle out of the gas stream due to density difference.
3. **Coalescing (Tertiary) Separation** — A mist extractor (vane or wire-mesh pad) removes the finest droplets (typically 5–10 µm).
4. **Liquid Smoothing** — The collected liquid is held in a calm chamber to allow dissolved gas to flash out before it exits the vessel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (1 mark lost):** Students often confuse "coalescing" with "secondary separation." Remember: secondary uses *gravity only*, while tertiary *coalesces* droplets on a mesh/vane element. The KTU examiner explicitly tests this distinction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022]**
*State the Souders–Brown equation and explain the significance of the K-coefficient. How does operating pressure affect the choice of K?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Souders–Brown equation** is:

$$v_g = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

where $v_g$ is the maximum permissible superficial gas velocity, $\rho_L$ and $\rho_V$ are liquid and gas densities, and $K$ is the Souders–Brown coefficient.

**Significance of K:** $K$ is an **empirical constant** that bundles together the influence of droplet size to be captured, gas viscosity, vessel geometry, and entrainment tolerance. It is **not dimensionless** and has units of velocity (m/s or ft/s).

**Effect of operating pressure:** As operating pressure **increases**, the gas density $\rho_V$ increases, which reduces the density difference $(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V$ and increases the drag on droplets. To maintain a safety margin against re-entrainment, the recommended **K value decreases with increasing pressure** (e.g., K ≈ 0.10 m/s for low pressure, but K ≈ 0.06 m/s for high pressure > 70 bar).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the Stokes' Law expression for the terminal settling velocity of a liquid droplet in a gas medium. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal two-phase separator handles a gas flow of $1.5 \times 10^5$ Sm³/day and a liquid flow of 80 m³/day. The operating conditions are **35 bar (abs) and 60 °C**. The gas density at operating conditions is **28 kg/m³** and the liquid density is **780 kg/m³**. Use $K = 0.08$ m/s and liquid retention time $t_{ret} = 3$ min. Design the separator for diameter and seam-to-seam length. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Stokes' Law Derivation:**

A spherical droplet of diameter *d* falls under gravity through a stagnant gas. At terminal velocity, the net force is zero.

**Assumptions:**
1. The droplet is **rigid and spherical** (no deformation or oscillation).
2. The flow regime around the droplet is **laminar** (Reynolds number < 2).
3. The gas is **quiescent** (no bulk flow contribution; only relative motion).
4. The droplet is **isolated** (no droplet–droplet interaction, dilute system).

**Force balance (downward positive):**

$$F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} - F_{drag} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Gravity force on droplet:**

$$F_{gravity} = m_d \cdot g = \rho_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Buoyancy force (Archimedes' principle):**

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_V \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Viscous drag (Stokes' drag in laminar regime):**

$$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_V \, d \, v_t$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into the force balance:**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_L g - \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_V g - 3 \pi \mu_V d \, v_t = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplifying:**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 g (\rho_L - \rho_V) = 3 \pi \mu_V d \, v_t$$

**Step 6 — Solving for $v_t$:**

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{18 \mu_V}$$

This is the **Stokes' Law terminal settling velocity**.

**Validity:** Strictly valid for Re < 2, i.e., for droplets typically < 100 µm in atmospheric air.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given Data:**
- $Q_g = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Sm³/day (standard conditions)
- $Q_{liq} = 80$ m³/day
- $P_{op} = 35$ bar (abs), $T_{op} = 60 + 273 = 333$ K
- $\rho_L = 780$ kg/m³, $\rho_V = 28$ kg/m³
- $K = 0.08$ m/s, $t_{ret} = 3$ min = 180 s

**Step 1: Convert gas flow to operating conditions using the ideal gas law.**

We need the standard flow rate and operating flow rate. Assume standard conditions: $P_{sc} = 1.013$ bar, $T_{sc} = 288$ K.

$$Q_{g,op} = Q_{g,sc} \times \frac{P_{sc}}{P_{op}} \times \frac{T_{op}}{T_{sc}}$$

$$Q_{g,op} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times \frac{1.013}{35} \times \frac{333}{288}$$

$$Q_{g,op} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.02894 \times 1.1563$$

$$Q_{g,op} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.03346 = 5019 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

Convert to m³/s:

$$Q_{g,op} = \frac{5019}{86400} = 0.0581 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Souders–Brown equation.**

$$v_g = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}} = 0.08 \sqrt{\frac{780 - 28}{28}}$$

$$v_g = 0.08 \times \sqrt{26.857} = 0.08 \times 5.183 = 0.4146 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the minimum cross-sectional area.**

$$A_{min} = \frac{Q_{g,op}}{v_g} = \frac{0.0581}{0.4146} = 0.1401 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Solve for the minimum diameter.**

$$D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{min}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.1401}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.1784} = 0.4223 \text{ m}$$

Round up to standard pipe size: $D = 0.50$ m (≈ 20 in).

**Step 5: Calculate the required liquid holdup volume.**

$$V_{liq} = Q_{liq} \times t_{ret} = \frac{80}{86400} \times 180 = 0.000926 \times 180 = 0.1667 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: Calculate the liquid length in the horizontal vessel.**

For a horizontal separator, the liquid volume occupies the lower portion. For approximate design assuming 50% full at normal level:

$$V_{liq} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L_{liq}$$

$$L_{liq} = \frac{V_{liq}}{(\pi/4) D^2} = \frac{0.1667}{(\pi/4) \times 0.50^2} = \frac{0.1667}{0.1963} = 0.849 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7: Add dry ends (mist extractor allowance + inlet/outlet dead space).**

Typical dry-end allowance: inlet = 0.3 D, outlet = 0.3 D, demister pad = 0.5 D.

$$L_{dry} = (0.3 + 0.3 + 0.5) \times D = 1.1 \times 0.50 = 0.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8: Total seam-to-seam length.**

$$L_{ss} = L_{liq} + L_{dry} = 0.849 + 0.55 = 1.40 \text{ m}$$

Round up to standard fabrication length: $L_{ss} = 1.5$ m.

**Final Design:**
- **Diameter, $D = 0.50$ m (20 in)**
- **Seam-to-seam length, $L_{ss} = 1.5$ m**

---

**QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam — December 2023 — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Explain the difference between horizontal and vertical separators. For a given gas and liquid flow, which type is preferred when the gas-to-liquid ratio (GLR) is high? Justify with design reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical two-phase separator is to be designed for a gas flow of $Q_g = 0.5$ m³/s at operating conditions and a liquid flow of $Q_{liq} = 0.008$ m³/s. The densities are $\rho_L = 820$ kg/m³ and $\rho_V = 12$ kg/m³. Use $K = 0.10$ m/s and $t_{ret} = 2$ min. Determine the vessel dimensions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Horizontal vs. Vertical Separators:**

| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| **Footprint** | Long and narrow; large floor area | Tall and slim; small floor area |
| **Liquid holdup** | Large volume per unit diameter | Smaller volume |
| **Foaming service** | Preferred (larger surface area) | Less preferred |
| **Solid/sand handling** | Difficult (sand settles along length) | Preferred (sand drops to bottom sump) |
| **High GLR (Gas-to-Liquid Ratio)** | Preferred (gas flows horizontally, low velocity) | Less preferred |
| **Slug flow** | Better (longer residence time) | Less tolerant |
| **Pressure cycling** | Less tolerant | More tolerant |
| **Cost per volume** | Lower for high liquid rates | Higher for high liquid rates |

**Recommendation for High GLR:**

A **horizontal separator** is preferred when GLR is high for the following reasons:

1. **Lower gas velocity:** The gas flows axially along the length, so the superficial velocity is naturally lower for the same cross-sectional area, reducing re-entrainment.
2. **Larger gas–liquid interface area:** The wide, flat interface enhances the disengagement of small droplets, allowing more gas to be processed per unit volume.
3. **Better gas quality:** A longer settling path at low velocity removes finer droplets — critical when the gas stream dominates.

**Exception:** If **solids (sand, scale)** are present, a **vertical separator** is preferred regardless of GLR, because solids can be removed through the bottom sump without spreading along the horizontal length.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 0.5$ m³/s, $Q_{liq} = 0.008$ m³/s
- $\rho_L = 820$ kg/m³, $\rho_V = 12$ kg/m³
- $K = 0.10$ m/s, $t_{ret} = 2$ min = 120 s

**Step 1: Apply Souders–Brown to find gas capacity velocity.**

$$v_g = 0.10 \times \sqrt{\frac{820 - 12}{12}} = 0.10 \times \sqrt{67.33} = 0.10 \times 8.205 = 0.8205 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Find minimum cross-sectional area.**

$$A_{min} = \frac{Q_g}{v_g} = \frac{0.5}{0.8205} = 0.6094 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Find minimum diameter.**

$$D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{min}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.6094}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.7760} = 0.881 \text{ m}$$

Round up to standard: $D = 0.90$ m (≈ 36 in).

**Step 4: Calculate liquid holdup volume.**

$$V_{liq} = Q_{liq} \times t_{ret} = 0.008 \times 120 = 0.96 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Calculate the liquid height.**

$$h_{liq} = \frac{V_{liq}}{(\pi/4) D^2} = \frac{0.96}{(\pi/4) \times 0.90^2} = \frac{0.96}{0.6362} = 1.509 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Check the $h_{liq} \leq D$ criterion.**

$h_{liq} = 1.509$ m vs. $D = 0.90$ m → **Violated.** Iterate.

Set $h_{liq} = D/2 = 0.45$ m and solve for $D$:

$$V_{liq} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times \frac{D}{2} = \frac{\pi D^3}{8}$$

$$D^3 = \frac{8 \times 0.96}{\pi} = 2.445$$

$$D = \sqrt[3]{2.445} = 1.347 \text{ m}$$

Round up: $D = 1.40$ m (≈ 54 in).

**Step 7: Recalculate gas velocity at $D = 1.40$ m.**

$$v_g = \frac{Q_g}{(\pi/4) D^2} = \frac{0.5}{(\pi/4) \times 1.40^2} = \frac{0.5}{1.539} = 0.325 \text{ m/s}$$

This is below the 0.8205 m/s allowable, so the gas-capacity criterion is satisfied.

**Step 8: Final liquid height check.**

$$h_{liq} = \frac{0.96}{(\pi/4) \times 1.40^2} = \frac{0.96}{1.539} = 0.624 \text{ m}$$

This is $\approx D/2$, so the criterion is satisfied.

**Step 9: Calculate seam-to-seam height.**

$$H_{ss} = 0.30 \text{ (bottom sump)} + 0.624 \text{ (liquid)} + 0.70 \text{ (vapor)} + 0.40 \text{ (mist)} + 0.20 \text{ (top)}$$

$$H_{ss} = 2.22 \text{ m}$$

Round up: $H_{ss} = 2.5$ m.

**Final Design:**
- **Diameter, $D = 1.40$ m (54 in)**
- **Seam-to-seam height, $H_{ss} = 2.5$ m**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the flash calculation** (1–2 marks): Many students plug in the standard gas flow rate (Sm³/s) directly into the Souders–Brown equation. **Always** convert to operating conditions using $PV = nRT$ first.
> 2. **Sign error in $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$** (1 mark): If the answer is negative, you have inverted the densities. Liquid is heavier; gas is lighter.
> 3. **Skipping the $h_{liq} \leq D$ check** (2 marks): The KTU key explicitly awards 1 mark for this check and 1 mark for the iteration step.
> 4. **Not rounding up to standard size** (1 mark): The vessel diameter must always be rounded **up** to the next standard pipe OD — never truncated.
> 5. **Unit confusion between Sm³ and Nm³** (1 mark): Standard cubic metres (Sm³) are at 15.6 °C and 1 atm; normal cubic metres (Nm³) are at 0 °C and 1 atm. The KTU convention uses **Sm³** unless otherwise stated.
> 6. **Mixing up retention time units** (1 mark): Liquid retention time is in **minutes** in design tables but in **seconds** in the $V = Q \cdot t$ equation. Always convert.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Separator Design (Module 2, PECHT522)**

- [ ] A **production separator** separates oil, gas, and water using gravity, momentum, and coalescence.
- [ ] The **four functional stages** are: inlet → gravity → mist extraction → liquid smoothing.
- [ ] **Two governing design criteria**: gas capacity (Souders–Brown) and liquid retention time.
- [ ] **Souders–Brown equation**: $v_g = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ — the *gas capacity* test.
- [ ] **Stokes' Law**: $v_t = g d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) / (18 \mu)$ — valid for Re < 2.
- [ ] **K-coefficient** *decreases* with increasing operating pressure (table values from 0.06 to 0.13 m/s).
- [ ] **Always perform a flash calculation** to convert gas flow from standard to operating conditions.
- [ ] **Vertical separator**: design diameter from gas capacity; design height from liquid retention.
- [ ] **Horizontal separator**: design diameter from gas capacity; design length from liquid retention.
- [ ] **Three-phase separators** add an **interface weir** for oil–water separation, with independent retention times for oil and water legs.
- [ ] **High GLR → horizontal**; **sand/solids → vertical**; **foaming crude → horizontal**.
- [ ] **Standard retention times**: 1–3 min for high-pressure gas/oil; 3–5 min for low-pressure/stock; 5–10 min for oil–water.
- [ ] **Standard diameters** are pipe ODs: 16", 20", 24", 30", 36", 42", 48" (round **up**, never down).
- [ ] **Validity check**: $h_{liq} \leq D$ for vertical vessels; Re < 2 for Stokes' Law.
- [ ] **Mist extractor** placed at gas outlet, with at least **one diameter** of straight settling length upstream.
- [ ] **Inlet diverter** uses sudden *momentum change* to perform primary bulk separation.
- [ ] **Final selection** = the **larger** of the two dimensions from gas capacity vs. liquid retention.
- [ ] **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1** is the design code for pressure integrity; **API 12J** specifies oil/gas separation equipment.
- [ ] **Valuation trap**: forgetting units, sign errors, skipping iteration when $h_{liq} > D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stage Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stage Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage Separation** is a sequential, multi-pressure-level hydrocarbon recovery process in which the wellhead fluid mixture (crude oil, dissolved natural gas, water, and sediment) is progressively separated into its constituent phases across a series of pressure vessels operating at successively lower pressures. Each vessel (called a "stage") operates at thermodynamic equilibrium, allowing controlled liberation of gas from the liquid phase, thereby maximizing liquid (oil) recovery, stabilizing the crude, and minimizing flash losses.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT522)**, stage separation is studied as the foundational oilfield processing technique bridging reservoir deliverability and downstream surface treatment. It is governed by the principles of **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)**, **flash vaporization**, and **phase behavior thermodynamics** of multicomponent hydrocarbon mixtures.

The key pressure stages typically observed in field practice are:

- **High-Pressure Stage (HPS):** Operates closest to wellhead flowing pressure (often **690 kPa to 3500 kPa**)
- **Intermediate-Pressure Stage (IPS):** Operates between HPS and LPS (typically **350 kPa to 1000 kPa**)
- **Low-Pressure Stage (LPS):** Final gas-liquid disengagement (often **100 kPa to 350 kPa**)
- **Atmospheric / Stock Tank Stage:** Final stabilization at approximately **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pressure-Release Soda Bottle Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a sealed soda bottle (the reservoir/wellbore) holding carbonated water (the live oil with dissolved gas). If you pop the cap all at once (single-stage separation), a large burst of fizz escapes chaotically, taking valuable liquid droplets with it — this is **flash loss** and **entrainment**.
>
> Now imagine you slowly unscrew the cap in three small steps (multi-stage separation): first the pressure drops gently (HPS), then a bit more (IPS), then finally fully released (LPS). The dissolved gas escapes gently, the liquid stays intact, and the beverage (recovered oil) is maximized.
>
> **In one line:** Stage separation = "Letting the gas out gently, one pressure step at a time, to save the oil."

---

## 1.3 Why Stage Separation? — Engineering Justification

| Reason | Engineering Benefit |
|--------|---------------------|
| Maximize stock-tank liquid recovery | Higher oil volume, increased revenue |
| Reduce recompression costs | Gas at higher pressure is easier to process |
| Stabilize crude for storage/shipping | Safer transport, lower vapor pressure |
| Minimize entrained liquid in gas | Protects downstream compressors/pipelines |
| Provide consistent feed to downstream units | Improves refinery efficiency |
| Reduce separator sizing stress on single vessel | Practical mechanical design limits |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions Used Throughout Stage Separation Calculations:**
> - **Standard Temperature:** **T_std = 288.15 K (15 °C / 60 °F)**
> - **Standard Pressure:** **P_std = 101.325 kPa (14.7 psia)**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Separator Operating Temperature:** Typically **15 °C to 60 °C** (depends on fluid and climate)
> - **Universal Quasi-Velocity (K-value definition):** **K_i = y_i / x_i**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation

The governing principle of stage separation is the **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**. At each stage, the vapor (gas) phase and liquid (oil) phase coexist at identical temperature and pressure, with each component *i* satisfying:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

Where:
- **K_i** = equilibrium constant (or K-value) of component *i*
- **y_i** = mole fraction of component *i* in the vapor phase
- **x_i** = mole fraction of component *i* in the liquid phase

The **Summation of Mole Fractions** must equal unity in both phases:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1 \quad \text{(Vapor)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 1 \quad \text{(Liquid)}$$

The **Rachford-Rice Equation** is used to find the vapor fraction β in a flash calculation:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

Where:
- **z_i** = overall mole fraction of component *i* in feed
- **β** = mole fraction of feed vaporized

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

> [!TIP]
> **Logical Flow of a 3-Stage Separation Train:**

1. **Wellhead fluid** arrives at the **High-Pressure Separator (HPS)** at flowing tubing pressure.
2. The HPS removes the bulk of **free gas** (those components already in vapor form at reservoir conditions).
3. The liquid **underflow** from HPS flows (via level-control valve) to the **Intermediate-Pressure Separator (IPS)**.
4. The pressure drop across this throttling valve causes **flash vaporization** — additional gas evolves from the liquid.
5. The IPS liquid underflow drops further to the **Low-Pressure Separator (LPS)**, repeating flash liberation.
6. The stabilized oil from LPS finally flows to the **stock tank** at atmospheric pressure, where residual gas is vented or captured.
7. Gas streams from HPS, IPS, and LPS are collected separately (since they have different heating values, compositions, and pressures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **thermodynamic driver** of stage separation is **isothermal flash vaporization** induced by sequential pressure reduction. The process is approximately isenthalpic (constant enthalpy) across the throttling valves, not isentropic.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU Part B and Part C problems on this module.**

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|----------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $K_i = y_i / x_i$ | Equilibrium constant definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sum y_i = \sum x_i = 1$ | Mole fraction closure | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sum \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$ | Rachford-Rice flash equation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $y_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$ | Vapor composition (flash) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$ | Liquid composition (flash) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $G_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} G_j$ | Total gas recovered (sum of all stages) | kg or scf |
| 7 | $GOR_j = G_j / L_j$ | Gas-Oil Ratio at stage *j* | scf/stb |
| 8 | $R_{so} = \frac{V_{gas,ST}}{V_{oil,ST}}$ | Solution gas-oil ratio | scf/stb |
| 9 | $n_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\frac{P_{wh}}{P_{st}}}$ | Optimum number of stages (approximate) | Stages |
| 10 | $E_{rec} = \frac{L_{ST}}{L_{ideal}}$ | Liquid recovery efficiency | Dimensionless (%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Units!** KTU valuation penalizes inconsistent units. Always report gas in **scf (standard cubic feet)** or **Sm³**, oil in **stb (stock tank barrel)** or **Sm³**, and GOR in **scf/stb** or **Sm³/Sm³**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stage separation is not a textbook abstraction — it is the **economic heart of every production platform, FPSO, and gathering station** in the world. Applications include:

- **Offshore platforms (Mumbai High, North Sea Brent, Gulf of Mexico):** Compact 3 or 4-stage trains are essential due to weight/space constraints.
- **Onshore tank batteries (Permian Basin, Assam fields):** Multi-stage separation upstream of storage.
- **FPSO vessels (Petrobras, Shell, ONGC):** Sequential separation stabilizes crude before offloading to shuttle tankers.
- **Gas condensate fields:** Reverse multi-stage (condensate stabilization) prevents liquid dropout in export pipelines.
- **Heavy oil / bitumen operations:** Low-pressure steam-heated separators handle viscous crudes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Flash Equation (Rachford-Rice)

### Starting Point — Component Material Balance

Consider a feed with overall mole fraction $z_i$ entering a flash drum operating at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. A fraction $\beta$ of the feed vaporizes; the remaining $(1 - \beta)$ stays liquid.

The component material balance per mole of feed is:

$$z_i = \beta \cdot y_i + (1 - \beta) \cdot x_i$$

### Substituting the K-Value Relation

Using the definition $y_i = K_i \cdot x_i$, substitute into the balance:

$$z_i = \beta (K_i x_i) + (1 - \beta) x_i$$

Factor out $x_i$:

$$z_i = x_i \left[ \beta K_i + 1 - \beta \right]$$

$$z_i = x_i \left[ 1 + \beta (K_i - 1) \right]$$

### Solving for Liquid Mole Fraction

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

### Solving for Vapor Mole Fraction

$$y_i = K_i x_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

### Applying the Summation Constraint

Since the vapor mole fractions must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 1$$

### Rearranging to Obtain Rachford-Rice

Subtract 1 from the left-hand side using the identity $1 = \sum z_i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i = 0$$

Combine into a single sum:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} - z_i \right] = 0$$

Factor $z_i$ inside each term:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i \left[ \frac{K_i - (1 + \beta (K_i - 1))}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} \right] = 0$$

Expand the numerator inside the bracket:

$$K_i - 1 - \beta K_i + \beta = (1 - \beta) K_i - (1 - \beta) = (1 - \beta)(K_i - 1)$$

Substituting back:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (1 - \beta)(K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

Since $(1 - \beta)$ is a common scalar (and non-trivial in physical flash), divide through:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The Rachford-Rice equation gives the **vapor fraction β** at flash equilibrium. It is solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson or bisection) since the K-values themselves depend on T, P, and composition.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Material Balance Across 3-Stage Separation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 2-mark conceptual lead-in):**
>
> A wellhead stream produces 10,000 stb/day of stock-tank oil with an associated GOR of 800 scf/stb. A 3-stage separation train operates at:
> - Stage 1 (HPS): 1000 psia, 80 °F → produces gas G₁ and liquid L₁
> - Stage 2 (IPS): 400 psia, 80 °F → produces gas G₂ and liquid L₂
> - Stage 3 (LPS): 50 psia, 80 °F → produces gas G₃ and liquid L₃
> - Stock tank: 14.7 psia, 60 °F → produces G₄ and final L_ST = 10,000 stb/day
>
> **Find:** Total gas produced per day and approximate gas rate from each stage.

### Step 1 — Total Gas Production

The total gas-oil ratio (total GOR) at the wellhead equals the total gas production divided by stock-tank oil:

$$G_{total} = R_{p} \times L_{ST}$$

$$G_{total} = 800 \, \frac{\text{scf}}{\text{stb}} \times 10{,}000 \, \text{stb/day}$$

$$G_{total} = 8{,}000{,}000 \, \text{scf/day} = 8.0 \times 10^6 \, \text{scf/day}$$

### Step 2 — Approximate Stage Distribution

Using empirical field correlations (or a PVT simulator), the gas distribution is approximately:

| Stage | Pressure (psia) | Gas (Mscf/day) | % of Total Gas | Oil Leaving (stb/day) |
|-------|----------------|----------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 (HPS) | 1000 | 4,800 | 60% | 10,400 |
| 2 (IPS) | 400 | 1,920 | 24% | 10,160 |
| 3 (LPS) | 50 | 960 | 12% | 10,080 |
| 4 (Stock) | 14.7 | 320 | 4% | 10,000 |
| **TOTAL** | — | **8,000** | **100%** | — |

### Step 3 — Mass Balance Check (Liquid Side)

The slight oil-volume expansion from dissolved-gas release is captured by the **formation volume factor Bo**:

$$V_{j-1} = V_j \times (1 + \Delta Bo_j)$$

For example, going from HPS liquid to IPS liquid:

$$V_{HPS, liquid} = 10{,}400 \, \text{stb equivalent}$$

$$V_{IPS, liquid} = 10{,}160 \, \text{stb equivalent}$$

The oil shrinks as gas evolves — a key physical signature of stage separation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that **oil volume shrinks from HPS to Stock Tank** as dissolved gas is released. The HPS liquid volume is *larger* than the stock-tank oil volume by typically 5–25%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Single-Stage vs. Multi-Stage Recovery

| Parameter | Single-Stage | 2-Stage | 3-Stage | 4-Stage | 5-Stage |
|-----------|--------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Liquid Recovery (% of maximum) | 100% (baseline) | ~105% | ~108% | ~110% | ~111% |
| GOR at first stage (scf/stb) | Highest | Lower | Lower | Lower | Lower |
| Recompression HP gas | — | Higher | Moderate | Lower | Lowest |
| Capital Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Higher | Higher | Highest |
| Optimum for Offshore | No | Sometimes | Yes | Yes | Rare |
| Optimum for Onshore | Rare | Yes | Yes | Sometimes | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "diminishing returns" rule:** Each additional stage recovers progressively less incremental oil. Beyond 4 stages, the capital cost outweighs the revenue benefit. The **optimum number of stages** is determined by **net present value (NPV)** analysis balancing incremental oil revenue against installed equipment cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Stage Separation Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wellhead Stream: Oil + Gas + Water + Sediment at P_wh"] --> B["High-Pressure Separator HPS"]
    B --> C["HP Gas Stream to Compressor 1"]
    B --> D["HP Liquid Underflow to Throttling Valve V1"]
    D --> E["Intermediate-Pressure Separator IPS"]
    E --> F["IP Gas Stream to Compressor 2"]
    E --> G["IP Liquid Underflow to Throttling Valve V2"]
    G --> H["Low-Pressure Separator LPS"]
    H --> I["LP Gas Stream to Compressor 3 or Vapor Recovery"]
    H --> J["LP Liquid Underflow to Throttling Valve V3"]
    J --> K["Stock Tank at Atmospheric Pressure"]
    K --> L["Stable Crude Oil to Storage or Pipeline"]
    K --> M["Vent or Vapor Recovery Unit VRU"]
    C --> N["Gas Gathering Manifold"]
    F --> N
    I --> N
    N --> O["Gas Processing Plant or Sales Line"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The feed enters the **HPS** at the highest pressure. Three gas streams (HP, IP, LP) merge at the gas manifold, while the liquid progresses through pressure-reducing valves into successively lower-pressure vessels. Final stabilized oil exits at the **stock tank**.

---

## 4.2 Process Logic: Decision Tree for Optimum Number of Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Determine Well Stream PVT Properties"] --> B["Calculate Bubble Point Pressure Pb"]
    B --> C{"Is P_whellhead > 1000 psia?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Consider 3-Stage or 4-Stage Train"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Consider 2-Stage Train"]
    D --> F["Estimate Liquid Recovery Increments"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Is Incremental Revenue > Added CAPEX?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Add the Additional Stage"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Stop — Current Configuration is Optimum"]
    H --> F
    I --> J["Final Design Locked"]
```

---

## 4.3 Pressure Profile Across a 3-Stage Train

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Wellhead: 1500 psia"] -->|Throttle Drop 1| B["HPS: 1000 psia"]
    B -->|Throttle Drop 2| C["IPS: 400 psia"]
    C -->|Throttle Drop 3| D["LPS: 50 psia"]
    D -->|Final Drop| E["Stock Tank: 14.7 psia"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Design heuristic:** The pressure drop between stages is typically **geometrically decreasing** — large drop first (wellhead → HPS), smaller drops thereafter. This balances gas recovery against compressor energy cost.

---

## 4.4 Component Flow Schematic with Mass Balance Labels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Feed F = F_oil + F_gas moles per unit time"] --> B["Stage 1 at P1, T1"]
    B --> C1["Vapor V1 = beta1 times F"]
    B --> L1["Liquid L1 = 1 minus beta1 times F"]
    L1 --> D["Stage 2 at P2 LT P1, T2"]
    D --> C2["Vapor V2 = beta2 times L1"]
    D --> L2["Liquid L2 = 1 minus beta2 times L1"]
    L2 --> E["Stage 3 at P3 LT P2, T3"]
    E --> C3["Vapor V3 = beta3 times L2"]
    E --> L3["Final Stock Tank Liquid L3 = 1 minus beta3 times L2"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **β values** are determined at each stage by solving the Rachford-Rice equation with the K-values corresponding to that stage's (P, T) and the inlet composition to that stage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Total Marks: 2 × 3 = 6 Marks | Time: ~6 Minutes**

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define stage separation. Mention any two advantages of multi-stage separation over single-stage separation.**

**Model Answer:**

Stage separation is a hydrocarbon production process in which a wellhead oil-gas mixture is progressively separated into gas and liquid phases across a series of pressure vessels (stages), each operating at a successively lower pressure. The objective is to recover maximum stock-tank liquid while delivering gas at useful pressures to gathering systems.

**Two advantages over single-stage separation:**

1. **Increased liquid recovery:** Multi-stage separation reduces flash losses, recovering 5–15% more stock-tank oil compared to a single-stage design.
2. **Lower recompression cost:** Gas evolved at higher pressures (HPS, IPS) requires less compression to reach sales-line pressure, reducing operating expenses.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Rachford-Rice equation used in flash calculations.**

**Model Answer:**

The Rachford-Rice equation is given by:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

Where $z_i$ is the overall feed mole fraction of component *i*, $K_i$ is the equilibrium constant, and $\beta$ is the mole fraction of feed vaporized. It is derived from combining the component material balance and the K-value definition with the closure condition $\sum y_i = 1$. It is solved iteratively for $\beta$ to determine the vapor fraction in a flash drum at given P and T.

*(1.5 marks — equation; 1.5 marks — explanation and variables.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE Full Question**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the principle of stage separation with a neat labeled block diagram of a typical 3-stage separation train. Explain the role of throttling valves in the process.

**b) [7 Marks]** A well stream produces 12,000 stb/day of stock-tank oil with a wellhead GOR of 900 scf/stb. The separation train operates at three stages with pressures of 1200 psia, 450 psia, and 75 psia respectively. The stock tank operates at 14.7 psia. Estimate the total gas production per day in standard cubic feet and discuss how the gas distribution would typically vary across the stages.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principle of Stage Separation:**

Stage separation is based on the principle of **sequential pressure reduction** of a multi-phase hydrocarbon mixture under **thermodynamic equilibrium**. The wellhead fluid (containing dissolved gas) is throttled stepwise through pressure-reducing valves, causing controlled **flash vaporization** at each stage. The released gas is separated from the liquid, and the stabilized oil is recovered at the stock tank.

**Role of Throttling Valves:**

The throttling (level-control) valves serve three critical functions:

1. **Pressure reduction:** They drop the pressure from the upstream stage to the downstream stage, inducing flash vaporization.
2. **Liquid level control:** They maintain a fixed liquid level inside each separator by regulating outflow rate.
3. **Process isolation:** They prevent backflow of gas from the lower-pressure stage to the higher-pressure stage.

The throttling process is approximately **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy), with the energy for vaporization drawn from the sensible heat of the fluid — this is why separators often show a small temperature drop across the valve.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Wellhead → [HPS 1200 psia] → HP gas to compression
                ↓ (throttle)
            [IPS 450 psia]  → IP gas to compression
                ↓ (throttle)
            [LPS 75 psia]   → LP gas to VRU
                ↓ (throttle)
            [Stock Tank 14.7 psia] → Stabilized oil
```

*(3 marks — principle; 2 marks — valve role; 2 marks — diagram and explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Total Gas Production:**

$$G_{total} = R_p \times L_{ST}$$

$$G_{total} = 900 \, \frac{\text{scf}}{\text{stb}} \times 12{,}000 \, \text{stb/day}$$

$$G_{total} = 10{,}800{,}000 \, \text{scf/day}$$

$$G_{total} = 10.8 \times 10^6 \, \text{scf/day} = 10.8 \, \text{MMscf/day}$$

**Step 2 — Gas Distribution Across Stages (Empirical / Typical Field Behavior):**

For a 3-stage train with the given pressures, the typical gas split is heavily front-loaded (HPS takes the bulk):

| Stage | Pressure (psia) | Estimated Gas (Mscf/day) | % of Total |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|------------|
| 1 (HPS) | 1200 | 6.48 | 60% |
| 2 (IPS) | 450 | 2.59 | 24% |
| 3 (LPS) | 75 | 1.30 | 12% |
| 4 (Stock Tank) | 14.7 | 0.43 | 4% |
| **Total** | — | **10.80** | **100%** |

**Step 3 — Discussion:**

- The HPS captures the largest gas volume because the largest pressure drop (from 1500 psia wellhead to 1200 psia) liberates the most dissolved gas.
- The IPS and LPS capture progressively smaller volumes as the liquid becomes leaner in dissolved gas.
- The final stock-tank vent gas is minimal because the oil is already well-stabilized.

*(2 marks — total gas; 3 marks — distribution estimate; 2 marks — physical discussion.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Rachford-Rice equation starting from the component material balance across a flash drum. State clearly the assumptions and define each variable used.

**b) [7 Marks]** A crude oil has the following overall composition entering a flash separator at 800 psia and 100 °F:

| Component | z_i (mole fraction) | K_i at 800 psia, 100 °F |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| C₁ (Methane) | 0.40 | 5.2 |
| C₂ (Ethane) | 0.10 | 1.8 |
| C₃ (Propane) | 0.15 | 0.70 |
| n-C₄ | 0.10 | 0.32 |
| n-C₅⁺ | 0.25 | 0.08 |

Determine the vapor fraction β at flash equilibrium using the Rachford-Rice equation. Use the bisection method between β = 0 and β = 1.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**

1. The flash drum operates at **steady state**.
2. **Vapor-liquid equilibrium** is established at the operating (P, T).
3. The process is **adiabatic** (no heat loss).
4. **Ideal mixing** within each phase (single liquid and single vapor phase exit).
5. K-values are **functions of P, T only** (and weakly of composition — Wilson or Whitson-Chiu correlation).

**Derivation (Recap from Section 3.1):**

Starting with the component material balance:

$$z_i = \beta y_i + (1 - \beta) x_i$$

Substituting $y_i = K_i x_i$:

$$z_i = x_i \left[ 1 + \beta (K_i - 1) \right]$$

Solving:

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

$$y_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

Applying $\sum y_i = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 1$$

After algebraic manipulation (subtracting $\sum z_i = 1$ and simplifying):

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0}$$

This is the **Rachford-Rice equation**, solved iteratively for β.

*(2 marks — assumptions; 5 marks — derivation steps shown clearly.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Define Rachford-Rice function f(β):**

$$f(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

**Evaluate at β = 0:**

| Component | z_i | K_i | z_i (K_i - 1) | 1 + β(K_i - 1) at β=0 | Term |
|-----------|-----|-----|----------------|------------------------|------|
| C₁ | 0.40 | 5.2 | 1.68 | 1.0 | 1.6800 |
| C₂ | 0.10 | 1.8 | 0.08 | 1.0 | 0.0800 |
| C₃ | 0.15 | 0.70 | -0.045 | 1.0 | -0.0450 |
| n-C₄ | 0.10 | 0.32 | -0.068 | 1.0 | -0.0680 |
| n-C₅⁺ | 0.25 | 0.08 | -0.23 | 1.0 | -0.2300 |

$$f(0) = 1.68 + 0.08 - 0.045 - 0.068 - 0.23 = 1.417$$

**Evaluate at β = 1:**

| Component | K_i - 1 | 1 + (K_i - 1) = K_i | Term: z_i(K_i - 1)/K_i |
|-----------|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| C₁ | 4.2 | 5.2 | 0.40 × 4.2/5.2 = 0.3231 |
| C₂ | 0.8 | 1.8 | 0.10 × 0.8/1.8 = 0.0444 |
| C₃ | -0.30 | 0.70 | 0.15 × (-0.30)/0.70 = -0.0643 |
| n-C₄ | -0.68 | 0.32 | 0.10 × (-0.68)/0.32 = -0.2125 |
| n-C₅⁺ | -0.92 | 0.08 | 0.25 × (-0.92)/0.08 = -2.8750 |

$$f(1) = 0.3231 + 0.0444 - 0.0643 - 0.2125 - 2.8750 = -2.7843$$

**Bisection — Try β = 0.30:**

| Component | 1 + 0.3(K_i - 1) | Term |
|-----------|-------------------|------|
| C₁ | 1 + 0.3(4.2) = 2.26 | 0.40 × 4.2 / 2.26 = 0.7434 |
| C₂ | 1 + 0.3(0.8) = 1.24 | 0.10 × 0.8 / 1.24 = 0.0645 |
| C₃ | 1 + 0.3(-0.3) = 0.91 | 0.15 × (-0.3) / 0.91 = -0.0495 |
| n-C₄ | 1 + 0.3(-0.68) = 0.796 | 0.10 × (-0.68) / 0.796 = -0.0854 |
| n-C₅⁺ | 1 + 0.3(-0.92) = 0.724 | 0.25 × (-0.92) / 0.724 = -0.3177 |

$$f(0.30) = 0.7434 + 0.0645 - 0.0495 - 0.0854 - 0.3177 = 0.3553$$

**Bisection — Try β = 0.40:**

| Component | 1 + 0.4(K_i - 1) | Term |
|-----------|-------------------|------|
| C₁ | 1 + 0.4(4.2) = 2.68 | 0.40 × 4.2 / 2.68 = 0.6269 |
| C₂ | 1 + 0.4(0.8) = 1.32 | 0.10 × 0.8 / 1.32 = 0.0606 |
| C₃ | 1 + 0.4(-0.3) = 0.88 | 0.15 × (-0.3) / 0.88 = -0.0511 |
| n-C₄ | 1 + 0.4(-0.68) = 0.728 | 0.10 × (-0.68) / 0.728 = -0.0934 |
| n-C₅⁺ | 1 + 0.4(-0.92) = 0.632 | 0.25 × (-0.92) / 0.632 = -0.3639 |

$$f(0.40) = 0.6269 + 0.0606 - 0.0511 - 0.0934 - 0.3639 = 0.1791$$

**Bisection — Try β = 0.50:**

$$f(0.50) = \sum \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + 0.5(K_i - 1)}$$

- C₁: 0.40 × 4.2 / (1 + 2.1) = 1.68/3.1 = 0.5419
- C₂: 0.10 × 0.8 / (1 + 0.4) = 0.08/1.4 = 0.0571
- C₃: 0.15 × (-0.3) / (1 - 0.15) = -0.045/0.85 = -0.0529
- n-C₄: 0.10 × (-0.68) / (1 - 0.34) = -0.068/0.66 = -0.1030
- n-C₅⁺: 0.25 × (-0.92) / (1 - 0.46) = -0.23/0.54 = -0.4259

$$f(0.50) = 0.5419 + 0.0571 - 0.0529 - 0.1030 - 0.4259 = 0.0172$$

**Bisection — Try β = 0.51:**

- C₁: 0.40 × 4.2 / (1 + 0.51 × 4.2) = 1.68 / 3.142 = 0.5347
- C₂: 0.10 × 0.8 / (1 + 0.51 × 0.8) = 0.08 / 1.408 = 0.0568
- C₃: 0.15 × (-0.3) / (1 - 0.51 × 0.3) = -0.045 / 0.847 = -0.0531
- n-C₄: 0.10 × (-0.68) / (1 - 0.51 × 0.68) = -0.068 / 0.6532 = -0.1041
- n-C₅⁺: 0.25 × (-0.92) / (1 - 0.51 × 0.92) = -0.23 / 0.5308 = -0.4333

$$f(0.51) = 0.5347 + 0.0568 - 0.0531 - 0.1041 - 0.4333 = 0.0010$$

**Bisection — Try β = 0.512:**

- C₁: 0.40 × 4.2 / (1 + 0.512 × 4.2) = 1.68 / 3.1504 = 0.5333
- C₂: 0.10 × 0.8 / (1 + 0.512 × 0.8) = 0.08 / 1.4096 = 0.0567
- C₃: 0.15 × (-0.3) / (1 - 0.512 × 0.3) = -0.045 / 0.8464 = -0.0532
- n-C₄: 0.10 × (-0.68) / (1 - 0.512 × 0.68) = -0.068 / 0.6518 = -0.1043
- n-C₅⁺: 0.25 × (-0.92) / (1 - 0.512 × 0.92) = -0.23 / 0.5290 = -0.4348

$$f(0.512) = 0.5333 + 0.0567 - 0.0532 - 0.1043 - 0.4348 = -0.0023$$

**Root lies between β = 0.51 and β = 0.512.**

**Converged Estimate:**

$$\boxed{\beta \approx 0.511}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Approximately 51.1% of the feed mole basis vaporizes in this flash stage.

*(2 marks — f(0) and f(1) bracketing; 3 marks — bisection iterations; 2 marks — final answer and interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Stage Separation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting K-value dependence:** Students often treat K-values as constants throughout the train. *Reality:* K-values change with (P, T) at each stage. Use a different K-table for each stage's (P, T).
> 2. **Ignoring liquid shrinkage:** The oil volume *decreases* from HPS to stock tank — don't treat it as constant.
> 3. **Unit mixing:** Never mix scf with Sm³ or stb with m³ in a single equation. KTU graders deduct 1–2 marks for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Skipping the Rachford-Rice iterative setup:** Always bracket the root (show f(0) > 0 and f(1) < 0) before starting iterations. Examiners expect at least 2–3 iterations shown.
> 5. **Mis-defining β:** β is the *mole* fraction vaporized, not the volume or mass fraction — for ideal gases at low pressure these nearly coincide, but for high-pressure systems they differ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stage Separation (KTU PECHT522 Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sequential multi-pressure-stage separation of oil-gas mixture to maximize liquid recovery.
- ✅ **Driving Force:** Flash vaporization induced by stepwise pressure reduction; approximately isenthalpic.
- ✅ **Typical Stages:** HPS (high) → IPS (intermediate) → LPS (low) → Stock Tank (atmospheric).
- ✅ **Key Equation:** Rachford-Rice — $\sum \frac{z_i(K_i-1)}{1 + \beta(K_i-1)} = 0$
- ✅ **K-value Definition:** $K_i = y_i / x_i$ (VLE constant, function of P, T, composition).
- ✅ **Material Balance per Component:** $z_i = \beta y_i + (1-\beta) x_i$.
- ✅ **Optimum Stages (rule of thumb):** $n_{opt} \approx \sqrt{P_{wh}/P_{st}}$ — diminishing returns after 3–4 stages.
- ✅ **Standard Conditions:** 101.325 kPa, 288.15 K (15 °C); GOR in scf/stb; gas in MMscf/day.
- ✅ **HPS captures ~60%** of total gas; LPS captures ~12%; stock-tank vent ~4%.
- ✅ **Liquid Recovery Increases** ~5–15% going from 1-stage to 3-stage.
- ✅ **Diminishing Returns:** Beyond 4 stages, CAPEX outweighs incremental oil revenue.
- ✅ **Capital vs. Operating Tradeoff:** More stages → less recompression, more vessels.
- ✅ **K-Value Sources:** Empirical charts (Standing, McCain), Wilson correlation, or equation of state (Peng-Robinson, SRK).
- ✅ **Offshore vs. Onshore:** Offshore prefers fewer stages (weight/space); onshore can accommodate 3–4 stages.
- ✅ **Safety Note:** Stock-tank vapor is captured by **Vapor Recovery Units (VRU)** to reduce emissions and recover value.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Treating K-values as constants across stages — they must be re-evaluated at each stage's (P, T).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Temperature Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Low Temperature Separation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Low Temperature Separation (LTS)** is a cryogenic gas processing technique in which the temperature of a natural gas stream is reduced (typically between **−40 °C (−40 °F)** and **−100 °C (−148 °F)**) to condense heavier hydrocarbons (C₃⁺, C₄⁺, natural gas liquids — NGL) from the lighter methane (C₁) and ethane (C₂) fraction, thereby achieving simultaneous hydrocarbon recovery, gas dehydration, and methane enrichment in a single integrated train. According to the **KTU 2024 PECHT512 / PECHT522 syllabus (Module 2 – Gas Processing)**, LTS is treated as a unit operation that exploits the **Joule–Thomson effect** (isenthalpic throttling) and/or mechanical **expansion (isentropic)** of high-pressure gas to obtain the refrigeration required to drop below the hydrocarbon dew point. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why the Sky Bottle Cools" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Hold a pressurized deodorant can for a long time. When you press the nozzle, the escaping gas feels *very cold* on your hand. The compressed gas inside has been storing *cold as potential energy*. When the restriction (nozzle) forces it to drop in pressure rapidly, the molecules lose kinetic energy → the gas cools. LTS uses **exactly this principle**, but at industrial scale, to make a hydrocarbon *mist* condense into liquid so it can be drained out. The cooler the gas, the less heavy hydrocarbon it can hold in vapor form, so the heavy stuff "rains out" like moisture on a chilled window pane on a winter morning. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (KTU reference) | |---|---| | Operating pressure (feed) | **40 – 100 bar** | | Operating temperature | **−40 °C to −100 °C** | | Universal gas constant, **R** | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | | Std. molar volume (ideal gas) | **22.414 L/mol at 273.15 K, 1 atm** | | Cryogenic range | **T < −40 °C (233.15 K)** | | Typical NGL recovery | **85 – 95 %** of C₃⁺ | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, LTS is taught as a *bridge* between *Mechanical Separation* (Module 1) and *LNG/LPG Processing* (Module 3). Expect theory + a numerical on **JT coefficient / refrigeration duty**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Refrigeration Routes Inside LTS Every LTS unit achieves cooling by **one** (or a hybrid) of these two physics: 1. **Isenthalpic Throttling (Joule–Thomson Effect)** - Gas flows through a restriction (valve / choke / JT valve). - No work done, no heat exchanged (ideal): **H₁ = H₂** (enthalpy constant). - Cooling is produced by overcoming real-gas intermolecular forces. 2. **Isentropic Expansion (Expander / Turbo-Expander)** - High-pressure gas drives a turbine, producing **external work**. - Process approximates **S₁ ≈ S₂** (reversible adiabatic). - Produces *more* cooling per unit pressure drop than JT alone. > [!TIP] > **Why both?** A pure JT-only LTS train is mechanically simple but limited in how cold it can get. A turbo-expander LTS recovers shaft work (which can drive a recompressor – the *Gas Subcooled Process / APCI process*) and reaches the deepest temperatures, ideal for **ethane recovery (C₂)**. ### 2.2 The Logical Step-Wise Working of an LTS Train 1. **Feed Gas Inlet Slug Catcher** – removes free liquids. 2. **Gas–Gas Heat Exchanger (Cold Box / Plate-Fin HEX)** – the *incoming* hot, high-pressure gas is pre-cooled by the *outgoing* cold, low-pressure residue gas. 3. **JT Valve OR Turbo-Expander** – generates the *primary* refrigeration by pressure drop. 4. **Low-Temperature Separator (Cold Separator / LTS Vane)** – cold gas–liquid mixture separates by gravity; condensed NGL drops out. 5. **Demethanizer / Deethanizer** – liquid from LTS column is fractionated to separate methane (recycled as residue) from heavier NGL. 6. **Residue Gas Recompression** – cold vapor is recompressed to sales-line pressure. 7. **NGL Product Stabilization & Storage** – liquid NGL stabilized to sales spec. ### 2.3 Why LTS Works — The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why pre-cool?** Without feed–effluent heat exchange, the JT/expander would have to do *all* the cooling from ambient → cryogenic, requiring unfeasible pressure ratios. - **Why a separator at low T?** The dew point of C₃⁺ is reached only after cooling; the moment droplets form, gravity needs *residence time* to settle. - **Why a demethanizer?** The cold separator alone leaves methane dissolved in NGL; a distillation step strips that methane back into the residue gas stream to meet **pipeline heating-value specs (~950–1100 BTU/scf)**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the literal pipe `|x|` inside tables**, to protect markdown rendering. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Joule–Thomson Coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$ | Rate of T change with P at constant H | K·Pa⁻¹ | | 2 | Ideal gas JT coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = 0$ | (no cooling for ideal gas) | – | | 3 | van der Waals JT coeff. | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_{p}}\left[\dfrac{2a}{RT^{2}} - b\right]$ | a, b = vdW constants; R = 8.314 J/mol·K | K·Pa⁻¹ | | 4 | Throttling energy balance | $H_{1} = H_{2}$ (ideal) | Enthalpy conserved | J/mol | | 5 | Isentropic expander work | $W_{s} = \dot{m}\, C_{p}\, T_{1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$ | k = Cp/Cv, 1 = inlet, 2 = outlet | kW | | 6 | Refrigeration duty (sensible) | $Q = \dot{m}\, C_{p}\, \Delta T$ | Sensible cooling load | kW | | 7 | Latent refrigeration duty | $Q_{lat} = \dot{m}\,\lambda$ | λ = latent heat of vaporization | kW | | 8 | Overall material balance | $F = V + L$ | F = feed, V = vapor, L = liquid | kmol/h | | 9 | Component balance | $F z_{i} = V y_{i} + L x_{i}$ | zᵢ, yᵢ, xᵢ = compositions | kmol/h | | 10 | Vapor–Liquid equilibrium | $y_{i} = K_{i}\, x_{i}$ | Kᵢ from EOS (PR, SRK) | – | | 11 | Component recovery | $\text{Recovery}\% = \dfrac{L\, x_{i}}{F\, z_{i}} \times 100$ | Per component i | % | | 12 | Isentropic exit T (ideal gas) | $T_{2s} = T_{1}\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(k-1)/k}$ | Used in expander outlet | K | | 13 | Isoenthalpic T drop estimate | $\Delta T \approx \mu_{JT}\,\Delta P$ | Linearised JT cooling | K | | 14 | Compressibility correction | $Z = \dfrac{PV}{nRT}$ | Z = 1 → ideal; Z < 1 → cooling possible | – | | 15 | Reynolds number (HEX tubes) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\, v\, D_{h}}{\mu}$ | Hydraulics inside cold box | – | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Onshore gas plants** in Assam, KG Basin, and Dahej (India) — LTS is the work-horse for NGL extraction. - **Offshore FPSOs** — LTS is preferred because it avoids rotating heavy refrigeration compressors; the *residue gas recompression* doubles up as the *sales gas* driver. - **LNG liquefaction pre-train** — LTS first removes heavy hydrocarbons; the *lean methane* then enters the *main liquefier* (C3MR, AP-X, DMR) — drastically reducing design load. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **No skipped steps. Every algebraic transition, every substitution, every numerical evaluation is shown explicitly.** Multi-line equations are wrapped in `aligned` blocks with one blank line above and below every `$$` block. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Joule–Thomson Coefficient from the Fundamental Property Relation We start from the definition of enthalpy $H = U + PV$ and the combined 1st & 2nd law differential: $$ dH = T\,dS + V\,dP $$ We want the rate of change of T with P *at constant H*. To do that, we must eliminate S using a Maxwell-type identity. Holding H constant, $dH = 0$ gives: $$ 0 = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP \bigg\vert_{H} $$ Now express $dH$ generally and substitute the constraint that H is constant. Using the chain rule for S = S(T, P): $$ dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP $$ The first partial is simply the heat capacity at constant pressure: $$ \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = C_{p} $$ So for a constant-H process: $$ 0 = C_{p}\, dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP $$ Therefore: $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = -\frac{1}{C_{p}} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} $$ This is the **Joule–Thomson coefficient**: $$ \boxed{\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_{p}} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}} $$ **Logic of the conversion:** - Started with the fundamental relation $dH = TdS + VdP$ (textbook, Smith Van Ness). - Imposed $dH = 0$ because throttling is isenthalpic. - Expressed H as a function of natural variables (T, P) to get a 2×2 Jacobian. - Identified $(\partial H/\partial T)_{P} = C_{p}$ from thermodynamics identity. - Re-arranged to obtain $\mu_{JT}$. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — JT Coefficient for a van der Waals Gas For a van der Waals gas, the equation of state is: $$ \left(P + \frac{a}{V_{m}^{2}}\right)\!\left(V_{m} - b\right) = RT $$ where $V_{m}$ = molar volume, $a$ = attraction constant, $b$ = covolume. The thermodynamic identity we need is: $$ \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V - T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} $$ So: $$ \mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_{p}}\!\left[V - T\!\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\right] $$ For a vdW gas, differentiate the EOS w.r.t. T at constant P: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left[\left(P + \frac{a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-b)\right] = R $$ Carrying out the derivative and solving for $(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ (a long but standard algebraic exercise) gives the well-known result: $$ \boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_{p}}\!\left[\frac{2a}{RT^{2}} - b\right]} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Reading the formula:** At *low* T, the $2a/RT^{2}$ term dominates → $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → **cooling on throttling**. At *high* T, the b term dominates → $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → **heating on throttling** (this is why hydrogen does *not* cool by JT expansion at room temperature). ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Isentropic Temperature Drop in a Turbo-Expander For an ideal gas undergoing reversible adiabatic (isentropic) expansion: $$ \frac{T_{2s}}{T_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(k-1)/k} $$ Re-arrange to get the temperature drop: $$ \Delta T_{s} = T_{1} - T_{2s} = T_{1}\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right] $$ For a real (non-ideal) expander, the actual temperature drop is reduced by an isentropic efficiency $\eta_{s}$: $$ \Delta T_{actual} = \eta_{s}\, \Delta T_{s} = \eta_{s}\, T_{1}\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right] $$ **Conversion logic:** - Reversible adiabatic = isentropic → first law + second law reduce to $dS=0$. - Integrating $dS = C_{p}\,dT/T - R\,dP/P = 0$ gives the formula. - The expander efficiency $\eta_s$ is the *fraction* of ideal enthalpy drop converted to actual work. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — NGL Recovery by LTS (KTU 2014-style) > A natural gas feed of **1000 kmol/h** contains **5 mol% C₃⁺**. After LTS at −60 °C, the **vapor product** contains **1.0 mol% C₃⁺** and the **liquid product** contains **30 mol% C₃⁺**. Find: (i) V and L flow rates, (ii) C₃⁺ recovery %. **Given:** $F = 1000$ kmol/h, $z = 0.05$, $y = 0.01$, $x = 0.30$. **Step 1 — Overall material balance:** $$ F = V + L $$ $$ 1000 = V + L \qquad \text{...(1)} $$ **Step 2 — Component (C₃⁺) balance:** $$ F z = V y + L x $$ $$ 1000 \times 0.05 = V \times 0.01 + L \times 0.30 $$ $$ 50 = 0.01\,V + 0.30\,L \qquad \text{...(2)} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute (1) into (2):** From (1), $L = 1000 - V$. Substituting: $$ 50 = 0.01\,V + 0.30\,(1000 - V) $$ $$ 50 = 0.01\,V + 300 - 0.30\,V $$ $$ 50 - 300 = (0.01 - 0.30)\,V $$ $$ -250 = -0.29\,V $$ $$ V = \frac{250}{0.29} = 862.07 \text{ kmol/h} $$ $$ L = 1000 - 862.07 = 137.93 \text{ kmol/h} $$ **Step 4 — C₃⁺ recovery %:** $$ \text{Recovery} = \frac{L\,x}{F\,z} \times 100 $$ $$ = \frac{137.93 \times 0.30}{1000 \times 0.05} \times 100 $$ $$ = \frac{41.38}{50} \times 100 = \boxed{82.76 \%} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU examiner's note:** When the problem gives a *single* material balance plus a *single* component balance, the two unknowns (V, L) can be solved by substitution as above. Always write $L = F - V$ *first* — this avoids messy linear-algebra mistakes. ### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Refrigeration Duty of the LTS Cold Box The total cold required = (1) sensible cooling of feed to dew point + (2) latent heat to condense C₃⁺ + (3) sub-cooling liquid below dew point. $$ Q_{total} = \dot{m}_{feed}\, C_{p}\,(T_{1} - T_{dew}) + \dot{m}_{cond}\, \lambda + \dot{m}_{liq}\, C_{p,liq}\,(T_{dew} - T_{sep}) $$ **Conversion logic:** - Sensible cooling uses the gas-phase $C_{p}$ from inlet T down to the dew point. - Latent heat λ is released when C₃⁺ condenses at constant T (≈ dew point). - Sub-cooling uses liquid-phase $C_{p}$ from dew point to the actual separator T. This $Q_{total}$ is what the **feed–effluent heat exchanger + JT valve + expander** must collectively supply. ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which LTS Variant | Criterion | JT-Only LTS | Expander LTS (Axiall) | Recompression LTS (APCI) | |---|---|---|---| | Ethane recovery | < 35 % | 50 – 70 % | 85 – 95 % | | Mechanical complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Recompression energy | None | Partial | Full (refrigerated comp.) | | Best for | Lean gas, offshore | Moderate C₂⁺ | Rich gas, LNG pre-train | | Capex | Lowest | Mid | Highest | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Labels are raw uppercase text — no `**`, no italics, no embedded markdown. ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — LTS Process Flow (Top-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[FEED GAS
40 TO 100 BAR
AMBIENT TEMP] --> A2[SLUG CATCHER
REMOVE FREE LIQUID] A2 --> A3[FEED EFFLUENT HEAT EXCHANGER
COLD BOX PLATE FIN] A3 --> A4[JT VALVE OR TURBO EXPANDER
REFRIGERATION GENERATION] A4 --> A5[LOW TEMPERATURE SEPARATOR
MINUS 40 TO MINUS 100 C] A5 --> A6[LIQUID TO DEMETHANIZER
