C2 PLUS C3 PLUS NGL RECOVERY] A5 --> A7[RESIDUE GAS COLD VAPOR
RICH IN METHANE] A7 --> A3 A3 --> A8[RESIDUE GAS RECOMPRESSOR
SALES LINE PRESSURE] A8 --> A9[PIPELINE SALES GAS
950 TO 1100 BTU PER SCF] A6 --> A10[STABILIZED NGL PRODUCT
LPG PLUS CONDENSATE] A10 --> A11[STORAGE AND EXPORT TERMINAL] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Material & Energy Streams) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT_BLOCK B1[HIGH PRESSURE FEED
F KMH ZI COMPOSITION] end subgraph REFRIGERATION_BLOCK B2[HEX COLD BOX
HEAT EXCHANGE Q KW] B3[EXPANDER OR JT
WORK W KW] end subgraph SEPARATION_BLOCK B4[FLASH SEPARATOR
AT T AND P] B5[FRACTIONATION TOWER
DEMETHANIZER] end subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK B6[RESIDUE GAS
V KMH YI] B7[NGL PRODUCT
L KMH XI] end B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B4 --> B6 B5 --> B7 B6 -. recycle cold .-> B2 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Refrigeration Decision Tree (Schematic of Choose-Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD C1[START FEED ANALYSIS] --> C2{C3 PLUS CONTENT} C2 --> C3[LT 1.5 GAL PER MCF] --> C4[JT ONLY LTS] C2 --> C5[1.5 TO 3 GAL PER MCF] --> C6[EXPANDER LTS] C2 --> C7[GT 3 GAL PER MCF] --> C8[RECOMPRESSION LTS] C4 --> C9[ETHANE RECOVERY LT 35 PERCENT] C6 --> C10[ETHANE RECOVERY 50 TO 70 PERCENT] C8 --> C11[ETHANE RECOVERY 85 TO 95 PERCENT] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q.1(a)]** **Q1. Define the Joule–Thomson coefficient. State its value for an ideal gas and explain the physical significance of a positive and negative coefficient.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > The **Joule–Thomson (JT) coefficient** is defined as the rate of change of temperature with pressure at constant enthalpy during a throttling process: > > $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$ > > - For an **ideal gas**, $\mu_{JT} = 0$ because there are no intermolecular forces and the gas does not change temperature on throttling. **(1 mark)** > - **Positive $\mu_{JT}$** → temperature *decreases* as pressure drops → **cooling** (used in LTS, LNG liquefaction). **(1 mark)** > - **Negative $\mu_{JT}$** → temperature *increases* as pressure drops → **heating** (e.g., hydrogen and helium at room temperature). **(1 mark)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q.1(b)]** **Q2. List the major process equipment that constitute a typical Low Temperature Separation (LTS) unit. State the function of each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **Slug Catcher** – removes intermittent liquid slugs from the feed gas. **(0.5 mark)** > 2. **Feed–Effluent Heat Exchanger (Cold Box)** – pre-cools the feed using cold residue gas. **(0.5 mark)** > 3. **JT Valve / Turbo-Expander** – generates the refrigeration by isenthalpic / isentropic pressure drop. **(0.5 mark)** > 4. **Low-Temperature Separator (Cold Vane)** – separates condensed NGL from methane-rich vapor. **(0.5 mark)** > 5. **Demethanizer / Deethanizer Column** – fractionates liquid to recover NGL and recycle methane. **(0.5 mark)** > 6. **Residue Gas Recompressor** – boosts cold vapor to sales-line pressure. **(0.5 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q.6]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Joule–Thomson coefficient** in terms of the heat capacity at constant pressure and the partial derivative of enthalpy with respect to pressure at constant temperature. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A natural gas stream at **60 bar** and **35 °C** is throttled to **10 bar** through a JT valve. The Joule–Thomson coefficient of the gas (assumed constant in this range) is **+0.40 °C/bar**. Calculate the temperature immediately downstream of the valve, and comment on whether the resulting temperature is sufficient to condense propane (normal boiling point ≈ −42 °C) at 10 bar. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Derivation (7 marks):** 1. Start from $dH = TdS + VdP$ — **(1 mark)** 2. For constant H, $0 = TdS + VdP$ → $dS = -V/T\,dP$ — **(1 mark)** 3. Express H = H(T,P): $dH = (\partial H/\partial T)_P dT + (\partial H/\partial P)_T dP = C_p\,dT + (\partial H/\partial P)_T\,dP$ — **(2 marks)** 4. Set dH = 0, solve for $(\partial T/\partial P)_H$ — **(2 marks)** 5. Final boxed result: $$ \mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_{p}} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} $$ **(b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $P_1 = 60$ bar, $T_1 = 35$ °C, $P_2 = 10$ bar, $\mu_{JT} = +0.40$ °C/bar. $$ \Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P = 0.40 \times (10 - 60) = 0.40 \times (-50) = -20\ ^\circ C $$ $$ T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 35 + (-20) = \boxed{15\ ^\circ C} $$ > **Comment (2 marks):** The downstream temperature (15 °C) is **far above** the boiling point of propane at 10 bar (which is in the range of 0 °C to −20 °C at 10 bar depending on mixture). Therefore, **JT throttling alone at this pressure drop is NOT sufficient** to condense propane. In practice, LTS uses either (i) a turbo-expander, (ii) much higher pressure drop, or (iii) prior pre-cooling + multi-stage JT to reach −40 °C and below. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With a neat block flow diagram, describe the working of a **turbo-expander based LTS unit** for natural gas processing. Clearly identify the functions of each equipment. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A feed of **5000 kg/h** of methane-rich natural gas ($C_p \approx 2.2$ kJ/kg·K) is to be cooled from **40 °C to −60 °C** in a feed–effluent heat exchanger prior to LTS separation. Estimate the **sensible refrigeration duty** required. If 40 % of this duty is to be supplied by a JT throttle and the rest by a turbo-expander (assumed isentropic, k = 1.30, η_s = 0.80, pressure drop from 50 to 8 bar, inlet at −20 °C), check whether the expander can deliver the remaining 60 % duty. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Block Diagram & Description (7 marks):** The block flow diagram (refer Section 4.1 mermaid) shows the following sequence with key functions: 1. **Feed Slug Catcher** — protects downstream rotating equipment from liquid slugs. 2. **Molecular Sieve Dehydration Unit (upstream of cold box)** — prevents hydrate/ice formation below 0 °C. 3. **Feed–Effluent Plate-Fin Heat Exchanger (Cold Box)** — cools feed against cold residue. 4. **Turbo-Expander** — provides the bulk of refrigeration (isentropic expansion of lean gas stream from high P to low P). The shaft work is recovered to drive the **residue gas recompressor** (a brilliant energy-integration trick). 5. **Cold Separator (LTS Vane)** — separates liquid NGL. 6. **Demethanizer** — strips CH₄ from NGL. 7. **Recompressor** — boosts cold residue gas to sales-line pressure. 8. **Sales Gas Cooler** — final trim cooling. > A clearly labelled schematic (2 marks) + stepwise working of each block (5 marks) = 7 marks. **(b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Sensible refrigeration duty (1 mark):** $$ Q = \dot{m}\, C_{p}\, \Delta T = 5000 \times 2.2 \times (40 - (-60)) = 5000 \times 2.2 \times 100 $$ $$ Q = 1{,}100{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{1{,}100{,}000}{3600} \approx 305.56 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — JT duty share (40 %):** $$ Q_{JT} = 0.40 \times 305.56 = 122.22 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Expander required duty (60 %):** $$ Q_{exp} = 0.60 \times 305.56 = 183.33 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4 — Isentropic temperature drop in expander (3 marks):** $$ \Delta T_s = T_1\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right] $$ With $T_1 = -20 + 273 = 253$ K, $P_1 = 50$ bar, $P_2 = 8$ bar, $k = 1.30$: $$ \frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{0.30}{1.30} = 0.2308 $$ $$ \left(\frac{8}{50}\right)^{0.2308} = (0.16)^{0.2308} $$ $$ \ln(0.16) = -1.8326; \quad -1.8326 \times 0.2308 = -0.4230; \quad e^{-0.4230} = 0.6551 $$ $$ \Delta T_s = 253 \times (1 - 0.6551) = 253 \times 0.3449 = 87.26 \text{ K} $$ **Actual drop with efficiency:** $$ \Delta T_{actual} = \eta_s \times \Delta T_s = 0.80 \times 87.26 = 69.81 \text{ K} $$ **Step 5 — Expander cooling capacity (1 mark):** Mass flow through expander (assumed 50 % of feed, typical split): $$ \dot{m}_{exp} = 2500 \text{ kg/h} = 0.694 \text{ kg/s} $$ $$ Q_{exp,\,provided} = \dot{m}_{exp}\, C_{p}\, \Delta T_{actual} = 0.694 \times 2.2 \times 69.81 $$ $$ = 106.6 \text{ kW} $$ > **Conclusion (1 mark):** The expander provides only ~107 kW of refrigeration, but the required share is **183.33 kW**. Therefore, **the expander alone (with the given flow split and pressure drop) is INSUFFICIENT**. To meet the 60 % duty share, the engineer must either (i) increase expander flow split, (ii) raise inlet pressure, or (iii) lower outlet pressure further (e.g., to 5 bar with recompression). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error in JT** — many students write $\Delta T = \mu_{JT}(P_1 - P_2)$ instead of $\mu_{JT}(P_2 - P_1)$. Always remember throttling is *pressure-drop*, so $P_2 < P_1$ and $\Delta P$ is *negative* for cooling. > 2. **Forgetting $\eta_s$** — for turbo-expanders, the *actual* temperature drop is **less** than the isentropic drop. Omitting $\eta_s$ yields an *over-sized* design → lose 2 marks. > 3. **Mis-assignment of compositions in component balance** — students often write $F y = V y + L x$ (typo of z with y). Mark the *feed* composition as $z_i$ and *vapor* as $y_i$ rigorously. > 4. **Units of $\mu_{JT}$** — must be consistent with $P$ in bar *or* Pa; mixing °C/bar with Pa drops marks. > 5. **Missing cold-box material** — in KTU viva, students often forget that LTS cold boxes use **aluminium plate-fin** (high conductivity, low mass) or **stainless steel** shells. Lose 1 mark if not mentioned. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Memorize before exam.** - **LTS** = cryogenic process (T < −40 °C) for NGL recovery + dehydration + methane enrichment in *one train*. - **Core physics:** throttling is **isenthalpic** ($H_1 = H_2$); expander is **isentropic** ($S_1 \approx S_2$). - **JT coefficient formula:** $\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_p}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\!\left[\dfrac{2a}{RT^2} - b\right]$ for vdW gas. - **Ideal gas:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ (no cooling — this is a favourite KTU conceptual question). - **Temperature drop by JT:** $\Delta T = \mu_{JT}\, \Delta P$ (with $\Delta P$ negative for cooling). - **Isentropic expander drop:** $\Delta T_s = T_1\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right]$; multiply by $\eta_s$ for actual. - **Material balance equations (F-V-L triangle):** - Total: $F = V + L$ - Component $i$: $F z_i = V y_i + L x_i$ - VLE: $y_i = K_i x_i$ - **Recovery %:** $\dfrac{L x_i}{F z_i} \times 100$. - **Refrigeration duty:** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (sensible) + $\dot{m} \lambda$ (latent). - **Major equipment (in order):** Slug Catcher → Dehydrator → Cold Box (HEX) → JT/Expander → Cold Separator → Demethanizer → Recompressor. - **JT-only LTS** gives <35 % C₂ recovery; **Expander LTS** 50–70 %; **Recompression LTS** 85–95 %. - **Cold box material:** aluminium plate-fin (lightweight, high heat-transfer area per m³). - **Operating envelope:** $P_{feed} = 40$–$100$ bar; $T_{sep} = -40$ to $-100$ °C; $C_{3+}$ recovery typically **82–95 %**. - **Real-world units:** ONGC Hazira, Reliance KG-D6, Dahej LNG (India); Gassi Touil (Algeria); Snøhvit (Norway). - **Viva-ready terms:** Joule–Thomson inversion curve, hydrate suppression, demethanizer reflux ratio, residue gas heating value (BTU/scf), mole-sieve dehydration, NGL stabilization, isentropic efficiency, LNG pre-train. - **Numerical traps:** (i) $\mu_{JT}$ is positive at low T, negative at high T (the *inversion temperature* separates them); (ii) expander duty must be matched with recompression power for energy balance; (iii) always include cold-box $\Delta T$ approach (typically 5–10 °C). ---

topics.title: Water Content of Natural Gas Streams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water Content of Natural Gas Streams --- # Module 3 — Water Content of Natural Gas Streams ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Water content of natural gas** (also called **moisture content** or **water dew point**) is defined as the mass of water vapor present per unit volume of natural gas under specified conditions of **pressure** and **temperature**. It quantifies the absolute amount of water (typically expressed in **lb water per MMscf** of gas, or in **parts per million by volume (ppmv)**, or in **pounds of water per million standard cubic feet (lb/MMscf)**) that the gas stream carries in its vapor phase. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$W = \frac{m_{H_2O}}{V_{gas}}$$ where $m_{H_2O}$ is the mass of water vapor and $V_{gas}$ is the volume of dry gas under standard conditions (**14.7 psia** and **60 °F / 520 °R**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Water content determination is a *core* topic because uncontrolled water in natural gas causes **hydrate formation**, **corrosion in pipelines**, **two-phase flow problems**, and **catalyst poisoning in downstream processing**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Invisible Passenger on a Bus"** > Imagine a passenger bus traveling from a humid coastal city to a cold hill station. Even though all visible seats are occupied, the bus air contains invisible water vapor (humidity). As the bus climbs higher (lower pressure) and the air gets colder, this moisture starts condensing on the windows — exactly like water dropping out of natural gas in cold pipelines. In natural gas engineering, we must "kick the invisible passenger out" (dehydrate the gas) before it causes problems like ice-like hydrate plugs that can completely block pipelines. ### 1.3 Why Water Content Matters — Engineering Significance The presence of water in natural gas is problematic for several reasons: | Issue | Mechanism | Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Gas Hydrate Formation** | Water + light hydrocarbons + low T/high P → crystalline solids | Pipeline blockage, safety hazard | | **Corrosion** | Water + CO₂ / H₂S → carbonic/sulfuric acid | Pipe wall thinning, leaks | | **Two-Phase Flow** | Condensed water + gas | Pressure drop, slug flow, erosion | | **Catalyst Poisoning** | Water in feed to gas processing units | Deactivation of catalysts in amine/methanol units | | **Measurement Errors** | Water affects gas chromatographs and orifice meters | Incorrect billing and accounting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Industry Specification:** Sales-quality natural gas is typically specified to have a water content of **less than 7 lb water per MMscf** (≈ 112 ppmv), corresponding to a water dew point of **−10 °C (14 °F)** at **1000 psia** (per GPSA / company pipeline tariff standards). ### 1.4 Sources of Water in Natural Gas - **Reservoir formation water** co-produced with the gas. - **Condensed water vapor** that was originally in the gas phase at reservoir conditions. - **Injection water** used in reservoir pressure maintenance. - **Treatment chemicals and completion fluids** that may contain water. - **Atmospheric moisture** picked up in gathering systems (especially in low-pressure, low-temperature pipelines). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Theoretical Basis of Water Content The equilibrium water content of natural gas depends on three primary variables: 1. **Pressure** of the gas stream. 2. **Temperature** of the gas stream. 3. **Composition** of the gas (presence of acid gases H₂S, CO₂, and heavier hydrocarbons increases water content; sweet dry gas has lower water content). At thermodynamic equilibrium, the partial pressure of water vapor in the gas equals the vapor pressure of pure water at the system temperature (modified by a fugacity correction and the Poynting effect due to total pressure). The fundamental relationship is: $$y_w = \frac{p_w^{sat}(T)}{P} \cdot \phi_w^{corr}$$ where: - $y_w$ = mole fraction of water in gas - $p_w^{sat}(T)$ = saturation vapor pressure of pure water at temperature $T$ - $P$ = total system pressure - $\phi_w^{corr}$ = fugacity / Poynting correction factor (≈ 1 at low pressure, significant at high pressure) ### 2.2 Key Correlations for Water Content #### (a) McKetta – Wehe Correlation (1958, GPSA standard) This empirical chart-based correlation provides the water content of a lean sweet natural gas (0.6 specific gravity) as a function of pressure and temperature. It is the most commonly used chart in the natural gas industry. **Reading approach:** 1. Enter the chart at the operating pressure (psia). 2. Move vertically to the operating temperature (°F). 3. Read off the water content in **lb water / MMscf** of gas. > [!NOTE] > **Correction for Gas Gravity and Acid Gases:** The McKetta–Wehe chart value must be corrected if the gas is sour or has a different gravity. The corrected water content is multiplied by correction factors: $C_{SG}$ (specific gravity) and $C_{acid}$ (acid gas content). #### (b) Bukacek Correlation (1983) A more rigorous, analytical correlation developed from the McKetta–Wehe chart data: $$W = \frac{1.0 \times 10^6 \cdot M_w \cdot p_w^{sat}(T)}{M_{air} \cdot P \cdot Z_{mix}}$$ where: - $W$ = water content in **lb water / MMscf** - $M_w = 18.015$ lb/lbmol (molecular weight of water) - $M_{air} = 28.97$ lb/lbmol - $p_w^{sat}(T)$ = vapor pressure of water at temperature $T$ (psia) - $P$ = total pressure (psia) - $Z_{mix}$ = compressibility factor of the gas mixture The water vapor pressure $p_w^{sat}(T)$ is obtained from steam tables or from the Antoine-type equation: $$\log_{10}(p_w^{sat}) = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$$ For water (with $p_w^{sat}$ in mmHg and $T$ in °C): $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$ (valid 1–100 °C). #### (c) Buck's Equation (1981) — for low-pressure water vapor $$p_w^{sat}(\text{psia}) = 0.00456 \cdot \exp\left(18.3036 - \frac{4111.357}{T_{Rankine}}\right)$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Water content (definition) | $W = m_{H_2O}/V_{gas}$ | lb / MMscf | | 2 | Mole fraction water | $y_w = p_w^{sat}(T) / (P \cdot \phi_w)$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Bukacek water content | $W = \dfrac{10^6 \, M_w \, p_w^{sat}}{M_{air} \, P \, Z_{mix}}$ | lb / MMscf | | 4 | Buck's vapor pressure | $p_w^{sat} = 0.00456 \, e^{18.3036 - 4111.357/T_R}$ | psia, $T_R$ in °R | | 5 | Antoine vapor pressure | $\log_{10} p_w^{sat} = A - B/(T + C)$ | mmHg, $T$ in °C | | 6 | Hydrate formation (rough) | $T_{hyd} \approx 12\,^{\circ}\text{C at 500 psia}$ | Decreases with inhibitors | | 7 | Gas gravity correction | $C_{SG} = 1.0$ (for SG=0.6); | dimensionless | | 8 | Standard condition (US) | 14.696 psia, 60 °F | Reference state | | 9 | Dehydration dew point target | $W \leq 7$ lb/MMscf → $-10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 1000 psia | Pipeline spec | | 10 | TEG circulation rule | 3 gal TEG / lb water removed | Design heuristic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to compute water content, *always* show the units conversion explicitly. KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for the units and standard-condition step alone. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pipeline design:** Required to size dehydration units and select operating pressures. - **Process simulation:** Input parameter to HYSYS / UniSim / ProMax models. - **Sales-gas contract compliance:** Gas must meet the water dew point specification. - **Hydrate prediction:** Water content is a critical input to hydrate equilibrium curves. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Bukacek Equation from First Principles We start from the **ideal gas relationship** for water vapor in the gas mixture: $$y_w = \frac{n_w}{n_{total}} = \frac{p_w}{P}$$ At equilibrium (assuming ideal behavior of water at low concentrations): $$p_w = p_w^{sat}(T)$$ Therefore the mole fraction of water in the gas is: $$y_w = \frac{p_w^{sat}(T)}{P}$$ The total number of moles of gas per unit volume at standard conditions (using ideal gas law) is: $$n_{total} = \frac{P_{sc} \cdot V_{sc}}{R \cdot T_{sc}}$$ The mass of water per unit standard volume is then: $$m_{H_2O} = n_w \cdot M_w = y_w \cdot n_{total} \cdot M_w$$ $$m_{H_2O} = \frac{p_w^{sat}(T)}{P} \cdot \frac{P_{sc} \cdot V_{sc}}{R \cdot T_{sc}} \cdot M_w$$ For a basis of $V_{sc} = 10^6$ scf (one MMscf), and recognizing that $R \cdot T_{sc} / P_{sc} = V_{m,sc}$ (molar volume at standard conditions, ≈ 379.5 scf/lbmol for ideal gas), and introducing the compressibility correction $Z_{mix}$ for real-gas behavior: $$W = \frac{m_{H_2O}}{V_{sc}} = \frac{10^6 \cdot M_w \cdot p_w^{sat}(T)}{M_{air} \cdot P \cdot Z_{mix}}$$ We used the identity $V_{m,sc} = R T_{sc} / P_{sc} = 379.5$ scf/lbmol and the conversion $1/\text{molar volume in scf/lbmol} = M_{air}/10^6$ when $W$ is in lb/MMscf. **Result — Bukacek Equation:** $$\boxed{\,W = \frac{(10^6) \cdot M_w \cdot p_w^{sat}(T)}{M_{air} \cdot P \cdot Z_{mix}} \quad \text{[lb water / MMscf]}\,}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Example — Bukacek Water Content Calculation **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):** > A natural gas stream leaves a glycol contactor at **1000 psia** and **100 °F** (560 °R). The gas gravity is **0.65** and the compressibility factor is **$Z_{mix} = 0.85$**. Calculate the equilibrium water content of the gas in **lb water / MMscf** using the Bukacek equation. **Step 1 — Find the saturation vapor pressure of water at 100 °F.** Convert temperature: $T = 100\,^{\circ}\text{F} = (100 - 32) \cdot 5/9 = 37.78\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, or directly $T_R = 100 + 459.67 = 559.67\,^{\circ}\text{R}$. Use Buck's equation (since temperature is moderate): $$p_w^{sat} = 0.00456 \cdot \exp\left(18.3036 - \frac{4111.357}{559.67}\right)$$ Compute the exponent term: $$\frac{4111.357}{559.67} = 7.3463$$ $$18.3036 - 7.3463 = 10.9573$$ $$p_w^{sat} = 0.00456 \cdot e^{10.9573} = 0.00456 \cdot 57080 = 260.3 \text{ psia}$$ Alternatively, from steam tables: at 100 °F, $p_w^{sat} \approx 0.9503$ psia. > [!NOTE] > **Note on units:** Buck's equation yields psia directly when the constants are used as given. The 260.3 psia value above is incorrect due to a units mismatch — the proper form is given below. Always cross-check with steam tables. **Corrected step using steam tables:** - At $T = 100\,^{\circ}\text{F}$: $p_w^{sat} = 0.9503$ psia (from steam tables). - At $T = 100\,^{\circ}\text{F}$: Antoine (mmHg, °C): $p_w^{sat} = 10^{8.07131 - 1730.63/(37.78+233.426)} = 49.79$ mmHg $= 0.963$ psia ✓ Use $p_w^{sat} = 0.9503$ psia. **Step 2 — Apply the Bukacek equation.** $$W = \frac{10^6 \cdot (18.015) \cdot (0.9503)}{(28.97) \cdot (1000) \cdot (0.85)}$$ Compute numerator: $$10^6 \cdot 18.015 \cdot 0.9503 = 1.7117 \times 10^7$$ Compute denominator: $$28.97 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.85 = 24624.5$$ $$W = \frac{1.7117 \times 10^7}{24624.5} = 695.1 \text{ lb water / MMscf}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to other units (optional for clarity).** Convert to ppmv (volume basis): $$y_w = \frac{p_w^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.9503}{1000} = 9.503 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$y_w \times 10^6 = 950 \text{ ppmv (mole basis)}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.** > [!IMPORTANT] > This water content (≈ 695 lb/MMscf) is **far above** the pipeline sales specification of 7 lb/MMscf. Therefore, the gas **must be dehydrated** (e.g., using TEG contactor) to reduce water content by ~99%. ### 3.3 Worked Example — Hydrate Formation Temperature Estimation **Problem:** Estimate the hydrate formation temperature for a gas at 1000 psia with a water content of 7 lb/MMscf. Using the Katz / GPSA hydrate correlation (simplified): $$T_{hyd} \approx 60 + 0.1 \cdot P \quad \text{[°F, P in psia, valid roughly 200–2000 psia]}$$ $$T_{hyd} = 60 + 0.1 \cdot 1000 = 160\,^{\circ}\text{F} = 71.1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$ But this is too high for a 0.65 SG sweet gas — the more accurate empirical GPSA equation (Baillie-Wichert): $$T_{hyd}(^{\circ}\text{F}) = 13.47 \cdot \ln(P) - 35.98 \quad \text{(for sweet gas)}$$ $$T_{hyd} = 13.47 \cdot \ln(1000) - 35.98 = 13.47 \cdot 6.908 - 35.98 = 93.07 - 35.98 = 57.1\,^{\circ}\text{F}$$ $$T_{hyd} \approx 13.9\,^{\circ}\text{C at 1000 psia}$$ This means the gas **must not be cooled below 13.9 °C** (or must be dehydrated) to prevent hydrate formation. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Dehydration Methods Comparison | Method | Principle | Typical Water Removed | Best Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Glycol (TEG) Contact** | Liquid desiccant absorbs water | To 7 lb/MMscf | Offshore, onshore gas plants | | **Solid Desiccant (Molecular Sieve)** | Adsorption on 3A/4A zeolite | To < 1 ppmv | Cryogenic, LNG feed | | **Methanol Injection** | Thermodynamic inhibitor | Suppresses hydrate | Cold subsea pipelines | | **Membrane Separation** | Selective permeation | To ~50–100 ppmv | Low-flow, offshore | | **Calcium Chloride** | Dry solid absorbs water | Crude dehydration | Wellhead, low volume | | **Supersonic / JT Cooling** | Pressure drop → condensation | To ~10–20 ppmv | Low-pressure, dew point control | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow — Natural Gas Dehydration Using TEG Contactor ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["WET INLET GAS
1000 PSIA 100 F
High water content"] -->|Flow into bottom| nodeB["ABSORBER CONTACTOR
TEG Packed Tower
Countercurrent contact"] nodeB -->|Lean TEG IN top| nodeC["RICH TEG OUT
Water + TEG mixture"] nodeB -->|Dry gas exits top| nodeD["DRY SALES GAS
1000 PSIA 100 F
7 lb H2O per MMscf"] nodeC -->|Pressure letdown| nodeE["FLASH SEPARATOR
Recover light HC"] nodeE -->|Rich TEG| nodeF["FILTER
Remove solids"] nodeF -->|Filtered TEG| nodeG["REGENERATOR REBOILER
Strip water at 400 F"] nodeG -->|Water vapor vent| nodeH["STACK VENT
Atmospheric water release"] nodeG -->|Hot lean TEG| nodeI["LEAN TEG COOLER
Cool to 180 F"] nodeI -->|Lean TEG to top| nodeC nodeI -.Recycle.-> nodeB ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block — Water Content Determination Workflow ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["STEP 1: OPERATING DATA
Pressure P and Temperature T"] --> nodeB["STEP 2: VAPOR PRESSURE
Use steam tables or Buck eqn
to get p_w_sat at T"] nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3: APPLY CORRELATION
McKetta-Wehe chart

specification met?"} nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG["GAS READY FOR
TRANSPORT"] nodeF -->|No| nodeH["DEHYDRATION
REQUIRED
TEG or Molecular Sieve"] nodeH -.Return to.-> nodeA ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mass Transfer in TEG Contactor ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["WET GAS

Bottom"] -->|Countercurrent| nodeB["TRAY OR
PACKING
SECTION"] nodeB -->|Water absorbed| nodeC["LEAN TEG

Top"] nodeB -->|Dry gas out| nodeD["DRY GAS
OUTLET"] nodeC -.->|Recycle lean TEG| nodeB ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Block — Variables Affecting Water Content ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["WATER CONTENT OF GAS W"] --> nodeB["TEMPERATURE

Higher P = Less water at fixed T"] nodeA --> nodeD["GAS COMPOSITION

Salty water = Less water vapor"] nodeB -.Combined.-> nodeF["USE CORRELATION
McKetta-Wehe
Bukacek
to compute W"] nodeC -.Combined.-> nodeF nodeD -.Combined.-> nodeF nodeE -.Combined.-> nodeF ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define the term "water content of natural gas." State its standard units and the typical sales-pipeline specification. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The water content of natural gas is the mass of water vapor carried per unit volume of gas at standard conditions (**14.7 psia and 60 °F**). It is commonly expressed in **pounds of water per million standard cubic feet (lb/MMscf)** or in **parts per million by volume (ppmv)**. The typical sales-pipeline specification requires the water content to be **less than 7 lb/MMscf**, corresponding to a water dew point of approximately **−10 °C at 1000 psia** (per GPSA standards). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** List any **three** methods for determining the water content of natural gas and state the principle of one of them. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **McKetta–Wehe chart method** (empirical, pressure–temperature chart). 2. **Bukacek equation** (analytical correlation using vapor pressure of water and compressibility). 3. **Dew point measurement** (using a chilled-mirror hygrometer or capacitance probe). **Principle of McKetta–Wehe:** It is an empirical correlation (developed in 1958) that gives the equilibrium water content of a 0.6 specific gravity sweet natural gas as a function of pressure and temperature. Correction factors are applied for differing gas gravity and acid-gas content. --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — Question A (14 marks)** > (a) Discuss in detail the various sources of water in natural gas and the problems caused by water in gas processing and transportation. (7 marks) > > (b) A natural gas stream is at 1500 psia and 120 °F. The compressibility factor is 0.92. Using the Bukacek equation, compute the equilibrium water content in lb/MMscf. The saturation vapor pressure of water at 120 °F is 1.694 psia. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Sources and problems of water in natural gas (7 marks):** **Sources (3.5 marks):** - **Formation water:** Co-produced from the reservoir along with hydrocarbon gas. - **Condensed water vapor:** Water that was in vapor phase at reservoir conditions but condenses on cooling. - **Injection water:** Used for reservoir pressure maintenance (water flooding) — may be co-produced. - **Completion and stimulation fluids:** Acidizing, fracturing fluids may contain water. - **Atmospheric moisture:** Leaks in low-pressure gathering systems. **Problems (3.5 marks):** - **Hydrate formation:** Crystalline ice-like solids (clathrates) formed by water + light hydrocarbons at low T / high P. Cause pipeline blockages. - **Corrosion:** CO₂ + H₂S + water → carbonic / sulfuric acid → pipe wall thinning. - **Two-phase flow:** Liquid water + gas in pipelines → pressure drop, slugging. - **Catalyst poisoning:** Water deactivates catalysts in downstream amine, molecular sieve, and reformer units. - **Measurement errors:** Affects orifice meters and gas chromatographs. - **Liquid loading in low-pressure wells:** Water accumulation can kill the well. **(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):** Given: $P = 1500$ psia, $T = 120\,^{\circ}\text{F}$, $Z_{mix} = 0.92$, $p_w^{sat} = 1.694$ psia. **Step 1:** Identify Bukacek equation: $$W = \frac{10^6 \cdot M_w \cdot p_w^{sat}(T)}{M_{air} \cdot P \cdot Z_{mix}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$W = \frac{10^6 \cdot 18.015 \cdot 1.694}{28.97 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.92}$$ **Step 3:** Compute numerator: $$10^6 \cdot 18.015 \cdot 1.694 = 3.0517 \times 10^7$$ **Step 4:** Compute denominator: $$28.97 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.92 = 39979.8$$ **Step 5:** Compute $W$: $$W = \frac{3.0517 \times 10^7}{39979.8} = 763.4 \text{ lb water / MMscf}$$ **Answer:** $W \approx \mathbf{763.4 \text{ lb water / MMscf}}$. This is far above the sales-gas specification; therefore, dehydration is mandatory. --- **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** > (a) With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the working of a Tri-Ethylene Glycol (TEG) contactor-based natural gas dehydration system. (7 marks) > > (b) Explain the McKetta–Wehe correlation for water content. A gas has 0.65 specific gravity, 2% CO₂ and is at 800 psia and 90 °F. If the McKetta–Wehe chart reads 65 lb/MMscf, compute the corrected water content using the gravity and acid-gas correction factors. (Use $C_{SG} = 1.04$ and $C_{acid} = 1.06$.) (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) TEG dehydration system (7 marks):** The TEG (Tri-Ethylene Glycol) contactor dehydration system removes water from natural gas by absorption. Main components (see diagram in SECTION_4.1): 1. **Absorber (Contactor):** A vertical packed/trayed tower. Wet gas enters at the bottom, lean TEG enters at the top. Countercurrent contact → water transfers from gas to glycol. 2. **Flash separator:** Rich TEG is depressurized to recover dissolved hydrocarbons. 3. **Particulate filter:** Removes solid contaminants from rich TEG. 4. **Lean-rich heat exchanger:** Pre-heats rich TEG using hot lean TEG from the regenerator. 5. **Regenerator (reboiler):** Rich TEG is heated to **~400 °F (204 °C)** at **atmospheric pressure**; water is vaporized and vented. 6. **Surge drum and lean TEG cooler:** Lean TEG is cooled and stored before re-injection. **Working principle:** Glycol is hygroscopic. The TEG absorbs water from the gas, producing dry gas meeting the spec (< 7 lb/MMscf) and rich TEG, which is regenerated for reuse. Typical TEG circulation: **2–5 gal TEG / lb water removed**. **(b) McKetta–Wehe correlation (7 marks):** **Theory (3 marks):** The McKetta–Wehe correlation (1958) is an empirical chart-based method that gives the equilibrium water content of natural gas as a function of **pressure and temperature**. The reference chart is developed for a **0.6 specific gravity sweet gas** (no acid gases). To apply to a real gas, two correction factors are introduced: $$W_{corrected} = W_{chart} \times C_{SG} \times C_{acid}$$ where $C_{SG}$ corrects for gas gravity (higher gravity → slightly more water) and $C_{acid}$ corrects for the presence of H₂S/CO₂ (acid gases increase water content because of chemical interaction and partial solubility). **Numerical part (4 marks):** Given: - $W_{chart} = 65$ lb/MMscf - $C_{SG} = 1.04$ - $C_{acid} = 1.06$ **Step 1:** Apply the correction: $$W_{corrected} = 65 \cdot 1.04 \cdot 1.06$$ **Step 2:** Compute: $$W_{corrected} = 65 \cdot 1.1024 = 71.66 \text{ lb/MMscf}$$ **Answer:** $W_{corrected} \approx \mathbf{71.66 \text{ lb water / MMscf}}$. This value is still above the sales specification, so the gas requires dehydration. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully** > - **Unit mismatch trap:** Many students forget to convert °F to °R (or °C to K) in Buck's equation. This single error costs 1–2 marks. > - **Sign convention:** In Buck's exponent, the term is *subtracted*, not added. A sign error gives wildly wrong vapor pressure (off by 10⁵). > - **Compressibility factor Z_mix:** Do not omit it at high pressure (>1000 psia); examiners deduct 1 mark if you skip it. > - **Correction factors:** Always multiply $W_{chart}$ by $C_{SG} \cdot C_{acid}$ — never by their sum or difference. > - **Standard conditions:** When expressing W in lb/MMscf, the reference is **14.7 psia and 60 °F** (US), not 1 atm and 15 °C (which is the metric standard). > - **Sketch quality:** In TEG question, a properly labeled sketch with arrows for inlet/outlet streams carries 2 marks. A bare paragraph without a sketch loses those marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Water content of natural gas** = mass of water vapor per unit volume of gas (lb/MMscf or ppmv). - ✅ **Standard sales specification:** ≤ 7 lb/MMscf → water dew point of **−10 °C @ 1000 psia**. - ✅ **Three main correlations:** McKetta–Wehe (chart), Bukacek (analytical), Buck's equation (vapor pressure of water). - ✅ **Effect of variables:** Higher T → more water; higher P → less water; more acid gas (H₂S, CO₂) → more water; salt in formation water → reduces vapor pressure (Raoult's law). - ✅ **Bukacek equation:** $W = \dfrac{10^6 \cdot M_w \cdot p_w^{sat}(T)}{M_{air} \cdot P \cdot Z_{mix}}$. - ✅ **Buck's equation:** $p_w^{sat} = 0.00456 \cdot e^{18.3036 - 4111.357/T_R}$ (with $T_R$ in °R). - ✅ **Sources of water:** formation water, condensed vapor, injection water, completion fluids, atmospheric leaks. - ✅ **Problems:** hydrates, corrosion, two-phase flow, catalyst poisoning, measurement errors, well loading. - ✅ **Dehydration methods:** TEG contact (most common), solid desiccant (molecular sieve), methanol injection (subsea), membrane, CaCl₂. - ✅ **TEG contactor:** absorption at ~100 °F and high pressure; regeneration at ~400 °F and atmospheric pressure; typical circulation 2–5 gal TEG/lb water. - ✅ **McKetta–Wehe correction:** $W_{corrected} = W_{chart} \cdot C_{SG} \cdot C_{acid}$. - ✅ **Hydrate prevention:** dehydration, pressure reduction, temperature increase, or thermodynamic inhibition (methanol, MEG). - ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $M_w = 18.015$, $M_{air} = 28.97$, $T_{sc} = 520$ °R, $P_{sc} = 14.696$ psia. - ✅ **Exam technique:** Always state the standard conditions, list given data explicitly, show units in every step, and use 3 significant figures in the final answer. ---

topics.title: Hydrate Control in Gas Production
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrate Control in Gas Production

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Gas Hydrates?

**Gas Hydrates** are crystalline, ice-like, non-stoichiometric inclusion compounds (clathrates) formed when small guest molecules (such as methane $\text{CH}_4$, ethane $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$, carbon dioxide $\text{CO}_2$, hydrogen sulfide $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, and nitrogen $\text{N}_2$) become physically trapped within a lattice of hydrogen-bonded water molecules, under conditions of low temperature and elevated pressure.

In the oil and gas industry, hydrates are a **major flow assurance problem** because they form in subsea pipelines, wellheads, flowlines, separators, and heat exchangers — blocking flow, damaging equipment, and creating safety hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT522 Module 3):**
> Gas hydrates are clathrate compounds of water and light hydrocarbon/non-hydrocarbon gases that form at temperatures above the freezing point of water when the system pressure is sufficiently high. Hydrate control is an essential flow-assurance discipline in natural gas production, processing, and transportation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honeycomb Cage" Picture

Imagine a beehive where bees (the **gas molecules** like $\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$) wander into a rigid honeycomb structure (the **water lattice**) and get locked inside. The honeycomb is *built by the bees themselves* — water molecules hydrogen-bond into cage-like lattices **only when gas molecules are present** to stabilize them.

- If temperature drops (cold seabed, Joule-Thomson cooling) **and** pressure is high → cages form → solid plugs.
- If the bees (gas) leave (temperature rises, pressure drops) → cages collapse → water returns to liquid.

**Real-world analogy:** Hydrates are like cholesterol in a blood vessel. The pipeline *needs* to flow gas + water, but under wrong conditions, solids precipitate and block the line — just as cholesterol blocks arteries.

## 1.3 Physical Conditions for Hydrate Formation

| Parameter | Typical Range (Offshore/Subsea) |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **0 °C to 30 °C** (above 0 °C!) |
| Pressure | **1 MPa to 30 MPa** (subsea wellhead to riser) |
| Water | Must be present (free or dissolved) |
| Agitation | Flow disturbances accelerate nucleation |
| Gas Composition | Heavier gases → easier hydrate formation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Industrial Constants (Bold for Memorization):**
> - **Hammerschmidt constant (K) = 2335 °R** (or **1297 K**) in the inhibitor depression equation
> - **Hydrate number n ≈ 5.75 to 7.4** water molecules per gas molecule (structure dependent)
> - **Density of methane hydrate ≈ 0.91 g/cm³** (lighter than water)
> - **Methane hydrate forms at ~ 2.5 MPa at 0 °C** (a typical subsea condition!)

## 1.4 Three Common Hydrate Crystal Structures

| Structure | Cavity Size | Typical Guests | Hydration Number n |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure I (sI)** | Small | $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | **5.75** |
| **Structure II (sII)** | Small + Large | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$, iso-butane, THF | **5.67** |
| **Structure H (sH)** | Small + Medium + Large | Neohexane, methylcyclopentane | **7.4** |

> [!TIP]
> Most natural gas systems form **Structure II (sII)** because natural gas typically contains propane, butane, and isobutane, all of which prefer sII cages.

## 1.5 Why Hydrate Control Matters in KTU Context

In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 examines hydrate control as part of gas processing. The four pillars of hydrate control are:
1. **Water Removal (Dehydration)**
2. **Chemical Inhibition (Thermodynamic Hydrate Inhibitors - THI)**
3. **Low Dosage Hydrate Inhibitors (LDHI)**
4. **Process Control (Insulation, Heat Tracing, Pressure Reduction)**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic "Why" Behind Hydrate Formation

Hydrate formation is governed by the **thermodynamic equilibrium** between three phases:
- **Liquid water (or ice)** — the host
- **Hydrate phase (H)** — the clathrate solid
- **Vapor (or liquid hydrocarbon) phase** — the guest supplier

The formation is essentially a **phase equilibrium** problem. The driving force is described by:

$$\Delta\mu_w^{H}(T,P) \;=\; \mu_w^{H} - \mu_w^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ denotes the water-rich liquid phase and $H$ the hydrate phase. Hydrate forms when $\Delta\mu_w^H < 0$.

## 2.2 The Hydrate Formation Loop — Step-by-Step

The kinetics of hydrate formation follow four sequential steps:
1. **Induction (Nucleation):** Dissolved gas molecules cluster with water; critical nuclei form (slowest step).
2. **Growth:** Hydrate crystals grow rapidly along the water–gas interface.
3. **Agglomeration:** Crystals stick together forming plugs.
4. **Plugging:** Solid mass blocks the flowline, causing shut-in or rupture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **induction time** is the critical parameter for design. Operators inject inhibitor or depressurize *before* the induction period elapses, NOT after plugging occurs.

## 2.3 The Four Pillars of Hydrate Control — Detailed Logic

### Pillar 1: Dehydration (Water Removal)

The most fundamental approach — if water is removed below the **hydrate-safe dew point**, no hydrate can form.

- **Methods:** Glycol absorption (TEG, DEG, MEG), Molecular Sieves (Zeolite 4A, 5A), Solid desiccants.
- **Specification:** Gas delivered to pipeline must have water content **< 0.1 lb $\text{H}_2\text{O}$/MMscf** (≈ 1.6 mg/Sm³) for subsea transport.

### Pillar 2: Thermodynamic Hydrate Inhibitors (THI)

Shift the hydrate equilibrium curve to **lower temperatures / higher pressures** by depressing the water activity.

| Inhibitor | Molecular Weight | Volatility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Methanol (MeOH)** | 32.04 | High (vaporizes) | Subsea, short tiebacks |
| **Mono-Ethylene Glycol (MEG)** | 62.07 | Low (regenerable) | Long subsea tiebacks |
| **Di-Ethylene Glycol (DEG)** | 106.12 | Very Low | Onshore, high T |
| **Tri-Ethylene Glycol (TEG)** | 150.17 | Very Low | Gas dehydration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vapor-Loss Trade-off:** Methanol partitions into the gas phase and is lost downstream → not regenerable. MEG is preferred for long tiebacks because it stays in the liquid phase and can be recovered/regenerated.

### Pillar 3: Low-Dosage Hydrate Inhibitors (LDHI)

Used at < 1% concentration (vs. THI at 10–60 wt%):
- **Kinetic Inhibitors (KHI):** Polymers (PVCap, PVP) that delay nucleation.
- **Anti-Agglomerants (AA):** Surfactants that prevent crystals from sticking.

### Pillar 4: Process Control

- **Insulation:** Reduces heat loss to seabed (subsea pipelines use multi-layer polypropylene, syntactic foam).
- **Heat Tracing:** Electric or hot-fluid tracing on flowlines.
- **Pressure Reduction:** Depressurization in shut-in (rapid blowdown below hydrate curve).
- **Dead-Oil Plugs:** Filling line with diesel/glycol during long shut-ins.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydrate Control Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield — commit to memory for ESE.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T_{dep} = \dfrac{K \cdot w}{M \cdot (100 - w)}$ | **Hammerschmidt Equation** — inhibitor-induced temperature depression | $\Delta T$ in °F, $w$ in wt%, $M$ in g/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{dep} = \dfrac{K \cdot X}{1 - X}$ | Hammerschmidt in **mole fraction form** | $\Delta T$ in °R, $X$ = mole fraction inhibitor |
| 3 | $w_{inh} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{inh} \cdot \Delta T_{dep}}{K + M_{inh} \cdot \Delta T_{dep}}$ | Re-arranged Hammerschmidt — required inhibitor wt% | wt% |
| 4 | $\ln P_{H,CH_4} = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Methane hydrate equilibrium pressure correlation (Barkan-Sheinin) | P in MPa, T in K |
| 5 | $P_H = \exp\left(A + \dfrac{B}{T}\right)$ | General hydrate formation pressure vs. T | depends on gas |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{inh} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot \dfrac{w_{inh}}{100 - w_{inh}}$ | Mass flow rate of inhibitor injection | kg/day |
| 7 | $X_{MEG,aqu} = \dfrac{m_{MEG}/62.07}{(m_{MEG}/62.07) + (m_w/18.015)}$ | Mole fraction of MEG in **aqueous phase** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{\text{moles } H_2O}{\text{moles gas in hydrate}}$ | Hydration number | mol/mol |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{dep} = \dfrac{1297 \cdot w / M}{100 - w}$ | Hammerschmidt in **SI units** (K vs. K·kg/mol) | K, kg/kmol |
| 10 | $P_{H,sea} = P_{abs} - P_{sw}$ | Subsea hydrate formation pressure (gauge vs. absolute) | MPa |

**Key Constants:**
- **$K = 2335 °F$** (Hammerschmidt) — empirical
- **$K = 1297 K \cdot kg/kmol$** in SI form
- **$P_{sw} \approx 0.01005$ MPa/m depth** (seawater hydrostatic)
- **Methane MW = 16.04 g/mol**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Hammerschmidt equation, despite being empirical, is **the industry standard** in offshore engineering for sizing methanol/MEG injection pumps. The hydration number and hydrate structure identification feed into:
- **OLGA / LedaFlow** multiphase flow simulations
- **PVTSim** flash calculations
- **CSMHYD** (Colorado School of Mines Hydrate Predictor)
- **Hydrate Safe Operating Envelope (SOE)** — the key deliverable in flow-assurance design

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Hammerschmidt Equation (Reformulation)

The original Hammerschmidt equation (1939) was empirically derived from experimental data on hydrate inhibition using methanol, glycol, and ammonia. The thermodynamic underpinning comes from the **freezing point depression** analogy — colligative property.

Starting from the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** for liquid–solid equilibrium in the presence of an inhibitor:

$$\dfrac{d(\ln a_w)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R T^2}$$

Integrating between pure water ($a_w = 1$, $T = T_0$) and inhibited system ($a_w$, $T$):

$$\ln a_w = \dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)$$

For dilute inhibitor (small $\Delta T$), using a series expansion, Hammerschmidt obtained the **linearized empirical form**:

$$\Delta T_{dep} \;=\; \dfrac{K \cdot w}{M (100 - w)}$$

The **SI form** uses $K = 1297$ K·kg/kmol and $w$ in wt%:

$$\Delta T_{dep} = \dfrac{1297 \cdot w}{M \cdot (100 - w)}$$

**Note on validity:** Best for $w < 50$ wt% and $T < 0 °C$ hydrate-region conditions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Hammerschmidt Application

**Problem (KTU Typical):**
A subsea flowline operates at **2 °C** and the hydrate formation temperature (at operating pressure) is **12 °C**. Methanol is to be used as the inhibitor. Calculate:
(a) Required temperature depression
(b) Required methanol concentration in the aqueous phase

**Given:**
- $T_{op} = 2 °C = 275.15$ K
- $T_{form} = 12 °C = 285.15$ K
- Inhibitor = Methanol, $M = 32.04$ g/mol

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Required depression
$$\Delta T_{dep} = T_{form} - T_{op} = 12 - 2 = 10 °C$$

Converting to °F: $\Delta T = 10 \times 1.8 = 18 °F$

**Step 2:** Apply Hammerschmidt equation (°F form)
$$18 = \dfrac{2335 \cdot w}{32.04 \cdot (100 - w)}$$

$$18 \cdot 32.04 \cdot (100 - w) = 2335 \cdot w$$

$$576.72 \cdot (100 - w) = 2335 \cdot w$$

$$57672 - 576.72 w = 2335 w$$

$$57672 = 2911.72 w$$

$$w = \dfrac{57672}{2911.72} \approx 19.81 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Step 3:** Verify with SI form
$$\Delta T_{dep} = 10 \text{ K}, \quad K = 1297, \quad M = 32.04 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

$$10 = \dfrac{1297 \cdot w}{32.04 \cdot (100 - w)}$$

$$w \approx 19.81 \text{ wt\%} \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Required methanol concentration in the aqueous phase ≈ **19.8 wt%**. This is the design basis for the methanol injection pump.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Methanol Injection Rate

**Problem:**
A gas well produces **15 MMscf/day** of gas with a water production rate of **300 bbl/day**. Methanol must be injected at the wellhead to achieve **25 wt%** concentration in the aqueous phase. Calculate:
(a) Mass flow rate of methanol required
(b) Volume flow rate of methanol (density 0.79 g/cm³)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert water flow to mass
$$\dot{m}_w = 300 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl} \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$\dot{m}_w = 47{,}700 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the inhibitor mass-balance formula
$$\dot{m}_{MeOH} = \dot{m}_w \cdot \dfrac{w_{inh}}{100 - w_{inh}} = 47{,}700 \times \dfrac{25}{75}$$

$$\dot{m}_{MeOH} = 15{,}900 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Volume flow rate
$$\dot{V}_{MeOH} = \dfrac{15{,}900}{0.79 \times 1000} = 20.13 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

In barrels: $\dot{V}_{MeOH} = 20.13 / 0.159 = 126.6$ **bbl/day** of methanol.

> [!WARNING]
> **Methanol Loss to Vapor Phase:** A correction factor of **1.2 to 1.6** is typically multiplied to account for methanol partitioning into the gas phase. True injection rate = $126.6 \times 1.4 \approx 177$ bbl/day.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — MEG Inhibition (Regenerable System)

**Problem:**
A subsea tieback uses MEG regeneration. The system requires 50 wt% MEG in the lean injection stream. The aqueous phase flow rate is **50 m³/day**. Calculate:
(a) Mass of MEG injected
(b) Mole fraction of MEG in the aqueous phase

**Given:** MEG MW = 62.07 kg/kmol; Water MW = 18.015 kg/kmol; $\rho_{MEG} = 1.113$ g/cm³

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of lean MEG stream (assuming 50 wt%)
For 50 m³/day water-equivalent aqueous phase, mass water = $50{,}000$ kg/day.
$$\dot{m}_{MEG} = 50{,}000 \times \dfrac{50}{50} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

(For 50 wt% solution: $\dot{m}_{MEG} = \dot{m}_w$ by definition.)

**Step 2:** Volume flow rate
$$\dot{V}_{MEG} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1.113 \times 1000} = 44.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3:** Mole fraction
$$X_{MEG} = \dfrac{50{,}000 / 62.07}{(50{,}000 / 62.07) + (50{,}000 / 18.015)}$$

$$X_{MEG} = \dfrac{805.55}{805.55 + 2775.46} = \dfrac{805.55}{3581.01}$$

$$X_{MEG} \approx 0.225 \;\; \text{(or 22.5 mol\%)}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 4 — Hydrate Formation Pressure (Methane)

**Problem (Barkan-Sheinin Correlation):**
For pure methane, the hydrate formation pressure can be estimated by:
$$\log_{10}(P_H / \text{MPa}) = A - \dfrac{B}{T(K)}$$
with $A = 4.2168$, $B = 1144$ K (for $T$ between 273 K and 290 K).

At $T = 280$ K, calculate hydrate formation pressure.

**Solution:**
$$\log_{10} P_H = 4.2168 - \dfrac{1144}{280}$$

$$\log_{10} P_H = 4.2168 - 4.0857 = 0.1311$$

$$P_H = 10^{0.1311} \approx 1.35 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Pure methane at 7 °C requires only 1.35 MPa to form hydrates — easily reached in subsea flowlines. This is why *every* deepwater project requires hydrate control.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem 5 — Hydration Number

**Problem:**
A hydrate sample contains 46 g of water and 8 g of methane trapped in the lattice. Calculate the hydration number $n$.

**Solution:**
$$n_{water} = \dfrac{46}{18.015} = 2.554 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{8}{16.04} = 0.499 \text{ mol}$$

$$n = \dfrac{n_{water}}{n_{CH_4}} = \dfrac{2.554}{0.499} \approx 5.12$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $n \approx 5.1$ — consistent with **Structure I (sI)** methane hydrate where theoretical $n = 5.75$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Inhibitor Selection Quick Reference

| Scenario | Recommended Inhibitor | Concentration | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subsea wellhead, short tieback (< 5 km) | **Methanol** | 20–50 wt% | Easy injection, no regeneration |
| Long subsea tieback (> 30 km) | **MEG** | 50–80 wt% | Regenerable, low vapor loss |
| Onshore cold winter operation | **DEG / TEG** | 60–90 wt% | High boiling point, low vapor loss |
| Gas processing plant (dehydration) | **TEG** | 99 wt% (lean) | Standard absorption process |
| High-pressure gas with H₂S | **MEG + corrosion inhibitor** | 50 wt% + 100–500 ppm | MEG is compatible with H₂S |
| Deepwater with high methanol cost | **LDHI (KHI)** | 0.5–1.0 wt% | 90% chemical savings |
| Emergency shut-in | **Depressurization** | N/A | Fastest hydrate avoidance |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Conceptual `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define gas hydrates. State the three common hydrate structures with their typical guest molecules.**

**Model Answer:**

Gas hydrates are crystalline, non-stoichiometric clathrate compounds in which small gas molecules (guests) are physically trapped inside cages formed by hydrogen-bonded water molecules (host). They form at temperatures above the freezing point of water when system pressure is sufficiently high.

The three common hydrate structures are:

1. **Structure I (sI):** Body-centered cubic, 46 water molecules per unit cell. Guests: $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$. Hydration number ≈ 5.75.
2. **Structure II (sII):** Diamond lattice, 136 water molecules per unit cell. Guests: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$, iso-$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$, $\text{N}_2$, THF. Hydration number ≈ 5.67.
3. **Structure H (sH):** Hexagonal, 34 water molecules per unit cell. Guests: neohexane, methylcyclopentane (require help gas like $\text{CH}_4$). Hydration number ≈ 7.4.

---

### Question 2 — Short Note `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Thermodynamic Hydrate Inhibitors (THI) and Low-Dosage Hydrate Inhibitors (LDHI).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | THI | LDHI |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration | 10–60 wt% in water | 0.1–1.0 wt% in water |
| Mechanism | Shifts hydrate equilibrium curve (thermodynamic) | Delays nucleation (kinetic) or prevents agglomeration |
| Examples | Methanol, MEG, DEG, TEG | PVCap, PVP, quaternary surfactants |
| Dosage cost | High (large volumes) | Low (small volumes) |
| Recovery | MEG/TEG regenerable; MeOH not | Generally not recovered |
| Application | Long tiebacks, deepwater | Tiebacks where THI logistics are difficult |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A subsea natural gas pipeline operates at 4 MPa and 5 °C. The hydrate formation temperature at 4 MPa for the given gas composition is 14 °C. Methanol is to be injected as a thermodynamic hydrate inhibitor.**

**(a) Calculate the minimum required methanol concentration in the aqueous phase to prevent hydrate formation. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the water production rate is 200 bbl/day, calculate the daily methanol injection rate (in kg/day and L/day) accounting for a vapor-phase loss factor of 1.4. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Methanol Concentration

**Step 1:** Required temperature depression
$$\Delta T = T_{form} - T_{op} = 14 - 5 = 9 °C = 16.2 °F$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hammerschmidt equation (°F form), $M_{MeOH} = 32.04$ g/mol
$$\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot w}{M(100 - w)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 16.2 = \dfrac{2335 \cdot w}{32.04 \cdot (100 - w)}$$

$$16.2 \times 32.04 \times (100 - w) = 2335 w$$
$$518.85 \times (100 - w) = 2335 w$$
$$51885 - 518.85 w = 2335 w$$
$$51885 = 2853.85 w$$
$$w = \dfrac{51885}{2853.85} \approx 18.18 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Answer:** Minimum methanol concentration ≈ **18.2 wt%** in aqueous phase.

---

#### Part (b) — Methanol Injection Rate

**Step 1:** Convert water flow to mass
$$\dot{m}_w = 200 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl} \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 31{,}800 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of methanol (aqueous balance)
$$\dot{m}_{MeOH} = 31{,}800 \times \dfrac{18.18}{100 - 18.18} = 31{,}800 \times \dfrac{18.18}{81.82}$$
$$\dot{m}_{MeOH} = 31{,}800 \times 0.2221 = 7063.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Apply vapor loss correction (1.4×)
$$\dot{m}_{MeOH,actual} = 7063.5 \times 1.4 = 9888.9 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4:** Volume flow rate ($\rho_{MeOH} = 0.79$ g/cm³ = 790 kg/m³)
$$\dot{V}_{MeOH} = \dfrac{9888.9}{790} = 12.52 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 12{,}520 \text{ L/day}$$

In barrels: $\dot{V}_{MeOH} = 12.52 / 0.159 = 78.7$ **bbl/day**

**Final Answer:** $\dot{m}_{MeOH} = 9889$ kg/day; $\dot{V}_{MeOH} = 12{,}520$ L/day ≈ 78.7 bbl/day.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the four primary methods of hydrate control used in natural gas production systems. Compare their applicability for offshore vs. onshore operations. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the design procedure for a MEG regeneration unit, including the material balance across the regen column. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Four Methods of Hydrate Control

**1. Dehydration (Water Removal)**
- Removes water below the hydrate-safe dew point using **glycol absorption** (TEG most common) or **molecular sieves**.
- Offshore limitation: large footprint, weight, regeneration columns difficult to install on fixed platforms.
- Onshore: standard for gas processing plants.

**2. Chemical Inhibition (THI)**
- Methanol (vaporized, lost) or MEG (regenerable) injected at the wellhead.
- Offshore: standard for subsea flowlines.
- Onshore: used when line temperature drops below hydrate T seasonally.

**3. Process Control (Insulation & Heat Tracing)**
- Pipeline insulation (PIP — Pipe-in-Pipe, syntactic foam) plus electric heat tracing.
- Offshore: very common for subsea flowlines to delay cooldown.
- Onshore: heat tracing in cold regions (e.g., Siberia, Alaska).

**4. Low-Dosage Hydrate Inhibitors (LDHI)**
- Kinetic Inhibitors (KHI) like Polyvinylcaprolactam (PVCap) delay nucleation.
- Anti-Agglomerants (AA) prevent crystal sticking.
- Offshore: growing application in deepwater where THI logistics are costly.
- Onshore: limited use due to cost per kg and uncertainty in field performance.

**Applicability Comparison Table (essential for KTU answer):**

| Method | Offshore Suitability | Onshore Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| Dehydration | Low–Medium | High |
| THI (Methanol/MEG) | High | Medium |
| Process Control (Insulation) | High | High (cold climates) |
| LDHI | High (modern) | Low–Medium |

---

#### Part (b) — MEG Regeneration Unit Design

**Block Flow:**
1. **Rich MEG Inlet** (~50 wt% MEG) from subsea flowline.
2. **Pre-Heater** (heat recovered from lean MEG).
3. **Regen Still Column** (with structured packing or trays).
4. **Reboiler** at bottom (~200 °C) drives off water.
5. **Condenser** at top condenses water vapor.
6. **Lean MEG Pump** returns 80–90 wt% MEG to subsea.

**Material Balance Across the Regen Column (typical design):**

Given: Rich MEG flow = 1000 kg/day at 50 wt% MEG, 50 wt% water.

$$m_{MEG,rich} = 500 \text{ kg/day}, \quad m_{w,rich} = 500 \text{ kg/day}$$

Target: Lean MEG at 80 wt% MEG.

Let $L$ = mass flow of lean MEG (kg/day) and $D$ = mass flow of distillate (water overhead, kg/day).

Component balances:
- Total: $L + D = 1000$
- MEG: $0.80 L = 500 \Rightarrow L = 625$ kg/day
- Water: $D = 1000 - 625 = 375$ kg/day (distillate composition ≈ 99.9% water)

**Design verification:**
- Reboiler duty: heat to vaporize 375 kg/day water at 200 °C from lean MEG.
- $Q_{reb} = m_w \times \lambda_w = 375 \text{ kg/day} \times 2200 \text{ kJ/kg} = 825{,}000$ kJ/day ≈ 9.55 kW.

**Answer:** Lean MEG = 625 kg/day at 80 wt%; Distillate = 375 kg/day water. Reboiler duty ≈ **9.55 kW** (ignoring sensible heat).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Hydrate Control Questions (ESE Module 3):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Hammerschmidt:** Using °C in the equation with $K = 2335$. Always convert to °F, OR use SI form with $K = 1297$ and $M$ in kg/kmol.
> 2. **Forgetting vapor-loss correction for methanol:** A pure mass balance underestimates by 20–60%. Always state the correction factor explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing THI with LDHI mechanism:** THI *shifts* the equilibrium curve thermodynamically; LDHI *delays* the kinetics. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Neglecting density of methanol** in volumetric conversion. Methanol $\rho = 0.79$ g/cm³, NOT 1.0.
> 5. **Inhibitor weight basis confusion:** "wt% in aqueous phase" is NOT the same as "wt% of total stream." Always read the problem statement carefully.
> 6. **Omitting the regeneration description** when MEG is used — the regeneration loop is mandatory in long-tieback designs.
> 7. **No "why" in theoretical answers:** KTU 2024 Scheme requires justification for each control method, not just a list.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gas hydrates** are ice-like clathrates of water + light gases, forming at high P and low T (even above 0 °C).
- [x] Three structures: **sI (CH₄, CO₂), sII (C₃H₈+), sH (large molecules + help gas)**.
- [x] Hydrate formation requires: **water + gas + low T + high P + nucleation time**.
- [x] **Hammerschmidt equation** is the industry standard for inhibitor dose calculation.
- [x] **$K = 2335 °F$ (US)** or **$K = 1297 K·kg/kmol (SI)** — memorize both.
- [x] **Methanol** is cheap but vaporizes (non-regenerable); **MEG** is regenerable, preferred for long tiebacks.
- [x] **LDHI** (KHI + AA) at < 1 wt% for new deepwater developments.
- [x] **Vapor-loss correction factor** for methanol: 1.2–1.6× of stoichiometric dose.
- [x] **Density of methanol = 0.79 g/cm³** (key for volume conversion).
- [x] **Hydration number** for sI methane ≈ 5.75; for sII ≈ 5.67; for sH ≈ 7.4.
- [x] **Depressurization** is the fastest emergency hydrate remediation method.
- [x] **Pipeline insulation (PIP)** keeps the fluid above hydrate T during steady-state and shut-in.
- [x] **Glycol dehydration (TEG)** removes water to < 0.1 lb/MMscf for hydrate-free pipeline spec.
- [x] **Barkan-Sheinin correlation** for methane hydrate: $\log P_H = A - B/T$.
- [x] **MEG regeneration column** typically operates at 200 °C reboiler, 80–90 wt% lean MEG.
- [x] Always state the **"why"** for every hydrate control method in KTU descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dehydration Systems: Glycol Dehydration and Solid Desiccant Dehydration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dehydration Systems: Glycol Dehydration and Solid Desiccant Dehydration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Natural Gas Dehydration?

**Dehydration** is the industrial process of removing water vapor from natural gas streams to prevent operational, safety, and economic problems in gas processing, transmission, and distribution systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Natural gas dehydration is a unit operation in gas processing where water content of the gas is reduced to a level that prevents hydrate formation (typically **< 7 lbs/MMSCF** for pipeline specification, equivalent to approximately **112 mg/Sm³**), corrosion, and two-phase flow problems in downstream equipment.

### 1.2 Why is Dehydration Critical in the Oil & Gas Industry?

Natural gas from wellheads is saturated with water vapor. If left untreated, the water causes:

| Problem | Mechanism | Consequence |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Gas Hydrate Formation** | Water + hydrocarbons (C₁, C₂, CO₂, H₂S) at low T, high P form ice-like crystalline solids | Plugging of pipelines, chokes, valves — operational shutdown |
| **Corrosion** | Dissolved CO₂ + H₂S + H₂O → carbonic/sulfuric acid | Pipeline pitting, equipment failure |
| **Two-Phase Flow** | Free water reduces line capacity | Pressure drop, slug flow in pipelines |
| **Operational Issues** | Water in processing units | Foaming in amine/regeneration columns |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Sieve" 🍝

Imagine a **wet kitchen sponge** (the natural gas) dripping water. You have two ways to dry it:

1. **Glycol Dehydration** → You **wash** the sponge with an absorbent chemical (glycol) that *attracts and dissolves* the water, then wring out the chemical to recover and reuse it. *(Absorption — bulk water removal)*
2. **Solid Desiccant Dehydration** → You place the sponge inside a box filled with **silica beads or zeolite crystals** that *trap* water molecules in their microscopic pores. When the beads are saturated, you heat them to release the water and reuse them. *(Adsorption — ultra-deep drying)*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Glycol dehydration is the **workhorse** for bulk water removal in offshore/onshore gas processing. Solid desiccant dehydration is reserved for **ultra-low dew point** applications (LNG, helium recovery, cryogenic NGL recovery) where pipeline-spec glycol systems are insufficient.

### 1.4 Selection Criteria — Glycol vs. Solid Desiccant

| Parameter | Glycol (TEG) Dehydration | Solid Desiccant |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Achievable Dew Point** | -20 °F to -40 °F (−29 °C to −40 °C) | -100 °F to −150 °F (−73 °C to −101 °C) |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High |
| **Operating Cost** | Low (glycol losses, energy) | High (heating, regeneration) |
| **Capacity Range** | 1 – 1000+ MMSCFD | Small to very large |
| **Application** | Pipeline spec gas, offshore platforms | LNG feed, cryogenic, helium units |
| **Regeneration** | Continuous (reboiler) | Cyclic (thermal swing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Glycol Dehydration — System Architecture & Operating Principle

The most widely used glycol is **Triethylene Glycol (TEG)** due to its high boiling point (549 °F / 287 °C), low vapor pressure, high affinity for water, and chemical stability.

#### 2.1.1 Major Process Components

**Step 1 — Inlet Separation:** Wet gas enters an **Inlet Scrubber (Slug Catcher)** where free liquids (hydrocarbon condensate, free water) are removed. This prevents glycol contamination and foaming.

**Step 2 — Gas–Glycol Contacting (Absorber):** Dry, lean TEG flows **downward** over bubble-cap, valve, or structured packing trays (typically 6–8 theoretical stages) inside the **Glycol Contactor (Absorber Column)**. Wet gas flows **upward** counter-currently. The TEG absorbs water from the gas through physical absorption.

**Step 3 — Rich Glycol Outlet:** The water-rich TEG (typically **95–99 wt% water absorbed**, leaving 0.5–2 wt% lean TEG after regeneration) leaves the column bottom at high pressure (700–1200 psig typical) and is depressurized through a **Level Control Valve**.

**Step 4 — Flash Separation:** Rich glycol enters a **Flash Separator** at ~50–75 psig, where dissolved hydrocarbons (methane, ethane) are liberated and sent to flare or fuel gas system. This prevents hydrocarbon pollution of the regenerator.

**Step 5 — Particulate Filtration:** Rich glycol passes through **Carbon Filter** (removes hydrocarbons, degradation products) and **Particulate Filter** (removes solids, salts, FeS).

**Step 6 — Glycol–Glycol Heat Exchange:** Rich glycol is pre-heated against **hot lean glycol** leaving the reboiler in a **Glycol–Glycol Exchanger** (energy integration).

**Step 7 — Regeneration (Stripping Column + Reboiler):** Pre-heated rich glycol enters the **Regenerator Column** (packed with Raschig rings or structured packing) where it trickles down into the **Reboiler** operating at **400 °F (204 °C)** at atmospheric pressure. Water is boiled off overhead; lean TEG (99.0–99.99 wt%) collects at the reboiler bottom.

**Step 8 — Stripping Gas Enhancement (Optional):** Dry stripping gas (typically 1–5 SCF/gal glycol) is injected into the reboiler to lower partial pressure of water → improves dehydration to -40 °F and below.

**Step 9 — Lean Glycol Cooling & Pumping:** Lean glycol is cooled in the exchanger, then pressurized to contactor pressure by the **Glycol Pump** (typically 100–125% of circulation rate).

### 2.2 Solid Desiccant Dehydration — System Architecture

#### 2.2.1 Common Desiccant Types

| Desiccant | Composition | Pore Size (Å) | Application |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Silica Gel** | SiO₂·xH₂O (amorphous) | 20–30 | Moderate dew point depression |
| **Activated Alumina** | Al₂O₃·H₂O (porous) | 30–50 | Low dew points, high capacity |
| **3A Molecular Sieve** | K-form Zeolite A | 3 Å | Drying of unsaturated hydrocarbons (olefins excluded) |
| **4A Molecular Sieve** | Na-form Zeolite A | 4 Å | General-purpose gas drying |
| **5A Molecular Sieve** | Ca-form Zeolite A | 5 Å | Drying + paraffin/iso-paraffin separation |
| **13X Molecular Sieve** | Na-form Zeolite X | 9–10 Å | Sour gas (H₂S, CO₂), large molecules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molecular sieves** are crystalline aluminosilicates with uniform pore openings. They adsorb molecules smaller than the pore diameter with extremely high selectivity — this is why they achieve ultra-low dew points inaccessible to glycol systems.

#### 2.2.2 Operating Modes

- **Conventional Two-Tower (A/B) Operation:** One tower adsorbs (on-line, 8–24 hr cycle) while the other regenerates and cools. Cycle duration depends on breakthrough.
- **Three-Tower Operation:** Adds a third tower for cooling/standby, used for high-pressure or high-throughput applications.
- **Layered Beds:** 4A on top + 13X at bottom — bulk water removal + polishing.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{W}{G} = \dfrac{(y_{in} - y_{out})}{x_{in}}$ | Glycol circulation ratio (lean-to-wet gas) | **gal TEG / lb H₂O** |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{Q_g (Y_{in} - Y_{out})}{1000}$ | Water to be removed per day | **lb/day** |
| 3 | $L = m \cdot W$ | Lean glycol circulation rate | **gal/hr** |
| 4 | $\mu_{glycol} = 10^{(A - B/T)}$ | TEG viscosity (A, B = empirical constants) | **cP** |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{(P_w - P_w^*)}{(P_w - P_w^{sat})}$ | Relative humidity / approach to saturation | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $y_w = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{total}}$ | Water mole fraction in gas (Raoult's law) | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $q = \dfrac{q_m K P}{(1 + KP)}$ | **Langmuir Isotherm** — solid desiccant capacity | **lb water / lb desiccant** |
| 8 | $q = K P^{1/n}$ | **Freundlich Isotherm** — empirical adsorption | **lb water / lb desiccant** |
| 9 | $t_b = \dfrac{L(\rho_b X_s)}{Q_g C_{in}}$ | Breakthrough time (stoichiometric) | **hours** |
| 10 | $L = \dfrac{M_{desiccant}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Desiccant bed volume | **ft³** |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{y_{in} - y_{out}}{y_{in}} \times 100$ | Dehydration efficiency | **%** |
| 12 | $\Delta T_{lean} = T_{reb} - T_{rich}^{out,exch}$ | Energy recovery effectiveness | **°F** |

> [!NOTE]
> For Langmuir isotherm, $q_m$ = monolayer capacity (lb/lb), $K$ = equilibrium constant, $P$ = partial pressure of water. For Freundlich, $K$ and $1/n$ are empirical, typically $0.1 \le 1/n \le 1.0$.

### 2.4 The Physical Chemistry Behind Each Method

**Glycol Dehydration** is governed by:
- **Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE):** TEG is hygroscopic → strong hydrogen bonding with water molecules (O···H–O bonds between glycol hydroxyl groups and water).
- **Equilibrium stages:** Calculated using the Kremser–Brown–Souders equation for multi-stage counter-current absorption.

**Solid Desiccant Dehydration** is governed by:
- **Physisorption (van der Waals forces):** Water molecules physically adhere to internal pore surfaces.
- **Polarity-driven selectivity:** Water (high dipole moment, **1.85 D**) is preferentially adsorbed over non-polar hydrocarbons.
- **Regeneration by Temperature Swing Adsorption (TSA):** Adsorption capacity decreases with temperature → heating at 500–600 °F (260–315 °C) drives off water.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG plants:** Require -150 °F dew point → solid desiccant mandatory.
- **Offshore platforms:** TEG preferred (compact, no moving parts except pump, vibration-tolerant).
- **Sour gas (high H₂S):** Solid desiccant with 13X molecular sieve (TEG would degrade with H₂S).
- **Helium recovery:** Deep drying with 4A/5A sieve essential to prevent ice formation in cryogenic distillation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Glycol Circulation Rate (TEG Mass Balance)

**Problem Statement:** A gas stream of 50 MMSCFD must be dehydrated from saturation at 1000 psia and 100 °F to a water content of 7 lb/MMSCF. Lean TEG concentration is 99.0 wt%; rich TEG leaves at 95.0 wt%. Determine the lean glycol circulation rate and the glycol-to-gas ratio.

**Step 1 — Establish Inlet Water Load:**

We use the ideal gas law approximation for water vapor content at saturation. First, water vapor pressure at 100 °F (using Antoine or tables): $P_w^{sat}(100°F) \approx 0.95$ psia.

$$y_{in} = \frac{P_w^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.95}{1000} = 9.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (lbmol H}_2\text{O / lbmol gas)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Mass Basis (lb H₂O / MMSCF):**

Using ideal gas molar volume at standard conditions (**379.5 ft³/lbmol** at 60 °F, 14.7 psia):

$$Y_{in} = y_{in} \times \frac{M_{H_2O}}{V_{std}} = \frac{9.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 18.015 \times 10^6 \text{ scf/MMSCF}}{379.5 \text{ scf/lbmol}}$$

$$Y_{in} = 45.1 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O / MMSCF}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Water to be Removed per Day:**

$$W = Q_g \times (Y_{in} - Y_{out}) = 50 \times (45.1 - 7.0) = 1905 \text{ lb/day}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Glycol Mass Balance Around Contactor:**

Let $L$ = lean glycol circulation rate (gal/hr), $\rho_{TEG} = 9.34$ lb/gal at 60 °F. Pure glycol mass flow: $L \rho_{TEG} = 9.34 L$ lb/hr.

Let $x_L$ = lean TEG mass fraction (= 0.990), $x_R$ = rich TEG mass fraction (= 0.950).

Mass balance on TEG (steady-state):
$$9.34 L \cdot x_L = 9.34 L \cdot x_R + \dot{m}_{H_2O,absorbed}$$

The water absorbed = $9.34 L (x_L - x_R) \cdot$ conversion to per-day basis:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2O,absorbed} = 9.34 L \cdot (0.990 - 0.950) = 0.3736 L \text{ lb/hr}$$

Converting to lb/day (×24):
$$\dot{m}_{H_2O,absorbed} = 8.97 L \text{ lb/day}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for L:**

Setting water absorbed = water to be removed:
$$8.97 L = 1905$$
$$L = \dfrac{1905}{8.97} = 212.4 \text{ gal/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Glycol-to-Gas Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{W}{G} = \dfrac{212.4 \text{ gal/hr} \times 24 \text{ hr/day}}{50 \text{ MMSCF/day} \times 1900 \text{ lb/MMSCF in dry gas basis}}$$

Wait — let me recompute using the standard TEG industry formula:

$$\dfrac{L}{Q_g} = \dfrac{(Y_{in} - Y_{out})}{\rho_{TEG}(x_L - x_R)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{24}$$

$$\dfrac{L}{Q_g} = \dfrac{(45.1 - 7.0)}{9.34 \times (0.990 - 0.950)} \times \dfrac{1}{24} = \dfrac{38.1}{0.3736} \times \dfrac{1}{24}$$

$$\dfrac{L}{Q_g} = 101.97 \times 0.04167 = 4.25 \text{ gal TEG / lb H}_2\text{O removed}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical industrial TEG-to-gas ratio is **2–6 gal TEG per lb H₂O removed**. Our computed value of **4.25** is squarely within design range. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Molecular Sieve Bed Breakthrough Time

**Problem Statement:** A 13X molecular sieve bed receives wet gas at 500 psig, 90 °F, with water loading of 50 lb H₂O / MMSCF. Gas flow = 20 MMSCFD. Bed diameter = 6 ft, bed height = 10 ft. Bulk density $\rho_b = 44$ lb/ft³. Equilibrium loading $X_s = 0.20$ lb H₂O / lb sieve. Compute the breakthrough time.

**Step 1 — Bed Volume and Mass of Desiccant:**

$$V_{bed} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6)^2 (10) = 282.7 \text{ ft}^3$$

$$M_{sieve} = V_{bed} \times \rho_b = 282.7 \times 44 = 12{,}440 \text{ lb sieve}$$

**Step 2 — Total Water Capacity of Bed:**

$$W_{cap} = M_{sieve} \times X_s = 12{,}440 \times 0.20 = 2488 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3 — Water Inlet Rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2O} = Q_g \times Y_{in} = 20 \text{ MMSCF/day} \times 50 \text{ lb/MMSCF} = 1000 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O / day}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Breakthrough Time:**

$$t_b = \dfrac{W_{cap}}{\dot{m}_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{2488}{1000} = 2.488 \text{ days} = 59.7 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Industrial Design Factor (Safety Margin):**

Real breakthrough occurs earlier due to mass transfer resistance and non-ideal plug flow. Industry uses a factor of 0.75–0.85:

$$t_{design} = 0.80 \times 59.7 = 47.8 \text{ hours}$$

A typical operating cycle = 24 hr adsorption + 8 hr regeneration + 4 hr cooling = ~36 hr total → design safe.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree

| If the Gas Stream Has... | Recommended System | Reason |
|--------------------------|--------------------|--------|
| CO₂ < 2 mol%, H₂S < 5 ppm, low pressure | TEG glycol | Economic, simple |
| H₂S > 5 ppm or CO₂ > 2 mol% | Solid desiccant (13X) | Glycol degradation, foaming |
| Required dew point < -40 °F | Solid desiccant (3A/4A) | Glycol cannot reach below -40 °F |
| NGL/LNG recovery downstream | Solid desiccant (molecular sieve) | Prevents ice & hydrate in cold box |
| Small flow (<5 MMSCFD), remote | TEG skid-mounted | Low OPEX, easy maintenance |
| Very large flow (>500 MMSCFD) | Solid desiccant with layered bed | Continuous, scalable |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Dew Point Calculation

**Given:** Wet gas at 800 psia, 80 °F, saturated with water. Find water content using Raoult's law.

$P_w^{sat}(80°F) \approx 0.5073$ psia (steam table data).

$$y_w = \dfrac{0.5073}{800} = 6.34 \times 10^{-4}$$

Convert to lb/MMSCF:
$$Y = y_w \times \dfrac{18.015}{379.5} \times 10^6 = 30.1 \text{ lb/MMSCF}$$

**Conclusion:** Gas contains 30.1 lb water per MMSCF — well above pipeline spec (7 lb/MMSCF) → dehydration required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TEG Glycol Dehydration Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WET GAS INLET FROM WELLHEAD"] --> nodeB["INLET SCRUBBER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GAS GAS HEATER OPTIONAL"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["GLYCOL CONTACTOR ABSORBER COLUMN"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DRY SALES GAS PIPELINE"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["RICH GLYCOL OUTLET"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["LEVEL CONTROL VALVE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["FLASH SEPARATOR 75 PSIG"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["FLASH GAS TO FLARE OR FUEL"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["RICH GLYCOL FILTER CARBON + PARTICULATE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["GLYCOL GLYCOL HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["REGENERATOR STRIPPING COLUMN"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["REBOILER 400 DEG F FIRED"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["WATER VENT OVERHEAD"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["LEAN GLYCOL STORAGE"]
    nodeO --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeP["GLYCOL PUMP HIGH PRESSURE"]
    nodeP --> nodeD
    nodeM -.-> nodeQ["STRIPPING GAS INJECTION OPTIONAL"]
    Q --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solid Desiccant (Two-Tower) Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WET GAS INLET"] --> nodeB["INLET SEPARATOR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FILTER COALESCER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TOWER A ON LINE ADSORPTION"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["TOWER B ON LINE ADSORPTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["DRY GAS OUTLET PIPELINE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG["REGENERATION GAS HEATER 600 DEG F"] --> nodeH["TOWER B REGENERATION MODE"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["REGEN GAS COOLER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["WATER SEPARATOR KNOCKOUT"]
```
