```mermaid
    nodeJ --> nodeK["REGEN GAS BLOWER"]
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeL["TOWER B COOLING MODE"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["COOL GAS RECYCLE"]
    nodeM --> nodeK
    nodeD -.-> nodeH
    nodeE -.-> nodeD
```

### 4.3 Process Comparison Schematic — TEG vs. Solid Desiccant Mass Transfer Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TEG["TEG GLYCOL ABSORPTION COLUMN"]
        TEG1["WET GAS IN BOTTOM"]
        TEG2["COUNTER CURRENT CONTACT"]
        TEG3["LEAN TEG IN TOP"]
        TEG4["DRY GAS OUT TOP"]
        TEG5["RICH TEG OUT BOTTOM"]
        TEG1 --> TEG2 --> TEG4
        TEG3 --> TEG2 --> TEG5
    end
    subgraph SIEVE["MOLECULAR SIEVE FIXED BED"]
        SV1["WET GAS IN"]
        SV2["ADSORPTION FRONT MOVING DOWN"]
        SV3["SATURATED ZONE BOTTOM"]
        SV4["FRESH SIEVE ZONE TOP"]
        SV5["DRY GAS OUT"]
        SV1 --> SV2 --> SV5
        SV4 --> SV2 --> SV3
    end
```

### 4.4 Heat & Mass Transfer Pathway Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INLET GAS TEMPERATURE"] --> nodeB["DEW POINT DEPRESSION TARGET"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MASS TRANSFER COEFFICIENT K_G A"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["THERMAL APPROACH T_GAS MINUS T_AMIN"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["REQUIRED GLYCOL CIRCULATION"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["REQUIRED SIEVE MASS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG["REBOILER DUTY Q REBOILER"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["REGEN GAS DUTY Q REGEN"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["OPERATING COST USD YEAR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always label your PFDs with **operating temperatures and pressures** at each major stream. KTU evaluators award full marks only when mass balance is implied across the boundary, not just when blocks are drawn.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What are the main reasons for dehydrating natural gas before transportation?**

**Model Answer:**
Natural gas dehydration is performed to:
1. **Prevent hydrate formation** — water + light hydrocarbons at high pressure and low temperature form solid crystalline hydrates that block pipelines and equipment.
2. **Prevent corrosion** — dissolved CO₂ and H₂S combined with water form weak acids (carbonic and hydrosulfuric acid) that corrode carbon steel piping.
3. **Improve heating value** — water vapor reduces combustion efficiency and adds non-combustible mass.
4. **Meet pipeline specification** — typically ≤ **7 lb H₂O / MMSCF** for sales gas.
5. **Protect downstream processes** — LNG, NGL, and cryogenic units require ultra-low moisture to prevent ice and hydrate formation.

(3 marks: 1 mark for each correctly explained point × 3 points)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between absorption and adsorption in the context of gas dehydration.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Absorption | Adsorption |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Mechanism** | Water dissolved into liquid (glycol) | Water molecules adhere to solid surface |
| **Medium** | Liquid desiccant (TEG, DEG, MEG) | Solid desiccant (silica, alumina, molecular sieve) |
| **Process** | Bulk separation via VLE | Surface phenomenon via pore filling |
| **Regeneration** | Reboiler + distillation | Thermal swing (heating) or pressure swing |
| **Selectivity** | Limited (water only) | Size & polarity selective (molecular sieves) |
| **Capacity** | High throughput, moderate dew point | Lower throughput, ultra-low dew point |

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for mechanism + 1 mark for application)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — Glycol Dehydration Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the working of a **Triethylene Glycol (TEG) dehydration unit**. State the operating temperature and pressure at the contactor and regenerator.

**Model Answer:**

The TEG dehydration unit consists of the following major components (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Inlet Scrubber (V-101):** Removes free liquids and entrained water. Operating at line pressure (700–1200 psig).
2. **Glycol Contactor (C-101):** Vertical tray-type (6–8 theoretical trays) or packed column. Wet gas enters bottom and rises; lean TEG enters top and falls counter-currently. Operating pressure = contactor pressure (≈ gas line pressure, typically 1000 psig). Operating temperature ≈ gas inlet temperature (80–120 °F).
3. **Flash Separator (V-102):** Rich glycol depressurized to 50–75 psig; dissolved hydrocarbons flash off.
4. **Filters (Carbon & Particulate):** Remove degradation products and solids.
5. **Glycol–Glycol Exchanger (E-101):** Pre-heats rich glycol using hot lean glycol (counter-current heat exchange).
6. **Regenerator (C-102) and Reboiler (E-102):** Rich glycol enters top; reboiler operates at **400 °F (204 °C)** at atmospheric pressure (≈ 1 atm). Water vapor exits overhead; lean TEG (99.0+ wt%) collects at reboiler sump.
7. **Glycol Pump (P-101):** Pressurizes lean TEG back to contactor pressure.

**Key Operating Parameters:**
- Contactor: 80–120 °F, 700–1200 psig
- Regenerator: 400 °F, ~1 atm
- Lean TEG concentration: 99.0 wt% (with stripping gas: 99.99 wt%)

(7 marks: 2 for PFD, 2 for component description, 2 for operating parameters, 1 for principle)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A natural gas stream of **75 MMSCFD** at **900 psia** and **110 °F** is saturated with water. It must be dehydrated to **7 lb/MMSCF** using lean TEG of **99.5 wt%** concentration. The rich glycol leaves at **96.0 wt%**. Compute:
  (i) Total water to be removed per day
  (ii) Lean TEG circulation rate (gal/hr)
  (iii) Glycol-to-gas ratio (gal TEG / lb H₂O removed)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $Q_g = 75$ MMSCFD
- $P = 900$ psia, $T = 110$ °F
- $P_w^{sat}(110°F) = 1.275$ psia (from steam tables)
- $x_L = 0.995$, $x_R = 0.960$, $\rho_{TEG} = 9.34$ lb/gal

**Step 1 — Inlet water content:**
$$y_{in} = \dfrac{1.275}{900} = 1.417 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Y_{in} = 1.417 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{18.015 \times 10^6}{379.5} = 67.27 \text{ lb/MMSCF}$$

**Step 2 — Water to be removed per day:**
$$W = 75 \times (67.27 - 7.0) = 4520 \text{ lb/day} = 188.3 \text{ lb/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Lean TEG circulation:**
$$L = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{TEG} \cdot (x_L - x_R) \times 24}$$

$$L = \dfrac{4520}{9.34 \times (0.995 - 0.960) \times 24}$$

$$L = \dfrac{4520}{9.34 \times 0.035 \times 24} = \dfrac{4520}{7.846} = 576.1 \text{ gal/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Glycol-to-gas ratio:**
$$\dfrac{L}{Q_g} = \dfrac{576.1 \times 24}{75 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{1}{379.5} \times 18.015 / 379.5 \text{ (mass of dry gas per MMSCF)}}$$

Simpler industry formula:
$$\text{GGR} = \dfrac{L \times 24 \text{ hr/day}}{Q_g \times \dfrac{Y_{in} - Y_{out}}{1000} \text{ (in 1000s)} \times \rho_{TEG}}$$

$$\text{GGR} = \dfrac{576.1 \times 24}{75 \times (67.27 - 7.0) / 1000 \times 9.34} = \dfrac{13{,}826}{42{,}217.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Actually using direct ratio:
$$\dfrac{L}{Q_g} = \dfrac{576.1}{75 \times 10^6 / 24 \text{ (per hr)}} = \dfrac{576.1}{3.125 \times 10^6} = 1.843 \times 10^{-4} \text{ gal/scf}$$

Convert to **gal/lb H₂O removed**:
$$\text{GGR} = \dfrac{576.1 \text{ gal/hr}}{188.3 \text{ lb/hr}} = 3.06 \text{ gal TEG / lb H}_2\text{O}$$

**Final Results:**
- $W = 4520$ lb/day
- $L = 576.1$ gal/hr
- GGR = 3.06 gal TEG / lb H₂O ✓ (within typical 2–6 range)

(7 marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 1 for Step 3, 2 for Step 4)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Solid Desiccant Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, types, and operating cycle** of solid desiccant dehydration using molecular sieves. Compare 3A, 4A, and 13X molecular sieves.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Molecular sieves are crystalline aluminosilicates (zeolites) with uniform pore openings. They dehydrate gas by **physical adsorption** of water molecules inside their cage-like crystal structure. Water (kinetic diameter ≈ 2.6 Å) is smaller than pore openings (3–10 Å depending on type), so it is selectively trapped while larger hydrocarbon molecules pass through.

**Types:**
- **3A (Potassium-form Zeolite A):** Pore size 3 Å. Used for drying **unsaturated hydrocarbons and polar compounds** (olefins excluded).
- **4A (Sodium-form Zeolite A):** Pore size 4 Å. **General-purpose** drying of natural gas, air, hydrogen.
- **13X (Sodium-form Zeolite X):** Pore size 9–10 Å. **Sour gas** drying, removes H₂S, CO₂, mercaptans.

**Operating Cycle (Two-Tower TSA):**
1. **Adsorption (8–24 hr):** Wet gas flows downward through bed; water adsorbed. Outlet dew point ≤ -100 °F.
2. **Depressurization:** Bed pressure reduced.
3. **Heating (Regeneration, 500–600 °F):** Hot regeneration gas flows upward through bed; adsorbed water desorbs.
4. **Cooling:** Bed cooled by cold gas stream to adsorption temperature.
5. **Repressurization:** Bed brought back to line pressure; cycle repeats.

(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for types, 3 for cycle description)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A molecular sieve dehydration unit processes **30 MMSCFD** of natural gas containing **80 lb H₂O/MMSCF** at 600 psig, 90 °F. The bed contains **15,000 lb** of 4A sieve with **equilibrium loading 0.18 lb H₂O/lb sieve**. Calculate:
  (i) Stoichiometric breakthrough time
  (ii) Design breakthrough time using 0.75 safety factor
  (iii) Daily water loading on the bed (in lbs/day)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 30$ MMSCFD
- $Y_{in} = 80$ lb/MMSCF
- $M_{sieve} = 15{,}000$ lb
- $X_s = 0.18$ lb H₂O/lb sieve

**Step 1 — Water capacity:**
$$W_{cap} = 15{,}000 \times 0.18 = 2700 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Daily water load:**
$$\dot{m} = 30 \times 80 = 2400 \text{ lb/day}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric breakthrough time:**
$$t_{stoich} = \dfrac{2700}{2400} = 1.125 \text{ days} = 27 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Design breakthrough time:**
$$t_{design} = 0.75 \times 27 = 20.25 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Operational implication:**
With 20.25 hr adsorption time, the second tower has:
- 8 hr regeneration
- 4 hr cooling
- Total cycle ≈ 32 hr

This suggests upgrading the bed mass or installing a third tower for continuous operation without bed saturation.

**Final Results:**
- Stoichiometric $t_b = 27$ hr
- Design $t_b = 20.25$ hr
- Daily water = 2400 lb/day

(7 marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 3, 2 for Step 4, 1 for operational interpretation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing lb/MMSCF with kg/m³ without conversion = **lose 2 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert MMSCFD → MMSC/hr** when computing rates per hour = **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing rich vs. lean TEG concentrations** ($x_R$ vs. $x_L$) in mass balance = **wrong answer**.
> 4. **Neglecting design safety factor** in breakthrough time = marks deducted (always use 0.7–0.85 factor).
> 5. **Not labeling streams** in PFDs with T and P = **lose 1 mark per unlabeled stream** (up to 3 marks).
> 6. **Missing the VLE basis (Raoult's law)** for dew point calculation = lose conceptual credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Quick-Recall Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ⭐ Natural gas must be dehydrated to **≤ 7 lb/MMSCF** for pipeline sales gas.
- ⭐ **TEG (Triethylene Glycol)** is the most common liquid desiccant; boiling point 549 °F, density 9.34 lb/gal.
- ⭐ TEG contactor operates at **line pressure (700–1200 psig)**, regenerator at **400 °F, 1 atm**.
- ⭐ Typical glycol-to-gas ratio: **2–6 gal TEG / lb H₂O removed**.
- ⭐ **Solid desiccants:** silica gel, activated alumina, molecular sieves (3A, 4A, 5A, 13X).
- ⭐ **Molecular sieve pore sizes:** 3A (3 Å), 4A (4 Å), 5A (5 Å), 13X (9–10 Å).
- ⭐ Molecular sieves achieve dew points **below -100 °F** — required for LNG/cryogenic service.
- ⭐ **Regeneration by TSA** (Temperature Swing Adsorption) at 500–600 °F.
- ⭐ **Langmuir** isotherm: $q = q_m K P / (1 + KP)$ — monolayer adsorption.
- ⭐ **Freundlich** isotherm: $q = K P^{1/n}$ — empirical, multilayer.
- ⭐ **Breakthrough time** design factor = 0.75–0.85 × stoichiometric value.
- ⭐ **Hydrate prevention** requires water content below equilibrium value at line T and P.
- ⭐ **Sour gas** dehydration prefers 13X molecular sieve (TEG degrades with H₂S).
- ⭐ **Major TEG components to memorize:** Inlet scrubber, contactor, flash separator, filters, glycol-glycol exchanger, regenerator column, reboiler, stripping gas (optional), glycol pump.
- ⭐ **Major Sieve Tower Components:** Adsorption bed, regeneration gas heater, regen gas cooler, water knockout drum, recycle blower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "I-G-F-E-R-R-L-P":**
> **I**nlet scrubber → **G**as-glycol contactor → **F**lash separator → **F**ilters → **E**xchanger → **R**egenerator → **R**eboiler → **L**ean glycol → **P**ump.
> This is the order of TEG process flow — recite this in the exam if you forget!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acid Gas Removal: Metal Oxide Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Acid Gas Removal: Metal Oxide Process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Metal Oxide Process Definition:** > The **Metal Oxide Process** (also called the **dry bed scavenging process** or **solid-bed absorption process**) is a non-catalytic, fixed-bed gas-treating technology used in natural gas processing to remove **acid gases — primarily hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)** and, in some cases, mercaptans (RSH) and carbonyl sulfide (COS) — by chemically reacting them with a **solid metal oxide** to form a corresponding **metal sulfide** and water. The most industrially significant metal oxides used are: | Metal Oxide | Chemical Formula | Key Application | |-------------|------------------|-----------------| | **Zinc Oxide** | **ZnO** | High-purity polishing of natural gas, LNG feed, synthesis gas | | **Iron Oxide** | **Fe₂O₃** | Bulk H₂S scavenging in older units and "iron sponge" towers | | **Copper Oxide** | **CuO** | Combined H₂S and trace HCN removal | | **Manganese Oxide** | **MnO** | Specialized sulfur polishing | | **Zinc Ferrite** | **ZnFe₂O₄** | High-temperature IGCC syngas cleanup | The general, idealized global stoichiometric reaction is: $$\text{MO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{S}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \text{MS}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$ where **M** is a bivalent metal (Zn, Fe, Mn, Cu, etc.) and the metal oxide–metal sulfide transformation is effectively **irreversible** at standard operating conditions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The Metal Oxide Process is classified under **"Reaction-Type Acid Gas Removal"** in Module 3, distinct from the **"Absorption-Type"** processes like Amine (MDEA, MEA), Selexol, Rectisol, and Sulfinol. KTU specifically tests the difference between **regenerative (chemical/physical solvent) processes** and **non-regenerative solid scavenger (metal oxide) processes.** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!TIP] > **"The Magnet That Swallows the Smelly Molecule" — A Real-World Analogy** > > Imagine you have a glass of water contaminated with a foul-smelling impurity. Instead of boiling the water and capturing the vapor (distillation), you pour the water through a **column packed with activated charcoal**. The charcoal **chemically binds** the impurity and the water that emerges is clean. Once the charcoal is "full," you throw it away. > > The **Metal Oxide Process works on exactly this principle**, but in the **gas phase** and at **elevated temperatures**: > - The **natural gas stream** = the contaminated water. > - The **metal oxide bed (ZnO, Fe₂O₃)** = the activated charcoal. > - The **H₂S molecule** = the foul-smelling impurity. > - The **metal sulfide (ZnS, FeS)** = the "spent" charcoal that must be replaced or regenerated. > > The key engineering insight: H₂S is **acidic** (it donates a proton to form HS⁻), and the metal oxide surface acts as a **base-like scavenger** that permanently locks the sulfur atom into a solid crystal lattice. --- ### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics & Physical Constants - **Operating temperature window:** **150 °C – 400 °C** (ZnO polishing units typically run at **200 °C – 400 °C**). - **Operating pressure:** **1 atm – 70 bar** (process is relatively pressure-insensitive because reaction is chemisorption-driven). - **Residual H₂S outlet concentration:** Down to **< 1 ppb** (parts per billion) for ZnO polishing beds. - **Bulk density of ZnO pellets:** ~**1.0 – 1.4 g/cm³** (industry standard). - **Standard heat of reaction (ZnO + H₂S):** **ΔH° ≈ −96.7 kJ/mol of H₂S** (moderately exothermic). - **Sulfur capacity (commercial ZnO):** **15 – 35 weight %** of the bed as retained sulfur before breakthrough. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Metal Oxide Process is **non-regenerative in most commercial applications** (the sulfide is sent to a metals smelter for sulfur recovery and metal recovery), although **regenerative variants** (using air/oxygen to oxidize MS back to MO with elemental sulfur release) exist for niche applications. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic The Metal Oxide Process is governed by a heterogeneous gas–solid reaction. The mechanism can be broken into the following logical steps, which KTU examiners frequently test in Part A and Part B: **Step 1 — Gas-Solid Contact (External Diffusion):** The sour natural gas (containing H₂S, CO₂, water vapor, and hydrocarbons) is distributed across a fixed bed of porous metal oxide pellets (typical pellet size: **3 – 6 mm**). H₂S molecules diffuse through the **gas film** surrounding each pellet. This is governed by an external mass transfer coefficient **k_g**. **Step 2 — Internal Diffusion (Pore Diffusion):** H₂S molecules diffuse into the internal pore network of the pellet. This step is characterized by an **effective pore diffusivity (D_eff)**, which depends on pellet porosity (typically **0.4 – 0.55**) and tortuosity. **Step 3 — Chemisorption and Chemical Reaction:** At the active internal surface, H₂S reacts with the metal oxide according to the surface reaction: $$\text{MO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{S}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \text{MS}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$ The reaction is **exothermic** and proceeds via proton transfer (H⁺ from H₂S to O²⁻ in the lattice, releasing H₂O and forming the sulfide anion S²⁻ in the lattice). **Step 4 — Water Vapor Desorption:** The product H₂O diffuses out of the pellet and is carried away by the gas stream. In dry-feed units, no liquid water is ever formed (operating above the dew point). **Step 5 — Bed Saturation & Breakthrough:** As the bed becomes saturated with sulfide, the **breakthrough curve** rises sharply. Operators monitor **outlet H₂S** and replace the bed once the breakthrough concentration exceeds the design specification (typically **1 ppmv** for pipeline gas, **< 50 ppb** for LNG feed). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Why" is the key KTU focus:** > The reaction is **thermodynamically favorable** at all practical natural gas treating temperatures (K_eq is huge for ZnO). The exothermic nature means the reaction is **self-favoring** at low temperatures, but kinetics (reaction rate) **increase** with temperature, leading to an **optimal operating window of 200 – 400 °C** for ZnO. --- ### 2.2 Why Metal Oxides? — The Thermodynamic & Kinetic "Why" - **Why ZnO for polishing?** ZnO has the **highest thermodynamic driving force** for H₂S scavenging among common oxides at low temperatures. The Gibbs free energy change ΔG° for ZnO + H₂S → ZnS + H₂O is approximately **−90 kJ/mol** at 25 °C, ensuring **irreversibility**. - **Why Fe₂O₃ for bulk removal?** Iron oxide is **cheap, abundant, and regenerable with air** (Fe₂S₃ + 1.5 O₂ → Fe₂O₃ + 3 S). The so-called "iron sponge" process uses wood-chip impregnated Fe₂O₃·H₂O. - **Why not CaO (lime)?** CaO works but forms CaSO₄ (calcium sulfate) in the presence of O₂, which **swells and plugs the bed**. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the Metal Oxide Process. Memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE and Continuous Assessment tests. | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|----------------------|---------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{MO} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \to \text{MS} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Global reaction stoichiometry | — | | 2 | $W_{ZnO} = \dfrac{m_{H_2S} \cdot M_{ZnO}}{M_{H_2S} \cdot \eta}$ | Theoretical mass of ZnO required to scavenge a given mass of H₂S | kg | | 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{actual sulfur captured}}{\text{theoretical sulfur capacity}}$ | Bed utilization efficiency (commercial: 0.85 – 0.95) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -96.7~\text{kJ/mol}$ for ZnO+H₂S | Heat released per mole of H₂S reacted | kJ/mol | | 5 | $\dot{Q}_{bed} = \dot{n}_{H_2S} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert$ | Heat generation rate in adiabatic bed | kW | | 6 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference for inter-stage cooling | K | | 7 | $X_{H_2S} = 1 - \exp(-k \cdot \tau)$ | Pseudo-first-order conversion in packed bed (Arrhenius k) | dimensionless | | 8 | $k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant for the surface reaction | s⁻¹ | | 9 | $S_{cap} = \dfrac{m_S^{retained}}{m_{bed}^{initial}} \times 100$ | Sulfur capacity of the bed (commercial ZnO: 15 – 35 wt%) | % | | 10 | $\tau_{breakthrough} = \dfrac{\rho_{bed} \cdot S_{cap}}{y_{H_2S,in} \cdot \dot{m}_{gas}}$ | Estimated bed life before breakthrough | hours | | 11 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A_c}$ | Superficial pressure drop in packed bed (Ergun equation use) | Pa | | 12 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon^3} v_s + \dfrac{1.75 \rho (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \varepsilon^3} v_s^2$ | Ergun equation for bed pressure drop | Pa/m | | 13 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert}{C_{p,gas}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise across the bed | K | | 14 | $M_{sulfur} = \dot{n}_{H_2S}^{removed} \cdot M_S \cdot t_{op}$ | Total sulfur mass captured during operating period | kg | > Where: > - $M_{ZnO}$ = **81.38 g/mol**, $M_{H_2S}$ = **34.08 g/mol**, $M_S$ = **32.06 g/mol** > - $\dot{n}_{H_2S}$ = molar flow rate of H₂S (mol/s) > - $\rho_{bed}$ = bed bulk density (kg/m³) > - $d_p$ = pellet diameter, $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity, $v_s$ = superficial velocity > - $y_{H_2S,in}$ = inlet H₂S mole fraction ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Mass of Metal Oxide Required **Problem Setup:** A natural gas stream flowing at $\dot{V}$ = **10,000 Nm³/h** (at STP) contains $y_{H_2S}$ = **50 ppmv**. It is to be polished in a **ZnO fixed-bed reactor**. The bed has a sulfur capacity of $S_{cap}$ = **30 wt%** and bed utilization efficiency $\eta$ = **0.90**. The bed is to operate for $t_{op}$ = **8000 hours** before change-out. Calculate: (a) the mass of ZnO required, (b) the daily sulfur mass captured, (c) the adiabatic temperature rise of the gas (assuming $C_{p,gas}$ = **2.5 kJ/(kg·K)** and average MW = **20 g/mol**). --- #### Part (a) — Mass of ZnO Required **Step 1: Convert gas flow to molar flow.** Using ideal gas law at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa): $$\dot{n}_{gas} = \dfrac{P \dot{V}}{R T} = \dfrac{(101.325~\text{kPa})(10{,}000~\text{Nm}^3/\text{h})}{8.314~\text{kJ/(kmol·K)} \cdot 273.15~\text{K}}$$ $$\dot{n}_{gas} = \dfrac{1{,}013{,}250}{2270.94} = 446.20~\text{kmol/h}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the molar flow of H₂S entering the bed.** $$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = y_{H_2S} \cdot \dot{n}_{gas} = (50 \times 10^{-6})(446.20)$$ $$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = 0.02231~\text{kmol/h} = 22.31~\text{mol/h}$$ **Step 3: Calculate total moles of H₂S to be captured over the operating period.** $$n_{H_2S}^{total} = \dot{n}_{H_2S} \cdot t_{op} = (0.02231~\text{kmol/h})(8000~\text{h})$$ $$n_{H_2S}^{total} = 178.48~\text{kmol}$$ **Step 4: Apply stoichiometry — 1 mol ZnO captures 1 mol H₂S.** $$n_{ZnO}^{stoich} = n_{H_2S}^{total} = 178.48~\text{kmol}$$ **Step 5: Convert to mass of pure ZnO.** $$m_{ZnO}^{pure} = n_{ZnO}^{stoich} \cdot M_{ZnO} = (178.48~\text{kmol})(81.38~\text{kg/kmol})$$ $$m_{ZnO}^{pure} = 14{,}524.5~\text{kg}$$ **Step 6: Account for bed utilization efficiency.** $$m_{ZnO}^{actual} = \dfrac{m_{ZnO}^{pure}}{\eta} = \dfrac{14{,}524.5}{0.90} = 16{,}138.4~\text{kg}$$ $$\boxed{m_{ZnO}^{actual} \approx 16{,}138~\text{kg} \approx 16.14~\text{tonnes}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Daily Sulfur Mass Captured **Step 1: Calculate daily H₂S moles removed.** $$n_{H_2S}^{day} = \dot{n}_{H_2S} \cdot 24~\text{h} = (0.02231~\text{kmol/h})(24~\text{h})$$ $$n_{H_2S}^{day} = 0.5354~\text{kmol/day}$$ **Step 2: Each H₂S molecule contains 1 sulfur atom (1:1 stoichiometry of S).** $$\dot{m}_S = n_{H_2S}^{day} \cdot M_S = (0.5354~\text{kmol/day})(32.06~\text{kg/kmol})$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_S \approx 17.17~\text{kg/day of sulfur captured}}$$ --- #### Part (c) — Adiabatic Temperature Rise **Step 1: Calculate heat generation rate.** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{n}_{H_2S} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert = (0.02231~\text{kmol/h})(96{,}700~\text{kJ/kmol})$$ $$\dot{Q} = 2157.4~\text{kJ/h} = 0.599~\text{kW}$$ **Step 2: Calculate mass flow rate of gas.** $$\dot{m}_{gas} = \dot{n}_{gas} \cdot MW_{gas} = (446.20~\text{kmol/h})(20~\text{kg/kmol}) = 8924~\text{kg/h}$$ **Step 3: Apply energy balance for adiabatic temperature rise.** $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas}} = \dfrac{2157.4~\text{kJ/h}}{(8924~\text{kg/h})(2.5~\text{kJ/(kg·K)})}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{2157.4}{22{,}310} = 0.0967~\text{K}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} \approx 0.097~\text{K (essentially isothermal at 50 ppmv)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** At only 50 ppmv H₂S, the adiabatic temperature rise is negligible (< 0.1 K), confirming that the bed operates essentially isothermally. However, for **sour gases with 1 – 5% H₂S**, $\Delta T_{ad}$ can reach **20 – 100 K**, requiring **inter-stage cooling** or careful thermal management. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Breakthrough Time for a Single Bed **Given:** - Bed mass: $m_{bed}$ = **5,000 kg of ZnO** - Sulfur capacity: $S_{cap}$ = **30 wt%** → maximum sulfur captured = **0.30 × 5000 = 1500 kg S** - Inlet H₂S mole fraction: $y_{H_2S,in}$ = **200 ppmv** - Gas flow: $\dot{n}_{gas}$ = **200 kmol/h** - Operating temperature: **T = 350 °C, P = 30 bar** **Step 1: Compute mass flow rate of H₂S entering the bed.** $$\dot{m}_{H_2S} = \dot{n}_{gas} \cdot y_{H_2S} \cdot M_{H_2S} = (200~\text{kmol/h})(200 \times 10^{-6})(34.08~\text{kg/kmol})$$ $$\dot{m}_{H_2S} = 1.3632~\text{kg/h}$$ **Step 2: Apply breakthrough time equation (Formula #10):** $$\tau_{breakthrough} = \dfrac{m_S^{retained}}{\dot{m}_{H_2S}} = \dfrac{1500~\text{kg}}{1.3632~\text{kg/h}}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{breakthrough} \approx 1100~\text{hours} \approx 45.8~\text{days}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Bed for High-Concentration H₂S (Engineering Design Check) For a **highly sour gas** with $y_{H_2S}$ = **2 mol%** at 30 bar and 40 °C: **Step 1: Adiabatic temperature rise per Formula #13.** $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert}{C_{p,gas}} = \dfrac{(0.02)(96{,}700)}{2.5 \times 20}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{1934}{50} = 38.7~\text{K}$$ > [!WARNING] > A 38.7 K temperature rise can **thermally sinter** the ZnO pellets and **sharply reduce** the active surface area. Industrial designs use **multiple beds with inter-stage cooling** to keep $\Delta T$ per bed below **20 K**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Metal Oxide Scavenging Train ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sour Natural Gas Inlet
H2S = 200 ppmv
T = 40 C, P = 30 bar] --> B[Inlet Gas Filter
Coalescer and Particulate Removal] B --> C[Pre-Heater
Trim Heater to 200 C] C --> D[Primary ZnO Reactor
Fixed Bed Vessel 1
V1 = 25 m3] D --> E[Inter-Stage Cooler
Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger] E --> F[Polishing ZnO Reactor
Fixed Bed Vessel 2
V2 = 10 m3] F --> G[Outlet H2S Analyzer
Continuous Online
Detection Limit 1 ppb] G --> H[Treated Gas to Pipeline
H2S less than 1 ppb
T = 180 C, P = 29.5 bar] D --> D1[Sulfur Mass Captured
Bed 1 = 1500 kg S
Before Changeout] F --> F1[Sulfur Mass Captured
Bed 2 = 600 kg S
Polishing Zone] D1 --> I[Spent ZnS Sent to
Zinc Smelter for
Metal Recovery and S Recovery] F1 --> I H --> J[Sales Gas Export
Pipeline Spec = 4 ppmv H2S
Achieved 1 ppb] ``` ### 4.2 Reactor Internal Architecture — Fixed-Bed ZnO Vessel ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VESSEL[Reactor Pressure Vessel — Carbon Steel, SA 516 Gr 70] direction TB TOP[Top Inlet Nozzle
Sour Gas Distributor] --> GB1[Inlet Distributor
Perforated Pipe] GB1 --> MASS1[Top Ceramic Balls
5 cm Support Layer] MASS1 --> BED1[Upper ZnO Bed
Height = 2.5 m
Pellet Size = 4 mm] BED1 --> BED2[Middle ZnO Bed
Height = 2.5 m
Pellet Size = 4 mm] BED2 --> MASS2[Bottom Ceramic Balls
5 cm Support Layer] MASS2 --> SCREEN[Outlet Screen
20 mesh SS316] SCREEN --> BTM[Bottom Outlet Nozzle
Treated Gas] end BED1 --> RTD1[Thermocouple TC1
Radial Bed Profile] BED1 --> RTD2[Thermocouple TC2
Mid-Bed Profile] BED1 --> RTD3[Thermocouple TC3
Near Outlet] TH1[Reaction ZnO + H2S to ZnS + H2O
Exothermic -96.7 kJ per mol] -.-> BED1 TH2[Spent Bed Removed at
S content greater than 30 wt%] -.-> BED2 ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Metal Oxide vs. Amine Process ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sour Natural Gas
10 to 10000 ppmv H2S] --> B{Selection Logic} B -->|Low H2S less than 50 ppmv
Polishing Required| C[Metal Oxide Process
ZnO Fixed Bed
Non-Regenerative] B -->|Moderate H2S 50 to 1000 ppmv
Bulk Removal| D[Amine Process
MDEA or MEA
Regenerative] B -->|High H2S greater than 1 percent
Claus Feed| E[Claus Process
Partial Oxidation
Elemental Sulfur] C --> F[Residual H2S less than 1 ppb] D --> G[Residual H2S 1 to 10 ppmv] E --> H[Sulfur Recovery 95 to 98 percent] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define the Metal Oxide Process for acid gas removal. List any **two** commercial metal oxides used in the process and state **one** advantage of the process over the conventional amine process.* **Model Answer:** The Metal Oxide Process is a **dry, fixed-bed gas treating technology** in which acid gases (primarily H₂S) present in natural gas are removed by **chemical reaction with a solid metal oxide** to form a **metal sulfide** and water. $$\text{MO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{S}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \text{MS}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$ **Two commercial metal oxides:** 1. **Zinc Oxide (ZnO)** — used for high-purity polishing (down to ppb levels). 2. **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃)** — used in the "iron sponge" process for bulk H₂S removal. **One advantage over the amine process:** - The metal oxide process achieves **ultra-low H₂S residuals (< 1 ppb)** which amine systems cannot reliably reach, and it requires **no aqueous solvent**, eliminating foaming, corrosion, and solvent degradation issues. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark question expects 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming two oxides, and 1 mark for the comparison. Avoid vague answers like "it is good" — quantify (e.g., "achieves < 1 ppb vs. 1 – 4 ppmv for amine"). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Explain why **zinc oxide** is preferred over **iron oxide** for the **final polishing** of natural gas intended for **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** production.* **Model Answer:** Zinc oxide is preferred for LNG polishing for the following reasons: 1. **Ultra-low residual H₂S:** ZnO achieves outlet H₂S concentrations below **1 ppb** (parts per billion), which is mandatory for LNG feed gas (H₂S must be < 3.5 mg/Nm³ to avoid corrosion of aluminum brazed heat exchangers, equivalent to < 2.4 ppmv — but ZnO over-shoots this comfortably). 2. **Higher thermodynamic driving force:** The reaction ZnO + H₂S → ZnS + H₂O has ΔG° ≈ **−90 kJ/mol** at 25 °C, which is more negative than the corresponding Fe₂O₃ reaction, making it **irreversible** at LNG polishing temperatures (200 – 350 °C). 3. **Higher sulfur capacity:** Commercial ZnO pellets have a sulfur capacity of **15 – 35 wt%**, compared to ~10 wt% for iron sponge. 4. **No regenerable sulfur buildup:** ZnO produces a stable, non-pyrophoric sulfide (ZnS) that can be safely sent to a zinc smelter, whereas iron sponge regeneration can produce **pyrophoric iron sulfide** and elemental sulfur deposits that pose safety hazards. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into two sub-parts: **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (Module Internal Choice). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]* **A(a).** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **working principle** of the **Metal Oxide Process** for H₂S removal from natural gas. Discuss the chemistry, operating temperature range, and product disposal strategy. **[7 Marks]** **A(b).** A natural gas plant treats **50,000 Nm³/h** of feed gas containing **150 ppmv of H₂S** using a **ZnO fixed-bed polishing unit**. The bed has a sulfur capacity of **28 wt%** and a bed utilization efficiency of **0.92**. Determine: - (i) The **mass of ZnO** required for **one year (8760 hours)** of continuous operation. **[4 Marks]** - (ii) The **mass of ZnS** generated as spent solid waste per year. **[3 Marks]** **Given:** $M_{ZnO}$ = 81.38 g/mol, $M_{ZnS}$ = 97.44 g/mol, $M_{H_2S}$ = 34.08 g/mol, $M_S$ = 32.06 g/mol. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Working Principle:** The Metal Oxide Process operates as a **fixed-bed heterogeneous reactor** where sour natural gas is passed through a packed bed of porous metal oxide pellets (typically ZnO, Fe₂O₃, or ZnFe₂O₄). H₂S in the gas reacts chemically with the metal oxide to form a metal sulfide, and the gas leaving the bed is virtually H₂S-free. **Flow Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sour Gas
H2S 150 ppmv
T 40 C] --> B[Filter] B --> C[Heater to 250 C] C --> D[ZnO Fixed Bed
Height 3 m, Diameter 2 m] D --> E[Treated Gas
H2S less than 1 ppb] D --> F[Spent Bed
ZnS Pellets] F --> G[Sent to Zinc Smelter] ``` **Chemistry:** $$\text{ZnO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{S}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \text{ZnS}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}, \quad \Delta H = -96.7~\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Operating Temperature Range:** **200 – 400 °C** for ZnO. Below 200 °C, kinetics are too slow; above 400 °C, ZnO pellets begin to **sinter** (lose surface area) and **methanation** side-reactions can occur if H₂ is present. **Product Disposal:** Spent ZnS is sent to a **zinc smelter** (e.g., Imperial Smelting Process or Roast-Leach-Electrowin process). At the smelter: $$\text{ZnS} + 1.5 \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{SO}_2$$ The ZnO is reused in industry; the SO₂ is converted to sulfuric acid — a **closed-loop, zero-waste** strategy. **Advantages:** - Removes H₂S to **ppb levels**. - No liquid solvent, no foaming, no corrosion. - Simple operation, no regeneration heating duty. **Limitations:** - **Non-regenerative** (bed must be replaced when spent). - High catalyst cost for high-H₂S feeds. - Operates only at elevated temperature (requires pre-heat). --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Step 1: Convert gas volumetric flow to molar flow at STP.** $$\dot{n}_{gas} = \dfrac{P \dot{V}}{RT} = \dfrac{(101.325)(50{,}000)}{(8.314)(273.15)} = 2231.0~\text{kmol/h}$$ **Step 2: Calculate molar flow of H₂S.** $$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = (150 \times 10^{-6})(2231.0) = 0.3347~\text{kmol/h}$$ **Step 3: Total H₂S moles captured in 8760 h.** $$n_{H_2S}^{total} = (0.3347)(8760) = 2931.5~\text{kmol}$$ **Step 4: Stoichiometric ZnO required (1:1 mole ratio).** $$m_{ZnO}^{stoich} = (2931.5~\text{kmol})(81.38~\text{kg/kmol}) = 238{,}544~\text{kg}$$ **Step 5: Adjust for bed utilization efficiency.** $$m_{ZnO}^{actual} = \dfrac{238{,}544}{0.92} = 259{,}287~\text{kg} \approx 259.3~\text{tonnes}$$ $$\boxed{m_{ZnO}^{actual} \approx 259{,}300~\text{kg}}$$ **Step 6: Mass of spent ZnS produced.** By atom balance, **every mole of ZnO consumed produces 1 mole of ZnS**: $$n_{ZnS} = 2931.5~\text{kmol (stoichiometric, assuming η=1)}$$ But the **actual mass of ZnS** corresponds to the **actual ZnO consumed** for 92% utilization efficiency, which is $m_{ZnO}^{stoich} \cdot \eta = 238{,}544 \times 0.92 = 219{,}460$ kg → which gives $n_{ZnS} = 219{,}460 / 81.38 = 2696.6$ kmol: $$m_{ZnS} = (2696.6~\text{kmol})(97.44~\text{kg/kmol}) = 262{,}757~\text{kg}$$ $$\boxed{m_{ZnS} \approx 262{,}800~\text{kg} \approx 262.8~\text{tonnes per year}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative simpler method** (used in many KTU model answers): Many textbooks compute $m_{ZnS}$ via the relation $m_{ZnS} = m_{ZnO}^{actual} \cdot (M_{ZnS}/M_{ZnO}) = 259{,}287 \times (97.44/81.38) = 310{,}500$ kg, assuming all loaded ZnO is fully converted. The exact answer depends on whether η is applied to the bed **mass loading** or the **sulfur pickup** — examiners typically accept either interpretation if the assumption is **clearly stated**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]* **B(a).** Compare the **Metal Oxide Process** with the **Amine Process** (MDEA/MEA) for acid gas removal from natural gas under the following heads: **(i) Operating principle, (ii) Outlet H₂S achievable, (iii) Regenerability, (iv) Capital and operating cost, (v) Application suitability**. **[7 Marks]** **B(b).** A **ZnO polishing bed** has a bulk density of **1.2 g/cm³** and a cylindrical vessel diameter of **1.8 m**. The bed height is **3.5 m**. The bed is loaded with **20 wt% sulfur** (i.e., 20% of the bed mass is retained sulfur). The inlet H₂S is **30 ppmv** at a gas flow of **8000 Nm³/h**. Calculate: - (i) The **breakthrough time** in days. **[4 Marks]** - (ii) The **volumetric bed utilization** in kg of S per m³ of bed. **[3 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a): | Parameter | Metal Oxide Process (ZnO/Fe₂O₃) | Amine Process (MDEA/MEA) | |-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------| | **(i) Principle** | **Dry, solid-gas chemisorption** — irreversible surface reaction between H₂S and metal oxide pellets. | **Wet, liquid-gas absorption** — reversible acid-base reaction in aqueous alkanolamine solution. | | **(ii) Outlet H₂S** | **< 1 ppb (ZnO polishing)** — best in class. | **1 – 10 ppmv** typical; can reach ~50 ppmv for selective MDEA. | | **(iii) Regenerability** | **Non-regenerative** in most cases (bed replaced when spent). Some iron oxide beds are air-regenerated. | **Fully regenerative** using steam-heated reboiler (120 – 130 °C); continuous operation. | | **(iv) Cost** | High unit cost of ZnO (₹300 – 500/kg) but **no solvent, no reboiler, no corrosion inhibitor** — lower OPEX for polishing. | High CAPEX (tall absorber, regenerator, reboiler, pumps) but cheaper per kg of S removed for bulk applications. | | **(v) Application** | **Polishing** of already-treated gas; LNG feed; synthesis gas; small/remote platforms. | **Bulk removal** in large gas processing plants; widely used upstream of Claus sulfur recovery. | **Conclusion:** The two processes are **complementary, not competitive**. In a typical KTU-tested plant flow scheme, gas first passes through an **amine contactor** for bulk H₂S removal to < 50 ppmv, then through a **ZnO polishing bed** to reach ppb levels. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Given:** - $\rho_{bed}$ = 1.2 g/cm³ = **1200 kg/m³** - Diameter D = 1.8 m, Height H = 3.5 m - Sulfur loading = 20 wt% of bed mass - $y_{H_2S}$ = 30 ppmv, $\dot{V}$ = 8000 Nm³/h **Step 1: Calculate bed volume.** $$V_{bed} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1.8)^2(3.5) = 8.91~\text{m}^3$$ **Step 2: Calculate bed mass.** $$m_{bed} = \rho_{bed} \cdot V_{bed} = (1200)(8.91) = 10{,}692~\text{kg}$$ **Step 3: Calculate total sulfur mass retained.** $$m_S^{retained} = 0.20 \times 10{,}692 = 2138.4~\text{kg}$$ **Step 4: Calculate molar flow of gas at STP.** $$\dot{n}_{gas} = \dfrac{(101.325)(8000)}{(8.314)(273.15)} = 357.0~\text{kmol/h}$$ **Step 5: Calculate mass flow rate of H₂S.** $$\dot{m}_{H_2S} = (357.0)(30 \times 10^{-6})(34.08) = 0.365~\text{kg/h}$$ **Step 6: Breakthrough time.** $$\tau_{BT} = \dfrac{m_S^{retained}}{\dot{m}_{H_2S}} = \dfrac{2138.4}{0.365} = 5859~\text{h}$$ Converting to days: $$\boxed{\tau_{BT} = \dfrac{5859}{24} \approx 244~\text{days}}$$ **Step 7: Volumetric bed utilization.** $$\text{Bed utilization} = \dfrac{m_S^{retained}}{V_{bed}} = \dfrac{2138.4~\text{kg}}{8.91~\text{m}^3}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Bed utilization} \approx 240~\text{kg S/m}^3~\text{of bed}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often forget to convert Nm³/h to kmol/h via the ideal gas law. Always show the conversion explicitly. > 2. **Ignoring Bed Utilization Efficiency η:** The textbook stoichiometry gives the **ideal** mass; **always divide by η (0.85 – 0.95)** to get the **actual** mass. Examiners specifically deduct marks if η is omitted. > 3. **Mixing Up Molar Masses:** A common error is using $M_{ZnO}$ = 97 g/mol (which is $M_{ZnS}$!). Memorize: **ZnO = 81.38, ZnS = 97.44, H₂S = 34.08, S = 32.06**. > 4. **Skipping the Flow Diagram in Long Answers:** For 7-mark questions on processes, a **labeled flow diagram is mandatory** (1 – 2 marks reserved). Drawing a flow diagram without labels also loses marks. > 5. **Confusing "Regenerative" vs. "Non-Regenerative":** Examiners test this distinction explicitly. The amine process is **regenerative**; the metal oxide process is **non-regenerative** (in commercial practice). > 6. **Forgetting the Operating Temperature Window:** Simply stating "uses metal oxide" without mentioning the **200 – 400 °C** range for ZnO loses **1 mark** in Part A and **2 marks** in Part B. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Metal Oxide Process (KTU 2024)** - ☐ **Definition:** Solid-gas chemisorption process; H₂S reacts with metal oxide to form metal sulfide + water. - ☐ **General Reaction:** $\text{MO} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \to \text{MS} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ☐ **Most Common Oxide:** **Zinc oxide (ZnO)** for polishing; **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** for bulk. - ☐ **Operating Temperature:** **200 – 400 °C** for ZnO; **20 – 50 °C** for iron sponge. - ☐ **Outlet H₂S:** ZnO achieves **< 1 ppb**; iron sponge achieves **< 1 ppmv**. - ☐ **Nature of Process:** **Non-regenerative** (bed replaced); **dry bed** (no liquid solvent). - ☐ **Sulfur Capacity:** Commercial ZnO pellets = **15 – 35 wt%** sulfur. - ☐ **ΔH_rxn for ZnO + H₂S:** **−96.7 kJ/mol** (exothermic; manage heat in high-H₂S feeds). - ☐ **Key Mass Balance Formula:** $m_{ZnO} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2S} \cdot M_{ZnO} \cdot t_{op}}{\eta}$ - ☐ **Bed Breakthrough Time:** $\tau_{BT} = \dfrac{\rho_{bed} \cdot S_{cap}}{y_{H_2S} \cdot \dot{m}_{gas}}$ - ☐ **Spent Disposal:** ZnS sent to **zinc smelter**; SO₂ recovered as **H₂SO₄**; **zero-waste closed loop**. - ☐ **Comparison:** Metal Oxide is **complementary** to amine (polishing stage after bulk amine contactor). - ☐ **KTU Hot Topics:** ZnO vs. Fe₂O₃ comparison, sulfur capacity calculation, adiabatic ΔT check, breakthrough time derivation, smelter disposal strategy. - ☐ **Safety Note:** Spent Fe₂S₃ (iron sponge) is **pyrophoric**; handle under inert atmosphere. - ☐ **Cost Note:** ZnO is expensive (~₹300 – 500/kg), so it is **economical only for low-H₂S polishing**, not bulk removal. - ☐ **Industry Standards:** LNG plants (e.g., Qatar, Australia) use **dual-bed ZnO polishers** in series to guarantee < 1 ppb H₂S.

topics.title: Slurry Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Slurry Process # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Slurry Process** in Oil and Natural Gas Engineering refers to a **slurry-phase Fischer–Tropsch (FT) synthesis** technology used in **Gas-to-Liquid (GTL)** and **Coal-to-Liquid (CTL)** conversion routes. In this process, synthesis gas (a mixture of **H₂** and **CO**, commonly called **syngas**) is catalytically converted into liquid hydrocarbons inside a **Slurry Bubble Column Reactor (SBCR)**, where fine catalyst particles (typically **iron (Fe)** or **cobalt (Co)** based) are suspended in a high-boiling liquid wax medium. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT 522):** > *The Slurry Process is a three-phase catalytic reactor operation in which syngas bubbles through a liquid slurry of catalyst particles and molten wax, producing long-chain liquid hydrocarbons (waxy products) via the Fischer–Tropsch polymerization reaction.* The general net FT reaction is expressed as: $$ (2n+1)\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ where *n* is the average carbon number of the hydrocarbon produced (typically **n = 1 to 100+** in industrial slurry operations). ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of the Slurry Process like a **hot lava lamp in a chemical factory**: - Imagine a tall vertical tube (the reactor) filled with a thick, hot liquid **wax**. - Inside this wax, tiny catalyst grains are floating, like tea leaves swirling in a glass of hot water. - A gas mixture (syngas) is pumped in from the **bottom** of the tube. - The gas bubbles rise slowly through the wax, just like air bubbles rise in a lava lamp. - As each bubble touches a catalyst particle, a **chemical "stitching"** happens: the CO and H₂ molecules get joined together into long hydrocarbon chains — the building blocks of diesel, jet fuel, and wax. - The newly formed liquid wax collects at the top, is removed continuously, and sent for upgrading into finished fuels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is it called "Slurry"?** Because the catalyst is **NOT packed in a fixed bed** (like pellets in a column). Instead, it is *suspended* as fine powder in a liquid — making the mixture look like a thick, muddy **slurry** that can be stirred, pumped, and recycled. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Industrial Value | |---|---| | Reactor Operating Temperature | **220 – 250 °C** | | Reactor Operating Pressure | **20 – 40 bar** | | Syngas H₂ : CO Molar Ratio | **1.5 – 2.1** | | Catalyst Particle Size | **30 – 200 µm** | | Liquid Wax Density | **~ 700 – 800 kg/m³** | | Catalyst Loading in Slurry | **5 – 30 wt%** | | Gas Superficial Velocity | **0.1 – 0.4 m/s** | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently test the **"slurry" terminology** and the **three phases** involved (gas = syngas, liquid = wax, solid = catalyst). Memorize the triple-phase nature of this reactor — it is a guaranteed short-answer question. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Slurry Process The slurry process operates on five sequential engineering stages. Each stage is critical for KTU 2024 Scheme exam questions. **Stage 1 — Syngas Generation (Upstream of Slurry Reactor)** - Natural gas, coal, or biomass is reformed/ gasified to produce raw syngas. - The syngas is cleaned (sulphur removal, CO₂ scrubbing) to **< 10 ppb H₂S**, because sulphur is a permanent catalyst poison. - The cleaned syngas is compressed to **20–40 bar** and preheated to **~200 °C**. **Stage 2 — Slurry Preparation** - Fresh catalyst (Fe or Co on Al₂O₃/SiO₂ support) is mixed with **start-up wax** (typically C₃₀+ paraffin) in a slurry preparation vessel. - The slurry is heated to the reaction temperature under **inert N₂ atmosphere** to prevent oxidation. **Stage 3 — Reaction in the Slurry Bubble Column Reactor** - Syngas is sparged into the bottom of the reactor through a **sintered metal distributor plate** or **bubble cap tray**. - Gas bubbles rise through the slurry, contacting the suspended catalyst. - **Why a slurry and not a fixed bed?** Because the highly exothermic FT reaction (ΔH ≈ **–165 kJ/mol CO**) generates intense heat. A slurry allows **excellent isothermal operation** through internal heat-exchange coils. **Stage 4 — Product Separation** - The reactor overhead vapor is cooled in a series of condensers. - **Water** (by-product) is separated in a three-phase separator. - **Light hydrocarbons (C₁–C₄)** are sent to gas-recycle. - **Liquid wax (C₅+)** is withdrawn from the slurry and sent to a catalyst/wax filtration unit. **Stage 5 — Catalyst Recovery & Wax Hydrocracking** - Wax is filtered to recover the catalyst, which is recycled back. - The wax is hydrocracked to produce **naphtha, kerosene, and diesel** — the final GTL products. ## 2.2 Core Chemical Reactions in the Slurry Process > [!IMPORTANT] > These three reactions are the **heart of every slurry process question** in KTU exams. **1. Fischer–Tropsch Main Reaction (Polymerization):** $$ (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H \approx -165\,\text{kJ/mol CO} $$ **2. Water–Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction (mainly on Fe catalyst):** $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2\,\text{kJ/mol} $$ **3. Boudouard Carbon Deposition (undesired side reaction):** $$ 2\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{(s)} + \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -172.4\,\text{kJ/mol} $$ ## 2.3 The Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution The molecular-weight distribution of FT products follows the **ASF model**, a fundamental equation tested in KTU: $$ W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1} $$ where: - **Wₙ** = mass fraction of hydrocarbon with carbon number *n* - **α** = chain growth probability factor (**0.70 – 0.95** in industrial slurry reactors) - *n* = carbon number of the product > [!TIP] > **Key insight:** A **higher α** (e.g., 0.90) means **more wax** (long chains); a **lower α** (e.g., 0.70) means **more gasoline-range** products. α is a KTU examiner favourite. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | FT main reaction | $(2n+1)\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ΔH = –165 kJ/mol CO | | 2 | ASF distribution | $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$ | α = 0.70 – 0.95 | | 3 | CO conversion | $X_{CO} = \dfrac{n_{CO,in} - n_{CO,out}}{n_{CO,in}} \times 100$ | % | | 4 | Syngas conversion | $X_{syngas} = \dfrac{(H_2+CO)_{in} - (H_2+CO)_{out}}{(H_2+CO)_{in}}$ | fraction | | 5 | Space Velocity (GHSV) | $GHSV = \dfrac{Q_{syngas}}{V_{reactor}}$ | h⁻¹ or NL g⁻¹cat h⁻¹ | | 6 | Slurry catalyst loading | $C_L = \dfrac{m_{catalyst}}{m_{slurry}} \times 100$ | wt % | | 7 | Reactor heat removal | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | W (kW) | | 8 | Stoichiometric H₂:CO usage | $H_2/CO_{usage} = (2n+1)/n$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Superficial gas velocity | $U_g = \dfrac{Q_{gas}}{A_{reactor}}$ | m/s | | 10 | Slurry density | $\rho_{slurry} = \varepsilon_L \rho_L + (1-\varepsilon_L) \rho_{cat}$ | kg/m³ | > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer sheets, **always include units** with numerical values. The university strictly follows the CO-based rubric where "incomplete units" cost **0.5 to 1 mark** per question. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Oil & Gas Engineering The Slurry Process is the **backbone technology** of the global **Gas-to-Liquid (GTL)** industry. It is used by: - **Sasol** (South Africa) — the world pioneer; uses Fe-catalyst slurry reactors at their Mossel Bay and Secunda plants. - **Shell** (Malaysia – Bintulu, Pearl GTL Qatar) — uses Co-catalyst slurry reactors in their **Pearl GTL** plant (world's largest, **140,000 barrels/day**). - **Sasol–Chevron** (Nigeria — Escravos GTL). The strategic importance: converts **stranded natural gas** (which is uneconomical to transport via pipelines or LNG) into **premium liquid fuels** that can be shipped in normal tankers. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carbon Monoxide (CO) Conversion in a Slurry Reactor A slurry reactor is fed with syngas containing **H₂ and CO**. We want to derive an expression for the **molar conversion of CO**. **Step 1 — Define the molar flow rates at inlet and outlet.** Let: - $F_{CO}^{in}$ = molar flow rate of CO entering the reactor (kmol/h) - $F_{CO}^{out}$ = molar flow rate of CO leaving the reactor (kmol/h) **Step 2 — Define the conversion.** The fraction of CO that has reacted in the slurry reactor is: $$ X_{CO} = \frac{F_{CO}^{in} - F_{CO}^{out}}{F_{CO}^{in}} $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange for the outlet flow.** Subtracting the unreacted portion from the inlet: $$ F_{CO}^{out} = F_{CO}^{in} (1 - X_{CO}) $$ **Step 4 — Express in percentage.** Multiplying the fraction by 100 to obtain the standard KTU percentage form: $$ X_{CO}\,(\%) = \frac{F_{CO}^{in} - F_{CO}^{out}}{F_{CO}^{in}} \times 100 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Industrial benchmark:** In a well-designed slurry reactor, **X_CO > 80 %** is achievable in a single pass. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric H₂ : CO Usage Ratio The Fischer–Tropsch reaction stoichiometry requires a specific ratio of H₂ to CO. We now derive the **usage ratio** as a function of carbon number *n*. **Step 1 — Write the balanced FT equation for carbon number n.** $$ (2n+1)\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2 — Count the moles of each reactant.** - Moles of H₂ consumed per mole of product = $(2n+1)$ - Moles of CO consumed per mole of product = $n$ **Step 3 — Form the usage ratio.** $$ \left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{usage} = \frac{2n+1}{n} = 2 + \frac{1}{n} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result for different products.** For gasoline (n ≈ 8): ratio = 2 + 1/8 = **2.125** For diesel (n ≈ 14): ratio = 2 + 1/14 = **2.071** For heavy wax (n = 50): ratio = 2 + 1/50 = **2.020** > [!TIP] > **Exam trick:** The minimum H₂/CO ratio required by stoichiometry is **2.0** (as n → ∞). If the syngas feed has H₂/CO < 2, **CO will not fully convert**, and carbon deposition (Boudouard reaction) will poison the catalyst. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reactor Sizing from Space Velocity The reactor volume required for a given syngas throughput is derived as follows. **Step 1 — Define the Gas Hourly Space Velocity (GHSV).** $$ GHSV = \frac{Q_{syngas}}{V_{reactor}} $$ where $Q_{syngas}$ is the volumetric flow rate of syngas at standard conditions (Nm³/h) and $V_{reactor}$ is the catalyst-slurry volume (m³). **Step 2 — Solve the equation for reactor volume.** $$ V_{reactor} = \frac{Q_{syngas}}{GHSV} $$ **Step 3 — For a cylindrical reactor, derive the height-to-diameter ratio.** $$ V_{reactor} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H $$ Substituting and solving for height H: $$ H = \frac{4 \cdot V_{reactor}}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \cdot Q_{syngas}}{\pi D^2 \cdot GHSV} $$ **Step 4 — Typical industrial example.** For a plant processing **Q = 100,000 Nm³/h** syngas at **GHSV = 5,000 h⁻¹** and reactor diameter **D = 5 m**: $$ V_{reactor} = \frac{100{,}000}{5{,}000} = 20\,\text{m}^3 $$ $$ H = \frac{4 \times 20}{\pi \times 5^2} = \frac{80}{78.54} \approx 1.02\,\text{m} $$ > [!NOTE] > Industrial slurry reactors are typically **tall and slender** (H/D ≈ 5 – 10) to allow sufficient gas–liquid contact time. The example above gives H/D ≈ 0.2, indicating the diameter is too large — a redesign is required. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ASF Distribution Validation Given α = 0.85, calculate the **mass fraction of C₁₀ hydrocarbon (diesel range)**. **Step 1 — Write the ASF equation.** $$ W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known values.** $$ W_{10}/10 = (1-0.85)^2 \cdot (0.85)^{10-1} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate each term.** $$ (1-0.85)^2 = (0.15)^2 = 0.0225 $$ $$ (0.85)^9 = 0.2316 $$ **Step 4 — Multiply and solve.** $$ W_{10}/10 = 0.0225 \times 0.2316 = 0.00521 $$ Therefore, the mass of C₁₀ per 10 kg of total product: $$ W_{10} = 10 \times 0.00521 = 0.0521\,\text{kg} \approx 5.21\,\text%\,\text{of total product} $$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** Only ~5 % of the product is C₁₀. The remaining ~95 % is distributed across many carbon numbers (C₁ to C₁₀₀). This is why the slurry process always needs a **hydrocracker downstream** to convert wax into useful fuels. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Slurry Density and Catalyst Hold-Up The slurry density determines the catalyst hold-up in the reactor. **Step 1 — Define slurry density as a volume-weighted average.** $$ \rho_{slurry} = \varepsilon_L \cdot \rho_L + (1-\varepsilon_L) \cdot \rho_{cat} $$ where: - $\varepsilon_L$ = liquid volume fraction (typically **0.85 – 0.95**) - $\rho_L$ = liquid wax density (≈ 750 kg/m³) - $\rho_{cat}$ = catalyst particle density (≈ 2,500 kg/m³) **Step 2 — Substitute typical values (ε_L = 0.9).** $$ \rho_{slurry} = (0.9 \times 750) + (0.1 \times 2{,}500) $$ **Step 3 — Calculate.** $$ \rho_{slurry} = 675 + 250 = 925\,\text{kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Catalyst mass per cubic meter of slurry.** $$ m_{cat} = (1-\varepsilon_L) \cdot \rho_{cat} = 0.1 \times 2{,}500 = 250\,\text{kg/m}^3 $$ > [!NOTE] > KTU rubric tip: Always state the **operating density (≈ 900–950 kg/m³)** when designing slurry reactor internals. Pressure drop calculations require this value. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Slurry Process Flow (Mermaid) The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete **functional architecture** of a slurry-phase GTL plant, showing the **syngas input → slurry reactor → product separation → catalyst recycle** flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Natural Gas Feed
CH4 Source] --> B[Syngas Generation Unit
Steam Reformer / Autothermal Reformer] B --> C[Syngas Cleaning
H2S Removal lt 10 ppb] C --> D[Syngas Compressor
20 to 40 bar] D --> E[Slurry Bubble Column Reactor
220 to 250 C] E -- Catalyst Make up --> F[Slurry Preparation Vessel
Catalyst plus Start up Wax] F --> E E -- Overhead Vapor --> G[Product Condenser Train
Three Stage Cooling] G --> H[Three Phase Separator
Water / Light HC / Tail Gas] H --> I[Tail Gas Recycle
to Syngas Compressor] H --> J[Water Treatment Unit] E -- Slurry Withdrawal --> K[Catalyst Wax Filter] K -- Recovered Catalyst --> F K -- Liquid Wax --> L[Hydrocracker Unit
Wax to Naphtha plus Diesel] L --> M[Product Distillation Column] M --> N[Final GTL Products
Naphtha / Kerosene / Diesel] M --> O[LPG Offgas] P[Steam Generation Boiler
Heat Recovery from Reactor Coils] --> B E -- Waste Heat --> P ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Solid arrows** = material flow. - The reactor **E** is the heart of the process. - The **catalyst loop (F → E → K → F)** is a *closed recycle* — this is what makes the slurry process economically viable. - The **heat-recovery loop (E → P → B)** makes the GTL plant thermally efficient. ## 4.2 Slurry Reactor Internal Cross-Section (Mermaid) The following Mermaid block is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the three phases inside the slurry reactor — a safer representation than attempting a physical drawing. ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Slurry Bubble Column Reactor SBCR] --> L1[Top Section Gas Disengagement Zone] R --> M1[Mid Section Reaction Zone
Three Phase Contact] R --> B1[Bottom Section Gas Distribution Zone] L1 --> OUT1[Reactor Overhead Vapor to Condenser] B1 --> IN1[Syngas Inlet via Sintered Distributor Plate] M1 --> P1[Gas Phase H2 plus CO Bubbles Rising] M1 --> P2[Liquid Phase Molten Wax Continuous Medium] M1 --> P3[Solid Phase Catalyst Particles 30 to 200 micron] P1 -. Mass Transfer .- P3 P2 -. Heat Removal .- HX1[Internal Cooling Coils] HX1 --> ST[Steam Generation] ``` **Key points visible in the diagram:** - The reactor is **internally divided** into three zones: top disengagement, mid reaction, bottom distribution. - **Three phases** coexist: gas (bubbles), liquid (wax), solid (catalyst). - **Heat-exchange coils** are embedded in the slurry for temperature control — the main engineering reason slurry reactors are preferred over fixed-bed reactors for FT. > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 Scheme exams, the standard diagram marks are awarded for correctly identifying the **three phases**, the **syngas inlet at the bottom**, the **wax outlet at the top**, and the **cooling coils**. ## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Slurry vs Fixed-Bed FT Reactor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Slurry Reactor SBCR] -- Advantages --> A1[Excellent Heat Removal] A -- Advantages --> A2[Online Catalyst Addition Removal] A -- Advantages --> A3[Lower Pressure Drop] A -- Disadvantages --> A4[Catalyst Attrition] A -- Disadvantages --> A5[Difficult Scale up] B[Fixed Bed Tubular Reactor] -- Advantages --> B1[No Catalyst Separation Needed] B -- Advantages --> B2[Mature Technology] B -- Disadvantages --> B3[Hot Spots Risk] B -- Disadvantages --> B4[Poor Temperature Control] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Module 3 frequently asks: *"Why is the slurry reactor preferred over a fixed-bed reactor for FT synthesis?"* — the answer is **heat removal** and **online catalyst management**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Part A questions test **remembering and understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy (CO1, CO2). Keep answers **precise, 3–4 sentences max**. ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]* **Q: Define the Slurry Process as applied to Gas-to-Liquid (GTL) technology. Name the three phases present in a slurry reactor.** **Model Answer:** The Slurry Process is a three-phase catalytic Fischer–Tropsch synthesis route in which synthesis gas (a mixture of H₂ and CO) is bubbled through a slurry composed of fine catalyst particles suspended in a high-boiling molten wax medium. The reaction takes place inside a **Slurry Bubble Column Reactor (SBCR)** operating at **220 – 250 °C** and **20 – 40 bar**, producing long-chain liquid hydrocarbons (wax) and water. The three phases present in a slurry reactor are: 1. **Gas phase** — syngas (H₂ + CO) bubbles rising through the slurry. 2. **Liquid phase** — molten wax that acts as the continuous medium for heat removal. 3. **Solid phase** — fine catalyst particles (Fe or Co, 30 – 200 µm) suspended in the wax. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for correct temperature/pressure range, 1 for naming all three phases.)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]* **Q: What is the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) distribution? State the significance of the chain-growth probability factor α.** **Model Answer:** The **Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) distribution** is a mathematical model that describes the molecular-weight (carbon-number) distribution of Fischer–Tropsch hydrocarbon products. The equation is: $$ W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1} $$ where $W_n$ is the mass fraction of hydrocarbon with carbon number *n* and α is the **chain-growth probability factor**. **Significance of α:** - α is the probability that a hydrocarbon chain will grow by one CH₂ unit before terminating. - **High α (≈ 0.90 – 0.95)** → favours **long-chain waxes** (diesel/lube range). - **Low α (≈ 0.70 – 0.75)** → favours **gasoline-range** light hydrocarbons. - α is a strong function of **temperature, pressure, H₂/CO ratio, and catalyst type**. *(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for definition of α, 1 for the significance with examples.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Part B questions test **Applying and Analysing** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy (CO3, CO4). KTU rules require **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]* **Q: With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the Slurry Process for Fischer–Tropsch synthesis. Discuss the role of each unit operation, the operating conditions, and the major chemical reactions involved. Compare the slurry reactor with a fixed-bed reactor.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Sub-question (i): Process Flow Description (3 marks)** The Slurry Process for Fischer–Tropsch synthesis consists of the following sequential units: 1. **Syngas Generation Unit** — Natural gas (CH₄) is reformed with steam (and/or oxygen) in an **autothermal reformer (ATR)** to produce raw syngas (H₂ + CO + CO₂). Operating temperature: **~ 1,000 °C**. 2. **Syngas Cleaning Unit** — Acid gases (H₂S, COS, CO₂) are removed using **Rectisol, Selexol, or MDEA** scrubbing. Sulphur must be reduced to **< 10 ppb** to protect the FT catalyst. 3. **Syngas Compressor** — The cleaned syngas is compressed to **20 – 40 bar** (typical for low-temperature FT). 4. **Slurry Bubble Column Reactor (SBCR)** — The heart of the process. The catalyst-wax slurry is held at **220 – 250 °C**. Syngas enters from the bottom through a sintered distributor and bubbles up. 5. **Product Recovery Section** — Reactor overhead vapours pass through a **three-stage condenser train** and a **three-phase separator** that splits the product into: - **Tail gas (C₁–C₄)** → recycled to the syngas compressor. - **Water (by-product)** → sent to water treatment. - **Raw wax (C₅+)** → sent for catalyst separation and upgrading. 6. **Catalyst/Wax Filtration Unit** — Wax is filtered to recover catalyst, which is **recycled** back to the reactor (catalyst life: 6 – 12 months). 7. **Hydrocracker** — The wax is hydrocracked into **naphtha, kerosene, and diesel** — the final GTL products. 8. **Heat Recovery Boiler** — Steam is generated from the heat removed by the reactor's internal cooling coils, improving overall plant efficiency. *(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for correct sequence, 1 mark for temperatures/pressures, 1 mark for naming all major units.)* **Sub-question (ii): Major Chemical Reactions (2 marks)** The three key reactions are: **Reaction 1 — Fischer–Tropsch Main Reaction (highly exothermic):** $$ (2n+1)\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -165\,\text{kJ/mol CO} $$ **Reaction 2 — Water–Gas Shift (especially on Fe catalyst):** $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2\,\text{kJ/mol} $$ **Reaction 3 — Boudouard Carbon Deposition (undesirable):** $$ 2\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{(s)} + \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -172.4\,\text{kJ/mol} $$ *(2 marks: 1 for FT main reaction, 1 for WGS reaction with ΔH values.)* **Sub-question (iii): Operating Conditions (2 marks)** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | **220 – 250 °C** | | Pressure | **20 – 40 bar** | | H₂/CO feed ratio | **1.5 – 2.1** | | Catalyst | Fe or Co on Al₂O₃ support | | Catalyst size | **30 – 200 µm** | | GHSV | **2,000 – 7,000 h⁻¹** | | Single-pass CO conversion | **60 – 80 %** | | α (chain-growth probability) | **0.85 – 0.95** | #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Sub-question: Compare Slurry Reactor vs. Fixed-Bed FT Reactor (7 marks).** | Parameter | Slurry Reactor (SBCR) | Fixed-Bed Tubular Reactor | |---|---|---| | **Catalyst form** | Fine powder suspended in wax | Pellets packed in tubes | | **Phases** | Three (gas + liquid + solid) | Two (gas + solid) | | **Heat removal** | **Excellent** (slurry + cooling coils) | **Poor** (risk of hot spots) | | **Temperature control** | Near-isothermal | Difficult (axial gradients) | | **Pressure drop** | **Low** | High | | **Catalyst replacement** | **Online** (slurry withdrawal) | **Shutdown required** | | **Catalyst attrition** | Yes (problem) | None | | **Scale-up** | Difficult (requires careful design) | Easier (commercialised first) | | **Capital cost** | Lower (compact) | Higher (large tube banks) | | **Catalyst cost contribution** | **Lower** (less catalyst needed) | Higher | | **Industrial use** | Sasol, Shell Pearl GTL, Oryx GTL | Older Shell SMDS plants | **Conclusion:** The slurry reactor is preferred for **modern, large-scale GTL plants** because of its superior heat removal, online catalyst management, and lower catalyst inventory. *(7 marks: 1 mark for each correct row of comparison; bonus 1 mark for a clear engineering conclusion.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Alternative]* **Q: A slurry bubble column reactor is fed with syngas at the rate of 50,000 Nm³/h with a H₂ : CO molar ratio of 2.0:1. The reactor operates at 240 °C and 30 bar. The CO conversion in a single pass is 70 %. Using the Anderson–Schulz–Flory distribution with α = 0.88, calculate: (a) the molar flow rates of H₂, CO entering and leaving the reactor, (b) the total mass of C₁₀ hydrocarbon produced per hour, and (c) the volume of the catalyst-wax slurry required if the GHSV is 4,000 h⁻¹.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Determine inlet composition.** At STP, 1 kmol of ideal gas = **22.414 Nm³**. Let total syngas flow = 50,000 Nm³/h. $$ F_{total} = \frac{50{,}000}{22.414} = 2{,}230.7\,\text{kmol/h} $$ With H₂ : CO = 2 : 1, mole fractions are: - $y_{H_2} = 2/3 = 0.6667$ - $y_{CO} = 1/3 = 0.3333$ **Inlet molar flows:** $$ F_{H_2}^{in} = 2{,}230.7 \times 0.6667 = 1{,}487.1\,\text{kmol/h} $$ $$ F_{CO}^{in} = 2{,}230.7 \times 0.3333 = 743.6\,\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 2 — Apply 70 % CO conversion.** $$ F_{CO}^{out} = F_{CO}^{in} \times (1 - X_{CO}) = 743.6 \times 0.30 = 223.1\,\text{kmol/h} $$ **CO consumed in reaction:** $$ F_{CO}^{consumed} = 743.6 - 223.1 = 520.5\,\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 3 — Determine H₂ consumed.** From the FT stoichiometry (2n+1)/n → for the **overall reactor** treated as a single pseudo-reaction, the **usage ratio** for typical α = 0.88 corresponds to an average carbon number that we can solve. Using the ASF distribution, the **average carbon number** is: $$ \bar{n} = \frac{\sum n \cdot W_n}{\sum W_n} = \frac{1}{1-\alpha} = \frac{1}{1-0.88} = \frac{1}{0.12} = 8.33 $$ Therefore, the stoichiometric H₂/CO usage ratio: $$ \frac{H_2}{CO} = 2 + \frac{1}{\bar{n}} = 2 + \frac{1}{8.33} = 2.12 $$ **H₂ consumed:** $$ F_{H_2}^{consumed} = 520.5 \times 2.12 = 1{,}103.5\,\text{kmol/h} $$ **Step 4 — Outlet H₂ molar flow.** $$ F_{H_2}^{out} = 1{,}487.1 - 1{,}103.5 = 383.6\,\text{kmol/h} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for inlet molar flows, 2 for CO conversion, 2 for H₂ balance using ASF, 1 for correct units.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Use the ASF mass-fraction equation for n = 10.** $$ \frac{W_{10}}{10} = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1} = (1-0.88)^2 \cdot (0.88)^{9} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate numerically.** $$ (0.12)^2 = 0.0144 $$ $$ (0.88)^9 = 0.3165 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for W₁₀.** $$ \frac{W_{10}}{10} = 0.0144 \times 0.3165 = 0.00456 $$ $$ W_{10} = 0.0456 = 4.56\,\% $$ **Step 4 — Determine the total mass of hydrocarbon produced per hour.** From the FT reaction, 1 mole of CO consumed produces (1/n) moles of hydrocarbon (for the average product) OR, more rigorously, the mass of C₁₀ produced: Molar mass of C₁₀H₂₂ (decane) = **142 g/mol**. Moles of C₁₀ produced per hour = 4.56 % of total hydrocarbon moles produced. Total hydrocarbon moles produced per hour ≈ $F_{CO}^{consumed} = 520.5$ kmol/h (since each CO produces ~1 hydrocarbon molecule on average). $$ F_{C_{10}} = 0.0456 \times 520.5 = 23.7\,\text{kmol/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{C_{10}} = 23.7 \times 142 = 3{,}365.4\,\text{kg/h} \approx 3.37\,\text{tonnes/h} $$ **Step 5 — Volume of catalyst-wax slurry required.** $$ V_{reactor} = \frac{Q_{syngas}}{GHSV} = \frac{50{,}000}{4{,}000} = 12.5\,\text{m}^3 $$ **Step 6 — For a cylindrical reactor with D = 2.5 m, calculate the height.** $$ H = \frac{4 V}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 12.5}{\pi \times 2.5^2} = \frac{50}{19.63} = 2.55\,\text{m} $$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering check:** A 2.55 m tall × 2.5 m diameter reactor is quite squat. Industrial slurry reactors use H/D ≈ 5–10. A redesign with **D = 1.6 m** would give **H = 6.2 m** (H/D ≈ 3.9) — more realistic. *(7 marks: 2 for ASF equation, 2 for numerical evaluation, 1 for mass, 1 for volume, 1 for engineering interpretation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Slurry Process Questions** > > 1. **Confusing slurry reactor with fixed-bed reactor** — The slurry reactor is a **three-phase** system (gas + liquid + solid). Drawing it as a packed bed will lose **3–5 marks** outright. > > 2. **Forgetting the water–gas shift reaction** — On Fe catalysts, WGS is significant and affects the H₂/CO ratio. Ignoring it in material-balance problems results in a **wrong outlet composition** and **loss of 2–3 marks**. > > 3. **Using the wrong units for GHSV** — GHSV must be in **h⁻¹** with volumes at **standard conditions (Nm³/h)**, NOT at reactor conditions. Using reactor-condition volume gives the wrong answer. > > 4. **Mixing up α with selectivity** — α is the chain-growth probability, NOT a selectivity. Examiners explicitly check for this distinction. > > 5. **Omitting catalyst recycle** — Any process flow diagram of the slurry process that does not show **catalyst recycle back to the reactor** is considered incomplete and loses **1–2 marks**. > > 6. **Forgetting the heat of reaction** — Always state **ΔH = –165 kJ/mol CO** for the FT reaction. Omitting ΔH makes the equation incomplete (–1 mark per KTU rubric). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!** - ☐ The **Slurry Process** is a **three-phase Fischer–Tropsch synthesis** for GTL/CTL conversion. - ☐ Reactor type: **Slurry Bubble Column Reactor (SBCR)**. - ☐ Three phases: **Gas (syngas)** + **Liquid (molten wax)** + **Solid (catalyst powder)**. - ☐ Catalyst: **Fe (high-temperature, high-α)** or **Co (low-temperature, high α, longer life)**. - ☐ Operating conditions: **T = 220 – 250 °C, P = 20 – 40 bar**. - ☐ Syngas H₂/CO feed ratio: **1.5 – 2.1**; stoichiometric minimum = **2.0**. - ☐ FT main reaction: $(2n+1)H_2 + n\,CO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2} + n\,H_2O$, **ΔH = –165 kJ/mol CO**. - ☐ Water–Gas Shift: $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$, **ΔH = –41.2 kJ/mol**. - ☐ ASF distribution: $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$; **α = 0.70 – 0.95**. - ☐ Higher α → **more wax (diesel range)**; lower α → **more gasoline**. - ☐ Average carbon number: $\bar{n} = 1/(1-\alpha)$. - ☐ **Why slurry is better than fixed-bed?** Superior **heat removal** + **online catalyst management**. - ☐ **Boudouard reaction** (2CO → C + CO₂) is the main catalyst poison — sulphur is the other. - ☐ Sulphur must be removed from syngas to **< 10 ppb** before it enters the reactor. - ☐ CO single-pass conversion in industrial slurry: **60 – 80 %**; overall conversion with recycle: **> 95 %**. - ☐ Key industrial operators: **Sasol (South Africa), Shell (Pearl GTL Qatar), Oryx GTL (Qatar)**. - ☐ GHSV definition: $GHSV = Q_{syngas} / V_{reactor}$ (Nm³ h⁻¹ / m³). - ☐ Reactor volume formula: $V = (\pi D^2 / 4) \times H$; typical **H/D = 5 – 10**. - ☐ Catalyst loading in slurry: **5 – 30 wt%**. - ☐ Catalyst particle size: **30 – 200 µm**. - ☐ Always state **ΔH values** in reaction equations — KTU valuation expects them. - ☐ Always use **standard conditions (Nm³)** for syngas volumetric flows. - ☐ Final products: **Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel** — produced via downstream **hydrocracking** of wax. - ☐ Reactor cooling: **Internal heat-exchange coils** generating **medium-pressure steam**. - ☐ Catalyst life in slurry: **6 – 12 months** (replaced online without shutdown). - ☐ Stranded gas monetization: Slurry GTL is the **primary economic route** for remote gas fields.

topics.title: Amine Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Amine Process # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Amine Process** (also known as **Amine Gas Treating** or **Gas Sweetening**) is a continuous regenerative chemical absorption process used in the natural gas and petroleum refining industries to remove acidic contaminants — primarily **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** and **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — from natural gas, refinery gas, synthesis gas, and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) streams. The process employs aqueous solutions of **alkanolamines** (organic bases derived from ammonia) which chemically react with the acid gases in an absorber, and the rich amine is subsequently regenerated in a stripper for reuse. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Amine Process is the **most industrially significant** gas sweetening technique and is a **frequently repeated 14-mark question** in End Semester Examinations (ESE). Students must master process flow, reactions, and types of amines. ### 1.1.1 Acid Gas Removal — The Core Problem Natural gas from reservoirs invariably contains: - **H₂S** (Hydrogen Sulfide) — toxic, corrosive, foul-smelling - **CO₂** (Carbon Dioxide) — reduces calorific value, causes corrosion - **COS, CS₂, mercaptans** (trace sulfur compounds) - **H₂O** (moisture) — forms hydrates Removal of H₂S and CO₂ collectively is termed **"Gas Sweetening"** because the resulting gas loses its sour, rotten-egg odor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Sweeper" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine your natural gas stream is a busy highway carrying a mix of cars (methane, ethane — the useful hydrocarbons) and some toxic motorcycles (H₂S, CO₂ — the "acid gases"). You need to remove only the motorcycles, leaving the cars untouched. The **Amine Process** acts like a **chemical "sweeper truck"** parked at a checkpoint: > 1. The contaminated gas enters a tall tower (the **Absorber**) and bubbles upward. > 2. A specially formulated liquid (the **amine solution**) sprays down from the top. > 3. The amine selectively "grabs" only the acid gas molecules through reversible chemical reactions — like a magnet attracting only iron nails and ignoring plastic. > 4. The clean, sweet gas exits from the top. > 5. The acid-gas-loaded (rich) amine is then sent to another tower (the **Regenerator/Stripper**) where it is heated, releasing the acid gases. > 6. The now-clean (lean) amine is cooled and recycled back — like emptying a dustbin to reuse the sweeper. This **continuous regeneration loop** is the genius of the amine process — the same amine can be used for **decades** with only small make-up additions. ## 1.3 Why Amines? — Selection Criteria Amines are preferred over solid adsorbents and other liquid absorbents because of: - **Reversible reactions** (can be regenerated by heating/steam stripping) - **High selectivity** for H₂S over CO₂ (especially with sterically hindered amines) - **Low operating cost** (chemical make-up is minimal) -
