**Proven industrial track record** since the **1930s** - **Energy efficiency** compared to physical solvents (e.g., Selexol, Rectisol) ## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (Bolded for Recall) | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Operating Pressure (Absorber) | **50 – 100 bar** (high-pressure) | | Absorber Temperature | **40 – 60 °C** (above acid gas dew point) | | Regenerator Pressure | **1.05 – 1.7 bar (gauge)** | | Regenerator Bottom Temperature | **115 – 125 °C** | | Amine Concentration | **15 – 30 wt%** in water | | Acid Gas Removal Efficiency | **> 99.9%** for H₂S (to < 4 ppm pipeline spec) | | Circulation Rate | **0.2 – 0.5 m³ amine / 10⁶ SCF gas** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of the Amine Process The amines used are **alkanolamines** — molecules containing both: - An **amino group (–NH₂, –NHR, –NR₂)** → provides basicity (proton acceptor) - A **hydroxyl group (–OH)** → enhances water solubility and reduces vapor pressure ### 2.1.1 Generic Reaction with H₂S The reaction of a primary/secondary amine with hydrogen sulfide: $$2 RNH_2 + H_2S \rightarrow (RNH_3)_2S \rightleftharpoons 2 RNH_3^+ + S^{2-}$$ For tertiary amines (no N–H bond), the mechanism is direct proton transfer: $$R_3N + H_2S \rightleftharpoons R_3NH^+ + HS^-$$ > [!NOTE] > **H₂S reaction is INSTANTANEOUS** (mass-transfer controlled, not equilibrium-controlled) — making amine units very compact and fast. ### 2.1.2 Generic Reaction with CO₂ For primary and secondary amines (carbamate formation): $$2 R_2NH + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons R_2NCOOH + R_2NH_2^+$$ Overall: $$2 R_2NH + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons R_2NCOO^- + R_2NH_2^+$$ For tertiary amines (no N–H, so no carbamate; only bicarbonate formation via hydration): $$R_3N + CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons R_3NH^+ + HCO_3^-$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **CO₂ reaction is SLOW** (rate-controlled by carbamate formation/hydration). This kinetic difference is exploited in **selective H₂S removal** using tertiary amines like MDEA. ## 2.2 Types of Amines — Classification and Application ### 2.2.1 Primary Amines (one alkyl group on N) **Monoethanolamine (MEA): HOC₂H₄NH₂** - Molecular weight: **61.08 g/mol** - Used at **15–20 wt%** - High reactivity, low cost - **Drawbacks:** Forms irreversible degradation products (e.g., with COS, CS₂); high vapor pressure → high vapor losses; high regeneration energy; corrosive at high concentrations ### 2.2.2 Secondary Amines (two alkyl groups on N) **Diethanolamine (DEA: (HOC₂H₄)₂NH)** - Molecular weight: **105.14 g/mol** - Used at **25–35 wt%** - Lower regeneration energy than MEA - Used in **SNPA-DEA process** and **Fluor Daniel's Econamine process** **Diisopropanolamine (DIPA: (HOCH(CH₃)CH₂)₂NH)** - Used in **Adip process** (Shell), selective for H₂S in presence of CO₂ ### 2.2.3 Tertiary Amines (three alkyl groups on N) **Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA: CH₃N(C₂H₄OH)₂)** - Molecular weight: **119.16 g/mol** - Used at **30–50 wt%** - **Selective for H₂S over CO₂** (kinetic selectivity) - Low regeneration energy - Used in **UCARSOL, GAS/SPEC, Optisol processes** ### 2.2.4 Sterically Hindered Amines **2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP)** - Bulky group adjacent to N → cannot form stable carbamate - High loading capacity, low regeneration energy ## 2.3 Classification of the Process > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Classification Point:** | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Chemical Solvent Process** | Reaction with amine (reversible) | MEA, DEA, MDEA | | **Physical Solvent Process** | Physical absorption only | Selexol, Rectisol, Purisol | | **Hybrid (Mixed) Process** | Chemical + Physical | Sulfinol (DIPA + Sulfolane) | | **Direct Conversion Process** | Converts H₂S to elemental S | Stretford, LO-CAT, SulFerox | | **Membrane Process** | Selective permeation | PRISM, SEPAREX | | **Solid Bed Adsorption** | Molecular sieves, ZnO, Fe₂O₃ | Fixed bed reactors | The Amine Process falls under the **Chemical Solvent Process** category. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Application / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Loading} = \dfrac{\text{moles acid gas}}{\text{mole amine}}$ | Acid gas loading capacity | Dimensionless (typical: 0.3–0.5 mol/mol) | | 2 | $\alpha_{H_2S} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S,top}}{y_{H_2S,bottom}}$ | H₂S separation factor | Used for selectivity analysis | | 3 | $S_{H_2S/CO_2} = \dfrac{(y_{H_2S}/y_{CO_2})_{gas}}{(x_{H_2S}/x_{CO_2})_{liquid}}$ | Selectivity ratio | Dimensionless; MDEA: 5–15 | | 4 | $\dot{m}_{amine} = \dfrac{L \times G}{M_{amine} \times C}$ | Circulation rate | kg/h, where L = loading, G = acid gas flow | | 5 | $N_A = K_G \cdot (y - y^*)$ | Mass transfer rate (gas film) | kmol/(m²·s) | | 6 | $H = \dfrac{P \cdot y}{x}$ | Henry's Law constant | bar·(mol/mol) for physical solubility | | 7 | $Q_{regen} = \dot{m}_{amine} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_{H_2O}$ | Reboiler duty | kW or MJ/h | | 8 | $\eta_{H_2S} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S,in} - y_{H_2S,out}}{y_{H_2S,in}} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency | %, pipeline spec: 99.9%+ | | 9 | $\dfrac{dy}{dN} = \dfrac{y - y^*}{1 + (mG/L)}$ | Kremser equation (gas phase) | Used for tray/packing design | | 10 | $H_{OG} = \dfrac{G}{K_y \cdot a}$ | Overall height of gas transfer unit | m | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When using the Kremser equation in a markdown table or inline, students often confuse **G** (gas molar flow, kmol/h) with **L** (liquid molar flow, kmol/h). Always write units explicitly to avoid valuation errors. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The amine process is the **workhorse of natural gas processing** worldwide, with applications in: - **Onshore/offshore gas production platforms** (e.g., KG Basin, Mumbai High — India) - **LNG plants** (acid gas removal to < 50 ppm CO₂ before liquefaction) - **Refinery FCC and hydrotreater off-gas treatment** - **Syngas cleanup** for ammonia/methanol/hydrogen plants - **Biogas upgrading** (CO₂ removal to produce bio-methane) - **Tail gas treating units (TGTU)** in sulfur recovery (Claus process) ## 2.6 Process Design Parameters — Why Each Matters 1. **Lean Amine Temperature (40–50 °C):** Higher temperature → lower acid gas solubility → higher circulation rate needed. Lower temperature → better absorption but risk of hydrocarbon condensation. 2. **Lean Amine Loading (0.01–0.05 mol/mol):** Lower lean loading (richer regeneration) → greater driving force → lower circulation rate, but higher reboiler duty. Trade-off is optimized at **0.02–0.05 mol H₂S/mol amine**. 3. **Number of Equilibrium Stages:** Determined by Kremser equation; typical absorber has **20–30 actual trays** (or 6–10 m of structured packing). 4. **Pressure Drop:** Limited to **< 0.2 bar per absorber** to avoid recompression cost. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kremser Equation for Absorber Design The Kremser equation relates inlet/outlet compositions to the number of transfer units (NTU) in a counter-current gas-liquid contactor. ### Step 1: Define Operating Lines and Equilibrium For counter-current absorber with gas (G) and liquid (L) molar flow rates, the operating line relates gas and liquid compositions: $$G \cdot dy = L \cdot dx$$ ### Step 2: Mass Transfer Driving Force The rate of mass transfer at any point is proportional to the deviation from equilibrium: $$dN_A = K_y \cdot a \cdot (y - y^*) \cdot dZ$$ where: - $K_y \cdot a$ = overall mass transfer coefficient × interfacial area (kmol/m³·s) - $y^*$ = gas composition in equilibrium with bulk liquid composition - $Z$ = height of packing or equivalent tray height ### Step 3: Express $dy^*$ in terms of $dx$ via Henry's Law For a linear equilibrium: $y^* = m \cdot x$, where $m$ is the equilibrium slope. Substituting the operating line $L \cdot dx = G \cdot dy$, so $dx = (G/L) \cdot dy$: $$y^* = m \cdot (G/L) \cdot y \cdot \text{(integrated)}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute and Integrate $$\dfrac{dy}{y - y^*} = \dfrac{K_y \cdot a \cdot dz}{G} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + (mG/L)}$$ The right-hand side, multiplied by total height $H$, gives the **number of transfer units (NTU)**: $$NTU_{OG} = \int_{y_{out}}^{y_{in}} \dfrac{dy}{y - y^*} = \dfrac{K_y \cdot a \cdot H}{G} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + (mG/L)}$$ ### Step 5: Final Kremser Form (Closed-Form) For a linear equilibrium relationship, integration yields: $$\dfrac{y_{in} - y^*_{out}}{y_{out} - y^*_{out}} = \left[\dfrac{A}{1 + A}\right]^{N+1}$$ or equivalently for absorption: $$N = \dfrac{\ln \left[\left(\dfrac{y_{in} - m \cdot x_{in}}{y_{out} - m \cdot x_{in}}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}$$ where: - $A = L / (m \cdot G)$ = **Absorption Factor** (dimensionless) - $N$ = number of theoretical stages - $m$ = slope of equilibrium line > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** When $A \to \infty$ (very soluble gas, e.g., H₂S in MEA), the number of stages approaches 1. When $A \to 0$ (insoluble gas), N grows without bound. Optimal design: $1.25 < A < 2.0$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amine Circulation Rate from Material Balance ### Given: - Acid gas flow rate: $\dot{n}_{acid}$ (kmol/h) - Lean amine loading: $L_{lean}$ (mol acid gas / mol amine) - Rich amine loading: $L_{rich}$ (mol acid gas / mol amine) - Amine concentration: $C$ (wt%) - Amine molecular weight: $M$ (g/mol) ### Step 1: Acid Gas Removed Per Mole of Amine $$\Delta L = L_{rich} - L_{lean}$$ ### Step 2: Total Moles of Amine Required Per Hour $$\dot{n}_{amine} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{acid}}{\Delta L}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to Mass Flow $$\dot{m}_{amine} = \dot{n}_{amine} \cdot M \cdot \dfrac{100}{C \cdot 1000} \times 1000 = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{acid} \cdot M \cdot 100}{C \cdot \Delta L}$$ Final result (in kg/h): $$\dot{m}_{amine} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M \cdot \dot{n}_{acid}}{C \cdot \Delta L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Common Error:** Students often forget the $\Delta L$ in the denominator. A typical KTU numerical problem gives you 5 m³/h of acid gas, 0.3 rich/0.05 lean loading, 30 wt% MDEA, and asks for kg/h of solution. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reboiler Duty Calculation The total heat duty of the regenerator reboiler has three components: $$Q_{total} = Q_{desorption} + Q_{sensible} + Q_{steam}$$ ### Step 3.1: Heat of Desorption (Reaction Reversal) $$Q_{desorption} = \dot{n}_{acid} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$$ where $\Delta H_{rxn}$ ≈ **1900 kJ/kg H₂S** (for MEA–H₂S) or **1900–2200 kJ/kg** for MDEA. ### Step 3.2: Sensible Heat to Raise Amine from 40 °C to 120 °C $$Q_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{amine,sol} \cdot C_{p,sol} \cdot (T_{reb} - T_{lean})$$ where $C_{p,sol} \approx$ **3.8–4.0 kJ/(kg·K)** for aqueous amine. ### Step 3.3: Steam Stripping Heat (Vaporizing Stripping Steam) $$Q_{steam} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_{steam}$$ where $\lambda_{steam} \approx$ **2200 kJ/kg** at 1.5 bar (saturated steam at 127 °C). ### Combined Working Equation: $$Q_{total} = \dot{n}_{acid} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} + \dot{m}_{sol} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + \dot{m}_{H_2O,strip} \cdot \lambda$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Numerical Pattern:** A typical 14-mark problem in KTU ESE asks for the amine circulation rate AND the reboiler duty, given gas composition, flow rate, and amine type. Practice this 2-step calculation together. ## 3.4 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem:** A natural gas stream at 70 bar contains 5 mol% H₂S and 3 mol% CO₂. Flow rate: 1.0 × 10⁶ SCFD (standard ft³/day) at 15.6 °C, 1 atm. Use 30 wt% MDEA solution. Lean loading: 0.005 mol H₂S/mol MDEA. Rich loading: 0.45 mol H₂S/mol MDEA. Determine: (a) Acid gas flow in kmol/h (b) MDEA circulation rate in kg/h of solution (c) Reboiler duty in kW **Solution:** ### Part (a): Acid Gas Flow Standard molar volume at 15.6 °C, 1 atm: **V_m ≈ 23.69 m³/kmol** $$\dot{V} = \dfrac{10^6 \text{ SCFD}}{24 \times 35.31} = \dfrac{10^6}{847.4} = 1180 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$\dot{n}_{total} = \dfrac{1180}{23.69} = 49.8 \text{ kmol/h}$$ H₂S in feed: $\dot{n}_{H_2S} = 0.05 \times 49.8 = 2.49$ kmol/h CO₂ in feed: $\dot{n}_{CO_2} = 0.03 \times 49.8 = 1.49$ kmol/h Total acid gas: $\dot{n}_{acid} = 3.98$ kmol/h ### Part (b): MDEA Circulation Rate $$\Delta L = 0.45 - 0.005 = 0.445 \text{ mol/mol}$$ MDEA molecular weight: $M$ = 119.16 g/mol $$\dot{m}_{MDEA,pure} = \dfrac{3.98 \times 119.16 \times 100}{30 \times 0.445} = \dfrac{47,425.7}{13.35} = 3553 \text{ kg/h (pure MDEA)}$$ For 30 wt% solution: $\dot{m}_{solution} = 3553 / 0.30 = 11{,}843$ kg/h ≈ **11.8 tonnes/h** ### Part (c): Reboiler Duty Heat of desorption for H₂S: 1900 kJ/kg = 1900 × 34 = 64,600 kJ/kmol H₂S Heat of desorption for CO₂: 1900 kJ/kg = 1900 × 44 = 83,600 kJ/kmol CO₂ $$Q_{desorption} = 2.49 \times 64{,}600 + 1.49 \times 83{,}600 = 160{,}854 + 124{,}564 = 285{,}418 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Sensible heat: $\dot{m}_{sol} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 11{,}843 \times 3.9 \times (120 - 50) = 11{,}843 \times 273 = 3{,}233{,}139$ kJ/h Assume 15% additional steam stripping: $Q_{steam} = 0.15 \times Q_{desorption+sensible} \approx 528{,}000$ kJ/h Total: $Q_{total} = 285{,}418 + 3{,}233{,}139 + 528{,}000 \approx 4{,}046{,}557$ kJ/h $$Q_{total} = \dfrac{4{,}046{,}557}{3600} = 1124 \text{ kW}$$ **Typical reboiler duty: 3.5 – 4.0 GJ per tonne of acid gas removed** (this is the industry benchmark). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Amine Process Unit The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional interactions of all major equipment in a typical amine gas treating unit. This is the **standard KTU-expected schematic** for any 14-mark process flow question. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[INLET GAS
Sour Natural Gas
H2S, CO2, CH4, C2H6]:::inlet B[INLET SEPARATOR
Knock-out Drum
Liquid + Slug Catcher]:::vessel C[AMINE ABSORBER
Counter-current Tower
20 to 30 Trays
40 to 60 deg C
50 to 100 bar]:::reactor D[OUTLET SEPARATOR
Flash Drum]:::vessel E[SWEET GAS OUTLET
Pipeline Spec
H2S less than 4 ppm]:::outlet F[FLASH TANK
Low Pressure Flash]:::vessel G[RICH AMINE PUMP
High Pressure Pump]:::pump H[LEAN RICH EXCHANGER
Plate Heat Exchanger]:::heater I[REGENERATOR TOWER
Stripper / Desorber
115 to 125 deg C
1.05 to 1.7 barg]:::reactor J[REBOILER
Steam Heated
Fired Heater]:::heater K[OVERHEAD CONDENSER

Phase Separator]:::vessel M[ACID GAS TO SULFUR PLANT
Claus Unit or Stack]:::outlet N[LEAN AMINE SURGE TANK]:::vessel O[LEAN AMINE PUMP
Circulation Pump]:::pump P[AMINE FILTER
Activated Carbon]:::vessel Q[LEAN AMINE COOLER
Air Fin Cooler]:::heater R[AMINE MAKE-UP TANK]:::vessel A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E C -. Rich Amine out bottom .-> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J I --> K K --> L L --> M L -. Reflux back .-> I J -. Lean Amine out bottom .-> H H -. Lean Amine out cold .-> N N --> P P --> O O --> Q Q --> C R -. Make-up amine .-> N classDef inlet fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outlet fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef reactor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vessel fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heater fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Process Stream Description Table (for reference when studying the diagram) | Stream # | Description | Composition / State | Phase | Approx. T (°C) | Approx. P (bar) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sour gas inlet | CH₄ + C₂H₆ + H₂S + CO₂ + H₂O | Gas | 30–40 | 50–100 | | 2 | Sweet gas outlet | CH₄ + C₂H₆ (< 4 ppm H₂S) | Gas | 40–50 | 49–99 | | 3 | Rich amine (loaded) | Amine + H₂S + CO₂ + H₂O | Liquid | 45–60 | 49–99 | | 4 | Lean amine (regenerated) | Amine + trace H₂S + H₂O | Liquid | 40–50 | 1.5–2.5 | | 5 | Acid gas to SRU | H₂S + CO₂ + H₂O vapor | Gas | 40–50 | 1.05–1.7 | | 6 | Reflux water | H₂O + trace amine | Liquid | 40–50 | 1.05–1.7 | | 7 | Reboiler steam | Saturated steam | Vapor | 127–150 | 1.5–4.0 | ## 4.3 Mass-Transfer Zones Inside the Absorber ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[ZONE 1: Top Section
Polishing / Pinch Zone
Low H2S residual
High driving force]:::zone A2[ZONE 2: Middle Section
Bulk Absorption
CO2 pickup begins
Selective for H2S in MDEA]:::zone A3[ZONE 3: Bottom Section
High Acid Gas Loading
Amine near saturation
May use pumparound cooling]:::zone A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 classDef zone fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Regenerator Internals (Stripper Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB B1[REFLUX INLET
Top of Tower]:::io B2[CHIMNEY TRAY
Liquid Distribution]:::io B3[UPPER BED
CO2 Stripping Zone
6 to 8 Trays]:::strip B4[LOWER BED
H2S Stripping Zone
6 to 8 Trays]:::strip B5[REBOILER RETURN
Two-Phase Mixture
Vapor plus Liquid]:::io B6[REBOILER
External Fired Type
Or Thermosyphon]:::io B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6 B6 -. Vapor rises .-> B3 classDef strip fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef io fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Key:** Each labeled block is a piece of equipment or stream. The dashed arrows represent **return streams** (recycle loops). The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to label stream compositions, temperatures, and pressures for full 14-mark credit. ## 4.5 Auxiliary Systems Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB C1[RECLAIMER
NaOH or Vacuum Type
Removes Heat Stable Salts]:::aux C2[AMINE STORAGE
Fresh Amine Make-up]:::aux C3[CHARCOAL FILTER
Removes Hydrocarbons
and Degradation Products]:::aux C4[MECHANICAL FILTER
Removes Particulates]:::aux C5[SURGE TANK
N2 Blanketed
Capacity 4 to 6 hours]:::aux C6[CORROSION MONITOR
Probe and Coupon Rack]:::aux C2 --> C5 C5 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C4 --> C1 C1 --> C5 C5 -. Returns to Main Loop .-> C6 C6 -. In Line .-> C5 classDef aux fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Amine Process. List the four main types of amines used industrially. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Amine Process** is a regenerative gas sweetening technique that uses aqueous alkanolamine solutions to absorb H₂S and CO₂ from natural gas streams in a high-pressure absorber, followed by thermal regeneration of the rich amine in a stripper column. The four main types of amines used industrially are: 1. **Primary amines** — e.g., Monoethanolamine (MEA) 2. **Secondary amines** — e.g., Diethanolamine (DEA), Diisopropanolamine (DIPA) 3. **Tertiary amines** — e.g., Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) 4. **Sterically hindered amines** — e.g., 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the classification) --- ### Question 2: Why is MDEA preferred over MEA for selective H₂S removal in the presence of CO₂? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** MDEA is a **tertiary amine** with no N–H bond, so it **cannot form a carbamate** with CO₂. The CO₂–MDEA reaction proceeds only via the slow **bicarbonate formation pathway** (rate-controlled by CO₂ hydration). In contrast, the H₂S–MDEA reaction is **instantaneous proton transfer**. This kinetic difference gives MDEA a **selectivity ratio of 5–15 (mol H₂S absorbed / mol CO₂ absorbed)** at typical operating conditions, making it ideal for cases where the CO₂ may be left in the sales gas or only partially removed. Additional benefits: lower regeneration energy, lower corrosion, higher usable acid gas loading (up to 0.5 mol/mol), and reduced foaming tendency. (1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for selectivity value, 1 mark for additional benefits) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Amine Gas Treating Process** for the removal of H₂S and CO₂ from natural gas. Explain the function of each major equipment. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A natural gas stream contains 8 mol% H₂S and 4 mol% CO₂. Flow rate: 5.0 × 10⁶ Nm³/day at standard conditions (0 °C, 1 atm). Using 30 wt% MDEA with lean loading 0.005 and rich loading 0.50, calculate the **MDEA circulation rate in kg/h** of solution. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question A — Model Solution **Part (a) — Process Flow and Equipment (7 marks):** The Amine Process operates as a **closed-loop regenerative absorption cycle**. The major equipment and their functions are: | Equipment | Function | Operating Condition | |---|---|---| | **Inlet Separator** | Removes free liquids, slugs, and solids from incoming sour gas | 50–100 bar, 30–40 °C | | **Amine Absorber** | Counter-current gas-liquid contact; H₂S and CO₂ are absorbed by lean amine | 50–100 bar, 40–60 °C, 20–30 trays | | **Outlet Separator (Flash Drum)** | Removes entrained amine from sweet gas | Same as absorber | | **Rich Amine Flash Tank** | Reduces pressure to flash dissolved hydrocarbons (preventing foaming in regenerator) | 4–7 bar | | **Rich/Lean Exchanger** | Pre-heats rich amine using hot lean amine from reboiler | Plate or shell-and-tube | | **Regenerator (Stripper)** | Reverses absorption by steam stripping; releases concentrated acid gas | 1.05–1.7 bar, 115–125 °C | | **Reboiler** | Supplies heat to drive off acid gas | Steam heated | | **Overhead Condenser + Reflux Drum** | Condenses water vapor from acid gas stream, returns reflux | Air or water cooled | | **Lean Amine Cooler** | Cools lean amine from ~120 °C to ~40 °C before re-entering absorber | Air fin cooler | | **Amine Pump** | Circulates amine through the loop; must handle corrosive service | Centrifugal, stainless steel | | **Amine Filter & Reclaimer** | Removes suspended solids, heat-stable salts, and degradation products | Side stream | **Process description:** 1. Sour gas enters the **inlet separator** to remove liquids. 2. It flows up the **absorber**, contacting down-flowing lean amine. 3. Sweet gas exits the top through a **mist eliminator** and **outlet separator**. 4. Rich amine leaves the absorber bottom, passes through a **flash tank** (hydrocarbon recovery), then through the **rich/lean exchanger**. 5. Pre-heated rich amine enters the top of the **regenerator**. 6. Steam from the **reboiler** strips out H₂S and CO₂ overhead. 7. Hot lean amine returns via the exchanger and cooler to the absorber. 8. Acid gas from the regenerator overhead is sent to a **Claus sulfur recovery unit** or **flare**. (3 marks for flow diagram and stream identification, 2 marks for equipment functions, 2 marks for operating conditions) --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $y_{H_2S} = 0.08$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.04$, Total acid gas fraction = 0.12 - $\dot{V} = 5.0 \times 10^6$ Nm³/day = $\dfrac{5.0 \times 10^6}{24} = 2.083 \times 10^5$ Nm³/h - Standard molar volume at 0 °C, 1 atm: **22.414 Nm³/kmol** **Step 1: Total molar flow** $$\dot{n}_{total} = \dfrac{2.083 \times 10^5}{22.414} = 9293.7 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 2: Acid gas flow** $$\dot{n}_{acid} = 0.12 \times 9293.7 = 1115.2 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 3: MDEA pure mass flow** $\Delta L = 0.50 - 0.005 = 0.495$ mol/mol MDEA molecular weight: $M = 119.16$ g/mol = 0.11916 kg/mol $$\dot{m}_{MDEA,pure} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{acid} \cdot M}{\Delta L} = \dfrac{1115.2 \times 0.11916}{0.495} = 268.4 \text{ kg/h per kmol basis}$$ Recomputing carefully: $$\dot{m}_{MDEA,pure} = \dfrac{1115.2 \times 119.16}{0.495} = \dfrac{132{,}874.6}{0.495} = 268{,}434 \text{ kg/h (pure MDEA)}$$ **Step 4: Solution mass flow (30 wt% solution)** $$\dot{m}_{solution} = \dfrac{268{,}434}{0.30} = 894{,}780 \text{ kg/h} \approx 895 \text{ tonnes/h}$$ **Answer: ~895,000 kg/h of 30 wt% MDEA solution** (approximately 900 TPD) (2 marks for setting up material balance, 3 marks for circulation rate calculation, 1 mark for unit handling, 1 mark for final answer with units) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Compare the **reactions of H₂S and CO₂ with primary, secondary, and tertiary amines**. Why is MDEA preferred for selective H₂S removal? What is the role of a **sterically hindered amine**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** The reboiler of an amine regenerator handles 50,000 kg/h of 30 wt% MDEA solution. The solution enters the rich/lean exchanger at 55 °C, leaves at 95 °C, enters the regenerator at 95 °C, and the reboiler operates at 120 °C. The acid gas flow is 800 kmol/h (H₂S + CO₂ combined). Estimate the **total reboiler duty in MW**. Use: $C_p$(solution) = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = 2000 kJ/kg acid gas, average MW of acid gas = 38 g/mol. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B — Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparative Reaction Chemistry (7 marks):** | Amine Type | Example | H₂S Reaction | CO₂ Reaction | Selectivity Behavior | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Primary** | MEA | Instantaneous, reversible salt formation ($RNH_3^+HS^-$) | Fast carbamate formation: $2 RNH_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow RNH_3^+ + RNHCOO^-$ | Non-selective; removes both H₂S and CO₂ | | **Secondary** | DEA | Same as MEA but slower | Forms carbamate; reaction is moderate | Slightly less reactive than MEA | | **Tertiary** | MDEA | Instantaneous proton transfer: $R_3N + H_2S \rightarrow R_3NH^+ + HS^-$ | Slow bicarbonate formation (no N–H bond → no carbamate): $R_3N + CO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow R_3NH^+ + HCO_3^-$ | **Highly selective for H₂S** (kinetic selectivity ~5–15) | | **Sterically Hindered** | AMP | Instantaneous | Carbamate unstable due to bulky group; free amine regenerates more easily | High capacity, low regeneration energy | **Why MDEA is preferred for selective H₂S removal (3 marks):** 1. **No N–H bond** → cannot form carbamate → CO₂ reaction is slow 2. **H₂S proton transfer is instantaneous** → high H₂S removal efficiency 3. **Selectivity ratio S = 5–15** for H₂S/CO₂ under field conditions 4. **Low regeneration energy** (1.5–2.0 GJ/tonne acid gas vs. 3.5–4.0 for MEA) 5. **Lower corrosion** at higher concentration (30–50 wt%) 6. **Less degradation** with COS and CS₂ **Role of sterically hindered amine (2 marks):** The bulky group adjacent to the N atom (e.g., methyl in AMP) sterically prevents the amine from forming a **stable carbamate** with CO₂. The unstable carbamate spontaneously hydrolyzes, releasing the amine for re-absorption. This gives a **higher effective acid gas loading** (up to 1.0 mol CO₂/mol amine) and **lower reboiler duty** (≈ 30% reduction vs. MEA). (4 marks for table/comparison, 3 marks for MDEA preference + role of hindered amines) --- **Part (b) — Reboiler Duty Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** - $\dot{m}_{sol} = 50{,}000$ kg/h of 30 wt% MDEA - $T_{cold} = 55$ °C, $T_{exchanger out} = 95$ °C, $T_{reg,in} = 95$ °C, $T_{reb} = 120$ °C - $\dot{n}_{acid} = 800$ kmol/h - $C_p = 3.9$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H_{rxn} = 2000$ kJ/kg acid gas, $MW_{acid} = 38$ g/mol **Step 1: Sensible Heat (raising lean amine from 95 °C to 120 °C)** $$Q_{sensible} = 50{,}000 \times 3.9 \times (120 - 95) = 50{,}000 \times 3.9 \times 25 = 4{,}875{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Step 2: Heat of Desorption** Acid gas mass flow: $\dot{m}_{acid} = 800 \times 0.038 = 30.4$ tonnes/h = 30,400 kg/h $$Q_{desorption} = 30{,}400 \times 2000 = 60{,}800{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Step 3: Steam Stripping Heat (assume 12% of sensible + desorption)** $$Q_{steam} = 0.12 \times (4{,}875{,}000 + 60{,}800{,}000) = 0.12 \times 65{,}675{,}000 = 7{,}881{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Step 4: Total Reboiler Duty** $$Q_{total} = 4{,}875{,}000 + 60{,}800{,}000 + 7{,}881{,}000 = 73{,}556{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Converting to MW: $$Q_{total} = \dfrac{73{,}556{,}000}{3600 \times 1000} = 20.43 \text{ MW}$$ **Answer: ~20.4 MW reboiler duty** (2 marks for sensible heat, 2 marks for desorption, 1 mark for steam term, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for final answer) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE — Amine Process:** > > 1. **Confusing primary/secondary/tertiary amine structures:** Students often write MDEA as a secondary amine. **MDEA = CH₃N(C₂H₄OH)₂** is **tertiary** (three carbon-containing groups on N). > 2. **Forgetting to convert Nm³/day to kmol/h:** A frequent 1-mark deduction. Always convert volumetric flow to molar flow using the standard molar volume (22.414 Nm³/kmol at 0 °C). > 3. **Using the wrong standard molar volume:** At 15.6 °C and 1 atm (SCFD/standard conditions), use **23.69 m³/kmol**, not 22.414. > 4. **Using rich loading as the absorption capacity:** The correct $\Delta L = L_{rich} - L_{lean}$. Ignoring $L_{lean}$ leads to underestimating circulation rate. > 5. **Not labeling stream compositions in the process flow diagram:** The KTU valuation key requires **stream-by-stream identification** of phases, temperatures, and pressures. > 6. **Writing "amine absorbs acid gas" without chemical equation:** Examiners expect **balanced reaction equations** for both H₂S and CO₂ with at least one amine. > 7. **Missing the regenerator in the flow diagram:** The amine process is **regenerative** — omitting the stripper column = loss of 3–4 marks. > 8. **Concentration error in wt% vs. mole fraction:** When amine is 30 wt%, the **mass of pure amine = 0.30 × mass of solution**. Mixing up this factor is a 1-mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Amine Process (Module 3)** - [x] The Amine Process is a **regenerative chemical absorption** technique using aqueous alkanolamines. - [x] Primary function: remove **H₂S and CO₂** (acid gases) from natural gas. - [x] Two main equipment: **Absorber (high pressure)** + **Regenerator (low pressure)**. - [x] H₂S reaction is **instantaneous** (mass-transfer controlled). - [x] CO₂ reaction is **slow** (kinetically controlled for tertiary amines). - [x] **MEA** → primary, low cost, high reactivity, high regeneration duty. - [x] **DEA** → secondary, used in SNPA and Econamine processes. - [x] **MDEA** → tertiary, **selective for H₂S**, low regeneration energy. - [x] **AMP** → sterically hindered, high capacity, low energy. - [x] Sulfinol process = **DIPA + Sulfolane** (hybrid). - [x] Absorber operates at **50–100 bar, 40–60 °C**. - [x] Regenerator operates at **~1.5 bar, 115–125 °C**. - [x] Sweet gas spec: **< 4 ppm H₂S** (pipeline quality). - [x] Amine concentration: **15–30 wt% MEA; 30–50 wt% MDEA**. - [x] **Acid gas loading** = mol acid gas / mol amine; typical rich: 0.3–0.5. - [x] **Kremser equation** determines number of theoretical stages. - [x] **Reboiler duty** = 3.5–4.0 GJ/tonne (MEA) or 1.5–2.0 GJ/tonne (MDEA). - [x] Side-stream equipment: **reclaimer, filter, surge tank, corrosion probes**. - [x] Modern trend: **MDEA-based mixed amines** + activators (e.g., piperazine) for enhanced performance. - [x] Always draw **stream arrows with phase labels (G/L), temperature, and pressure** in PFDs.

topics.title: Carbonate Washing Process
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbonate Washing Process

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Carbonate Washing Process** is a regenerative chemical absorption–desorption method used in natural gas and synthesis gas treating to remove acidic impurities — primarily **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** and **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)** — by contacting the sour gas stream with a hot, aqueous solution of **potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)**. The lean (regenerated) solvent absorbs the acid gases in a high-pressure **absorber (contactor)**, producing a sweet product gas overhead and a rich (acid-gas-loaded) solvent bottoms stream, which is then stripped of the acid gases in a low-pressure, high-temperature **regenerator (stripper)**, releasing a lean solvent for recycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most commercially dominant variant of this process is the **Hot Potassium Carbonate (HPC / "Hot Pot") Process**, also known by trade names such as the **Benfield Process** (UOP/Honeywell), **Catacarb**, and **Lurgi Process**. The "activated" or "modified" versions add a small quantity of a rate-promoting amine (DEA, MEA, or hindered amines) to accelerate the otherwise slow CO₂ hydration kinetics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large, hot, salty "sponge bath"** for natural gas. The sour gas (which stings like vinegar because of the CO₂ and smells like rotten eggs because of the H₂S) is bubbled up through a hot alkaline bath of potassium carbonate. The acid gases — being "acidic" — are chemically neutralised and held in the liquid phase just like dirt in a sponge. The cleaned (sweet) gas exits the top. The dirty, spent liquid is then routed to a low-pressure "washing machine" (the stripper) where the heat and the low pressure squeeze the acid gases back out of the liquid, regenerating the sponge for another cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hot and not cold?** Unlike amine systems that operate at moderate temperatures (40–60 °C), the carbonate process is intentionally run hot (typically **100–120 °C / 212–248 °F**). Heating the solvent provides the activation energy needed to break the bicarbonate bond during regeneration, so the process uses **heat as the stripping agent** rather than steam. This is what makes the process thermally efficient and economically attractive for high-pressure, high-CO₂ bulk removal applications.

## 1.3 Operating Range and Standard Metrics

| Operating Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Solvent** | 25–35 wt% K₂CO₃ in water | Plus corrosion inhibitors (e.g., V₂O₅) |
| **Absorber Pressure** | **20–70 bar (290–1015 psia)** | Driven by upstream pipeline |
| **Absorber Temperature** | **100–120 °C (212–248 °F)** | Hot end of solvent |
| **Stripper Pressure** | **1.0–2.5 bar (15–36 psia)** | Slightly above atmospheric |
| **Stripper Temperature** | **115–120 °C (239–248 °F)** | Heat-driven regeneration |
| **Acid Gas Loading** | 0.3–0.5 mol CO₂ / mol K₂CO₃ | Equilibrium-limited |
| **Energy Duty** | **2.0–3.5 MJ per Nm³ of CO₂ removed** | Lower than MEA (≈ 4.0 MJ) |

> [!TIP]
> Always quote the **Hot Pot Process** alongside the generic term "Carbonate Washing" in KTU board answers — examiners reward precise nomenclature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Acid-Gas Absorption

The carbonate washing process is governed by two reversible, equilibrium-controlled reactions. The reversibility is what allows regeneration.

**Reaction 1 — Absorption of CO₂:**

$$
K_2CO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\,KHCO_3
$$

**Reaction 2 — Absorption of H₂S:**

$$
K_2CO_3 + H_2S \;\rightleftharpoons\; KHCO_3 + KHS
$$

The "rich" solvent leaving the bottom of the absorber is therefore a mixture of **potassium bicarbonate (KHCO₃)** and, when H₂S is present, **potassium bisulphide (KHS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reactions are **exothermic in the forward (absorption) direction** and **endothermic in the reverse (regeneration) direction**. The enthalpy of reaction for CO₂ absorption with K₂CO₃ is approximately **ΔH ≈ −95 kJ/mol CO₂**. This heat must be supplied in the stripper and is the reason reboiler duty is the dominant operating cost.

## 2.2 Why "Hot" Carbonate? — A Thermodynamic Justification

Three reasons dictate the elevated operating temperature:

1. **Reversal of equilibrium loading.** The CO₂ equilibrium partial pressure over K₂CO₃ solutions rises steeply with temperature. Operating hot means we can attain very low equilibrium CO₂ partial pressures in the stripper at only mildly reduced total pressure.
2. **Solvent stability.** Potassium carbonate is **thermally and chemically stable** at 100–120 °C — unlike MEA/DEA, it does not degrade to form heat-stable salts.
3. **Low vapour pressure.** Hot K₂CO₃ has negligible vapour pressure, so solvent losses with the sweet gas are essentially zero.

## 2.3 Mass-Transfer and Reaction Kinetics

The carbonate process is **mass-transfer-limited with a slow chemical step**. The rate-controlling step is the hydration of dissolved CO₂ to form bicarbonate:

$$
CO_2 + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; H^+ + HCO_3^- \quad (slow)
$$

Because the hydration of CO₂ at 100 °C is too slow for commercial column heights, **rate promoters / activators** are added:

- **Benfield (UOP):** adds ~3 wt% diethanolamine (DEA) or a hindered amine
- **Catacarb (Air Products):** adds borate/arsenite-free catalyst
- **Lurgi:** adds a small amount of a sterically hindered amine
- **Giammarco-Vetrocoke:** adds arsenite as activator (now discontinued for environmental reasons)

The **enhancement factor (E)** for an activated system can be expressed as:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{(R_{CO_2,\,activated})}{(R_{CO_2,\,unactivated})} \;\approx\; 5 \text{ to } 10
$$

This permits a dramatic reduction in the number of actual absorber trays (often from 25 down to 10–12 in activated systems).

## 2.4 Process Flow Scheme — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Sweet-gas outlet at the top of the absorber.**
Lean K₂CO₃ enters the top tray and trickles downward. Sour gas enters at the bottom and rises. Acid gases are absorbed. Sweet gas exits the top meeting a pipeline spec of < 50 ppm H₂S and < 2 vol% CO₂.

**Step 2 — Rich solvent exits the absorber bottom.**
The rich solvent is saturated with KHCO₃ and KHS. Pressure let-down is accomplished through a **hydraulic turbine (energy-recovery turbine)**, recovering shaft work for the lean-solvent pump.

**Step 3 — Heat exchange.**
Rich solvent passes through a **rich/lean heat exchanger** where it is preheated by the hot, regenerated lean solvent leaving the stripper reboiler. This is the largest single energy-recovery element.

**Step 4 — Stripper top vapour.**
Hot, low-pressure regeneration in the stripper releases the CO₂ and H₂S overhead. This stream is routed to a sulphur recovery unit (Claus plant) when H₂S is significant, or vented (after water wash) for bulk CO₂ removal applications.

**Step 5 — Lean solvent recycle.**
Stripper bottoms, the regenerated lean solvent, is cooled in the heat exchanger, cooled further in a trim cooler, and pumped back to the absorber top.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric K₂CO₃ requirement | $n_{K_2CO_3} \;\geq\; n_{CO_2} + n_{H_2S}$ | kmol/h | Theoretical minimum |
| Heat of reaction (CO₂) | $\Delta H_{CO_2} \;\approx\; -95 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Exothermic absorption |
| Heat of reaction (H₂S) | $\Delta H_{H_2S} \;\approx\; -40 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Less exothermic |
| Equilibrium loading | $\alpha = \dfrac{n_{CO_2,\,absorbed}}{n_{K_2CO_3,\,free}}$ | mol/mol | 0.3–0.5 typical |
| Working capacity | $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{rich} - \alpha_{lean}$ | mol/mol | Maximise for low circulation |
| Reboiler duty (rule of thumb) | $Q_{reb} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5 \text{ GJ per tonne CO}_2$ | GJ/t | Activated: lower end |
| Enhancement factor | $E = k_{obs} \big/ k_{unactivated}$ | — | 5–10 for activated HPC |
| Mass balance on absorber | $G \cdot y_{in} = G \cdot y_{out} + L \cdot \Delta x$ | — | Operating line basis |
| Kremser equation (stages) | $N = \dfrac{\ln[(y_{in}-m x_{in})/(y_{out}-m x_{out})]}{\ln(A)}$ | — | With $A = L/(mG)$ |
| Circulation rate (Kremser) | $L = \dfrac{G (y_{in} - y_{out})}{\alpha_{rich} - \alpha_{lean}}$ | kmol/h | Per mol K₂CO₃ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key intuition for circulation rate:** Because the working capacity $\Delta\alpha$ of carbonate systems is much larger than that of amine systems (≈ 0.4 vs ≈ 0.02), the solvent circulation rate is **roughly 5–10× lower** than an equivalent amine unit. This is the principal economic advantage at high acid-gas partial pressures.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility and Industrial Niche

The Hot Pot process is the workhorse for **bulk CO₂ removal** in the following applications:

- **Ammonia synthesis gas** (where CO₂ must be < 10 ppm to protect the methanator)
- **Hydrogen and methanol plants** (downstream of shift conversion)
- **Natural gas processing** for gases with high CO₂ partial pressure (> 5 bar)
- **Ethylene oxide plants** (CO₂ removal from recycle ethylene)
- **SNG (Substitute Natural Gas) production** from coal gasification

> [!TIP]
> In KTU ESE answers, **always justify the selection** of the carbonate process over an amine system by quoting the **acid-gas partial pressure criterion**: choose hot pot when the CO₂ partial pressure exceeds ≈ 3.5 bar (≈ 50 psia) AND when deep H₂S removal is **not** required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equilibrium Loading Relationship

The reaction of CO₂ with the carbonate ion in solution proceeds through the following mechanistic sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A (slow):} \quad & CO_{2\,(aq)} + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; H^+ + HCO_3^- \\
\text{Step B (fast):} \quad & H^+ + CO_3^{2-} \;\rightleftharpoons\; HCO_3^- \\
\text{Net:} \quad & CO_2 + CO_3^{2-} + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\,HCO_3^-
\end{aligned}
$$

The **thermodynamic equilibrium constant** for the net reaction is:

$$
K_{eq} = \frac{[HCO_3^-]^2}{[CO_3^{2-}] \cdot p_{CO_2}}
$$

Expressing in terms of molalities (m) and Henry's law (p = H·x):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Take the logarithm:} \quad \ln K_{eq} = 2 \ln [HCO_3^-] - \ln [CO_3^{2-}] - \ln p_{CO_2} \\
& \text{Rearrange for the equilibrium CO}_2 \text{ partial pressure:} \quad p_{CO_2}^{\ast} = \frac{[HCO_3^-]^2}{K_{eq}\,[CO_3^{2-}]} \\
& \text{Introduce the loading} \quad \alpha = \dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_3^{2-}] + 0.5\,[HCO_3^-]} \;\text{(mol CO}_2/\text{mol K}_2\text{CO}_3\text{ basis)} \\
& \text{Substituting and simplifying for the free-carbonate fraction} \quad \theta = 1 - \alpha: \\
& \quad p_{CO_2}^{\ast} = \frac{4\,\alpha^{2}\,C_T^{2}}{K_{eq}\,(1-\alpha)\,C_T} = \frac{4\,\alpha^{2}\,C_T}{K_{eq}\,(1-\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_T$ is the total analytical K₂CO₃ concentration (mol/kg solvent) and $K_{eq}$ is the temperature-dependent equilibrium constant.

**Final analytical expression for equilibrium partial pressure of CO₂:**

$$
p_{CO_2}^{\ast} \;=\; \frac{4\,C_T\,\alpha^{2}}{K_{eq}\,(1-\alpha)}
$$

The temperature dependence of $K_{eq}$ is well fitted by:

$$
\ln K_{eq} \;=\; a + \frac{b}{T} \quad \text{with typical constants } a \approx 22.4, \; b \approx -3700 \text{ K}
$$

Because $K_{eq}$ **decreases** as temperature **increases**, the equilibrium $p_{CO_2}^{\ast}$ **rises** with temperature — exactly the property exploited in hot regeneration.

## 3.2 Derivation of Solvent Circulation Rate (Working-Capacity Method)

**Given:**
- Sour-gas flow: $G$ (Nm³/h) with inlet CO₂ mole fraction $y_{in}$ and outlet $y_{out}$
- Required rich loading: $\alpha_{rich}$
- Achievable lean loading: $\alpha_{lean}$ (from stripper equilibrium at $T_{strip}$)

**Step 1 — Molar flux of CO₂ to be removed:**

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,(y_{in} - y_{out})}{22.414} \quad [\text{kmol/h}]
$$

**Step 2 — Working capacity of the solvent:**

$$
\Delta\alpha \;=\; \alpha_{rich} - \alpha_{lean} \quad [\text{mol CO}_2 / \text{mol K}_2\text{CO}_3]
$$

**Step 3 — Required total moles of K₂CO₃ in circulation (per pass):**

$$
n_{K_2CO_3} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{n}_{CO_2}}{\Delta\alpha} \quad [\text{kmol/h}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass flow and volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{solvent} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{K_2CO_3} \cdot M_{K_2CO_3}}{w_{K_2CO_3}} \quad [\text{kg/h}]
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{solvent} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{solvent}}{\rho_{solvent}} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{h}]
$$

where $M_{K_2CO_3} = 138.21$ g/mol, $w_{K_2CO_3} \approx 0.30$ (mass fraction), and $\rho_{solvent} \approx 1250$ kg/m³ for a 30 wt% solution at 110 °C.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Carbonate Circulation for a Natural-Gas Stream

> [!NOTE]
> The following fully worked numerical example is the type of calculation KTU examiners set in Part B. Note the multi-paragraph text justification that is expected in board answers.

**Problem statement:**
A natural gas stream contains **35 mol% CO₂** at a flow rate of **50,000 Nm³/h** at **40 bar** and **110 °C**. The treated gas must contain **≤ 2 mol% CO₂**. The hot carbonate process uses a 30 wt% K₂CO₃ solution operating with a rich loading of 0.45 mol/mol and a lean loading of 0.05 mol/mol. Compute (a) the CO₂ removal rate, (b) the K₂CO₃ circulation rate, (c) the mass and volumetric flow rate of the solvent.

**Solution:**

**(a) Molar flow of feed gas (using the ideal-gas molar volume $V_m = 22.414$ Nm³/kmol):**

$$
\dot{n}_{feed} \;=\; \frac{50{,}000}{22.414} \;=\; 2230.8 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**(b) Molar flow of CO₂ in feed and product:**

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,in} \;=\; 2230.8 \times 0.35 \;=\; 780.8 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,out} \;=\; 2230.8 \times 0.02 \;=\; 44.6 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**(c) Rate of CO₂ to be removed (this is the duty on the solvent):**

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,removed} \;=\; 780.8 - 44.6 \;=\; 736.2 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**(d) Working capacity of the solvent (difference between rich and lean loadings):**

$$
\Delta\alpha \;=\; 0.45 - 0.05 \;=\; 0.40 \text{ mol CO}_2 / \text{mol K}_2\text{CO}_3
$$

**(e) K₂CO₃ circulation rate:**

$$
\dot{n}_{K_2CO_3} \;=\; \frac{736.2}{0.40} \;=\; 1840.5 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**(f) Mass flow rate of pure K₂CO₃:**

$$
\dot{m}_{K_2CO_3} \;=\; 1840.5 \times 138.21 \;=\; 254{,}373 \text{ kg/h} \;\approx\; 254.4 \text{ t/h}
$$

**(g) Mass flow rate of 30 wt% solvent solution:**

$$
\dot{m}_{soln} \;=\; \frac{254{,}373}{0.30} \;=\; 847{,}910 \text{ kg/h} \;\approx\; 848 \text{ t/h}
$$

**(h) Volumetric flow rate of solvent ($\rho \approx 1250$ kg/m³ at 110 °C):**

$$
\dot{V}_{soln} \;=\; \frac{847{,}910}{1250} \;=\; 678 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering sanity check:** This is ≈ 11.4 m³ of solvent per million Nm³ of feed gas — comfortably within the 5–20 m³/MNm³ range typical of HPC units. If your calculated value is outside this band, re-check the working-capacity assumption; the most common error in board answers is forgetting to subtract the lean loading.

## 3.4 Reboiler Duty Estimation

The reboiler heat duty has two components: the sensible heat to bring the stripper bottoms to boil, and the endothermic heat of reversing the bicarbonate reaction. The lumped empirical correlation is:

$$
Q_{reb} \;=\; \dot{V}_{soln} \cdot \rho_{soln} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{strip} + \dot{n}_{CO_2} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}
$$

For the worked example above, with $c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta T_{strip} \approx 10$ K, and $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx 95$ kJ/mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sensible} &= 847{,}910 \times 3.5 \times 10 = 2.97 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ/h} = 8.24 \text{ MW} \\
Q_{rxn} &= 736.2 \times 95 = 69{,}939 \text{ kJ/h} \times 1000 \;=\; 1.94 \times 10^{4} \text{ kJ/h} \;\text{(negligible per-pass, accumulates as circulation duty)} \\
Q_{reb,tot} &\approx 8.24 \text{ MW} \;\approx\; 2.23 \text{ GJ per tonne CO}_2 \text{ removed}
\end{aligned}
$$

This falls at the low end of the literature range (2.0–3.5 GJ/t), confirming that **heat is the major operating cost driver** and that minimising solvent circulation (by maximising $\Delta\alpha$) is the central design objective.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hot Carbonate Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete process-architectural flow of a Hot Potassium Carbonate unit, mapping all the major equipment items, the acid-gas mass transfer paths, and the heat-integration streams. The labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown formatting) to comply with the V10 mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedgas[FEED GAS\nSOUR NATURAL GAS\nH2S AND CO2 RICH] --> comp1[FEED GAS KNOCKOUT\nREMOVE ENTRAINED LIQUID]
    comp1 --> absbot[ABSORBER BOTTOM\nHIGH PRESSURE 40 BAR]
    absbot --> abscol[ABSORBER COLUMN\nCOUNTERCURRENT TRAY OR PACKED]
    abscol --> sweetgas[SWEET GAS OUTLET\nTO PIPELINE SPEC]
    abscol --> richsol[LEAVING RICH SOLVENT\nKHCO3 AND KHS LOADED]
    richsol --> hturbo[HYDRAULIC TURBINE\nPRESSURE LET DOWN\nENERGY RECOVERY]
    hturbo --> rlhx1[RICHT TO LEAN HEAT EXCHANGER\nPREHEAT INCOMING RICH STREAM]
    rlhx1 --> stripftd[STRIPPER FEED\nTOP OF REGENERATOR]
    stripftd --> stripcol[STRIPPER COLUMN\nLOW PRESSURE 1.5 BAR\nHOT 120 DEG C]
    stripcol --> acidgasoff[ACID GAS OFF GAS\nCO2 AND H2S TO SULPHUR RECOVERY]
    stripcol --> leanext[LEAN SOLVENT BOTTOM\nK2CO3 REGENERATED]
    leanext --> rlhx2[LEAN TO RICH HEAT EXCHANGER\nCOOL LEAN STREAM]
    rlhx2 --> trimclr[TRIM COOLER\nFINAL COOLING TO 110 DEG C]
    trimclr --> leanpump[LEAN SOLVENT PUMP\nBOOST TO ABSORBER PRESSURE]
    leanpump --> abscol
    stripcol --> reboil[REBOILER\nHEAT INPUT 8 MW CLASS\nHEATING MEDIUM STEAM]
    hturbo -.SHAFT POWER.-> leanpump
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** The two horizontal streams at the centre — `richsol → rlhx1 → stripftd` and `leanext → rlhx2 → trimclr → leanpump → abscol` — form the **solvent loop**, while the gas-phase path is `feedgas → comp1 → absbot → abscol → sweetgas`. The acid-gas desorption path exits at the top of the stripper, and heat integration is centred on the rich/lean exchanger.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Mass and Heat Transfer Mapping

The diagram below maps the *transport phenomena* layer — where chemistry, mass transfer, and energy transfer occur — onto the unit operations of the plant. This is a higher-order conceptual view that KTU examiners appreciate in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourgasNode[SOUR GAS INLET] -->|COUNTERCURRENT FLOW| absNode[ABSORBER\nGAS LIQUID CONTACT]
    absNode -->|SWEET GAS| sweetNode[SWEET GAS OUTLET]
    absNode -->|RICH SOLVENT| richNode[RICH SOLVENT EXIT]
    richNode -->|HEAT EXCHANGE| hxNode[RICHT TO LEAN HX]
    hxNode -->|PREHEATED RICH| stripNode[STRIPPER\nDESORPTION]
    stripNode -->|ACID GAS VENT| ventNode[ACID GAS TO SRU]
    stripNode -->|LEAN SOLVENT| leanNode[LEAN SOLVENT EXIT]
    leanNode -->|PUMP AND COOL| absNode
    hxNode -.COUNTER FLOW HEAT.- leanNode
    absNode -.EXOTHERMIC RXN.-> coolNode[ABSORBER INTERCOOLER\nREMOVES HEAT OF REACTION]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intercoolers on the absorber** are an often-asked feature: because the absorption reactions are exothermic, the temperature rises up the absorber. Hot K₂CO₃ has favourable equilibrium at high T, but **kinetics improve when the temperature is reduced**, so the column is often split into two beds with an **intercooler (or side cooler)** removing the heat of reaction to maintain ≈ 110 °C throughout.

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Choose the Carbonate Process

| Criterion | Choose Hot Pot (HPC) | Choose Amine (MEA / DEA / MDEA) |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ partial pressure** | > 3.5 bar (bulk removal) | < 3.5 bar (trace removal) |
| **Required CO₂ outlet** | 0.1 – 2.0 vol% | < 100 ppm |
| **H₂S specification** | < 50 ppm acceptable | < 4 ppm (down to ppb with specialty amines) |
| **Operating temperature** | Hot service 100–120 °C | Moderate 40–80 °C |
| **Solvent stability** | No degradation | MEA degrades; HSS forms |
| **Energy efficiency** | Better for high loadings | Better for low loadings |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (stainless steel) | Lower (carbon steel) |
| **Solvent cost** | Very low (potash is cheap) | Higher (amine cost) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the Hot Potassium Carbonate Process. State any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hot Potassium Carbonate Process is a regenerative acid-gas-removal process that uses an aqueous solution of **potassium carbonate (25–35 wt%)** at elevated temperature (**100–120 °C**) to absorb **CO₂ and H₂S** from natural and synthesis gas streams, followed by thermal regeneration of the rich solvent at lower pressure in a stripper. Two industrial applications: (i) bulk CO₂ removal from ammonia synthesis gas, and (ii) CO₂ removal from natural gas streams with high CO₂ partial pressure (> 5 bar) such as those from enhanced oil recovery (EOR) projects.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — operating window with numerical data; 1 mark — applications.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**Write the absorption and regeneration reactions involved in the carbonate washing process for CO₂ removal.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absorption:} \quad & K_2CO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,KHCO_3 \\
\text{Regeneration:} \quad & 2\,KHCO_3 \rightarrow K_2CO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O
\end{aligned}
$$

The reactions are reversible and equilibrium-controlled. The forward (absorption) reaction is exothermic (ΔH ≈ −95 kJ/mol), favouring low temperature, while the reverse (regeneration) is endothermic, favouring high temperature — which is the thermodynamic basis for hot operation in the stripper.

*(1.5 marks — equations; 1.5 marks — thermodynamic comment.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Suggested]

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the working of the **Hot Potassium Carbonate Process** for the removal of CO₂ and H₂S from natural gas. Discuss the chemistry, operating conditions, and the role of activators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas stream at **60 bar and 35 °C** contains **30 mol% CO₂** and is to be treated to give a sweet gas with **≤ 0.5 mol% CO₂** using a **30 wt% K₂CO₃** solution. The solvent enters the absorber at a lean loading of **0.05 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃** and leaves at a rich loading of **0.45 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃**. If the gas flow rate is **1,00,000 Nm³/h**, determine (i) the kmol/h of CO₂ to be removed, (ii) the required K₂CO₃ circulation rate, and (iii) the mass flow rate of the 30 wt% solvent solution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Process description (7 marks):**

The Hot Potassium Carbonate Process consists of two main columns: a high-pressure **absorber** and a low-pressure **stripper**, coupled by a rich/lean heat exchanger, a lean-solvent pump, and an energy-recovery hydraulic turbine.

**Operating window (3 marks):**
- Solvent: 25–35 wt% K₂CO₃ + corrosion inhibitor (e.g., V₂O₅ at 0.5 wt%)
- Absorber: 100–120 °C, 20–70 bar
- Stripper: 110–120 °C, 1.0–2.5 bar
- Activator: 2–5 wt% DEA (Benfield) or sterically hindered amine (Catacarb)

**Role of activators (2 marks):**
Pure K₂CO₃ solutions suffer from slow CO₂ hydration kinetics. Adding 2–5 wt% of a primary or secondary amine (DEA, MEA, or hindered amines) increases the rate of CO₂ absorption by 5–10 times, allowing shorter columns and reducing capital cost. The activator is not consumed; it merely acts as a homogeneous catalyst via the formation and rapid hydrolysis of a carbamate intermediate.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**

$$
K_2CO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons 2\,KHCO_3
$$

$$
K_2CO_3 + H_2S \rightleftharpoons KHCO_3 + KHS
$$

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Molar flow of feed gas:**

$$
\dot{n}_{feed} = \frac{100{,}000}{22.414} = 4461.6 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Molar flow of CO₂ in and out:**

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,in} = 4461.6 \times 0.30 = 1338.5 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,out} = 4461.6 \times 0.005 = 22.3 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ removal rate:**

$$
\dot{n}_{CO_2,\,removed} = 1338.5 - 22.3 = 1316.2 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Working capacity:**

$$
\Delta\alpha = 0.45 - 0.05 = 0.40 \text{ mol CO}_2/\text{mol K}_2\text{CO}_3
$$

**Step 5 — K₂CO₃ circulation rate:**

$$
\dot{n}_{K_2CO_3} = \frac{1316.2}{0.40} = 3290.5 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of pure K₂CO₃:**

$$
\dot{m}_{K_2CO_3} = 3290.5 \times 138.21 = 454{,}768 \text{ kg/h} \approx 454.8 \text{ t/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Mass of 30 wt% solvent:**

$$
\dot{m}_{soln} = \frac{454{,}768}{0.30} = 1{,}515{,}894 \text{ kg/h} \approx 1516 \text{ t/h}
$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — December 2023 / Suggested]

**(a)** Compare the **Hot Potassium Carbonate Process** with the **Amine Absorption Process** for natural gas sweetening. Discuss the advantages, limitations, and suitable operating regimes of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The equilibrium partial pressure of CO₂ over a **25 wt% K₂CO₃** solution at **115 °C** is given by:

$$
p^{\ast}_{CO_2} = \frac{4\,C_T\,\alpha^{2}}{K_{eq}\,(1-\alpha)} \quad \text{bar}
$$

where $C_T = 2.0$ mol/kg solvent and $K_{eq} = 0.080$ at 115 °C. If the absorber operates at **40 bar** and the rich loading is **0.42 mol/mol**, determine the **driving force for mass transfer** ($p_{CO_2,\,bulk} - p^{\ast}_{CO_2,\,rich}$) at the bottom of the absorber, where the gas contains **32 mol% CO₂**. Comment on whether the operating conditions are feasible. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Hot Pot (HPC) | Amine (MEA / DEA / MDEA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating T** | 100–120 °C | 40–80 °C |
| **Working capacity** | 0.3–0.5 mol/mol | 0.01–0.05 mol/mol (for MEA) |
| **Solvent cost** | Very low | Moderate to high |
| **Solvent stability** | Excellent (no degradation) | MEA forms heat-stable salts |
| **Acid-gas partial pressure suitability** | High (> 3.5 bar CO₂) | Low to moderate |
| **CO₂ outlet achievable** | 0.1–2.0 vol% | < 100 ppm |
| **H₂S selectivity** | Low (co-removes CO₂) | High (MDEA selective) |
| **Reboiler duty** | 2.0–3.5 GJ/t CO₂ | 3.5–4.5 GJ/t CO₂ (MEA) |
| **Corrosion** | Aggressive — needs V₂O₅ inhibitor | Mild — carbon steel adequate |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (stainless) | Lower (carbon steel) |

**Conclusions (2 marks):** Hot pot is preferred for **bulk CO₂ removal at high partial pressure** (ammonia, hydrogen, methanol plants). Amine is preferred for **pipeline-spec sweetening with strict H₂S controls** (LNG feed gas, sales gas).

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of CO₂ in the bulk gas at the bottom of the absorber:**

$$
p_{CO_2,\,bulk} = y_{CO_2} \times P = 0.32 \times 40 = 12.8 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium partial pressure at rich loading α = 0.42:**

$$
p^{\ast}_{CO_2} = \frac{4 \times 2.0 \times (0.42)^2}{0.080 \times (1 - 0.42)}
$$

Calculate numerator:

$$
4 \times 2.0 \times 0.1764 = 1.4112
$$

Calculate denominator:

$$
0.080 \times 0.58 = 0.0464
$$

Divide:

$$
p^{\ast}_{CO_2} = \frac{1.4112}{0.0464} = 30.41 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Driving force for mass transfer:**

$$
\Delta p = p_{CO_2,\,bulk} - p^{\ast}_{CO_2,\,rich} = 12.8 - 30.41 = -17.61 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4 — Feasibility comment:**

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative** driving force means that the bulk gas partial pressure is *less* than the equilibrium partial pressure at the chosen rich loading. In other words, at the bottom of the absorber, the rich solvent is **already more heavily loaded than the equilibrium allows** for the prevailing gas composition. The chosen rich loading of 0.42 mol/mol is **infeasible** at 40 bar total pressure. The student must reduce the rich loading to a value that brings $p^{\ast}_{CO_2}$ below 12.8 bar, or alternatively the equilibrium expression indicates that this simple formula is mis-specified for the conditions.

> **[Board Examiner's Note — Pitfall Callout]**
> - **Do not** plug α values > 0.5 into this simplified formula without re-deriving the bicarbonate mass balance; the assumption of dilute KHCO₃ breaks down.
> - **Always** state clearly the units of $K_{eq}$ and $C_T$ — inconsistent units (e.g., mol/kg vs. mol/L) are the single most common error in KTU answers on this topic.
> - **Always** show the **driving-force sign** explicitly; examiners award a full mark for the correct sign and a *comment* on what the sign means physically.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in carbonate-washing questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lean loading** when computing the working capacity. The solvent working capacity is $\alpha_{rich} - \alpha_{lean}$, **not** just $\alpha_{rich}$. This single error inflates the calculated circulation rate by an order of magnitude and is the most heavily penalised mistake.
> 2. **Wrong units of molar volume.** Use $V_m = 22.414$ Nm³/kmol only at **NTP (0 °C, 1 atm)**. If the problem gives the gas flow in Sm³/h at 15.6 °C, use $V_m = 23.69$ Sm³/kmol.
> 3. **Confusing the Benfield process with the Catacarb process.** Both are variants of the Hot Pot process but use different activators. Writing "Benfield uses arsenite activator" will lose marks — Benfield uses DEA; Giammarco-Vetrocoke used arsenite.
> 4. **Not justifying process selection.** The 14-mark Part-B question almost always demands a comparative justification. Always end with one explicit sentence selecting the process and quoting a numerical criterion.
> 5. **Omitting heat-integration features.** The rich/lean heat exchanger and the hydraulic turbine are mandatory diagram elements; their absence costs at least 2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (board-ready):**
> - The **Carbonate Washing Process** uses **hot aqueous K₂CO₃** to absorb CO₂ and H₂S — the chemistry is the reversible formation of **KHCO₃** and **KHS**.
> - The process is also called the **Hot Pot Process**; commercial variants include **Benfield (DEA-activated), Catacarb, Lurgi**, and (historically) **Giammarco-Vetrocoke**.
> - **Operating window:** absorber 100–120 °C at high pressure; stripper 110–120 °C at near-atmospheric pressure.
> - **Why hot?** The reactions are reversible and exothermic in the absorption direction. Hot operation *favours regeneration* thermodynamically and *avoids solvent degradation*.
> - **Activators** (DEA, hindered amines) accelerate the otherwise slow CO₂ hydration step and increase the **enhancement factor E** by 5–10×.
> - **Working capacity** is large (0.3–0.5 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃), giving **5–10× lower circulation** than amine systems.
> - **Best suited for:** bulk CO₂ removal at high partial pressure (> 3.5 bar) — ammonia, hydrogen, methanol, SNG, and high-CO₂ natural gas applications.
> - **Not suited for:** pipeline-spec H₂S (< 4 ppm) — use amines.
> - **Major cost driver:** **reboiler heat duty** (2.0–3.5 GJ per tonne CO₂), minimised by maximising $\Delta\alpha$.
> - **Corrosion control:** add 0.5 wt% **V₂O₅** as inhibitor; the solvent is otherwise aggressive to carbon steel.
> - **Energy recovery:** **hydraulic turbine** on the rich-solvent pressure let-down recovers shaft work for the lean-solvent pump.
> - **Diagram essentials:** absorber, stripper, rich/lean HX, trim cooler, lean pump, hydraulic turbine, reboiler, acid-gas outlet to SRU.
> - **Kremser / McCabe–Thiele** approach: use $N = \ln[(y_{in} - m x_{in})/(y_{out} - m x_{out})] / \ln(L/mG)$ for stage calculation.
> - **Key formula:** $p^{\ast}_{CO_2} = 4 C_T \alpha^{2} / [K_{eq} (1 - \alpha)]$ for equilibrium partial pressure of CO₂ over K₂CO₃.
> - **Always end** the numerical answer with a **sanity check** on the volumetric circulation rate (5–20 m³ solvent per MNm³ feed gas).
> - **Comparison phrase to memorise:** *Hot Pot — bulk CO₂ at high partial pressure; Amine — deep H₂S removal at low partial pressure.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Methanol-Based Process in Natural Gas Engineering

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
The **Methanol-Based Process** in natural gas engineering refers to a chemical engineering operation where **methanol (CH₃OH)** is deployed as a **thermodynamic hydrate inhibitor** (anti-freeze agent) or as a **chemical feedstock** in natural gas processing trains. It encompasses three distinct engineering applications:
1. **Methanol Injection System** (downstream of wellhead, upstream of pipeline/gas plant) for hydrate suppression.
2. **Methanol Regeneration/Recovery Unit (MRU)** to recover injected methanol for reuse.
3. **Methanol Synthesis from Natural Gas** (Steam Reforming → Syngas → Methanol synthesis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The module specifically covers the **mechanism of hydrate inhibition, methanol regeneration cycles, and the Steam Reforming–Methanol Synthesis pathway from natural gas feedstock.**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of natural gas pipelines running through cold deep-sea floors or arctic regions. The natural gas contains water vapor. When temperature drops and pressure rises, water molecules "crystallize" into cage-like ice structures called **gas hydrates** — essentially, ice that traps methane inside. These ice plugs can completely block pipelines, just like cholesterol blocking an artery.

**Methanol acts like "anti-freeze" in a car radiator.** In winter, you pour antifreeze (ethylene glycol) into a car's cooling system to prevent water from freezing. Similarly, methanol is injected into wet natural gas streams so that the water-alcohol mixture has a much lower freezing point, preventing the formation of these dangerous ice-like hydrate plugs.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Methanol Molecular Weight:** 32.04 g/mol
- **Boiling Point of Methanol:** **64.7 °C (337.85 K)**
- **Density of Methanol at 20 °C:** **0.792 g/cm³**
- **Flash Point:** **11 °C** (highly flammable — important for plant safety)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** **19.9 MJ/kg**
- **Typical Methanol Injection Rate:** **0.25–1.0 L per million standard cubic feet (MMSCF)** of natural gas
- **Hydrate Inhibition Concentration:** **10–50 wt% methanol in aqueous phase**

### Methanol in the Natural Gas Value Chain
Methanol's role is dual-purpose:

| Stage | Role of Methanol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Wellhead | Injection | Prevent hydrate formation during choke cooling |
| Gathering line | Continuous injection | Suppress hydrate in subsea flowlines |
| Gas plant | Recovery column | Strip methanol from gas for reuse |
| Petrochemical | Feedstock (syngas) | Convert CH₄ → CH₃OH (fuel/chemical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not glycol like MEG/DEG?** Methanol is preferred for **low-temperature, short-duration** applications (e.g., wellhead start-ups, deep-water) because it has lower viscosity and better mass transfer at sub-zero temperatures, even though glycols (monoethylene glycol) are preferred for **long pipelines** due to lower vapor losses.

---

## 1.2 Sulphur Recovery Process

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Sulphur Recovery Process** is a critical gas-processing operation designed to convert toxic **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)** extracted from natural gas (via amine treating) into **elemental sulphur (S)** or **sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)**. The dominant industrial technology is the **Modified Claus Process**, which combines a **thermal oxidation stage** with multiple **catalytic conversion stages** to achieve 95–99% sulphur recovery efficiency.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine H₂S as toxic, foul-smelling "garbage" produced when natural gas is "cleaned" of sulphur contaminants. You cannot simply release this garbage into the atmosphere because H₂S is deadly, corrosive, and smells like rotten eggs. The Sulphur Recovery Unit (SRU) is essentially a high-tech **recycling plant** that:

1. **Burns** about one-third of the H₂S in a furnace (turning it into SO₂).
2. **Mixes** the remaining two-thirds H₂S with this SO₂ in a reactor.
3. Uses a **catalyst** (like a matchmaker) to make them react and form **pure yellow sulphur** (the same element in matchstick heads).

The end product — bright yellow sulphur — is sold for fertilizers, sulphuric acid production, and rubber vulcanization. It's a beautiful example of turning a hazardous pollutant into a valuable commodity.

### Process Stream Composition
- **Feedstock:** Acid gas from amine regenerator (typically **50–90% H₂S**, balance CO₂, H₂O, hydrocarbons).
- **Products:** Liquid elemental sulphur (**99.5–99.9% pure**).
- **By-products:** Water vapour, small amounts of COS and CS₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Mandate:** Modern SRUs must achieve **>99.9% recovery** to comply with environmental regulations (e.g., US EPA NSPS, KTU-relevant SO₂ emission norms). The unconverted tail gas requires additional treatment via **Tail Gas Treating Units (TGTU)** such as **SCOT (Shell Claus Offgas Treating)** or **SULFREEN** processes.

### Standard Industrial Metrics
- **Claus Reaction Stoichiometry:** 2 H₂S + SO₂ → 3 S + 2 H₂O
- **Operating Furnace Temperature:** **950–1250 °C**
- **Catalytic Converter (Reactor) Temperature:** **200–350 °C**
- **Catalyst:** **Activated alumina (Al₂O₃)** or **Titanium dioxide (TiO₂)**
- **Overall Sulphur Recovery Efficiency (3-stage Claus):** **95–98%**
- **With TGTU (SCOT):** **>99.9%**

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis: Methanol-Based Process

### 2.1.1 Hydrate Formation Mechanism (Foundational Theory)
Natural gas hydrates are **non-stoichiometric clathrate compounds** where water molecules form hydrogen-bonded cages (cages I, II, or H) that encapsulate small gas molecules (CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, H₂S, N₂).

The thermodynamic condition for hydrate formation is governed by the **pressure–temperature equilibrium curve**. For a given pressure, there exists a **hydrate formation temperature (T_eq)**. If the operating temperature drops below T_eq, hydrates form spontaneously.

### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Methanol Inhibition
Methanol acts as a **thermodynamic inhibitor** through the following mechanism:

1. **Hydrogen Bond Disruption:** Methanol (CH₃–OH) has an –OH group that competes with water for hydrogen bonding. Methanol molecules integrate into the water lattice, **disrupting the formation of water cages**.

2. **Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium Shift:** By adding methanol to the aqueous phase, the **water activity (a_w)** is reduced. Since hydrate formation requires a critical water activity, lowering a_w suppresses hydrate formation at lower temperatures.

3. **Colligative Property Effect:** The presence of methanol lowers the **freezing point of water**, extending the operating envelope to lower temperatures.

### 2.1.3 Hammerschmidt Equation (Key Quantitative Tool)
The depression in hydrate formation temperature due to methanol is calculated using the **Hammerschmidt Equation**:

$$\Delta T = \frac{K \cdot X}{M \cdot (100 - X)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T$ = Depression in hydrate formation temperature (°C or °F)
- $K$ = Hammerschmidt constant = **2335** (°C·kg/mol) or **1297** (°F·lb/mol)
- $X$ = Mass % of methanol inhibitor in the aqueous phase
- $M$ = Molecular weight of inhibitor (Methanol = **32.04 g/mol**)

### 2.1.4 Methanol Synthesis from Natural Gas (Steam Reforming Route)
The industrial production of methanol from natural gas follows a **3-step pathway**:

**Step 1: Steam Reforming (Primary Reformer)**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2: Water-Gas Shift Reaction (Secondary Reformer)**
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3: Methanol Synthesis (Catalytic, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst)**
$$CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH \quad \Delta H = -90.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$CO_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH + H_2O \quad \Delta H = -49.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.1.5 Methanol Recovery/Regeneration
After injection, methanol-rich water is recovered using a **Methanol Recovery Unit (MRU)** consisting of:
- **3-Phase Separator** (gas–oil–water separation)
- **Distillation Column** (regenerate pure methanol overhead, water bottoms)
- **Reflux System**

Typical recovery efficiency: **85–95%**

---

## 2.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis: Sulphur Recovery (Modified Claus Process)

### 2.2.1 Process Philosophy
The Modified Claus Process exploits the **Claus reaction**:
$$2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow \frac{3}{x} S_x + 2H_2O$$

This reaction is the cornerstone of all modern sulphur recovery units.

### 2.2.2 Stage-Wise Process Description

**Stage 1: Thermal Stage (Reaction Furnace)**
- Operating Temperature: **950–1250 °C**
- Operating Pressure: **0.7–1.5 bar (gauge)**
- About **1/3 of H₂S** is burned with air/oxygen:
$$H_2S + \frac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O$$
- The remaining **2/3 H₂S** reacts with SO₂ to form elemental sulphur.

**Stage 2: First Catalytic Converter (Condenser + Reactor)**
- Gas cooled in **waste-heat boiler (WHB)** to **~350 °C**.
- Sulphur condensed in **sulphur condenser** and removed as liquid.
- Remaining gas reheated to **~200–250 °C** and passed over **Al₂O₃ catalyst**.
- Reaction: $2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$

**Stage 3: Second & Third Catalytic Converters**
- Similar to Stage 2, but at progressively **lower temperatures** (~200 °C, then ~190 °C) to push equilibrium toward more sulphur.
- Each stage can recover an additional **20–30%** of the remaining sulphur.

### 2.2.3 Tail Gas Treatment
Unconverted H₂S/SO₂ in the final tail gas (typically 1–3% of inlet) is treated by:
- **SCOT Process:** Reduction of all sulphur compounds to H₂S using H₂ + Co-Mo catalyst, then amine absorption.
- **SULFREEN / Cold Bed Absorption:** Sub-dewpoint Claus reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reaction Engineering Insight:** The Claus reaction is **exothermic and equilibrium-limited**. Lower temperatures favour higher conversion (Le Chatelier's principle), but temperatures must remain above the **sulphur dew point (~120–160 °C)** to prevent liquid sulphur deposition and catalyst fouling.

### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta T = \frac{K \cdot X}{M(100-X)}$ | Hammerschmidt Equation | Methanol dose for hydrate suppression |
| $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ | Steam Reforming | Methanol synthesis step 1 |
| $CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$ | Methanol Synthesis | Methanol production (main rxn) |
| $2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$ | Claus Reaction (overall) | Sulphur recovery core |
| $H_2S + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O$ | Thermal furnace combustion | SRU thermal stage |
| $\eta_{Claus} = \frac{S_{produced}}{S_{feed}} \times 100$ | Sulphur recovery efficiency | SRU performance metric |
| $Air/Fuel\ ratio = \frac{mol\ O_2}{2 \cdot mol\ H_2S + 0.5 \cdot mol\ CH_4}$ | Stoichiometric combustion | Acid gas burner design |
| $H_2S:S\text{-to-}SO_2$ molar ratio target | 2:1 for maximum conversion | Process control |
| $K_{eq}(T) = \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | Claus equilibrium constant | Reactor design |
| $T_{dew,sulphur} \approx 160 + 0.1 \cdot P_{sulphur}$ | Sulphur dew point | Reactor outlet T control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the vertical pipe symbol `|` in this table.** All delimiter symbols are kept outside the LaTeX/math environment to prevent markdown breakage.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| Methanol Injection | Sub-sea gas wellheads (e.g., North Sea, KG Basin), LNG plants, cold-weather pipelines |
| Methanol Recovery | Offshore platforms, FPSOs |
| Methanol Synthesis | DME production, biodiesel, MTBE feedstock |
| Claus SRU | All natural gas plants, refineries (FCC, hydrocracker off-gas) |
| SCOT Tail Gas | Environmental compliance (zero-emission plants) |
