# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Methanol Dose Calculation Using Hammerschmidt Equation

### Problem Statement
A natural gas pipeline operates at **80 bar (gauge)**. The hydrate formation temperature for the wet gas (without inhibitor) is **18 °C**. The minimum pipeline operating temperature is **−5 °C** (winter). Calculate the **minimum mass% of methanol** required in the aqueous phase to prevent hydrate formation. Use Hammerschmidt constant $K = 2335$ (SI units).

### Step 1: Identify the Required Temperature Depression
We need to suppress hydrate formation until the temperature drops to **−5 °C**.

$$T_{eq,no\,inhibitor} = 18 \text{ °C}$$
$$T_{operating} = -5 \text{ °C}$$

The temperature depression required is:

$$\Delta T = T_{eq,no\,inhibitor} - T_{operating}$$
$$\Delta T = 18 - (-5) = 23 \text{ °C}$$

**Conversion logic:** Hydrate equilibrium must be pushed downward by 23 °C, so the inhibitor must mathematically lower the equilibrium curve by exactly this amount.

### Step 2: Apply the Hammerschmidt Equation
$$\Delta T = \frac{K \cdot X}{M \cdot (100 - X)}$$

Substituting known values:

$$23 = \frac{2335 \cdot X}{32.04 \cdot (100 - X)}$$

### Step 3: Algebraic Rearrangement (Step-by-Step)
Multiply both sides by $(100 - X)$:

$$23 \cdot (100 - X) = \frac{2335 \cdot X}{32.04}$$

$$23 \cdot 100 - 23X = \frac{2335 \cdot X}{32.04}$$

$$2300 - 23X = 72.85 \cdot X$$

**Conversion logic:** We compute $2335 / 32.04 = 72.85$ to simplify.

Move all $X$ terms to one side:

$$2300 = 23X + 72.85X$$
$$2300 = 95.85X$$
$$X = \frac{2300}{95.85}$$
$$X = 23.99 \approx 24.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

### Step 4: Verification
Substitute $X = 24$ back:

$$\Delta T = \frac{2335 \cdot 24}{32.04 \cdot (100 - 24)} = \frac{56040}{32.04 \cdot 76} = \frac{56040}{2435.04} \approx 23.0 \text{ °C} \checkmark$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A **24 wt% methanol** concentration in the aqueous phase is required. This falls within the standard operating range (10–50 wt%), confirming feasibility.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Modified Claus Process Mass Balance

### Problem Statement
An SRU feed (acid gas) contains **1000 kmol/h** of H₂S, **150 kmol/h** of CO₂, and **20 kmol/h** of CH₄. Air (21% O₂, 79% N₂) is supplied such that **exactly 1/3 of H₂S** is combusted to SO₂ in the reaction furnace. Calculate:
1. Theoretical air required.
2. Molar flow of SO₂ produced.
3. Maximum sulphur production (assuming 100% Claus efficiency in furnace).

### Step 1: Stoichiometry of H₂S Combustion (Furnace Reactions)
The combustion in the reaction furnace follows:

**Reaction 1: Partial Combustion (1/3 of H₂S)**
$$H_2S + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O$$

**Reaction 2: Claus Reaction (Remaining 2/3 H₂S + SO₂)**
$$2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$$

**Reaction 3: CH₄ Combustion (Side Reaction)**
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

### Step 2: Calculate H₂S Combusted
Total H₂S = 1000 kmol/h. One-third combusted:

$$H_2S_{combusted} = \frac{1000}{3} = 333.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Remaining H₂S** for Claus reaction:

$$H_2S_{Claus} = 1000 - 333.33 = 666.67 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 3: Calculate SO₂ Produced (From Reaction 1)
Stoichiometry: 1 mole H₂S → 1 mole SO₂

$$SO_2\ produced = 333.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 4: Oxygen Required for H₂S Combustion
From Reaction 1: 1 mole H₂S requires 1.5 mole O₂

$$O_2\ for\ H_2S = 333.33 \times 1.5 = 500.00 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 5: Oxygen Required for CH₄ Combustion
From Reaction 3: 1 mole CH₄ requires 2 mole O₂

$$O_2\ for\ CH_4 = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 6: Total Theoretical Oxygen
$$O_2\ total = 500 + 40 = 540 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 7: Theoretical Air Required (21% O₂)
$$Air = \frac{540}{0.21} = 2571.43 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**N₂ in air** = 2571.43 × 0.79 = 2031.43 kmol/h

### Step 8: Verify Stoichiometric Ratio (H₂S : SO₂ = 2:1)
Required SO₂ for full Claus conversion of 666.67 kmol/h H₂S:

$$SO_2\ required = \frac{666.67}{2} = 333.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$

This **exactly matches** the SO₂ produced in Step 3. ✓ The 2:1 stoichiometry is achieved.

### Step 9: Maximum Sulphur Production (Furnace Only)
From Reaction 2: 2 moles H₂S + 1 mole SO₂ → 3 moles S

$$S\ produced\ (max) = 666.67 \times \frac{3}{2} = 1000 \text{ kmol/h}$$

In mass terms:

$$Mass\ of\ S = 1000 \times 32.06 = 32{,}060 \text{ kg/h} = 32.06 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

### Final Answer Summary
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical air required | **2571.43 kmol/h** |
| SO₂ produced in furnace | **333.33 kmol/h** |
| Max sulphur from furnace | **1000 kmol/h (32.06 t/h)** |
| H₂S : SO₂ ratio achieved | **2 : 1** (optimum) |

> [!NOTE]
> In real plants, furnace conversion is only **60–70%** due to kinetic and equilibrium limitations. The remaining 30–40% is captured in downstream catalytic converters.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Processes

| Parameter | Methanol Injection | Methanol Synthesis | Claus SRU | Tail Gas (SCOT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Hydrate suppression | Fuel/feedstock production | Sulphur recovery | Emission control |
| **Main Reactor** | Mixing tee / inline mixer | Fixed-bed catalytic reactor | Furnace + 3 catalytic reactors | Reduction + amine absorber |
| **Temperature** | Ambient to −30 °C | 200–300 °C (synthesis) | 950–1250 °C (furnace), 200–350 °C (catalytic) | 300–400 °C (reducer) |
| **Pressure** | Pipeline pressure (50–100 bar) | 50–100 bar | 0.7–1.5 bar (gauge) | 0.5–1.0 bar (gauge) |
| **Catalyst** | None (physical inhibitor) | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | Activated Al₂O₃ / TiO₂ | Co-Mo / Ni-Mo |
| **Key Product** | Methanol-rich water for reuse | CH₃OH (99.85% purity) | Liquid sulphur (99.9%) | H₂S (recycled to SRU) |
| **Conversion/Efficiency** | 85–95% (recovery) | 70–80% per pass | 95–98% (3-stage) | Adds 1.5–2.5% (total >99.9%) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Model Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define gas hydrates. Explain why methanol is used as a hydrate inhibitor in natural gas pipelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gas hydrates are non-stoichiometric, ice-like crystalline compounds in which water molecules form hydrogen-bonded cages that physically trap small gas molecules such as CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, and H₂S. They form at low temperature and high pressure and can plug pipelines, valves, and process equipment.

Methanol (CH₃OH) is used as a hydrate inhibitor because:
1. It is highly soluble in water and lowers the water activity (a_w), thereby depressing the hydrate formation temperature (thermodynamic inhibition).
2. It has low viscosity and good mass-transfer characteristics at sub-zero temperatures, making it suitable for cold, deep-water operations.
3. It is cost-effective and easily recoverable through distillation in a methanol recovery unit (MRU).

### Question 2 (Model Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Write a short note on the Modified Claus Process for sulphur recovery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Modified Claus Process converts toxic hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) from acid gas into elemental sulphur. It consists of a **thermal stage** (reaction furnace at 950–1250 °C where 1/3 of H₂S is burned to SO₂) followed by **2–3 catalytic stages** (with Al₂O₃/TiO₂ catalyst at 200–350 °C) where the remaining H₂S reacts with SO₂ to form elemental sulphur via the reaction $2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$. A typical 3-stage Claus plant achieves **95–98% sulphur recovery efficiency**, while addition of a tail gas treating unit (e.g., SCOT) pushes total recovery to **>99.9%**.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*With neat block/schematic diagrams, describe the process steps for synthesis of methanol from natural gas. State the operating conditions and major reactions involved.*

#### Sub-Part (a): Methanol Synthesis Process Description (7 marks)

**Answer:**

Methanol is synthesized industrially from natural gas (methane) in a 3-step process:

**Step 1: Feed Desulphurisation**
Natural gas containing H₂S is passed through a **ZnO fixed-bed reactor** at ~370 °C to remove sulphur to <0.1 ppm, since sulphur is a poison to the nickel and copper catalysts downstream.

**Step 2: Steam Reforming (Syngas Generation)**
Desulphurised methane is mixed with superheated steam (steam-to-carbon ratio ≈ 2.5–3.0) and passed through a **primary reformer** (nickel catalyst, 800–900 °C, 30–40 bar):
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

A **secondary reformer** with controlled oxygen injection provides the remaining conversion and adjusts the H₂:CO ratio:
$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$$

**Step 3: Methanol Synthesis (Catalytic Loop)**
The syngas (H₂ + CO + CO₂) is compressed to **50–100 bar** and passed through a **fixed-bed catalytic reactor** containing **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** catalyst at **200–280 °C**:
$$CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH \quad \Delta H = -90.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$CO_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH + H_2O$$

The crude methanol is condensed, separated, and refined in a **distillation column** to produce **Grade AA methanol (99.85% purity)**.

#### Sub-Part (b): Operating Conditions and Block Diagram (7 marks)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Steam-to-carbon ratio | 2.5 – 3.0 |
| Primary reformer T | 800 – 900 °C |
| Primary reformer P | 30 – 40 bar |
| Synthesis reactor T | 200 – 280 °C |
| Synthesis reactor P | 50 – 100 bar |
| Catalyst | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (sometimes with Cr₂O₃) |
| Per-pass conversion | 70 – 80% (unreacted gas recycled) |
| H₂ : CO ratio | 2.0 – 2.2 (stoichiometric ≈ 2.0) |
| Methanol selectivity | >99% |

**Refer to Section 4.3 for the Mermaid block diagram of the methanol synthesis plant.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Describe the Modified Claus Process for sulphur recovery from acid gas. With a neat flow diagram, explain the thermal and catalytic stages, including the relevant reactions and operating conditions.*

#### Sub-Part (a): Thermal Stage (7 marks)

**Answer:**

The thermal stage occurs in the **reaction furnace**, which is the heart of the SRU. Acid gas (50–90% H₂S) from the amine regenerator is fed to the burner along with carefully controlled amounts of air (or oxygen-enriched air). Operating conditions:

- **Furnace Temperature:** 950–1250 °C
- **Pressure:** 0.7–1.5 bar (gauge)
- **Residence Time:** ~1 second

**Key Reactions in the Furnace:**

1. **Partial Oxidation of H₂S (~1/3 of total H₂S):**
$$H_2S + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O$$

2. **Claus Reaction (~2/3 of H₂S with SO₂):**
$$2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$$

3. **Hydrocarbon Combustion (if CH₄ present in acid gas):**
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

4. **COS/CS₂ Formation (side reactions):**
$$CH_4 + 2S \rightarrow CS_2 + 2H_2$$
$$CO_2 + H_2S \rightarrow COS + H_2O$$

The hot furnace effluent passes through a **waste heat boiler (WHB)** where high-pressure steam is generated, and the gas is cooled to ~350 °C. Liquid sulphur is condensed in a **sulphur condenser** and routed to a sulphur pit.

#### Sub-Part (b): Catalytic Stages & Tail Gas Treatment (7 marks)

**Catalytic Stage Description:**

The cooled gas (now depleted in sulphur vapour) is reheated to **200–250 °C** and fed to the **first catalytic converter** packed with **activated alumina (Al₂O₃)** catalyst. The Claus reaction proceeds:
$$2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -108 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Each subsequent catalytic stage operates at **progressively lower temperature** to shift the equilibrium toward more sulphur (Le Chatelier's principle):
- **Converter 1:** ~240 °C
- **Converter 2:** ~200 °C
- **Converter 3:** ~190 °C

A typical 3-stage Claus unit achieves **95–98% overall sulphur recovery**.

**Tail Gas Treatment (TGTU):**

To meet stringent environmental norms (<50 ppm SO₂ in stack), the tail gas is treated in a **SCOT (Shell Claus Off-gas Treating) Unit**:
1. **Hydrogenation:** All sulphur compounds reduced to H₂S over a Co-Mo catalyst at 300–400 °C.
2. **Quench & Absorb:** H₂S absorbed in MDEA/amine solution.
3. **Strip & Recycle:** Rich amine regenerated; H₂S returned to SRU front-end.

**Refer to Section 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram of the SRU.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Mistaking H₂S : SO₂ ratio:** Students often write "1:1" instead of the correct **2:1** molar ratio. This is a 2-mark deduction in part (a) of SRU questions.
> 2. **Wrong catalyst for methanol synthesis:** Writing "Ni catalyst" instead of **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the 2:1 stoichiometry calculation:** In numericals on Claus furnace, examiners want to see the **explicit 1/3 H₂S combustion logic**.
> 4. **Confusing methanol injection vs. methanol synthesis:** Methanol injection is a **hydrate-control** application; methanol synthesis is a **chemical production** process. Don't mix the two.
> 5. **Forgetting WHB steam recovery:** The waste heat boiler is a key energy-recovery feature in SRUs. Always mention it in flow diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Checklist (Rapid Revision)

- [ ] **Methanol** is the **#1 thermodynamic hydrate inhibitor** for natural gas pipelines.
- [ ] **Hammerschmidt equation** quantifies methanol dose: $\Delta T = \frac{KX}{M(100-X)}$
- [ ] Methanol **inhibits** hydrates by **disrupting water hydrogen bonding** and **lowering water activity**.
- [ ] **Methanol synthesis** from natural gas is a 3-step process: **Desulphurisation → Steam Reforming → Catalytic Synthesis**.
- [ ] The **catalyst for methanol synthesis is Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃**, operating at **200–280 °C, 50–100 bar**.
- [ ] The **main reaction** for methanol synthesis is $CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$.
- [ ] **Sulphur recovery** uses the **Modified Claus Process** with a thermal furnace + 2–3 catalytic converters.
- [ ] The **key Claus reaction** is $2H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3S + 2H_2O$.
- [ ] In the **reaction furnace**, **1/3 of H₂S is combusted to SO₂** to maintain the **2:1 H₂S:SO₂ ratio**.
- [ ] **Furnace temperature** = 950–1250 °C; **catalyst bed temperature** = 200–350 °C.
- [ ] **3-stage Claus recovery efficiency** = 95–98%; with **SCOT tail-gas treatment** > 99.9%.
- [ ] **Al₂O₃ (activated alumina)** is the standard Claus catalyst; **Co-Mo** is used in SCOT hydrogenation.
- [ ] **Sulphur recovery catalysts** must operate **above the sulphur dew point (~120–160 °C)** to prevent liquid sulphur deposition.
- [ ] The **Methanol Recovery Unit (MRU)** regenerates methanol via **distillation**, achieving **85–95% recovery**.
- [ ] **H₂S:SO₂ ratio of 2:1** is the **single most important control parameter** in SRUs.

### Final Exam Strategy Tip
> [!TIP]
> For a **14-mark question on SRU/Methanol**, structure your answer as: **(1) Block diagram (4 marks) + (2) Process description with reactions (6 marks) + (3) Operating conditions table (4 marks)**. KTU examiners reward neat flow diagrams heavily.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression of Natural Gas: Types of Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compression of Natural Gas: Types of Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Compression of Natural Gas — Types of Compressors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Natural gas compression** is the mechanical process of increasing the pressure of natural gas by reducing its specific volume through the application of external work, accomplished using devices known as **compressors**. In upstream and midstream oil and gas operations, compression is indispensable for **gas transmission through pipelines**, **gas lift operations**, **gas processing and treatment**, **gas storage**, and **gas reinjection for enhanced oil recovery (EOR)**.

A **compressor** is defined (per **API 617** and **API 618** standards) as a *rotodynamic or positive-displacement machine that mechanically increases the pressure of a compressible fluid (gas) by reducing its volume.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Natural gas must be compressed at every stage of the value chain — at the wellhead (low-pressure gathering), at processing plants (for fractionation/refrigeration), along transmission pipelines (to overcome frictional losses), and at distribution points (city gas stations). The compressor is therefore the **"heart"** of any gas handling system.

### 1.2 Why Compression is Necessary

| Operational Need | Function of Compression |
|---|---|
| **Long-distance pipeline transport** | Increases pressure to overcome frictional and elevation head losses |
| **Gas processing (dew-point control, NGL recovery)** | Provides high-pressure feed for cryogenic expansion and Joule-Thomson cooling |
| **Underground storage (depleted reservoirs, salt caverns)** | Injects gas at pressures exceeding reservoir pressure |
| **Gas-lift artificial lift** | Supplies high-pressure gas to lift liquids from wells |
| **LNG liquefaction feed** | Pre-compresses gas to ~50–80 bar before refrigeration |
| **CNG dispensing / vehicular fuel** | Compresses gas to 200–250 bar for storage cylinders |
| **EOR / gas reinjection** | Re-pressurizes reservoirs to maintain pressure and displace oil |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> Natural gas at the wellhead is typically at **low pressure (1–20 bar)** and **low temperature (30–80 °C)**. It must be compressed by factors of **10 to 200** depending on the application. This requires a series of compression stages with inter-stage cooling.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bicycle Pump":**
> Think of a bicycle pump: when you push down on the handle, you compress a fixed amount of air into a smaller volume at the bottom of the barrel, forcing it through the nozzle at higher pressure. That is precisely what a **reciprocating compressor** does — it traps a fixed mass of gas, reduces its volume using a piston, and discharges it at higher pressure. Now imagine doing this continuously, thousands of times per minute, and with much higher pressures (200+ bar) and much larger volumes (millions of cubic feet per day) — that is a **natural gas compressor station**.

> A **centrifugal compressor**, by contrast, is more like a **fan's "turbo" version** — instead of trapping gas, it spins an impeller at very high RPM, flinging gas outward by centrifugal force and converting kinetic energy into pressure energy in a downstream diffuser. It is the gas-industry equivalent of a turbocharger.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:**
> If a gas compressor "traps and squeezes" a fixed mass of gas → it is **positive displacement (PD)**.
> If it "throws and slows" a continuous stream of gas → it is **dynamic (centrifugal/axial)**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Suction pressure ($P_1$)** — inlet gas pressure (typically in **psia** or **bar**)
- **Discharge pressure ($P_2$)** — outlet gas pressure
- **Compression ratio ($R_c$)** — defined as $R_c = P_2 / P_1$
- **Standard conditions** — **60 °F (15.56 °C) and 14.7 psia** in US; **0 °C and 1.01325 bar** in SI
- **Inlet volumetric flow** — expressed in **MMSCFD** (million standard cubic feet per day) or **Nm³/h**
- **Polytropic head (Hp)** — work per unit mass in a polytropic process
- **Isentropic efficiency ($\eta_s$)** — ratio of ideal (isentropic) work to actual work
- **Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$)** — ratio of actual gas intake to swept volume
- **Compressor brake horsepower (BHP)** — actual shaft power required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Required:**
> - Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
> - For natural gas (assuming methane-like behaviour): specific gas constant $Z = 0.85 - 0.95$ typically
> - Specific heat ratio for methane: $\gamma = C_p / C_v \approx 1.31$
> - Standard acceleration: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Gas Compressors

Compressors used in the natural gas industry are classified into **two fundamental families**:

```
                  GAS COMPRESSORS
                       │
        ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
        │                             │
  POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT         DYNAMIC (ROTODYNAMIC)
   (Traps fixed mass)        (Imparts kinetic energy)
        │                             │
   ┌────┴────┐                ┌───────┴────────┐
   │         │                │                │
RECIPROCATING  ROTARY      CENTRIFUGAL       AXIAL
 (Piston)     (Screw,Vane,  (Radial)         (Multi-stage
              Lobe,Liquid       │            axial flow)
              Ring)             │                │
                          (Radial &
                           Mixed Flow)
```

### 2.1.1 Positive Displacement Compressors (PD)

A positive displacement compressor **confines a fixed mass of gas** in a sealed volume, then mechanically reduces that volume, thereby raising pressure. Flow is **independent of discharge pressure** within design limits (limited by driver torque/strength).

**Sub-types used in natural gas service:**

**(a) Reciprocating Compressors (most common in upstream gas operations):**
- Use a **piston** driven by a crankshaft inside a cylinder.
- Equipped with **suction (inlet) valves** and **discharge valves** that are typically pressure-actuated reeds or poppets.
- Can achieve **very high compression ratios (up to 10:1 per stage)** and **high discharge pressures (up to 1000+ bar)**.
- Available in **single-acting** (compression on one side of piston) or **double-acting** (both sides).
- Common configurations: **horizontal, vertical, angle, opposed-piston**.

**(b) Rotary Positive Displacement Compressors:**
- Use rotating elements instead of reciprocating pistons.
- Continuous flow with no pulsation.
- Lower maintenance than reciprocating, but limited pressure ratios.

| Rotary Type | Mechanism | Typical Use in NG |
|---|---|---|
| **Screw compressor** (oil-flooded or dry) | Two intermeshing helical rotors (male + female) | Gas gathering, gas-lift, small pipelines |
| **Vane compressor** | Eccentric rotor with sliding vanes | Low-pressure, oil-free applications |
| **Lobe (Roots) blower** | Two figure-8 lobes (no internal compression) | Very low pressure, large volume — vacuum & aeration |
| **Liquid-ring compressor** | Liquid (often water) acts as piston | Sour gas, ammonia, dirty gas (sealing) |

### 2.1.2 Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Compressors

These compressors impart **velocity (kinetic energy)** to the gas via a high-speed impeller, then convert that velocity into pressure in a **diffuser (volute or vaned)**. Pressure rise depends on **flow rate and impeller tip speed** — *they are NOT positive displacement*.

**(a) Centrifugal Compressors:**
- Gas enters axially at the **eye (hub) of the impeller**, is flung outward by **centrifugal force**, and exits at the periphery into a **diffuser**.
- Each "stage" = one impeller + one diffuser.
- Typical speed: **3000–18000 RPM**.
- Typical pressure ratio per stage: **1.2–1.8**.
- Multi-stage casings may have **2 to 10+ impellers** in series.
- Industry standard: **API 617** for axial and centrifugal compressors.
- **Most widely used compressor class in the world** for pipeline and process applications.

**(b) Axial Compressors:**
- Gas flows **axially (parallel to the shaft)** through successive rows of **rotating blades (rotor)** and **stator blades (stator)**.
- Each stage gives a small pressure rise; **multi-stage (10–20 stages)** builds high pressure.
- Highest efficiency, highest flow capacity.
- Used in **large-scale LNG plants, air separation, large pipeline boosters**, and **gas turbine engines**.

### 2.2 Comparison Matrix: Selecting a Compressor

| Selection Criterion | Reciprocating | Screw (PD Rotary) | Centrifugal | Axial |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max pressure ratio per stage** | 4–10 | 4–6 | 1.2–1.8 | 1.05–1.25 |
| **Max discharge pressure (bar)** | 1000+ | 25–40 | 200+ | 30+ |
| **Flow capacity (MMSCFD)** | 0.1–50 | 0.1–20 | 5–500+ | 50–1000+ |
| **Isentropic efficiency (%)** | 70–90 | 70–85 | 75–88 | 85–92 |
| **Rotational speed (RPM)** | 300–1800 | 1500–6000 | 3000–18000 | 3000–15000 |
| **Pulsation** | Yes (severe) | Low | None | None |
| **Gas fouling tolerance** | Good | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Capital cost (high flow)** | High | High | Moderate | High |
| **Best suited for** | High pressure, low flow, intermittent | Mid-pressure, dirty gas | High flow, continuous duty | Very high flow, axial direction |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "RAPID" for Compressor Selection:**
> - **R**eliability needs → reciprocating
> - **A**bility to handle dirty/sour gas → liquid ring or screw
> - **P**ipeline mainline transmission → centrifugal
> - **I**ntegrated with gas turbine / jet engine → axial
> - **D**eep-well gas-lift high pressure → reciprocating (multi-stage)

### 2.3 Theoretical Thermodynamic Analysis of Compression

#### 2.3.1 Polytropic Process

Real gas compression is neither perfectly isothermal nor perfectly isentropic. It follows a **polytropic process** defined by:

$$P V^n = \text{constant}$$

where $n$ is the **polytropic exponent** ($1 < n < \gamma$ for compression; $n = 1$ = isothermal; $n = \gamma$ = isentropic).

#### 2.3.2 Work of Compression (Reversible, Polytropic)

For an ideal gas undergoing a polytropic process, the **specific work of compression** is:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $W$ = specific work (J/kg)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for the gas (J/kg·K)
- $T_1$ = inlet (suction) temperature (K)
- $P_1, P_2$ = suction and discharge pressures (absolute)
- $n$ = polytropic exponent

#### 2.3.3 Real-Gas Correction Using Compressibility Factor (Z)

For real natural gas, the equation of state uses the **compressibility factor Z**:

$$PV = Z n_u RT$$

The polytropic head (work per unit mass) becomes:

$$H_p = \frac{n}{n-1} Z R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \cdot \frac{1}{g}$$

where $g$ converts J/kg to metres of head (m of gas column).

#### 2.3.4 Polytropic Exponent in Terms of Specific Heats

The relationship between $n$, $\gamma$, and the polytropic efficiency $\eta_p$ is:

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \eta_p \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$$

or equivalently:

$$n = \frac{1}{1 - \eta_p \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

For typical natural gas ($\gamma \approx 1.31$) and $\eta_p \approx 0.78$, the value of $n$ lies between **1.20 and 1.31**.

#### 2.3.5 Isentropic (Ideal) Work

For an ideal isentropic compression of an ideal gas:

$$W_s = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

#### 2.3.6 Isentropic Efficiency

$$\eta_s = \frac{W_s}{W_{\text{actual}}}$$

#### 2.3.7 Polytropic Efficiency

$$\eta_p = \frac{W_{\text{polytropic, ideal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$$

#### 2.3.8 Volumetric Efficiency (Reciprocating Compressors)

$$\eta_v = V_{\text{actual}} / V_{\text{swept}} = 1 - C \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

where $C$ is the **clearance ratio** (typically 1%–10%).

#### 2.3.9 Brake Horsepower (BHP)

For mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s):

$$\text{BHP} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{mech}}}$$

For volumetric flow at inlet, $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{Z R T_1}$.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_c = P_2 / P_1$ | Compression ratio | dimensionless | Single-stage limit check |
| 2 | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | Polytropic process | — | Real compression |
| 3 | $W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | Specific compression work | J/kg | Reciprocating & rotary |
| 4 | $W_s = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic work | J/kg | Ideal reference |
| 5 | $\eta_s = W_s / W_{\text{actual}}$ | Isentropic efficiency | dimensionless | Performance eval. |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{1}{1 - \eta_p \cdot \dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Polytropic exponent | dimensionless | Intermediate calcs |
| 7 | $H_p = \dfrac{n}{n-1} Z R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{g}$ | Polytropic head | m | Centrifugal selection |
| 8 | $\eta_v = 1 - C \left[ (R_c)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | Reciprocating design |
| 9 | $V_d = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{act}}}{\eta_v \cdot N \cdot k}$ | Required displacement | m³/s | Reciprocating sizing |
| 10 | $\text{BHP} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, W_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{mech}}}$ | Brake horsepower | W (÷746 → hp) | Driver selection |
| 11 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (R_c)^{(n-1)/n}$ | Discharge temperature | K | Cooling duty |
| 12 | $N_s = N \cdot \sqrt{Q} / (H_p)^{3/4}$ | Specific speed | dimensionless | Centrifugal selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:**
> $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate, $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate, $C$ = clearance ratio, $N$ = RPM, $k$ = number of cylinders / action, $Z$ = compressibility factor, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in the Real World:**
> This formula set is used daily by **process engineers at GAIL, ONGC, Saudi Aramco, Shell, BP, ExxonMobil, and Petronas** to: (i) size new compressor stations, (ii) evaluate field performance, (iii) decide on driver selection (electric motor vs. gas turbine vs. engine), and (iv) design inter-stage coolers and scrubbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Work of Polytropic Compression (Closed-System)

We derive the specific work required to compress an ideal gas from state $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ to $(P_2, V_2, T_2)$ following a polytropic path $PV^n = \text{const} = K$.

**Step 1 — Start from the First Law for a closed system (per unit mass):**

$$W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $P$ from the polytropic relation** $P = K V^{-n}$ where $K = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} K V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$W = -K \cdot \frac{V^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \bigg|_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{K}{n-1} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Use $K = P_1 V_1^n$ so that $K V_2^{1-n} = P_1 V_1^n V_2^{1-n} = P_1 \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^n \cdot V_2 = P_2 V_2$:**

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{n - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Use the ideal-gas law** $P_1 V_1 = R T_1$ and $P_2 V_2 = R T_2$ (per unit mass):

$$W = \frac{R(T_2 - T_1)}{n - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate $T_2$ using the polytropic temperature-pressure relation** $T_2 = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$:

$$\boxed{\,W = \frac{n}{n-1} \, R \, T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]\,}$$

> This is the **fundamental compressor-work equation**. It is the formula most often tested in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Polytropic Exponent from Polytropic Efficiency

The polytropic efficiency is defined as the ratio of the **polytropic (ideal) compression work** to the **actual compression work**, and is given by the relationship:

$$\eta_p = \frac{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}{\frac{n - 1}{n}}$$

**Rearranging for $n$:**

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}$$

**Step:** Take reciprocal and rearrange:

$$1 - \frac{1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}$$

**Step:** Solve for $n$:

$$\frac{1}{n} = 1 - \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p} = \frac{\gamma \cdot \eta_p - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}$$

$$\boxed{\,n = \frac{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p - (\gamma - 1)}\,}$$

**Numerical example:** For methane ($\gamma = 1.31$) and $\eta_p = 0.78$:

$$n = \frac{1.31 \times 0.78}{1.31 \times 0.78 - 0.31} = \frac{1.0218}{0.7118} \approx 1.436$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** The value of $n$ must always lie between **1** (isothermal) and **$\gamma$** (isentropic). If your calculated $n > \gamma$ or $n < 1$, the value of $\eta_p$ used is **physically impossible** — re-check inputs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Polytropic Head for Centrifugal Compressor

For a centrifugal compressor, it is conventional to express the work as a **head** (in metres of gas), $H_p$:

**Step 1 — Begin with the specific work** (J/kg) from Derivation 1:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the real-gas correction** $Z$ and the molecular weight $M$ (to convert $\bar{R} = 8.314$ J/mol·K to $R = \bar{R}/M$):

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{Z \bar{R}}{M} \cdot T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Convert J/kg to metre-head** by dividing by $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$\boxed{\,H_p = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{Z \bar{R} T_1}{M g} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]\,}$$

**Step 4 — Compute horsepower** for inlet volumetric flow $\dot{V}_1$ (m³/s) at suction conditions using inlet density $\rho_1 = P_1 M / (Z \bar{R} T_1)$:

$$\text{Power} = \dot{m} \cdot W = \rho_1 \dot{V}_1 \cdot W = \frac{P_1 M}{Z \bar{R} T_1} \cdot \dot{V}_1 \cdot W$$

Substituting $W$ from above, the **shaft power** in watts is:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 \dot{V}_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_p}$$

> The factor $\eta_p$ converts polytropic-ideal head to actual head required.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor

The swept volume per cycle per cylinder is $V_s$. A fraction $C$ of $V_s$ is left as **clearance volume** at the end of discharge. During the **expansion stroke** (return of piston), the trapped gas re-expands from $P_2$ to $P_1$ following approximately a polytropic path $PV^{n_v} = \text{const}$ (with $n_v \approx \gamma$).

**Step 1 — Clearance volume** $V_c = C \cdot V_s$.

**Step 2 — Volume at which suction valves open** (i.e. when expanded gas reaches $P_1$):

$$V_4 = V_c \cdot \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n_v}$$

**Step 3 — Actual gas intake per cycle**:

$$V_{\text{actual}} = V_s - (V_4 - V_c) = V_s - V_c \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n_v} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency**:

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_s} = 1 - C \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n_v} - 1 \right]$$

> This is the **key formula** for sizing a reciprocating compressor. Note that $\eta_v$ decreases as $R_c$ increases or as clearance $C$ increases.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A natural gas (assume $\gamma = 1.30$, $M = 18$ g/mol = 0.018 kg/mol) is to be compressed from **$P_1 = 1.05$ bar** and **$T_1 = 305$ K** to **$P_2 = 8.5$ bar**. Polytropic efficiency $\eta_p = 0.78$, $Z = 0.92$, flow rate = **0.50 kg/s**. Mechanical efficiency = 95%. Determine: (i) polytropic exponent, (ii) discharge temperature, (iii) specific work, (iv) brake power.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) Polytropic exponent:**

$$n = \frac{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p - (\gamma - 1)} = \frac{1.30 \times 0.78}{1.30 \times 0.78 - 0.30} = \frac{1.014}{0.714} = 1.420$$

**Step (ii) Discharge temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 305 \times \left( \frac{8.5}{1.05} \right)^{\frac{0.420}{1.420}}$$

$$\frac{0.420}{1.420} = 0.2958, \quad \frac{8.5}{1.05} = 8.095$$

$$(8.095)^{0.2958} = e^{0.2958 \ln 8.095} = e^{0.2958 \times 2.0916} = e^{0.6187} = 1.857$$

$$T_2 = 305 \times 1.857 = 566.4 \text{ K} \approx 293 °C$$

**Step (iii) Specific work (J/kg):**

Specific gas constant: $R = \bar{R}/M = 8.314/0.018 = 461.9$ J/(kg·K).

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

$$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1.420}{0.420} = 3.381$$

$$W = 3.381 \times 461.9 \times 305 \times (1.857 - 1) = 3.381 \times 461.9 \times 305 \times 0.857$$

$$W = 3.381 \times 140{,}879.5 \times 0.857 \approx 3.381 \times 120{,}733.6 = 408{,}201 \text{ J/kg} \approx 408.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step (iv) Brake power (incorporating $\eta_p$ and $\eta_{\text{mech}}$):**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot W}{\eta_p \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}} = \frac{0.50 \times 408.2}{0.78 \times 0.95} = \frac{204.1}{0.741} \approx 275.4 \text{ kW}$$

Converting to horsepower: $275.4 / 0.7457 = 369.4$ hp.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show units at every step. Examiners in KTU deduct marks for missing units in intermediate steps even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

| Step | Reasoning | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Start with $dW = -P \, dV$ | First Law for quasi-static process |
| 2 | Substitute $P = K V^{-n}$ | Polytropic constraint |
| 3 | Integrate to get $W = (P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(n-1)$ | Definite integration |
| 4 | Replace $PV$ with $RT$ | Ideal gas law |
| 5 | Replace $T_2$ in terms of $P_2/P_1$ | Polytropic $T$–$P$ relation |
| 6 | Final form in terms of $(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ | Ready for compressor design |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Dynamic Rotodynamic]:::branch
    B --> D[Reciprocating Piston]:::leaf

    B --> F[Rotary Vane]:::leaf
    B --> G[Rotary Lobe Roots]:::leaf
    B --> H[Liquid Ring]:::leaf
    C --> I[Centrifugal Radial]:::leaf
    C --> J[Axial Flow]:::leaf
    I --> K[Single Stage Casing]:::leaf
    I --> L[Multi Stage Casing]:::leaf
    L --> M[2 to 10 Impellers in Series]:::leaf
    J --> N[10 to 20 Stage Rotor Stator]:::leaf
    D --> O[Horizontal Vertical Angle]:::leaf
    E --> P[Oil Flooded Dry Screw]:::leaf
    classDef root fill:#0B3D91,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#1F77B4,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000,stroke:#555555,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Multi-Stage Compressor Station with Inter-stage Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INLET[Suction Scrubber and KO Drum]:::unit --> S1[Stage 1 Cylinder or Impeller]:::unit
    S1 --> IC1[Inter Cooler 1]:::unit
    IC1 --> ICK1[Inter-stage KO Drum 1]:::unit
    ICK1 --> S2[Stage 2 Cylinder or Impeller]:::unit
    S2 --> IC2[Inter Cooler 2]:::unit
    IC2 --> ICK2[Inter-stage KO Drum 2]:::unit
    ICK2 --> S3[Stage 3 Cylinder or Impeller]:::unit
    S3 --> DCH[Discharge Cooler and Scrubber]:::unit
    DCH --> OUT[To Pipeline or Process]:::unit
    DRV[Driver Motor Gas Turbine Engine]:::power --> S1
    DRV --> S2
    DRV --> S3
    CTL[Station Control Panel PLC and ESD]:::ctrl --> DRV
    CTL --> S1
    CTL --> S2
    CTL --> S3
    classDef unit fill:#E0F2FE,color:#000000,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:1px
    classDef power fill:#FED7AA,color:#000000,stroke:#C2410C,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ctrl fill:#D9F99D,color:#000000,stroke:#4D7C0F,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** Each compression stage raises the gas pressure incrementally. The inter-stage coolers reduce the gas temperature back to near-ambient before the next stage — this **drastically reduces the specific work** and brings the process closer to isothermal (most efficient). Inter-stage K.O. (knock-out) drums remove condensed liquids (water, hydrocarbons) to prevent damage to the next stage.

## 4.3 Reciprocating Compressor Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PISTON[Piston]:::piston --> CYL[Cylinder Liner]:::cyl
    SVALV[Suction Valve]:::valve --> CYL
    CYL --> DVALV[Discharge Valve]:::valve
    CRANK[Crankshaft]:::rot --> CONN[Connecting Rod]:::rot
    CONN --> PISTON
    COOL[Cooling Water Jacket]:::aux --- CYL
    LUB[Lubrication System]:::aux --- CRANK
    GASIN[Suction Gas In]:::io --> SVALV
    DVALV --> GASOUT[Discharge Gas Out]:::io

    classDef valve fill:#D1FAE5,color:#000000,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:1px

    classDef io fill:#F3F4F6,color:#000000,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Centrifugal Compressor — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Gas Axial]:::io --> EYE[Impeller Eye Hub]:::imp
    EYE --> IMP[Rotating Impeller Blades]:::imp
    IMP --> DIFF[Stationary Diffuser Vanes]:::diff
    DIFF --> VOL[Volute Casing Scroll]:::diff
    VOL --> RTN[Return Channel for Multi Stage]:::diff
    RTN --> NXT[Next Stage Eye]:::imp
    NXT --> OUT[Discharge Nozzle]:::io
    SHAFT[Drive Shaft from Driver]:::rot --- IMP
    classDef io fill:#F3F4F6,color:#000000,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1px
    classDef imp fill:#FED7AA,color:#000000,stroke:#C2410C,stroke-width:1px
    classDef diff fill:#E0F2FE,color:#000000,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rot fill:#DBEAFE,color:#000000,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Conversion Sequence in a Centrifugal Stage:**
> 1. **Kinetic energy gain** — impeller accelerates the gas (velocity rises).
> 2. **Pressure recovery** — diffuser slows the gas (velocity falls, pressure rises, by Bernoulli).
> 3. **Collection** — volute collects the flow and directs it to the next stage or out the discharge nozzle.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a centrifugal compressor used in natural gas service. State any two applications."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **centrifugal compressor** consists of a **rotating impeller** mounted on a shaft driven by a motor or gas turbine, a **stationary diffuser**, and a **volute casing** for collecting the discharge.

**Working principle (2 marks):**
1. Gas enters the impeller axially at the **eye (suction)**.
2. The impeller rotates at high speed (3000–18000 RPM), **imparting kinetic energy** to the gas; gas moves radially outward by centrifugal force, leaving the impeller periphery at high velocity.
3. The gas then enters the **stationary diffuser** where kinetic energy is converted into **pressure energy** (velocity falls, pressure rises — Bernoulli's principle).
4. From the diffuser, the gas flows into the **volute** and exits through the discharge nozzle at elevated pressure.

**Two applications (1 mark):**
- Mainline natural gas transmission pipeline boosting stations.
- Gas processing plants (e.g. NGL recovery, LNG feed-gas compression).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'volumetric efficiency' of a reciprocating compressor and state the factors affecting it."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) is the **ratio of the actual volume of gas drawn in per cycle** (at suction conditions) to the **swept volume of the piston**, i.e.

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{swept}}}$$

It is a measure of how effectively the cylinder volume is utilized for gas intake.

**Factors affecting $\eta_v$ (1.5 marks):**
1. **Clearance volume** ($C$) — higher clearance reduces $\eta_v$ because more gas remains trapped and re-expands.
2. **Compression ratio** ($P_2/P_1$) — higher ratios decrease $\eta_v$ sharply.
3. **Suction temperature and pressure** — warmer or lower-pressure suction reduces the mass drawn in.
4. **Polytropic / expansion exponent** ($n_v$) — influences the re-expansion volume.
5. **Leakage past piston rings and valves** — reduces actual volume intake.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** Classify the various types of compressors used in the natural gas industry. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **reciprocating compressor**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses natural gas from **$P_1 = 1.02$ bar** and **$T_1 = 300$ K** to **$P_2 = 9.18$ bar** ($\gamma = 1.30$, $R = 0.518$ kJ/kg·K). Assuming **optimum inter-stage pressure** and **polytropic compression with $n = 1.36$**, calculate: (i) optimum inter-stage pressure, (ii) total specific work, (iii) total work if compression were single-stage. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Family | Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement** | Reciprocating (piston) | Traps fixed mass, high pressure ratio per stage |
| | Rotary — Screw | Continuous flow, two intermeshing rotors |
| | Rotary — Vane | Eccentric rotor with sliding vanes |
| | Rotary — Lobe (Roots) | No internal compression, low pressure |
| | Liquid Ring | Liquid seal, handles dirty/sour gas |
| **Dynamic** | Centrifugal (radial) | Continuous flow, multi-stage for high pressure |
| | Axial | High flow, multi-stage, gas turbine-like |

**Construction and Working of Reciprocating Compressor (4 marks):**

Major components: **cylinder, piston, suction valve, discharge valve, connecting rod, crankshaft, flywheel, cooling water jacket, lubricating system.**

**Working cycle (4-stroke or 2-stroke):**
1. **Suction stroke** — piston moves outward, suction valve opens, gas at $P_1$ fills the cylinder.
2. **Compression stroke** — piston moves inward with both valves closed; gas pressure rises from $P_1$ to $P_2$.
3. **Discharge stroke** — discharge valve opens when cylinder pressure reaches $P_2$, gas is pushed out.
4. **Re-expansion stroke** — trapped gas in clearance volume re-expands as piston returns; suction valve opens only when pressure falls back to $P_1$.

*Sketch:* a labelled vertical cross-section showing cylinder, piston, valves, and crankshaft (as shown in Section 4.3).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $P_1 = 1.02$ bar, $P_2 = 9.18$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $R = 0.518$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.30$, $n = 1.36$.

**(i) Optimum inter-stage pressure (2 marks):**

For **minimum total work** in multi-stage compression, the optimum intermediate pressure $P_x$ is the **geometric mean** of $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1.02 \times 9.18} = \sqrt{9.3636} = 3.060 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Total specific work for two stages (3 marks):**

Since the optimum is used, **each stage has equal work** and the ratio $P_x/P_1 = P_2/P_x = \sqrt{9} = 3.0$.

Single-stage specific work formula:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{0.36}{1.36} = 0.2647, \quad R_c = 3.0$$

$$(3.0)^{0.2647} = e^{0.2647 \ln 3.0} = e^{0.2647 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.2908} = 1.3376$$

$$W_{\text{per stage}} = \frac{1.36}{0.36} \times 0.518 \times 300 \times (1.3376 - 1) = 3.778 \times 155.4 \times 0.3376$$

$$W_{\text{per stage}} = 3.778 \times 52.46 = 198.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Total two-stage work:**

$$W_{\text{total, 2-stage}} = 2 \times 198.2 = 396.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Total work if single-stage (2 marks):**

$$R_c = \dfrac{9.18}{1.02} = 9.0, \quad (9.0)^{0.2647} = e^{0.2647 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.5816} = 1.789$$

$$W_{\text{single-stage}} = 3.778 \times 0.518 \times 300 \times (1.789 - 1) = 3.778 \times 155.4 \times 0.789$$

$$W_{\text{single-stage}} = 3.778 \times 122.6 = 463.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Conclusion:** Two-stage compression saves $463.2 - 396.4 = 66.8$ kJ/kg ≈ **14.4% work reduction**. This proves the engineering rationale for staging.

---

### Question B1 (B) [Alternative — Internal Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Differentiate between **positive displacement** and **dynamic** compressors. Compare reciprocating and centrifugal compressors on the basis of **flow capacity, pressure ratio per stage, efficiency, and gas-handling capability**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A centrifugal compressor handles **150 MMSCFD** of natural gas ($\gamma = 1.30$, $M = 20$ g/mol, $Z = 0.90$) at suction conditions **$P_1 = 35$ bar, $T_1 = 310$ K**, with discharge pressure **$P_2 = 75$ bar** and **polytropic efficiency $\eta_p = 0.80$**. Calculate: (i) polytropic exponent, (ii) polytropic head, (iii) shaft power required. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Difference between PD and Dynamic compressors (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Positive Displacement | Dynamic (Rotodynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Traps fixed mass and reduces its volume | Imparts kinetic energy, then converts to pressure |
| Flow vs. pressure | Flow nearly independent of discharge pressure | Flow and pressure are interdependent |
| Compression ratio per stage | High (4–10) | Low (1.2–1.8) |
| Surge / pulsation | Severe pulsation in reciprocating | No pulsation, but prone to surge |
| Typical use | High pressure, low flow | High flow, moderate pressure |

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Reciprocating | Centrifugal |
|---|---|---|
| Flow capacity | 0.1–50 MMSCFD | 5–500+ MMSCFD |
| Pressure ratio per stage | 4–10 | 1.2–1.8 |
| Isentropic efficiency | 70–90% | 75–88% |
| Gas fouling tolerance | Excellent (handles dirty, sour, wet gas) | Poor (requires clean, dry gas) |
| Pulsation | Yes (requires pulsation bottles) | None |
| Maintenance | High (valves, rings, bearings) | Lower |
| Driver | Electric motor, gas engine | Steam/gas turbine, large motor |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $\dot{V} = 150$ MMSCFD, $\gamma = 1.30$, $M = 0.020$ kg/mol, $Z = 0.90$, $P_1 = 35$ bar = $3.5 \times 10^6$ Pa, $T_1 = 310$ K, $P_2 = 75$ bar, $\eta_p = 0.80$.

**Standard-condition conversion:** 1 MMSCFD = 1,000,000 scf/day. At standard $T_s = 288.7$ K and $P_s = 1.01325$ bar:

$$\dot{V}_s = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ scf/day} \times 0.02832 \text{ m}^3/\text{scf} = 4.248 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 49.17 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(i) Polytropic exponent (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{\gamma \cdot \eta_p}{\gamma \cdot \eta_p - (\gamma - 1)} = \frac{1.30 \times 0.80}{1.30 \times 0.80 - 0.30} = \frac{1.04}{0.74} = 1.4054$$

**(ii) Polytropic head (2 marks):**

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{0.4054}{1.4054} = 0.2885, \quad R_c = \frac{75}{35} = 2.1429$$

$$(2.1429)^{0.2885} = e^{0.2885 \ln 2.1429} = e^{0.2885 \times 0.7621} = e^{0.2198} = 1.2458$$

$$H_p = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{Z \bar{R} T_1}{M g} \cdot (R_c)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \text{ [evaluated]}$$

$$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1.4054}{0.4054} = 3.4667$$

$$H_p = 3.4667 \times \frac{0.90 \times 8.314 \times 310}{0.020 \times 9.81} \times (1.2458 - 1)$$

$$H_p = 3.4667 \times \frac{2319.5}{0.1962} \times 0.2458 = 3.4667 \times 11{,}824 \times 0.2458$$

$$H_p = 3.4667 \times 2906.3 = 10{,}072.5 \text{ m} \approx 10.07 \text{ km of gas column}$$

**(iii) Shaft power (3 marks):**

Inlet density:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1 M}{Z \bar{R} T_1} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.020}{0.90 \times 8.314 \times 310} = \frac{70{,}000}{2319.6} = 30.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 \cdot \dot{V}_1 = 30.18 \times 49.17 = 1483.8 \text{ kg/s}$$

Specific work:

$$W = g H_p = 9.81 \times 10072.5 = 98{,}811 \text{ J/kg} = 98.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Polytropic ideal head gives polytropic ideal work. Actual work:

$$W_{\text{act}} = W / \eta_p = 98.81 / 0.80 = 123.51 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Shaft power:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{\text{act}} = 1483.8 \times 123.51 = 183{,}265 \text{ kW} \approx 183.3 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $Z$ and $M$** in the head formula — full marks lost. Always show them explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing $P_1, P_2$ units** (using bar instead of Pa, or vice versa) — *inconsistency in units is the #1 reason for zero marks on numerical problems.*
> 3. **Using isentropic exponent $\gamma$ instead of polytropic exponent $n$** — when polytropic data is given, the polytropic exponent **must** be used. The relationship $n = \gamma \eta_p / [\gamma \eta_p - (\gamma-1)]$ is the most-skipped derivation in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Not using geometric-mean for optimum inter-stage pressure** — students often assume equal pressure ratio per stage without proving it; *show the derivation for full marks*.
> 5. **Drawing reciprocating compressor sketch without valves and piston rings** — board examiners specifically look for suction valve, discharge valve, cylinder, and piston (each worth 0.5 marks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 High-Yield Rapid-Fire Points:**

- **Compressor** is a device that increases gas pressure by **reducing its specific volume through external work**.
- **Two master classes**: **Positive Displacement (PD)** and **Dynamic (Rotodynamic)**.
- **PD subtypes**: Reciprocating, Screw, Vane, Lobe (Roots), Liquid Ring.
- **Dynamic subtypes**: **Centrifugal (radial)** and **Axial flow**.
- **Reciprocating compressor** = high pressure ratio per stage, high efficiency, handles dirty gas, but pulsates.
- **Centrifugal compressor** = continuous flow, low pressure ratio per stage, requires clean dry gas, but ideal for high-volume pipeline duty.
- **Axial compressor** = highest efficiency and flow capacity, used in LNG and large process plants.
- **Compression ratio** $R_c = P_2/P_1$. Reciprocating: $R_c \leq 10$ per stage. Centrifugal: $R_c \leq 1.8$ per stage.
- **Polytropic process** $PV^n = \text{const}$ with $1 < n < \gamma$.
- **Specific work** $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ — *the formula most tested in KTU*.
- **Isentropic work** $W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ (R_c)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$.
- **Isentropic efficiency** $\eta_s = W_s / W_{\text{actual}}$.
- **Polytropic efficiency** $\eta_p$ is *not* the same as $\eta_s$ — the relationship is $\eta_p \approx \eta_s$ for low $R_c$, but **$\eta_p > \eta_s$ for high $R_c$**.
- **Polytropic exponent** $n = \dfrac{\gamma \eta_p}{\gamma \eta_p - (\gamma - 1)}$.
- **Optimum inter-stage pressure** for minimum work in N stages: $P_x = (P_1 \cdot P_2^N)^{1/(N+1)}$. For 2 stages, $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.
- **Volumetric efficiency** of reciprocating compressors: $\eta_v = 1 - C[(P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma} - 1]$.
- **Discharge temperature** $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ — must be limited (typically < 200 °C) to avoid lubricant breakdown.
- **Multi-stage with inter-cooling** is always more efficient than single-stage for the same overall pressure ratio.
- **API 617** governs centrifugal/axial; **API 618** governs reciprocating compressors.
- **BHP** (Brake Horsepower) = $\dot{m} \cdot W_{\text{actual}} / \eta_{\text{mech}}$.
- **Polytropic head** $H_p$ (in metres) is the standard sizing parameter for centrifugal compressors.
- **Surge** is a low-flow instability in centrifugal compressors — must be avoided by ensuring operation to the right of the surge line.
- **Driver selection**: electric motor (clean grid), gas engine (remote), gas turbine (high power), steam turbine (waste heat available).
- **Compressibility factor $Z$** for natural gas at typical pipeline conditions ≈ 0.85–0.95 (use Standing-Katz or Hall-Yarborough chart for accuracy).
- **Screw compressors** are often used in **gas-lift** and **CNG stations** because of compactness and oil-flooded sealing.
- **Liquid-ring compressors** are used for **sour gas (H₂S)** and **flammable gases** because the seal liquid prevents leakage and is non-sparking.
- **Lobe (Roots) blowers** are *not* true compressors — they provide volume transfer with minimal compression, used in aeration and vacuum duty.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Memorize the four boxed formulas (Specific work, Polytropic exponent, Volumetric efficiency, Optimum inter-stage pressure) — they cover ~70% of marks.
> 2. Always show units; *missing unit = zero credit for that step*.
> 3. For diagrams, label **every** component: cylinder, piston, valves, crankshaft, connecting rod, cooling jacket.
> 4. For multi-stage problems, compute optimum $P_x$ first, then verify that $P_x/P_1 = (P_2/P_x)$ (i.e. equal stage ratios).
> 5. For centrifugal problems, use the head formula (in metres) — *do not* mix it up with the J/kg formula.
> 6. End with a short concluding sentence stating **practical significance** — board examiners award **1 bonus mark** for application awareness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Reciprocating and Centrifugal Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Reciprocating and Centrifugal Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Compressor Selection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection of a Compressor** is the systematic engineering process of determining the most appropriate type (reciprocating, centrifugal, axial, screw, etc.) and design configuration (number of stages, driver type, cooling arrangement, material of construction) for a given service in the **oil and natural gas industry**. The selection is governed by the **required suction flow rate (Q)**, **discharge pressure (P₂)**, **suction conditions**, **gas properties (MW, k, Z)**, **turndown ratio**, **capital cost**, and **operational reliability**.

**The Universal Selection Problem Statement:**

Given a process duty:
- **Inlet (suction)** pressure $P_1$, temperature $T_1$, volumetric flow $Q_1$ (in **MMSCFD**, **Nm³/hr**, or **ACFM**)
- **Outlet (discharge)** pressure $P_2$, desired temperature $T_2$
- **Gas properties**: Molecular weight $M$, specific heat ratio $k = C_p/C_v$, compressibility factor $Z$

Choose between **Reciprocating** and **Centrifugal** compressors that minimize the **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** while satisfying mechanical, thermodynamic, and process constraints.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Two Ways to Climb a Mountain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of compressing gas as climbing a steep mountain of pressure:**
>
> 🏔️ **Reciprocating Compressor = The Staircase Climber**
> - Takes deliberate, powerful **discrete steps** (piston strokes)
> - Each step (stroke) delivers a precise amount of work
> - **Best for:** steep, narrow, twisting trails (high pressure ratios, low-to-medium flow)
> - Slow but powerful — can climb vertical cliffs (pressure ratios up to **1000:1** in multistage)
>
> 🌊 **Centrifugal Compressor = The Helicopter Pilot**
> - Uses continuous, dynamic **rotational momentum** (impeller vanes fling gas outward)
> - Smooth, continuous delivery of flow
> - **Best for:** wide, open valleys (high flow rates, moderate pressure ratios)
> - Fast and smooth — covers distance quickly (flow up to **500,000 ACFM**), but limited altitude (pressure ratio typically **< 4.5 per casing**)

---

## 1.3 The Oil & Gas Industry Context

In **upstream, midstream, and downstream** operations, compressors are the **workhorses**:

| Application | Typical Service | Preferred Compressor |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Gas lift** in oil wells | Low P ratio, high Q | **Centrifugal** |
| **Gas gathering** | Moderate P, high Q | **Centrifugal** |
| **Gas transmission pipelines** | High Q, moderate ΔP | **Centrifugal / Axial** |
| **Gas reinjection (EOR)** | Very high P (3000–10,000 psi) | **Reciprocating** |
| **Gas processing (NGL recovery)** | Moderate P, varying Q | **Both** |
| **Refinery process air / H₂** | High P, low Q | **Reciprocating** |
| **LNG liquefaction feed** | Very high Q, moderate P | **Centrifugal** (large axial-centrifugal trains) |
| **Vapor recovery / flare gas** | Low Q, varying | **Reciprocating** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Industry Metric:** Compressor discharge pressure is often quoted in **psig** (US oilfield) or **barg** (SI). Conversions: **1 barg = 14.504 psig**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024 Scheme

The **2024 Scheme** explicitly tests the student's ability to:
1. **Compare** thermodynamic and mechanical performance of the two compressor classes.
2. **Justify** a selection decision based on duty, cost, and operability.
3. **Calculate** power, efficiency, and compression ratio.

The topic maps to **CO3** of PECHT522: *"Apply the principles of fluid machinery for selection and operation of compressors in oil and gas processing."* — Bloom Level **Apply / Analyze**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Comparison Framework

The selection between reciprocating and centrifugal compressors is **NOT arbitrary**. It is governed by the following **five primary decision variables**:

### Variable 1: **Pressure Ratio ($R_c$)**

$$R_c = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Reciprocating:** Can handle $R_c$ from **1.1 to 1000+** (multistage), per stage typically **3 to 5**.
> - **Centrifugal:** Limited to **$R_c \leq 4.5$ per casing**, typically **1.1 to 3.5** per impeller.

### Variable 2: **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)**

- **Reciprocating:** Best at low-to-moderate flow: **50 to 30,000 ACFM** (≈ 85 to 50,000 m³/hr).
- **Centrifugal:** Excellent at high flow: **1,000 to 500,000 ACFM**.

### Variable 3: **Gas Molecular Weight (M) and Specific Heat Ratio ($k$)**

- **Light gases** (e.g., H₂, M = 2): Centrifugal becomes inefficient → **Reciprocating preferred**.
- **Heavy hydrocarbon gases** (e.g., propane, M = 44): Centrifugal is efficient.
- **High $k$** (e.g., monoatomic gases, $k = 1.67$): More work per stage → Reciprocating favored.

### Variable 4: **Turndown Ratio (TDR)**

$$TDR = \frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}$$

- **Reciprocating:** TDR of **10:1 or higher** (clearance pockets allow infinite step-wise capacity control).
- **Centrifugal:** TDR of **3:1 to 4:1** (limited by surge line at low flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Surge** is the operational instability in centrifugal compressors where flow reversal damages the impeller. The **surge line** is the lower bound of the operating envelope.

### Variable 5: **Capital + Operating Cost (Life-Cycle Cost)**

For the **same duty**, capital cost of centrifugal is higher at low flow, but its operating cost (maintenance, downtime) is lower at high flow due to:
- Fewer moving parts
- No wearing rings or valves
- Continuous duty capability
- Lower vibration → no special foundation

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **verbatim KTU-syllabus standards**. Memorize units.

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_c = P_2 / P_1$ | Compression ratio (per stage or overall) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_{iso} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression work | kJ, Btu |
| 3 | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | Polytropic compression work (real machine) | kJ, Btu |
| 4 | $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{(n-1)/n}{(k-1)/k}$ | Polytropic efficiency from isentropic data | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot (P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ | Volumetric efficiency of reciprocating compressor (C = clearance fraction) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $H_{ad} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} \cdot Z \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$ | Adiabatic head (centrifugal compressor) | J/kg, ft·lbf/lbm |
| 7 | $Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1}$ | Gas volumetric flow conversion between conditions (Ideal Gas + Z correction) | m³/s, ACFM |
| 8 | $N_s = N \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (dimensionless, for dynamic machine selection) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{brake} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{poly}}{\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{driver}}$ | Brake power required at the coupling | kW, hp |
| 10 | Affinity: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ | Centrifugal compressor speed scaling | — |
| 11 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot Q_1 \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1}$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s, lb/hr |
| 12 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H_{ad}}{\eta_{ad}}$ | Shaft power of centrifugal compressor | W, hp |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal gas constant**: $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** = **1545 ft·lbf/(lbmol·°R)**. Gas-specific $R = R_u / M$.

---

## 2.3 The Decision Flow Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When the question says *"Discuss the criteria for selection"*, the examiner expects this **structured 6-point framework**:

**Step 1 — Quantify the duty:** Determine $Q_1$ (ACFM at suction), $P_1$, $P_2$, and compute $R_c$.

**Step 2 — Check $R_c$:**
- If $R_c \leq 4.5$ → Centrifugal is a candidate.
- If $R_c > 4.5$ per stage → Must use **multistage centrifugal** or **reciprocating**.

**Step 3 — Check Flow Rate Q:**
- If $Q > 5{,}000$ ACFM and $R_c < 4$ → **Centrifugal is almost always selected**.
- If $Q < 1{,}000$ ACFM → **Reciprocating preferred** (small centrifugals are inefficient).

**Step 4 — Check Turndown:**
- If process flow varies widely (e.g., gas-lift well ramping up) → **Reciprocating** (step-less capacity control).
- If flow is essentially constant → **Centrifugal** advantage (no need for clearance pockets).

**Step 5 — Check Gas Properties:**
- If MW < 20 (light gas) or $k > 1.4$ → **Reciprocating** (centrifugal efficiency drops).
- If MW > 20 and $k < 1.3$ → **Centrifugal** excellent.

**Step 6 — Cost & Maintenance:**
- Reciprocating: lower capital at low flow, higher maintenance (valves, piston rings every 8000 hr).
- Centrifugal: higher capital at low flow, lower maintenance, 25,000–40,000 hr MTBF.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Oil & Gas Engineering

In a **typical offshore platform**, a **3-stage centrifugal compressor** driven by a **25 MW gas turbine** may deliver **200 MMSCFD** of gas at **1500 psig** for **gas export** or **reinjection**. The same platform may also have **two reciprocating compressors** rated at **1500 hp** each for **vapor recovery** and **low-pressure gas lift** at **400–800 psig**. Selecting the wrong type can cost **millions of dollars** in capital, plus **millions in lost production** from unscheduled downtime.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Polytropic Compression Work

**Starting point:** The first law of thermodynamics for a steady-flow open system (compressor control volume), per unit mass:

$$W_s = -\int_1^2 V \, dP$$

For a **polytropic process** defined as $P V^n = \text{constant}$, the specific volume is:

$$V = \dfrac{P_1^{1/n} V_1}{P^{1/n}}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the polytropic relation into the work integral:

$$W_s = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{P_1^{1/n} V_1}{P^{1/n}} \, dP$$

**Step 2:** Pull constants out of the integral:

$$W_s = -P_1^{1/n} V_1 \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/n} \, dP$$

**Step 3:** Apply the power rule of integration, $\int P^{-1/n} \, dP = \dfrac{P^{(n-1)/n}}{(n-1)/n}$:

$$W_s = -P_1^{1/n} V_1 \left[ \dfrac{P^{(n-1)/n}}{(n-1)/n} \right]_{P_1}^{P_2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the definite integral:

$$W_s = -\dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1^{1/n} V_1 \left[ P_2^{(n-1)/n} - P_1^{(n-1)/n} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $P_1^{(n-1)/n}$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** disappears because $P_2 > P_1$ makes the bracket positive, and work input to a compressor is **positive by convention** (work done ON the system). For a turbine the sign convention reverses.

**Result (the canonical polytropic work equation):**

$$\boxed{W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]}$$

For an **isentropic** process, simply replace the polytropic exponent $n$ with the specific heat ratio $k$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor

The volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ accounts for the **expansion of trapped clearance gas** during the piston return stroke. The clearance fraction is:

$$C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$$

where $V_c$ = clearance volume, $V_s$ = swept (displacement) volume.

**Step 1:** At end of discharge stroke, the clearance pocket contains gas at $P_2$ in volume $V_c$.

**Step 2:** During the return stroke, this gas expands polytropically (or isentropically) from $P_2$ back to suction pressure $P_1$. The expanded volume is:

$$V_e = V_c \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/k}$$

**Step 3:** The piston must travel a portion of the return stroke just to "suck back" this expanded gas. The **effective intake volume** is therefore:

$$V_{eff} = V_s - V_c + V_c = V_s - V_c \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/k} - 1 \right]$$

Wait — re-examine: the piston moves forward, expelling $V_s$ of gas + $V_c$ of clearance. On return, the $V_c$ of trapped gas expands. The volume of fresh gas drawn in is $V_s - (V_e - V_c)$.

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{eff}}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/k}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the equation **most frequently tested** in KTU for reciprocating compressors. Note that at $P_2 = P_1$ (no compression), $\eta_v = 1$, as physically expected. As $P_2/P_1$ rises, $\eta_v$ drops — motivating **multistage** design with intercooling.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU 3-Mark Style

**Problem:** A single-stage reciprocating compressor handles methane ($k = 1.3$, $M = 16$) at $P_1 = 100$ **kPa**, $T_1 = 300$ **K**, $Q_1 = 5$ **m³/min**. Suction pressure ratio is $R_c = 4$. Clearance fraction $C = 0.05$. Calculate the **polytropic work per cycle** and the **volumetric efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Identify inlet conditions:**
$P_1 = 100$ kPa, $V_1 = 5$ m³/min = $5/60 = 0.0833$ m³/s.

**Step 2 — Volumetric efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \cdot 4^{1/1.3}$$

Compute $4^{1/1.3} = 4^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.7692 \cdot 1.3863} = e^{1.0663} = 2.903$

$$\eta_v = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot 2.903 = 1.05 - 0.1452 = 0.9048$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v \approx 0.905 \;\; (90.5\%)}$$

**Step 3 — Polytropic work (assume $n = 1.32$, close to isentropic for methane):**

$W_{poly} = \dfrac{1.32}{1.32 - 1} \cdot 100 \cdot 0.0833 \cdot \left[ 4^{(1.32-1)/1.32} - 1 \right]$

$W_{poly} = \dfrac{1.32}{0.32} \cdot 8.33 \cdot \left[ 4^{0.2424} - 1 \right]$

$W_{poly} = 4.125 \cdot 8.33 \cdot \left[ e^{0.2424 \cdot 1.3863} - 1 \right]$

$W_{poly} = 4.125 \cdot 8.33 \cdot \left[ e^{0.3360} - 1 \right]$

$W_{poly} = 4.125 \cdot 8.33 \cdot \left[ 1.3994 - 1 \right]$

$W_{poly} = 4.125 \cdot 8.33 \cdot 0.3994$

$$\boxed{W_{poly} \approx 13.72 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most exam-useful table for KTU 2024**. It directly answers *"Compare reciprocating and centrifugal compressors"* in 14-mark Part B questions.

| Parameter | **Reciprocating Compressor** | **Centrifugal Compressor** |
|-----------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Working principle** | Positive displacement (piston-cylinder) | Dynamic (impeller-diffuser) |
| **Pressure ratio per stage** | 3 to 5 (very high overall in multistage) | 1.1 to 4.5 (limited) |
| **Flow rate range** | 50 to 30,000 ACFM | 1,000 to 500,000 ACFM |
| **Turndown ratio** | Excellent: 10:1 or infinite | Poor: 3:1 to 4:1 (surge-limited) |
| **Capacity control** | Step-less: clearance pockets, valve unloading, speed | Inlet guide vanes, variable speed, recycle |
| **Mechanical efficiency** | 85–92% (well-designed) | 75–85% (polytropic), 80–88% (adiabatic) |
| **Volumetric efficiency** | Drops sharply with $R_c$ (use multistage) | N/A (dynamic machine) |
| **Maintenance cost** | High (rings, valves every 6–12 months) | Low (only bearings, seals) |
| **Capital cost (low flow)** | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| **Capital cost (high flow)** | **Higher** (multi-cylinder) | **Lower** (single machine) |
| **Gas molecular weight suitability** | Excellent for light gases (H₂, He) | Excellent for heavy hydrocarbons |
| **Discharge pulsation** | Severe (needs pulsation bottles) | Smooth, continuous |
| **Footprint / Weight** | Bulky, heavy | Compact, light |
| **Lubrication oil contamination** | Possible (use labyrinth if dry) | None (oil-free design) |
| **Surge vulnerability** | None | Yes — critical protection system |
| **Typical applications in O&G** | Gas lift, reinjection, vapor recovery, H₂ make-up | Pipeline, LNG, gas processing, gas turbine inlet air |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Selection Decision Flowchart

The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the algorithmic decision process an engineer follows in practice (and what KTU expects in 14-mark answers on "selection criteria"):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Process Duty Defined: Q1, P1, P2]) --> calcA[Calculate Rc = P2 over P1]
    calcA --> checkA{Rc greater than 4.5?}
    checkA -- No --> flowA{Q greater than 5000 ACFM?}
    checkA -- Yes --> stageA[Use Multistage Centrifugal OR Reciprocating]
    stageA --> recA[Reciprocating preferred if Q less than 1000]
    stageA --> centA[Centrifugal preferred if Q greater than 5000]
    flowA -- Yes --> centB[Select CENTRIFUGAL: Check Surge Margin]
    flowA -- No --> recB{Variable flow / turndown needed?}
    recB -- Yes --> recC[Select RECIPROCATING: Use Clearance Pockets]
    recB -- No --> centC[Check Gas MW: less than 20?]
    centC -- Yes --> recD[Select RECIPROCATING: Light gas]
    centC -- No --> centD[Select CENTRIFUGAL: Economic optimum]
    centB --> costA[Compute Life-Cycle Cost LCC]
    centC --> costA
    recC --> costA
    recD --> costA
    centD --> costA
    costA --> finalA[Final Selection Report]
    style startA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style finalA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```
