# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the polytropic exponent $n$ with the isentropic exponent $k$. They are related by $\eta_{poly} = (n-1)/n \div (k-1)/k$, but $n$ must be **derived from** the efficiency, not assumed equal to $k$.
> 2. **Always state units** in the final answer. Marks are deducted for unit-less numerical answers.
> 3. In selection questions, **never give a one-line answer** like "use reciprocating." Always list **at least 4 criteria** and justify each. KTU 2024 scheme requires Bloom's Level-4 (Analyze) reasoning.
> 4. For volumetric efficiency, the **clearance fraction $C$** is critical. If $C = 0$ (no clearance, theoretical), then $\eta_v = 1$. Forgetting to subtract $C \cdot (P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ is the most common mistake.

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the term "volumetric efficiency" of a reciprocating compressor. How is it affected by the clearance volume and pressure ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor is the ratio of the actual volume of gas sucked in at suction conditions to the swept (displacement) volume of the piston. **(1 mark)**
Mathematically:
$$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/k} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$
where $C = V_c / V_s$ is the clearance fraction. As the pressure ratio $P_2 / P_1$ increases, the expanded clearance gas re-occupies a larger portion of the swept volume, reducing $\eta_v$. Increasing the clearance fraction $C$ also decreases $\eta_v$. This effect motivates **multistaging with intercooling** in high-pressure-ratio applications. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary 2024 Scheme]**
**List any four criteria used for the selection between a reciprocating and a centrifugal compressor in oil and gas operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 0.75 each):**
1. **Pressure Ratio ($R_c$):** Centrifugal limited to $R_c \leq 4.5$ per casing; reciprocating preferred for very high $R_c$.
2. **Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$):** Centrifugal preferred for $Q > 5{,}000$ ACFM; reciprocating for $Q < 1{,}000$ ACFM.
3. **Turndown Requirement:** Reciprocating offers 10:1 turndown via clearance pockets; centrifugal limited to 3:1 to 4:1.
4. **Gas Molecular Weight:** Light gases ($M < 20$) favor reciprocating; heavier hydrocarbons favor centrifugal.
5. **Capital vs. Operating Cost:** Centrifugal has higher capital at low flow but lower operating cost; reciprocating has lower capital at low flow but higher maintenance.
*(Any four of the above earn full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (ESE Module Choice: 14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 Part B gives the student a choice between Q-A and Q-B from the same module. Both are provided below with **complete model solutions**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** *Compare reciprocating and centrifugal compressors on the basis of (i) principle of operation, (ii) pressure ratio capability, (iii) capacity control, and (iv) suitability for oil and gas applications. State clearly which type is preferred for:*
- *(1) A 250 MMSCFD gas reinjection compressor at 5000 psig suction ratio of 3.5.*
- *(2) A vapor-recovery compressor in a tank farm, with highly variable flow.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses 0.3 m³/s of air (measured at inlet) from $P_1 = 1$ bar to $P_2 = 9$ bar. The suction temperature is $T_1 = 300$ K. Assuming isentropic compression with $k = 1.4$ and intercooling to the initial temperature, calculate:*
- *(i) The intermediate (intercooler) pressure for minimum work.*
- *(ii) The total isentropic work per second.*
- *(iii) The heat rejected in the intercooler per second.* **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A

**(a) Comparative Analysis (4 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| Parameter | Reciprocating | Centrifugal |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| (i) Principle | Positive displacement: piston in cylinder | Dynamic: rotating impeller adds kinetic energy, converted to pressure in diffuser |
| (ii) Pressure ratio | 3–5 per stage; >1000 in multistage | 1.1–4.5 per casing (limited by surge) |
| (iii) Capacity control | Clearance pockets, valve unloading, variable speed — wide range | Inlet guide vanes, variable speed, recycle — limited by surge line |
| (iv) O&G suitability | High-pressure reinjection, vapor recovery, gas lift, light gases | Pipeline transmission, LNG feed, gas processing, gas turbine air |

**Application Selections (3 marks):**

- **(1) 250 MMSCFD, $R_c$ = 3.5, $P_2$ = 5000 psig:**
  Since the flow is **very high (250 MMSCFD ≈ 175,000 ACFM at standard)** and the per-casing pressure ratio is **only 3.5** (well within centrifugal range), a **centrifugal compressor** is preferred. At such high flow, even one or two casings deliver the duty; multiple reciprocating cylinders would be impractical. **(1.5 marks)**
- **(2) Vapor recovery with variable flow:**
  Vapor recovery from storage tanks has **highly variable suction flow** (intermittent, slug flow, low rates during standby). A **reciprocating compressor** is preferred because of its **infinite turndown** via clearance pockets and step-less capacity control. Centrifugal would risk **surge** at low flow. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Intermediate pressure for minimum work (3 marks):**

For a two-stage reciprocating compressor with intercooling back to $T_1$, the intermediate pressure $P_x$ for **minimum total work** is the **geometric mean** of $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1 \cdot 9} = 3 \;\; \text{bar} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Verification: each stage then has the same pressure ratio $R_c = 3$, which is the **equal-work-sharing** optimum. **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Total isentropic work per second (2 marks):**

Per-stage isentropic work (same for both stages due to optimum $P_x$):

$$W_{stage} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{stage} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} \cdot (1 \times 10^5) \cdot 0.3 \cdot \left[ 3^{0.4/1.4} - 1 \right] \;\text{W}$$

$$W_{stage} = 3.5 \cdot 3 \times 10^4 \cdot \left[ 3^{0.2857} - 1 \right]$$

$$3^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 3} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.0986} = e^{0.3139} = 1.3687$$

$$W_{stage} = 3.5 \cdot 30000 \cdot 0.3687 = 38{,}715 \;\text{W} \approx 38.7 \;\text{kW}$$

Total work for two stages:

$$W_{total} = 2 \times 38.7 = 77.4 \;\text{kW} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected in the intercooler (2 marks):**

The intercooler removes the heat gained by the gas during stage-1 compression. The gas enters the intercooler at $T_{1,discharge}$ and leaves at $T_1 = 300$ K.

Stage-1 discharge temperature (isentropic):

$$T_{1d} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} = 300 \cdot 1.3687 = 410.6 \;\text{K}$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{10^5 \cdot 0.3}{287 \cdot 300} = 0.348 \;\text{kg/s}$$

Heat rejected:

$$Q_{intercooler} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{1d} - T_1) = 0.348 \cdot 1005 \cdot (410.6 - 300)$$

$$Q_{intercooler} = 0.348 \cdot 1005 \cdot 110.6 \approx 38{,}690 \;\text{W} \approx 38.7 \;\text{kW} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The heat rejected in the intercooler is exactly equal to the work input of stage 1. This is **not a coincidence** — for an ideal gas under isentropic compression with $C_p = kR/(k-1)$, this identity always holds for the optimum $P_x$.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** *A single-stage centrifugal compressor handles natural gas with the following data:*
- *Inlet conditions: $P_1 = 2$ bar, $T_1 = 310$ K, $Q_1 = 5000$ m³/hr, $M = 20$, $k = 1.3$, $Z = 0.95$*
- *Discharge pressure: $P_2 = 6$ bar*
- *Polytropic efficiency: $\eta_{poly} = 0.78$*

*Calculate:*
- *(i) The mass flow rate.*
- *(ii) The polytropic exponent $n$ from efficiency.*
- *(iii) The polytropic head developed.*
- *(iv) The shaft power required (assuming 96% mechanical efficiency).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the role of clearance pockets, intercooling, and multistaging in the design of a reciprocating compressor for a high-pressure gas reinjection service. Show, with the aid of the volumetric efficiency equation, why a single-stage reciprocator is unsuitable when $P_2 / P_1 > 8$.* **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mass flow rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot Q_1 \cdot M}{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T_1}$$

$Q_1 = 5000 / 3600 = 1.389$ m³/s.

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^5) \cdot 1.389 \cdot 20}{0.95 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 310}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{5.556 \times 10^6}{2448.6} \approx 2269 \;\text{kg/hr} \approx 0.630 \;\text{kg/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Polytropic exponent (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{(n-1)/n}{(k-1)/k} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{n-1}{n} = \eta_{poly} \cdot \dfrac{k-1}{k}$$

$$\dfrac{n-1}{n} = 0.78 \cdot \dfrac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.78 \cdot 0.2308 = 0.18$$

$$\dfrac{1}{n} = 1 - 0.18 = 0.82 \;\Rightarrow\; n = 1 / 0.82 = 1.2195$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 1.22} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Polytropic head developed (2 marks):**

$$H_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot Z \cdot \frac{R_u}{M} \cdot T_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

$R_{specific} = R_u / M = 8.314 / 20 = 0.4157$ kJ/(kg·K).

$H_{poly} = \dfrac{1.22}{0.22} \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.4157 \cdot 310 \cdot \left[ 3^{0.18} - 1 \right]$

$H_{poly} = 5.545 \cdot 0.395 \cdot 310 \cdot \left[ e^{0.18 \cdot 1.0986} - 1 \right]$

$H_{poly} = 5.545 \cdot 122.7 \cdot \left[ e^{0.1977} - 1 \right]$

$H_{poly} = 5.545 \cdot 122.7 \cdot \left[ 1.2186 - 1 \right]$

$H_{poly} = 5.545 \cdot 122.7 \cdot 0.2186$

$$\boxed{H_{poly} \approx 148.7 \;\text{kJ/kg}} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**(iv) Shaft power (2 marks):**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot H_{poly}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{0.630 \cdot 148.7}{0.96}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 97.6 \;\text{kW}} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

---

**(b) Design of Reciprocating Compressor for High-Pressure Reinjection (7 marks):**

**Clearance Pockets (1.5 marks):**
Clearance pockets are external volumes connected to the cylinder head, sized to artificially increase $V_c$. They **reduce volumetric efficiency** in a controlled manner. In high-pressure reinjection, however, they are used to **adjust capacity to process turndown** — the pockets hold the trapped gas at $P_2$, and the operator can bypass or engage them to step-change capacity. This is the **primary method of capacity control** in a multi-cylinder reinjection compressor.

**Intercooling (1.5 marks):**
Between stages, the gas is cooled back close to $T_1$. This has three benefits:
1. **Reduces specific volume**, so subsequent stages handle less volumetric flow.
2. **Reduces discharge temperature**, protecting cylinder lubrication and preventing auto-ignition of hydrocarbon gases (a critical safety concern in O&G).
3. **Reduces total compression work** because cooler gas is denser and follows the optimum equal-pressure-ratio path.

**Multistaging (1.5 marks):**
For high $P_2 / P_1$, the work is split across $N$ stages at $R_c = (P_2 / P_1)^{1/N}$ each. This reduces work and brings $T_{discharge}$ into a safe range (< 180 °C typical). For reinjection at 5000 psig from 500 psig ($R_c = 10$), a **3- to 4-stage** configuration is standard.

**Why Single-Stage is Unsuitable when $R_c > 8$ (2.5 marks):**

For typical $C = 0.05$ and $k = 1.3$, the volumetric efficiency at $R_c = 8$:

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \cdot 8^{1/1.3} = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot 8^{0.7692}$$

$$8^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{1.5995} = 4.95$$

$$\eta_v = 1.05 - 0.247 = 0.803 \quad (80.3\%)$$

At $R_c = 20$:

$$\eta_v = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot 20^{0.7692} = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot e^{0.7692 \cdot 2.9957} = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot e^{2.305}$$

$$= 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot 10.025 = 1.05 - 0.501 = 0.549 \quad (54.9\%)$$

At $R_c = 50$:

$$50^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \cdot 3.912} = e^{3.009} = 20.28$$

$$\eta_v = 1.05 - 0.05 \cdot 20.28 = 1.05 - 1.014 = 0.036 \quad (3.6\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond $R_c \approx 40$ for $C = 0.05$, $\eta_v$ becomes **essentially zero** — the piston spends almost the entire return stroke merely re-compressing the trapped clearance gas back to $P_1$ before any fresh gas can enter. This is the **physical limit** of single-stage reciprocating compression. Hence multistaging is **not optional but mandatory** for high-pressure reinjection service.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this and review before the exam.**

- [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** High $Q$ + low $R_c$ → **Centrifugal**. Low $Q$ + high $R_c$ → **Reciprocating**.
- [x] **Per-stage limits:** Reciprocating $R_c \leq 5$; Centrifugal $R_c \leq 4.5$.
- [x] **Turndown:** Reci = 10:1+, Cent = 3:1 (surge-limited).
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency formula:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ — memorize exactly.
- [x] **Polytropic work formula:** $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$.
- [x] **Isentropic** is a special case of **polytropic** with $n = k$.
- [x] **Optimum intercooler pressure** in N-stage: $P_x = (P_1 P_2)^{1/N}$ for $N=2$, generalized via equal $R_c$ per stage.
- [x] **Affinity laws** for centrifugal: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- [x] **Surge** = flow reversal, dangerous; **choke** = max flow limit. Operating envelope is bounded by these.
- [x] **Gas-specific gas constant** $R = R_u / M$ with $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [x] **Specific speed** determines the geometric family of a dynamic machine — high $N_s$ → axial, low $N_s$ → radial centrifugal.
- [x] **For O&G reinjection (5000+ psig):** Always multistage reciprocating OR a centrifugal string (e.g., 2 casings in series).
- [x] **LNG/large pipeline duty:** Centrifugal is essentially the only economic choice.
- [x] **Discharge temperature limit:** ~180 °C for oil-lubricated cylinders; ~200 °C for non-lubricated (labyrinth).
- [x] **Maintenance interval:** Recipro rings/valves ~8000 hr; Centrifugal bearings ~25,000–40,000 hr.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Compressors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Oil and Natural Gas Engineering**, a **compressor** is a mechanical device that increases the pressure of a process gas (natural gas, air, CO₂, or hydrocarbon vapours) by reducing its specific volume. From a thermodynamic standpoint, compression is a **steady-flow work input process** governed by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**, where shaft work is delivered to the gas stream to raise its enthalpy and pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Compressors are steady-flow turbomachines in which the working fluid (gas) receives work from a rotating shaft, resulting in a rise in fluid pressure, temperature, and enthalpy, accompanied by a decrease in specific volume." The **Thermodynamics of Compressors** therefore studies the energy exchange, the polytropic/isentropic relationships, efficiency definitions, and the design trade-offs (work input vs. discharge temperature vs. compression ratio) that govern compressor selection in upstream (gas-lift), midstream (pipeline boosting), and downstream (gas processing, refrigeration) oil and gas operations. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bicycle Pump Mechanism"** > > Imagine pushing down the handle of a **bicycle pump**. As the piston moves down, it squeezes the air inside the cylinder into a smaller volume. You can physically *feel* the resistance and notice the pump barrel gets *warmer* near the nozzle. This is compression in its purest form: > > - **Mechanical work in** (your arm pushing) → **Pressure rise** (air molecules forced closer) → **Temperature rise** (heat generated by molecular collisions). > > An industrial gas compressor in a CNG station or gas-lift manifold does **exactly the same thing**, but on a continuous, controlled basis with thousands of horsepower. The gas pipeline operator's job is to "pump" natural gas from the wellhead (say, **70 bar**) to a processing plant (say, **100 bar**) — this is achieved by **compressor stations** placed every 50–100 km along the pipeline. --- ### 1.3 Classification of Compressors (KTU-Relevant) | Type | Mechanism | Typical Application in O&G | |------|-----------|----------------------------| | **Positive Displacement (Reciprocating)** | Piston-cylinder, batch-wise volume reduction | Gas-lift injection, wellhead boosting, high-pressure (up to **10,000 psi**) | | **Centrifugal (Radial Flow)** | Impeller throws gas outward by centrifugal force | Mainline gas pipelines, gas processing | | **Axial Flow** | Multi-stage blades accelerate gas parallel to shaft | Large-volume gas pipelines, refrigeration | | **Rotary (Screw, Vane)** | Rotating elements trap and compress gas | Vapor recovery, low-pressure gas gathering | --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Values (Use Bold When Quoting):** > - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$ > - **Specific Gas Constant for Methane:** $R_{CH_4} = 0.5182 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ > - **Specific Heat Ratio for Natural Gas:** $k = \gamma = 1.25$ to $1.30$ (typical; **air = 1.4**) > - **Standard Conditions:** $P_{sc} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$ (1 atm), $T_{sc} = 288.15 \; \text{K}$ (15 °C) > - **API Standard Compressor Rating Basis:** $T_1 = 15.55 \; °C$ (60 °F), $P_1 = 14.7 \; \text{psia}$ > - **Isentropic exponent for natural gas** is commonly taken as **k = 1.28** when the composition is unknown. --- ### 1.5 Why Thermodynamics of Compressors is Critical in O&G 1. **Pipeline Transport** – Long-distance gas transmission requires intermediate compressor stations to overcome frictional pressure drop. 2. **Gas-Lift Operations** – Compressed gas injected down the annulus to lift wellbore fluids. 3. **Gas Processing** – Refrigeration compressors in NGL/LPG recovery plants (ethane, propane recovery). 4. **CNG/LNG Operations** – High-pressure compression to **200–250 bar** for CNG; liquefaction compressors. 5. **Vapour Recovery Units (VRU)** – Low-pressure compression of flash gas/associated gas. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Processes in Compression A real compression process in a compressor is neither perfectly isothermal nor perfectly isentropic. The **actual process is Polytropic**, defined as: $$P \cdot v^n = \text{constant}$$ where the **polytropic index** $n$ depends on the heat transfer conditions: | Process | Index | Heat Transfer | Realistic? | |---------|-------|---------------|------------| | **Isothermal** | $n = 1$ | Maximum heat removed | Hypothetical ideal | | **Polytropic** | $1 < n < k$ | Some heat removed | **Most realistic** (with cooling) | | **Isentropic (Adiabatic)** | $n = k$ | No heat transfer | Theoretical ideal (no cooling) | | **Actual** | $n > k$ (rare) | Heat added | Friction-dominated (uncommon) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Most numerical problems assume **isentropic compression** ($n = k$) unless explicitly stated as "with intercooling" or "polytropic." --- ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of Compressor Work **Step 1 — Identify Inlet State (State 1):** - Given: $P_1$, $T_1$, gas composition → compute $R$, $k$, $C_p$, $C_v$ **Step 2 — Identify the Process:** - Isentropic? Isothermal? Polytropic? Multi-stage with intercooling? **Step 3 — Apply the Process Equation** to find State 2 (or intermediate states): - For isentropic: $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ **Step 4 — Apply SFEE (Steady Flow Energy Equation)** for work input: - $W_s = -\Delta H = -C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ (for adiabatic, ideal gas) **Step 5 — Apply Efficiency Definition:** - Isentropic Efficiency: $\eta_{isen} = W_{isen}/W_{actual}$ - Mechanical/Electrical efficiency accounted separately. **Step 6 — Compute Volumetric Flow & Mass Flow** if needed: - $\dot{m} = \rho_1 \cdot \dot{V}_1 = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{R T_1}$ --- ### 2.3 The Compressor Work Equation (Detailed) For a **steady-flow, adiabatic** compressor handling an ideal gas, the **SFEE** reduces to: $$w_s = -\int_1^2 v \, dP = -\dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 v_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign indicates **work is done ON the system** (compressor consumes power). KTU board examiners expect a **positive magnitude** to be reported: $W_{in} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$. --- ### 2.4 Compressor Efficiency Definitions (High-Yield) #### (a) Isentropic (Adiabatic) Efficiency $$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1} = \dfrac{\text{Isentropic Work}}{\text{Actual Work}}$$ > Compressor isentropic efficiency typically ranges **70–85%** for centrifugal units and **80–90%** for reciprocating units. #### (b) Polytropic Efficiency $$\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{\dfrac{k-1}{k}}{\dfrac{n-1}{n}}$$ This is useful when the **actual process is polytropic** and the polytropic index $n$ is measured experimentally. #### (c) Mechanical Efficiency $$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{Brake Power (Shaft Power)}}{\text{Indicated Power}}$$ #### (d) Volumetric Efficiency (Reciprocating Compressors) $$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/k}$$ where $C$ is the **clearance ratio** (typical: **0.03–0.10**). --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam-Ready Formula Table (KTU Board Standard)** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Specific work, polytropic | $w = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | kJ/kg | For ideal gas | | 2 | Specific work, isentropic | $w_{isen} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$ | kJ/kg | Set $n = k$ | | 3 | Specific work, isothermal | $w_{iso} = R T_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | kJ/kg | Limit $n \to 1$ | | 4 | Discharge temp, isentropic | $T_{2s} = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ | K | Theory | | 5 | Discharge temp, actual | $T_{2a} = T_1 + \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen}}$ | K | Actual | | 6 | Polytropic efficiency | $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{(k-1)/k}{(n-1)/n}$ | — | — | | 7 | Isentropic efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$ | — | — | | 8 | Power required | $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{actual}$ | kW | Brake power if $\eta_{mech}$ included | | 9 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{R T_1}$ | kg/s | At inlet | | 10 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C (P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ | — | Reciprocating only | | 11 | Ideal-stage pressure ratio (min work) | $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ | — | $N$ = number of stages | | 12 | Polytropic relation | $P v^n = \text{const}$ | — | Process definition | > **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** All absolute values and conditional expressions are written using $\vert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table. --- ### 2.6 Multi-Stage Compression with Intercooling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why multi-stage compression?** A single-stage compressor achieving a high pressure ratio (say, $P_2/P_1 = 64$) would discharge gas at an impractically high temperature, and compressor work would be excessive. **Multi-staging with intercooling** dramatically reduces both work and discharge temperature. **Logic of Intercooling:** 1. Compress gas in **Stage 1** from $P_1$ to intermediate $P_i$. 2. Cool gas in an **intercooler** back to $T_1$ (or near $T_1$). 3. Compress in **Stage 2** from $P_i$ to $P_2$. **Optimum Inter-stage Pressure (for minimum total work in N stages):** $$P_i = (P_1 \cdot P_2^{N-1})^{1/N} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$$ where $r$ is the **pressure ratio per stage** (equal for all stages for minimum work). **Total Work for N stages with perfect intercooling (T restored to T₁):** $$W_{total} = N \cdot \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/(Nk)} - 1 \right]$$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Polytropic Work of Compression We start from the SFEE for an adiabatic (or any) steady-flow process: $$w_s = -\int_1^2 v \, dP$$ For a polytropic process $P v^n = C$ (constant), we have $v = C P^{-1/n}$, hence: $$dv = -\dfrac{C}{n} P^{-(1+n)/n} \, dP = -\dfrac{v}{nP} \, dP$$ Substituting $v \, dP$: $$w_s = -\int_1^2 v \, dP = -\int_1^2 C P^{-1/n} \, dP$$ $$w_s = -C \left[ \dfrac{P^{(n-1)/n}}{(n-1)/n} \right]_1^2 = -\dfrac{n C}{n-1} \left[ P_2^{(n-1)/n} - P_1^{(n-1)/n} \right]$$ Using the constraint $C = P_1 v_1^n = P_1 v_1 \cdot v_1^{n-1}$ and rearranging: $$w_s = -\dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 v_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$ > **Reasoning Step:** The integration uses the **chain rule of the polytropic relation** to express $v$ in terms of $P$ alone, allowing direct integration. The sign convention is that **negative $w_s$ = work done on the gas**. The **magnitude of work input** per unit mass (positive for compressor input): $$\boxed{w_{in} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]}$$ This is valid for an **ideal gas** with constant specific heats. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Isentropic Discharge Temperature For an isentropic process, the relation between temperature and pressure for an ideal gas is: $$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}$$ **Derivation:** Starting from the ideal gas isentropic relation: $$T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$$ This is derived from the first and second laws for an ideal gas: $$ds = C_p \dfrac{dT}{T} - R \dfrac{dP}{P} = 0 \quad (\text{isentropic})$$ Integrating: $$C_p \ln(T_2/T_1) = R \ln(P_2/P_1)$$ $$\ln(T_2/T_1) = \dfrac{R}{C_p} \ln(P_2/P_1) = \dfrac{k-1}{k} \ln(P_2/P_1)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Isentropic Efficiency in Terms of Temperatures By definition: $$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}}$$ Since $w_{isen} = C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$ and $w_{actual} = C_p (T_{2a} - T_1)$: $$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)}{C_p (T_{2a} - T_1)}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}}$$ This is a **fundamental board-exam formula**. The actual discharge temperature is then: $$T_{2a} = T_1 + \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimum Stage Pressure Ratio for Multi-Stage Compression Consider $N$ stages compressing from $P_1$ to $P_2$ with **complete intercooling** to $T_1$ between stages. Total work: $$W_T = N \cdot \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$$ where $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ is the per-stage pressure ratio. **To minimize $W_T$**, we differentiate with respect to $r$. Note that $N$ is constrained by the fact that $r^N = P_2/P_1 = $ constant, so $N = \ln(P_2/P_1)/\ln r$. Substituting and minimizing (or using symmetry argument) yields the elegant result: $$\boxed{r_{opt} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/N}}$$ > **Insight:** For minimum work, **each stage must have the same pressure ratio** when intercooling brings the gas back to $T_1$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Compression Processes | Parameter | Isothermal | Polytropic | Isentropic | |-----------|------------|------------|------------| | Process index $n$ | 1 | 1 < n < k | $k$ | | Heat transfer | Max cooling | Some cooling | None | | Work input | **Minimum** | Intermediate | **Maximum** | | Discharge temperature | $T_1$ (no rise) | $T_1 < T_2 < T_{2s}$ | $T_{2s} > T_1$ | | Realism | Hypothetical | **Most realistic** | Theoretical ideal | > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** $W_{isothermal} < W_{polytropic} < W_{isentropic}$. Hence, **cooling saves work** — this is why every industrial compressor has intercoolers/aftercoolers. --- ### 3.6 Numerical Demonstration — A Typical KTU Board Problem **Problem:** A centrifugal compressor compresses methane from **1 bar, 20 °C** to **5 bar**. Take $k = 1.3$, $R = 0.5182 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Assume isentropic compression. Calculate: (a) Discharge temperature (b) Specific work input (c) Actual power for $\dot{m} = 2 \; \text{kg/s}$ with $\eta_{isen} = 0.80$ **Given:** $P_1 = 1 \; \text{bar} = 100 \; \text{kPa}$, $P_2 = 5 \; \text{bar} = 500 \; \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293 \; \text{K}$ **(a) Discharge Temperature:** $$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} = 293 \times (5)^{0.3/1.3}$$ $$= 293 \times (5)^{0.2308} = 293 \times 1.4385 = 421.5 \; \text{K}$$ $$T_{2s} = 421.5 - 273 = 148.5 \; °C$$ **(b) Specific Work (Isentropic):** $$w_{isen} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$$ $$= \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.5182 \times 293 \times (1.4385 - 1)$$ $$= 4.333 \times 0.5182 \times 293 \times 0.4385 = 287.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **(c) Actual Power:** $$w_{actual} = \dfrac{w_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}} = \dfrac{287.6}{0.80} = 359.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{W}_{actual} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{actual} = 2 \times 359.5 = 719 \; \text{kW}$$ > **Answer Summary:** $T_{2s} = 421.5 \; \text{K}$, $w_{isen} = 287.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$, $\dot{W}_{actual} = 719 \; \text{kW}$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Single-Stage Compressor System ```mermaid flowchart LR INLET[Suction Inlet
P1, T1] --> FILTER[Inlet Filter
and Knockout Drum] FILTER --> SUCVAL[Suction Valve
Throttling Control] SUCVAL --> STG1[Compressor Stage 1
Impeller / Piston] STG1 --> DISCHV[Discharge Valve
Check Valve] DISCHV --> AFTCL[Aftercooler
Heat Exchanger] AFTCL --> SCRUB[Discharge Scrubber
Liquid Removal] SCRUB --> OUTLET[Delivery to Pipeline
or Process] SHAFT[Prime Mover
Gas Turbine or Motor] -. Shaft Power .-> STG1 CTRLIN[Suction Throttle
Anti-Surge Valve] -. Control Signal .-> SUCVAL classDef nodeA fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0B5FAE,color:#000 classDef nodeB fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45309,color:#000 class INLET,FILTER,SUCVAL,STG1,DISCHV,AFTCL,SCRUB,OUTLET nodeA class SHAFT,CTRLIN nodeB ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Gas enters at low pressure (suction), passes through filtration, enters the compressor where shaft work raises its pressure, is cooled in the aftercooler, and is delivered to the next destination. The anti-surge line is a critical control element in centrifugal compressors. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermodynamic States in Compression ```mermaid flowchart TD ST1[State 1: Suction
P1, T1, v1, h1] -->|Isentropic Compression
w_in isentropic| ST2S[State 2s: Ideal Discharge
P2, T2s, v2s, h2s] ST1 -->|Actual Compression
w_in actual less efficient| ST2A[State 2a: Actual Discharge
P2, T2a, v2a, h2a] ST2S -. Isentropic Efficiency .-> ST2A ST2A --> COOLER[Aftercooler / Intercooler
Q out] COOLER --> ST3[State 3: Cooled
P2, T3, h3] classDef stateNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef coolNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 class ST1,ST2S,ST2A,ST3 stateNode class COOLER coolNode ``` --- ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Multi-Stage Compressor with Intercooling ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1
P1 to Pi
Compressor] --> IC1[Intercooler 1
Cool to T1] IC1 --> S2[Stage 2
Pi to Pi2
Compressor] S2 --> IC2[Intercooler 2
Cool to T1] IC2 --> S3[Stage N
Pi_N-1 to P2
Compressor] S3 --> AC[Aftercooler
Final Cooling] AC --> DEL[Delivery
P2, T near T1] PM[Prime Mover
Shaft Driver] -. Coupled .-> S1 PM -. Coupled .-> S2 PM -. Coupled .-> S3 classDef stageNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 classDef coolNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 class S1,S2,S3,DEL stageNode class IC1,IC2,AC,PM coolNode ``` > **Engineering Note:** Notice how each intercooler drops the gas temperature back to (or near) the original $T_1$, while the pressure keeps stepping up. This **T–s diagram equivalence** is what reduces total work and prevents excessive discharge temperatures. --- ### 4.4 Comparison Chart — Work vs Pressure Ratio for Different Processes ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pressure Ratio P2/P1] --> B{Process Type} B -->|Isothermal| W1[Minimum Work
w = R T1 ln Pr] B -->|Polytropic| W2[Intermediate Work
w = n R T1 r n-1 over n minus 1] B -->|Isentropic| W3[Maximum Work
w = k R T1 r k-1 over k minus 1] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define **isentropic efficiency** of a compressor. Why is the discharge temperature of a real compressor higher than the isentropic discharge temperature? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Isentropic efficiency of a compressor is defined as the ratio of the isentropic (ideal) work input to the actual work input, both measured per unit mass of gas: $$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$$ **Reason for higher actual discharge temperature:** In a real compressor, internal irreversibilities such as fluid friction, turbulence, and internal leakage produce additional entropy. The actual process is **not isentropic** but has $\Delta s > 0$, meaning the actual enthalpy rise $h_{2a} - h_1$ is greater than the isentropic rise $h_{2s} - h_1$. Since $h$ increases more for the same pressure rise, the discharge temperature must be higher to satisfy the ideal gas equation of state. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** State any **three advantages** of multi-stage compression with intercooling over single-stage compression in natural gas pipelines. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Reduced work input** — Intercooling reduces the specific volume of the gas before the next stage, hence less work is required in subsequent stages. The total work approaches the isothermal limit. 2. **Lower discharge temperature** — Prevents the gas from reaching dangerously high temperatures (which could damage seals, lubricants, and downstream equipment). 3. **Higher volumetric efficiency** in reciprocating compressors — Lower pressure ratios per stage mean better cylinder filling and reduced clearance-loss effects. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** A two-stage reciprocating compressor with complete intercooling compresses natural gas from **1.2 bar, 25 °C** to **12 bar**. The gas has $\gamma = 1.3$ and $R = 0.5 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$. The isentropic efficiency of each stage is **82%**, and the mechanical efficiency is **90%**. The mass flow rate is **1.5 kg/s**. Calculate: **(a)** The optimum intermediate pressure and the isentropic temperature at the end of each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The total actual power required (brake power) and the actual discharge temperature from each stage. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Question A:** **Given:** $P_1 = 1.2 \; \text{bar}$, $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \; \text{K}$, $P_2 = 12 \; \text{bar}$, $k = 1.3$, $R = 0.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.82$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.90$, $\dot{m} = 1.5 \; \text{kg/s}$ **Part (a) — Optimum Intermediate Pressure and Isentropic Temperatures (7 marks):** The optimum intermediate pressure $P_i$ for two stages with complete intercooling is the **geometric mean**: $$P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 12} = \sqrt{14.4} = 3.795 \; \text{bar}$$ **Stage 1:** Pressure ratio $r_1 = P_i/P_1 = 3.795/1.2 = 3.1625$ $$T_{2s,1} = T_1 \cdot r_1^{(k-1)/k} = 298 \times (3.1625)^{0.3/1.3}$$ $$= 298 \times (3.1625)^{0.2308} = 298 \times 1.2766 = 380.4 \; \text{K}$$ **Stage 2:** Pressure ratio $r_2 = P_2/P_i = 12/3.795 = 3.1625$ (equal ratio — confirms optimum) After perfect intercooling, $T_3 = T_1 = 298 \; \text{K}$ (inlet to Stage 2) $$T_{4s,2} = T_3 \cdot r_2^{(k-1)/k} = 298 \times 1.2766 = 380.4 \; \text{K}$$ > **Result:** $P_i = 3.795 \; \text{bar}$, $T_{2s,1} = 380.4 \; \text{K}$, $T_{4s,2} = 380.4 \; \text{K}$ --- **Part (b) — Brake Power and Actual Discharge Temperatures (7 marks):** **Isentropic work per stage:** $$w_{isen,1} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ r_1^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right] = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.5 \times 298 \times (1.2766 - 1)$$ $$= 4.333 \times 0.5 \times 298 \times 0.2766 = 178.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ Same for stage 2 (since $T_3 = T_1$ and $r_2 = r_1$): $$w_{isen,2} = 178.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Total isentropic work per kg:** $$w_{isen,total} = 2 \times 178.6 = 357.2 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Total actual (indicated) work per kg:** $$w_{actual,total} = \dfrac{w_{isen,total}}{\eta_{isen}} = \dfrac{357.2}{0.82} = 435.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Indicated Power (IP):** $$\dot{W}_{IP} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{actual,total} = 1.5 \times 435.6 = 653.4 \; \text{kW}$$ **Brake Power (BP):** $$\dot{W}_{BP} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{IP}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{653.4}{0.90} = 726.0 \; \text{kW}$$ **Actual Discharge Temperatures:** For each stage, $T_{actual} = T_1 + \dfrac{T_{s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen}}$ Stage 1: $T_{2a} = 298 + \dfrac{380.4 - 298}{0.82} = 298 + \dfrac{82.4}{0.82} = 298 + 100.5 = 398.5 \; \text{K}$ Stage 2: $T_{4a} = 298 + 100.5 = 398.5 \; \text{K}$ > **Final Result:** $\dot{W}_{BP} = 726 \; \text{kW}$, $T_{2a} = T_{4a} = 398.5 \; \text{K} \; (125.5 \; °C)$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]** A centrifugal compressor handles **0.5 kg/s** of natural gas ($k = 1.28$, $R = 0.49 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) from **150 kPa, 30 °C** to **600 kPa**. Assuming isentropic compression with a polytropic efficiency of **85%**, calculate: **(a)** The actual discharge temperature and the polytropic index $n$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The actual power input and the isentropic work of compression per kg. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Question B:** **Given:** $\dot{m} = 0.5 \; \text{kg/s}$, $k = 1.28$, $R = 0.49 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $P_1 = 150 \; \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 303 \; \text{K}$, $P_2 = 600 \; \text{kPa}$, $\eta_{poly} = 0.85$ **Part (a) — Polytropic Index and Actual Discharge Temperature (7 marks):** The polytropic efficiency is defined as: $$\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{(k-1)/k}{(n-1)/n}$$ Solving for $n$: $$(n-1)/n = \dfrac{(k-1)/k}{\eta_{poly}} = \dfrac{0.28/1.28}{0.85} = \dfrac{0.21875}{0.85} = 0.2574$$ $$1 - 1/n = 0.2574 \Rightarrow 1/n = 0.7426 \Rightarrow n = 1.3466$$ > **Result:** $n = 1.347$ **Actual Discharge Temperature (using polytropic relation):** $$T_{2a} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} = 303 \times (4)^{0.2574}$$ $$= 303 \times 4^{0.2574} = 303 \times 1.4448 = 437.8 \; \text{K}$$ $$T_{2a} = 437.8 - 273 = 164.8 \; °C$$ --- **Part (b) — Actual Power Input and Isentropic Work (7 marks):** **Polytropic (actual) work per kg:** $$w_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$ $$= \dfrac{1.347}{0.347} \times 0.49 \times 303 \times (1.4448 - 1)$$ $$= 3.882 \times 0.49 \times 303 \times 0.4448 = 256.1 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Isentropic work per kg:** $$w_{isen} = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$$ Exponent: $(k-1)/k = 0.28/1.28 = 0.21875$ $$(P_2/P_1)^{0.21875} = 4^{0.21875} = 1.3767$$ $$w_{isen} = \dfrac{1.28}{0.28} \times 0.49 \times 303 \times (1.3767 - 1) = 4.571 \times 0.49 \times 303 \times 0.3767$$ $$= 256.1 \times \eta_{poly} = 256.1 \times 0.85 = 217.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Verification:** $w_{isen} = \eta_{poly} \times w_{poly} = 0.85 \times 256.1 = 217.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ ✓ **Actual Power Input:** $$\dot{W}_{actual} = \dot{m} \times w_{poly} = 0.5 \times 256.1 = 128.05 \; \text{kW}$$ > **Final Result:** $n = 1.347$, $T_{2a} = 437.8 \; \text{K}$, $\dot{W}_{actual} = 128.05 \; \text{kW}$, $w_{isen} = 217.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the Board Exam:** > > 1. **Sign convention error** — Compressor work is **input to the system**; if you forget the negative sign convention in SFEE, you will be marked **–1 mark** on the result. Always state: "work done **on** the gas is positive magnitude". > > 2. **Confusing $k$ and $n$** — A common KTU trap is to give $k$ in the data but the question asks for a **polytropic** process. Always identify whether the process is **isentropic ($k$), isothermal ($n=1$), or polytropic ($n$)** at the very start of the solution. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert temperature units** — $T_1$ **must** be in Kelvin. **25 °C ≠ 25 K**; this single error costs **1 mark** and propagates through all answers. > > 4. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency in Part B** — If $\eta_{mech}$ is given (e.g., 0.90), the **Brake Power** is what the motor must supply: $BP = IP / \eta_{mech}$. Showing the IP without the BP loses **1–2 marks**. > > 5. **Wrong intercooling assumption** — "Complete intercooling" means $T_{inlet,stage 2} = T_1$. "Perfect intercooling" sometimes means **cooler exit temperature = $T_1$**; some problems give an intercooler exit temperature **higher than $T_1$** — read carefully. > > 6. **Mixing $\eta_{isen}$ and $\eta_{poly}$** — These are **different efficiencies** with **different definitions**. KTU examiners distinguish them. In a single problem, use **one** consistently. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thermodynamics of Compressors** #### Core Concepts - ☐ A compressor is a **steady-flow work-input device** (shaft work → pressure rise). - ☐ For an **ideal gas**, compressor work is governed by $w = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1]$. - ☐ The SFEE for an adiabatic compressor reduces to $w_s = -\Delta h = -C_p \Delta T$. - ☐ Real compression is **polytropic** ($1 < n < k$); isentropic ($n = k$) is the **ideal** reference. #### Key Temperatures & Efficiencies - ☐ $T_{2s} = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ — Isentropic discharge temperature. - ☐ $T_{2a} = T_1 + (T_{2s} - T_1)/\eta_{isen}$ — Actual discharge temperature. - ☐ Isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{isen} = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_{2a} - T_1)$. - ☐ Polytropic efficiency: $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{(k-1)/k}{(n-1)/n}$. #### Multi-Stage Compression - ☐ Optimum stage pressure ratio for minimum work: $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$. - ☐ For 2 stages: $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$ (geometric mean of end pressures). - ☐ Intercooling reduces **both** work input and discharge temperature. - ☐ For complete intercooling, total work = $N \cdot w_{single stage}$ with $T_1$ at inlet to each stage. #### Numerical & Units - ☐ Always convert $T$ to **Kelvin**; $P$ to a **consistent unit** (kPa or bar). - ☐ For natural gas, typical $k = 1.25$–$1.30$, $R = 0.49$–$0.52 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. - ☐ Reciprocating compressor $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ with $C$ = clearance ratio. - ☐ Mass flow: $\dot{m} = P_1 \dot{V}_1 / (R T_1)$; Power: $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{actual}$. #### O&G Specific Context - ☐ **Mainline gas pipelines** use centrifugal/axial compressors with intercooling. - ☐ **Gas-lift** uses reciprocating compressors for high-pressure injection gas. - ☐ **VRUs and refrigeration** in gas processing use rotary/screw compressors. - ☐ Typical pipeline discharge pressure: **70–100 bar**; CNG compression: **200–250 bar**. > **Final Exam Mantra:** "Identify the **process** (isentropic/isothermal/polytropic), then **the efficiency** (isentropic/polytropic/mechanical), then **apply the right formula** in consistent SI units."

topics.title: Compression Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compression Calculations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Compression Calculations** in the context of oil and natural gas engineering refer to the quantitative thermodynamic analysis of mechanical work, power requirements, volumetric efficiency, and discharge conditions required to compress natural gas (or associated petroleum gas) from a low suction pressure to a higher discharge pressure using reciprocating or centrifugal compressors. These calculations form the engineering backbone of: - **Gas-lift operations** (injecting high-pressure gas into wells to lift crude oil) - **Gas gathering and transportation pipelines** - **Gas reinjection schemes** (for pressure maintenance or EOR) - **Gas processing plant suction and discharge systems** - **CNG compression stations** The compression process is governed by the **Polytropic Law**: $$PV^n = \text{constant}$$ where $n$ is the polytropic exponent, which assumes a specific value depending on the heat transfer conditions during compression. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Compression calculations are tested under the course outcome **CO3** — *Apply thermodynamic principles to compute work, power, and efficiency of gas compression systems used in petroleum production operations.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are using a **bicycle pump** to inflate a tire. The air inside the pump gets squashed (compressed) and feels warm — that is the heat of compression. To pump harder, you must push against higher pressure, so the work you do increases as the tire gets firmer. Now scale this up to a **giant industrial gas compressor** at a gas-lift manifold. It is essentially a heavy-duty, multi-stage version of your bicycle pump, with cooling between stages to remove the heat you felt on the pump barrel. The "Compression Calculations" tell the engineer: 1. **How much work** the motor must do (in kW or HP) 2. **How many stages** are economically optimal 3. **How hot** the gas will get (and whether intercoolers are needed) 4. **How much gas** actually enters the cylinder versus how much leaks back due to clearance volume > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** Compression calculations are the engineering math that tells us *how much electricity* and *how much steel* (compressor size) we need to squeeze natural gas from one pressure to another. Just like pumping a bicycle tire, but with numbers attached. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Reference | |-----------|----------------------|---------------| | Universal gas constant, $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Universal | | Universal gas constant (engineering) | **10.732 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)** | Field units | | Specific gas constant for air, $R_{air}$ | **53.35 ft·lbf/(lbm·°R)** | Field units | | Standard temperature, $T_{sc}$ | **288.15 K (60°F, 520°R)** | KTU Standard | | Standard pressure, $P_{sc}$ | **101.325 kPa (14.7 psia)** | KTU Standard | | Specific heat ratio for natural gas, $k = C_p/C_v$ | **1.25 to 1.30** | Field typical | | Polytropic exponent, $n$ | **1.20 to 1.40** | Compression design | | Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_m$ | **0.85 to 0.95** | KTU standard | | Compression (isentropic) efficiency, $\eta_c$ | **0.70 to 0.85** | KTU standard | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Processes of Compression A compressor operates along one of three fundamental thermodynamic paths depending on heat removal: ### 2.1.1 Isothermal Compression (n = 1) - **Happens when:** Heat generated during compression is removed *perfectly* and *instantaneously*, so gas temperature stays constant. - **Theoretical ideal**, but practically unattainable. - **Lowest work requirement** — this is the *theoretical minimum work* a compressor could ever consume. - Used as the **benchmark** for evaluating real compressor performance. ### 2.1.2 Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression (n = k = Cp/Cv) - **Happens when:** No heat is exchanged with surroundings (perfectly insulated cylinder). - **Temperature rises significantly** during compression. - **Highest work requirement** among ideal processes. - Real compressors approximate this when running fast with no cooling. ### 2.1.3 Polytropic Compression (1 < n < k) - **Happens when:** *Partial* heat removal occurs — heat is added to gas by friction/viscous dissipation but also removed by cylinder walls and intercoolers. - **Most realistic case** in field practice. - $n$ is empirically determined using logs of test data. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Compression Logic (Single-Stage) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Logic Chain** — memorize this 4-step flow: **Step 1 — Establish Inlet Conditions:** Measure or specify suction pressure $P_1$, suction temperature $T_1$, and volumetric flow rate $Q_1$ (at suction conditions). **Step 2 — Apply Polytropic Relation:** Determine the discharge pressure $P_2$ required by the process. Use $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ to find discharge temperature. **Step 3 — Calculate Work Done:** Use the generalized formula to find work per unit mass, then multiply by mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ to get power. **Step 4 — Account for Real-World Losses:** Divide by mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ and isentropic efficiency $\eta_c$ to get brake power (motor input). ## 2.3 Why Multi-Stage Compression? When the pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ is large (typically greater than **4 to 5**), single-stage compression becomes inefficient because: 1. **Excessive discharge temperature** — can damage valves, lubricants, and exceed safe limits (~180°C for recip compressors). 2. **Higher work input** — single-stage adiabatic work is greater than the sum of multi-stage work. 3. **Low volumetric efficiency** — clearance volume re-expansion steals too much capacity. **Solution:** Split compression into $N$ equal stages with **intercoolers** between stages that return gas to inlet temperature $T_1$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the single most important reference for this topic. Memorize the units and conditions of applicability.** | # | Formula | Description | Applicability | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | All ideal-gas compressors | | 2 | $W_{iso} = P_1V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Work of isothermal compression (per unit mass) | $n=1$ | | 3 | $W_{iso} = mRT_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Work of isothermal compression (per cycle) | $n=1$ | | 4 | $W_{adia} = \dfrac{mRT_1\, n}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | Work of polytropic/adiabatic compression | $n = k$ or $n$ | | 5 | $T_2 = T_1\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n}$ | Discharge temperature relation | Polytropic | | 6 | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency with clearance $c$ | Reciprocating only | | 7 | $r_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/N}$ | Optimum pressure ratio per stage | Multi-stage design | | 8 | $W_{total,N} = N \cdot W_{single}$ | Total work for $N$ equal stages with intercooling | Multi-stage | | 9 | $W_{total,N} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{n-1}\, P_1V_1\left[(r_{opt})^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | Total work in $N$ stages (intercooled) | Multi-stage | | 10 | $BHP = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{indicated}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_c}$ | Brake horsepower from indicated power | Real compressors | | 11 | $P_{after\,intercooler} = P_2 / r_{opt}^{\,N-1}$ | Pressure at any intercooler stage | Multi-stage design | | 12 | $N_{min\,work} = \dfrac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln(r_{max})}$ | Minimum stages for $r_{max}$ constraint | Design check | **Notation Used:** - $P_1, P_2$ — suction and discharge pressures (Pa or psia) - $V_1$ — suction volume per cycle (m³ or ft³) - $T_1, T_2$ — suction and discharge temperatures (K or °R) - $m$ — mass of gas compressed per cycle (kg or lbm) - $n$ — polytropic exponent; $c$ — clearance fraction - $N$ — number of compression stages - $r$ — pressure ratio per stage > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *volumetric efficiency* $\eta_v$ (depends on clearance) with *isentropic efficiency* $\eta_c$ (depends on thermodynamics). They are completely different concepts! $\eta_v$ is mechanical/clearance-based; $\eta_c$ is energy-based. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Petroleum Engineering | Application | Typical Pressure Ratio | Typical Stages | Why Compression Needed | |-------------|------------------------|----------------|------------------------| | Gas-lift injection | 5 to 25 | 2 to 4 | Inject gas down well to lift oil | | Gas gathering line entry | 1.5 to 4 | 1 to 2 | Boost line pressure for transport | | Gas reinjection (EOR) | 3 to 15 | 2 to 3 | Maintain reservoir pressure | | Gas processing | 2 to 6 | 1 to 2 | Meet sales-line pressure spec | | CNG cascade | up to 250 | 3 to 4 | Compress to ~250 bar for vehicles | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A: Work of Polytropic Compression (Single-Stage) **Starting Point — First Law for Open System (control volume):** For a compressor treated as a control volume, the steady-flow energy equation gives shaft work as: $$\dot{W}_s = \dot{m} \cdot h_2 - \dot{m} \cdot h_1 = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$ **Step 1 — Express Enthalpy Difference via Polytropic Relations:** For an ideal gas with $h = C_p T$ and using $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ : $$h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1) = C_p T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $C_p$ in terms of $R$ and $n$:** From thermodynamic identity: $$C_p = \frac{n}{n-1} R$$ This identity holds because for a polytropic process: $$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{C_p}{C_v} = n$$ Solving these simultaneously yields $C_p = nR/(n-1)$. **Step 3 — Combine to Get Work per Unit Mass:** $$w_s = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ **Step 4 — Convert to Volume Basis Using $P_1V_1 = mRT_1$ :** $$W = m \cdot w_s = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is the *exact* formula that appears in every KTU numerical. Note that for $n \to 1$ (isothermal), using L'Hôpital's rule on the bracketed term yields $W = P_1V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$, the classical isothermal work formula. ## 3.2 Derivation B: Optimum Pressure Ratio in Multi-Stage Compression **Problem Setup:** A gas is compressed from $P_1$ to $P_2$ in $N$ equal stages with ideal intercooling (each intercooler returns gas to $T_1$). Let $r$ be the pressure ratio per stage, so $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$. **Step 1 — Write Work per Stage:** For a single stage with inlet $P$ and outlet $rP$ : $$W_{stage} = \frac{n}{n-1} P V_1\left[(r)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ where $V_1$ is the volume entering that stage. **Step 2 — Apply Ideal Intercooling Assumption:** With intercooling, the *volume* entering each successive stage is the same $V_1$ because temperature returns to $T_1$ between stages. Only the *pressure* entering each stage changes. **Step 3 — Total Work = N × Work per Stage:** $$W_{total} = N \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1\left[(r)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ **Step 4 — Minimize $W_{total}$ with Respect to $r$ :** Treat $N$ as constant. Differentiate with respect to $r$ and set derivative to zero. $$\frac{dW_{total}}{dr} = N \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \cdot \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot r^{(n-1)/n - 1} = 0$$ This derivative cannot equal zero for any finite $r > 0$ — the bracket must be re-examined. The correct approach uses the fact that the suction volume to each stage scales as $P_1/P_{stage}$ for fixed mass flow. A more rigorous optimization gives: $$\boxed{\,r_{opt} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Takeaway:** For minimum total work in $N$ equal stages with intercooling, all stages must have **equal pressure ratios**, and that ratio equals the **Nth root of the overall pressure ratio**. This is one of the most-tested facts on the KTU exam. ## 3.3 Derivation C: Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance **Physical Setup:** A reciprocating compressor cylinder has: - Swept volume $V_s$ (piston displacement) - Clearance volume $V_c$ (volume at end of discharge stroke) - Clearance fraction $c = V_c / V_s$ **Step 1 — Mass Balance Over One Cycle:** At the start of suction stroke, the cylinder contains gas at discharge pressure $P_2$ occupying clearance volume $V_c$. This gas must *re-expand* isentropically to suction pressure $P_1$ before the piston can begin to draw in fresh gas. **Step 2 — Re-expansion Volume:** Using $P V^n = \text{const}$ for the re-expansion: $$V_{re-exp} = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Step 3 — Effective Suction Volume:** The piston can only draw in fresh gas for the portion of the stroke *after* the re-expanded gas has been pushed back. Therefore: $$V_{effective} = V_s - V_{re-exp} + V_c = V_s - V_c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} + V_c$$ Wait — that needs correction. The effective suction volume is the swept volume *minus* the re-expansion volume. But accounting carefully for the *full* indicator diagram: $$\eta_v = \frac{V_{effective}}{V_s} = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** As $P_2/P_1 \to 1$, $\eta_v \to 1$ (no compression, full sweep is effective). As $P_2/P_1$ increases, $\eta_v$ drops. Multi-staging with intermediate pressures keeps each stage's pressure ratio low, preserving $\eta_v$ at high values. ## 3.4 KTU-Style Numerical: Single-Stage Compression > **Problem (KTU ESE Pattern — 14 marks style, single-stage focus):** A natural gas compressor takes in gas at $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, and compresses it to $P_2 = 800$ kPa following a polytropic process with $n = 1.30$. The gas flow rate is $Q_1 = 0.5$ m³/s at suction conditions. The gas constant is $R = 0.5$ kJ/(kg·K). Calculate: - (a) The mass flow rate (3 marks) - (b) The discharge temperature (3 marks) - (c) The indicated power required (4 marks) - (d) The brake power assuming $\eta_m = 0.90$ and $\eta_c = 0.80$ (4 marks) **Solution:** **Part (a) — Mass Flow Rate:** Using ideal gas law at suction: $P_1 Q_1 = \dot{m} R T_1$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{P_1 Q_1}{R T_1} = \frac{(100)(0.5)}{(0.5)(300)} = \frac{50}{150} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Part (b) — Discharge Temperature:** $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} = 300 \left(\frac{800}{100}\right)^{(0.30/1.30)}$$ $$T_2 = 300 \cdot (8)^{0.2308} = 300 \cdot 1.6262 = 487.86 \text{ K}$$ **Part (c) — Indicated Power (Polytropic Work Rate):** $$\dot{W}_{ind} = \frac{n}{n-1} \dot{m} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = \frac{1.30}{0.30} \cdot (0.3333)(0.5)(300)\left[(8)^{0.2308} - 1\right]$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = 4.333 \cdot 50 \cdot (1.6262 - 1) = 4.333 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.6262$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = 135.69 \text{ kW}$$ **Part (d) — Brake Power:** $$\dot{W}_{brake} = \frac{\dot{W}_{ind}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_c} = \frac{135.69}{0.90 \times 0.80} = \frac{135.69}{0.72} = 188.46 \text{ kW}$$ --- ## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical: Multi-Stage Compression > **Problem (KTU ESE Pattern — 14 marks style, multi-stage focus):** Natural gas is to be compressed from $P_1 = 1.0$ bar to $P_2 = 64$ bar. Determine the *minimum number of equal stages* required if the maximum allowable pressure ratio per stage is 4, and compute the *optimum pressure ratio per stage* for $N=3$. Also calculate the total compression work using $n = 1.25$, $V_1 = 1.0$ m³ per cycle, and compare with single-stage isothermal work. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Number of Stages:** $$N = \frac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln(r_{max})} = \frac{\ln(64/1)}{\ln(4)} = \frac{4.1589}{1.3863} = 3.0$$ So $N_{min} = 3$ stages. (If the calculation gives a non-integer, round UP to next integer.) **Step 2 — Optimum Pressure Ratio for N = 3:** $$r_{opt} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N} = (64)^{1/3} = 4$$ Conveniently, $r_{opt} = r_{max} = 4$ in this case. **Step 3 — Total Work (3-stage with intercooling, $n = 1.25$):** $$W_{total} = 3 \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1\left[r_{opt}^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$W_{total} = 3 \cdot \frac{1.25}{0.25} \cdot (1.0 \times 10^5)(1.0)\left[(4)^{0.20} - 1\right]$$ $$W_{total} = 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 10^5 \cdot (1.3195 - 1) = 15 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.3195$$ $$W_{total} = 4.793 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 479.3 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4 — Isothermal Work (Single-Stage Benchmark):** $$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = (1.0 \times 10^5)(1.0)\ln(64) = 10^5 \times 4.1589 = 415.89 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5 — Comparison:** The 3-stage polytropic work (479.3 kJ) is **15.3% more** than the ideal isothermal work (415.9 kJ), demonstrating that even with optimal staging and intercooling, real compression always exceeds the isothermal minimum. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Single-Stage Polytropic Work for Comparison:** > $$W_{single} = \frac{1.25}{0.25} \cdot 10^5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot [(64)^{0.20} - 1] = 5 \times 10^5 \times (2.2974 - 1) = 648.7 \text{ kJ}$$ > So multi-staging saves $\frac{648.7 - 479.3}{648.7} \times 100 = 26.1\%$ of the work compared to single-stage at the same polytropic exponent. This is *the* key engineering justification for multi-staging. ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Process | Work Formula | $T_2/T_1$ Relation | $\eta_v$ When Cleared | Real-World? | |---------|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|-------------| | Isothermal ($n=1$) | $P_1V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | $T_2 = T_1$ | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)$ | No (ideal) | | Polytropic ($1 < n < k$) | $\frac{n}{n-1}P_1V_1[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}-1]$ | $T_2 > T_1$ | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | **YES (most realistic)** | | Adiabatic ($n=k$) | $\frac{k}{k-1}P_1V_1[(P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}-1]$ | $T_2 \gg T_1$ | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ | Approximate (fast recip) | | Multi-stage equal $N$ | $N \cdot W_{stage}$ with $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ | Each stage to same $T_1$ | Per-stage formula | **YES (industry standard)** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Single-Stage Compression Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Suction Inlet

State its range of values for a natural gas compressor with intercooling, and explain why a value of $n=1$ is the ideal but practically unattainable limit. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The polytropic exponent $n$ is the exponent in the equation $PV^n = \text{constant}$ that describes the actual thermodynamic path followed by a gas during compression. For natural gas compression with intercooling between stages, the value of $n$ typically lies in the range **$1.20 \le n \le 1.35$** (closer to 1 for well-cooled compressors, closer to $k \approx 1.30$ for poorly cooled ones). A value of $n=1$ represents the **isothermal process** (PV = constant), which is the theoretical minimum work condition. It is practically unattainable because it would require *instantaneous* and *complete* removal of all heat generated by compression — physically impossible since finite temperature gradients drive heat transfer. (3 marks) ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is *volumetric efficiency* of a reciprocating compressor? Write the formula including the effect of clearance volume and explain what happens to it as the pressure ratio increases. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ is the ratio of the actual volume of gas drawn in at suction conditions to the swept volume (piston displacement) of the compressor. It accounts for the loss of intake capacity due to re-expansion of gas trapped in the clearance volume. Formula: $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ where $c = V_c/V_s$ is the clearance fraction. As pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ increases, the re-expansion volume $V_c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ increases, so $\eta_v$ **decreases**. This is why multi-staging (which keeps each stage's pressure ratio low) is essential for high overall pressure ratios. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the *work done per cycle* in a single-stage reciprocating compressor operating on a polytropic process with exponent $n$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling compresses methane from $P_1 = 1.5$ bar to $P_2 = 12$ bar. The suction conditions are $T_1 = 305$ K. Take the polytropic exponent as $n = 1.28$ and $R = 0.518$ kJ/(kg·K) for methane. Calculate: - (i) The optimum intermediate pressure $P_i$ (2 marks) - (ii) The total work of compression per kg of gas (3 marks) - (iii) The discharge temperature leaving each stage (2 marks) ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Assumptions:** 1. Ideal gas behavior. 2. Polytropic process: $PV^n = \text{constant}$. 3. Steady-flow, single-stage, single-acting compressor. 4. Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes. 5. No clearance volume loss for this derivation (purely thermodynamic). **Derivation:** For a steady-flow control volume around the compressor cylinder, the shaft work per unit mass is: $$w_s = h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ For a polytropic process: $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}$$ Substituting: $$w_s = C_p T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ Using the thermodynamic identity $C_p = \dfrac{n}{n-1}R$ (derived from $C_p - C_v = R$ and $C_p/C_v = n$ for the polytropic process): $$w_s = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ For one cycle, mass compressed = $m$, total work: $$W = m \cdot w_s = \frac{n}{n-1} m R T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ Using $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$: $$\boxed{W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]}$$ (7 marks — full derivation + assumptions + final boxed formula) #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Optimum Intermediate Pressure:** For $N = 2$ stages with equal pressure ratios: $$r_{opt} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/2} = \left(\frac{12}{1.5}\right)^{1/2} = (8)^{0.5} = 2.828$$ $$P_i = r_{opt} \cdot P_1 = 2.828 \times 1.5 = 4.243 \text{ bar}$$ **(2 marks)** **(ii) Total Work of Compression per kg:** With perfect intercooling, total work per kg: $$w_{total} = 2 \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1\left[(r_{opt})^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$w_{total} = 2 \cdot \frac{1.28}{0.28} \cdot (0.518)(305)\left[(2.828)^{(0.28/1.28)} - 1\right]$$ Calculate the exponent: $0.28/1.28 = 0.21875$ $$(2.828)^{0.21875} = e^{0.21875 \cdot \ln(2.828)} = e^{0.21875 \times 1.0397} = e^{0.2274} = 1.2553$$ $$w_{total} = 2 \cdot 4.5714 \cdot 0.518 \cdot 305 \cdot (1.2553 - 1)$$ $$w_{total} = 2 \cdot 4.5714 \cdot 158.0 \cdot 0.2553$$ $$w_{total} = 368.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(3 marks)** **(iii) Discharge Temperatures:** With perfect intercooling, gas leaves each intercooler at $T_1 = 305$ K. Hence discharge from both Stage 1 and Stage 2 is the same: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_{opt})^{(n-1)/n} = 305 \cdot 1.2553 = 382.9 \text{ K}$$ **(2 marks)** --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch of the indicator (P-V) diagram, explain how the *volumetric efficiency* of a reciprocating compressor is derived. Write the final expression. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-stage double-acting reciprocating compressor handles natural gas with the following specifications: - Suction pressure $P_1 = 120$ kPa - Discharge pressure $P_2 = 720$ kPa - Clearance fraction $c = 0.06$ - Polytropic exponent $n = 1.32$ - Cylinder bore $D = 0.30$ m, stroke $L = 0.40$ m - Suction temperature $T_1 = 305$ K - Gas constant $R = 0.50$ kJ/(kg·K) - Compressor speed $N = 300$ rpm Calculate: (i) Volumetric efficiency (2 marks) (ii) Volume of gas compressed per second at suction (3 marks) (iii) Indicated power in kW (2 marks) ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — Indicator Diagram and $\eta_v$ Derivation (7 marks) **Indicator Diagram Description:** The P-V diagram (indicator card) of a real reciprocating compressor has **four distinct processes** per cycle: 1. **Suction stroke (1→2):** Piston moves from inner dead centre (IDC) to outer dead centre (ODC) at constant suction pressure $P_1$. Gas enters through the suction valve. 2. **Compression stroke (2→3):** Both valves closed. Gas compressed polytropically ($PV^n = \text{const}$) from $P_1$ to $P_2$. 3. **Discharge stroke (3→4):** Piston moves from ODC back to IDC at constant discharge pressure $P_2$. Gas leaves through the discharge valve. 4. **Re-expansion stroke (4→1):** Both valves closed. Gas trapped in clearance volume $V_c$ re-expands polytropically from $P_2$ back to $P_1$, ready to begin the next suction stroke. **Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency:** Let $V_s$ = swept volume (piston displacement) and $V_c$ = clearance volume, with $c = V_c/V_s$. At the end of the discharge stroke, clearance volume contains gas at $P_2$ and volume $V_c$. After re-expansion to $P_1$: $$V_{re\text{-}exp} = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ Fresh gas can only enter when piston has pushed out the re-expanded gas. So *effective* suction volume per cycle: $$V_{eff} = V_s - \left(V_{re\text{-}exp} - V_c\right) = V_s - V_c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} + V_c$$ Volumetric efficiency: $$\eta_v = \frac{V_{eff}}{V_s} = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ (7 marks — diagram + derivation + final formula) #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Volumetric Efficiency:** $$\eta_v = 1 + 0.06 - 0.06 \left(\frac{720}{120}\right)^{1/1.32}$$ $$\eta_v = 1.06 - 0.06 \cdot (6)^{0.7576}$$ Calculate: $(6)^{0.7576} = e^{0.7576 \cdot \ln 6} = e^{0.7576 \times 1.7918} = e^{1.3574} = 3.886$ $$\eta_v = 1.06 - 0.06 \times 3.886 = 1.06 - 0.2331 = 0.8269 \text{ or } 82.69\%$$ **(2 marks)** **(ii) Volume of Gas Compressed per Second:** Swept volume per stroke (single-acting assumption for one cylinder face): $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 (0.40) = 0.02827 \text{ m}^3$$ Since it is double-acting, total swept volume per revolution = $2 V_s = 0.05655$ m³/rev. Volumetric flow at suction: $$Q_1 = \eta_v \cdot 2V_s \cdot N = 0.8269 \times 0.05655 \times \frac{300}{60}$$ $$Q_1 = 0.8269 \times 0.05655 \times 5 = 0.2338 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **(3 marks)** **(iii) Indicated Power:** Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 Q_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{(120)(0.2338)}{(0.50 \times 1000)(305)} = \dfrac{28.06}{152500} = 1.840 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$ Wait — let me recompute. Using SI units: $P_1 = 120$ kPa = 120,000 Pa, $R = 500$ J/(kg·K). $$\dot{m} = \frac{P_1 Q_1}{R T_1} = \frac{120000 \times 0.2338}{500 \times 305} = \frac{28056}{152500} = 0.1840 \text{ kg/s}$$ Indicated power: $$\dot{W}_{ind} = \frac{n}{n-1} \dot{m} R T_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = \frac{1.32}{0.32} \times 0.1840 \times 500 \times 305 \left[(6)^{0.2424} - 1\right]$$ $(6)^{0.2424} = e^{0.2424 \times 1.7918} = e^{0.4344} = 1.5439$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = 4.125 \times 0.1840 \times 500 \times 305 \times (1.5439 - 1)$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = 4.125 \times 0.1840 \times 152500 \times 0.5439$$ $$\dot{W}_{ind} = 4.125 \times 0.1840 \times 82944.75 = 62956 \text{ W} \approx 63.0 \text{ kW}$$ **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse $R$ in different units.** In SI, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). For *specific* gas constant of natural gas, $R \approx 0.5$ kJ/(kg·K). Mixing these gives 16× errors. (Common: 3 marks lost per occurrence.) > 2. **Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa** when using $R$ in J/(kg·K). Watch units obsessively. > 3. **Polytropic exponent $n$ vs specific heat ratio $k$:** Many students write $k$ when the problem says $n$. They are *equal only for adiabatic*. If the problem specifies intercooling, you must use the *given* $n$, not $k$. > 4. **For multi-stage problems, do NOT forget to multiply by $N$** the single-stage work. This is the single most common error in 14-mark questions. > 5. **In Part (a) derivations, you must explicitly state the assumption of ideal gas, polytropic process, and steady state.** Skipping assumptions costs 1-2 marks even if the algebra is perfect. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compression Calculations** ### Core Concepts to Memorize - **Polytropic equation:** $PV^n = \text{constant}$ (the master equation) - **Three process limits:** $n = 1$ (isothermal, lowest work) $\rightarrow$ $n = k$ (adiabatic, highest work) $\rightarrow$ $1 < n < k$ (polytropic, real) - **Single-stage work formula:** $W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1\left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ - **Discharge temperature:** $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ - **Volumetric efficiency (with clearance):** $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ - **Optimum multi-stage ratio:** $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ - **Minimum number of stages:** $N = \lceil \ln(P_2/P_1) / \ln(r_{max}) \rceil$ - **Multi-stage total work:** $N \times$ (work of one stage with $r_{opt}$) ### Numerical Problem-Solving Checklist 1. **Identify** $P_1, P_2, T_1, n, c$ (and $\eta_m, \eta_c$ if asked for BHP) 2. **Convert units consistently** — Pa vs kPa vs bar vs psia; K vs °C 3. **Calculate work per cycle or per second** using the polytropic formula 4. **Check discharge temperature** — must be below 180°C for safe operation 5. **If pressure ratio > 4**, automatically switch to multi-stage analysis 6. **For multi-stage, find $N$ first**, then $r_{opt}$, then work per stage, then $W_{total} = N \times W_{stage}$ 7. **Apply efficiencies** at the very end to convert indicated work to brake work ### Conceptual "Why" Questions KTU May Ask - **Why isothermal not used in practice?** Requires infinite heat transfer rate — physically impossible. - **Why multi-stage with intercooling?** Reduces work, lowers discharge temperature, improves $\eta_v$. - **Why does $\eta_v$ decrease with pressure ratio?** Larger re-expansion of clearance gas from higher $P_2$. - **Why use $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$?** It minimizes total work for $N$ equal stages with intercooling. - **Difference between $\eta_v$ and $\eta_c$?** $\eta_v$ = geometric/clearance; $\eta_c$ = thermodynamic/path efficiency. ### Quick Reference — KTU-Favorite Numerical Combinations | Question Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | |---------------|-------|------|-------------| | Single-stage power | $P_1, P_2, T_1, n, Q_1$ | Indicated power kW | $\dot{W} = \frac{n}{n-1}\dot{m}RT_1[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}-1]$ | | Discharge temperature | $P_1, P_2, T_1, n$ | $T_2$ | $T_2 = T_1(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ | | Optimum staging | $P_1, P_2, N$ | $r_{opt}$ | $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ | | Number of stages | $P_1, P_2, r_{max}$ | $N_{min}$ | $N = \ln(P_2/P_1)/\ln(r_{max})$ | | $\eta_v$ calculation | $P_1, P_2, c, n$ | $\eta_v$ | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | | Brake power | Indicated power, $\eta_m, \eta_c$ | $BHP$ | $BHP = \dot{W}_{ind}/(\eta_m \eta_c)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always (1) state assumptions, (2) show all formula substitutions with units, (3) compute $\eta_v$ and $T_2$ even if not asked — examiners reward thoroughness, (4) end with a comparative statement ("multi-stage saves X% work compared to single-stage"). This signals deep understanding and typically earns full or near-full marks.

topics.title: Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Natural Gas Engineering Fundamentals

**Natural Gas (NG)** is a naturally occurring hydrocarbon gas mixture consisting primarily of **methane (CH₄)** along with varying amounts of higher alkanes (ethane, propane, butane), carbon dioxide (CO₂), hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), nitrogen (N₂), and trace quantities of noble gases and water vapor. From an engineering perspective, the "Fundamentals" of Oil and Natural Gas Engineering encompass the foundational physical, chemical, and thermodynamic principles governing the exploration, production, processing, transportation, and utilization of natural gas resources.

$$ \text{Natural Gas} = \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{N}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamentals module establishes the foundational vocabulary and quantitative basis for all subsequent processing, LNG conversion, and gas-to-liquid (GTL) modules. Mastery of gas property calculations and phase behavior is mandatory for higher-order modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of natural gas engineering as **plumbing for invisible fuel**. Just as municipal water supply requires understanding of pipe networks, pressure ratings, water quality (impurities), and flow rates, natural gas engineering requires analogous understanding of:

- **Reservoir** → the underground "tank" storing the gas
- **Wellhead** → the "tap" controlling extraction
- **Separator** → the "filter" removing liquids and solids
- **Pipeline** → the "pipe" transporting the product
- **Compressor station** → the "pump" boosting pressure
- **Processing plant** → the "treatment facility" purifying the gas

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A natural gas pipeline system is conceptually similar to a city water distribution network. The compressor station corresponds to the water pumping station, the gas processing plant corresponds to the water treatment plant, and the regulator stations correspond to pressure-reducing valves in household plumbing. The key difference is that gas is **compressible**, unlike water, which makes its behavior fundamentally different and more complex.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and standard conditions are critical for all calculations in natural gas engineering:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Definition |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** or **10.732 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)** |
| Standard Temperature | T_sc | **288.15 K (15°C / 59°F)** |
| Standard Pressure (KTU/ISO) | P_sc | **101.325 kPa (14.696 psia)** |
| Normal Boiling Point of Methane | T_b | **111.7 K (-161.5°C)** |
| Critical Temp. of Methane | T_c | **190.4 K (-82.7°C)** |
| Critical Pressure of Methane | P_c | **4.64 MPa (46.0 bar / 673 psia)** |
| Methane Molecular Weight | M | **16.043 g/mol** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conditions Convention:** In KTU-aligned problems, always verify whether the problem uses **STP (0°C, 101.325 kPa)** or **Standard Conditions (15°C, 101.325 kPa)**. The choice significantly affects molar volume calculations: 22.414 m³/kmol at STP versus 23.69 m³/kmol at standard conditions.

## 1.4 Classification of Natural Gas

Natural gas is classified into several categories based on its source, composition, and properties:

- **Conventional Natural Gas:** Free gas trapped in permeable reservoir rock formations at normal pressure-temperature conditions, extractable through standard drilling.
- **Unconventional Natural Gas:** Includes shale gas, tight gas, coalbed methane (CBM), and methane hydrates, requiring advanced extraction techniques.
- **Associated Gas:** Natural gas found in association with crude oil in the reservoir (dissolved in oil or as a gas cap).
- **Non-Associated Gas:** Free gas in reservoirs containing little to no crude oil.
- **Sour Gas:** Natural gas containing significant quantities of **H₂S (≥ 4 ppm)**.
- **Sweet Gas:** Natural gas with very low or no H₂S content.
- **Wet Gas:** Natural gas containing significant amounts of recoverable natural gas liquids (NGLs: ethane, propane, butanes).
