```mermaid
    M3 --> DOC
    M4 --> DOC
    style M1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000000
    style M2 fill:#FFD700,color:#000000
    style M3 fill:#FF8C00,color:#FFFFFF
    style M4 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: List and briefly explain the four key criteria used in the selection of process equipment in oil and natural gas processing facilities.**

**Model Answer:**

The four key criteria are:

1. **Operating Conditions:** Pressure, temperature, and flow rate envelope of the process. Equipment must be rated for the maximum design pressure and temperature with appropriate safety factors (typically 1.1 × design pressure per ASME Sec VIII).

2. **Fluid Properties:** Density, viscosity, corrosiveness, and phase behavior. For instance, highly viscous crudes (> 400 cSt) mandate PD pumps, while sour fluids (H₂S > 0.0003 MPa) require CRAs per NACE MR0175.

3. **Economic Considerations:** CAPEX, OPEX, lifecycle cost, and availability. Centrifugal pumps have lower CAPEX but PD pumps are preferred for high-pressure, low-flow applications.

4. **Safety and Environmental Compliance:** Adherence to API, ASME, NACE, ISO 15156, and EPA norms. Includes flame-proof enclosures, ATEX/IECEx certification for hazardous area classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3M):** 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for explaining each with example (½ mark per criterion).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: A centrifugal pump has NPSHR = 4.5 m. The available suction head conditions are: tank pressure = 110 kPa, vapor pressure = 5 kPa, friction loss = 1.8 m, velocity head = 0.3 m, fluid density = 950 kg/m³. Compute NPSHA and determine whether the pump is safe from cavitation. State the safety margin requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute pressure head at the tank:
$$\frac{P_{tank}}{\rho g} = \frac{110{,}000}{950 \times 9.81} = 11.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute vapor pressure head:
$$\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{5{,}000}{950 \times 9.81} = 0.536 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute NPSHA:
$$NPSH_A = 11.80 - 0.536 - 1.8 - 0.3 = 9.16 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Apply safety margin (typically 0.5 to 1.0 m):
$$NPSH_A - NPSH_R = 9.16 - 4.5 = 4.66 \text{ m} > 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The pump is **safe from cavitation** with a margin of **4.66 m**, which exceeds the standard 1.0 m minimum requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3M):** 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for conclusion with safety margin justification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Design of a Production Separator

**[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice, May 2024]**

**A production train in a Western Indian offshore platform handles 15,000 Sm³/day of associated gas and 3,000 m³/day of liquid (oil 850 kg/m³ + water 1,020 kg/m³). The separator operates at 15 bara and 60 °C. Gas density = 2.2 kg/m³, gas viscosity = 1.5 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. Design droplet diameter is 120 μm.**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the terminal settling velocity of the oil droplet in the gas phase. Justify the selection of a **horizontal** separator for this application.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **minimum gas retention time** and the **minimum vessel length** for a 1.2 m diameter vessel (assume 80% effective gas area). If the calculated vessel length is non-standard, propose a redesign.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Stokes' Law Application**

$$V_t = \frac{g d^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{18 \mu}$$

Given:
- $d = 120 \text{ μm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_L = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_V = 2.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

$$V_t = \frac{9.81 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (850 - 2.2)}{18 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$V_t = \frac{9.81 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-8} \times 847.8}{2.7 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_t = \frac{1.198 \times 10^{-4}}{2.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4437 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Justification for Horizontal Selection**

A horizontal separator is selected because:

1. **High gas–liquid ratio** ($\text{GOR} = 15{,}000/3{,}000 = 5$): Horizontal vessels provide longer gas flow path for separation of entrained droplets.

2. **Three-phase separation required** (oil + water + gas): Horizontal design provides a long interface area between oil and water phases, enhancing water droplet settling.

3. **Slugging flow conditions typical of offshore platforms:** The horizontal gas section dampens inlet slugs and provides smoother downstream operation.

4. **Lower vertical height requirement:** Critical for offshore platforms with limited deck space and weight constraints.

5. **Higher liquid handling capacity:** For the given 3,000 m³/day, a vertical vessel would be impractically tall.

$$\boxed{V_t = 0.444 \text{ m/s; Select HORIZONTAL three-phase separator.}}$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert Gas Flow to Operating Conditions**

Standard: 101.325 kPa, 288.15 K
Operating: 15 bara = 1,500 kPa, 333.15 K

$$V_{op} = Q_{std} \times \frac{P_{std}}{P_{op}} \times \frac{T_{op}}{T_{std}}$$

$$V_{op} = \left(\frac{15{,}000}{86{,}400}\right) \times \frac{101.325}{1500} \times \frac{333.15}{288.15}$$

$$V_{op} = 0.1736 \times 0.06755 \times 1.1562 = 0.01356 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Allowable Gas Velocity**

For mist elimination, gas velocity should be limited to **0.1 × Vt**:

$$V_{gas,allow} = 0.1 \times 0.444 = 0.0444 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Effective Gas Cross-Section**

For D = 1.2 m:
$$A_{total} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4} = 1.131 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{gas,eff} = 0.80 \times 1.131 = 0.9048 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Vessel Length for 60-Second Retention**

$$L_{gas} = \frac{V_{op} \times t}{A_{gas,eff}} = \frac{0.01356 \times 60}{0.9048} = 0.899 \text{ m}$$

Add **liquid section length** for 2 minutes retention:
$$Q_{liq} = 3000/86400 = 0.03472 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$V_{liq} = 0.03472 \times 120 = 4.166 \text{ m}^3$$
$$L_{liq} = 4.166 / (0.20 \times 1.131) = 18.42 \text{ m (excessive!)}$$

**Step 5: Redesign Proposal**

The liquid section length is impractical. **Redesign options:**

- **Option 1:** Increase vessel diameter to D = 2.0 m → $A = 3.14$ m², $L_{liq} = 6.63$ m (acceptable)
- **Option 2:** Add a **pre-separation vessel** upstream
- **Option 3:** Use **internals (weir plates, baffles)** to reduce liquid retention requirement to 1 minute

**Final recommendation:** Select a horizontal vessel with D = 2.0 m, L = 8.0 m (standard size), with internal weir and inlet diverter.

$$\boxed{D = 2.0 \text{ m}, \quad L = 8.0 \text{ m}, \quad V_t = 0.444 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Many students forget to convert gas flow from **standard to operating conditions** using ideal gas law. This single error can shift the answer by 10× and result in a vessel undersized by an order of magnitude. **Always convert before sizing.**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Compressor and Heat Exchanger Selection

**[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice, Dec 2023]**

**A natural gas processing plant compresses methane ($\gamma = 1.31$, $R = 518$ J/kg·K, $Z_{avg} = 0.92$) from 8 bara to 32 bara. Inlet temperature is 40 °C, mass flow rate is 4.0 kg/s. Cooling water available: 25 °C inlet, 60 °C outlet. A gas cooler is required to reduce the discharge temperature to 50 °C before the gas enters an adsorption unit. Assume polytropic compression with $n = 1.32$ and exchanger has one shell pass, two tube passes.**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the type of compressor to be selected. Calculate the polytropic work and actual power assuming 80% isentropic efficiency. Verify the discharge temperature is within the safe limit of 177 °C for single-stage operation.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For the gas cooler, determine the LMTD and correction factor $F_T$. State whether the design is acceptable. Compute the required heat exchanger area if $U = 250$ W/m²·K.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compression Ratio**

$$R_c = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{32}{8} = 4$$

Since **3 < R_c = 4 < 5**, select a **multi-stage centrifugal compressor** (or a 2-stage centrifugal).

**Step 2: Polytropic Work per kg**

$$W_{pol} = \frac{n}{n-1} Z R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

$$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1.32}{0.32} = 4.125$$

$$T_1 = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$$

$$(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} = (4)^{0.32/1.32} = (4)^{0.2424} = 1.5195$$

$$W_{pol} = 4.125 \times 0.92 \times 518 \times 313.15 \times (1.5195 - 1)$$

$$W_{pol} = 4.125 \times 0.92 \times 162{,}171.7 \times 0.5195$$

$$W_{pol} = 3.795 \times 162{,}171.7 \times 0.5195 = 3.795 \times 84{,}240.2 = 319{,}693 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 3: Total Polytropic Power**

$$\dot{W}_{pol} = \dot{m} \times W_{pol} = 4.0 \times 319{,}693 = 1{,}278{,}772 \text{ W} \approx 1279 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Actual Power at 80% Isentropic Efficiency**

First, compute isentropic work for efficiency reference:
$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} Z R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

$$\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} = \frac{1.31}{0.31} = 4.226$$

$$(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (4)^{0.31/1.31} = (4)^{0.2366} = 1.5222$$

$$W_{isen} = 4.226 \times 0.92 \times 518 \times 313.15 \times 0.5222 = 4.226 \times 149{,}030.7 \times 0.5222 = 328{,}998 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\dot{W}_{isen} = 4.0 \times 328{,}998 = 1{,}315{,}993 \text{ W} \approx 1316 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{W}_{actual} = \frac{\dot{W}_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{1316}{0.80} = 1645 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Discharge Temperature Verification**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 313.15 \times 1.5222 = 476.6 \text{ K} = 203.4 \text{ °C}$$

Since **T₂ = 203.4 °C > 177 °C limit** ✗, **multi-staging with intercooling is mandatory**.

**Recommendation:** A **2-stage centrifugal compressor with intercooler** is required. The intercooler reduces temperature between stages to approximately 40 °C, bringing the final discharge well within limits.

$$\boxed{\text{2-stage centrifugal with intercooler}; \quad \dot{W}_{actual} = 1645 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** Gas enters cooler at **T₂ = 203.4 °C**, leaves at **T_g,out = 50 °C** (assumption — actual would be after intercooling). Water enters at 25 °C, leaves at 60 °C.

**Step 1: Identify Temperature Differences**

- $\Delta T_1 = T_{g,in} - T_{w,out} = 203.4 - 60 = 143.4$ °C
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{g,out} - T_{w,in} = 50 - 25 = 25$ °C

**Step 2: LMTD**

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{143.4 - 25}{\ln(143.4/25)} = \frac{118.4}{\ln(5.736)} = \frac{118.4}{1.7460} = 67.81 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3: Correction Factor Parameters**

$$S = \frac{T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}}{T_{g,in} - T_{w,in}} = \frac{60 - 25}{203.4 - 25} = \frac{35}{178.4} = 0.1962$$

$$R = \frac{T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}}{T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}} = \frac{203.4 - 50}{60 - 25} = \frac{153.4}{35} = 4.383$$

**Step 4: Compute $F_T$**

$$\sqrt{R^2 + 1} = \sqrt{19.21 + 1} = \sqrt{20.21} = 4.496$$

$$1 - RS = 1 - 4.383 \times 0.1962 = 1 - 0.860 = 0.140$$

$$1 - S = 0.804$$

$$\ln(0.804/0.140) = \ln(5.743) = 1.7472$$

Numerator: $4.496 \times 1.7472 = 7.857$

Denominator:
$$R - 1 = 3.383$$

Denominator argument:
$$R + 1 - \sqrt{R^2 + 1} = 4.383 + 1 - 4.496 = 0.887$$
$$R + 1 + \sqrt{R^2 + 1} = 4.383 + 1 + 4.496 = 9.879$$

$$2 - S \times 0.887 = 2 - 0.1962 \times 0.887 = 2 - 0.174 = 1.826$$
$$2 - S \times 9.879 = 2 - 0.1962 \times 9.879 = 2 - 1.938 = 0.062$$

$$\ln(1.826/0.062) = \ln(29.45) = 3.383$$

Denominator: $3.383 \times 3.383 = 11.448$

$$F_T = \frac{7.857}{11.448} = 0.686$$

**Step 5: Acceptability Check**

Since **F_T = 0.686 < 0.75** ✗, a **single 1-2 shell-and-tube exchanger is NOT acceptable**.

**Step 6: Heat Duty and Area Calculation**

Heat duty:
$$Q = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{gas}$$

For methane, $C_p \approx 2.5$ kJ/kg·K:
$$Q = 4.0 \times 2500 \times 153.4 = 1{,}534{,}000 \text{ W} = 1534 \text{ kW}$$

Area (using corrected MTD, even though design is non-ideal):
$$\Delta T_{m,\text{corr}} = 0.686 \times 67.81 = 46.52 \text{ °C}$$

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{m,\text{corr}}} = \frac{1{,}534{,}000}{250 \times 46.52} = 131.9 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7: Redesign Recommendation**

Since F_T < 0.75, redesign using:
- **Two shells in series** (each with F_T > 0.9 individually), or
- **Plate heat exchanger** (typically achieves F_T ≈ 0.95), or
- **Air-cooled fin-fan exchanger** (eliminates water requirement)

For the **series-staged two-shell** option, the required area per shell is approximately **66 m²** each, leading to a much more compact and reliable design.

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = 67.8 \text{ °C}; \quad F_T = 0.686 < 0.75 \Rightarrow \text{Series-staging required}; \quad A_{req} = 132 \text{ m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Z-factor:** Real natural gas is not ideal. Always multiply R by Z in compressor work equations. (~2 marks lost if omitted)
> 2. **Confusing polytropic with isentropic exponents:** $n$ (polytropic) and $\gamma$ (isentropic) are different; using the wrong one gives incorrect T₂.
> 3. **Ignoring the 177 °C auto-ignition limit:** Students often compute power without checking T₂, missing a critical safety question.
> 4. **Not using corrected MTD:** Using $\Delta T_{lm}$ without $F_T$ correction overstates the heat exchanger's actual performance.
> 5. **Improper F_T formula selection:** Using 1-1 formula instead of 1-2 formula for shell-and-tube with two tube passes leads to wrong correction factor.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Selection**

### Separator Selection
- ✅ Stokes' Law: $V_t \propto d^2$ (droplet size dominates settling)
- ✅ Horizontal for high GOR, three-phase, slugging
- ✅ Vertical for low GOR, sand handling, test service
- ✅ Retention time: oil = 1–3 min, gas = 1–2 min

### Pump Selection
- ✅ Centrifugal for low viscosity, high flow, low-to-moderate pressure
- ✅ PD for high viscosity, low flow, high pressure
- ✅ Always check **NPSHₐ ≥ NPSHᵣ + 0.5 to 1.0 m**
- ✅ Specific speed $N_s$ guides impeller type selection
- ✅ Affinity laws for variable speed: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$

### Compressor Selection
- ✅ Centrifugal for $R_c < 3$; multi-stage for $R_c = 3$–$5$; reciprocating for $R_c > 5$
- ✅ Discharge temperature limit **T₂ ≤ 177 °C (350 °F)**
- ✅ Use $W_{pol} = \frac{n}{n-1} ZRT_1 [(R_c)^{(n-1)/n} - 1]$ for real gas
- ✅ Isentropic efficiency $\eta = W_{isen}/W_{actual}$
- ✅ Multi-staging with intercooling when $R_c > 3$ or $T_2$ exceeds limit

### Heat Exchanger Selection
- ✅ LMTD = $(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$
- ✅ $F_T \geq 0.75$ mandatory (HEI guideline)
- ✅ Use **corrected MTD** in $Q = U A F_T \Delta T_{lm}$
- ✅ If $F_T < 0.75$: series-staging, plate HX, or air-cooled
- ✅ $U$ is service-specific (10 to 5000 W/m²·K)

### Material Selection (NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156)
- ✅ Carbon steel valid only for PH₂S < **0.0003 MPa** and T < **65 °C**
- ✅ 13Cr for CO₂ + trace H₂S (T < 175 °C)
- ✅ 22Cr/25Cr Duplex for moderate sour service
- ✅ Alloy 625 for severe sour / unrestricted PH₂S

### Numerical Constants to Memorize
- ✅ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ✅ $R_{universal} = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- ✅ $R_{methane} = 518$ J/(kg·K)
- ✅ $\gamma_{methane} = 1.31$
- ✅ $T_{auto-ignition} = 177$ °C (350 °F)
- ✅ $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 14.696 psia
- ✅ $F_T$ threshold = 0.75

### Key Selection Logic Mantra
> *"Define the operating envelope → characterize the fluid → apply the appropriate equation → verify constraints (NPSH, T₂, F_T) → match to equipment type → document the justification."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recording Charts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recording Charts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Recording Charts** in the context of oil and natural gas drilling are the calibrated, time-based or depth-based graphical documents produced by surface and downhole sensors that permanently capture the dynamic operational parameters of a well during the drilling process. These charts serve as the primary legal, engineering, and geological record of the wellbore's history and form the foundation for operational decision-making, formation evaluation, and post-well analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT522 Module 4):**
> Recording charts are physical or digital output media (paper strips, log prints, or digital curves) generated by instruments such as the **Martin Decker Drilling Recorder**, **mud logging units (MLU)**, and **wireline logging tools (SP, GR, Resistivity)** to continuously log parameters like **Rate of Penetration (ROP), Weight on Bit (WOB), Rotary Speed (RPM), Hook Load, Mud Flow Rate (gal/min), Mud Weight (ppg), and Total Gas (units)** against depth or time.

### Types of Recording Charts Used in the Field

| S.No. | Chart Type | Primary Function | Key Recording Medium |
|:-----:|:-----------|:-----------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Drilling Recorder Chart (Martin Decker)** | Monitors mechanical drilling parameters | Strip chart paper (rectilinear) |
| 2 | **Mud Log (Formation Log)** | Records geological & hydrocarbon data | Depth-based log printout |
| 3 | **Gas Chromatograph Chart** | Detects hydrocarbon gas shows | Roll chart paper |
| 4 | **Pressure Chart (Drill Stem Test)** | Records BHP & pressure buildup | Circular or strip chart |
| 5 | **Wireline Well Log (e-log)** | Records petrophysical properties | Depth-based log curves |
| 6 | **Cement Bond Log (CBL)** | Evaluates cement sheath quality | Depth-based log |
| 7 | **Deviation Survey Chart** | Records wellbore inclination | Plotted as vertical section |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Black Box" of Drilling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Airplane Black Box of a Drilling Rig"**
> Imagine you are flying a commercial airplane from Kochi to Delhi. The cockpit has dozens of dials — altitude, airspeed, fuel, engine temperature. None of these dials store information after the flight. That is why every airplane carries a **Flight Data Recorder (Black Box)** — it permanently stores every parameter on a continuous tape so investigators can replay the flight second-by-second if something goes wrong.
> 
> A **recording chart does exactly the same thing for a drilling rig**. Every parameter — how fast the bit is drilling (ROP), how hard the bit is being pushed (WOB), how much the rig is pulling (Hook Load), and what gases are emerging from the well (Total Gas) — is continuously drawn on a paper chart or stored as a digital curve. If the well kicks, blows out, or hits a high-pressure zone, engineers can roll back the chart and reconstruct the event second-by-second. **The chart is the legal, operational, and forensic memory of the well.**

### Why Recording Charts Are Critical — The Five Engineering Purposes

1. **Safety (Kick Detection):** A sudden rise in the gas curve or drop in hook load is the first warning of a kick or wellbore instability.
2. **Operational Optimization:** Cross-plotting ROP vs. WOB on the drilling recorder helps the driller identify the optimum drilling parameters.
3. **Formation Evaluation:** Mud logs and wireline logs are the foundation of deciding whether a zone is producible hydrocarbon.
4. **Legal Documentation:** Charts are admissible as legal evidence in court for disputes between operator, contractor, and service company.
5. **Post-Well Analysis & Reservoir Characterization:** Charts feed into geological models, drilling performance reports (DPR), and PVT analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:**
> - **Drilling depth is always recorded in feet (ft) or meters (m).** KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **ft as the primary unit** since most Indian operators (ONGC, OIL India) historically use API standards.
> - **Time is recorded in minutes or hours of rotation.**
> - **Gas units are recorded in ppm or percentage of hydrocarbon.**
> - **Mud weight is recorded in pounds per gallon (ppg)** or specific gravity (SG).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Drilling Recorder (Martin Decker) — Operational Block

The **Martin Decker Drilling Recorder** is the most iconic recording chart in petroleum engineering. It is a **multi-pen, rectilinear strip chart recorder** installed on the rig floor that simultaneously traces six to eight critical drilling parameters against **time** (not depth) on a single continuous roll of paper.

**Six Standard Parameters Recorded:**

1. **Weight on Bit (WOB)** — measured in **klbs (1000 lbs)** using a hydraulic pressure sensor in the dead line anchor.
2. **Rotary Speed (RPM)** — measured in **revolutions per minute** using a tachogenerator on the rotary table.
3. **Rate of Penetration (ROP)** — measured in **ft/hr** using a crown block motion sensor or hook position encoder.
4. **Hook Load** — measured in **klbs** using a hydraulic load cell on the deadline.
5. **Standpipe Pressure (SPP)** — measured in **psi** using a pressure transducer on the standpipe.
6. **Torque on the Kelly / Rotary Table** — measured in **ft-lbs** or **amps** (for electric drives).

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The recorder works on a **null-balance potentiometric principle**:

> **Why this principle?** — Drilling parameters swing rapidly (e.g., ROP can jump from 5 ft/hr to 80 ft/hr in seconds when a porous sand is hit). A simple voltmeter-style pen would lag. A null-balance system continuously adjusts a sliding contact on a precision resistor until the input signal matches a reference voltage, giving **near-zero mechanical inertia** and **high-fidelity response**.

**Logical Steps:**

1. **Sensor Transduction:** Each physical parameter (WOB, RPM, ROP, etc.) is converted by its dedicated transducer into a low-level electrical signal (typically 0–5 V DC or 4–20 mA).
2. **Signal Conditioning:** The signal is amplified, filtered, and linearized in the recorder's chassis.
3. **Null-Balance Comparison:** The conditioned signal is compared with an internal reference voltage in a Wheatstone bridge.
4. **Servo Motor Actuation:** A servo motor drives the pen carriage until the bridge is balanced (null condition). The pen position is therefore **directly proportional to the input parameter**.
5. **Chart Advancement:** A precision stepping motor advances the chart paper at a constant rate (typically **1 inch = 30 minutes** or **2 inches = 1 hour**).
6. **Time Marking:** A separate event marker draws a vertical pips line every minute, and an "hour tick" at every hour, allowing time reconstruction.

### 2.3 The Mud Log — Geological Recording Chart

The **Mud Logging Unit (MLU)** produces a **depth-based recording chart** called the **mud log** or **geological log**. Unlike the Martin Decker (which is time-based), the mud log's horizontal axis is **measured depth in feet**, marked every 10 ft, and the vertical axis is divided into **time and lithology tracks**.

**Key Tracks on a Mud Log:**

| Track | Content | Sensor / Source |
|:------|:--------|:----------------|
| **Depth Track** | Measured Depth (MD) & True Vertical Depth (TVD) | Geolograph / hook position sensor |
| **ROP Track** | Penetration rate vs. depth | Mechanical timer on the drilling line |
| **Lithology Track** | Rock type (sandstone, shale, limestone) | Microscopic cutting examination by geologist |
| **Gas Track (Total Gas)** | Total hydrocarbon gas (in ppm or %) | Hot-wire or infrared gas detector in the mud return line |
| **Chromatograph Track** | C₁ (Methane), C₂ (Ethane), C₃ (Propane), iC₄, nC₄, iC₅, nC₅ | Gas chromatograph |
| **Mud Weight & Flow Track** | MW (ppg), Flow Rate (gpm) | Mud engineer sensors |
| **Temperature Track** | Mud temperature in/out (°F) | RTD sensors on flow line |
| **Show Description** | Free oil staining, fluorescence, cut fluorescence | Visual & UV light examination |
| **Engineering Track** | WOB, RPM, SPP, Torque, Hook Load | Mirrored from Martin Decker |
| **Remarks / Events** | Bit trips, connection gases, lost circulation | Hand-written by mud logger |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection Gas & Background Gas — The Two Critical Baseline Readings:**
> - **Background Gas (BG):** The steady, low-level gas reading that persists during normal drilling. It represents the **formation background hydrocarbon content**. A typical BG is **50–500 ppm** in a non-reservoir section.
> - **Connection Gas:** A sharp spike in total gas observed when the driller stops the pumps to make a drill string connection. As pressure drops, formation gas **exhales** into the wellbore. Connection gas magnitude = **reservoir productivity indicator**. **C₁/C₂ ratio > 100 suggests dry gas; ratio < 50 suggests oil-prone.**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|:-----:|:--------|:------------|:---------------|
| 1 | $ROP = \dfrac{\Delta D}{\Delta t}$ | Rate of Penetration — change in depth per unit time | $\text{ft/hr}$ |
| 2 | $WOB = \dfrac{P_{hyd} \times A_{piston}}{N_{lines}}$ | Weight on Bit — hydraulic pressure × piston area / number of fast lines | $\text{klbs}$ |
| 3 | $HL = (W_{string} \times g) - F_{buoyancy}$ | Hook Load = string weight minus buoyancy | $\text{klbs}$ |
| 4 | $SPP = P_{friction} + P_{hydrostatic} + P_{bit}$ | Standpipe Pressure = friction + hydrostatic + bit pressure drop | $\text{psi}$ |
| 5 | $P_{hydrostatic} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$ | Hydrostatic mud pressure | $\text{psi}$ |
| 6 | $MW = \dfrac{\rho_{mud}}{8.33}$ | Mud Weight conversion from lb/ft³ to ppg | $\text{ppg}$ |
| 7 | $Q = A \times v$ | Mud Flow Rate = flow line area × velocity | $\text{gal/min (gpm)}$ |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{P \times D}{2}$ | Rotary Torque (approximate) — pressure × diameter / 2 | $\text{ft-lbs}$ |
| 9 | $d_{exponent} = \dfrac{\log(\frac{ROP}{60 \times RPM})}{\log(\frac{12 \times WOB}{10^6 \times D_{bit}})}$ | d-exponent — normalization of ROP for compaction trend | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_1/C_2 = \dfrac{\%\,CH_4}{\%\,C_2H_6}$ | Gas wetness ratio (low = oil, high = dry gas) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_{c} = D_{bit} \times \sqrt{\frac{ROP_{norm}}{ROP_{act}}}$ | Corrected d-exponent (for mud weight & flow) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $V_{annular} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{annulus}} = \dfrac{Q}{\frac{\pi}{4}(D_{hole}^2 - D_{pipe}^2)}$ | Annular velocity | $\text{ft/min}$ |
| 13 | $ECD = MW + \dfrac{P_{friction}}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | Equivalent Circulating Density | $\text{ppg}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Note — Vertical Pipe Symbol Restriction:**
> When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in the table, always use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Example: the absolute hook-load swing is $\vert \Delta HL \vert \le 10\%\,HL_{static}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drilling Optimization (D-exponent):** Plotting d-exponent vs. depth allows real-time identification of **overpressure zones** before a kick occurs. A **sharp leftward deviation** in d-exponent = abnormal pore pressure = **kick warning**.
- **Cross-Plotting Method:** On a Martin Decker chart, plotting **ROP vs. WOB** at a fixed RPM produces a "drillability curve." The point of maximum curvature is the **optimum WOB** for that bit/formation combination.
- **Pore Pressure Prediction:** The d-exponent vs. depth trend line is the basis of the **Rehm and McClendon normal compaction trend**, from which abnormal pressures are detected.
- **Reservoir Identification:** Gas peaks on the mud log that correspond to increased ROP and oil-stained cuttings are **direct hydrocarbon indicators (DHI)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the d-exponent (Rehm-McClendon Method)

The **d-exponent** is a normalized rate of penetration (ROP) parameter used to detect abnormal formation pore pressures while drilling. The full derivation proceeds in three stages.

**Stage 1 — Bingham's ROP Equation (Empirical Starting Point):**

Bingham's empirical equation relates ROP to WOB, RPM, and bit diameter:

$$
ROP = K \times \left(\dfrac{WOB}{D_{bit}}\right)^{a_1} \times \left(\dfrac{RPM \times 60}{D_{bit}}\right)^{a_2}
$$

where $K$ is a formation strength constant, $a_1$ and $a_2$ are lithology exponents, and $D_{bit}$ is the bit diameter in inches.

**Stage 2 — Solving for the d-exponent:**

Take the logarithm of both sides:

$$
\log(ROP) = \log K + a_1 \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{WOB}{D_{bit}}\right) + a_2 \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{60 \times RPM}{D_{bit}}\right)
$$

By definition, the **d-exponent** is the term representing the composite exponent of the right-hand expression:

$$
d = \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60 \times RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12 \times WOB}{10^6 \times D_{bit}}\right)}
$$

**Stage 3 — Substitution and Simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60\,RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12 \times WOB}{10^6 \times D_{bit}}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\log(ROP) - \log(60\,RPM)}{\log(12\,WOB) - \log(10^6\,D_{bit})} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\log(ROP) - \log(60) - \log(RPM)}{\log(12) + \log(WOB) - 6 - \log(D_{bit})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\,d = \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60\,RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12\,WOB}{10^6\,D_{bit}}\right)}\,}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $d$ is dimensionless.
- In a **normally compacted shale**, $d$ increases linearly with depth (log-linear trend).
- A **sudden decrease** in $d$ indicates either **faster penetration** (softer rock) or **abnormal pore pressure**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Corrected d-exponent (dc-exponent)

The original d-exponent does not compensate for changes in mud weight and flow rate, both of which affect ROP. The **Rehm and McClendon (1971)** correction introduces a normalization factor:

$$
d_c = d \times \dfrac{N_m}{N_c}
$$

where $N_m$ is the **actual mud weight (ppg)** and $N_c$ is the **normal mud weight at that depth (ppg)** predicted by the normal compaction trend.

**Expanded final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,d_c = d \times \dfrac{N_{mud,actual}}{N_{mud,normal}}\,}
$$

**Detailed Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_c &= \left[\dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60\,RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12\,WOB}{10^6\,D_{bit}}\right)}\right] \times \dfrac{MW_{actual}}{MW_{normal}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{MW_{actual}}{MW_{normal}} \times \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60\,RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12\,WOB}{10^6\,D_{bit}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Use:** When $d_c$ vs. depth is plotted, the slope of the normal trend line gives the **normal compaction gradient**. Departure of $d_c$ from this trend is then converted to **equivalent mud weight (EMW)** using:

$$
EMW = MW_{actual} - \left(\dfrac{d_{c,trend} - d_{c,actual}}{d_{c,trend}}\right) \times MW_{actual}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:**
A well is being drilled with the following parameters at 10,000 ft MD:
- $ROP = 15\,ft/hr$
- $RPM = 120$
- $WOB = 25{,}000\,lbs$
- $D_{bit} = 8.5\,in$
- $MW_{actual} = 9.5\,ppg$
- $MW_{normal} = 8.8\,ppg$

**Calculate (a) the d-exponent, (b) the corrected d-exponent $d_c$, and (c) the equivalent mud weight if the normal trend predicts $d_{c,trend} = 1.45$.**

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — d-exponent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60 \times RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12 \times WOB}{10^6 \times D_{bit}}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{15}{60 \times 120}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12 \times 25{,}000}{10^6 \times 8.5}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{15}{7200}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{300{,}000}{8.5 \times 10^6}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\log(0.002083)}{\log(0.03529)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{-2.6812}{-1.4522} \\[6pt]
&= 1.846
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Corrected d-exponent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_c &= d \times \dfrac{MW_{actual}}{MW_{normal}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.846 \times \dfrac{9.5}{8.8} \\[4pt]
&= 1.846 \times 1.0795 \\[4pt]
&= 1.993
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Equivalent Mud Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMW &= MW_{actual} - \left(\dfrac{d_{c,trend} - d_{c,actual}}{d_{c,trend}}\right) \times MW_{actual} \\[4pt]
&= 9.5 - \left(\dfrac{1.45 - 1.993}{1.45}\right) \times 9.5 \\[4pt]
&= 9.5 - \left(\dfrac{-0.543}{1.45}\right) \times 9.5 \\[4pt]
&= 9.5 - (-0.3745 \times 9.5) \\[4pt]
&= 9.5 + 3.558 \\[4pt]
&= 13.058\,ppg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The formation pore pressure is equivalent to **13.06 ppg mud column**, which is **above** the actual mud weight of 9.5 ppg. **The well is underbalanced and at kick risk.** Engineer should increase mud weight to at least 13.1 ppg to balance the formation.

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Module 4 Choice Option A*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the operating principle of a Martin Decker drilling recorder with a neat functional block diagram. Explain how the null-balance principle enables high-fidelity recording of rapidly changing parameters such as Rate of Penetration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A well is being drilled with the following parameters at 9,500 ft MD: $ROP = 12\,ft/hr$, $RPM = 110$, $WOB = 28{,}000\,lbs$, $D_{bit} = 8.5\,in$, $MW_{actual} = 10.0\,ppg$, $MW_{normal} = 9.0\,ppg$. The normal $d_c$ trend at this depth is **1.50**. Calculate the d-exponent, the corrected d-exponent $d_c$, and the Equivalent Mud Weight (EMW). Comment on the kick risk.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Operating Principle [7 marks]:**

The Martin Decker recorder operates on the **null-balance potentiometric principle** in six stages:

1. **Transduction [1 mark]:** Each parameter (WOB, RPM, ROP, Hook Load, SPP, Torque) is sensed by a dedicated transducer that converts the physical variable to a low-level electrical signal (0–5 V DC or 4–20 mA).

2. **Signal Conditioning [1 mark]:** The signal passes through amplification, linearization, and noise filtering circuits.

3. **Null-Balance Comparison [2 marks]:** The conditioned signal is fed into one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. A precision servo-driven slide-wire resistor forms the opposing arm. A comparator continuously adjusts the slide-wire until the bridge is at null (zero differential). This **eliminates the inertia of the pen** because the slide-wire does the moving, not the pen directly.

4. **Servo Motor Actuation [1 mark]:** A two-phase AC servo motor drives both the slide-wire contact and the pen carriage simultaneously. The pen position is **directly proportional** to the input signal magnitude.

5. **Chart Advancement [1 mark]:** A stepping motor feeds the strip chart paper at a calibrated constant rate (typically 1 inch = 30 minutes).

6. **Time Marking & Annotations [1 mark]:** A separate event marker pen draws a **vertical pips line** every minute and a **larger pip** every hour, allowing time reconstruction after the fact.

**Why null-balance is essential for ROP:**
ROP can change from 5 ft/hr to 80 ft/hr in 2 seconds when entering a porous sand. A direct deflection (D'Arsonval) galvanometer pen would **overshoot and oscillate** due to mechanical inertia. The null-balance system has effectively **zero inertia** because the slide-wire does the work, allowing **step-function response** with negligible lag.

---

**Part (b) — Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
$ROP = 12\,ft/hr$, $RPM = 110$, $WOB = 28{,}000\,lbs$, $D_{bit} = 8.5\,in$, $MW_{actual} = 10.0\,ppg$, $MW_{normal} = 9.0\,ppg$, $d_{c,trend} = 1.50$.

**Step 1 — Compute d-exponent [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{ROP}{60 \times RPM}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{12 \times WOB}{10^6 \times D_{bit}}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\log\left(\dfrac{12}{6600}\right)}{\log\left(\dfrac{336{,}000}{8.5 \times 10^6}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\log(0.001818)}{\log(0.03953)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{-2.7404}{-1.4030} \\[4pt]
&= 1.953
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute corrected d-exponent [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_c &= d \times \dfrac{MW_{actual}}{MW_{normal}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.953 \times \dfrac{10.0}{9.0} \\[4pt]
&= 1.953 \times 1.1111 \\[4pt]
&= 2.170
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Mud Weight [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMW &= MW_{actual} - \left(\dfrac{d_{c,trend} - d_{c,actual}}{d_{c,trend}}\right) \times MW_{actual} \\[4pt]
&= 10.0 - \left(\dfrac{1.50 - 2.170}{1.50}\right) \times 10.0 \\[4pt]
&= 10.0 - (-0.4467 \times 10.0) \\[4pt]
&= 10.0 + 4.467 \\[4pt]
&= 14.467\,ppg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Kick Risk [1 mark]:**
The formation pore pressure (14.47 ppg) is **far higher** than the actual mud weight (10.0 ppg). The well is **severely underbalanced**. **Kick risk is extremely high**; mud weight must be raised to at least 14.5 ppg to balance the formation before drilling ahead.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Module 4 Choice Option B*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw a labelled mud log showing the major tracks and describe the function of each track. Explain how **background gas** and **connection gas** are interpreted in formation evaluation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **d-exponent method** of pore pressure detection. Plot a typical d-exponent vs. depth curve and mark (i) normal compaction trend, (ii) transition zone, and (iii) abnormal pressure zone.

**Model Solution Outline (student to elaborate):**

**Part (a) — Mud Log Tracks [7 marks]:**

A mud log is a multi-track chart with **depth (ft) on the horizontal axis**. The standard tracks are:

1. **Depth Track [1 mark]:** MD and TVD markings every 10 ft; total depth (TD) marker.
2. **ROP Track [0.5 mark]:** Pen trace in ft/hr; sharp increases = porous zones.
3. **Lithology Track [1 mark]:** Graphical symbols + written description (sandstone, shale, limestone, dolomite).
4. **Gas Track — Total Gas [1 mark]:** Hot-wire detector signal in ppm or %; baseline = BG, peaks = shows.
5. **Chromatograph Track [0.5 mark]:** C₁, C₂, C₃, iC₄, nC₄ individual concentrations.
6. **Engineering Track [0.5 mark]:** WOB, RPM, SPP, Torque (mirrored from Martin Decker).
7. **Mud Weight & Flow Track [0.5 mark]:** MW (ppg) and Flow Out (gpm).
8. **Show Description [0.5 mark]:** Handwritten or printed free oil, fluorescence, cut fluorescence notes.
9. **Remarks/Events [0.5 mark]:** Bit trips, connections, mud losses, etc.
10. **Temperature Track [0.5 mark]:** Mud temperature in vs. out — flags gas expansion.
11. **C₁/C₂ Ratio & d-exponent [0.5 mark]:** Computed petrophysical indicators.

**Background Gas & Connection Gas Interpretation [included in 7 marks]:**

- **Background Gas (BG):** Steady, low-level gas during normal drilling. Indicates **in-situ hydrocarbon saturation** in the rock being drilled. Typical BG: 50–500 ppm.
- **Connection Gas:** Sharp gas spike when the driller stops the rotary to make a connection. As annular pressure drops, **formation gas exhales** into the wellbore. The **magnitude of connection gas** is a **direct indicator of reservoir permeability and productivity**. Large connection gas (>5× BG) = good reservoir.

---

**Part (b) — d-exponent Curve [7 marks]:**

```
 d-exponent (vertical axis)
  |
 2.0 -                                (i) NORMAL TREND
      |                            /
 1.8 -                          /
      |                        /
 1.6 -                      /   
      |                    /  ← (ii) TRANSITION ZONE
 1.4 -                  /
      |                /      (iii) ABNORMAL PRESSURE ZONE
 1.2 -              /←←←← ←←← ←← ←←
      |          /  
 1.0 -        /
      |      /
 0.8 -    /
      |  /
 0.6 -
      |________________________________________
         5000     7000     9000   11000  13000   Depth (ft)
```

- **(i) Normal Compaction Trend [2 marks]:** Log-linear increase in d with depth due to increasing compaction of shale.
- **(ii) Transition Zone [2 marks]:** Curvature change as the d-exponent begins deviating from the trend.
- **(iii) Abnormal Pressure Zone [2 marks]:** Sharp leftward deviation = decrease in d = undercompacted shale = **abnormally high pore pressure**. Immediate kick risk.
- **Pore pressure from d-exponent [1 mark]:** $P_{pore} = \text{EMW} \times 0.052 \times TVD$ where EMW is calculated from the d-exponent deviation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark each instance):** Students often mix **klbs and lbs**, **ppg and SG**, or use **ROP in m/hr** when the formula expects **ft/hr**. Always write the unit next to every numerical substitution.
> 2. **Logarithm Base Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** d-exponent uses **base-10 logarithm**, not natural log. Writing $\ln$ instead of $\log$ is a common error.
> 3. **Sign Error in EMW Equation (–2 marks):** The formula has a **negative sign** in front of the deviation term. A $d_c$ that is **lower** than the trend gives a **negative deviation** which, when subtracted, gives a **positive increment** to mud weight. Reversing this sign gives a wrong (and dangerous) result.
> 4. **Confusing Time-Based vs Depth-Based Charts (–1 mark):** Martin Decker = time axis; Mud Log = depth axis. Many students answer "time" for both, losing the discrimination mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert to Consistent Units in the Bingham Equation (–1 mark):** The constants 60, 12, and 10⁶ are unit-conversion factors (min to hr, klbs to lbs, in to ft). Omitting the WOB in lbs gives an error of 1000×.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Recording Chart** = the permanent, time-based or depth-based graphical record of drilling parameters and geological observations.
- ✅ **Three Major Charts in Oil & Gas Engineering:**
  1. **Martin Decker Drilling Recorder** (time-based, mechanical parameters)
  2. **Mud Log** (depth-based, geological + engineering)
  3. **Wireline Well Log** (depth-based, petrophysical)
- ✅ **Six Standard Parameters on Martin Decker:** WOB (klbs), RPM, ROP (ft/hr), Hook Load (klbs), SPP (psi), Torque (ft-lbs).
- ✅ **Operating Principle:** Null-balance potentiometric — **zero inertia** for step-function response.
- ✅ **Mud Log Tracks:** Depth, ROP, Lithology, Total Gas, Chromatograph, MW, Flow, Temp, Engineering, Remarks.
- ✅ **Background Gas (BG):** Steady, low-level gas indicating in-situ hydrocarbon content.
- ✅ **Connection Gas:** Sharp spike on round-trip or connection = **reservoir permeability indicator**.
- ✅ **C₁/C₂ Ratio:** < 50 = oil-prone, > 100 = dry gas.
- ✅ **d-exponent Formula:** $d = \dfrac{\log(ROP/60\,RPM)}{\log(12\,WOB/10^6\,D_{bit})}$ — **base-10 logarithm only**.
- ✅ **Corrected d-exponent:** $d_c = d \times (MW_{actual}/MW_{normal})$.
- ✅ **EMW Formula:** $EMW = MW_{actual} - \left(\dfrac{d_{c,trend} - d_{c,actual}}{d_{c,trend}}\right) \times MW_{actual}$.
- ✅ **Normal Trend:** Log-linear increase of d with depth; **leftward deviation = abnormal pore pressure**.
- ✅ **Standard Units to Memorize:** ROP in **ft/hr**, WOB in **klbs**, MW in **ppg**, SPP in **psi**, HL in **klbs**, gas in **ppm or %**.
- ✅ **Engineering Use of Charts:** Safety (kick detection), optimization (drillability), formation evaluation, legal record, post-well reservoir characterization.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** Always draw a labelled block diagram in the operating-principle question (carries **2 marks** separately in the marking scheme).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uncertainties in Flow
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uncertainties in Flow

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measurement Uncertainty** in oil and natural gas flow is formally defined (per **ISO/IEC Guide 98-3 (GUM)** and **API MPMS Chapter 13** adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus) as a *parameter associated with the result of a measurement that characterizes the dispersion of the values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand*. In petroleum engineering, this translates to the quantified doubt about the true volumetric or mass flow rate of crude oil, natural gas, or multiphase mixtures at custody transfer points, wellheads, or process facilities.

The **measurand** is the particular quantity subject to measurement (e.g., volume flow rate $Q$ in $\text{Sm}^3/\text{day}$), while the **uncertainty** is the interval around the reported value within which the true value is expected to lie with a stated probability (commonly **95% confidence level**).

### Classification of Uncertainty in Oil & Gas Flow

| Type | Definition | Example in Flow Measurement |
|------|------------|-----------------------------|
| **Standard Uncertainty $u(x)$** | Uncertainty expressed as one standard deviation | Repeatability of orifice meter reading |
| **Combined Standard Uncertainty $u_c(y)$** | Standard uncertainty of a result obtained from other quantities | Total flow rate computed from DP, $\rho$, $C_d$ |
| **Expanded Uncertainty $U$** | $U = k \cdot u_c$, where $k$ is the coverage factor | Reported as $\pm 0.5\%$ at $k=2$ |
| **Systematic Error** | Reproducible error from calibration bias | Orifice bore diameter offset |
| **Random Error** | Statistical fluctuation, reducible by averaging | Turbulence-induced noise in DP signal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must distinguish between **error** (deviation from true value) and **uncertainty** (doubt about the result). A measurement can have a small error but large uncertainty, or vice versa.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are filling a tanker truck with crude oil at a lease automatic custody transfer (LACT) unit. The meter says **1000.0 barrels**. But the truck driver and the producer both want to know: *"Is it really 1000 barrels? How much could it be off?"*

**Uncertainty in flow** is the engineering answer to that question. It is the honest "fence of doubt" around the measured number.

> **Analogy – The Hitting-the-Target Story:**
> - **Systematic error** = The rifle scope is misaligned. Every shot lands 2 inches to the left. (Bias – can be calibrated out.)
> - **Random error** = The shooter's hands shake. Shots scatter randomly. (Noise – reduced by repeated measurement.)
> - **Uncertainty** = The size of the cluster of bullet holes, *plus* the residual misalignment that calibration could not fully remove.
> - **Expanded uncertainty (k=2)** = Aiming a "fence" so wide that it captures ~95% of all possible truth.

In oil and gas, even a **0.1% uncertainty** in a gas pipeline carrying **100 MMSCMD** translates to **100,000 SCM/day** of disputed gas — worth millions of rupees per month. This is why uncertainty quantification is a mandatory part of every custody transfer contract under **API MPMS** and **ISO 5167**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standards Bodies for Flow Uncertainty:**
> - **GUM** (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) — ISO/IEC Guide 98-3
> - **ISO 5167** — Differential pressure flowmeters (orifice, Venturi, nozzle)
> - **API MPMS Ch. 13** — Statistical aspects of metering
> - **AGA Report No. 9** — Measurement of gas by multiphase flowmeters
> - **ISO 17089** — Ultrasonic transit-time gas meters
> - **Coverage factor $k$**: typically **2** for 95% confidence (normal distribution)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **coverage factor $k$** when reporting expanded uncertainty. A bare number without $k$ is incomplete and will be marked down.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Uncertainty in Oil & Gas Flow

The measurand (flow rate $Q$) is rarely measured directly. It is **calculated from several measured or assumed quantities**, each of which carries its own uncertainty. The total uncertainty is a *combination* of all these contributions.

### 2.1.1 Primary Sources (in order of typical magnitude)

1. **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter Uncertainty**
   - Reading the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across an orifice plate.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.1\%$ of calibrated span.
   - Major contributor because flow $\propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ — small errors get amplified.

2. **Pipe Bore Diameter $D$ Uncertainty**
   - Measured at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and corrected for operating temperature.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.2\%$.
   - Enters as $D^2$ in mass flow.

3. **Orifice Bore Diameter $d$ Uncertainty**
   - Critical because flow $\propto d^2$.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.1\%$.
   - Doubling the relative error of $d$ effectively squares its contribution.

4. **Discharge Coefficient $C_d$ Uncertainty**
   - Empirical; from ISO 5167-2 correlations (Stahl, Reader-Harris/Gallagher, or ISO 2003).
   - Typical: $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 0.7\%$ (1$\sigma$).
   - **Largest single contributor** for orifice meters.

5. **Fluid Density $\rho_1$ Uncertainty**
   - From composition analysis, pressure, temperature, and equation of state.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$.

6. **Pressure $P_1$ and Temperature $T_1$ Uncertainty**
   - Required to compute density and to convert to standard conditions.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.1\%$ each.

7. **Isentropic Exponent $\kappa$ (for gas)**
   - Affects the expansibility factor $\varepsilon$.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.5\%$.

8. **Composition Uncertainty (Gas)**
   - Chromatograph repeatability; affects $Z$-factor, calorific value, density.
   - Typical: $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.3\%$ in density.

9. **Calibration Drift and Meter Repeatability**
   - Long-term drift of primary elements; need periodic re-calibration.

10. **Installation Effects (for multiphase)**
    - Slug flow, partial pipe fill, pulsations, swirl, vibration.

## 2.2 The Step-by-Step Logic: From Measured to Reported

For an **ISO 5167 orifice meter** measuring gas mass flow:

> Step 1 → Measure $\Delta P$, $P_1$, $T_1$ with calibrated instruments (each has its own $u$).
>
> Step 2 → Compute upstream density $\rho_1$ from $P_1$, $T_1$, composition using an EOS (AGA 8 for gas).
>
> Step 3 → Apply the **ISO 5167 mass flow equation** to obtain $q_m$.
>
> Step 4 → Apply the **law of propagation of uncertainty (GUM)** to combine individual $u(x_i)$ into $u_c(q_m)$.
>
> Step 5 → Multiply by the **coverage factor $k=2$** to obtain the **expanded uncertainty $U$** at 95% confidence.
>
> Step 6 → Report the result as $q_m \pm U$ with units and $k$ stated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Contracts are written in terms of energy (MJ) or mass (kg), not raw $\Delta P$. Each conversion step is a *new source* of uncertainty. Ignoring any step is a contractual and financial risk.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Law of Propagation of Uncertainty (GUM Equation 10):**
> For a measurand $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N)$, the combined variance is
> $$u_c^2(Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u^2(x_i) + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j} u(x_i, x_j)$$
> For **independent** inputs (no correlation), the cross-terms vanish and:
> $$u_c^2(Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u^2(x_i)$$
> Each term is called a **sensitivity coefficient times standard uncertainty**, or simply a *contribution*.

### Complete Formula Table for KTU Examination

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols | Typical $u$ (1$\sigma$) |
|---|----------|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Mass flow rate (ISO 5167 orifice) | $q_m = \dfrac{C_d \, \varepsilon \, \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P \, \rho_1}$ | $C_d$ discharge coeff., $\varepsilon$ expansibility, $d$ orifice dia, $\beta = d/D$, $\rho_1$ density | $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1.0\%$ |
| 2 | Volumetric flow (liquid) | $q_V = C_d \, \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | — | $\pm 0.3\%$ to \pm 0.6\% |
| 3 | Expansibility factor (gas) | $\varepsilon = \left[ \left(1-\beta^4\right)\left(\kappa \tau^{2/\kappa}/(\kappa-1)\right)\left(\frac{1-\tau^{(\kappa-1)/\kappa}}{1-\tau}\right) \right]^{1/2}$ | $\tau = P_2/P_1$, $\kappa$ isentropic exp. | — |
| 4 | Relative combined uncertainty | $\left( \dfrac{u_c(q)}{q} \right)^2 = \sum_i \left( s_i \cdot \dfrac{u(x_i)}{x_i} \right)^2$ | $s_i$ sensitivity exponent | — |
| 5 | Sensitivity exponents (orifice) | $q \propto C_d^1 \, \varepsilon^1 \, d^2 \, \rho_1^{1/2}$ | $s_{C_d}=1$, $s_\varepsilon=1$, $s_d=2$, $s_{\rho_1}=0.5$ | — |
| 6 | Expanded uncertainty | $U = k \, u_c$ | $k=2$ (95.45% conf.) | — |
| 7 | Combined standard uncertainty (correlated) | $u_c^2(y) = \sum_i (c_i u_i)^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} c_i c_j u(x_i, x_j)$ | $c_i$ sensitivity coeff. | — |
| 8 | Coverage factor (normal, 95%) | $k=2$ | — | — |
| 9 | Reynolds number correction | $C_d = f(\text{Re}_D, \beta)$ (Reader-Harris/Wallis) | — | — |
| 10 | Multiphase uncertainty (TPM) | $\dfrac{u^2(\text{GVF})}{(\text{GVF})^2} = \sum (\text{contributions})$ | GVF Gas Volume Fraction | $\pm 2\%$ to \pm 5\% |
| 11 | Combined standard for sum $y = a+b$ | $u_c^2 = u_a^2 + u_b^2$ | Independent | — |
| 12 | Combined standard for product $y = a \cdot b$ | $(u_c/y)^2 = (u_a/a)^2 + (u_b/b)^2$ | Independent | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical notation warning:** In markdown tables, never write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value — use $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table cell.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Used in the Field

| Industry Context | Application of Flow Uncertainty |
|------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Custody Transfer** | Contractual settlement at $k=2$; legal admissibility |
| **Reservoir Engineering** | Material balance and decline-curve analysis require low flow uncertainty |
| **Allocation Metering** | Multi-well production sharing (well-level uncertainty compounds to platform level) |
| **Process Control** | Flow uncertainty defines control-loop tuning margins |
| **Fiscal Metering** | Government royalty calculations; ISO 17089 for ultrasonic |
| **Multiphase Flow** | Subsea tiebacks where test separators are infeasible |
| **Flare Metering** | Regulatory emissions reporting (CEMS + uncertainty) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: GUM Law of Propagation of Uncertainty (GUM Eq. 10)

### Starting Point

We have a measurand $Y$ that is a function of $N$ independent input quantities $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N$:
$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N)$$

Each $X_i$ has an associated standard uncertainty $u(x_i)$ (1$\sigma$). We want the standard uncertainty of $Y$, called the **combined standard uncertainty** $u_c(y)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Expansion (First Order)**

Expand $f$ around the estimates $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N) \\
  &= f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_i}(X_i - x_i) \\
  &\quad + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial X_i \partial X_j}(X_i - x_i)(X_j - x_j) + \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

The partial derivatives are evaluated at the estimated values $x_i$ and are called the **sensitivity coefficients** $c_i$:
$$c_i \equiv \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} \right|_{X_1=x_1, \ldots, X_N=x_N}$$

**Step 2 — Truncate to First Order**

The GUM assumes the function is sufficiently linear in the neighborhood of $x_i$ that higher-order terms are negligible. Dropping terms beyond first order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y - y &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i (X_i - x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the deviation of $Y$ from its estimate is a **linear combination** of the input deviations.

**Step 3 — Compute the Variance**

The variance of a linear combination of (independent) random variables is the sum of the squares of the coefficients times the variances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_c^2(y) &= \text{Var}(Y) = \text{Var}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i (X_i - x_i)\right] \\
         &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i^2 \, \text{Var}(X_i) \\
         &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-terms vanish because the inputs are independent and $\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

**Step 4 — Final GUM Formula (Independent Inputs)**

$$
\boxed{u_c^2(y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)}
$$

This is the **GUM Equation 10** (independent case).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Each input uncertainty is "weighted" by its sensitivity. An input that has a small $u(x_i)$ but a large sensitivity coefficient (e.g., $d$ in orifice flow) can still dominate the total.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relative Uncertainty of ISO 5167 Orifice Mass Flow

### Starting Equation (ISO 5167-1:2022)

$$
q_m = \frac{C_d \, \varepsilon \, A_d}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P \, \rho_1}
$$

where $A_d = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ is the orifice bore area.

### Step 1 — Identify the Functional Form

The variables in the formula are: $C_d$, $\varepsilon$, $d$, $\Delta P$, $\rho_1$. The diameter ratio $\beta = d/D$ depends on both $d$ and $D$, but for a single calculation we treat $\beta$ as a function of $d$ and $D$:

$$q_m = f(C_d, \varepsilon, d, D, \Delta P, \rho_1)$$

The dependence on each variable is of a **power-law form**:
$$q_m \propto C_d^1 \, \varepsilon^1 \, d^2 \, D^{-2} \, \Delta P^{1/2} \, \rho_1^{1/2}$$

### Step 2 — Sensitivity Coefficients via Logarithmic Differentiation

For a power-law form $y = A \, x^p$, the relative sensitivity is:
$$\frac{x}{y} \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{p \cdot y}{x}$$

Therefore the **sensitivity exponents** are:

| Input $x_i$ | Exponent $p_i$ |
|--------------|----------------|
| $C_d$ | $+1$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | $+1$ |
| $d$ | $+2$ |
| $D$ | $-2$ |
| $\Delta P$ | $+1/2$ |
| $\rho_1$ | $+1/2$ |

### Step 3 — Relative Combined Uncertainty

Dividing the GUM formula by $q_m^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m}\right)^2 &= \sum_i \left(\frac{x_i}{q_m} \frac{\partial q_m}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 \left(\frac{u(x_i)}{x_i}\right)^2 \\
&= \sum_i p_i^2 \left(\frac{u(x_i)}{x_i}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

So the relative combined standard uncertainty is:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m}\right)^2 = 1 \cdot \left(\frac{u(C_d)}{C_d}\right)^2 + 1 \cdot \left(\frac{u(\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon}\right)^2 + 4 \cdot \left(\frac{u(d)}{d}\right)^2 + 4 \cdot \left(\frac{u(D)}{D}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{4} \left(\frac{u(\Delta P)}{\Delta P}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{4} \left(\frac{u(\rho_1)}{\rho_1}\right)^2}
$$

**This is the workhorse equation for KTU problems.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The squared exponent in the formula** is critical: an uncertainty of $\pm 0.1\%$ in $d$ becomes a contribution of $\pm 0.2\%$ in $q_m$. Doubling the relative error from $d$ has the same effect as quadrupling the absolute uncertainty.

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A natural gas pipeline uses an orifice meter with the following data and uncertainties (1$\sigma$):

| Input | Value | Relative $u$ (1$\sigma$) |
|-------|-------|---------------------------|
| $C_d$ | 0.61 | 0.6% |
| $\varepsilon$ | 0.998 | 0.1% |
| $d$ | 100 mm | 0.05% |
| $D$ | 200 mm | 0.05% |
| $\Delta P$ | 20 kPa | 0.07% |
| $\rho_1$ | 25 kg/m³ | 0.3% |

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m}\right)^2 &= (1)(0.6)^2 + (1)(0.1)^2 + (4)(0.05)^2 + (4)(0.05)^2 + (0.25)(0.07)^2 + (0.25)(0.3)^2 \\
&= 0.36 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.001225 + 0.0225 \\
&= 0.413725
\end{aligned}
$$

So:
$$
\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m} = \sqrt{0.413725} = 0.6432\% \quad \text{(1$\sigma$)}
$$

Expanded uncertainty at $k=2$:
$$U = 2 \times 0.6432\% = \pm 1.286\% \quad \text{(at 95\% confidence)}$$

**Dominant contributors (in order of magnitude):**

| Rank | Source | Contribution to $u_c^2$ | % of total |
|------|--------|--------------------------|------------|
| 1 | $C_d$ | 0.36 | 87.0% |
| 2 | $\rho_1$ | 0.0225 | 5.4% |
| 3 | $d$ | 0.01 | 2.4% |
| 4 | $D$ | 0.01 | 2.4% |
| 5 | $\varepsilon$ | 0.01 | 2.4% |
| 6 | $\Delta P$ | 0.001225 | 0.3% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** For an orifice meter, **$C_d$ dominates** by an order of magnitude. Improving $C_d$ correlation (e.g., switching to Reader-Harris/Wallis) yields bigger gains than upgrading the DP transmitter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Uncertainty in Product of Uncorrelated Quantities

### Setup

Suppose $y = x_1 \cdot x_2 \cdot \ldots \cdot x_N$ (e.g., mass flow = volume flow $\times$ density). The natural logarithm linearizes products:
$$\ln y = \sum_i \ln x_i$$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Differentiate:**
$$
\frac{dy}{y} = \sum_i \frac{dx_i}{x_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Variance of a sum (independent variables):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(y)}{y}\right)^2 &= \sum_i \left(\frac{u(x_i)}{x_i}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For a power law $y = x^p$, $\dfrac{u(y)}{y} = \lvert p \rvert \dfrac{u(x)}{x}$.**

This is the basis of the *sensitivity exponent* approach used in §3.2.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Multiphase Flow Uncertainty (GVF Sensitivity)

### Setup

For a multiphase flowmeter (MPFM) measuring Gas Volume Fraction (GVF) $\gamma$ and total flow $Q_t$:
$$Q_g = \gamma \cdot Q_t, \qquad Q_l = (1-\gamma) \cdot Q_t$$

The relative uncertainty of the gas flow is:
$$
\left(\frac{u(Q_g)}{Q_g}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{u(\gamma)}{\gamma}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{u(Q_t)}{Q_t}\right)^2 - 2\,\rho_{\gamma Q_t}\,\frac{u(\gamma)}{\gamma}\,\frac{u(Q_t)}{Q_t}
$$

Where $\rho_{\gamma Q_t}$ is the correlation coefficient. For an **independent** MPFM, $\rho = 0$.

**Critical observation:** When GVF is small (e.g., $0.01$ in a wet gas well), the term $u(\gamma)/\gamma$ is **amplified by orders of magnitude**, leading to very large relative uncertainty in $Q_g$. This is why low-GVF wells have large gas-rate uncertainty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical rule of thumb:** For GVF $< 5\%$, the MPFM is operating in the "wet-gas" regime, and uncertainty in liquid phase is much smaller than that in gas phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sources of Uncertainty in Orifice Flow Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurand: Mass Flow qm] --> B1[Discharge Coeff Cd]
    A --> B2[Expansibility Factor epsilon]
    A --> B3[Orifice Diameter d]
    A --> B4[Pipe Diameter D]
    A --> B5[Differential Pressure dP]
    A --> B6[Upstream Density rho1]

    B1 --> C1[Cd Correlation - Reader Harris]
    B1 --> C2[Re Sensitivity]
    B1 --> C3[Beta Sensitivity]

    B2 --> C4[Isentropic Exponent kappa]
    B2 --> C5[Pressure Ratio tau]

    B3 --> C6[Bore Measurement T]
    B3 --> C7[Thermal Expansion]

    B4 --> C8[Circumference Taps]
    B4 --> C9[Pipe Schedule]

    B5 --> C10[DP Transmitter Calib]
    B5 --> C11[Line Pressure Pulsation]

    B6 --> C12[Composition GC]
    B6 --> C13[Pressure P1]
    B6 --> C14[Temperature T1]
    B6 --> C15[EOS - AGA 8]

    C1 --> D[Combined Uncertainty uc qm]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    C9 --> D
    C10 --> D
    C11 --> D
    C12 --> D
    C13 --> D
    C14 --> D
    C15 --> D

    D --> E[Coverage Factor k]
    E --> F[Expanded Uncertainty U]
    F --> G[Reported: qm +/- U at 95% CL]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Uncertainty Quantification Workflow (per GUM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Define Measurand] --> P2[Step 2: Identify Inputs]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Assign u xi from Calib Cert]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Establish Model y = f x]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Sensitivity Coeff]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply GUM Eq 10]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Combined Standard uc]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Choose Coverage Factor k]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Expanded U = k uc]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Report with Statement]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Uncertainties in Multiphase Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MP[Multiphase Flow Rate Qt] --> SG[Single-Phase Models]
    MP --> SP[Slip and Mixing Models]
    MP --> CL[Calibration Database]

    SG --> SG1[Venturi - Liquid]
    SG --> SG2[Venturi - Gas]
    SG --> SG3[Cross Correlation]

    SP --> SP1[Drift Flux]
    SP --> SP2[Homogeneous]
    SP --> SP3[Slug Tracking]

    CL --> CL1[Multiphase Test Loop]
    CL --> CL2[Field Tracer]
    CL --> CL3[Back-Allocation]

    SG1 --> UQ[Total Uncertainty U]
    SG2 --> UQ
    SG3 --> UQ
    SP1 --> UQ
    SP2 --> UQ
    SP3 --> UQ
    CL1 --> UQ
    CL2 --> UQ
    CL3 --> UQ
```

## 4.4 Sensitivity Contribution Map (Orifice Meter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[qm Total] --> CONT[Contributions]
    CONT --> Cd[Cd: 87 percent]
    CONT --> rho[rho1: 5 percent]
    CONT --> dBore[d: 2.4 percent]
    CONT --> DPipe[D: 2.4 percent]
    CONT --> eps[epsilon: 2.4 percent]
    CONT --> dP[dP: 0.3 percent]
    Cd -->|Dominant| ACT[Action: Use Reader-Harris Correlation]
    ACT --> OUT[Improves uc by 30 to 50 percent]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define measurement uncertainty in the context of oil and natural gas flow measurement. Distinguish between standard uncertainty, combined standard uncertainty, and expanded uncertainty.**

**Model Answer:**

Measurement uncertainty is a parameter that characterizes the dispersion of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand (e.g., flow rate). The three principal types are:

1. **Standard Uncertainty $u(x)$:** The uncertainty of a single input quantity $x$, expressed as one standard deviation (1$\sigma$). It quantifies the spread of possible values of $x$ about its estimate.

2. **Combined Standard Uncertainty $u_c(y)$:** The standard uncertainty of the measurand $y = f(x_1, \ldots, x_N)$, computed from the standard uncertainties of the inputs using the GUM law of propagation of uncertainty:
   $$u_c^2(y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)$$
   For independent inputs, cross-terms vanish.

3. **Expanded Uncertainty $U$:** A multiple of $u_c$ defining an interval with a specified level of confidence:
   $$U = k \cdot u_c$$
   For a normal distribution, $k=2$ corresponds to approximately **95.45% confidence**. Always report $U$ together with $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Mention that the value of $k$ must always accompany any reported expanded uncertainty. Reporting $U$ alone is incomplete.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four major sources of uncertainty in orifice meter measurement of natural gas flow rate.**

**Model Answer:**

Four major sources of uncertainty in ISO 5167 orifice measurement of natural gas flow:

1. **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** uncertainty: typically $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 0.7\%$ (1$\sigma$); the largest single contributor. Source: empirical correlation (Reader-Harris/Wallis) and Reynolds number sensitivity.

2. **Orifice bore diameter $d$** uncertainty: typically $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.1\%$. Source: bore measurement at reference temperature and thermal expansion correction. Contributes as $d^2$, doubling its relative effect.

3. **Differential pressure $\Delta P$** uncertainty: typically $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.1\%$ from DP transmitter calibration, line pressure pulsation, and zero drift. Contributes as $\sqrt{\Delta P}$, halving its relative effect.

4. **Upstream density $\rho_1$** uncertainty: typically $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ from composition analysis (gas chromatograph), pressure, temperature measurements, and equation-of-state (AGA 8) calculation. Contributes as $\sqrt{\rho_1}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive the GUM law of propagation of uncertainty for a measurand that is a function of multiple independent input quantities. Hence, compute the relative combined uncertainty of the mass flow rate measured by an ISO 5167 orifice meter for the data given below.**

| Input | Value | Relative Standard Uncertainty (1$\sigma$) |
|-------|-------|---------------------------------------------|
| Discharge coefficient $C_d$ | 0.6095 | 0.6% |
| Expansibility $\varepsilon$ | 0.9975 | 0.10% |
| Orifice diameter $d$ | 152.40 mm | 0.05% |
| Pipe diameter $D$ | 304.80 mm | 0.07% |
| Differential pressure $\Delta P$ | 25.0 kPa | 0.08% |
| Upstream density $\rho_1$ | 22.5 kg/m³ | 0.30% |

**Compute the expanded uncertainty at $k=2$ and identify the dominant contributor.**

---

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the GUM Propagation Law (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let the measurand be $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N)$. Expand $f$ about the estimated values $x_i$ via Taylor series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} \right|_{x_i} (X_i - x_i) \\
  &\quad + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \left. \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial X_i \partial X_j}\right|_{x_i, x_j} (X_i - x_i)(X_j - x_j) + \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the **sensitivity coefficient** $c_i \equiv \left. \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} \right|_{x_i}$.

**Step 2:** Truncate to first order (GUM linearity assumption):
$$Y - y \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i (X_i - x_i)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the variance. For independent inputs, the variance of a sum is the sum of variances:
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_c^2(y) &= \text{Var}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i (X_i - x_i)\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i^2 \, \text{Var}(X_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $u^2(x_i) = \text{Var}(X_i)$ and $c_i = \partial f / \partial x_i$:

$$
\boxed{u_c^2(y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u^2(x_i)}
$$

This is the **GUM combined standard uncertainty** for independent inputs.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the ISO 5167 mass flow equation:**
$$q_m = \frac{C_d \, \varepsilon}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P \, \rho_1}$$

The functional dependence is power-law:
$$q_m \propto C_d^1 \, \varepsilon^1 \, d^2 \, D^{-2} \, \Delta P^{1/2} \, \rho_1^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity exponents (squared for relative uncertainty):**

| Input $x_i$ | Exponent $p_i$ | $p_i^2$ |
|--------------|----------------|---------|
| $C_d$ | $+1$ | $1$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | $+1$ | $1$ |
| $d$ | $+2$ | $4$ |
| $D$ | $-2$ | $4$ |
| $\Delta P$ | $+1/2$ | $1/4$ |
| $\rho_1$ | $+1/2$ | $1/4$ |

**Step 3 — Apply the relative uncertainty formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m}\right)^2 &= \sum_i p_i^2 \left(\frac{u(x_i)}{x_i}\right)^2 \\
&= (1)(0.6)^2 + (1)(0.10)^2 + (4)(0.05)^2 \\
&\quad + (4)(0.07)^2 + (0.25)(0.08)^2 + (0.25)(0.30)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1 (}C_d\text{):} &\quad 0.36 \\
\text{Term 2 (}\varepsilon\text{):} &\quad 0.0100 \\
\text{Term 3 (}d\text{):} &\quad 4 \times 0.0025 = 0.0100 \\
\text{Term 4 (}D\text{):} &\quad 4 \times 0.0049 = 0.0196 \\
\text{Term 5 (}\Delta P\text{):} &\quad 0.25 \times 0.0064 = 0.0016 \\
\text{Term 6 (}\rho_1\text{):} &\quad 0.25 \times 0.09 = 0.0225
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum and take square root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m}\right)^2 &= 0.36 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 + 0.0196 + 0.0016 + 0.0225 \\
&= 0.4237
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{u_c(q_m)}{q_m} = \sqrt{0.4237} = 0.6509\% \quad (1\sigma)$$

**Step 6 — Expanded uncertainty at $k=2$:**
$$U = k \cdot u_c = 2 \times 0.6509\% = \pm 1.302\% \quad \text{at 95.45\% confidence}$$

**Step 7 — Identify dominant contributor (percentage of total $u_c^2$):**

| Source | Contribution to $u_c^2$ | % of total |
|--------|--------------------------|------------|
| $C_d$ | 0.3600 | **84.97%** |
| $\rho_1$ | 0.0225 | 5.31% |
| $D$ | 0.0196 | 4.63% |
| $d$ | 0.0100 | 2.36% |
| $\varepsilon$ | 0.0100 | 2.36% |
| $\Delta P$ | 0.0016 | 0.38% |

**Conclusion:** The discharge coefficient $C_d$ is overwhelmingly the dominant source of uncertainty, contributing ~85% of the total variance. Improving the $C_d$ correlation (e.g., Reader-Harris/Wallis) yields the largest gain in reducing total flow uncertainty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A multiphase flowmeter (MPFM) measures the total flow rate of an oil well as $Q_t = 1500 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$ with a relative standard uncertainty of $\pm 1.5\%$. The gas volume fraction (GVF) is measured as $\gamma = 0.20$ with relative standard uncertainty $\pm 5.0\%$. Assuming the two measurements are independent, compute:**

**(a)** The gas flow rate $Q_g$ and its combined standard uncertainty.

**(b)** The liquid flow rate $Q_l$ and its combined standard uncertainty.

**(c)** Comment on which phase has the higher relative uncertainty and why this is typical of low-GVF wells.

---

#### Part (a) — Gas Flow Rate $Q_g$ (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the gas flow rate:**
$$Q_g = \gamma \cdot Q_t = 0.20 \times 1500 = 300 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the GUM propagation for a product (independent inputs):**
$$\left(\frac{u_c(Q_g)}{Q_g}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{u(\gamma)}{\gamma}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{u(Q_t)}{Q_t}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given uncertainties:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(Q_g)}{Q_g}\right)^2 &= (0.05)^2 + (0.015)^2 \\
&= 0.0025 + 0.000225 \\
&= 0.002725
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined standard uncertainty (relative):**
$$\frac{u_c(Q_g)}{Q_g} = \sqrt{0.002725} = 0.05220 = 5.22\%$$

**Step 5 — Absolute standard uncertainty:**
$$u_c(Q_g) = 0.05220 \times 300 = 15.66 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 6 — Expanded uncertainty at $k=2$:**
$$U = 2 \times 15.66 = \pm 31.32 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day (95\% CL)}$$

**Result:** $Q_g = 300 \pm 31.3 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$ at 95% confidence.

---

#### Part (b) — Liquid Flow Rate $Q_l$ (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the liquid flow rate:**
$$Q_l = (1-\gamma) \cdot Q_t = (1 - 0.20) \times 1500 = 1200 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Express $Q_l$ as a function of $\gamma$ and $Q_t$ and derive the sensitivity coefficients:**

Take the natural logarithm:
$$\ln Q_l = \ln(1-\gamma) + \ln Q_t$$

Differentiate:
$$\frac{dQ_l}{Q_l} = -\frac{d\gamma}{1-\gamma} + \frac{dQ_t}{Q_t}$$

So the **sensitivity exponents** are:
- For $\gamma$: $p_\gamma = -\dfrac{\gamma}{1-\gamma} = -\dfrac{0.20}{0.80} = -0.25$
- For $Q_t$: $p_{Q_t} = +1$

**Step 3 — Apply the GUM formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{u_c(Q_l)}{Q_l}\right)^2 &= p_\gamma^2 \left(\frac{u(\gamma)}{\gamma}\right)^2 + (1)^2 \left(\frac{u(Q_t)}{Q_t}\right)^2 \\
&= (-0.25)^2 (0.05)^2 + (1)^2 (0.015)^2 \\
&= 0.0625 \times 0.0025 + 0.000225 \\
&= 0.00015625 + 0.000225 \\
&= 0.00038125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined standard uncertainty (relative):**
$$\frac{u_c(Q_l)}{Q_l} = \sqrt{0.00038125} = 0.01953 = 1.95\%$$

**Step 5 — Absolute standard uncertainty:**
$$u_c(Q_l) = 0.01953 \times 1200 = 23.43 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 6 — Expanded uncertainty at $k=2$:**
$$U = 2 \times 23.43 = \pm 46.87 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day (95\% CL)}$$

**Result:** $Q_l = 1200 \pm 46.9 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{day}$ at 95% confidence.

---

#### Part (c) — Comparison and Discussion

**Model Solution:**

- Relative uncertainty in gas flow: **5.22%**
- Relative uncertainty in liquid flow: **1.95%**

The **gas phase has a much higher relative uncertainty** than the liquid phase, despite both phases being derived from the same MPFM. This is because:

1. **GVF is small (0.20)**, so the relative uncertainty in $\gamma$ is amplified when computing $Q_g = \gamma \cdot Q_t$. For $Q_g$, the sensitivity exponent on $\gamma$ is $+1$, so the full $5\%$ uncertainty in $\gamma$ passes through.

2. **For the liquid phase**, the sensitivity exponent on $\gamma$ is only $-0.25$ (because $1-\gamma = 0.80$ is large), which **dampens** the contribution of $\gamma$-uncertainty to $Q_l$. The $5\%$ uncertainty in $\gamma$ contributes only $\sim 0.25 \times 5\% = 1.25\%$ to $Q_l$.

3. **Engineering implication:** In low-GVF wells (gas-condensate, wet-gas), the gas rate carries very large relative uncertainty. Allocation contracts often specify the *liquid rate* as the primary settlement quantity precisely because it has tighter uncertainty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the exponent:** Many students write $u(Q_g)/Q_g = 0.05 + 0.015 = 6.5\%$. This is wrong. **Errors add in quadrature (RSS)**, not linearly. The correct answer is $\sqrt{0.05^2 + 0.015^2} = 5.22\%$.
> 2. **Using $p_\gamma = +1$ for the liquid phase:** Some students forget that $Q_l = (1-\gamma) Q_t$ has a different sensitivity exponent than $Q_g$. Always differentiate.
> 3. **Not stating $k$:** Report expanded uncertainty as $\pm 1.302\%$ at $k=2$ (95% CL), not just $\pm 1.302\%$.
> 4. **Confusing relative and absolute uncertainty:** Mark final answer as either relative (%) or absolute (Sm³/day), not both mixed up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Uncertainty ≠ Error. Error is deviation; uncertainty is the doubt interval.
> - Standard uncertainty $u(x)$ = 1$\sigma$; Expanded $U = k \cdot u_c$, with $k=2$ for 95% CL.
> - Always report $U$ with its coverage factor $k$.
>
> **Key Equation (must memorize):**
> - **GUM propagation (independent):** $u_c^2(y) = \sum \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)$
> - **Relative form (power law):** $\left(\dfrac{u_c(y)}{y}\right)^2 = \sum p_i^2 \left(\dfrac{u(x_i)}{x_i}\right)^2$
> - **ISO 5167 orifice mass flow:** $q_m = \dfrac{C_d \varepsilon A_d}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \Delta P \rho_1}$
>
> **Sensitivity Exponents for Orifice Meter (most important table):**
> - $C_d$: 1 (linear)
> - $\varepsilon$: 1
> - $d$: 2 (squared)
> - $D$: $-2$
> - $\Delta P$: $1/2$ (square-root)
> - $\rho_1$: $1/2$
>
> **Dominant Contributor Rule:** For orifice meters, **$C_d$ dominates** (~85% of variance). For GVF-based multiphase, **GVF** uncertainty dominates at low GVF.
>
> **Multiphase Specifics:**
> - Gas rate relative uncertainty $\approx \sqrt{(u_\gamma/\gamma)^2 + (u_{Qt}/Q_t)^2}$
> - Liquid rate has damped $\gamma$ sensitivity: $p_\gamma = -\gamma/(1-\gamma)$
> - Low-GVF wells (gas-condensate) → liquid rate is the more precise quantity
>
> **Cross-Cutting Engineering Points:**
> - Standards: GUM (ISO/IEC 98-3), ISO 5167, API MPMS Ch. 13, AGA Report No. 9
> - For independent inputs, **sum variances in quadrature** (root-sum-square, RSS)
> - Correlated inputs require covariance terms: $u_c^2 = \sum c_i^2 u_i^2 + 2\sum c_i c_j u_{ij}$
> - Always state assumptions: independence, linearity, normal distribution

---
