topics.title: Gas Gathering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Gathering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gas Gathering** is defined as the integrated network of low-to-medium pressure pipelines, flowlines, manifolds, wellhead separation equipment, compression stations, and auxiliary processing units (such as scrubbers, heaters, and dehydration units) that collectively collect raw, untreated, or partially treated natural gas — often as a multiphase mixture of hydrocarbon gas, water vapor, condensate, and entrained solids — from individual wellheads or satellite field production clusters, and transport it to a central gas processing plant, a main transmission trunk line, or a custody transfer metering station.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, gas gathering is treated as the **first operational link** in the natural gas value chain, sitting between the upstream (production) and midstream (processing and transmission) sectors. The system is engineered to operate under one of the two pressure regimes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Pressure Gathering:** Operates at wellhead flowing pressures (typically **500 psig to 1500 psig**), where reservoir energy alone is sufficient to drive flow through the gathering network.
>
> **Low-Pressure Gathering:** Operates below **500 psig** (often **50 psig to 200 psig**), where field-installed **compressor stations** are mandatory to overcome pipeline frictional losses and maintain deliverability to the central facility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city water supply network, but in reverse. Instead of one big reservoir sending water out to thousands of houses through a branching tree of pipes, the **gas gathering system** works like a **reverse funnel**: dozens of small water taps (wellheads) all over a hilly rural area each produce a small trickle of water. These trickles feed into small pipes (flowlines), which converge into larger pipes (gathering trunks), which ultimately deliver the combined stream to a central water treatment plant (gas processing facility).

Three real-world analogies that make the concept crystal clear:

- **Analogy 1 — The Village Water Co-op:** Several farmers each pump groundwater from their own wells. Small flexible hoses carry the water to a junction box at the road, where the combined flow goes through one large main pipe to the village tank. The gas gathering system is the oilfield equivalent.

- **Analogy 2 — The City's Storm Drain System:** Rainwater hits multiple street inlets (wellheads), flows through small lateral drains (flowlines), enters progressively larger branch sewers (gathering mains), and finally reaches the treatment plant (gas plant). The flow direction is similar.

- **Analogy 3 — The Human Circulatory System:** Capillaries (flowlines) collect blood from tissues, merge into venules, then veins, and finally the vena cava delivers it to the heart (gas plant). The gas gathering system is the "veins" of the production field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The fundamental engineering challenge of gas gathering is **NOT** the transport of gas itself — it is the simultaneous handling of a **multiphase, multi-composition, pressure-depleting, erosive, corrosive, and hydrate-prone** fluid stream over **variable terrain**, from **depleting reservoir pressures**, while **minimizing capital expenditure and operational risk**.

## 1.3 System Boundaries and Engineering Scope

The scope of "Gas Gathering" in the KTU PECHT522 syllabus spans:

| Boundary Element | Description |
|------------------|-------------|
| **Upstream Boundary** | The wellhead Christmas tree / production choke |
| **Downstream Boundary** | The custody transfer meter at the inlet of the central processing plant or main transmission line |
| **Typical Operating Pressure** | 50 psig to 1500 psig (low and high pressure gathering) |
| **Typical Pipe Diameter** | 2 inches to 16 inches nominal |
| **Operating Temperature** | 40 °F to 180 °F (4 °C to 82 °C) |
| **Standard Reference Conditions** | 60 °F, 14.7 psia (US); 15 °C, 1.01325 bar (SI) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conditions (S.I. vs. Field Units):** The natural gas industry uses **60 °F and 14.7 psia** as standard conditions in the United States. All flow rates in gas-gathering calculations are reported in **MSCFD (thousand standard cubic feet per day)** or **MMSCFD (million standard cubic feet per day)**. KTU problems typically use these US field units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of a Gas Gathering System

A complete gas gathering system is composed of seven distinct functional sub-units, each with a defined engineering role:

1. **Wellhead Assembly (Christmas Tree):** Houses the production choke, master valves, and pressure sensors. The choke is the **first pressure-reduction point** and governs the well's deliverability into the flowline.

2. **Flowline (Choke Manifold Line):** A small-diameter (typically 2" to 6") pipe connecting an individual wellhead to a satellite manifold. Length usually 0.5 km to 5 km per well.

3. **Satellite Manifold / Gathering Point:** A multi-port piping header where 4 to 20 flowlines converge. Performs initial bulk separation of gas, liquid hydrocarbon (condensate), and free water.

4. **Gathering Main / Trunk Line:** A larger-diameter (6" to 16") pipe that carries combined production from one or more manifolds to the central facility. Lengths can be **5 km to 80 km**.

5. **Field Compressor Station (Boosters):** Reciprocating or centrifugal compressors that re-pressurize the gas to overcome frictional and elevation losses along the trunk line. Essential in **low-pressure gathering** and as the field matures and reservoir pressure declines.

6. **Line Heater / Heater Treater:** Indirect-fired heat exchangers that prevent **gas hydrate formation** by maintaining the gas temperature above the hydrate formation temperature for the given pressure and composition.

7. **Central Gathering / Processing Station:** The terminal point where gas is metered, dehydrated, sweetened (H₂S/CO₂ removal), and NGL-recovered before entering the main transmission pipeline.

## 2.2 Classification of Gas Gathering Network Topologies

The three classical network geometries are:

- **Radial (Star) Topology:** Each well connects directly to a central hub. Simple, but high capital cost due to many long flowlines. Used in compact fields with few wells.

- **Trunk (Branch / Dendritic) Topology:** A main gathering trunk runs through the field, with smaller flowlines branching off to individual wells. **Most common in offshore and onshore strip-field developments.**

- **Loop (Ring) Topology:** A closed loop gathering line provides redundancy and balanced pressure distribution. Used in mature fields with high deliverability and where uninterrupted production is critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The choice of topology is governed by **(a) well spacing, (b) terrain, (c) capital cost, (d) operational flexibility, and (e) future infill-drilling plans.** The trunk/branch topology dominates because it minimizes total pipe length per well.

## 2.3 Pressure Profile in a Gathering System

The pressure of the gas decreases continuously from the wellhead to the central facility due to three irreversible losses:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{elevation} + \Delta P_{acceleration}$$

The **elevation term** is non-trivial in hilly onshore fields. For a horizontal pipe, only the friction term is present. For a downward-sloping pipe, the elevation term acts as a pressure *recovery* (gas gains pressure). The acceleration term is usually negligible.

The governing steady-state energy equation (mechanical energy balance) for a gas pipeline is:

$$\frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L} = \frac{25 \gamma_g Q^2 Z T f}{d^5} \cdot \frac{1}{E^2}$$

where **E** is the pipeline efficiency factor (typically **0.85 to 0.95** for new, clean steel pipe in gas service).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving gas gathering numerical problems in KTU examinations.

| Equation Name | Formula (US Field Units) | Applicability | Key Variables |
|---------------|--------------------------|---------------|---------------|
| **General Flow Equation** | $Q = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^n}{\gamma_g^{m_1} \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z \cdot f^{m_2}}\right]^{m_3}$ | All gas pipelines | Q = flow rate, P = pressure, d = diameter |
| **Weymouth Equation** | $Q = 433.5 \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{P_b} \cdot \left[\dfrac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^{16/3}}{\gamma_g \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z}\right]^{0.5}$ | Short gathering lines (<20 mi), high-pressure, low-to-moderate flow | d in inches; L in miles; P in psia; T in °R; Q in SCFD |
| **Panhandle A** | $Q = 435.87 \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{P_b} \cdot \left[\dfrac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^{2.6182}}{\gamma_g^{0.8539} \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z}\right]^{0.5394}$ | Long lines (20–100 mi), medium-to-high pressure | Same units as Weymouth |
| **Panhandle B** | $Q = 737 \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{P_b} \cdot \left[\dfrac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^{2.6182}}{\gamma_g^{0.9611} \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z}\right]^{0.5394}$ | Long lines, high pressure, large diameter | Same units |
| **Spitzglass (Low-Pressure)** | $Q = 565 \cdot d^{8/3} \cdot \left[\dfrac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot \gamma_g \cdot T}\right]^{0.5}$ | Low-pressure gathering (<100 psig), small mains | Used inside buildings and field gathering |
| **Friction Factor (Weymouth)** | $f = \dfrac{4 \cdot (12) \cdot g}{d}$ (laminar, in Weymouth form) | Embedded inside Weymouth constant | — |
| **Hydrate Formation Temp. (Approx.)** | $T_{hydrate} \approx 60 \, P^{0.5} - 40$ (°F, with P in psia) | Quick field estimate for sweet gas | Use phase-equilibrium charts for accuracy |
| **Compressor Power (Field)** | $W = \dfrac{0.0857 \cdot Q \cdot Z \cdot T_1}{\eta}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2857} - 1\right]$ | Field compression (adiabatic, hp) | Q in MMSCFD, W in hp, η = efficiency |
| **Standard Volume Conversion** | $Q_{sc} = Q \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{Z}$ | Real gas → standard conditions | P, T at flowing conditions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Exam:** When using the Weymouth equation in SI units, the constant changes from **433.5** to approximately **1.162 × 10⁶** when Q is in m³/day, d is in mm, L is in km, T is in K, and P is in kPa. The KTU board typically expects **US field units**, but always verify the units stated in the problem.

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Weymouth** is the most-used equation in **gas gathering** because most gathering lines are short and operate at high Reynolds numbers in the fully-turbulent region where the friction factor is nearly independent of Reynolds number.
- **Panhandle B** is the industry standard for **long-distance transmission lines** (>50 miles) where the smooth-pipe assumption of the law-of-the-wall is more accurate.
- **Spitzglass** is specifically designed for **low-pressure field gathering mains** (less than ~100 psig) where a simpler form suffices and accuracy is acceptable.

## 2.6 Multiphase Flow Considerations in Gathering Systems

In real gas gathering, the flowing stream is rarely dry gas. It typically contains:

- **Liquid water** (formation water + condensed water vapor)
- **Liquid hydrocarbon** (condensate)
- **Solid particulates** (sand, scale, corrosion debris)

The presence of these phases changes the calculation methodology significantly. The four standard multiphase-flow correlations used in gas gathering design are:

1. **Beggs and Brill Correlation** — Most widely used for hilly terrain.
2. **Mukherjee and Brill Correlation** — Improved version for inclined pipes.
3. **Eaton Correlation** — Simplified, suitable for quick field estimates.
4. **Olmez-Gregory Correlation** — Used for wet-gas pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment Rule:** When the gas-liquid ratio (GLR) is greater than **5000 SCF/STB**, the flow behaves as gas-dominated and a single-phase gas flow equation (Weymouth) with the addition of a **liquid-loading correction factor** is usually sufficient for gathering-line sizing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Gas Flow Equation (Starting from Bernoulli + Friction)

The general gas flow equation is derived by combining the **mechanical energy balance** with the **ideal gas law** and the **Darcy-Weisbach friction equation**, with the assumption of isothermal, steady-state, horizontal flow.

### Step 1: Write the steady-state mechanical energy balance

For isothermal, horizontal flow with negligible elevation change and acceleration, the energy balance between points 1 (inlet) and 2 (outlet) reduces to:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{2 f v^2 L}{d \cdot g_c}$$

This is the **Darcy-Weisbach** equation in mechanical energy form, where the left side is the pressure loss expressed in terms of energy per unit mass, and the right side is the friction loss. We treat the **fluid density as a variable** because the gas expands as pressure drops along the pipe.

### Step 2: Express density via the Real Gas Law

The real gas law gives the local density at any point along the pipe:

$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T} = \frac{28.97 \cdot \gamma_g \cdot P}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

The molecular weight of air is **M_air = 28.97 lbm/lbmol**, and the gas constant **R = 10.732 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)**.

### Step 3: Express velocity in terms of mass flow rate and density

The superficial velocity is related to the mass flow rate by:

$$v = \frac{\dot{m}}{A \cdot \rho} = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi d^2 \cdot \rho}$$

This substitution is essential because the gas density changes along the pipe, so velocity changes too, even for steady mass flow.

### Step 4: Substitute density and velocity into the energy balance

Substituting Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1 yields:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{(\rho)} = \frac{2 f L}{d g_c} \cdot \left(\frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi d^2 \rho}\right)^2$$

Because $\rho$ is a function of $P$, the differential form must be used. The differential pressure drop over an infinitesimal length is:

$$-dP = \frac{2 f v^2 \rho}{d g_c} \, dL$$

### Step 5: Use continuity and real gas law to express v·ρ

From continuity, the mass flow rate is constant. We use:

$$v = \frac{Q_{sc} P_{sc} T Z}{A T_{sc} P}$$

Squaring and multiplying by density:

$$v^2 \rho = \left(\frac{Q_{sc} P_{sc} T Z}{A T_{sc} P}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{28.97 \gamma_g P}{Z R T} = \frac{(Q_{sc} P_{sc})^2 T Z \cdot 28.97 \gamma_g}{A^2 T_{sc}^2 \cdot P \cdot R}$$

### Step 6: Substitute back and integrate

The differential form becomes:

$$-P \, dP = \frac{f L \cdot 28.97 \gamma_g T Z (Q_{sc} P_{sc})^2}{g_c A^2 T_{sc}^2 R \, d} \, dL$$

Integrating from 0 to L, and from P₁ to P₂, and using $A = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$P_1^2 - P_2^2 = \frac{16 f L \cdot 28.97 \gamma_g T Z (Q_{sc} P_{sc})^2 \cdot 2}{\pi^2 d^5 g_c R T_{sc}^2}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the integration of dL from 0 to L (since the LHS is a square-difference, the factor becomes 2).

### Step 7: Rearrange to solve for Q_sc

Collecting all constants into a single numerical constant and rearranging for Q_sc yields the **Weymouth equation**:

$$Q_{sc} = K \cdot \left(\frac{T_{sc}}{P_{sc}}\right) \cdot \left[\frac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \, d^5}{\gamma_g \, T \, L \, Z \, f}\right]^{0.5}$$

The constant K for the Weymouth equation evaluates to **433.5** when Q is in **SCFD**, d is in **inches**, P is in **psia**, L is in **miles**, T is in **°R**, and the friction factor f corresponds to an effective roughness of 0.0007 inches (commercial steel).

### Step 8: The empirical exponent 16/3 on diameter

Weymouth observed empirically that for fully turbulent flow in commercial steel pipe, the friction factor scales approximately as $f \propto d^{-1/3}$, which when combined with the $d^5$ in the denominator of the rearranged equation yields an effective exponent of $5 + 1/3 = 16/3$. This is the **defining characteristic of the Weymouth equation**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Weymouth Equation Application (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem Statement:** A high-pressure gas gathering trunk line has the following specifications:
- Inlet pressure: P₁ = 1200 psia
- Outlet pressure: P₂ = 800 psia
- Length: L = 10 miles
- Inside diameter: d = 8.0 inches
- Gas specific gravity: γ_g = 0.65
- Average temperature: T = 100 °F = 560 °R
- Compressibility factor: Z = 0.92
- Standard conditions: T_b = 520 °R, P_b = 14.7 psia

**Find:** The gas flow rate in **MMSCFD**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 433.5 \cdot \frac{T_b}{P_b} \cdot \left[\frac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^{16/3}}{\gamma_g \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z}\right]^{0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step A: Compute the pressure-squared difference

$$P_1^2 = (1200)^2 = 1{,}440{,}000$$
$$P_2^2 = (800)^2 = 640{,}000$$
$$P_1^2 - P_2^2 = 800{,}000 \text{ psia}^2$$

### Step B: Compute the diameter term

$$d = 8.0 \text{ inches}$$
$$d^{16/3} = 8^{16/3} = (2^3)^{16/3} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$$

### Step C: Compute the denominator

$$\gamma_g \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z = 0.65 \times 560 \times 10 \times 0.92 = 3348.8$$

### Step D: Compute the bracketed term

$$\frac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot d^{16/3}}{\gamma_g \cdot T \cdot L \cdot Z} = \frac{800{,}000 \times 65{,}536}{3348.8} = 1.5656 \times 10^7$$

### Step E: Take the square root

$$\left[1.5656 \times 10^7\right]^{0.5} = 3956.8$$

### Step F: Apply the leading constant

$$Q = 433.5 \times \frac{520}{14.7} \times 3956.8$$
$$Q = 433.5 \times 35.374 \times 3956.8$$
$$Q = 6.066 \times 10^7 \text{ SCFD}$$
$$Q = 60.66 \text{ MMSCFD}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The gathering trunk can deliver **60.66 MMSCFD** at the given pressure conditions. This is a typical KTU numerical answer and demonstrates the dominant role of pressure-squared difference in gas flow capacity.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Flow Equation

| Operating Condition | Recommended Equation | Reason |
|---------------------|----------------------|--------|
| Short line (<20 mi), high pressure | **Weymouth** | Conservative estimate, well-suited for gathering |
| Long line (20–100 mi), high pressure | **Panhandle A or B** | Lower friction factor assumed, more accurate |
| Low pressure (<100 psig), gathering mains | **Spitzglass** | Designed specifically for this regime |
| Multiphase flow, hilly terrain | **Beggs-Brill** | Accounts for flow pattern and elevation |
| Offshore riser systems | **Mukherjee-Brill** | Improved inclined-flow treatment |

## 3.4 The Compressor Power Equation (Adiabatic Compression)

When the gathering line cannot deliver the required flow at the available wellhead pressure, a **field booster compressor** is installed. The theoretical power required for adiabatic (isentropic) compression of a real gas is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{ideal} &= \frac{k}{k-1} \cdot \frac{P_1 Q_1}{33000 \eta} \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]
\end{aligned}$$

For natural gas with $k = 1.285$ (approximate ratio of specific heats), $(k-1)/k = 0.222$, so:

$$W_{ideal} = 4.5 \cdot \frac{P_1 Q_1}{33000 \eta} \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.222} - 1\right]$$

For **field units with Q in MMSCFD and P in psia, W in horsepower**, the constant changes to:

$$W = \frac{0.0857 \cdot Q \cdot Z \cdot T_1}{\eta} \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2857} - 1\right]$$

The polytropic exponent for natural gas is typically **n = 1.28 to 1.32** for field compression.

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define gas gathering. List any four major components of a gas gathering system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Gas gathering is the integrated system of low-to-medium pressure pipelines and auxiliary equipment that collects raw natural gas from individual wellheads and transports it to a central processing facility or main transmission line.

**Four Major Components:**
1. Wellhead Christmas tree and production choke
2. Flowlines connecting individual wells to manifolds
3. Satellite manifolds with initial phase separation
4. Gathering trunk lines
5. Field compressor stations (boosters)
6. Line heaters for hydrate prevention

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Distinguish between high-pressure and low-pressure gas gathering systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | High-Pressure Gathering | Low-Pressure Gathering |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Operating Pressure | 500 to 1500 psig | Below 500 psig (typically 50 to 200 psig) |
| Driving Force | Reservoir energy | Field compressor boosters required |
| Typical Field Stage | Early field life | Mature/depleted field |
| Capital Cost | Lower (no compression needed) | Higher (compressors required) |
| Application | New wells, high deliverability | Stripper wells, low-permeability reservoirs |
