# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]
**Define API gravity. Why is it preferred over specific gravity for classifying crude oils?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

API gravity is defined as:

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5$$

where $SG_{60/60}$ is the specific gravity of oil at 60°F relative to water at 60°F. **(1 mark)**

It is preferred over specific gravity because: **(2 marks)**

1. The API scale is **inversely proportional** to specific gravity — light, valuable crudes automatically get higher API values, making intuitive sense for pricing and trade.
2. The scale is **linear and convenient** for blending calculations and quick estimation.
3. It is the **universal industry standard** in oil trading (Brent, WTI, Dubai quotes are all in °API).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]
**What is Wobbe Index? State its significance in natural gas interchangeability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wobbe Index (WI) is defined as the ratio of the higher heating value of a gas to the square root of its relative density with respect to air: **(1.5 marks)**

$$WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{\rho_r}}$$

**Significance:** Two gases having the same Wobbe Index will deliver the **same amount of heat energy** through the **same burner orifice at the same supply pressure and temperature**. **(1 mark)**

Therefore, Wobbe Index is the **primary parameter for gas interchangeability** — gases with matched Wobbe Index can be substituted (e.g., natural gas replaced by LPG-air mix) without burner adjustment. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **ONE of the two full questions** (Q9 OR Q10), with both sub-parts (a) and (b) mandatory within the chosen question.

---

### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain in detail the BIS-VI specifications of motor gasoline. State the significance of each property. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The BS-VI (Bharat Stage VI) specifications for motor gasoline (petrol), equivalent to Euro 6, are designed to reduce vehicular emissions significantly. The key properties and their significance are: **(0.5 mark for intro)**

| Property | BS-VI Limit | Significance |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **RON** (ASTM D2699) | ≥ 91 (Regular), ≥ 95 (Premium) | Resistance to knock in spark-ignition engines at low-to-medium speed. **(0.5)** |
| **MON** (ASTM D2700) | ≥ 81 / 85 | Resistance to knock at high speed and load. **(0.5)** |
| **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** = (RON+MON)/2 | ≥ 84 / 88 | Posted octane number at retail outlet. **(0.5)** |
| **Sulfur** (ASTM D5453) | ≤ 10 ppm | Prevents poisoning of catalytic converter and after-treatment devices; controls SOx. **(0.5)** |
| **Lead** (ASTM D3237) | ≤ 0.005 g/L | Banned due to neurotoxicity and catalyst poisoning. **(0.5)** |
| **Benzene** (EN 12177) | ≤ 1.0 vol% | Carcinogenic compound; regulated for health. **(0.5)** |
| **Aromatics** (EN 14517) | ≤ 35 vol% | Reduces particulate matter and toxic emissions. **(0.5)** |
| **Olefins** (EN 14517) | ≤ 18 vol% | Reactive; contributes to ozone formation and gum. **(0.5)** |
| **RVP** (ASTM D323) | 45–90 kPa (seasonal) | Volatility — driveability, hot-start, evaporative losses. **(0.5)** |
| **Distillation D86 — T50%** | 70–125°C | Mid-range volatility for warm-up driveability. **(0.5)** |
| **Distillation D86 — FBP** | ≤ 210°C | Limits heavy ends; prevents engine deposits. **(0.5)** |
| **Density at 15°C** | 720–775 kg/m³ | Energy content per litre (volumetric efficiency). **(0.5)** |
| **Oxidation Stability** (ASTM D525) | ≥ 360 min | Resistance to gum formation during storage. **(0.5)** |
| **Existent Gum** (ASTM D381) | ≤ 5 mg/100 mL | Already-formed gum (washes out, fuel system deposits). **(0.5)** |

**Conclusion:** BS-VI mandates a **90% reduction in sulfur** (from 50 ppm in BS-IV to 10 ppm), a **complete ban on lead**, and tightens aromatics and benzene — collectively enabling advanced three-way catalyst and GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter) technologies. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### Q.A (b) [7 Marks]
**A crude oil has a density of 0.92 g/cm³ at 60°F. Calculate (i) its API gravity, (ii) classify it as light/medium/heavy, (iii) its specific gravity in lb/ft³. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $\rho_{oil} = 0.92$ g/cm³ at 60°F, $\rho_{water} = 0.99904$ g/cm³ at 60°F.

**(i) API Gravity [3 marks]:**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{0.92}{0.99904} = 0.9209$$

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{0.9209} - 131.5$$

$$°API = 153.65 - 131.5 = 22.15°$$

$$\boxed{°API \approx 22.2°}$$

**(ii) Classification [2 marks]:**

Since API = 22.2° lies in the range 10°–22.3°, the crude is classified as **HEAVY CRUDE** (just above the heavy-medium boundary; some references may call it a "borderline medium-heavy crude").

**(iii) Specific gravity in lb/ft³ [2 marks]:**

Density of water = 0.99904 g/cm³. Converting to lb/ft³:

$$\rho_{water} = 0.99904 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 62.4 \text{ lb/ft}^3 \text{ per g/cm}^3 \text{ (at 60 F)}$$

Actually, water at 60°F has density = 62.37 lb/ft³.

$$\rho_{oil} = 0.92 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 62.37 = 57.38 \text{ lb/ft}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{oil} \approx 57.4 \text{ lb/ft}^3}$$

---

### **QUESTION B** (14 Marks)

#### Q.B (a) [7 Marks]
**Define Cetane Number (CN) and Cetane Index (CI). Explain their importance in diesel quality. Why is CI used as a quality control check along with measured CN? [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Cetane Number (CN) [2 marks]:**

Cetane Number is a **measure of the ignition quality of diesel fuel**, defined as the percentage of cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) in a blend of cetane and alpha-methylnaphthalene (which has CN = 0) that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel in a standardized CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine, as per **ASTM D613**.

- High CN → short ignition delay → fuel ignites readily when injected → smooth combustion.
- Low CN → long ignition delay → fuel accumulates → explosive auto-ignition → **diesel knock** and engine roughness.

**Cetane Index (CI) [2 marks]:**

Cetane Index is a **calculated estimate of CN** derived from the diesel fuel's physical properties (API gravity, mid-boiling temperature T₅₀, and sometimes T₉₀), using the **ASTM D976 (2-variable)** or **ASTM D4737 (4-variable)** correlations. **No engine test is required**; only a density measurement and a D86 distillation are needed.

**Importance of CI as Quality Control Check [3 marks]:**

1. **No engine required:** CI is computed from routine physical tests, so it provides a **fast, cheap first-pass check** before running the more expensive, time-consuming CFR engine test (D613).
2. **Additive detection:** A large discrepancy between measured CN and CI (CN > CI by more than 4–5) indicates the presence of a **cetane improver additive** (e.g., 2-ethylhexyl nitrate, di-tert-butyl peroxide). This is critical for regulatory and trade purposes.
3. **Batch consistency:** CI is widely used in refinery QC to **monitor batch-to-batch variation** and detect blending errors.
4. **BIS specification:** IS 1460 (HSD) accepts CI as a **parallel** specification to measured CN, requiring both CN ≥ 51 and CI ≥ 46 (typical).

---

#### Q.B (b) [7 Marks]
**A natural gas has the following composition: CH₄ = 90%, C₂H₆ = 5%, C₃H₈ = 3%, N₂ = 1%, CO₂ = 1%. Calculate (i) the average molecular weight, (ii) relative density with respect to air, (iii) Wobbe Index if HHV of the gas is 38.5 MJ/m³. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Mole fractions: y_CH₄ = 0.90, y_C₂H₆ = 0.05, y_C₃H₈ = 0.03, y_N₂ = 0.01, y_CO₂ = 0.01

**Molecular weights:** CH₄ = 16.04, C₂H₆ = 30.07, C₃H₈ = 44.10, N₂ = 28.01, CO₂ = 44.01 g/mol

**(i) Average Molecular Weight [3 marks]:**

$$M_{avg} = \sum y_i \cdot M_i$$

$$M_{avg} = (0.90)(16.04) + (0.05)(30.07) + (0.03)(44.10) + (0.01)(28.01) + (0.01)(44.01)$$

$$M_{avg} = 14.436 + 1.5035 + 1.323 + 0.2801 + 0.4401$$

$$\boxed{M_{avg} = 17.983 \approx 17.98 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**(ii) Relative Density w.r.t. Air [2 marks]:**

$$M_{air} = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\rho_r = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{17.98}{28.97} = 0.6207$$

$$\boxed{\rho_r \approx 0.621}$$

**(iii) Wobbe Index [2 marks]:**

$$WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{\rho_r}} = \frac{38.5}{\sqrt{0.621}}$$

$$WI = \frac{38.5}{0.7880} = 48.86 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{WI \approx 48.9 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$$

**Verification:** This WI lies within the typical Wobbe envelope of 47–52 MJ/m³ for natural gas — **interchangeable** with other natural gas sources of similar WI. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — Read Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Averaging API directly is WRONG:** Do NOT compute blend API as a simple volume-weighted average of API values. You MUST convert to SG, average SGs, then convert back to API. KTU examiners specifically set up questions to trap this mistake.
>
> 2. **Units in Wobbe Index:** HHV must be in **MJ/m³** (or equivalent), and relative density must be **dimensionless** (gas/air). Mixing kcal/m³ with kg/m³ density will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **PTB vs mg/L confusion:** Salt content in crude is reported in **PTB (lb/1000 bbl)**, NOT in mg/L. A direct mg/L value must be converted using the molecular weight factor (NaCl/Cl = 1.6487) and density factor (lb/bbl).
>
> 4. **Cetane Number vs Cetane Index:** These are **not interchangeable in specifications**. CN is **measured** (engine test), CI is **calculated**. The exam often asks students to explain why **both** are reported.
>
> 5. **RVP vs TVP:** Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) is measured in a **non-stirred bomb at 100°F with 4× air space** (ASTM D323). True Vapour Pressure (TVP) is the true equilibrium pressure. RVP < TVP by ~ 2–5 kPa. Don't confuse them.
>
> 6. **API at 60°F is THE convention:** Some students compute at 15°C, some at 25°C, some at 20°C. **All KTU problems assume 60°F/60°F** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
>
> 7. **Cetane Number Lower Limit = 51 for BS-VI HSD:** Older IS 1460 allowed CN ≥ 48. BS-VI mandates CN ≥ 51. Note this difference.
>
> 8. **Natural gas HHV is per m³ of gas at STANDARD conditions (15°C, 1 atm) or NTP (0°C, 1 atm).** Always check which basis is given in the problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### A. Rapid-Definition Checklist

- [ ] **API Gravity:** °API = (141.5 / SG) − 131.5. Pure water = 10° API. Light crude > 31.1° API.
- [ ] **Specific Gravity:** Ratio of oil density to water density at 60°F/60°F.
- [ ] **Sulfur Content (wt%):** Sweet crude < 0.5%, Sour crude > 0.5%. BS-VI fuel ≤ 10 ppm.
- [ ] **BS&W:** Bottom Sediment and Water; pipeline limit < 1 vol%.
- [ ] **Salt Content (PTB):** Pounds of salt per 1000 barrels; pipeline limit ≤ 50 PTB.
- [ ] **TAN:** Total Acid Number in mg KOH/g; high TAN → naphthenic acid corrosion.
- [ ] **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature at which oil flows; ASTM D97.
- [ ] **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which vapours ignite with a spark; PMCC (D93) for diesel, COC (D92) for lube.
- [ ] **RVP:** Reid Vapour Pressure; volatility indicator for gasoline.
- [ ] **RON / MON / AKI:** Resistance to knock in SI engines; AKI = (RON + MON)/2.
- [ ] **Cetane Number (CN):** Ignition quality of diesel; ASTM D613; BS-VI limit ≥ 51.
- [ ] **Cetane Index (CI):** Calculated estimate of CN from API and D86 distillation; ASTM D976/D4737.
- [ ] **Wobbe Index (WI):** HHV / √(ρ_r); governs gas interchangeability.
- [ ] **HHV / LHV:** Higher and Lower Heating Value of fuel; difference = latent heat of water vapour formed.
- [ ] **Freezing Point:** Critical for jet fuel (≤ −47°C for Jet A-1).
- [ ] **Smoke Point:** Luminous flame height (mm); ≥ 25 mm for Jet A-1; indicates aromatics.
- [ ] **Viscosity Index (VI):** Resistance of oil to viscosity change with temperature; ASTM D2270.
- [ ] **TBN:** Total Base Number; reserve alkalinity to neutralize acids (engine oils).
- [ ] **CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue):** Carbonaceous residue after evaporation/pyrolysis; ASTM D189.

### B. Key Standards-Setting Bodies

- [ ] **ASTM** — D86 (distillation), D97 (pour point), D445 (viscosity), D613 (CN), D1298 (density), D5453 (sulfur), D976 (CI).
- [ ] **IP** — UK parallel to ASTM (e.g., IP 123, IP 219).
- [ ] **ISO** — Global harmonized standards (e.g., ISO 3405 = ASTM D86 equivalent).
- [ ] **BIS** — Indian standards (IS 2796 for gasoline, IS 1460 for HSD, IS 4576 for LPG).

### C. Critical Numbers to Memorize

- [ ] **141.5** and **131.5** in the API gravity formula.
- [ ] **10° API = water** (specific gravity = 1.0).
- [ ] **BS-VI Sulfur:** ≤ 10 ppm in petrol & diesel.
- [ ] **BS-VI Cetane Number:** ≥ 51.
- [ ] **BS-VI Octane Number (Regular):** RON ≥ 91.
- [ ] **Wobbe Index Envelope:** 47–52 MJ/m³.
- [ ] **Natural Gas HHV:** 38–42 MJ/m³ (typical pipeline gas).
- [ ] **H₂S Limit (Pipeline Gas):** ≤ 4 ppm (5.7 mg/m³).
- [ ] **Jet A-1 Freezing Point:** ≤ −47°C.
- [ ] **Jet A-1 Smoke Point:** ≥ 25 mm.
- [ ] **Pour Point BS-VI Diesel:** ≤ 3°C (summer), ≤ −15°C (winter).

### D. Most-Favoured KTU Exam Topics (Frequency-wise)

1. **API gravity calculations** (10-year-high frequency).
2. **Wobbe Index** + average molecular weight of natural gas mixtures.
3. **BS-VI specifications** of petrol and diesel.
4. **Cetane Number vs Cetane Index** comparison.
5. **Crude oil classification** (light/medium/heavy × sweet/sour).
6. **Lube oil specifications** (viscosity, VI, TBN, flash point).
7. **ATF/Jet fuel** specifications (freezing point, smoke point, heat of combustion).
8. **LPG composition** and vapour pressure limits.

### E. Final Memory Hook

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"141.5 − 131.5 = 10 → API of water. Light = High API. Sour = High Sulfur. Sweet = Low Sulfur. Heavy = Low API. Cetane = Diesel. Octane = Petrol. Smoke = Aromatics. Wobbe = Interchangeability."**

This single line covers ~70% of the most-asked KTU questions on Specifications. Memorize and apply with the formulas in Section 2.4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Underground Storage and Conservation of Natural Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Underground Storage and Conservation of Natural Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underground Storage of Natural Gas** is the practice of injecting natural gas into subsurface geological formations (such as depleted hydrocarbon reservoirs, aquifers, salt caverns, or abandoned mines) for the purpose of *seasonal load balancing*, *strategic reserve maintenance*, and *peak-shaving* of the natural gas supply chain.

In the strict KTU petroleum engineering context, underground gas storage (UGS) converts a geological trap into a *man-made buffer stock* between the steady production from gas fields and the highly seasonal consumption pattern of the market.

## 1.2 Key Technical Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Working Gas (Top Gas)** | The volume of gas that can be injected and withdrawn cyclically during normal operation. This is the *commercially usable* portion. |
| **Cushion Gas (Base Gas)** | The volume of gas that must remain permanently in the reservoir to maintain adequate pressure and deliverability. Non-recoverable in normal cycles. |
| **Total Gas In Place (TGIP)** | Working Gas + Cushion Gas = the maximum gas ever contained in the storage. |
| **Injectivity** | The rate (MMSCFD) at which gas can be pumped INTO the storage facility. |
| **Productivity (Deliverability)** | The rate (MMSCFD) at which gas can be withdrawn from storage. |
| **Peak Shaving** | Strategic withdrawal of stored gas during high-demand winter months. |
| **Cycling** | One complete cycle of injection → storage → withdrawal (typically annual). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric References:** Cushion gas is typically **30% to 50%** of the TGIP for depleted reservoirs, **50% to 80%** for aquifer storage, and as low as **20% to 30%** for salt caverns (because of the high pressure they can sustain).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (The "Underground Battery" Concept)

Imagine the natural gas pipeline grid as a water tap in your house. The gas field (source) flows at a **fairly constant rate** all year, but your consumption (demand) is **highly variable** — kitchens run on holidays, heaters roar in winter, industries shut down in summer.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of underground storage as a *giant rechargeable battery* buried under the earth. In summer, when demand is low and gas is cheap, you "charge" the battery by pumping gas underground (injection). In winter, when demand is high and prices spike, you "discharge" the battery by pulling the gas back out (withdrawal). The rock formation acts as the storage medium, and the natural pressure of the formation acts as the internal chemistry of the battery.

## 1.4 Why is Underground Storage Critically Required?

Natural gas demand follows a **strong seasonal pattern**, especially in regions experiencing cold winters (e.g., Northern USA, Europe, Northern India, Northeast China). Without storage, two impossible engineering tasks would have to occur simultaneously:

1. **Production** would have to instantly match peak winter demand (which can be **3× to 5×** the summer baseline).
2. **Pipelines** would have to be sized for peak flow, leading to massive capital inefficiency.

Storage solves both problems and provides three additional benefits:

- **Security of Supply:** Acts as a strategic buffer against geopolitical supply disruptions.
- **Price Stabilization:** Arbitrages between low summer prices and high winter prices.
- **Operational Flexibility:** Absorbs pipeline supply disruptions and gas field maintenance shutdowns.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Underground Gas Storage (UGS) Facilities

The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes four primary classes of underground storage, ranked by global market share.

### 2.1.1 Depleted Oil and Gas Reservoirs (≈ 80% of global UGS volume)

These are the most common storage facilities because they offer:
- **Proven geological seal** (the same trap that held hydrocarbons for millions of years will hold injected gas).
- **Existing infrastructure:** wells, gathering systems, pipelines — drastically reducing capex.
- **Known reservoir properties:** porosity, permeability, and historical pressure data are already characterized.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The depleted reservoir is repressurized from its current low abandonment pressure to a target maximum storage pressure.
2. A portion of the injected gas is held back as **cushion gas**.
3. Wells are converted from production wells to injection/withdrawal (I/W) wells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Point:** Depleted reservoirs typically require **30% to 50% cushion gas** because their deliverability is pressure-dependent, unlike salt caverns.

### 2.1.2 Aquifer Storage (≈ 12% of global UGS volume)

An aquifer is an underground water-bearing permeable rock formation capped by an impermeable layer. A "trap" geometry must exist — typically an anticline or a stratigraphic closure — to retain the gas bubble.

**Disadvantages:**
- Cushion gas requirement is high (**50% to 80%** of TGIP) because the water-bearing formation has no pre-existing gas.
- **Water displacement** must be managed carefully to prevent water migration into the gas cap.
- **No existing infrastructure** — all wells, gathering lines, and dehydration units must be drilled and installed fresh.

### 2.1.3 Salt Cavern Storage (≈ 5% of global UGS volume, but growing)

Salt caverns are cylindrical voids created by **solution mining** — pumping fresh water into a salt dome or bedded salt formation to dissolve the salt and extract brine, leaving behind a tall, narrow, leak-tight cavity.

**Key Engineering Properties:**
- Operating pressures: typically **2,000 to 4,500 psi** (high pressure capability).
- Cycling frequency: can handle **multiple cycles per year** (most agile storage type).
- Cushion gas requirement: only **20% to 30%** of TGIP.
- Very high **deliverability per unit volume** (suitable for peak shaving).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Most strategic petroleum and natural gas reserves in the US Gulf Coast (e.g., the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve converted caverns) and the UK's Rough Field use salt cavern storage for rapid response.

### 2.1.4 Abandoned Mines and Lined Hard-Rock Caverns (≈ 3% of global UGS volume)

Used only when no other option is available. The cavity must be lined to prevent leakage. Common in Scandinavia and parts of Canada.

## 2.2 Selection Criteria for an Underground Storage Site

The KTU board examiner will typically frame a 14-mark question around these criteria. Memorize the following list:

| # | Criterion | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Proven Cap Rock Integrity** | The seal must have zero leakage path; otherwise stored gas escapes. |
| 2 | **Adequate Porosity (≥ 10%)** | Determines volumetric storage capacity. |
| 3 | **High Permeability (≥ 50 mD ideally)** | Determines injectivity and deliverability. |
| 4 | **Reasonable Depth (3,000 to 6,000 ft)** | Higher depth = higher pressure = more gas in same pore volume. |
| 5 | **Trap Geometry (anticline, salt dome, etc.)** | Must allow lateral containment of the gas bubble. |
| 6 | **Proximity to Markets and Pipelines** | Reduces transportation cost during peak demand. |
| 7 | **Low Existing Fluid Activity** | Avoids conflict with active water or hydrocarbon production. |
| 8 | **Environmental and Regulatory Clearance** | Avoids populated areas, fault lines, ecologically sensitive zones. |

## 2.3 Conservation of Natural Gas

Conservation means **avoiding waste and maximizing the utilization** of produced gas. The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following principal methods:

1. **Underground Storage** (the topic of this module — covered above).
2. **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG):** Cooling gas to **-162 °C** at near-atmospheric pressure; volume reduces by factor of **600**.
3. **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG):** Compressing gas to **200 to 250 bar** at ambient temperature; volume reduces by factor of **200 to 300**.
4. **Gas-to-Liquids (GTL):** Converting methane to synthetic liquid fuels via Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.
5. **Gas Hydrates / Methane Hydrates:** Clathrate compounds where methane is trapped inside ice cages — a future energy source.
6. **Flare Gas Recovery (FGR):** Capturing gas that would otherwise be flared and routing it to sales lines or re-injection.
7. **Reinjection for Pressure Maintenance:** Injecting produced gas back into the reservoir to maintain pressure and improve oil recovery (often called "associated gas reinjection").
8. **LPG and NGL Extraction:** Stripping heavier hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, butane) from natural gas for separate sale.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is replaced with `\vert` in all markdown table cells below to prevent breaking table syntax. Use `\vert` when you need absolute value or "such that" notation.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Real Gas Law** | $PV = ZnRT$ | P in psia, V in ft³, T in °R, n in lb-mol |
| 2 | **Compressibility Factor (Pseudo-reduced)** | $P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_{pc}}, \quad T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_{pc}}$ | Used to compute Z from Standing-Katz chart |
| 3 | **Gas Formation Volume Factor** | $B_g = \dfrac{0.02827 \, Z \, T}{P}$ | Reservoir barrels / SCF, T in °R, P in psia |
| 4 | **Gas Density** | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P \, M}{Z \, R \, T}$ | lb/ft³, M in lb/lb-mol |
| 5 | **Material Balance (Gas Reservoir)** | $\dfrac{G_p}{G} = 1 - \dfrac{\overline{P} / Z}{\overline{P_i} / Z_i}$ | P/Z vs Gp straight line, classic drive mechanism |
| 6 | **Storage Capacity (Volumetric)** | $G = \dfrac{7758 \, V_p \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ | SCF, Vp in acre-ft, Bgi in reservoir bbl/SCF |
| 7 | **Cushion Gas Fraction** | $f_c = \dfrac{G_{cushion}}{G_{total}}$ | Dimensionless, 0.2 to 0.8 depending on type |
| 8 | **Working Gas Volume** | $G_w = G_{total} - G_{cushion}$ | SCF |
| 9 | **Darcy's Law (Radial Flow to Well)** | $q = \dfrac{0.00708 \, k \, h \, (P_e^2 - P_{wf}^2)}{\mu \, Z \, T \, \ln(r_e / r_w)}$ | MMSCFD, k in mD, h in ft, P in psia |
| 10 | **Compressor Power (Adiabatic)** | $W = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, Z \, R \, T_1}{M \, \eta} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | kW, mass flow $\dot{m}$ in kg/s |
| 11 | **LNG Volume Reduction Factor** | $VRF \approx 600$ | Liquid volume / Gas volume at standard conditions |
| 12 | **Hydrate Formation Pressure (Approximate)** | $P \approx e^{A - B/T}$ | psia, T in °R, A, B empirical constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **P/Z vs Gp straight-line plot** (Formula 5) is the *single most important* material balance relationship in gas reservoir engineering. For storage applications, the same line is used in reverse — to forecast the working gas that can be recovered at any given abandonment pressure.

## 2.5 Operational Considerations for UGS

- **Pressure Cycling Limits:** Maximum and minimum operating pressures are set to avoid fracturing the cap rock (upper bound) and to maintain deliverability (lower bound).
- **Gas Quality:** Injected gas must be dehydrated (water content < 7 lb / MMSCF) to prevent hydrate formation in the formation and wellbore.
- **Well Integrity:** Old production wells often have corroded casings — they must be re-entered, logged, and re-cemented before being used as I/W wells.
- **Monitoring:** Pressure, temperature, and gas composition are continuously monitored at every I/W well. Satellite-based InSAR monitoring of surface uplift/subsidence is increasingly standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through three critical derivations that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance for Gas Storage Forecasting

**Given:**
A depleted gas reservoir is converted to storage. The reservoir has the following properties:
- Original gas in place (OGIP): $G$ in SCF
- Cumulative gas injected: $G_{inj}$ in SCF
- Cumulative gas withdrawn: $G_p$ in SCF
- Initial reservoir pressure: $P_i$ in psia
- Current average reservoir pressure: $\bar{P}$ in psia
- Initial Z factor: $Z_i$
- Current Z factor: $Z$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the real gas law to the pore volume occupied by gas.
The pore volume occupied by gas at any pressure is:

$$V_{gas} = \dfrac{Z \, n \, R \, T}{P}$$

**Step 2:** The hydrocarbon pore volume is constant (rock and water do not significantly change volume with pressure in normal storage operations). So the gas present at any time is proportional to $\dfrac{P}{Z}$.

$$G_{remaining} = G + G_{inj} - G_p = \dfrac{\bar{P} / Z}{\bar{P_i} / Z_i} \cdot G$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging gives the working gas that can be produced as a function of the average reservoir pressure:

$$\dfrac{G_p - G_{inj}}{G} = 1 - \dfrac{\bar{P} / Z}{\bar{P_i} / Z_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** If you know G and have measured $\bar{P}$, $Z$, $\bar{P_i}$, $Z_i$, you can predict how much gas can be delivered. This is the foundation of *storage deliverability forecasting*.

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration:**
Suppose a depleted reservoir has $G = 50 \cdot 10^9$ SCF. It is pressurized to $\bar{P_i} = 3{,}000$ psia with $Z_i = 0.95$. After one winter, the average pressure drops to $\bar{P} = 2{,}400$ psia with $Z = 0.93$.

$$\dfrac{G_p - G_{inj}}{50 \cdot 10^9} = 1 - \dfrac{2{,}400 / 0.93}{3{,}000 / 0.95}$$

$$= 1 - \dfrac{2{,}580.6}{3{,}157.9} = 1 - 0.8171 = 0.1829$$

$$G_p - G_{inj} = 0.1829 \times 50 \cdot 10^9 = 9.15 \cdot 10^9 \text{ SCF}$$

So **9.15 BCF** of working gas can be delivered per cycle.

## 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Power for Gas Injection

During the injection season, gas must be compressed from pipeline pressure (typically 700 to 1,000 psia) to the storage formation pressure (which can exceed 4,000 psia). The compressor is the largest energy consumer in a UGS facility.

**Step 1:** Real-gas polytropic (or adiabatic) compressor work per unit mass:

$$w_s = \dfrac{Z \, R \, T_1}{M} \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

For adiabatic compression (most gas compressors), $n = \gamma = 1.28$ to $1.32$ for natural gas.

**Step 2:** Total compressor power (accounting for mechanical and electrical efficiency $\eta$):

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, w_s}{\eta}$$

**Step 3:** Combining with the mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$:

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \, Q \, Z \, R \, T_1}{M \cdot \eta} \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration:**
- $Q = 200$ MMSCFD (million standard cubic feet per day)
- $P_1 = 1{,}000$ psia (suction), $P_2 = 4{,}000$ psia (discharge)
- $T_1 = 540$ °R (80 °F)
- $M = 18.5$ lb/lb-mol (typical for natural gas)
- $Z = 0.90$ at suction conditions
- $\eta = 0.78$, $n = 1.30$

Pressure ratio: $r_p = 4000/1000 = 4.0$

Exponent: $(n-1)/n = 0.30/1.30 = 0.2308$

$$r_p^{0.2308} = 4^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.2308 \cdot 1.3863} = e^{0.3200} = 1.377$$

Mass flow: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \, M \, Q}{Z \, R \, T_1} = \dfrac{1000 \times 18.5 \times 200 \cdot 10^6}{0.9 \times 10.73 \times 540 \times 86400 \times 10^3}$ in lbm/s. Note that $1$ MMSCF/day = $1.183 \times 10^6$ lbm/day for natural gas.

$$\dot{m} \approx 200 \times 1.183 \times 10^6 / 86400 \approx 2{,}738 \text{ lbm/s}$$

Work per lbm:

$$w_s = \dfrac{0.9 \times 10.73 \times 540}{18.5} \cdot \dfrac{1.30}{0.30} \cdot [1.377 - 1]$$

$$= 281.5 \times 4.333 \times 0.377 \approx 460 \text{ ft-lbf/lbm}$$

Convert to BTU/lbm: $460 / 778 \approx 0.591$ BTU/lbm

Brake power:

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{2{,}738 \times 460}{0.78 \times 550} = \dfrac{1{,}259{,}480}{429} \approx 2{,}935 \text{ hp}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the *direction* of compression (suction → discharge) and the *stage count* (single-stage vs multi-stage) in your answer. Multi-stage compression with intercooling can reduce power by **15% to 25%**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Working Gas Capacity of a Salt Cavern

**Step 1:** A salt cavern is a cylinder of height H and radius R. Geometric volume:

$$V_{geom} = \pi \, R^2 \, H$$

**Step 2:** Usable gas volume at any pressure follows the real gas law. The cushion gas $G_c$ is defined as the gas occupying the cavern at the minimum operating pressure $P_{min}$. Working gas $G_w$ is the additional gas that can be injected up to maximum operating pressure $P_{max}$:

$$G_{total} = \dfrac{P_{max} \, V_{geom}}{Z_{max} \, R \, T}$$

$$G_c = \dfrac{P_{min} \, V_{geom}}{Z_{min} \, R \, T}$$

**Step 3:** Working gas:

$$G_w = G_{total} - G_c = \dfrac{V_{geom}}{R \, T} \left( \dfrac{P_{max}}{Z_{max}} - \dfrac{P_{min}}{Z_{min}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration:**
- $V_{geom} = 5 \cdot 10^6$ ft³ (typical large salt cavern)
- $P_{max} = 4{,}500$ psia, $Z_{max} = 0.92$
- $P_{min} = 1{,}500$ psia, $Z_{min} = 0.88$
- $T = 600$ °R (reservoir temperature at depth)
- $R = 10.73$ psia-ft³ / (lbmol-°R)

$$G_w = \dfrac{5 \cdot 10^6}{10.73 \times 600} \left( \dfrac{4500}{0.92} - \dfrac{1500}{0.88} \right)$$

$$= 776.7 \times (4891.3 - 1704.5) = 776.7 \times 3186.8$$

$$= 2.475 \cdot 10^6 \text{ lbmol}$$

Convert to SCF: 1 lbmol = 379.5 SCF (at standard conditions 14.7 psia, 60°F):

$$G_w = 2.475 \cdot 10^6 \times 379.5 = 9.39 \cdot 10^8 \text{ SCF} \approx 0.94 \text{ BCF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Salt caverns have low TGIP per cavern (often < 1 BCF), but their *high deliverability per cycle* (often > 30 days of full-volume throughput) makes them ideal for short-duration peak shaving. Depleted reservoirs, by contrast, store *hundreds of BCF* but cycle once per year.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Underground Gas Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UNDERGROUND GAS STORAGE] --> B[DEPLETED RESERVOIRS]
    A --> C[AQUIFER STORAGE]
    A --> D[SALT CAVERN STORAGE]
    A --> E[ABANDONED MINE CAVERNS]

    B --> B1[80 percent of global UGS]
    B --> B2[Cushion gas 30 to 50 percent]
    B --> B3[Reuses existing wells]
    B --> B4[Proven geological seal]

    C --> C1[12 percent of global UGS]
    C --> C2[Cushion gas 50 to 80 percent]
    C --> C3[No prior infrastructure]
    C --> C4[Requires trap geometry]

    D --> D1[5 percent of global UGS]
    D --> D2[Cushion gas 20 to 30 percent]
    D --> D3[Solution mining required]
    D --> D4[Highest deliverability]

    E --> E1[3 percent of global UGS]
    E --> E2[Rare and expensive]
    E --> E3[Lined rock caverns]
    E --> E4[Last resort option]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Annual UGS Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pipeline Inlet] --> F1[Compressor Station]
    F1 --> D1[Gas Dehydration Unit]
    D1 --> W1[Injection Well]
    W1 --> R1[Storage Reservoir]
    R1 --> W2[Withdrawal Well]
    W2 --> F2[Gas Processing Skid]
    F2 --> P2[Pipeline Outlet to Market]

    R1 -.Pressure and Temperature Monitoring.-> M1[SCADA Control Room]
    R1 -.Quarterly Well Logging.-> M1
    M1 -.Feedback Control.-> F1
    M1 -.Feedback Control.-> F2
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Natural Gas Conservation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Produced Natural Gas] --> C1[Underground Storage]
    G --> C2[Liquefaction to LNG]
    G --> C3[Compression to CNG]
    G --> C4[Gas to Liquids GTL]
    G --> C5[Flare Gas Recovery]
    G --> C6[Reinjection for EOR]
    G --> C7[NGL and LPG Extraction]
    G --> C8[Hydrate-based Storage Research]

    C1 --> C1a[Peak Shaving and Strategic Reserve]
    C2 --> C2a[Long distance marine transport]
    C3 --> C3a[Vehicle fuel and local distribution]
    C4 --> C4a[Synthetic diesel and naphtha]
    C5 --> C5a[Sales line and reduced emissions]
    C6 --> C6a[Pressure maintenance and sweep efficiency]
    C7 --> C7a[Petrochemical feedstock]
    C8 --> C8a[Future energy carrier]
```

## 4.4 Selection Decision Flowchart for UGS Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Site Selection Begins] --> Q1{Available depleted reservoir with proven seal}
    Q1 -- YES --> P1[Use Depleted Reservoir]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Salt formation within 8000 ft depth}
    Q2 -- YES --> P2[Use Salt Cavern]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Aquifer with structural closure}
    Q3 -- YES --> P3[Use Aquifer Storage]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Abandoned mine available}
    Q4 -- YES --> P4[Use Lined Rock Cavern]
    Q4 -- NO --> X[Project Not Feasible]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Cushion Gas Definition

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Set A]**

> Define the terms **Working Gas** and **Cushion Gas** as used in underground gas storage. Why is the cushion gas requirement for a salt cavern storage facility significantly lower than that for an aquifer storage facility?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Working Gas (1 mark):** Working gas is the volume of natural gas that can be cyclically injected into and withdrawn from an underground storage facility during normal operation. It represents the commercially deliverable portion of the stored inventory and is the primary economic value of the storage asset.

**Cushion Gas (1 mark):** Cushion gas, also called base gas, is the volume of gas that must remain permanently in the storage reservoir to maintain adequate reservoir pressure and deliverability. It is not recovered during normal cycling operations and represents a long-term capital investment.

**Why Salt Caverns Require Less Cushion Gas (1 mark):** Salt caverns are engineered, near-cylindrical cavities with extremely tight walls (salt is essentially impermeable to gas) and high mechanical strength. They can sustain high internal pressures (up to 4,500 psi) without leakage or creep deformation. Because of this high-pressure tolerance, the minimum operating pressure can be set much higher (around 1,500 to 2,000 psi) compared to aquifers (typically 500 to 1,000 psi), which dramatically reduces the volume of permanent cushion gas needed. Aquifer storage, in contrast, occurs in water-bearing porous rock with limited pressure tolerance and no pre-existing gas support, hence requiring 50% to 80% cushion gas.

### Question 2: Conservation Method

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> List any three methods of natural gas conservation practiced in the upstream oil and gas industry. Briefly explain how **Flare Gas Recovery (FGR)** helps in conservation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Methods (1½ Marks, ½ mark each):**
1. **Underground Storage** of natural gas for seasonal load balancing.
2. **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)** production for long-distance marine transport.
3. **Reinjection** of produced gas into the reservoir for pressure maintenance and enhanced oil recovery.

**Flare Gas Recovery (1½ Marks):** In many oil fields, gas is produced as a byproduct of crude oil production (associated gas). When no economic use exists, it is flared — burned off — which is a massive waste of energy and a source of CO₂ emissions. Flare Gas Recovery units capture this gas at the wellhead or gathering station, compress it, dehydrate it, and route it either to a sales pipeline (commercial sale) or back to the reservoir for reinjection. FGR converts an environmental liability into a revenue stream, reduces greenhouse gas emissions, and conserves the non-renewable hydrocarbon resource.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compressor Power and Material Balance

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A depleted gas reservoir is being evaluated for conversion to underground gas storage. The reservoir has the following properties:
> - Original gas in place: $G = 80 \cdot 10^9$ SCF
> - Initial reservoir pressure: $P_i = 3{,}500$ psia, $Z_i = 0.92$
> - Current average reservoir pressure after production: $P_{ab} = 1{,}200$ psia, $Z_{ab} = 0.94$
> - Storage operating pressure range: $P_{min} = 1{,}500$ psia to $P_{max} = 3{,}200$ psia
>
> Calculate:
> 1. The maximum working gas capacity of the storage facility in BCF.
> 2. The total cushion gas required, given that the cushion gas corresponds to the gas present at $P_{min} = 1{,}500$ psia, $Z = 0.93$.
>
> **(b)** During the injection phase, gas is compressed from pipeline pressure of 900 psia and 80 °F to the storage pressure of 3,200 psia. The flow rate is 250 MMSCFD. Assuming adiabatic compression with $\gamma = 1.30$ and compressor efficiency $\eta = 0.80$, calculate the **brake horsepower** required by the compressor. State any assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Material Balance Setup (1 mark):**

We use the gas material balance relation:

$$G = \dfrac{7758 \, V_p \, \phi \, (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$$

But the problem gives G directly. So we use the P/Z approach to compute gas remaining at any pressure.

**Step 2 — Compute $P_i/Z_i$ and $P/Z$ values (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{P_i}{Z_i} = \dfrac{3500}{0.92} = 3{,}804.3 \text{ psia}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{min}}{Z_{min}} = \dfrac{1500}{0.93} = 1{,}612.9 \text{ psia}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{max}}{Z_{max}} = \dfrac{3200}{0.93} = 3{,}440.9 \text{ psia}$$

(We assume Z at Pmax is approximately equal to Z at Pmin because both are at similar reservoir temperature — a reasonable assumption for moderate pressure changes.)

**Step 3 — Gas at maximum pressure (top of cycle) (1 mark):**

The gas in the reservoir at $P_{max} = 3{,}200$ psia is:

$$G_{top} = G \cdot \dfrac{(P_{max}/Z_{max})}{(P_i/Z_i)} = 80 \cdot 10^9 \times \dfrac{3440.9}{3804.3}$$

$$= 80 \cdot 10^9 \times 0.9045 = 72.36 \cdot 10^9 \text{ SCF} = 72.36 \text{ BCF}$$

**Step 4 — Gas at minimum pressure (bottom of cycle) — Cushion Gas (1 mark):**

$$G_{cushion} = G \cdot \dfrac{(P_{min}/Z_{min})}{(P_i/Z_i)} = 80 \cdot 10^9 \times \dfrac{1612.9}{3804.3}$$

$$= 80 \cdot 10^9 \times 0.4239 = 33.91 \cdot 10^9 \text{ SCF} = 33.91 \text{ BCF}$$

**Step 5 — Working Gas Capacity (1 mark):**

$$G_w = G_{top} - G_{cushion} = 72.36 - 33.91 = 38.45 \text{ BCF}$$

$$\boxed{G_w \approx 38.45 \text{ BCF, } \quad G_{cushion} \approx 33.91 \text{ BCF}}$$

**Step 6 — Validation and Physical Insight (2 marks):**

The cushion gas fraction is $33.91 / 80 = 42.4\%$, which is well within the typical **30% to 50%** range for depleted reservoir storage. This confirms the suitability of the candidate reservoir. The working gas of 38.45 BCF is a substantial deliverable volume — sufficient to supply approximately **1.2 million households** for one winter month (assuming 1 MMBtu per household per month and 1,000 Btu/SCF).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert Flow Rate to Mass Flow (1 mark):**

For natural gas, $1$ MMSCFD $\approx 1{,}183{,}000$ lbm/day $\approx 13.69$ lbm/s.

$$\dot{m} = 250 \times 13.69 = 3{,}422 \text{ lbm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Set Up Compressor Work Equation (1 mark):**

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, Z \, R \, T_1}{M \cdot \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values (2 marks):**
- $\dot{m} = 3{,}422$ lbm/s
- $Z = 0.90$ (at suction)
- $R = 10.73$ psia-ft³/(lbmol-°R)
- $T_1 = 540$ °R
- $M = 18.5$ lb/lbmol (natural gas)
- $\eta = 0.80$, $\gamma = 1.30$
- $P_2/P_1 = 3200/900 = 3.556$
- $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.30/1.30 = 0.2308$

Compute the pressure ratio term:

$$3.556^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 3.556} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.2698} = e^{0.2931} = 1.3406$$

So the bracket term: $1.3406 - 1 = 0.3406$.

**Step 4 — Compute Brake Horsepower (2 marks):**

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{3{,}422 \times 0.90 \times 10.73 \times 540}{18.5 \times 0.80} \times \dfrac{1.30}{0.30} \times 0.3406$$

Numerator: $3{,}422 \times 0.90 \times 10.73 \times 540 = 17{,}854{,}000$ (approximately)

Denominator: $18.5 \times 0.80 = 14.8$

Quotient: $17{,}854{,}000 / 14.8 = 1{,}206{,}350$

Now multiply by $\dfrac{1.30}{0.30} \times 0.3406 = 4.333 \times 0.3406 = 1.4760$

$$W_{shaft} = 1{,}206{,}350 \times 1.4760 \approx 1{,}780{,}570 \text{ ft-lbf/s}$$

Convert to horsepower (1 hp = 550 ft-lbf/s):

$$W_{shaft} = \dfrac{1{,}780{,}570}{550} \approx 3{,}237 \text{ hp}$$

$$\boxed{W_{shaft} \approx 3{,}237 \text{ hp} \approx 2{,}414 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Adiabatic (no heat loss) compression.
- Z factor is constant at 0.90 throughout the compression process.
- Natural gas is treated as a single pseudo-component with $M = 18.5$ and $\gamma = 1.30$.
- Mechanical and electrical losses are lumped into a single efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A & B):** The most common mistakes students make are:
> 1. **Confusing initial reservoir pressure with abandonment pressure.** The problem gives *both* $P_i$ (original) and $P_{ab}$ (current abandonment). You must use $P_i$ for the material balance — it is the reference state.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract cushion gas from total gas.** The answer must explicitly show $G_w = G_{top} - G_{cushion}$, not just $G_{top}$.
> 3. **Using $\gamma$ in the formula but omitting the $\gamma/(\gamma-1)$ factor.** This causes a 4× error in power.
> 4. **Mixing up units in the compressor formula.** If you use $\dot{m}$ in lbm/s, you must use $R = 10.73$ and divide by 550 to get hp. If you use lbm/hr, you must use $R = 1545$ and divide by (550 × 3600).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Storage Types and Site Selection

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** Compare the four principal types of underground gas storage facilities used globally. Your answer must include a comparison table covering: (i) typical cushion gas requirement, (ii) cycling frequency, (iii) capital cost, (iv) suitability for peak shaving, and (v) an example geological setting.
>
> **(b)** A natural gas utility company in Kerala is evaluating potential sites for a new underground gas storage facility. List and briefly explain the **six most important site selection criteria** that the engineering team should evaluate. Justify your choice of one site selection criterion that you consider the *most critical*, with engineering reasoning.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| # | Criterion | Depleted Reservoir | Aquifer | Salt Cavern | Abandoned Mine |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cushion Gas (% of TGIP) | 30 – 50% | 50 – 80% | 20 – 30% | 30 – 50% |
| 2 | Cycling Frequency (per year) | 1 (annual) | 1 (annual) | 6 – 12 (multi) | 1 – 2 |
| 3 | Relative Capex | Low (reuses infrastructure) | High (new infrastructure) | Moderate (solution mining) | Very High (lining + sealing) |
| 4 | Peak Shaving Suitability | Moderate | Low | Excellent | Low |
| 5 | Geological Setting | Structural/stratigraphic trap with prior HC | Anticline in water-bearing permeable rock | Salt dome or bedded salt formation | Lined hard-rock chamber |

**Key Explanations (2 marks):**

- **Depleted reservoirs** are economically the most attractive because the geological seal has been proven over geological time (millions of years) and existing wells, gathering networks, and surface facilities can be reused. However, they have relatively low deliverability per unit volume and a single annual cycle, making them best suited for *seasonal* (winter vs summer) load balancing.

- **Salt caverns** offer the *highest operational agility* — the strong, impermeable salt walls allow multiple cycles per year and rapid injection/withdrawal. They are the preferred choice for *short-duration peak shaving* and emergency gas release, but their TGIP per cavern is small (typically < 1 BCF per cavern).

- **Aquifer storage** is the most expensive and slowest to develop because the trap geometry must be characterized from scratch and no infrastructure exists. It is used only when no depleted reservoir is conveniently located.

- **Abandoned mines** are a last-resort option because lining the rock walls to prevent leakage is technically challenging and expensive. They are used in countries with limited geology options (e.g., parts of Scandinavia).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Six Site Selection Criteria (3 marks, ½ mark each):**

1. **Cap Rock Integrity and Seal Quality:** The seal rock must have zero leakage. The trap must have held hydrocarbons (or any fluid) for geological time, or be proven impermeable by independent testing. Without a perfect seal, stored gas migrates upward and is lost.

2. **Reservoir Porosity and Permeability:** Adequate porosity (≥ 10%) is needed for sufficient gas storage volume. Adequate permeability (≥ 50 mD for depleted reservoirs) is needed for high injectivity and deliverability.

3. **Depth and Pressure:** Storage depth typically ranges from 3,000 to 6,000 ft. Deeper storage = higher pressure = more gas mass per unit pore volume, but also higher drilling cost and risk of cap rock fracturing.

4. **Trap Geometry (Lateral Containment):** A structural closure (anticline, salt dome, fault-bounded block) or stratigraphic trap is essential. Without lateral containment, gas migrates laterally away from the wellbore.

5. **Proximity to Pipeline Network and Markets:** Storage should be close to consumption centers to minimize pipeline construction cost and ensure rapid response during peak demand. For Kerala, proximity to the upcoming Kochi LNG terminal and the southern gas grid would be a major advantage.

6. **Environmental, Safety, and Regulatory Constraints:** Avoidance of populated areas, ecologically sensitive zones (backwaters, forests), seismic fault lines, and other environmentally protected regions. Compliance with the Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD) standards and the Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board (PNGRB) requirements is mandatory.

**Most Critical Criterion (2 marks):**

The *most critical* site selection criterion is **Cap Rock Integrity and Seal Quality**. Reasoning:

- A storage facility with mediocre porosity or permeability can still operate, albeit with higher compression cost and more wells. But a facility with a leaking cap rock is *fundamentally unsafe and uneconomical*: gas escapes to the surface, creating explosion hazards in populated areas and contaminating shallow aquifers.
- Geological seals have been proven over millions of years in depleted hydrocarbon reservoirs — this is why depleted reservoirs are the *most trusted* storage type globally.
- Loss of cushion gas (which is non-recoverable) from cap rock leakage represents a permanent capital loss that cannot be recovered by any operational adjustment.
- Public safety risks from gas migration to basements, sewers, or confined spaces are catastrophic and unacceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B — Question B):**
> 1. **Don't write a paragraph for the table.** Use a properly formatted markdown table; marks are awarded for the comparative structure.
> 2. **Don't omit the cushion gas percentages.** These numerical ranges are what differentiates one storage type from another in the KTU answer key.
> 3. **In Part (b), the question asks for a *justified choice* of the most critical criterion.** A common mistake is to list all six criteria and stop. You must explicitly *pick one* and provide 2 to 3 sentences of *engineering reasoning* — not just assertion.
> 4. **Do not write about criteria not asked for.** The question specifically asks for *six* criteria. Writing eight criteria dilutes your answer and may cause the examiner to award partial credit only for the first six.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU End-Semester Examination**

### Quick-Reference Summary

- **Why underground storage?** Solve the supply-demand mismatch caused by seasonal demand variation; provide strategic reserves; enable price arbitrage.
- **Four types (in order of global share):** Depleted reservoirs (80%) > Aquifers (12%) > Salt caverns (5%) > Abandoned mines (3%).
- **Cushion gas fraction (lowest to highest):** Salt cavern (20–30%) < Depleted reservoir (30–50%) < Abandoned mine (30–50%) < Aquifer (50–80%).
- **Two key gas volumes to remember:**
  - $G_{total}$ = gas at maximum operating pressure
  - $G_{cushion}$ = gas at minimum operating pressure (non-recoverable)
  - $G_w = G_{total} - G_{cushion}$ (the deliverable working gas)
- **Master formula for material balance:** $\dfrac{G_p - G_{inj}}{G} = 1 - \dfrac{\bar{P}/Z}{\bar{P_i}/Z_i}$
- **Master formula for compressor power:** Always include the $\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}$ factor and the efficiency $\eta$ in the denominator.
- **Conservation methods (must memorize):** UGS, LNG, CNG, GTL, Flare Gas Recovery, Reinjection, NGL/LPG Extraction, Hydrates.
- **Most critical site selection criterion:** Cap rock integrity — no compromise on this.
- **Engineering units to always carry in exam answers:** P in psia, T in °R, V in ft³, Q in MMSCFD, $\dot{m}$ in lbm/s.
- **Common numerical mistake to avoid:** Confusing $P_i$ (original) with $P_{ab}$ (current abandonment) in material balance problems.

### Final Exam Strategy

1. **Read the question completely** before writing — note whether initial or abandonment pressure is given.
2. **Always draw a free-body diagram or process flow** for 14-mark questions, even if not explicitly asked.
3. **State assumptions explicitly** in numerical problems — it earns you at least 1 mark.
4. **Box your final answer** — it makes the examiner's job easy and increases full-credit probability.
5. **Use standard KTU textbook terminology:** Cushion Gas, Working Gas, Material Balance, Compressibility Factor, Volumetric Sweep Efficiency, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquefied Natural Gas: Gas Treating Before Liquefaction: Liquefaction Cycles: Storage of LNG
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liquefied Natural Gas: Gas Treating Before Liquefaction: Liquefaction Cycles: Storage of LNG

---

## 1.1 Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) — Formal Definition

**Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)** is natural gas that has been cooled down to approximately **−162 °C (111 K)** at near-atmospheric pressure (slightly above 1 atm), converting it from a gaseous state into a cryogenic liquid. LNG is predominantly **methane (CH₄, ~85–95 mol%)** with smaller fractions of ethane, propane, butane, and trace inerts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT522 Module 4):**
> LNG is the cryogenic liquid form of natural gas obtained by removing heavy hydrocarbons, acid gases (CO₂, H₂S), water, mercury, and other impurities from raw natural gas, followed by refrigeration-based liquefaction. Its volume is reduced to approximately **1/600th** of its gaseous state at standard conditions, making long-distance transport economical.

### Why Liquefy?
- **Volume Reduction:** $V_{\text{LNG}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{gas}}}{600}$ at STP.
- **Transport Viability:** Pipeline transport is uneconomical across oceans; LNG is shipped in cryogenic carriers.
- **Storage Efficiency:** Storing energy in liquid form is denser and safer in large quantities.
- **Market Flexibility:** LNG enables decoupling of production (stranded gas fields) from consumption markets.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Balloon to Ice Cube Journey"

Imagine a **giant balloon** filled with invisible gas particles bouncing around at high speed — that is natural gas at room temperature. To "shrink" this balloon into an **ice cube** that you can fit in your palm, you need to:

1. **Clean the balloon** first (remove bad/dangerous impurities like sour gases and water).
2. **Squeeze and chill it** to extreme cold (liquefaction cycle).
3. **Store the ice cube** in a special thermos (cryogenic storage tank) so it stays frozen.

This three-step philosophy (Treat → Liquefy → Store) defines the LNG value chain.

---

## 1.3 LNG Composition (Typical Feed vs. LNG Product)

| Component | Feed Gas (mol%) | LNG Product (mol%) | Purpose of Removal |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Methane (CH₄) | 85–95 | 95–99 | Main product |
| Ethane (C₂H₆) | 3–8 | < 1–4 | Recovered as NGL |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | 1–3 | < 0.5 | Recovered as NGL |
| Butanes+ (C₄+) | 0.5–2 | < 0.1 | Recovered as NGL |
| CO₂ | 0.1–2 | < 50 ppm | Freeze-out prevention |
| H₂S | 0–1 | < 3.7 mg/Nm³ | Corrosion & safety |
| H₂O | Saturated | < 1 ppm | Hydrate / freeze-out |
| Hg (Mercury) | trace | < 10 ng/Nm³ | Embrittlement of Al |
| N₂, He | 0.5–5 | < 1 | Heating value control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryogenic Hazard:** At −162 °C, CO₂ freezes at −78 °C, water freezes at 0 °C, and mercury freezes at −38.8 °C — all of which would **plug heat exchangers and damage equipment**. Hence gas treating is non-negotiable before liquefaction.

---

## 1.4 The LNG Value Chain (Roadmap of This Module)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Gas Well] --> B[Gas Treating]
    B --> C[Liquefaction]
    C --> D[LNG Storage]
    D --> E[Loading & Shipping]
    E --> F[Regasification Terminal]
```

**Module 4 coverage:**
1. **Gas Treating** — Section 1.5
2. **Liquefaction Cycles** — Section 2
3. **Storage of LNG** — Section 3

---

## 1.5 Gas Treating Before Liquefaction — Overview

Gas treating prepares the feed gas for cryogenic processing. **Each impurity has a specific tolerated limit** because of phase behavior at LNG temperatures.

### 1.5.1 Acid Gas Removal (CO₂ and H₂S Removal)

**Purpose:** Prevent freeze-out, corrosion, and meet product specifications.

| Process | Chemical Basis | Typical Solvent | CO₂ Removal Efficiency |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Amine Treating | Chemical absorption | MEA, DEA, MDEA, DIPA | > 99% |
| Benfield Process | Chemical absorption | Hot K₂CO₃ + activator (DEA/MEA) | 95–99% |
| Selexol | Physical absorption | Dimethyl ether of polyethylene glycol | High partial pressure |
| Sulfinol | Hybrid (chem + phys) | Sulfolane + DIPA/MEA | > 99% |
| Molecular Sieve | Physical adsorption | Zeolite 5A/13X | Very high |
| Membrane Separation | Perm-selective | Cellulose acetate / polyimide | 80–95% |

**Amine Treating Reaction (for CO₂):**
$$\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{R}_2\text{NH} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NCOO}^- + \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEA vs MDEA:** MEA is more reactive but corrosive and energy-intensive. MDEA is selective toward H₂S in presence of CO₂ and is preferred for **selective H₂S removal** with bulk CO₂ slip.

### 1.5.2 Dehydration (Water Removal)

**Purpose:** Prevent hydrate formation, ice plugging, and freeze-out.

| Process | Mechanism | Outlet Dew Point |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| Glycol (TEG) Absorption | Chemical affinity | −40 °C |
| Molecular Sieve Adsorption | Adsorption on zeolite | −80 to −100 °C |
| Supersonic / Membrane | Physical separation | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** LNG plants almost universally use **Molecular Sieve (MS) dryers** downstream of amine units to achieve the stringent **< 1 ppmv H₂O** requirement.

### 1.5.3 Mercury Removal

**Purpose:** Prevent liquid metal embrittlement (LME) of cryogenic aluminum heat exchangers.

- **Process:** Fixed-bed adsorption using **sulfur-impregnated activated carbon** or **metal sulfide (CuS, Ag) on support**.
- **Specification:** < 10 ng/Nm³ Hg.
- **Failure mode:** Amine corrosion + downstream failure of brazed aluminum heat exchangers (BAHX).

### 1.5.4 Heavy Hydrocarbon (NGL) Recovery

**Purpose:** Remove C₂+ (and especially C₅+) which would freeze in main cryogenic heat exchangers (MCHE).

- **Process:** Cryogenic turbo-expander or Joule–Thomson (J–T) plants.
- **Products:** Ethane, LPG (C₃ + C₄), Natural Gasoline (C₅+).
- **Note:** LNG plant is typically designed as a **hybrid** — NGL recovery integrated with liquefaction.

### 1.5.5 Nitrogen and Helium Removal

**Purpose:** Increase heating value (HHV) and reduce sloshing during shipping.

- **Process:** Cryogenic distillation (N₂ column) or **LNG flash / sub-cooling** for N₂ rejection.
- **LNG specification:** HHV typically 38–42 MJ/Nm³.

---

## 1.6 The "Why" Behind Each Treating Step — At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Logic:** Every treating step addresses a **specific failure mode at −162 °C** that would otherwise destroy the liquefaction plant within hours to days. Treating is the **insurance premium** of LNG.

| Impurity | Failure at −162 °C | Treating Step |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|
| CO₂ | Solid CO₂ plugs MCHE | Amine / Molecular Sieve |
| H₂O | Hydrate / ice plugs | TEG / Molecular Sieve |
| Hg | Aluminum embrittlement | Sulfur-impregnated carbon |
| C₅+ | Wax / freeze-out | NGL recovery (turbo-expander) |
| H₂S | Stress corrosion cracking | Amine / scavenger |
| N₂ | Lower HHV, sloshing | N₂ rejection column |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Liquefaction Thermodynamics — The Refrigeration Principle

Liquefaction of natural gas is fundamentally a **refrigeration process**. Work must be supplied to extract heat from the gas at low temperatures.

**General Liquefaction Work Equation (Ideal Work):**
$$W_{\text{ideal}} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S - \Delta H$$

Where:
- $T_0$ = Ambient (sink) temperature, **≈ 300 K**
- $\Delta S$ = Entropy change of the gas
- $\Delta H$ = Enthalpy change during liquefaction

For methane at 1 atm:
- $\Delta H_{\text{liquefaction}} \approx 8.5$ kJ/mol
- $T_0 \cdot \Delta S \approx 19.4$ kJ/mol
- Hence $W_{\text{ideal, methane}} \approx 10.9$ kJ/mol

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of an LNG plant is defined as:
> $$\text{COP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{removed from gas}}}{\dot{W}_{\text{compressor input}}}$$
> Real LNG plants achieve a thermodynamic efficiency of **~ 40–55%** of the ideal work.

---

## 2.2 The Three Core Refrigeration Architectures

Every liquefaction cycle combines three thermodynamic operations:

1. **Compression** (gas → high pressure)
2. **Heat Rejection** (to ambient/cooling medium)
3. **Expansion** (with cooling: J–T or isentropic in turbo-expander)

The "cycle" is defined by **how and where** these operations are combined, and the **refrigerant(s)** used.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Note |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Volume reduction ratio | $V_{\text{LNG}} \approx V_{\text{gas}} / 600$ | Transport feasibility |
| 2 | Ideal liquefaction work | $W = T_0 \Delta S - \Delta H$ | Thermodynamic minimum |
| 3 | Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$ | Determines cooling on throttling |
| 4 | Isentropic work of compressor | $W_s = \frac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$ | Compressor power sizing |
| 5 | Actual compressor work | $W_a = W_s / \eta_{\text{isentropic}}$ | With $\eta_s = 0.70\text{–}0.85$ |
| 6 | Methane boiling point | $T_b = -161.5\ ^\circ\text{C} = 111.7\ \text{K}$ | LNG base temperature |
| 7 | LNG density (at BP) | $\rho_{\text{LNG}} \approx 425\ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Tank sizing |
| 8 | Methane critical point | $T_c = -82.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 46.4\ \text{bar}$ | Phase envelope reference |
| 9 | Mass flow of LNG | $\dot{m}_{\text{LNG}} = \rho_{\text{LNG}} \cdot Q_{\text{vol}}$ | Throughput |
| 10 | Energy in LNG (HHV) | $\text{HHV} \approx 50\text{–}55\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Combustion duty |
| 11 | Acid gas equilibrium (MEA) | $\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{RNH}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{RNHCOO}^- + \text{RNH}_3^+$ | Amine treating Kinetics |
| 12 | Dew point depression | $\text{Dew point} \leq -80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Spec for MS dryer |
| 13 | Tank boil-off rate (BOR) | $\text{BOR} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{boil}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{total}}} \times 100\ \%\ \text{per day}$ | Storage design |
| 14 | Rayleigh number (tank) | $\text{Ra} = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu \alpha}$ | Stratification analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU, you must remember:** LNG density, methane boiling point, volume reduction, and the differences between cascade, mixed-refrigerant, and expander cycles.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Trade-offs in Cycle Selection

| Criterion | Why it Matters |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Specific Power (kWh/kg LNG)** | Lower = more efficient, lower OPEX |
| **Capital Cost** | Capex per MTPA (million tonnes per annum) |
| **Reliability / Train Size** | Large trains demand proven cycles |
| **Turndown capability** | Ability to operate at < 100% load |
| **Feed gas composition** | Lean vs. rich affects cycle choice |
| **Ambient temperature** | Tropical vs. arctic plant design |

**Typical Specific Power Consumption (benchmark for KTU):**
- Cascade: **0.32–0.36 kWh/kg LNG**
- Propane Pre-cooled Mixed Refrigerant (C3MR): **0.28–0.32 kWh/kg LNG**
- Single Mixed Refrigerant (SMR): **0.35–0.40 kWh/kg LNG**
- Dual Mixed Refrigerant (DMR): **0.27–0.30 kWh/kg LNG**
- APCI C3MR (industry standard, largest installed): **≈ 0.29 kWh/kg LNG**

---

## 2.5 Real-world Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The selection of a liquefaction cycle is **the most critical decision** in LNG plant design. It determines the OPEX (compressor power dominates), CAPEX (refrigerant inventory, exchanger size), and operability of the entire plant.

Liquefaction cycles are also used in:
- **Hydrogen liquefaction** (similar cascade cycles).
- **Air separation (LOX/LIN/LAr)** — mixed refrigerant cycles.
- **Peak shaving LNG plants** — small scale SMR/C3MR.
- **Floating LNG (FLNG)** — compact mixed refrigerant cycles dominate.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liquefaction Work for a Simple Linde (J–T) Cycle

The simplest theoretical model is the **Linde cycle**: cool the gas, throttle it, and recover cold from the cold product to cool incoming gas via heat exchanger.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume (Three-Stream Heat Exchanger + J–T Valve)

- Stream A: High-pressure feed entering HX at $T_1 = T_{\text{ambient}}$
- Stream B: High-pressure feed exiting HX, entering J–T valve at $T_2$
- Stream C: Low-pressure liquid + vapor leaving J–T valve, split into liquid (LNG) and recycle vapor

### Step 2 — Energy Balance on the Liquefaction Cold Box

For steady state, ignoring heat loss and pump work, the **First Law** gives:

$$\dot{n}_A h_A + \dot{n}_{\text{ref}} h_{\text{ref,in}} = \dot{n}_L h_L + \dot{n}_V h_V + \dot{n}_{\text{ref}} h_{\text{ref,out}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Isenthalpic Throttling (J–T Valve)

The J–T expansion is isenthalpic, so:
$$h_{\text{feed, at J-T inlet}} = h_{\text{after J-T}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Mass Fractions

Let $x$ = fraction of feed liquefied. Then:
- Liquid fraction = $x$
- Vapor fraction recycled to HX = $(1 - x)$

**Energy balance on HX (ideal, 100% cold recovery):**

$$\dot{m} h_1 = x \dot{m} h_L + (1 - x) \dot{m} h_1$$
which implies **all the cold is recovered** in the ideal case. The real liquefaction work is supplied externally by the **recycle compressor** and **refrigerant**.

### Step 5 — Derive the Liquefaction Fraction

For a simple Linde cycle with cold recovery in heat exchanger:

$$x = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_L}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy of feed at ambient (pre-cooling stage inlet)
- $h_2$ = enthalpy of feed at the J–T valve inlet (after HX)
- $h_L$ = enthalpy of saturated liquid LNG

### Step 6 — Expressed in Temperature Differences

Using $C_p \approx$ const for methane in relevant range:

$$x = \frac{C_p (T_1 - T_2)}{h_1 - h_L}$$

For methane (typical values):
- $C_p \approx 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $h_1 - h_L \approx 510$ kJ/kg (from 300 K gas to LNG at 111 K)
- If pre-cooled to $T_2 = 200$ K: $x = 2.2 \times 100 / 510 \approx 0.43$ (43%)

### Step 7 — Work Required per kg of LNG

The compressor work to bring recycle vapor from $P_{\text{low}}$ to $P_{\text{high}}$ isentropically:

$$W_{\text{specific}} = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_s} \left[ \left( \frac{P_H}{P_L} \right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right] \cdot \frac{1}{x}$$

For typical Linde cycle at 200 bar: $W_s \approx 0.8$ kWh/kg LNG.

> [!NOTE]
> The simple Linde cycle is **impractical for methane** (high pressure + poor efficiency) and is the conceptual foundation upon which all modern cycles are built by adding **multi-stage cooling** and **mixed refrigerants**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mixed Refrigerant (MR) Cycle Simplified Model

A mixed refrigerant is a **blend of C₁, C₂, C₃, i-C₄, n-C₄, N₂** chosen to "match" the cooling curve of the natural gas.

### Step 1 — Composition Tuning

The MR composition is tuned so that, at each pressure level, the MR **boils and condenses** across a temperature range that closely matches the natural gas cooling curve.

**Lean MR (more N₂)** → cold curve at low T
**Rich MR (more C₄+)** → cold curve at higher T

### Step 2 — Energy Balance on the MR Loop

For the closed refrigerant loop (steady state, no leakage):

$$\dot{W}_{\text{MR comp}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{rejected, hot}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{rejected, cold}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{absorbed in cold box}}$$

Where $\dot{Q}_{\text{absorbed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{NG}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{liquefaction}}$.

### Step 3 — Mixed Refrigerant Design Equation

The heat duty in the main cryogenic heat exchanger (MCHE):

$$Q_{\text{MCHE}} = \dot{m}_{\text{MR}} \int_{T_{\text{cold,in}}}^{T_{\text{cold,out}}} C_{p,\text{MR}}(T) dT \approx \dot{m}_{\text{NG}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{NG}}$$

Since the MR is a blend, $C_{p,\text{MR}}$ is composition-weighted:
$$C_{p,\text{MR}}(T) = \sum_{i} y_i \cdot C_{p,i}(T)$$

### Step 4 — Approximate Specific Power for C3MR

In a **C3MR** cycle, the refrigerant mass flow is dominated by propane pre-cooling duty. A heuristic:

$$\text{Specific Power (kWh/kg LNG)} \approx \frac{C_{p,\text{avg}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{overall}}}{\eta_{\text{comp}} \cdot 3600} + \text{MR compressor term}$$

Numerically: **~ 0.29 kWh/kg LNG** for APCI C3MR (typical large plant).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Tank Boil-Off Rate (BOR)

For an LNG storage tank, the boil-off rate is set by:
1. Heat ingress from ambient through insulation.
2. Pressure control venting.

### Step 1 — Heat Influx Through Insulation

For a cylindrical double-wall tank with perlite or foam insulation in the annular space:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L \cdot (T_o - T_i)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{\text{ins}}}}$$

### Step 2 — Vapor Generation

The heat absorbed vaporizes LNG:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{boil}} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{\text{vap}}}$$

Where $\lambda_{\text{vap, methane}} \approx 510$ kJ/kg.

### Step 3 — BOR Percentage

$$\text{BOR (\%/day)} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{boil}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{total LNG}}} \times 100 \times 24$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical BOR for modern LNG tanks:** **0.05–0.10 %/day** (equates to about 0.2% of total energy). Above 0.1 %/day, the design is considered inefficient.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem (solved):** An LNG plant processes 100 million standard m³/day of natural gas. The methane mole fraction in feed is 92%. Calculate LNG production in MTPA (million tonnes per annum).

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Compute daily methane mass flow

Using the ideal gas law at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm):
- $V_{\text{molar}} = 22.414$ m³/kmol
- Methane molar flow: $\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = (100 \times 10^6 / 22.414) \times 0.92$

$$\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{22.414} \times 0.92 = 4.10 \times 10^6\ \text{kmol/day}$$

### Step 2 — Compute methane mass flow

Molecular weight of methane: $M_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.04$ kg/kmol.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CH}_4} = 4.10 \times 10^6 \times 16.04 = 6.58 \times 10^7\ \text{kg/day}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to MTPA

$$\text{Annual} = 6.58 \times 10^7 \times 365 = 2.40 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kg/yr} = 24.0\ \text{MTPA}$$

Including NGL recovery (NGL ≈ 5% of feed) and assuming ~98% recovery, the **LNG product (mostly methane) ≈ 23–24 MTPA**. ✓

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pre-Liquefaction Gas Treating Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Natural Gas from Pipeline] --> B[Inlet Slug Catcher & Filter]
    B --> C[Amine Contactor - Acid Gas Removal]
    C --> D[Amine Regenerator - Stripper]
    D --> E[Acid Gas to Sulfur Recovery / Injection]
    C --> F[Lean Gas Outlet]
    F --> G[Mercury Removal Bed - S-impregnated Carbon]
    G --> H[Dehydration - Molecular Sieve 4A/13X]
    H --> I[NGL Recovery - Turbo Expander Plant]
    I --> J[Heavy NGL to Fractionation]
    I --> K[Lean Dry Sweet Gas to Liquefaction]
    K --> L[N2 Rejection Column]
    L --> M[Liquefaction Unit]
    M --> N[LNG Storage Tank]
```

## 4.2 Propane Pre-Cooled Mixed Refrigerant (C3MR / APCI) Cycle — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Gas at ~40 bar, 30C] --> B[Acid Gas Removal Unit]
    B --> C[Dehydration & Hg Removal]
    C --> D[Propane Pre-Cooler Shell Side]
    D --> E[Main Cryogenic Heat Exchanger MCHE]
    E --> F[Phase Separator - LNG / Vapor]
    F --> G[LNG Product Pump]
    G --> H[LNG Storage]
    F --> I[Flash Vapor Recycle Compressor]

    J[Propane Vapor from Pool] --> K[Propane Compressor K1]
    K --> L[Propane Cooler Air/Water]
    L --> D

    M[MR Vapor from Pool] --> N[MR Compressor K2]
    N --> O[MR Cooler]
    O --> E

    P[MR Liquid from Pool] --> E
    E --> Q[MR Phase Separator]
    Q --> R[MR Recycle to Compressor K2]
```

**Working Principle:**
1. Feed gas is **pre-cooled by boiling propane** at three pressure levels.
2. Pre-cooled gas enters the **MCHE** where mixed refrigerant (MR) boils and absorbs heat.
3. The cold LNG leaves the MCHE bottom, the cold MR vapor returns to its compressor.
4. Two refrigerant loops (propane + MR) operate independently.

## 4.3 Cascade Liquefaction Cycle (Three Refrigerant Loops)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NG Feed at 30 bar] --> B[Pre-Cooling Ethylene Loop]
    B --> C[Methane Loop Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[LNG Phase Separator]
    D --> E[LNG Storage]
    D --> F[Flash Gas Recycle]

    G[Ethylene Loop Compressor] --> H[Ethylene Cooler]
    H --> B

    I[Methane Loop Compressor] --> J[Methane Cooler]
    J --> C

    K[Propylene Loop Compressor] --> L[Propylene Cooler]
    L --> B

    style B fill:#e6f3ff
    style C fill:#e6f3ff
    style G fill:#ffe6e6
    style I fill:#ffe6e6
    style K fill:#ffe6e6
```

**Three Cascading Loops:**
- **Top Loop (Propylene / R22):** Pre-cools NG to ~ −30 °C.
- **Middle Loop (Ethylene):** Cools NG to ~ −90 °C.
- **Bottom Loop (Methane / R23 / R14):** Cools NG to liquefaction (~ −162 °C).

## 4.4 Dual Mixed Refrigerant (DMR) Cycle — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed NG] --> B[Pre-Cool MR Loop - Heavy MR]
    B --> C[Main MR Loop - Light MR]
    C --> D[MCHE]
    D --> E[LNG Storage]
    D --> F[Recycle Compressor]

    G[Heavy MR Compressor] --> H[Heavy MR Cooler]
    H --> B

    I[Light MR Compressor] --> J[Light MR Cooler]
    J --> C

    style B fill:#fff4e6
    style C fill:#e6f7ff
    style G fill:#f0e6ff
    style I fill:#f0e6ff
```

**Key advantage of DMR:** Decoupling pre-cooling and main cooling MR compositions enables higher efficiency (lowest specific power) and is the cycle of choice for **Qatargas 4, Sabine Pass, and Prelude FLNG**.

## 4.5 LNG Storage Tank (Full Containment) — Cross Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concrete Outer Wall - Prestressed] --> B[Insulation - Perlite Vacuum]
    B --> C[Secondary Liner - 9% Ni Steel]
    C --> D[Insulation - Foam Glass or RGL]
    D --> E[Inner Tank - 9% Ni Steel]
    E --> F[LNG at -162C]
    G[Roof - Suspended Deck] --> A
    H[Boil-Off Gas BOG Line] --> E
    I[Bottom Insulation - Load Bearing] --> E
```

**Layers (from outside in):**
1. **Prestressed concrete outer shell** (resists LNG spill + hydrostatic load).
2. **Perlite vacuum insulation** (annular space, often 1.0–1.5 m).
3. **Secondary container (9% Ni steel)** — backup in case primary leaks.
4. **Foam glass / suspended deck insulation.**
5. **Primary inner tank (9% Ni steel)** — holds LNG at −162 °C.
6. **Bottom insulation** — supports tank on concrete pad.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LNG storage tanks must be designed for:** BOG handling, rollover prevention, sloshing (offshore), thermal stratification, and seismic/blast loads (per NFPA 59A / EN 1473).

## 4.6 LNG Value Chain (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Gas Field] --> B[Pipeline / Gathering]
    B --> C[Gas Treating Acid Gas, H2O, Hg]
    C --> D[Liquefaction Plant]
    D --> E[LNG Storage Tank]
    E --> F[Loading Jetty]
    F --> G[LNG Carrier Tanker]
    G --> H[Regasification Terminal]
    H --> I[Gas Distribution Network]
    I --> J[End User Power, Industry, City Gas]
```
