# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the necessity of gas treating before natural gas liquefaction. List the main impurities removed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Raw natural gas contains impurities that cause severe operational and safety problems at cryogenic temperatures of −162 °C. The main reasons for gas treating are:

1. **Freeze-out prevention** — CO₂ freezes at −78 °C, water at 0 °C, and heavy hydrocarbons at higher temperatures. These would plug heat exchangers and process lines.
2. **Corrosion control** — H₂S and CO₂ in presence of water cause severe corrosion in pipelines and equipment.
3. **Equipment protection** — Mercury causes liquid metal embrittlement (LME) of aluminum heat exchangers.
4. **Product specification** — LNG must meet HHV (38–42 MJ/Nm³) and impurity limits (H₂S < 3.7 mg/Nm³).

**Main impurities removed:**
- Acid gases (CO₂, H₂S) → **amine treating** (MEA, MDEA, DEA).
- Water → **glycol (TEG) / molecular sieve** dehydration.
- Mercury → **sulfur-impregnated activated carbon** bed.
- Heavy hydrocarbons (C₃+) → **NGL recovery** (turbo-expander).
- Nitrogen → **cryogenic distillation** (N₂ rejection column).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU):** Students often mention only "removing CO₂" or "removing water." You **must list at least 4 impurities and the corresponding process** to earn full 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare Cascade, C3MR, and DMR liquefaction cycles in terms of number of refrigerant loops, specific power consumption, and typical train capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Cascade | C3MR (APCI) | DMR (Shell) |
|----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| No. of refrigerant loops | 3 (R-22/R-23/R-14 or C₂H₄/C₃H₆/CH₄) | 2 (Propane + MR) | 2 (Heavy MR + Light MR) |
| Specific power (kWh/kg LNG) | 0.32–0.36 | 0.28–0.32 | 0.27–0.30 |
| Train capacity (MTPA) | 1.5–3.0 | 3.0–5.0 | 3.0–7.5 (single train) |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | High |
| Driver | Electric | Steam turbine | Steam turbine |

The **C3MR** is the most widely installed cycle (used in Sakhalin II, Qatar, Gorgon), while the **DMR** offers the highest efficiency for very large trains. The **Cascade** cycle is simple and reliable but has higher energy consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "specific power" with "total power." Specify per kg of LNG produced. Also, do NOT write "single refrigerant" for cascade — it has **three distinct loops**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Module Choice Option 1)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe in detail the C3MR (Propane Pre-cooled Mixed Refrigerant) liquefaction cycle. Include a flow diagram, working principle, refrigerant compositions, and operating conditions. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**An LNG plant processes 1.0 × 10⁹ Nm³/day of natural gas with 92% methane. The methane is liquefied to LNG at 111.7 K with density 425 kg/m³. Calculate:**
- (i) The daily mass of LNG produced (assuming 100% methane).
- (ii) The volume of LNG produced per day.
- (iii) The number of standard tankers (each 145,000 m³ LNG) required per month.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — C3MR Cycle (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The C3MR cycle, developed by Air Products and Chemicals Inc. (APCI), combines **propane vapor compression refrigeration** with a **mixed refrigerant (MR) loop**. It is the **industry workhorse** for large-scale base-load LNG plants, with capacities up to 5 MTPA per train.

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):**
[See SECTION 4.2 of these notes for the schematic block diagram.]

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. **Feed gas treatment:** Acid gases, water, mercury, and heavy hydrocarbons are removed upstream. The dry, sweet gas enters at ~30–40 bar and ~30 °C.

2. **Propane pre-cooling loop:** The feed gas passes through the shell side of a kettle-type **propane vaporizer**, where liquid propane boils at three successive pressure levels (typically 1.5, 5, and 12 bar). The gas exits pre-cooled to about **−35 °C**.

3. **Main Cryogenic Heat Exchanger (MCHE):** The pre-cooled gas flows through the **tube side** of a large spiral-wound brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX), where it is cooled by boiling mixed refrigerant on the shell side.

4. **Phase separation:** The now-cold gas (~−162 °C) enters a **phase separator**; liquid LNG is withdrawn by the LNG product pump to storage pressure (~4 bar), while vapor is recycled to the MR compressor as **flash gas**.

5. **Mixed Refrigerant loop:** The MR (a blend of **N₂, CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, i-C₄, n-C₄**) is compressed, cooled against air/cooling water, condensed, sub-cooled, and returned to the MCHE shell side. It boils across the temperature range from +30 °C down to −162 °C, closely tracking the natural gas cooling curve.

**Refrigerant Compositions (1 mark):**

| Component | Propane Loop | MR Loop |
|-----------|--------------|---------|
| C₃H₈ | 100% (pure) | 20–40 mol% |
| i-C₄H₁₀ | — | 10–20 mol% |
| n-C₄H₁₀ | — | 10–20 mol% |
| C₂H₆ | — | 20–40 mol% |
| CH₄ | — | 5–15 mol% |
| N₂ | — | 0–5 mol% |

**Operating Conditions (0.5 mark):**
- Pre-cooling: 30 °C → −35 °C (propane)
- Main cooling: −35 °C → −162 °C (MR)
- Specific power: 0.28–0.32 kWh/kg LNG

**Advantages (0.5 mark):**
- Proven at large scale (3–5 MTPA per train).
- Lower specific power than cascade.
- Two-loop design is easier to control than DMR.

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Propane inventory is large (fire hazard, inventory cost).
- MR composition control is critical for efficiency.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\dot{V}_{\text{feed}} = 1.0 \times 10^9\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$
- $y_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.92$
- $T_{\text{LNG}} = 111.7\ \text{K}$
- $\rho_{\text{LNG}} = 425\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Molar volume at STP: $V_m = 22.414\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{kmol}$
- $M_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.04\ \text{kg/kmol}$

**Step 1 — Methane molar flow rate (1.5 marks):**
$$\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}} \times y_{\text{CH}_4}}{V_m} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^9 \times 0.92}{22.414}$$

$$\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = 4.10 \times 10^7\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily mass of LNG (assuming 100% methane recovery) (1.5 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{LNG}} = \dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} \times M_{\text{CH}_4} = 4.10 \times 10^7 \times 16.04$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{LNG}} = 6.58 \times 10^8\ \text{kg/day} = 6.58 \times 10^5\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 3 — Daily volume of LNG (1.5 marks):**
$$\dot{V}_{\text{LNG}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{LNG}}}{\rho_{\text{LNG}}} = \frac{6.58 \times 10^8}{425}$$

$$\dot{V}_{\text{LNG}} = 1.55 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly tanker requirement (1.5 marks):**

Tanker volume: $V_{\text{tanker}} = 145{,}000\ \text{m}^3$

Monthly volume of LNG = $1.55 \times 10^6 \times 30 = 4.65 \times 10^7\ \text{m}^3$

$$N_{\text{tankers/month}} = \frac{4.65 \times 10^7}{1.45 \times 10^5} \approx 320.7$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{tankers}} \approx 321\ \text{per month}}$$

**Answer Summary:**
- (i) Daily LNG mass: **6.58 × 10⁸ kg/day = 6.58 × 10⁵ tonnes/day**
- (ii) Daily LNG volume: **1.55 × 10⁶ m³/day**
- (iii) Tanker requirement: **≈ 321 tankers/month**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently **forget to multiply by 30** for the monthly tanker count, or use 31 days. **Always state the assumption (days/month)** and use 30 for safe answers. Also, the volume reduction factor (1/600) is a **quick estimate** only — use exact calculation for full marks.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Module Choice Option 2)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the different types of LNG storage tanks. Compare single containment, double containment, and full containment tanks. Discuss the materials of construction, insulation, and boil-off rate (BOR).**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A full-containment LNG storage tank has an inner diameter of 80 m, height 35 m, and is filled to 90% capacity. The boil-off rate (BOR) is 0.08 %/day. LNG density is 425 kg/m³. Calculate:**
- (i) The mass of LNG stored.
- (ii) The daily mass of boil-off gas (BOG) generated.
- (iii) The volume of BOG generated at standard conditions (T = 273 K, P = 1 atm) per day, assuming methane as the BOG component. Use ideal gas law.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — LNG Storage Tanks (7 marks)**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):** LNG storage tanks are **double-walled, vacuum-insulated, cryogenic pressure vessels** designed to hold LNG at −162 °C with minimal heat ingress and boil-off. They are classified by the level of secondary containment.

**Types of LNG Storage Tanks (3 marks):**

**1. Single Containment Tank:**
- **Inner tank:** 9% nickel steel (or aluminum).
- **Outer containment:** A peripheral **bund wall / dike** (concrete or earthen).
- **Insulation:** Polyurethane foam on inner tank.
- **Use:** Small peak-shaving plants; requires large exclusion zone.
- **Risk:** LNG leak spreads across dike, vapor cloud can travel.

**2. Double Containment Tank:**
- **Inner tank:** 9% Ni steel (primary).
- **Outer tank:** 9% Ni steel or carbon steel, designed to hold entire inner tank contents in case of failure.
- **Insulation:** Perlite in annular vacuum space.
- **Use:** Mid-size plants, on-shore terminals.
- **Advantage:** Reduced safety buffer zone.

**3. Full Containment Tank:**
- **Inner tank:** 9% Ni steel (primary).
- **Outer tank:** Prestressed concrete, designed to hold inner tank contents + hydrostatic + seismic loads.
- **Insulation:** Perlite + foam glass, suspended deck.
- **Use:** Large base-load export plants, FLNG.
- **Advantage:** No buffer zone required; can be located near populated areas.

**4. Membrane Tank (GTT / Technigaz Mark III / NO96):**
- **Primary containment:** Stainless steel corrugated membrane.
- **Secondary:** Secondary membrane + insulation panels.
- **Use:** Large LNG carriers, FLNG, on-shore.
- **Advantage:** Lightweight, suited to marine applications.

**Materials of Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **9% Ni steel:** Excellent low-temperature toughness, used for primary tank down to −196 °C.
- **Prestressed concrete:** Outer shell resists cryogenic shock and BOG explosion.
- **Aluminum (5052/5083):** Used in some membrane systems.
- **Stainless steel 304L:** Membrane inner liners.
- **Perlite:** Vacuum insulation, very low thermal conductivity.
- **Foam glass:** Bottom and roof insulation, non-combustible.

**Boil-Off Rate (BOR) (1.5 marks):**
- Defined as $\text{BOR} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{BOG}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{LNG}}} \times 100\ \%\ \text{per day}$
- Typical BOR: **0.05 – 0.10 %/day** (modern tanks).
- BOG is collected and used as:
  - Fuel gas for plant drivers.
  - Re-liquefaction in MR cycle.
  - Re-injection to gas grid.
- Causes of BOR:
  1. Heat conduction through insulation.
  2. Roll-over (rapid mixing of stratified layers).
  3. Sloshing (offshore).
  4. Pump/line heat leaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **BOR with recondensation rate**. BOR is the mass fraction lost per day as vapor, NOT the vapor flow rate in m³/h. State units clearly.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 80\ \text{m}$ (inner diameter)
- $H = 35\ \text{m}$ (tank height)
- Fill fraction: 90%
- $\text{BOR} = 0.08\ \%/\text{day}$
- $\rho_{\text{LNG}} = 425\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Tank inner cross-sectional area (0.5 mark):**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = 5026.5\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Effective LNG height at 90% fill (0.5 mark):**
$$h_{\text{eff}} = 0.90 \times 35 = 31.5\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — LNG volume stored (1 mark):**
$$V_{\text{LNG}} = A \times h_{\text{eff}} = 5026.5 \times 31.5 = 158{,}335\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Mass of LNG stored (1 mark):**
$$m_{\text{LNG}} = V_{\text{LNG}} \times \rho_{\text{LNG}} = 158{,}335 \times 425$$

$$m_{\text{LNG}} = 6.73 \times 10^7\ \text{kg} = 67{,}292\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Daily BOG mass (1.5 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{BOG}} = \frac{\text{BOR}}{100} \times m_{\text{LNG}} = \frac{0.08}{100} \times 6.73 \times 10^7$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{BOG}} = 5.38 \times 10^4\ \text{kg/day} = 53{,}800\ \text{kg/day}$$

Convert to kg/hr:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{BOG}} = \frac{53{,}800}{24} = 2241.7\ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — BOG volume at STP using ideal gas law (2.5 marks):**

Using $P V = n R T$ with $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $M_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.04\ \text{g/mol}$.

Moles of BOG per day:
$$n = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{BOG, daily}}}{M} = \frac{53{,}800}{0.01604} = 3.355 \times 10^6\ \text{mol/day}$$

Volume at STP (T = 273 K, P = 101,325 Pa):
$$V_{\text{BOG, STP}} = \frac{n R T}{P} = \frac{3.355 \times 10^6 \times 8.314 \times 273}{101{,}325}$$

$$V_{\text{BOG, STP}} = \frac{7.617 \times 10^9}{101{,}325} = 75{,}170\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Convert to $\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$:
$$V_{\text{BOG, STP, hourly}} = \frac{75{,}170}{24} \approx 3132\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Answer Summary:**
- (i) Mass of LNG stored: **6.73 × 10⁷ kg (≈ 67,292 tonnes)**
- (ii) Daily BOG mass: **5.38 × 10⁴ kg/day (≈ 2242 kg/hr)**
- (iii) BOG volume at STP: **≈ 75,170 Nm³/day (≈ 3132 Nm³/hr)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students use **volume fraction** (0.08) instead of **mass fraction**. BOR is a **mass-based** percentage. Also, use **standard conditions (273 K, 1 atm)** for the ideal gas law unless stated otherwise. If you assume BOG is pure methane vapor, you must state it explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (LNG Engineering)**

### **A. Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) Fundamentals**
- LNG is methane-rich cryogenic liquid at **−162 °C (111.7 K)** and ~ 1 atm.
- Volume reduced to **~ 1/600** of gaseous state.
- Methane: **85–95 mol%** in feed; **95–99 mol%** in LNG product.
- LNG density ≈ **425 kg/m³**; HHV ≈ **50–55 MJ/kg**.

### **B. Gas Treating Before Liquefaction**
- **Acid Gas Removal (CO₂, H₂S):** Amine treating (MEA, MDEA, DEA), Benfield, Sulfinol, Selexol, Molecular Sieve.
- **Dehydration:** TEG absorption, Molecular Sieve 4A/13X (LNG plants use MS to < 1 ppmv H₂O).
- **Mercury Removal:** Sulfur-impregnated activated carbon; spec < 10 ng/Nm³.
- **Heavy HC / NGL Recovery:** Cryogenic turbo-expander plant.
- **Nitrogen / Helium Removal:** Cryogenic distillation.

### **C. Liquefaction Cycles**
- **Cascade Cycle:** 3 refrigerant loops (R-22/R-23/R-14); specific power 0.32–0.36 kWh/kg.
- **C3MR (APCI):** Propane pre-cooling + MR; most widely installed; 0.28–0.32 kWh/kg.
- **Single Mixed Refrigerant (SMR):** Single MR loop; simple, low capex; 0.35–0.40 kWh/kg.
- **Dual Mixed Refrigerant (DMR — Shell DMR):** Heavy MR + Light MR; lowest specific power 0.27–0.30; for very large trains.
- **LM2 / LIMUM:** Compact mixed refrigerant for FLNG/small scale.
- **APCI AP-N² / AP-DUAL:** Latest APCI cycles for very large trains.

### **D. Storage of LNG**
- **Single containment:** 9% Ni steel inner + dike; small scale.
- **Double containment:** 9% Ni inner + 9% Ni / carbon outer; medium scale.
- **Full containment:** 9% Ni inner + prestressed concrete outer; large base-load, no buffer zone.
- **Membrane (GTT Mark III, NO96):** Stainless membrane + insulation; for LNG carriers and FLNG.
- **Insulation:** Perlite vacuum + foam glass; bottom: load-bearing foam glass.
- **BOR target:** 0.05–0.10 %/day.
- **BOG uses:** Fuel gas, re-liquefaction, re-injection.

### **E. Critical Numerical Facts to Memorize**
- Methane boiling point: **−161.5 °C = 111.7 K**.
- Methane critical point: $T_c = -82.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 46.4\ \text{bar}$.
- LNG density: **425 kg/m³**.
- Volume reduction: **1/600**.
- Specific power benchmark (C3MR): **0.29 kWh/kg LNG**.
- HHV of LNG: **~ 50–55 MJ/kg**.

### **F. KTU-Favorite Mnemonics**
- **"CASH-HeM-N²"** = **C**O₂/H₂S (amine), **H**₂O (molecular sieve), **H**g (carbon), **M**ercaptans (scavenger), **N**₂ (rejection column).
- **"3C-D-MS"** = Cascade / C3MR / DMR are the three "big" cycles; **MS** = Membrane Storage (membrane tanks).

### **G. Common KTU Exam Traps**
- Confusing "**specific power**" (per kg) vs "**total power**" (per train).
- Using **volume fraction** for BOR (must be **mass fraction**).
- Forgetting that CO₂ freezes at **−78 °C**, well above LNG temperature, hence removal is essential.
- Mistaking **double-wall** for **double containment** in tank classification.
- Calling the MR loop "single refrigerant" when it is actually a **blend** of 4–6 components.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation: Regasification and Cold Utilization of LNG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation: Regasification and Cold Utilization of LNG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Regasification and Cold Utilization of LNG

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of LNG Regasification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LNG Regasification** is the terminal industrial process by which Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG), transported and stored at cryogenic temperatures (approximately **−162 °C** at near-atmospheric pressure), is converted back into its gaseous (vapor) phase so that it can be delivered through conventional natural gas pipelines to end-users (power plants, industrial boilers, city gas distribution networks, and CNG stations).

The regasification process is the final and most critical step in the LNG value chain, bridging the gap between the cryogenic shipping/storage stage and the ambient-temperature gas distribution grid. It is essentially the **reverse operation** of the natural gas liquefaction process, and its efficiency is governed by the **availability of a heat source** capable of providing the required latent heat of vaporization and sensible heat to warm the gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Regasification of LNG is the only practical means of delivering natural gas across oceans where pipelines are infeasible. Every molecule of LNG reaching an import terminal must pass through a regasification plant.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of LNG Cold Utilization (Cold Energy Recovery)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LNG Cold Utilization** (also called **Cryogenic Exergy Recovery** or **Cold Energy Recovery**) refers to the systematic capture and productive use of the vast amount of refrigeration energy stored in LNG (approximately **830–850 kJ/kg** of cold exergy at the receiving terminal) before it is wasted as the gas warms to ambient temperature in the regasifier.

In thermodynamic terms, LNG arriving at the terminal carries enormous **exergy** (availability) because it is at a temperature **far below** the ambient environment. The Second Law of Thermodynamics dictates that this temperature gradient is a usable resource. Instead of letting this cold energy dissipate into seawater or the atmosphere, modern terminals couple the regasification step to secondary processes such as:
- Power generation (using the cold to condense working fluids).
- Air separation (producing liquid oxygen, nitrogen, and argon).
- CO₂ liquefaction (carbon capture supply chain).
- Cold storage warehouses.
- Seawater desalination.
- District cooling systems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Melting Ice-Cube Water Cooler**
> Imagine a sealed cooler filled with ice-cold water bottles at **−5 °C** arriving at your house. You have two choices:
> 1. **Wasteful Path (Conventional Regasification):** Leave the cooler on the porch. The bottles slowly warm to room temperature by absorbing heat from the surroundings. The "cold" that was once stored simply leaks away.
> 2. **Smart Path (Cold Utilization):** Before opening the cooler, you place it in contact with a tray of seawater, freeze the seawater into ice (using the bottles as a heat sink), and then use that ice to cool your house at night. You have extracted a **free, useful side-product** from what would otherwise be wasted cooling.
>
> LNG regasification is identical. The "wasted cold" can be tapped and converted into electricity, industrial refrigeration, or even drinking water — turning a thermodynamic liability into an economic asset.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Values for KTU Board Examinations (Commit to Memory):**
> - **Boiling point of LNG (pure methane):** **−161.5 °C** (111.65 K)
> - **Storage pressure of LNG:** Near-atmospheric, **~1 atm (101.325 kPa)** (slightly above atmospheric to prevent air ingress)
> - **Density of LNG:** **~430–480 kg/m³** (about **1/600th** the volume of gaseous natural gas at standard conditions)
> - **LNG composition by volume:** ~85–99% **methane (CH₄)**, with ethane, propane, butane, and trace nitrogen
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of regasified LNG:** **~52–55 MJ/kg** (≈ **38–42 MJ/m³** gas)
> - **LNG-to-gas volume expansion ratio:** **≈ 600 : 1**
> - **Specific latent heat of vaporization of LNG:** **~510 kJ/kg**
> - **Specific heat capacity of LNG (liquid):** **~3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Cold exergy available in LNG:** **~830 kJ/kg** (varies with terminal temperature and ambient conditions)
> - **Typical regasification plant capacity:** **2.5–20 million tonnes per annum (MTPA)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Regasification Process — Theory and Operation

Regasification is fundamentally a **heat-input** process. The LNG must receive energy in the form of heat from a warmer medium to undergo two sequential thermodynamic changes:

1. **Sensible heating** of sub-cooled liquid LNG from its storage temperature (often **−162 °C** to **−158 °C**, depending on tank pressure) up to its boiling point.
2. **Latent heat of vaporization** at near-constant temperature to convert the saturated liquid to saturated vapor (gaseous methane).
3. **Superheating** of the vapor to the final pipeline delivery temperature (typically **5 °C to 25 °C above ambient**, depending on pipeline operator specifications and gas-quality contracts).

### 2.2 Types of Regasification Technologies

The technology classification is based on the **heat source** used to vaporize the LNG. The three principal industrial technologies are:

#### A. Open-Loop (Seawater) Regasification — Most Common
- **Principle:** LNG is pumped through heat exchanger tubes (vaporizers) immersed in a continuous flow of seawater drawn from the adjacent sea.
- **Heat Source:** Unlimited, free, ambient-temperature seawater (typically **10–30 °C** depending on location).
- **Components:** Submarine intake pipeline → screening facility → circulation pumps → Shell-and-tube or Plate-Frame Heat Exchanger (PFHE) → seawater outfall.
- **Advantages:** No external fuel required, simple, low operating cost, very high thermal efficiency.
- **Disadvantages:** Chlorine and marine-life entrainment; environmental concerns about thermal discharge; only viable at coastal sites.
- **Example:** Most of the world's existing terminals (e.g., **Soyo, Hazira, Dahej**).

#### B. Closed-Loop (Intermediate Fluid) Regasification
- **Principle:** A secondary working fluid (usually **propane**, **butane**, or a **glycol-water mixture**) is heated by an external source (boiler, waste heat, or combustion). The hot intermediate fluid then vaporizes the LNG through a heat exchanger.
- **Heat Source:** Combustion of natural gas (a fraction of the regasified LNG itself), or waste heat from a power plant.
- **Advantages:** Independent of seawater — can be built inland; uses compact **Shell-and-Tube Vaporizers (STV)** or **Submerged Combustion Vaporizers (SCV)**.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires fuel input; higher operating cost; more complex.
- **Example:** Inland terminals, peak-shaving plants, small-scale satellite terminals.

#### C. Air-Based (Ambient Air) Regasification
- **Principle:** LNG is vaporized using heat from ambient air drawn by fans over finned-tube heat exchangers.
- **Heat Source:** Atmospheric air.
- **Advantages:** No water consumption, no marine impact, modular, scalable.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited by ambient air temperature; large footprint; can form ice/frost on fins in humid climates.
- **Example:** Some small-scale LNG satellite plants; remote sites.

### 2.3 Energy Demand of Regasification

The total heat required to regasify LNG per unit mass is calculated as the sum of three contributions. This is the **most-tested** numerical concept in KTU board papers on this topic.

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible,\,liquid} + Q_{latent} + Q_{sensible,\,vapor}$$

Where:
$$Q_{sensible,\,liquid} = m \cdot c_{p,\,liq} \cdot (T_{bp} - T_{storage})$$
$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot \lambda_{vap}$$
$$Q_{sensible,\,vapor} = m \cdot c_{p,\,vap} \cdot (T_{delivery} - T_{bp})$$

### 2.4 Cold Utilization (Cryogenic Energy Recovery) — Theory

The cold exergy of LNG is the most underutilized thermodynamic resource in the gas industry. The **available cold energy** per kilogram of LNG can be expressed as the minimum work required to bring the regasified LNG from ambient back to its cryogenic state (a Carnot-equivalent process):

$$E_{cold} = \int_{T_{LNG}}^{T_{ambient}} \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T}\right) dH$$

For engineering estimation, a simplified usable cold-energy expression is:

$$Q_{cold,\,available} = m \cdot c_{p,\,vap} \cdot (T_{ambient} - T_{LNG}) + m \cdot \lambda_{vap}$$

This corresponds to recovering the **refrigeration duty** of vaporizing the LNG and cooling ambient vapors back to its cryogenic temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cold Utilization Matters (Real-World Utility):**
> 1. **Power Generation:** The cold can condense a working fluid (e.g., CO₂, propane, or a binary mixture) in a closed Brayton/Rankine cycle, producing **200–280 kWh of electricity per tonne of LNG**.
> 2. **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Air, when cooled by LNG cold, liquefies its components, yielding **liquid O₂, N₂, and Ar** — products with high industrial value (steelmaking, hospitals, electronics).
> 3. **CO₂ Liquefaction:** Carbon dioxide captured from flue gas can be liquefied using LNG cold, enabling **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)**.
> 4. **Seawater Desalination:** Freezing seawater onto cold surfaces produces freshwater ice, leaving salt brine — **energy-efficient desalination**.
> 5. **Cold Warehousing:** LNG cold can refrigerate warehouses storing food, pharmaceuticals, or even **LNG-powered ships** using boil-off gas (BOG) reliquefaction.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for Board Exam Numerical Problems**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible heat (liquid warming) | $Q_s = m \cdot c_{p,L} \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ or kW |
| 2 | Latent heat of vaporization | $Q_L = m \cdot \lambda_{vap}$ | kJ; $\lambda \approx 510$ kJ/kg |
| 3 | Sensible heat (vapor superheating) | $Q_v = m \cdot c_{p,V} \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ or kW |
| 4 | Total regasification duty | $Q_{tot} = Q_s + Q_L + Q_v$ | kW |
| 5 | Cold exergy per kg of LNG | $E_{cold} = \int (1 - T_0/T) dH$ | ≈ 830 kJ/kg |
| 6 | Cold energy available (approx.) | $E \approx m \cdot c_{p,V} \cdot (T_{amb} - T_{LNG})$ | kJ |
| 7 | Volumetric gas expansion | $V_{gas} / V_{LNG} \approx 600$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Mass flow from volumetric flow | $\dot{m} = \rho_{LNG} \cdot \dot{V}$ | kg/s |
| 9 | Power recovery from cold cycle | $\dot{W} = \eta_{cycle} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot E_{cold}$ | kW |
| 10 | Seawater required (open loop) | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \dot{Q}_{tot} / (c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw})$ | kg/s |
| 11 | LNG storage temperature | — | **−162 °C** (≈ 111 K) |
| 12 | Boiling point of methane | — | **−161.5 °C** |
| 13 | Methane HHV | — | **55.5 MJ/kg** |
| 14 | LNG density (typical) | — | **~450 kg/m³** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Regasification Heat Duty

**Problem:** Calculate the total heat required to regasify **1 kg of LNG** stored at **−162 °C** and deliver methane gas at **+10 °C** to the pipeline.

**Given Data:**
- Storage temperature: $T_{storage} = -162 \text{ °C} = 111 \text{ K}$
- Boiling point: $T_{bp} = -161.5 \text{ °C} \approx 111.65 \text{ K}$
- Delivery temperature: $T_{del} = +10 \text{ °C} = 283 \text{ K}$
- Mass: $m = 1 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat of liquid methane: $c_{p,L} = 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of vaporization: $\lambda_{vap} = 510 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Specific heat of methane vapor: $c_{p,V} = 2.22 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Sensible heating of liquid:**
The liquid is already at, or very near, its boiling point, so the sensible heat for the liquid is small.
$$\Delta T_L = T_{bp} - T_{storage} = -161.5 - (-162) = 0.5 \text{ K}$$
$$Q_s = m \cdot c_{p,L} \cdot \Delta T_L = 1 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 0.5$$
$$Q_s = 1.75 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of vaporization (liquid → vapor at constant T):**
$$Q_L = m \cdot \lambda_{vap} = 1 \cdot 510 = 510 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heating of vapor (from −161.5 °C to +10 °C):**
$$\Delta T_V = T_{del} - T_{bp} = 283 - 111.65 = 171.35 \text{ K}$$
$$Q_v = m \cdot c_{p,V} \cdot \Delta T_V = 1 \cdot 2.22 \cdot 171.35$$
$$Q_v = 380.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total regasification duty:**
$$Q_{tot} = Q_s + Q_L + Q_v = 1.75 + 510 + 380.4$$
$$Q_{tot} = 892.15 \text{ kJ/kg of LNG}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Vaporizing LNG at the terminal requires roughly **~890 kJ of heat per kg**, which is comparable to the **chemical energy** of gasoline of similar mass. This is why regasification plants are large industrial heat exchangers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cold Energy Recovery — Power Generation Estimate

**Problem:** A regasification terminal handles **1 MTPA (million tonnes per annum)** of LNG. Estimate the **electricity (in MW)** that can be generated if **10% of the cold exergy** is converted at a cycle efficiency of **40%**.

**Given Data:**
- Annual throughput: $M = 1{,}000{,}000$ tonnes/year
- Cold exergy: $E_{cold} = 830 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (engineering estimate)
- Recovery fraction: $f = 0.10$
- Cycle efficiency: $\eta = 0.40$

**Step 1 — Convert annual mass to mass flow rate (kg/s):**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{M \times 10^3 \text{ kg}}{365 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s}}$$
$$\dot{m} = \frac{10^9}{3.1536 \times 10^7}$$
$$\dot{m} = 31.71 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Cold exergy flow available in the LNG stream:**
$$\dot{E}_{cold} = \dot{m} \cdot E_{cold} = 31.71 \cdot 830$$
$$\dot{E}_{cold} = 26{,}320 \text{ kW} = 26.32 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 3 — Usable cold (10% recovery):**
$$\dot{E}_{usable} = f \cdot \dot{E}_{cold} = 0.10 \cdot 26.32$$
$$\dot{E}_{usable} = 2.632 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 4 — Net electrical power output (40% cycle efficiency):**
$$\dot{W}_{elec} = \eta \cdot \dot{E}_{usable} = 0.40 \cdot 2.632$$
$$\dot{W}_{elec} = 1.053 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a modest 1 MTPA terminal can produce **~1 MW of "free" electricity** from cold exergy. Larger terminals (e.g., 10 MTPA) can exceed **20–30 MW** — sufficient to power a small town or the entire terminal's auxiliary load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Seawater Flow Rate (Open-Loop Regasifier Sizing)

**Problem:** An open-loop regasification terminal processes **5 MTPA** of LNG with an average regasification duty of **890 kJ/kg**. Seawater enters at **20 °C** and may be discharged at a maximum of **+5 °C temperature rise** (to comply with thermal pollution regulations). Calculate the required seawater mass flow rate.

**Given Data:**
- Annual LNG: $M = 5 \times 10^9$ kg/yr
- Regasification duty: $q = 890 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\Delta T_{sw} = 5 \text{ K}$
- $c_{p,\,sw} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Mass flow of LNG:**
$$\dot{m}_{LNG} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{3.1536 \times 10^7}$$
$$\dot{m}_{LNG} = 158.55 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Total heat to be transferred to LNG (per second):**
$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot q = 158.55 \cdot 890$$
$$\dot{Q} = 141{,}110 \text{ kW} \approx 141.1 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance (seawater side):**
$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}$$
$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}$$
$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{141{,}110}{4.18 \cdot 5}$$
$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{141{,}110}{20.9}$$
$$\dot{m}_{sw} = 6{,}752 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow (seawater density ≈ 1025 kg/m³):**
$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{6{,}752}{1025} = 6.59 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\dot{V}_{sw} = 6.59 \times 3600 = 23{,}710 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 23.7 \text{ ML/h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** A single 5 MTPA terminal withdraws nearly **24 million litres of seawater per hour**. This is why environmental impact assessments mandate maximum **ΔT = 3–5 °C** and **multi-mesh intake screens** to protect marine ecosystems (fish impingement, plankton entrainment).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Regasification Technologies

| Criterion | Open-Loop (Seawater) | Closed-Loop (Intermediate Fluid) | Air-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Ambient seawater | Combustion / waste heat + secondary fluid | Ambient air |
| **Energy Penalty** | Negligible (no fuel) | ~1–2% of regasified gas burned | Negligible (no fuel) |
| **Suitable for Inland Sites** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Environmental Footprint** | Marine thermal discharge | CO₂ from combustion fan | Ice formation, noise |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium (intake/outfall) | High (boilers, fluid loop) | Low–Medium |
| **Operating Cost** | Lowest | Highest (fuel + fluid) | Low |
| **Cold Utilization Compatibility** | Easy (cold seawater + air) | Possible (with extra exchangers) | Difficult (warm air) |
| **Capacity Range** | Very large (baseload) | Medium to large | Small to medium |
| **Examples** | Dahej, Hazira, Gate | Lake Charles, Tobata | Small peak-shaving plants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Open-Loop LNG Regasification Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LNG1["LNG Carrier Ship"] --> UNLOAD["Unloading Arms and Sendout Pumps"]
    UNLOAD --> STORAGE["LNG Storage Tank minus 162 C"]
    STORAGE --> HP1["HP Sendout Pump 70 bara"]
    HP1 --> VAP1["Seawater Vaporizer ORV or STV"]
    SW1["Seawater Intake 20 C"] --> VAP1
    VAP1 --> SW2["Seawater Outfall 25 C max"]
    VAP1 --> METER["Gas Metering and Quality Skid"]
    METER --> SCENT["Odorization Mercaptan Injection"]
    SCENT --> PIPELINE["Gas Delivery Pipeline 5 to 25 C"]
    VAP1 -.Cold Exergy.-> COLDUSE["Cold Utilization Block ASU or Power Gen"]
    COLDUSE --> PRODUCTS["Liquid O2 N2 Ar or Electricity"]
    PIPELINE --> GRID["City Gas Distribution Network"]
```

**Caption:** Functional flow of a typical coastal LNG regasification terminal. The cold exergy from the vaporizer is tapped and routed to a cold-utilization subsystem, which converts cryogenic energy into industrial products or electricity. The gas is odorized (mercaptan added for leak detection) before pipeline injection.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cold Utilization Cascade (Multi-Product Recovery)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LNG2["Cryogenic LNG minus 162 C"] --> STEP1["Stage 1 Vaporizer minus 130 C"]
    STEP1 --> PROD1["Cold Storage Warehouse minus 25 C"]
    STEP1 --> STEP2["Stage 2 Heat Exchanger minus 80 C"]
    STEP2 --> PROD2["Air Separation Unit minus 180 C ASU"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["Stage 3 LNG to Gas minus 30 C"]
    STEP3 --> PROD3["CO2 Liquefaction minus 55 C"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4["Stage 4 Submerged Combustion Vaporizer"]
    STEP4 --> PROD4["Power Generation Cycle Kovalev or Brayton"]
    STEP4 --> PIPE["Pipeline Delivery 5 to 25 C"]
    ASU --> LN2["Liquid Nitrogen and Liquid Oxygen"]
    CO2L["CO2 Capture Plant"] --> STEP3
```

**Caption:** Multi-stage cold-utilization cascade. Each stage extracts a portion of LNG's cold for a different temperature-grade application. The **cascading use** maximizes the total exergy recovered — a classic application of the Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy minimization by matching temperature levels).

## 4.3 Schematic — Seawater Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Seawater Spray Header Top"] --> B["Tube Bundle of Finned Aluminum Panels"]
    B --> C["Seawater Collection Sump Bottom"]
    LNGX["LNG Inlet minus 162 C"] --> B
    B --> G["Natural Gas Outlet 5 C"]
    FAN["Draft Fan Optional Forced Draft"] --> B
```

**Caption:** In an **Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV)**, LNG flows inside finned tubes while seawater cascades down the outside as a film. The falling seawater evaporates a small fraction, providing the latent heat of vaporization. ORVs are zero-fuel consumers and the industry workhorse for baseload terminals.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LNG regasification. List any two industrial methods used for it.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
LNG regasification is the industrial process of converting LNG from its cryogenic liquid state (stored at approximately **−162 °C**) back to gaseous natural gas at ambient temperature and pipeline pressure (typically **5–80 bar**), so that it can be transported via conventional gas pipelines to end-users.

Two industrial methods:
1. **Open-Loop (Seawater) Regasification** — Uses ambient seawater as the heat source in shell-and-tube or open-rack vaporizers. Common at coastal terminals.
2. **Closed-Loop (Intermediate Fluid) Regasification** — Uses a heated secondary fluid (propane, glycol) in a heat exchanger to vaporize the LNG. Used at inland or peak-shaving terminals.
*(Air-based regasification is also an acceptable third method.)*

### Question 2: What is meant by cold utilization of LNG? Give two examples.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Cold utilization of LNG is the recovery and productive use of the refrigeration energy (cold exergy ≈ **830 kJ/kg**) carried by LNG before it warms to ambient temperature during regasification. Instead of wasting this cold, it is coupled to industrial processes requiring refrigeration.

Two examples:
1. **Air Separation Units (ASU):** LNG cools air to cryogenic temperatures, liquefying O₂, N₂, and Ar for industrial sale.
2. **Power Generation:** A working fluid (e.g., CO₂ or propane) is condensed by LNG cold, then vaporized through a turbine, producing electricity at **200–280 kWh per tonne of LNG**.

*(Other acceptable examples: cold warehousing, seawater desalination, CO₂ liquefaction, BOG reliquefaction on LNG ships.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Regasification Energy Balance and Cold Utilization

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a typical LNG regasification plant based on open-rack vaporizer (ORV) using seawater as the heat source. Discuss the environmental concerns. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A regasification terminal receives LNG at −162 °C and delivers gas at +15 °C to the pipeline. If the throughput is 3 MTPA, calculate (i) the total regasification duty in MW (thermal), and (ii) the mass flow rate of seawater required if seawater enters at 18 °C and leaves at 28 °C. Use: $c_{p,liq} = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_{p,vap} = 2.22$ kJ/(kg·K), $\lambda_{vap} = 510$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,sw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Working of an Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV) Plant:**
1. **LNG Carrier Arrival:** A specialized LNG carrier docks at the marine jetty. LNG is unloaded via marine loading arms into the terminal's insulated storage tank at **−162 °C**, **~1 atm**.
2. **Sendout Pumping:** High-pressure sendout pumps (typical discharge **60–80 bar**) push LNG from the storage tank to the vaporizer battery.
3. **Seawater Circuit:** Ambient seawater (15–30 °C) is drawn through submerged intake structures fitted with **velocity-cap screens** to prevent fish impingement, then pumped to the top of the ORV units.
4. **Heat Transfer in ORV:** LNG circulates inside **finned aluminum tubes** arranged in a vertical rack. Seawater cascades down the outer surface of the tubes as a thin film. Heat transfers from seawater to LNG, vaporizing it. Some seawater evaporates (taking additional latent heat from seawater itself), increasing thermal efficiency.
5. **Gas Conditioning:** The vaporized gas leaves at **5–25 °C** above ambient. It is then metered, odorized (mercaptan injection for leak detection), and delivered to the gas distribution grid.
6. **Discharge:** The warmed seawater is returned to the sea through an outfall diffuser designed to promote rapid mixing.

**Environmental Concerns:**
- **Thermal Discharge:** Returning seawater at +5 to +10 °C above ambient can disrupt local marine ecology (coral bleaching, fish migration shifts).
- **Biocide Use:** Chlorination of intake seawater to prevent biofouling introduces chlorine residuals into the marine environment.
- **Entrainment & Impingement:** Plankton, fish larvae, and small organisms can be sucked into intake pipes or struck against screens.
- **Noise:** Intake pumps and large vaporizer fans generate underwater noise affecting marine mammals.
- **Remedy:** Modern terminals use **5 µm wedge-wire screens**, low-velocity intakes, and **multi-port diffusers** to mitigate impact.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:** $T_{storage} = -162$ °C, $T_{bp} = -161.5$ °C, $T_{del} = +15$ °C = 288.15 K, $M = 3 \times 10^9$ kg/yr.

**Step 1 — Mass flow of LNG (kg/s):**
$$\dot{m}_{LNG} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{365 \times 24 \times 3600} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{3.1536 \times 10^7} = 95.13 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat (liquid, 0.5 K rise):**
$$Q_s = 95.13 \times 3.5 \times 0.5 = 166.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat of vaporization:**
$$Q_L = 95.13 \times 510 = 48{,}516 \text{ kW} = 48.52 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat (vapor, 176.65 K rise):**
$$Q_v = 95.13 \times 2.22 \times (288.15 - 111.65) = 95.13 \times 2.22 \times 176.5$$
$$Q_v = 37{,}279 \text{ kW} = 37.28 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Total duty (i):**
$$\dot{Q}_{tot} = 0.1665 + 48.52 + 37.28 \approx 85.97 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 6 — Seawater flow (ii):** $\Delta T_{sw} = 28 - 18 = 10$ °C
$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{85{,}970}{4.18 \times 10} = \frac{85{,}970}{41.8} = 2{,}057 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{2057}{1025} = 2.007 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 7{,}225 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) **~86 MW (thermal)**, (ii) **~2057 kg/s or ~7225 m³/h of seawater**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Cold Utilization Strategies and Power Generation

**(a)** *Explain the concept of cold exergy of LNG. With a neat sketch, describe how LNG cold can be utilized in (i) power generation, and (ii) air separation. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Calculate the electrical power that can be generated from cold energy recovery at a 4 MTPA terminal. Assume cold exergy = 830 kJ/kg, recovery fraction = 15%, and Rankine cycle efficiency = 38%. Express your answer in MW. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Concept of Cold Exergy:**
Cold exergy (or cryogenic exergy) of LNG is the **maximum useful work** that can be extracted as the LNG warms from its cryogenic storage temperature (~111 K) to the ambient environment temperature (~298 K). Mathematically:

$$E_{cold} = \dot{m} \left[ (h_{amb} - h_{LNG}) - T_0 (s_{amb} - s_{LNG}) \right]$$

For LNG, this value is approximately **830 kJ/kg**, which is comparable to the energy content of small-mass hydrocarbons. Cold exergy represents a **thermodynamically free resource** if the LNG must be warmed anyway during regasification.

**(i) Power Generation (Kovalev Process / Cryogenic Brayton):**
- A working fluid (often **CO₂** or **propane**) is condensed by LNG cold.
- The condensed liquid is then pumped to high pressure and vaporized by ambient seawater or waste heat.
- The high-pressure vapor drives a **turbine**, generating electricity.
- The vapor is then re-condensed by LNG cold, closing the loop.
- **Net Output:** ~200–280 kWh of electricity per tonne of LNG.

**(ii) Air Separation Unit (ASU):**
- Compressed air (at ~6 bar) is cooled in stages against vaporizing LNG.
- The cryogenic air is expanded through a Joule–Thomson valve or turbo-expander, liquefying it.
- Distillation columns separate **O₂ (bp −183 °C)**, **N₂ (bp −196 °C)**, and **Ar (bp −186 °C)** based on boiling points.
- These are stored as cryogenic liquids for industrial sale (steel mills, hospitals, food freezing).

*(Sketch: Multi-stage heat exchanger with cold streams tapping LNG at progressively higher temperatures.)*

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $M = 4 \times 10^9$ kg/yr
- $E_{cold} = 830$ kJ/kg
- $f = 0.15$
- $\eta = 0.38$

**Step 1 — LNG mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{3.1536 \times 10^7} = 126.84 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Total cold exergy flow:**
$$\dot{E}_{cold} = 126.84 \times 830 = 105{,}277 \text{ kW} = 105.28 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 3 — Usable cold (15% recovery):**
$$\dot{E}_{usable} = 0.15 \times 105.28 = 15.79 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 4 — Net electrical output (38% efficiency):**
$$\dot{W}_{elec} = 0.38 \times 15.79 = 6.00 \text{ MW}$$

**Final Answer:** **~6.0 MW of electricity** can be continuously generated from cold exergy recovery at this 4 MTPA terminal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Numerical Problems:** Converting MTPA (million tonnes per annum) to kg/s is the most-skipped step. A wrong time unit (using hours instead of seconds) leads to a 3600× error and **zero marks** in the numerical sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting the Latent Heat Term:** Students often write only $Q = mc_p\Delta T$ and omit $\lambda_{vap}$, losing **3 of 7 marks** in the calculation.
> 3. **Confusing Sensible-Heating Limits:** The liquid is essentially at its boiling point; the liquid-side $Q_s$ is tiny (~0.2% of the total). Students sometimes allocate 50% of the heat to the liquid, which is physically wrong.
> 4. **Reversing the Energy Balance:** In seawater-based questions, students sometimes set $\dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot c_{p,LNG} = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw}$, which is dimensionally inconsistent. The correct balance is on **total duty**, not just one phase.
> 5. **Omitting the Diagram in Part (a):** KTU examiners **mandate a block diagram/flowchart** for full marks on "explain with a neat sketch" questions. A textual-only answer loses **2 marks minimum**.
> 6. **Ignoring the "T0/T" Term in Exergy:** A formal definition of cold exergy must include the Carnot factor $(1 - T_0/T)$. Writing $E_{cold} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ alone is the **refrigeration duty**, not the **exergy**.
> 7. **Environmental Section Skipped:** Even in technical questions, KTU values discussion of thermal pollution, marine impact, and mitigation. Two lines on this topic can be the difference between **12/14 and 14/14**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **LNG regasification** = converting LNG (cryogenic liquid at **−162 °C**) back to natural gas (ambient vapor) for pipeline transport.
- ✅ Regasification is a **heat-input** process requiring **~890 kJ/kg** of LNG (sum of sensible-liquid + latent + sensible-vapor).
- ✅ **Latent heat of vaporization** (~510 kJ/kg) is the **largest** contributor (~57% of total duty).
- ✅ **Three main regasification technologies:** Open-loop (seawater), Closed-loop (intermediate fluid), Air-based.
- ✅ **Open-loop ORV** is the most common — no fuel needed, but environmental concerns (thermal discharge, marine life).
- ✅ **Cold exergy of LNG** ≈ **830 kJ/kg** (Carnot-equivalent available work).
- ✅ **Cold utilization = turning thermodynamic waste into profit** — applications include power generation, ASU, CO₂ liquefaction, desalination, cold storage.
- ✅ **Power generation from cold:** ~200–280 kWh per tonne LNG (cycle efficiency 30–40%).
- ✅ **Seawater flow rule of thumb:** 1 MTPA terminal needs ~**1500–2000 kg/s** of seawater for open-loop regasification.
- ✅ **Volume expansion:** 1 m³ LNG → ~600 m³ of natural gas at STP.
- ✅ **Storage conditions:** Atmospheric pressure (slight positive pressure for safety), boil-off gas (BOG) managed by reliquefaction or as fuel.
- ✅ **Pipeline delivery conditions:** 5–25 °C above ambient, 30–80 bar (typical Indian CGD specifications).
- ✅ **BOG (Boil-Off Gas):** Inevitable evaporation from storage tanks; ~0.05–0.15% per day of inventory. Either reliquefied or used as fuel for terminal boilers/pumps.
- ✅ **Environmental rule:** Maximum seawater ΔT at discharge = **+3 to +5 °C** (regulatory threshold).
- ✅ **Carbon footprint advantage:** Cold utilization in CCS (CO₂ liquefaction) makes LNG a **net-zero pathway** when paired with bio-methane.
- ✅ **Indian context:** Major regasification terminals — **Dahej, Hazira, Kochi, Dabhol (Ratnagiri), Mundra, Chhara** — predominantly open-loop ORV-based.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When answering a Part B question on this topic, always start with a labeled **block diagram** (3 marks reserved), follow with the **process explanation** (3 marks), and end with the **numerical calculation** in neat, unit-consistent steps (8 marks). Marks are awarded for **clarity, units, and logical flow** — not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics: Plant Efficiency: Safety and Environmental Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT522 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economics: Plant Efficiency: Safety and Environmental Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plant Economics in Oil & Gas Engineering

**Plant Economics** in the context of oil and natural gas engineering refers to the systematic analysis of capital investment, operating costs, revenue generation, and profitability of hydrocarbon processing facilities — encompassing upstream (wellhead), midstream (pipelines, storage), and downstream (refineries, petrochemical plants) operations. It integrates **process engineering**, **financial analysis**, and **lifecycle cost management** to ensure that an oil and gas plant delivers maximum economic value while meeting safety and environmental benchmarks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *Plant economics is the discipline of evaluating the financial viability of an oil and gas processing facility by combining technical performance parameters (yield, efficiency, throughput) with cost parameters (CAPEX, OPEX, depreciation) to determine profitability, payback, and return on investment.*

**Intuitive Analogy** 🛢️
> Think of an oil refinery as a "money-printing machine" — but this machine eats up huge amounts of capital to build (CAPEX), consumes fuel, labour, and chemicals daily (OPEX), and has a fixed lifespan. **Plant economics is the "financial doctor"** that monitors whether the machine is healthy enough to keep printing money profitably. Just like a car's mileage (km/litre) tells you its efficiency, *plant efficiency* tells you how much hydrocarbon product is recovered per unit of feedstock — directly dictating profitability.

---

## 1.2 Plant Efficiency

**Plant Efficiency (η_plant)** is defined as the ratio of the actual useful output (product yield, energy recovered, or throughput) obtained from an oil and gas facility to the maximum theoretically possible output, expressed as a percentage. Standard metrics used in KTU-evaluated problems include:

- **Thermal Efficiency** ($\eta_{th}$)
- **Overall Energy Efficiency** ($\eta_{overall}$)
- **Conversion Efficiency** ($\eta_{conv}$)
- **Recovery Efficiency** ($\eta_{rec}$)
- **Plant Availability / Uptime Factor**

**Key Standard Metrics (Industry-Recognized):**

| Metric | Symbol | Industry Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Refinery Utilization | $U$ | **85–95%** |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | **60–92%** |
| Plant Availability | $A_p$ | **>95%** for continuous plants |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC$ | **GJ/tonne** of product |
| Mass Yield | $Y_m$ | **%** of feedstock to product |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A "plant" in the upstream oil & gas context includes drilling rigs, gas processing plants (GTP), LNG liquefaction trains, crude oil stabilization units, and natural gas sweetening units. Each of these has unique efficiency and economic profiles.

---

## 1.3 Safety Considerations

**Process Safety** in oil and gas plants is the disciplined application of engineering, management, and operational principles to prevent catastrophic releases of hydrocarbons that may cause fire, explosion, or toxic exposure. It is governed by frameworks such as **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)**, **API RP 14C**, and **IEC 61511 (SIS)**.

**Core Safety Frameworks:**

1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** — A structured team-based review using guide-words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) on each process line to identify deviations.
2. **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** — Quantitative risk assessment that determines if existing safeguards meet target SIL.
3. **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** — A discrete four-level rating (SIL-1 to SIL-4) measuring the reliability of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
4. **ESD (Emergency Shutdown System)** — Hierarchical shutdown (Levels 0, 1, 2, 3) for rapid plant isolation.
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Last line of defence: helmets, flame-retardant clothing, respirators, gas detectors.

**Intuitive Analogy** 🦺
> Safety systems in an oil plant work like the **airbags, seatbelts, and ABS brakes in a car**. You hope you never need them, but they must work perfectly the *one time* they are called upon. A refinery without proper safety is like a car driving at 150 km/h with no brakes — eventually catastrophic.

---

## 1.4 Environmental Considerations

**Environmental Engineering in Oil & Gas** encompasses the prevention, control, and remediation of pollution arising from exploration, drilling, production, processing, and transportation of hydrocarbons. The discipline ensures compliance with statutory norms (EPA, MoEFCC, CPCB/SPCB) and aligns with global frameworks such as **Paris Agreement (CO₂ reduction)**, **MARPOL**, and **UNEP** guidelines.

**Major Environmental Impact Categories:**

- **Air Emissions:** CO₂, CH₄, SOₓ, NOₓ, VOC, HAPs (Hazardous Air Pollutants)
- **Water Pollution:** Produced water, drilling mud, oil spill, thermal discharge
- **Soil Contamination:** Crude/condensate spill, heavy metal accumulation
- **Noise Pollution:** Compressor stations, drilling rigs, flare noise
- **Solid/Hazardous Waste:** Spent catalysts, slop oil, contaminated soil, drill cuttings

> [!NOTE]
> **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** of methane (CH₄) is approximately **28–36 times** that of CO₂ over a 100-year horizon. Hence, fugitive emissions in oil & gas are aggressively targeted in modern environmental management.

**Intuitive Analogy** 🌱
> An oil plant's environmental footprint is its **"ecological shadow"** — just as a person casts a shadow while walking under the sun, a plant casts a "shadow" of emissions, effluents, and waste. Modern sustainable design aims to make this shadow as small as possible — a concept known as **"Minimum Environmental Footprint Design"**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plant Economics — Capital and Operating Cost Structure

### 2.1.1 Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)

CAPEX represents the **one-time, upfront investment** required to design, procure, construct, and commission an oil and gas facility. It includes:

- **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI):** Equipment cost ($C_{eq}$), installation, instrumentation, piping, electrical, civil works, insulation, painting.
- **Working Capital (WC):** Raw materials inventory, finished goods, cash reserves for operations.
- **Start-up Expenses:** Pre-production trials, commissioning losses, operator training.

**Lang Factor Method (for total plant cost estimation — KTU frequently asked):**

$$C_{plant} = F_{lang} \times C_{eq}$$

Where:
- $C_{plant}$ = Total plant cost (₹ or $)
- $C_{eq}$ = Delivered equipment cost
- $F_{lang}$ = Lang factor (depends on plant type)

| Plant Type | $F_{lang}$ |
|---|---|
| Solid Processing | **3.10** |
| Fluid Processing | **4.74** |
| Both Solid & Fluid | **3.63** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **oil refineries and gas processing plants (fluid processing)**, $F_{lang} = 4.74$ is the KTU-mandated value to remember.

### 2.1.2 Operating Expenditure (OPEX)

OPEX is the **recurring annual cost** to run the plant, including:

- **Raw Materials (Feedstock):** Crude oil, natural gas, catalysts, chemicals
- **Utilities:** Steam, cooling water, electricity, fuel
- **Labour:** Direct + Indirect + Supervisory
- **Maintenance:** Routine, preventive, breakdown
- **Insurance, Taxes, Royalties**
- **Depreciation**

**Annual Operating Cost Equation:**

$$C_{OPEX} = C_{RM} + C_{util} + C_{lab} + C_{mnt} + C_{ins} + C_{dep} + C_{oth}$$

---

## 2.2 Profitability Analysis — The "Why and How"

Profitability metrics are the **decision-making engine** of any oil and gas project. Each metric answers a different question:

| Metric | Question Answered | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Payback Period (PBP)** | "How fast do I get my money back?" | Quick financial screening |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | "Is the project value-positive today?" | Long-term viability test |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | "What is the true earning rate?" | Comparing competing projects |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | "How much profit per ₹ invested?" | Performance benchmarking |
| **Profitability Index (PI)** | "Value per rupee invested?" | Ranking mutually exclusive projects |
| **Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR)** | "Time-value-adjusted return?" | Rigorous economic comparison |

### 2.2.1 Simple Payback Period

$$PBP = \frac{Initial\ Investment\ (CAPEX)}{Annual\ Net\ Cash\ Inflow}$$

- **How it works:** Divides the total capital outlay by the *uniform annual cash profit* to estimate the number of years needed to recover the investment.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the *time value of money* and post-payback cash flows.

### 2.2.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_0$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$
- $i$ = Discount rate (hurdle rate / WACC)
- $C_0$ = Initial capital investment at $t=0$
- $n$ = Project life in years

**Decision Criterion:** Accept the project if $NPV \geq 0$; reject if $NPV < 0$.

### 2.2.3 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$ROI\ (\%) = \frac{Net\ Annual\ Profit}{Total\ Capital\ Investment} \times 100$$

### 2.2.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The IRR is the discount rate $i^*$ at which $NPV = 0$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$$

**Decision Criterion:** Accept if $i^* >$ hurdle rate.

---

## 2.3 Plant Efficiency Formulations

### 2.3.1 Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100\%$$

For a **combustion heater** in a refinery:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{m_{fuel} \times LHV - Q_{losses}}{m_{fuel} \times LHV} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3.2 Plant Availability

$$A_p = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{operating} + T_{downtime}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **5% drop in availability** for a typical Indian refinery processing 10 MMTPA can translate to revenue loss of **₹ 200+ crore/year** at current crude prices — making uptime engineering directly an economic decision.

### 2.3.3 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

$$SEC = \frac{E_{consumed}}{m_{product}} \quad \left[\frac{GJ}{tonne\ of\ product}\right]$$

SEC is widely tracked under **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme of India's Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).

### 2.3.4 Recovery Efficiency (Separators)

$$\eta_{rec} = \frac{m_{product\ recovered}}{m_{feed\ introduced}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 2.4 Safety Frameworks — Risk Quantification

### 2.4.1 Hazard Rate and Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)

The PFD of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) at a given SIL is governed by IEC 61511:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times \lambda_{DU} \times T_{proof}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{proof}$ = Proof test interval (hours)

### 2.4.2 SIL — Target Risk Reduction Factor

| SIL | PFD Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| SIL-1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | **10–100** |
| SIL-2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | **100–1000** |
| SIL-3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | **1000–10,000** |
| SIL-4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | **10,000–100,000** |

### 2.4.3 Individual Risk and Societal Risk

$$IR = f \times P \quad \text{(probability/year)}$$

Where $f$ = frequency of incident, $P$ = probability of fatality given exposure.

> [!NOTE]
> Tolerable individual risk in the oil & gas sector is typically **$10^{-5}$/year** (workers) and **$10^{-6}$/year** (public) per UK HSE standards.

---

## 2.5 Environmental Quantification

### 2.5.1 Carbon Footprint (CO₂-equivalent)

$$CO_{2e} = \sum_{i} m_i \times GWP_i$$

Where $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of pollutant $i$ (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = **28–36**, N₂O = **265–298**).

### 2.5.2 Air Quality Index (AQI) — Key Pollutants

For refinery operations, regulated pollutants are **PM, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, and Benzene**.

### 2.5.3 Oil Spill Dispersion (Simple Fay Equation)

For a spill in calm water, the slick radius $R(t)$ at time $t$:

$$R(t) = k \cdot t^{1/2}$$

Where $k$ depends on oil type, water temperature, and spreading regime (gravity–inertial, gravity–viscous, surface tension–viscous).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plant Cost (Lang) | $C_{plant} = F_{lang} \cdot C_{eq}$ | $F_{lang} = 4.74$ (fluid) |
| 2 | Simple Payback | $PBP = CAPEX / Annual\ Cash\ Inflow$ | years |
| 3 | NPV | $NPV = \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_0$ | ₹ / $ |
| 4 | ROI | $ROI = \frac{Net\ Profit}{CAPEX} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | IRR | $\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$ | % (solve iteratively) |
| 6 | Plant Availability | $A_p = \frac{T_{op}}{T_{op} + T_{down}}$ | % |
| 7 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | SEC | $SEC = E_{consumed} / m_{product}$ | GJ/tonne |
| 9 | Recovery Efficiency | $\eta_{rec} = m_{product}/m_{feed} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | CO₂-Equivalent | $CO_{2e} = \sum m_i \cdot GWP_i$ | tonnes CO₂e |
| 11 | SIL — PFD | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Individual Risk | $IR = f \cdot P$ | per year |
| 13 | Capital Recovery | $CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Annualized Cost | $A = CAPEX \times CRF + OPEX$ | ₹/year |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineers use these formulas during **Feasibility Studies (FEL-1, FEL-2, FEL-3)**, **FEED (Front-End Engineering Design)**, and **EPC tender evaluation**. A 14-mark KTU question on plant economics often revolves around Lang factor, PBP, and NPV — so prioritize these three.

---

## 2.7 Why Each Concept Matters in Oil & Gas Engineering

- **CAPEX vs OPEX trade-off:** A well-designed heat exchanger network (HEN) increases CAPEX by 5% but reduces OPEX (steam) by 20% — payback within 2 years. This *is* plant economics in practice.
- **Plant Availability:** Every unplanned shutdown in a gas processing plant can cost **\$1–5 million/day** in lost product.
- **Safety Systems:** A single refinery accident (e.g., Jaipur 2009) can cause losses exceeding **₹ 2,000 crore** plus reputational damage — SIL-3 systems are non-negotiable for hydrocarbon service.
- **Environmental Compliance:** Non-compliance with MoEFCC norms can lead to **plant shutdown orders**, as seen in several CPCB enforcement actions in India.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) — The Heart of Annuitized Economics

The **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** converts a present-value lump sum (CAPEX) into an equivalent uniform annual payment over $n$ years at interest rate $i$. This is essential for comparing projects with different lifespans.

### Step 1: Begin with a single future-value relationship

The future value $F$ of a present sum $P$ after $n$ years at interest rate $i$ is:

$$F = P \cdot (1 + i)^n$$

### Step 2: Define equivalent uniform annual payment $A$

We seek an annual payment $A$ such that, accumulated at interest $i$ for $n$ years, it equals $F$:

$$F = A \cdot \left[ \frac{(1 + i)^n - 1}{i} \right]$$

This is the **standard future-value of annuity** formula.

### Step 3: Equate the two expressions for $F$

$$P \cdot (1 + i)^n = A \cdot \left[ \frac{(1 + i)^n - 1}{i} \right]$$

### Step 4: Solve for $A/P$ ratio (the CRF)

$$\frac{A}{P} = \frac{i \cdot (1 + i)^n}{(1 + i)^n - 1}$$

$$\boxed{CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

### Step 5: Validate with a numerical check

Suppose $i = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 5$ years.

$$\begin{aligned}
(1+i)^n &= (1.10)^5 = 1.61051 \\
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1.00} \\
&= \frac{0.161051}{0.61051} \\
&= 0.26380
\end{aligned}$$

This means: a present investment of ₹ 1,00,000 demands an equivalent annual payment of ₹ 26,380 over 5 years at 10% interest. ✓

### Step 6: Annuitized Total Cost

For an oil and gas plant:

$$C_{annualized} = CAPEX \times CRF + OPEX$$

This is the **total annual cost** used in **KTO (Kremser-Treybal Optimization)** and equipment-replacement studies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Present Value (NPV) — Discounted Cash Flow Method

### Step 1: Recognize that money in the future is worth less than money today

A cash flow $CF_t$ received at the end of year $t$ has a *present value* $PV_t$:

$$PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1 + i)^t}$$

### Step 2: Sum all present values over project life $n$

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} PV_t = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + i)^t}$$

### Step 3: Subtract the initial investment (CF₀ is conventionally negative)

$$NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + i)^t}$$

### Step 4: Numerical example (typical KTU 14-mark problem)

**Given:** Initial investment $C_0 = ₹ 1,00,000$. Annual cash inflows of ₹ 30,000 for 5 years. Discount rate $i = 12\%$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.12)^1} + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.12)^2} + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.12)^3} + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.12)^4} + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.12)^5}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each discount factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV_1 = 26{,}785.71 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV_2 = 23{,}915.82 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV_3 = 21{,}353.41 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV_4 = 19{,}065.54 \\
(1.12)^5 &= 1.7623 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV_5 = 17{,}022.81
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of PVs:

$$\sum PV_t = 26{,}785.71 + 23{,}915.82 + 21{,}353.41 + 19{,}065.54 + 17{,}022.81 = 1{,}08{,}143.29$$

Therefore:

$$NPV = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}143.29 = +₹\ 8{,}143.29$$

**Decision:** $NPV > 0$ → **Project is accepted.** ✓

### Step 5: Comparison with simple Payback (Same Problem)

$$PBP = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = 3.33\ years$$

But this ignores time value of money and post-payback benefit. NPV is therefore the **superior metric**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Efficiency of a Fired Heater (Refinery Application)

### Step 1: Energy balance on the fired heater

The heater receives fuel of mass $m_f$ with lower heating value $LHV$ and delivers heat $Q_{useful}$ to the process stream. Heat losses occur through flue gas, radiation, and convection.

$$m_f \cdot LHV = Q_{useful} + Q_{loss}$$

### Step 2: Express losses

$$Q_{loss} = m_f \cdot LHV \cdot \left(1 - \eta_{th}\right)$$

### Step 3: Definition

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{m_f \cdot LHV} \times 100$$

### Step 4: Alternative form via flue gas oxygen

For a heater with measured excess air and stack temperature $T_s$:

$$\eta_{th} \approx 100 - \left( \frac{T_s - T_a}{CO_2\%} \right) \cdot k$$

Where $T_a$ = ambient, $CO_2\%$ = flue gas CO₂ concentration, $k$ = empirical constant (Siegert's formula variant).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: PFD for a 1oo1 (One-out-of-One) SIL System

### Step 1: Failure rate model

A single sensor has a constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures/hour).

### Step 2: Average probability of failure on demand

Between proof tests of interval $T_{proof}$, the failure rate integrates linearly:

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Numerical example

If $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year):

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \\
&= 0.0219 \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls in the **SIL-1 range** ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$). ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: CO₂-Equivalent (CO₂e) Emission Calculation

### Step 1: Define the metric

$$CO_{2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i \times GWP_i$$

### Step 2: Numerical example (a gas processing plant emitting CH₄ and CO₂)

- $m_{CO_2} = 50{,}000$ tonnes/year, $GWP_{CO_2} = 1$
- $m_{CH_4} = 200$ tonnes/year, $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_{2e} &= 50{,}000 \times 1 + 200 \times 28 \\
&= 50{,}000 + 5{,}600 \\
&= 55{,}600\ \text{tonnes CO₂e/year}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Even small CH₄ leaks matter — 200 tonnes of CH₄ contributes 5,600 tonnes CO₂e, equivalent to the annual emissions of ~1,200 cars.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Safety Strategies

| Strategy | Capital Cost | Risk Reduction | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BPCS only** (Basic Control) | Low | $RRF = 1$ | Often insufficient for hydrocarbon |
| **BPCS + Alarm + Operator** | Low–Medium | $RRF \approx 2$–$10$ | One "layer" in LOPA |
| **Add SIL-1 SIF** | Medium | $RRF = 10$–$100$ | For low-hazard service |
| **Add SIL-2 SIF** | Medium–High | $RRF = 100$–$1000$ | Typical for separators, pumps |
| **Add SIL-3 SIF + Fire Protection** | High | $RRF = 1000$–$10000$ | For compressors, fired heaters |
| **Mechanical Relief (PSV/PVRV)** | Variable | Last line of defence | Always required regardless |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Environmental Control Technologies

| Pollutant | Source in O&G Plant | Control Technology | Removal Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | Sulfur in fuel | **Wet FGD, Dry SDA** | 90–98% |
| NOₓ | Combustion | **SCR, SNCR, Low-NOₓ burners** | 60–90% |
| PM | Catalyst dust, soot | **ESP, Baghouse, Cyclone** | 99%+ |
| VOC | Storage tanks, fugitive | **Vapour Recovery Unit (VRU)** | 95–99% |
| CO₂ | Combustion, venting | **Amine-based CCS, MEA/MDEA** | 85–95% |
| CH₄ (fugitive) | Leaks, vents | **LDAR programme, Vapor recovery** | 40–80% |
| Produced water | Oil-water separator | **API, CPI, TPI, Membranes** | 95–99% oil removal |
| Spent catalyst | Hydrocracker | **Metal reclamation, secure landfill** | NA |

```mermaid
    wwtp -.DISCHARGE.-> envMonitor
    vru -.EMISSION.-> envMonitor
    envMonitor --> report[COMPLIANCE REPORT TO CPCB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Solid lines represent **process flow** of hydrocarbons. Dotted lines represent **safety/environmental streams** (relief, drains, emissions). The CEMS block at the end symbolizes the *continuous monitoring layer* mandated by India's MoEFCC under the 2017 OCEMS notification.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define plant availability. A gas processing plant operates for 8,400 hours in a year and records 360 hours of planned shutdown plus 240 hours of unplanned shutdown. Calculate the plant availability.**

**Model Answer:**

> Plant availability is the percentage of total time that a plant is actually available for production, including both operating time and standby time, but excluding scheduled maintenance downtime.
>
> **Given:** Operating time = 8,400 hr, Planned shutdown = 360 hr, Unplanned shutdown = 240 hr.
>
> **Total time in year = 8,400 + 360 + 240 = 9,000 hours**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_p &= \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{operating} + T_{downtime}} \times 100 \\
> &= \frac{8{,}400}{9{,}000} \times 100 \\
> &= 93.33\%
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Plant availability = 93.33%** ✓

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any four major sources of air emissions from a crude oil refinery and state one control technology for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Source | Pollutant | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fired Heaters/Boilers** | NOₓ, SO₂, CO₂ | Low-NOₓ burners, Wet FGD |
| 2 | **FCC Unit Regenerator** | PM, CO, SOₓ | Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) |
| 3 | **Storage Tanks** | VOC | Vapour Recovery Unit (VRU) |
| 4 | **Flare System** | HC, CO₂, soot | Flare Gas Recovery System |
| 5 | **Fugitive Equipment Leaks** | VOC, CH₄ | LDAR Programme (Leak Detection and Repair) |
