## 5.2 KTU Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** A proposed gas processing plant requires a total capital investment of **₹ 50 crore** and is expected to generate a uniform annual after-tax cash inflow of **₹ 8 crore** for a project life of **10 years**. The salvage value at the end of 10 years is **₹ 2 crore**. The corporate hurdle rate is **12% per annum**.

Compute:
- (i) The simple Payback Period
- (ii) The Net Present Value (NPV)
- (iii) Comment on the project's acceptability. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) study methodology** as applied to an oil and gas processing plant. List the standard guide-words used and explain the role of the multidisciplinary team. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Given:** CAPEX = ₹ 50,00,00,000 (₹ 50 Cr); Annual Cash Inflow (A) = ₹ 8,00,00,000 (₹ 8 Cr); n = 10 years; Salvage (S) = ₹ 2,00,00,000 (₹ 2 Cr); i = 12% = 0.12.

**(i) Simple Payback Period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= \frac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow} \\
&= \frac{50}{8} \\
&= 6.25\ years
\end{aligned}$$

**PBP = 6.25 years** ✓

**(ii) NPV Calculation:**

The cash flow in year 10 includes both the annual inflow and the salvage value.

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -50 + \sum_{t=1}^{9} \frac{8}{(1.12)^t} + \frac{(8+2)}{(1.12)^{10}}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the discount factors:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^5 &= 1.7623 \\
(1.12)^6 &= 1.9738 \\
(1.12)^7 &= 2.2107 \\
(1.12)^8 &= 2.4760 \\
(1.12)^9 &= 2.7731 \\
(1.12)^{10} &= 3.1058
\end{aligned}$$

The present-value annuity factor for 9 years (at 12%):

$$\begin{aligned}
PVA_{9,12\%} &= \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-9}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.3606}{0.12} = \frac{0.6394}{0.12} = 5.3282
\end{aligned}$$

The PV of years 1–9 cash flows:

$$PV_{1-9} = 8 \times 5.3282 = 42.6256\ \text{Cr}$$

The PV of year 10 cash flow (including salvage):

$$PV_{10} = \frac{10}{3.1058} = 3.2195\ \text{Cr}$$

**Total NPV:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -50 + 42.6256 + 3.2195 \\
&= -50 + 45.8451 \\
&= -4.1549\ \text{Crore}
\end{aligned}$$

**NPV = −₹ 4.15 crore (Negative)** ✗

**(iii) Comment:** Since NPV < 0, **the project is NOT financially acceptable** at a 12% discount rate. The simple payback of 6.25 years is also longer than the typical 5-year threshold for O&G projects. Management should either negotiate a lower CAPEX, a higher throughput, or seek a tax holiday to improve viability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **add the salvage value to the year-n cash flow** in the NPV formula. This leads to wrong sign of NPV. Also, ensure the PVA factor is calculated for **(n−1) years** if the salvage is added separately in year *n*. (Alternatively, treat year-10 cash as 8 + 2 = ₹ 10 Cr and use PVA for 10 years plus a salvage adjustment — both methods give the same answer.)

---

#### **Model Solution to (b): HAZOP Study**

**Definition:** HAZOP is a **structured and systematic qualitative risk assessment technique** used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant by examining deviations from design intent.

**Standard HAZOP Guide-Words and their meanings:**

| Guide-Word | Meaning | Example Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of design intent | NO FLOW through pipeline |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | MORE TEMPERATURE (overheating) |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | LESS PRESSURE (vacuum) |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional component | TWO PHASES AS WELL AS liquid in gas line |
| **PART OF** | Only part of the design intent achieved | PART OF composition (impure feed) |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | REVERSE FLOW (backflow) |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | OTHER THAN water (wrong chemical) |

**HAZOP Team Composition (Multidisciplinary):**
- HAZOP Team Leader (independent, experienced)
- Process Engineer
- Operations/Production Engineer
- Instrument & Control Engineer
- Safety Engineer
- Maintenance Engineer
- Process Chemist
- Scribe / Recorder

**HAZOP Procedure:**
1. Divide the plant into **nodes** (sections of piping/equipment).
2. Apply each guide-word to each process parameter (flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition).
3. Identify **causes, consequences, and existing safeguards**.
4. Recommend **actions** to close any safety gaps.
5. Document everything in a **HAZOP worksheet**.

**Application to Oil & Gas:** HAZOP is mandatory for any new or modified process unit handling hydrocarbons above their flash point, as per OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) standards in India.

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** methodology. A separator operates with a base failure rate of **$1 \times 10^{-1}$/year** for overpressure. The following independent protection layers (IPLs) are proposed:

- BPCS control loop: PFD = **$1 \times 10^{-1}$**
- Operator intervention: PFD = **$1 \times 10^{-1}$**
- SIL-2 SIF: PFD = **$1 \times 10^{-2}$**
- Pressure Safety Valve (PSV): PFD = **$1 \times 10^{-2}$**

The corporate tolerable risk for overpressure is **$1 \times 10^{-5}$/year**. Determine if the total risk reduction is sufficient. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **major environmental impacts of an offshore oil drilling platform** and the mitigation measures employed. Compare **EIA (Environment Impact Assessment)** and **EMP (Environment Management Plan)**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a): LOPA**

**Concept:** LOPA is a **semi-quantitative risk assessment** technique that uses order-of-magnitude estimates of event frequency and protection layer PFDs to evaluate if the mitigated event frequency meets a target.

**Total Mitigated Frequency Formula:**

$$f_{mitigated} = f_{initiating\ event} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

**Given:**
- Initiating event frequency $f_{IE} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$/year
- 4 IPLs with PFDs: $10^{-1}, 10^{-1}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-2}$

**Step 1: Compute combined PFD of all IPLs**

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{total} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute mitigated event frequency**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{mitigated} &= f_{IE} \times PFD_{total} \\
&= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{events/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compare with tolerable risk**

$$f_{mitigated} = 1 \times 10^{-7} < f_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Result:** The mitigated frequency is **100 times lower** than the corporate tolerable risk. **The protection is more than sufficient.** ✓

**Conclusion:** The LOPA study demonstrates that the layered safeguards collectively reduce the overpressure risk to well within the corporate acceptance criteria. No additional IPL is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — KTU Valuation:** Students often add PFDs instead of multiplying them. **PFDs of INDEPENDENT layers are multiplied, not added.** Also remember: BPCS is considered as the *initiating event* in some texts but as an IPL in others — be consistent with the convention used in your course module. Here, BPCS is treated as the first IPL (per OISD).

---

#### **Model Solution to (b): Environmental Impacts of Offshore Drilling**

**Major Environmental Impacts:**

| # | Impact | Cause | Mitigation Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Marine water pollution** | Drill cuttings, mud, oil spill, produced water | **Zero-discharge drilling fluid; Cuttings re-injection; BWRO treatment** |
| 2 | **Air emissions** | Power generation, flaring, venting | **Gas turbine with low-NOₓ; flare gas recovery; vapor return lines** |
| 3 | **Seabed disturbance** | Anchoring, jack-up spud cans, pipeline laying | **Routed survey; seasonal restrictions; rerouting** |
| 4 | **Noise to marine life** | Pile driving, drilling, shipping | **Acoustic dampeners, marine mammal observers (MMO), soft-start piling** |
| 5 | **Discharge of chemicals** | Mud additives, biocides | **Substitution with green chemicals; closed-loop systems** |
| 6 | **Oil spill risk** | Blowout, collision, pipeline rupture | **BOPS (Blowout Preventer); SBM/OBP; OPRC compliance** |
| 7 | **Solid waste** | Spent chemicals, food waste | **Onshore disposal; incinerators; compactors** |

**Comparison: EIA vs. EMP**

| Feature | **EIA (Environment Impact Assessment)** | **EMP (Environment Management Plan)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Predict and evaluate likely environmental impacts *before* project execution | Define the actions, responsibilities, and monitoring plan *during* execution and operation |
| **Stage** | Pre-project (Feasibility / Design) | Construction + Operation phase |
| **Focus** | Identification & quantification of impacts | Implementation of mitigations |
| **Statutory basis (India)** | EIA Notification 2006 (MoEFCC) | Part of consent conditions under Water Act, Air Act, EP Act |
| **Output** | EIA Report (predicts impact magnitude) | EMP Document (lists mitigations, schedule, budget) |
| **Example** | "Drilling will increase TSS to 50 mg/L in 1 km radius" | "Use SBM mud; install silt curtain; monitor TSS daily" |

**EIA is the "What could happen?"** — EMP is the "**What will we do about it?**"

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting salvage value in NPV:** Always add it to year-n cash flow. −2 to −3 marks if missed.
> 2. **Using ₹ vs. Crore inconsistently:** Keep all numbers in the **same unit** (Crore OR Lakh OR ₹) throughout the solution.
> 3. **Wrong Lang factor:** $F_{lang} = 4.74$ for fluid processing (oil refineries, gas plants). Using 3.10 or 3.63 will yield wrong cost and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Misapplying Plant Availability formula:** Use the correct denominator. Some students use 8,760 hr/year as denominator; the correct denominator is *total time* the plant was required to operate, which may be less (e.g., 8,400 hours actual operation + planned shutdown + unplanned shutdown).
> 5. **Confusing PBP with Discounted PBP:** Simple PBP does NOT use present values. Discounted PBP does. KTU often asks both — label clearly.
> 6. **Mixing up PFD with PFD_avg:** For 1oo1 architecture, $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}$, not just $\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}$.
> 7. **GWP values must be current IPCC AR6 values:** CH₄ = 27.9 (fossil) or 29.8 (biogenic), not older 21 or 25. Loss of 1 mark in Part A.
> 8. **HAZOP guide-words:** Always list ALL 7 standard guide-words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN). Partial list = partial marks.
> 9. **LOPA — Adding PFDs:** This is the single most common conceptual error. PFDs are multiplied across *independent* layers. +1 to +2 marks lost per error.
> 10. **EIA vs EMP confusion:** KTU often sets this as a 3-mark Part A question. Remember: EIA is *predictive*, EMP is *operational*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

### **Module 4 — Quick Revision Checklist**

- [x] **CAPEX** = one-time setup cost (Lang: $C_{plant} = F_{lang} \cdot C_{eq}$, $F_{lang} = 4.74$ for fluid plants)
- [x] **OPEX** = annual recurring cost (raw materials, utilities, labour, maintenance)
- [x] **Payback Period** = CAPEX / Annual Cash Inflow (no time value)
- [x] **NPV** = $\sum CF_t / (1+i)^t - C_0$; accept if NPV ≥ 0
- [x] **IRR** = discount rate at NPV = 0
- [x] **ROI** = (Net Profit / Investment) × 100
- [x] **CRF** = $i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$
- [x] **Plant Availability** = $T_{op} / (T_{op} + T_{down})$; typical target **>95%**
- [x] **Thermal Efficiency** = $Q_{useful} / Q_{input}$
- [x] **SEC** = Energy per unit product (GJ/tonne) — lower is better
- [x] **HAZOP** uses 7 guide-words and a multidisciplinary team
- [x] **LOPA** multiplies PFDs of independent layers
- [x] **SIL** ratings: SIL-1 to SIL-4; PFD drops by factor of 10 per level
- [x] **ESD** hierarchy: ESD-0, ESD-1, ESD-2, ESD-3
- [x] **GWP** of CH₄ ≈ 28–36 (IPCC AR6: 27.9 fossil, 29.8 biogenic)
- [x] **CO₂e** = $\sum m_i \cdot GWP_i$
- [x] **Air pollution control:** ESP (PM), FGD (SO₂), SCR (NOₓ), VRU (VOC)
- [x] **Water pollution control:** API separator, CPI, TPI, biological treatment, RO
- [x] **EIA is predictive, EMP is operational** — KTU's favourite 3-mark distinction
- [x] **Inherent safety > Engineered safety > Procedural safety > PPE** (hierarchy of controls)
- [x] **Tolerable individual risk:** $10^{-5}$/year (worker), $10^{-6}$/year (public)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **HAZOP guide-words:** "**N**o **M**ore **L**anguage, **A**s Well As **P**lease, **R**ead **O**ther Books" → **N**, **M**, **L**, **A**, **P**, **R**, **O**
> - **Hierarchy of controls:** "**I**n **E**ngineered **P**rocess, **P**eople **P**rotect themselves with **PPE**" → Inherent > Engineered > Administrative > PPE
> - **EIA vs EMP:** "EIA is **E**valuation; EMP is **E**xecution"
